It’s a heartfelt novel, tender and painful and cathartic all at once, and even if the characters belong to New York, the story belongs to us all.

title: Wolf Hollow
author: Lauren Wolk
description: In your lifetime as a reader, you have probably heard many books likened to Harper Lee’s classic, To Kill a Mockingbird, and you’ve probably been let down by most of them. Not all books with literary aspirations told from a child’s point of view are created equal. But when reviewers draw a parallel between Mockingbird and Lauren Wolk’s Wolf Hollow, they are being neither hyperbolic, nor lazy. They are merely doing justice to Wolk’s beautiful story about 11-year-old Annabelle and the year she spends terrorized by the town bully, attempting to protect the shell-shocked World War I veteran who has retreated to Annabelle’s corner of rural Pennsylvania to try and find himself again. (For ages 10 and up)

title: You Will Know Me
author: Megan Abbott
description: There is a moment in this thriller/mystery/work of literary fiction (enough with the labels — it’s a good book, people!) when a mother discovers the diary of her teenage daughter, a star gymnast. Of course, she reads it. Wouldn’t you?  How much does this mother know, or not know about her daughter?  How much can we ever know about those closest to us? Author Megan Abbott will draw you in with plot — there’s a crime, a hit-and-run — and keep you reading with weightier questions.

title: You'll Grow Out Of It
author: Jessi Klein
description: As I read You’ll Grow Out of It, I kept saying to myself: “I’ve been there! This is my life!” In this smart, funny collection of essays, Jessi Klein describes disastrous wedding dress fittings, her dislike of baths, and why she still attends exercise classes, knowing full well she’ll never look like the instructor. Even when she tackles serious topics — including her struggles with infertility and ageism in Hollywood — Klein will keep you laughing.

title: 18 And Life On Skid Row
author: Sebastian Bach
description: Rock-and-roll memoirs should have lots of sex, drugs and drama, and Sebastian Bach’s 18 and Life on Skid Row hits all the marks. I was never really a fan of his band, Skid Row, but Bach’s memories of his exploits – from getting punched in the nose by (and then befriending) a Hells Angel to doing a mountain of blow with Metallica and having his grandma walk in – keep the story moving at a frenzied clip. But what I really found intriguing was his insight into the machinery, hard work and genetic luck that creates a frontman. (That said, he is a ‘90s rock-and-roll star: Some of his stories about women are hard to read.)

title: 4 3 2 1: A Novel
author: Paul Auster
description: Archibald Isaac Ferguson is born on March 3, 1947, at the start of Paul Auster’s miraculous 4 3 2 1. After that, this book splits into four narratives as the same boy lives out four different lives, shaped by notable events such as the assassination of JFK, riots in New York and the student revolt at Columbia. Each Ferguson makes different decisions and follows different passions. It’s a massive book (866 pages!), but try it anyway: Auster pulls it off with tenderness and skill and adds a delightful twist at the end as a reward for finally arriving.

title: 5 Worlds Book 1: The Sand Warrior
author: Alexis Siegel and Mark Siegel, illustrated by Xanthe Bouma, Matt Rockefeller and Boya Sun
description: After only a couple of pages of this epic graphic novel, I was hooked. The Five Worlds can only be saved if five iconic beacons are lit by three unlikely young heroes. 5 Worlds Book 1: The Sand Warrior by Alexis Siegel and Mark Siegel, will keep you turning the pages, even as the gorgeous art by Xanthe Bouma, Matt Rockefeller and Boya Sun draws you in. Be prepared to be dazzled by this tale of mystery, intrigue and heart. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: A Conspiracy In Belgravia (The Lady Sherlock Series)
author: Sherry Thomas
description: Sherry Thomas’ Lady Sherlock series, of which this is the second book, takes a straightforward premise – what if Sherlock Holmes had been a woman instead of a man – and explores every possible facet of it in a pitch-perfect, fascinating and gloriously historically accurate manner. True, Charlotte Holmes, her sister Livia and her companion, Mrs. Watson, do get to have adventures few women could have in 1880. But rather than erasing the oppressive systems designed to stamp out lives like theirs, Thomas shows her characters walking the era’s sociocultural tightropes, which raises the stakes. And that’s in addition to telling a mystery that Arthur Conan Doyle would be proud to call his own – and developing not one, but two love stories he could never have created in his wildest dreams.

title: A Separation: A Novel
author: Katie Kitamura
description: Must we merge completely when we partner up? What do we really know about one another? A mystery undergirds this tale of a wife who goes to a foreign locale to piece together what happened to her husband, from whom she was quietly separating. A Separation makes you feel a little off balance throughout the read. Katie Kitamura creates a specific sense of place and a story that moves, but don’t pick it up unless you’re comfortable with a few questions left unanswered.

title: Affluence Without Abundance: The Disappearing World Of The Bushmen
author: James Suzman
description: After spending 25 years studying one of the last hunter-gatherers on Earth, James Suzman has an incredibly unique perspective. He has glimpsed what life was like for our ancestors tens of thousands of years ago – and it was surprisingly wonderful! Learning about the Bushmen’s way of life changed the way I think about our modern culture. It made me realize what parts of my life bring genuine joy and how a slight shift in the perception of time can bring peace and comfort.

title: After Kathy Acker: A Literary Biography
author: Chris Kraus
description: How do you recount the life of someone who obsessively cultivated a self-mythology? Kathy Acker was an author of the punk literary scene who seemingly rubbed shoulders and bumped uglies with everyone before she died in 1997. I tore through this biography as if it were a mystery novel. Chris Kraus pieces together Acker’s story through the evidence left behind: her books, her letters, her financial records and the memories of her contemporaries. It’s hard to tell whether Kraus uncovers the events of Acker’s life or the fable she left behind – it’s a slippery matter, and perhaps, in the end, they’re one and the same.

title: Age Of Anger: A History Of the Present
author: Pankaj Mishra
description: Why is the world so angry? Why Brexit? Why the rise of nationalism? Why are large swaths of the world suddenly so attracted to demagogues? Pankaj Mishra, attempting to make sense of this confusing world of ours, makes a compelling, erudite case that the disrupting forces of globalization and rising income inequality inevitably provoke backlashes – and points to the turn of the last century, when as the world was modernizing, those left behind turned in frightening ways to demagoguery, violence, hatred of the other and a desired return to some mythical past.

title: Ali: A Life
author: Jonathan Eig
description: It has been more than 35 years since Muhammad Ali’s last match, but he is still considered “The Greatest,” not just because of his one-time dominance in the ring, but because of his outsize personality. Jonathan Eig’s brilliant, exhaustive look at the boxer’s life is the biography the champ deserves: a beautiful portrait of a man whose name will never be forgotten, who carried a torch for equality and justice and lit a fire that will never go out.

title: All Grown Up
author: Jami Attenberg
description: Andrea Bern is a single, childless, almost 40-year-old art school dropout working in advertising. She is remarkably good at avoiding her life – not taking on promotions at her job, not visiting her family while they care for a sick child – which can make her a frustrating protagonist. Regardless, All Grown Up is impossible to put down. Clever, funny and occasionally cringe-inducing, this novel is a referendum on a culture in which being a narcissist is more socially acceptable than being a single woman.

title: Amanda Wakes Up: A Novel
author: Alisyn Camerota
description: Amanda Gallo is a local TV anchor until she covers a breaking-news story, without pants, and lands her dream job on a national network run by a ratings-obsessed media mogul. Also it’s campaign season, and the two front-runners are a female senator and a wild-card candidate, a former actor. Sound familiar yet? It’s the 2016 election, but much more fun and with more juicy romantic entanglements. And author Alisyn Camerota, who is herself a veteran journalist, promises she isn’t psychic, even though she started writing this book years ago.

title: American Fire: Love, Arson, And Life In A Vanishing Land
author: Monica Hesse
description: Monica Hesse’s book reads like a great article you never want to end. The Washington Post feature writer has turned a one-off assignment in rural Accomack County, Va., into a stunning read. This true crime love story gets in the heads of two arsonists who lit more than 80 fires on Virginia’s Eastern Shore. Hesse spent several months reporting on the community that lived through the fires, from volunteer firefighters to overworked police officers to the people whose homes burned to the ground. This story of love, betrayal, economic hardship and desolation offers a nuanced view of life in small-town America.

title: American War: A Novel
author: Omar El Akkad
description: I am not a big reader of post-apocalyptic fiction. I don’t have much patience for all that world building. But this novel by Omar El Akkad breaks all the conventions. It’s America split in two by another civil war and destroying itself, but it is told as a close portrait of a young girl who is hero, pawn and devil.

title: An American Sickness: How Healthcare Became Big Business And How You Can Take It Back
author: Elisabeth Rosenthal
description: If you’ve ever wanted to understand why hospitals charge $20 for one measly Advil, or why they’re busily upgrading the marble in their lobbies, here’s a book for you. Elisabeth Rosenthal built her career as a reporter at The New York Times and has amassed a long catalog of horror stories from the world of hospitals, doctors and pharmaceutical companies. But An American Sickness does more than that. Dr. Rosenthal (she has an M.D.) looks at the wasteful incentives built into our health care system. This book promises tips to help you fight back, but be prepared to engage when you’re sick and at your most vulnerable. (A word of warning: If you’re prone to high blood pressure, consider consuming this book in small doses.)

title: An Odyssey: A Father, A Son, And An Epic
author: Daniel Mendelsohn
description: Along with his work as a contributor and critic at The New Yorker and The New York Review of Books, Daniel Mendelsohn teaches classics at Bard College. He was preparing to teach his seminar on The Odyssey when he got a call from his 81-year-old father, Jay, who wanted to join the class. Mendelsohn let him in, but soon regretted it: He proved to be a disruptive influence, often challenging the professor, his own son. After the class ended, father and son went on a Mediterranean cruise that toured sites from The Odyssey, and Mendelsohn saw a new side of his dad: a charming storyteller who delighted the ship’s other passengers. In this memoir, Mendelsohn beautifully weaves the story of his relationship with his father with a scholarly but accessible reading of Homer’s epic.

title: An Unkindness Of Ghosts
author: Rivers Solomon
description: Transposing the cruelties of a Southern plantation to outer space, this book was the best debut I read this year. The white people on the massive spaceship Matilda’s upper decks live in luxury and keep the black people on the lower decks enslaved. Aster is black, neuroatypical, ambiguously gendered according to her society’s mores, and an orphan of the lower decks. While an outsider in her social groups, she is also a medical genius, and with the support and friendship of a light-skinned surgeon from the upper decks, she navigates Matilda’s horrors to bring succour and healing where she can.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture"]

title: An Unkindness Of Magicians
author: Kat Howard
description: World Fantasy Award nominee Kat Howard takes sorcery to the streets of New York in her urban flight of fancy, An Unkindness of Magicians. Briskly paced yet intricately plotted, the novel imagines a conspiracy of magic lurking amid backstreets and skyscrapers – a tale that’s thrilling, densely packed and reminiscent of Neil Gaiman in its imaginative power.

title: And Your Daughters Shall Prophesy: Stories From The Byways Of American Women And Religion
author: Adrian Shirk
description: Adrian Shirk’s debut is a lyrical pilgrimage through faith traditions and the stories of the female spiritual leaders, astrologers and preachers who helped shape America’s religious identity. It’s also a fascinating memoir about the author’s own search for truth and community. Shirk is a generous writer whose penchant for detail and poetic observation will surprise even the staunchest skeptic; the chapter titled “Our Bodies, Our Smoke” is a nicotine-tinged portrait that delivers some of the best writing on the connection between smoking and the physical act of writing.

title: Anne Boleyn, A King's Obsession: A Novel
author: Alison Weir
description: Alison Weir’s novel is a persuasive attempt to restore the humanity of a tragic, misrepresented figure, one of history’s original nasty women. Anne Boleyn ruled England alongside Henry VIII for only three years, from 1533 to 1536, before she famously lost her head. But the couple had a bumpy six-year courtship before that, an extended, aphrodisiacal game of hide-and-seek. Weir’s doomed Anne is ferociously smart and guilty of nothing but craving the power that’s rightfully hers to claim. The book suggests that beheading might be the price to pay for having a mind of one’s own.

title: Anything Is Possible: A Novel
author: Elizabeth Strout
description: This book of short stories grew out of Elizabeth Strout’s novel My Name is Lucy Barton. The stories are linked by the town where the characters grew up, and where many still live, and by its most famous resident Lucy Barton, who is now a successful writer living in New York. The stories fill in the details of Lucy’s impoverished and abused childhood. Each introduces a different character: a Vietnam vet who never really recovered from the war, the former school janitor who learns a faith-shattering truth when he visits Lucy’s brother. Shame, regret, anger and pain lurk beneath the surface of these lives, but so do love, kindness and redemption. Elizabeth Strout has a way of looking into the soul of her characters and finding something to make you care.

title: Arabia Felix: The Danish Expedition Of 1761-1767
author: Thorkild Hansen, translated by James McFarlane and Kathleen McFarlane
description: Look, I get it: The name doesn’t exactly scream page turner. I admit I was skeptical, too, before I cracked this one open. But don’t fall prey to the same misconception I did. This tale of an 18th century scientific expedition gone awry (first published in Danish in 1962 and back out in a new English edition) is a brutal, beautiful story of human ideals – and all the clever ways we manage to muck them up. Part history, part fiction, part travelogue, this book balances its central tragedy with some winks and smirks. And yes, it’s true: Before long, I found those pages practically turned themselves.

title: At The Lightning Field
author: Laura Raicovich
description: The sublime, as novelist Mary Shelley would describe it, marks a strange midpoint of sensations that occur when mixing both fear and beauty – that state of wooziness spurned by witnessing things like waterfalls, canyons or great works of art. Moved by  The Lightning Field, an enormous work of  “Land Art” by sculptor Walter De Maria, Laura Raicovich’s essay swallows its subject and gains its strength. It is a testament to At the Lightning Field’s power that the artwork somehow feels exponentially more beautiful experienced through Raicovich; the overall effect is that of a warm, glowing tingle – a paean to the things that, regardless of their gravity or size, awe us.

title: Autonomous: A Novel
author: Annalee Newitz
description: In a near-future, post-apocalyptic world where patents last indefinitely, governments are indistinguishable from corporations and the creation of artificial intelligence has paved the way for people to become property on a global scale, Judith “Jack” Chen is a pirate who has dedicated her life to the development and distribution of free drugs. When one of those drugs starts causing people to literally work themselves to death, she calls on old friends to help fix her mistake and take down the big corporation responsible – all while two militant members of the International Property Coalition are hot on her trail. Full of unbearably tender musings on AI intimacy and bubbling with ideas, Autonomous is a thought-provoking ride.

title: Autumn
author: Karl Ove Knausgaard
description: The not-quite-poems, not-quite-essays that make up Autumn are a gentle introduction to this Norwegian author who famously wrote a six-volume autobiographical series called My Struggle. Each chapter of this book is just a few pages, with deep observations of subjects ranging from forgiveness to toilet bowls. Readers can be cultish in their devotion to Karl Ove Knausgaard’s work, and while I’m still not a member of the cult, this slim book encouraged me to look more closely at the world. I don’t think I’ll ever take a bite of an apple in quite the same way.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters"]

title: Autumn: A Novel
author: Ali Smith
description: Shortlisted for this year’s Man Booker Prize, Ali Smith’s beguiling, beautifully written novel is about a dying centenarian and a young woman who used to be his neighbor. It’s an elusive, kind-of love story – just not in the way most people are used to. It’s also a post-Brexit novel, with that summer’s events performing as both the background hum and the power that propels the narrative forward.
tags: ["Art", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Bad Dreams And Other Stories
author: Tessa Hadley
description: The 10 instantly immersive tales in Tessa Hadley’s latest meticulously observed collection zoom forward and back decades, resulting in rich complexity, breadth and satisfying closure – rare in short fiction. Hadley is especially drawn to regretted moments or subtly life-altering experiences: a bored 15-year-old girl’s disturbing sexual initiation back in the 1960s or an unhappy young wife’s awkward attempt to shed her sheltered innocence, which she sees as “a sign of something maimed or unfinished” in her. There’s nothing maimed or unfinished about these stories.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Based On A True Story: A Novel
author: Delphine de Vigan
description: Delphine de Vigan’s Based on a True Story is a powerful novel of suspense that may or may not be based on truth. Like the author, “Delphine” is a middle-aged Parisian author who has written a tell-all memoir. Here, Delphine-the-author is haunted by her success and exhausted, too. When she falls in with an acquaintance named L., who pushes the boundaries of friendship, de Vigan plays with the idea of who tells a story, and how, with epigrams from Stephen King’s Misery to underscore that play. A creepy tale of female friendship gone wrong; vampirism of the emotional kind.

title: Basketball (and Other Things): A Collection Of Questions Asked, Answered, Illustrated
author: Shea Serrano, illustrated by Arturo Torres
description: Shea Serrano’s Basketball (And Other Things) is equal parts hilarious and enlightening. Paired with gorgeous illustrations by Arturo Torres, the book is an absorbing 33-chapter compilation of basketball questions and pop culture musings. Serrano’s riffs and tangents – there’s a partial screenplay for an action film titled Death Hammer 2: Hammergeddon – are often purposefully ridiculous, but always entertaining. From breaking down what might have been had the Orlando Magic’s Nick Anderson made even one of his free throws at the close of Game 1 of the 1995 NBA finals, to ranking the most disrespectful dunks ever, this is the ultimate book on basketball fandom.

title: Bellevue: Three Centuries Of Medicine And Mayhem At America's Most Storied Hospital
author: David Oshinsky
description: For many New Yorkers, “Bellevue” is synonymous with “hospital,” and the institution has had an outsized effect on the evolution of modern medicine, treating presidents and paupers alike. Hospital history might not sound like the recipe for a gripping read, but David Oshinsky, a Pulitzer Prize-winning historian, knows that people like me love a morbid tale of pestilence and human failings, especially when it’s leavened with compassion and innovation. Though Bellevue started out an 18th-century poorhouse where people went to die, it became an incubator of advances that helped create modern medicine, including the germ theory of infection, ambulances, maternity wards and forensic pathology.

title: Between Them: Remembering My Parents
author: Richard Ford
description: Nobody gives “ordinary” human beings their due with the grace and precision that Richard Ford does. His slim new memoir about his parents is so gently spellbinding that I’ve already read it twice. Ford’s father, Parker, was a traveling bleach salesman in places like Mobile, Ala., and Little Rock, Ark., during the 1930s and early ‘40s. Ford’s mother, Edna, (and later young Richard) would often go along for the ride, stopping at the grocery stores that dotted his father’s route and staying at the small hotels and rooming houses that were the homes-away-from-home to traveling salesmen. Part of the bonus of this little book is that it sits readers down in the company car and takes us on an unsentimental but enchanted journey through the long-ago landscape of the American South.

title: Beyond The Bright Sea
author: Lauren Wolk
description: An abandoned baby set adrift in an old boat, the whisper of pirate treasure, and a cat named Mouse? Be warned, once you pick up Beyond The Bright Sea, you won’t be able to put it down. The baby, adopted by a man with his own mysteries, is now 12 years old. Shunned by the locals all her life, Crow discovers the shocking reason why. Lauren Wolk’s writing is at once lyrical and fraught with danger and suspense, as Crow follows her instincts to hunt down a past that has eluded her and face an unknown future. (For ages 10 and up)

title: Botox Nation: Changing The Face Of America
author: Dana Berkowitz
description: Fifteen years ago, almost no one had heard of Botox. Then the FDA approved using botulinum toxin to temporarily smooth wrinkles, and today, millions of Americans get injections each year. It has become as routine as getting your hair colored. But why are we so freaked out by aging? Berkowitz unpacks the anti-aging industry in ways that will make you think.

title: Breathless
author: Beverly Jenkins
description: Ambitious, cautious Portia Carmichael manages a hotel in the Arizona Territory. Kent Randolph, who may have finally grown up and wised up, is courting her, one step at a time – and his respect for Portia’s intellect adds a warmth that grows as readers spend time in their community. Women raise one another up in vivid, resonant scenes, feeding and caring for one another. Jenkins always delivers comfort reading with a hefty dose of historical clarity, and this book reminded me when I needed it most that through rough days and painful moments that there are always people who can help you rediscover happiness.

title: Brimstone
author: Cherie Priest
description: Alice Dartle is a witch, from a long line of witches, seeking her fortune as an independent woman of the 1920s in Cassadega, Fla., learning from and living among a thriving community of clairvoyants. Tomás Cordero is a bereaved tailor recently back from the Great War, haunted by a spirit who communicates in random acts of arson. Tomás travels to Cassadega seeking Alice’s help, but fires start easily in Cassadega and its witches refuse to burn. Though muddled by pacing issues in its middle, this is a warm, charming, very loving book, with well-drawn characters whose happiness I cheered on throughout.

title: Bunk: The Rise of Hoaxes, Humbug, Plagiarists, Phonies, Post-Facts, And Fake News
author: Kevin Young
description: It’s no secret that America is going through a fake news moment. There are people in this country who continue to believe that Barack Obama was born in Kenya and that Sept. 11 was an inside job. In Bunk, New Yorker poetry editor Kevin Young explores America’s obsession with falsehoods. He tells NPR that there’s something particularly American about hoaxes. “There is, in our culture, this notion of you can become anything, you can change,” he says. “And I think that gets intertwined with the hoax.”

title: Chemistry: A Novel
author: Weike Wang
description: I loved this book by first-time novelist Weike Wang, one of the National Book Foundation’s “5 Under 35.” Full of fun science facts, Chemistry is about a Chinese immigrant who was raised to be an overachiever. The unnamed storyteller, a scientist in a Boston chemistry lab, has just melted down in said lab, shattering beakers and freaking out her co-workers. This is a character I can relate to: ambivalent about commitments, crushed by parental expectations and hilarious in a nerdy scientist way.

title: Chester B. Himes: A Biography
author: Lawrence P. Jackson
description:
Chester Himes’ life story, as described in this vivid biography, is just as surreal as those detective novels (like *Cotton Comes to Harlem*) that boosted his literary reputation. While attending Ohio State University in the late 1920s, Himes fell into bad company. After a couple of serious brushes with the law, he robbed a white couple inside their home. The upshot was the stuff of nightmares: In a little over the space of one year, Himes went from the state university to the state penitentiary. In prison, he began writing stories that eventually were published in *Esquire*. 
Himes, like the literature he created, was difficult and sometimes cruel; but biographer Lawrence P. Jackson demonstrates why he’s well worth the trouble.

title: Cinemaps: An Atlas Of 35 Great Movies
author: Andrew DeGraff, with essays by A.D. Jameson
description: With artist Andrew DeGraff’s whimsically stylized yet painstakingly constructed maps, you can track Luke Skywalker’s circuitous path from Tatooine to Yavin IV, follow Roger Thornhill’s route from New York City to Lincoln’s nose atop Mount Rushmore, and walk in the footsteps of Jack Torrance through the hedge maze of The Shining’s Overlook Hotel. These maps aren’t drawn to scale, but what they lack in rigorous precision they more than make up for in color, clarity and crazily detailed thoroughness.

title: Conversations With Friends: A Novel
author: Sally Rooney
description: Sally Rooney is an astute observer of conversation – in this case, the conversations of 21-year-old Frances, a brilliant (but occasionally self-destructive) college student and poet living in Dublin. This book is thoroughly modern – it’s sprinkled with instant messages and references to Greta Gerwig films and Tinder – but it also feels classic in its keen observations on relationships, jealousy and writing itself. It is essentially a novel about adultery and friendship, but the plot is secondary to the sparkling dialogue. Rooney is just a few years older than her protagonist, and the book feels so honest and specific one can’t help but wonder whether it’s somewhat autobiographical.

title: Dadland
author: Keggie Carew
description: This riveting blend of personal memoir, biography and World War II military history grew from Keggie Carew’s determination to get a handle on her irrepressible firebrand of a father as he lapsed into dementia in his 80s. As a member of the Jedburghs, an elite multinational special operations resistance force that parachuted behind enemy lines in France and Burma, Tom Carew was a true hero – at war, if not at home. One of the issues his daughter explores with sensitivity and humor is the challenge of following such death-defying exploits with the relative doldrums of domestic life.

title: Dare Not Linger: The Presidential Years
author: Nelson Mandela and Mandla Langa
description: It has been four years since Nelson Mandela died and 19 since he began chronicling his tenure as South Africa’s first democratically elected (and first black) president. Writer Mandla Langa has worked with Mandela’s unfinished manuscript to complete Dare Not Linger, a sequel to 1994’s Long Walk to Freedom. Dare Not Linger is not the sort of book you can swallow in one gulp, so don’t be in a hurry.  In an interview, Langa said he wants the book to help South Africans remember when “we held the moral high ground, when we walked with our heads held high.” The book’s title is taken from the final few paragraphs of Long Walk, where Mandela writes: “I can rest only for a moment, for with freedom comes responsibilities, and I dare not linger, for my long walk is not ended.” A warning, perhaps, to South Africa’s future leaders not to forget what it took to jettison colonialism and apartheid and build a nation based on equality.

title: Dark At The Crossing
author: Elliot Ackerman
description: Of all the new generation of vet writers, Elliot Ackerman has seen the most war – he earned a Silver Star in the second battle of Fallujah and later hunted Taliban with Afghan irregulars along the Pakistani border. But he skipped the traditional war-story-debut-novel in favor of more daring stuff in both his first novel, and now again with Dark At The Crossing.  The book follows Haris, an Iraqi-American at the blurry edge of the war in Syria, where contractors, militants, aid workers and refugees wait and conspire on the Turkish side of the border. He catches every detail of the exile purgatory, where most residents don’t know whether they’re moving toward heaven or hell, or which direction is which. With the shooting war echoing from the south, Ackerman looks at the civil war inside his characters as they fall into an uncertain love triangle. It’s a great read and timely.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Love & Romance"]

title: Days Without End: A Novel
author: Sebastian Barry
description: The novel Days Without End follows the life of Thomas McNulty, who flees the Great Famine of Ireland as a boy and winds up a soldier on the Great Plains of the United States fighting in the Indian Wars, and later in the Civil War. McNulty finds companionship and love in one of his fellow soldiers, Handsome John Cole. (That part of the novel was inspired by Sebastian Barry’s son, Toby, who came out to him while Barry was researching the book.) McNulty is a lyrical and companionable narrator for this bloody part of America’s history.

title: Dear Ijeawele, Or A Feminist Manifesto In Fifteen Suggestions
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
description: Nigerian writer Chimamanda Adichie offers advice we all need in Dear Ijeawele, which is structured as tips for parenting a girl growing up in a man’s world. There are reminders that women are more than objects to be liked/disliked, that caregiving isn’t inherently female and, more than anything, that we should live our lives on our own terms. One of her hopes for the girl she addresses is that “she will be full of opinions and that her opinions will come from an informed, humane and broad-minded place.” If you’re short on time but want to be inspired by one of the strongest voices for gender equality today, this book will help you get to that informed, humane, broad-minded place.

title: Devil's Bargain: Steve Bannon, Donald Trump, And The Storming Of The Presidency
author: Joshua Green
description: Devil’s Bargain* by Joshua Green is a fascinating investigation into the ideological bricks with which Steve Bannon has built his ethnonationalist house – and how Donald Trump moved in without a second thought. Trump was so irritated by the book that it was reportedly one of the reasons he fired Bannon.

title: Do I Make Myself Clear?: Why Writing Well Matters
author: Harold Evans
description: Sentences can be about anything, but writing is about clarity. That’s the central idea of Do I Make Myself Clear?, Sir Harold Evans’ memoir-cum-craft manual in which he rollicks – with all the joy and adventurousness of a rock ’n’ roll tell-all – through lessons in English usage gleaned from decades at the helm of The Sunday Times. Of the truly silly number of hours I’ve spent with my nose in the binding of books on the craft of writing, those I spent with Do I Make Myself Clear? were the only I spent smiling, in search of someone I could read aloud to.

title: Draft No. 4: On The Writing Process
author: John McPhee
description: There are lots of books out there by writers and about writing, but this one is the best (and not just because it’s funny). Longtime New Yorker contributor John McPhee re-edits some of his own work for our benefit. Want to know what poor word choice looks like? McPhee shows you an example from his own writing. The main thing I love about this book is that it doesn’t pretend writing is fun. It’s not fun. It’s maddening. And it’s still worth doing well.

title: Dreaming The Beatles: The Love Story Of One Band And The Whole World
author: Rob Sheffield
description: Don’t care about The Beatles? Weird! But totally fine. Love and know everything about The Beatles? This book might make you re-evaluate how much you thought you knew. Rob Sheffield is a Rolling Stone music critic and author, perhaps best known for writing devastatingly personal, music-reference-drenched books about love and loss. Where a lot of music writing can be dry – or worse, mired in sap or blind fandom – Sheffield successfully avoids these traps to deliver a salacious, surprising, chronological look into the the Fab Four, their music and the impact they have and will continue to have, forever.

title: Dunbar (Hogarth Shakespeare)
author: Edward St. Aubyn
description: No one is better suited to adapting King Lear – Shakespeare’s most excruciating family tragedy – than Edward St. Aubyn, a writer whose career was built on familial strife with the exquisite Patrick Melrose novels. His language has a wonderful poetic density, dry, expansive, self-conscious and savage, all at once. The result is Dunbar, a moving, brutal and apt adaptation of the play, and the first really successful installment of the Hogarth Shakespeare series.

title: Easternization: Asia's Rise And America's Decline From Obama To Trump And Beyond
author: Gideon Rachman
description: With lucid, easily readable prose – as befits the chief foreign affairs columnist of the Financial Times – Easternization examines the ways in which global power is shifting to the East – read mostly China and, to some extent, India – and the challenge that poses to the West and its established order. Donald Trump’s election, and his inclination toward isolationism, only make this book, and its argument, more relevant.

title: Eat Only When You're Hungry: A Novel
author: Lindsay Hunter
description: The fourth book from Chicago author Lindsay Hunter follows Greg, an emotionally distant West Virginia accountant who eats and drinks too much, as he searches for his son, a feckless drug addict who has disappeared somewhere in Florida. Hunter treats all of her characters with understanding and compassion, and her novel is a stunning and unsparing look at substance abuse, leavened by the dark humor that has made her a favorite of the indie-lit world.

title: Eleanor Oliphant Is Completely Fine
author: Gail Honeyman
description: Eleanor Oliphant is a quirky loner and a model of efficiency with her routine of frozen pizza, vodka and weekly phone calls with Mummy. But that all changes when she sees The Musician, upon whom she develops a crush. And when she meets Raymond, the IT guy at her office and her first real friend, Eleanor steps out and starts to blossom, experimenting with hair, makeup and human interaction. But this isn’t just another woman-finds-makeup-and-falls-in-love story – Eleanor is a woman beginning to heal from unimaginable childhood tragedy, with a voice that is deadpan, heartbreaking and humorous all at once.

title: Enemy Of The Good
author: Matthew Palmer
description: Matthew Palmer is a master of the diplomatic thriller. In an era of increasingly dramatic and hyperbolic headlines, he takes us to Central Asia for a fictional look at a villainous autocrat challenged by a young, idealistic democracy movement. The plot goes inside an embassy and stretches the limits (hopefully) to which real life diplomats mix conflicts of interest, romance and clever subterfuge. It is stylishly written with just enough adrenaline to keep you rooting for everyone to do the right thing, even if it isn’t always clear what that really means.

title: Exit West: A Novel
author: Mohsin Hamid
description: Critics have rightly heaped praise on this slender treasure of a novel. It’s a tale of war and refugees and how we carry ourselves in the places we call home. It is also, powerfully, a love story. I read with a pen in hand, even when I read for pleasure, and my copy of Exit West is riddled with notes; more than once, I paused to scribble “lovely” next to passages such as this: “Saeed noticed that Nadia had a beauty mark on her neck, a tawny oval that sometimes, rarely but not never, moved with her pulse.”
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Felix Yz
author: Lisa Bunker
description: Middle school and first crushes are hard enough to navigate without the added inconvenience of having an alien welded to your DNA, but such is Felix’s lot. All he really wants to do is get to know the boy he likes a little better, but looming over his head is an upcoming surgery that will either remove the rather sweet Zyx from his body or kill them both. Weaving together questions of gender expression, science fiction and just a smidgen of the old bildungsroman format to boot, Lisa Bunker plumbs the heart of what truly makes us human. (For ages 10 to 14)

title: Fierce Kingdom: A Novel
author: Gin Phillips
description: A mother and her little boy are enjoying an afternoon at the zoo when shooting erupts. I don’t want to share anything more about the plot of Gin Phillips’ novel, which could so easily have gone bad or over the top. This is no salacious, ripped-from-the-headlines tale. It’s a beautiful – and yes, fierce – portrait of motherly love and smarts.

title: Five-Carat Soul
author: James McBride
description: Five-Carat Soul*  is a jubilant and generous story collection from James McBride, who won the National Book Award for his novel about John Brown’s uprising, * The Good Lord Bird* , in 2013. His writing is so affectionate, weird, confident and funny that characters as different and strange as Abraham Lincoln, zoo animals and teenage boys all plausibly come alive.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Music", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Franklin D. Roosevelt: A Political Life
author: Robert Dallek
description: Franklin D. Roosevelt: A Political Life gives the eminent historian Robert Dallek the chance to pull together generations of scholarship and write a through-line narrative for the dominant presidential figure of the 20th century. Dallek recounts the facts and relives the legend, all with the stated mission of showing contemporary Americans “what great presidential leadership looks like.” But he keeps his FDR always in human scale, recounting his failures and flaws along with his gifts and determination.  Throughout, Dallek’s emphasis is on how FDR developed and deployed his personal skills to achieve what he wanted – and what he thought the country and the world needed.

title: From Here To Eternity: Traveling The World To Find The Good Death
author: Caitlin Doughty
description: In From Here To Eternity, mortician Caitlin Doughty travels the word to learn how other cultures care for their dead. She meets women in Bolivia who collect ñatitas, wish-granting human skulls. And she visits a community in Indonesia where the dead are mummified and often kept in the home for years at a time. These rituals might shock us, but they are all deeply human. Doughty writes bluntly about open-air cremations, natural burials and body composting, bringing a little more clarity and a little less mystery to the question, “What happens to us after we die?” (In case you were wondering: She would choose to be eaten by animals.)

title: Frontier
author: Can Xue, translated by Karen Gernant and Chen Zeping
description: Like an abstract painting that reveals new colors and shapes the longer you look at it, Karen Gernant and Chen Zeping’s translation of Can Xue’s Frontier is more experience than narrative, with characters and elements of setting recurring in quiet, moving combination. Haunted by Snow Mountain and the poplar grove and hunting a mysterious tropical garden that might live inside a small boy named Roy, the citizens of Pebble Town try to understand their neighbors – and end up more distant from themselves. Reading it is like trying to solve a mystery in a dream; talking about it is an attempt to describe a forest floor stirring at the cusp of winter.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Ghost Of The Innocent Man: A True Story Of Trial And Redemption
author: Benjamin Rachlin
description: Innocence cases spotlight the many corruptions of our justice system: Mistakes beget mistakes – some intentional, some not. An epic bureaucracy protects a deeply flawed system. In this crisply written page turner, Benjamin Rachlin reports from two perspectives: one man’s personal tragedy, and the story of North Carolina’s criminal justice system sluggishly lumbering toward the acknowledgment that innocent people might be incarcerated. Ghost of the Innocent Man opens a window on how errors, both innocent and malign, taint our criminal justice system at every level, raising the question: Shouldn’t we be better than this?

title: Ghosts Of The Tsunami: Death And Life In Japan's Disaster Zone
author: Richard Lloyd Parry
description: On March 11, 2011, an unprecedented earthquake and tsunami slammed the Tohoku region of northern Japan, leaving towns destroyed and thousands killed, including most of the students and faculty of one elementary school. Richard Lloyd Parry’s book about the tsunami and its aftermath is a brilliant chronicle of one of the modern world’s worst disasters. Ghosts Of The Tsunami is also a necessary act of witness and a beautiful meditation on grief and how it tears families and communities apart.

title: Glass Houses: A Novel
author: Louise Penny
description:
*Glass Houses*, the 13th in Louise Penny’s series about Chief Inspector (now Superintendent) Armand Gamache, is what I think of as one of the “Great Gamaches.” This latest entry offers an intricately braided plot and a near apocalyptic climax. 
A macabre figure materializes at a Halloween party in Gamache’s home village of Three Pines. The black-robed stranger has come to collect “a debt of conscience.” Things swiftly go downhill from there in a tale that involves the current opioid crisis, as well as Three Pines’ violent history during Prohibition. Enough. Any plot summary falls short of conveying the dark magic of this series.

title: Golden Hill: A Novel Of Old New York
author: Francis Spufford
description:
Francis Spufford’s ingenious historical novel, *Golden Hill,* is set in 1746, in the teeming streets of what is now Lower Manhattan – a place of dark alleys and twisted virtue. On a gloomy November evening, a ship sails into the harbor and a stranger disembarks. Rumors swirl that “Mr. Smith” is in town to stir up pre-Revolutionary trouble against the crown. 
Before his adventures end, Smith will get a tour of the city’s taverns, theaters, debtor’s prison and dueling grounds. Ultimately, the mystery of Smith’s identity will turn out to be as intricate as that of old New York itself.

title: Good Booty: Love And Sex, Black And White, Body And Soul In American Music
author: Ann Powers
description: From the Creole sounds of New Orleans in the early 1900s to the cyborg-like divas of the 21st century, NPR Music critic and correspondent Ann Powers considers the history of sexuality in American music in Good Booty. With passion and scope, Powers paints a portrait of America’s obsession with sex as it manifests itself in our anthems, dances and celebrity worship – and how that obsession has much to tell us about our collective desires, fears, inhibitions and beliefs.

title: Goodbye, Vitamin: A Novel
author: Rachel Khong
description: Rachel Khong’s debut novel is a book I know I will read over and over again. It’s a stunning and hilarious portrait of Ruth, a woman who has to reset her expectations of reality when she moves home with her parents. She’s looking to take a break from a flailing career and spend more time with her father, who has Alzheimer’s. Told through diary entries, Khong’s prose is crisp and never earnest. She writes tenderly about the moment when the parent-child relationship shifts, and Ruth is forced to puzzle over the secrets her father has kept and determine how best to help him when he’s most vulnerable.

title: Gorbachev: His Life And Times
author: William Taubman
description: Don’t judge a book by its … length. At 880 pages, and with an old photo of a Soviet leader on the cover, I was intimidated, too. But * Gorbachev: His Life And Times* flies by and is gripping. It reads less like a biography and more like a political thriller with drama, romance and – Russia being Russia – vodka. William Taubman has written what feels like the definitive book on one of the most transformative figures of the 20th century – and he has made it accessible and hard to put down.

title: Greater Gotham: A History Of New York City From 1898 To 1919
author: Mike Wallace
description: Mike Wallace’s fabulous new work of encyclopedic nonfiction gives readers kaleidoscopic glimpses of turn-of-the-last-century New York. The 1,196-page telling is especially rich because of his “determination to include all the historical actors … left out of earlier studies,” with equal attention to women as well as dead white men. The result is both scholarly and pop: Were you aware that workers on the IRT subway line unearthed mastodon bones at Dyckman Street? Wallace summons great tycoons, ingrate robber barons and the Great War alike out of the gas-lit Gilded Age into the bright electric light of an exuberant 20th century.

title: Harmless Like You: A Novel
author: Rowan Hisayo Buchanan
description: Author Rowan Hisayo Buchanan was struck by lightning as she finished Harmless Like You. I’m not setting up an overburdened metaphor, I just want you to know it’s a thing that literally happened to someone as she finished her smash-hit debut. (She’s fine, by the way.) Plus, Harmless Like You isn’t a lightning strike, it’s a perpetual shudder – a frisson that won’t abate until days after you’ve set the book down and, at last, started to reckon with all that Buchanan has to say.

title: Her Body And Other Parties: Stories
author: Carmen Maria Machado
description: Her Body and Other Parties – a finalist for the National Book Award – is an abrupt, original and wild collection of stories that somehow catch at familiar, unspoken truths about being women that more straightforward writing wouldn’t. I love how Machado draws on ghost stories, Girl Scout stories, camp stories, urban legends and outlandish myths to reinforce and enshrine a particular kind of young female culture.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Her Every Fear: A Novel
author: Peter Swanson
description: I read a lot of mysteries, and Peter Swanson’s The Kind Worth Killing was one of my absolute favorites of the last several years. This new book doesn’t quite hit that mark, but it’s still a cracking good read. There is a heroine with a well-earned case of anxiety, newly arrived in a drafty Boston apartment. There is a neighbor’s cat wandering in and out of this apartment, sometimes when the protagonist is sure, just sure the front door was closed and locked. Oh, and there is also the discovery of a body in another apartment across the hall. Her Every Fear has atmosphere and tension to spare.

title: Her Right Foot
author: Dave Eggers, illustrated by Shawn Harris
description: “Her Right Foot” appeared at just the right time, serving as a palate cleanser to increasingly caustic headlines around immigration. With a disarmingly conversational style, Dave Eggers and Shawn Harris reveal a little-known quirk about the Statue of Liberty (namely, that she is stepping forward) and use it as a call to action – a reminder that when it comes to the issues that truly matter, standing still is not an option. (For ages 6 to 9)

title: History of Wolves: A Novel
author: Emily Fridlund
description: Emily Fridlund’s electrifying debut novel doesn’t follow the now-familiar narrative arc that other coming-of-age novels do. The book tells the story of 14-year-old Madeline, who befriends a mysterious family that has just moved across the lake from the mostly abandoned commune where she lives. The relationship doesn’t end well. Madeline is a fascinating and unique character, and Fridlund’s thriller is as beautiful and as icy as the Minnesota woods where it’s set.

title: Hollow: A Novel
author: Owen Egerton
description: The title of Owen Egerton’s new novel refers to the idea that our earth is hollow – we live on its crust, just above another world accessible only through special ports at the North and South poles; protagonist Ollie and his friend Lyle are attempting to join an expedition to that fabled North Pole entrance. Egerton invokes a lot of things with this central metaphor – a grieving heart, modern life’s futility and the divine. But Hollow is also a fast-paced mystery/thriller following Ollie’s attempt to save a dying cancer patient. How much can a person take before suffering changes him?
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Home Fire: A Novel
author: Kamila Shamsie
description: This book pulled me in with its girl-meets-boy story, but it quickly becomes something bigger and more complicated than I expected. With a range of perspectives and a plot that moves from Massachusetts to London to Syria and back, it’s a gutsy novel. I don’t want to give too much away – just pick it up and be swept into an absurdly readable tale of love and geopolitics.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: House Of Names: A Novel
author: Colm Tóibín
description: Colm Tóibín, no stranger to fraught family dynamics and themes of loss, exile and return, wanders far from the Ireland and Brooklyn we’ve seen in his earlier books to take on the House of Atreus, the mother of all dysfunctional families, in this violent, psychologically astute page turner. In visceral, accessible language, Tóibín reanimates the revenge tragedies of Clytemnestra, Agamemnon and their three children, Iphigeneia, Electra and Orestes. He makes us feel Clytemnestra’s anguished outrage when her husband sacrifices their eldest daughter in hopes of favorable winds for his warships and adds intimate sagas of suffering not found in the ancient Greeks.

title: Human Acts: A Novel
author: Han Kang
description: Mercy is a human impulse, but so is murder. In Human Acts, Han Kang’s novel of the 1980 Gwangju Uprising in South Korea, people spill blood and people brave death to donate it. With a sensitivity so sharp that it’s painful, Human Acts sets out to reconcile these paradoxical and coexisting humanities.

title: Hunger: A Memoir Of (My) Body
author: Roxane Gay
description: Roxane Gay’s highly anticipated memoir chronicles her relationship with her body, her ongoing struggle with weight and a rape that left her reeling for decades, which she revisits several times throughout the book. Gay lingers on some of her life’s most painful moments to give voice to our collective grief and fury at the trauma we feel when something is taken from us.

title: I Survived The American Revolution, 1776
author: Lauren Tarshis
description: The 15th in Lauren Tarshis’ meticulously researched series is, like its predecessors, a clever marriage of history and character. The title is a spoiler: Each book follows the thrilling (terrifying) path of a young person encountering an historical event, from Pompeii to 9/11. In this one, a boy named Nate wanders into the Battle of Brooklyn in 1776. It’s filled with the kinds of detail that will pleasantly surprise adults and transfix middle-graders who have grown up bopping to Hamilton. (My 6-year-old found answers to lyrics that had been vexing him – “Mom, what’s a bayonet?”) Tarshis’ treatment of the heroes of the Revolution and their relationship to slavery – the country’s original sin – is smart and straightforward and a great way to take the political conversation of the moment, into the personal.

title: Ill Will: A Novel
author: Dan Chaon
description: Dan Chaon’s Ill Will is an intelligent, layered and painfully suspenseful thriller about a psychologist whose childhood was shattered by a series of murders in the family. Chaon writes especially well about conspiracy theories, showing how they can rebound and refract. “[W]e were only peeping through a keyhole of our lives, and the majority of the truth, the reality of what happened to us, was hidden,” says one character. “Memories were no more solid than dreams.”

title: Inside Studio 54
author: Mark Fleischman
description: Inside Studio 54 is true to its title, taking us past the velvet rope, into the heat and sweat and coke and poppers of the dance floor where hundreds of bodies ground against each other, and into the dark alcoves and out-of-the way balconies, where a slightly smaller number of bodies ground against each other with greater assiduousness. Author Mark Fleischman, who took over the club in 1980, proves an eager, garrulous host, gleefully sharing his love of the place, and the time, and the drugs. The drugs, especially.

title: Iraq +100: The First Anthology Of Science Fiction To Have Emerged From Iraq
author: Hassan Blasim (editor)
description: Writer, filmmaker and Iraqi expatriate Hassan Blasim asked 10 Iraqi authors to answer a simple, yet complicated question: What do you imagine our country looking like 100 years after the U.S. invasion of 2003? The answer is Iraq + 100, a collection of science fiction short stories that tackle disparity, brutality, national identity and totalitarianism with equally grim and hopeful voices.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: Isadora: A Novel
author: Amelia Gray
description: Legendary American dancer Isadora Duncan lost all three of her children before she died in a freak accident; Amelia Gray’s biographical novel about the dancer covers the period between the deaths of her first two children in a car accident and the birth of her third. Gray is a gutsy, utterly original writer, and this is the finest work she has done so far. Isadora is a masterful portrait of one of America’s greatest artists, and it’s also a beautiful reflection on what it means to be suffocated by grief, but not quite willing to give up.

title: Jade City
author: Fonda Lee
description: Every year there are books we discover far too late and have to cram them into the Book Concierge at the last minute because there’s just no way we can let them slide again. Fonda Lee’s Jade City is one of those. The island of Kekon won its independence from an occupying empire decades ago – but now the proud rebels who fought for freedom have split into two rival clans, each drawing fearsome fighting powers from a magical jade that’s unique to Kekon. An uneasy peace prevails as the book opens, but of course that can’t last. Jade City is an instantly absorbing tale of blood, honor, family and magic, spiced with unexpectedly tender character beats. In my mind’s eye, it all unspooled in the grainy, supersaturated colors of a classic Shaw Brothers kung fu film.

title: Janesville: An American Story
author: Amy Goldstein
description: Janesville is narrative-driven long-form reporting at its best. Amy Goldstein spent years following roughly a dozen workers who were affected by the closing of General Motors’ Janesville Assembly Plant just before Christmas 2008, at the height of the economic crisis. The plant was the center of economic, social and civic life, employing generations of family members for more than 80 years. The Wisconsin city also has a history of civic responsibility – looking out for those who didn’t get the comfortable middle-class life the plant provided. Goldstein follows workers determined to find jobs either through re-invention and significant obstacles, or unyielding belief in the American auto industry. Janesville’s pride is tested at every turn.

title: Keeping On Keeping On
author: Alan Bennett
description: In this, Alan Bennett’s third volume of nonfiction prose, the British screenwriter/playwright/cultural institution proves as wry, self-deprecating and fabulously fusty as ever. In excerpts from diaries kept between 2005 and 2015, he tuts and clucks at the Tories, deplores garish new architecture and gets miffed at hearing himself described as “twee” – only to follow that entry with this one: “On Saturday we blackberry up Crummock Lane.” And later, “I’ve taken to eating the occasional date, though it’s not a fruit I wholly like.” Bennett’s fully aware of his charming tea-cosy of a writerly persona, making Keeping On Keeping On the perfect book to curl up with as the nights get longer, and colder. “One does try not to be an Old Git,” he sighs, “but they don’t make it easy.”
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Tales From Around The World", "Art", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: Kill All Normies: Online Culture Wars From 4Chan And Tumblr To Trump And The Alt-Right
author: Angela Nagle
description: We are always looking for the roots of the Trump revolution, and in this book, Angela Nagle locates them online. This is a close ethnography of the alt-right and their rebellion against the “normies” (the rest of us). It’s clever, alarming and totally nonjudgmental – or, more accurately, it judges all sides equally harshly. And for me, it explained my world.

title: Killers Of The Flower Moon: The Osage Murders And The Birth Of The FBI
author: David Grann
description: In the 1920s, members of the Osage Nation seemed fortunate: They were getting huge royalties on the oil that had been discovered under their lands. Then they started dying, one by one. This is easily my favorite book of the year. It reads – sadly – like crime fiction (if only it were fiction). Read this and you will learn something vital about our country’s history that reverberates to this day.

title: Lady Killers: Deadly Women Throughout History
author: Tori Telfer
description: Men are far more likely than women to kill, making Lady Killers’ title a grim pun – though Tori Telfer does her gruesome best to even up the body count. For ages, the received wisdom had it that female serial killers did not actually exist (Aileen Wuornos aside). Like female rocket scientists and cage fighters, these women had to wait for the new millennium to be properly recognized. With selections that are exceptionally well-sourced and engaging, if gory, Lady Killers profiles more than 14 subjects with widely differing motivations for ending multiple lives.

title: Lea: A Novel
author: Pascal Mercier
description: Lea is a descent into that place where obsession and brilliance collide to produce the heights of creative expression in one moment … and self destruction in the next. This is the story of two gifted, grief-stricken people unable to save each other from the self-imprisonment of their talents. Lea’s father tells a man he meets in a cafe the story of his daughter’s rise to musical brilliance, and her descent into mental illness. Mercier’s books are populated with people who are obsessed and awkward; Lea is no exception. He brings to life the worlds of people who possess a single-minded focus on the perfection of an idea, a phrase, a game or a note. Like his previous novel Night Train to Lisbon, *Lea * is full of searing images that will stick in my mind long after the details of the plot and characters fade from memory.

title: Leopard At The Door: A Novel
author: Jennifer McVeigh
description: A few times while reading Jennifer McVeigh’s page turner, I wondered whether this was what might happen if you mashed up Karen Blixen’s memoir Out of Africa with Alexandra Fuller’s Don’t Let’s Go to the Dogs Tonight  and turned them into fiction. Set in pre-independence Kenya in the 1950s, Leopard at the Door tells the story of a teenage girl who returns to her family’s rural farm after four years in England, amid major upheaval both personal and political. McVeigh gracefully captures the era’s turmoil with well-researched, vivid accounts of land fights and systemic racism. Her characters are equally complex and conflicted (though some are just downright awful). This is one of those books you’ll almost hate to see end – but you’ll feel kind of relieved when it does.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: Lilli De Jong: A Novel
author: Janet Benton
description: Janet Benton’s wonderfully imagined debut examines the consequences of a Quaker woman’s decision to keep her baby born out of wedlock. Set in 1880s Philadelphia, this elegantly crafted book immerses the reader in Lilli’s agonizing fight to achieve economic security for herself and her daughter, against a society that condemns both her pregnancy and her decision to mother. Amid contemporary efforts to narrow reproductive freedoms and demean women who assert their right to be free from sexual violence, Lilli’s struggles to abide by her principles are eerily relevant.

title: Lincoln In The Bardo: A Novel
author: George Saunders
description: This is how George Saunders will get you: He’ll start with some premise that sounds absurd or gimmicky (previously – human lawn ornaments! This time – Abraham Lincoln in purgatory!) and so you’ll go in ungirded, expecting something pleasantly wacky. Then he’ll spin a story so humane and generous and moving that you will be unraveled completely.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "History", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Lines
author: Suzy Lee
description: A lonely figure skater glides across the ice, cutting an elegant and dynamic path across the blank page. Suzy Lee’s Lines uses this simple premise to launch a surprising meditation on the artistic process. Indeed, midway through the book,when Lee attempts a tricky metafictional twist, readers may find themselves holding their breath and preparing for a crash, but luckily, Lee sticks the landing in style. (For ages 5 to 6)

title: literally show me a healthy person
author: Darcie Wilder
description: You know those “impressive” literary passages that are 13 million words long with no punctuation? Darcie Wilder’s literally show me a healthy person is kind of like that, except not as full of itself and actually entertaining to read. It’s packed with brutal self-owns and critical reflections on culture, and it barely hides a tight narrative about family, relationships and mental health. Despite being a slim book, it took me forever to get through because there were so many moments that left me emotionally drained – in a very good way.

title: Literary Witches: A Celebration Of Magical Women Writers
author: Taisia Kitaiskaia, illustrated by Katy Horan
description: The phenomenon of the witch – once used as a shorthand for women who fall outside of certain acceptable parameters, now reclaimed as a label of pride and power – has spilled over to lots of places in contemporary culture. One of the more delightful iterations of this movement is Literary Witches, an art book for book nerds. Gorgeous illustrations accompany profiles of female writers from every genre, identity and era conceivable. As necessary a project as I can imagine in this day and age, this is art, poetry and history marshaled together in tremendous, joyful celebration.

title: Literature Class, Berkeley 1980
author: Julio Cortázar , translated by Katherine Silver
description: There’s no question that Julio Cortázar is among the most revered Latin American writers of any age. In Literature Class, readers are treated to a series of talks the author gave at Berkeley circa 1980. They range from meditations on the writer’s path to the elements of an effective short story. “I’m not a critic or theorist,” he writes, “which means that in my work, I look for solutions as problems arise.” While this may be true, it’s hard to imagine anyone better suited to tackle the endless possibilities of literature and language itself.

title: Little Fires Everywhere
author: Celeste Ng
description: Celeste Ng’s second novel about family and adoption benefits from perfect timing and great, immersive writing. The opening pages – a beautiful suburban house in flames – are both mission statement and the beginning of a carefully plotted novel. A look at the lives of two families in Shaker Heights, Ohio, in the 1990s, it is a meditation on rules, race, class, insiders, outsiders, motherhood. There is no throwaway character. And after you’ve raced to the end of the book, you’ll want to read it again, to take the ideas and hold them up to the harsh light of 2017. Ng’s novel would be a great read in any time period – but if you’re struggling with the present moment and how we got here, this novel will do what any good piece of fiction does: illuminate.

title: Locking Up Our Own: Crime and Punishment in Black America
author: James Forman Jr.
description: James Forman Jr. may change the way you think about the mass incarceration of African-Americans on drug charges. Forman is a Yale University law professor who used to be a public defender in Washington, D.C. In his new book, he tells the story of how blacks in law enforcement – people who had battled for the right to serve as police and judges, as well as politicians – made the war on drugs very much their own.

title: Lotus: A Novel
author: Lijia Zhang
description: Readers will find Lijia Zhang’s debut novel rich in Chinese proverbs. After the titular Lotus loses face, she keeps on going despite the hurt, because “a cracked jar doesn’t mind being smashed again.” These lines delight, as does so much of this sensitively drawn world. Lotus herself is a charmer; she enters the oldest profession on an inebriated whim, and a magazine photo of her face changes her life when the photographer falls for her. But this is, above all, Lotus’ story, and Zhang has a light touch even when portraying the well-researched underbelly of contemporary Chinese culture.

title: Lucky Boy
author: Shanthi Sekaran
description: I wasn’t sure who to root for in Lucky Boy; my allegiances kept changing, which kept me reading all 61 chapters and 400-plus pages. Who is the victim? Who is the victor? I need an answer! But in this novel about race, class, immigration and fierce motherly love, there are no easy answers. Shanthi Sekaran’s writing is simple and beautiful, but her book is complicated and messy. You’ll keep turning it over in your head long after it’s over. It’s a story of and for our time.

title: Lucky You: A Novel
author: Erika Carter
description: Erika Carter’s debut novel revolves around Ellie, Chloe and Rachel, three sort-of-friends navigating their 20s in Fayetteville, Ark., who drink maybe too much and attempt to have relationships with a series of unfortunate men. It’s a charming, understated novel that perfectly captures the feeling of being young, depressed and adrift. Carter is both unsparing and compassionate, and among her greatest gifts is an ability to find a savage kind of beauty in the unlikeliest of places.

title: Luna: Wolf Moon: A Novel
author: Ian McDonald
description: No one builds a world like Ian McDonald does. Piece by piece, brick by brick, spare and simple when he needs to be, deep and meaty when he wants to be. Here, he gives us exactly what his title promises: the moon. A future moon colonized by battling corporate families recovering after the devastation wrought by war in the previous novel in this series, but already plotting more bloodshed. But what elevates this above being just another moon-war sci-fi trope fest is the way McDonald’s characters feel precious, real and alive, which just makes it all the more gutting when you see the dwindling page count and know that not all of them (or even most of them) are going to make it through to the last page alive.

title: Madame President: The Extraordinary Journey Of Ellen Johnson Sirleaf
author: Helene Cooper
description: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf – a Nobel peace laureate and Africa’s first democratically elected female president – is poised to bow out of power after 12 years in charge of postwar Liberia. But in Madame President, Helene Cooper (who was born in Liberia) exposes a vulnerable Johnson Sirleaf who survived an abusive marriage, political imprisonment and living away from her young sons so she could study and acquire qualifications in the U.S. This biography, with a backstory about the influence of Liberian women, chronicles Johnson Sirleaf’s triumphs and failures (including accusations of nepotism) without holding back, making Madame President a refreshing read.

title: Made For Love: A Novel
author: Alissa Nutting
description: Made For Love is a philosophical and outrageously imaginative comic novel about love, mortality, surveillance and dolphin fetishism. Having escaped her billionaire, evil-genius husband, Hazel desperately seeks normalcy – but her aging, bereaved father (who is consoling himself with sex dolls) makes that impossible. It may sound high-concept, but Alissa Nutting’s empathetic imagination makes you care about each character who crosses the page.

title: Magpie Murders: A Novel
author: Anthony Horowitz
description: Magpie Murders is a whodunit about whodunits and a mystery within a mystery. It opens on a rainy day in London as an editor at a small publishing company settles in to read the latest from a best-selling mystery writer. But a chapter is missing, and when the editor-turned-sleuth sets out to track it down she stumbles into a real murder mystery that parallels the fictional one. Anthony Horowitz, a master of the genre, packs his story with all the elements of a good whodunit: a quaint English village, a slew of suspects, convoluted motives and alibis and a clever plot twist. He seemed to have a really good time writing this book and so will fans of mystery novels when they read it.

title: Manhattan Beach: A Novel
author: Jennifer Egan
description: Manhattan Beach is less structurally daring than Jennifer Egan’s Pulitzer Prize-winning A Visit From The Goon Squad, but she started researching this novel years before that one came out. It follows a father, his daughter and a mobster whose lives intersect in New York around World War II. Anna, the daughter, works at the Brooklyn Navy Yard, and in one scene she struggles under the crushing weight of a diving suit. As she enters the water, the weight disappears, and the suit lets her explore an unfamiliar world. Egan pulls off a similar trick with the mountains of research that went into this project: Instead of weighing the reader down, the historical details buoy the story, and the narrative flows like an easy current.

title: Marlena: A Novel
author: Julie Buntin
description: Julie Buntin understands that a brief but intense best-friendship is not necessarily a coming-of-age tale, but rather an origin story. Fifteen-year-old Cat moves to rural Michigan and strikes up a friendship with the beautiful and charismatic Marlena. We know that a year later, Marlena will drown (that’s not a spoiler, I promise). Cat is now a 30-something Brooklynite who is pulling at the threads of her time Marlena, examining the ways a wonderful, magnetic friendship can also be treacherous and devastating. Reading this book wrecked me, but now I can’t stop thinking about how my own teenage friends shaped me.

title: Meddling Kids: A Novel
author: Edgar Cantero
description: Meddling Kids is a story about what might’ve happened to the Scooby Doo gang if they’d ever actually grown up. It’s about the surviving members of the Blyton Summer Detective Club returning to their hometown as adults to finally solve the one case that beat them. And it is awesome. It’s a book that lives completely in the sweet recollections of childhood summers and that exists in the power band of maximum pop-culture self-referentialism. It trades on memory and nostalgia with almost zero ironic distance, just embracing the ridiculousness of its own backstory with both arms, making the grown characters wear all their damage and all their scars in plain view. They were kids who saw some messed up stuff. They suffered horribly for it, and mostly in silence. Now they’re going back to mend those things in them that have been broken.

title: Meet Me In The Bathroom: Rebirth And Rock And Roll In New York City 2001-2011
author: Lizzy Goodman
description: It may feel too soon to look back at the New York music scene of the 2000s with a nostalgic or historical eye: For one thing, many of the most influential and popular bands of that era – The Strokes and Interpol, Yeah Yeah Yeahs and LCD Soundsystem – are still making music. But one of the more surprising realizations that emerges from Lizzy Goodman’s meticulously reported oral history is how much has changed: The decade began with a pre-mp3, pre-social-media music industry. Goodman documents a city in transition from pre-9\11 Manhattan to gentrifying Williamsburg. She interviews both the scene’s biggest names and its forgotten artists as they reflect on the creative ambitions and behind-the-scenes dramas, the self-destructive behavior and messy rivalries. She also tracks down an exhaustive cast of narrators including managers and publicists, fellow journalists and bloggers, promoters and partygoers, who collectively offer up the conflicting points of view that mark the best oral histories. A captivating deep dive for anyone who loves (or despises) this music, Meet Me In The Bathroom is also a love letter to a New York City that no longer exists.

title: Memory's Last Breath: Field Notes On My Dementia
author: Gerda Saunders
description: I know what you’re thinking. A memoir about dementia? So depressing. But trust me on this: Gerda Saunders doesn’t wallow in her mental slippage. Instead, she writes with clear-eyed honesty and, yes, humor about living with early-onset microvascular disease. She lays out her intent in what she calls her Dementia Field Notes, sprinkled throughout the book: “No whimpering, no whining, no despair. Just the facts.” Interspersed with vignettes about her mental decline are vivid memories of Saunders’ childhood in South Africa. The contrast brilliantly illuminates the gulf between memories richly preserved and memory lost.

title: Mikhail And Margarita: A Novel
author: Julie Lekstrom Himes
description: Julie Lekstrom Himes’ thought-provoking novel Mikhail and Margarita explores one of the most oppressive periods in Russian history – the Stalinist regime.  Riffing off the great physician-writer Mikhail Bulgakov’s The Master and Margarita, Himes creates extraordinary atmospherics, immersing her readers in Soviet Russia and all the fears and anxieties of real life writers and artists who faced the Faustian choice of staying true to themselves and their craft, or avoiding the Gulag and surviving. Himes, herself a physician, renders years of research into a gripping narrative about art, politics, and above all, love.

title: Minecraft: The Island: A Novel
author: Max Brooks
description: Max Brooks made his name writing (in almost clinical detail) about the aftermath of the war against the zombies (in World War Z). Now he’s writing a branded novel operating in the universe of the video game Minecraft that’s full of life lessons and poop jokes. But it is still Max Brooks writing this book, which means he focuses all of his considerable talent and imagination on making a Minecraft version of  Robinson Crusoe – the story of one man lost on an island and learning how to survive according to the very strange physical rules of the Minecraft universe.

title: Miss Kopp's Midnight Confessions
author: Amy Stewart
description: Amy Stewart has written a series of novels inspired by Constance Kopp, a real woman and sheriff’s deputy who regularly made headlines in early 20th century New Jersey. In this third installment, Constance takes on society’s crushing expectations of women as Hackensack jail fills up with female prisoners who have been arrested on “morality” charges. (I hope Stewart resists the pressure she alludes to in this book to marry Constance off to the sheriff – she’ll find her happiness without the usual formula.) If you enjoy action, humor and forgotten history, you’ll love Miss Kopp’s Midnight Confessions.

title: Mozart's Starling
author: Lyanda Lynn Haupt
description: Pity the poor starling. Considered an invasive species, it may be the world’s most despised bird. But naturalist Lyanda Lynn Haupt’s book offers a delightful balm to the haters. Inspired by Mozart, who kept a pet starling, Haupt adopted a nestling of her own, named it Carmen and raised it in her own home. (Talk about invasive!) Warmly underscoring the bonds between humans and animals, Mozart’s Starling blends committed nature writing with personal reflection, music and humor – and guarantees you will never look the small, speckled birds in quite the same way.

title: Mrs. Fletcher: A Novel
author: Tom Perrotta
description: Once again, the author of The Leftovers and Little Children has written what feels like a narrow peek into a wide world, just one chapter in a vast, ongoing soap opera. There are many voices here, but the primary characters are a middle-aged mom exploring her sexuality after her son heads to college and the son, a jock who’s baffled when his pleasure-chasing self-absorption alienates people he wanted to impress. Both characters struggle with neediness and deal with rejection, but Perrotta’s bright, humorous edge and impeccably detailed characterization turn these seeming personal tragedies into a wry satire of modern relationships.

title: My Absolute Darling: A Novel
author: Gabriel Tallent
