description: Turtle is a 14-year-old girl who eats raw eggs for breakfast and carries a gun. She has learned everything about survival from her gritty father, which doesn’t always mean good things. As Turtle starts to make inroads with her classmates, her widower father starts to see his control over his daughter slip. My Absolute Darling is imbued with a beautiful tension that evolves unexpectedly, transforming it from a coming-of-age novel to a thriller that had me gasping in shock so frequently that I got looks on the plane.

title: My Darling Detective
author: Howard Norman
description: Howard Norman’s newest novel is set in Halifax, Nova Scotia, and revolves around a photograph at auction. The image shows a dead World War II soldier, and during the auction the protagonist’s mother throws black ink all over it. From there we try to figure out the story of both mother and son.

title: My Life, My Love, My Legacy
author: Coretta Scott King, as told to Barbara Reynolds
description: This memoir is a bracing account of one of the civil rights movement’s greatest heroines. It’s no surprise to learn about Coretta Scott King’s brave and bounteous spirituality, but she was also conflicted and complex. A leading pacifist of her era, King got in trouble as a little girl for hitting other children. She didn’t want to date Martin Luther King Jr. when she first met him because he was too short, and even her husband failed to understand the profundity and the depth of his wife’s inner calling.  In one of the book’s most painful passages, she recounts her self-doubt about how she handled talking to her children about their father’s assassination. King’s decades-long friendship with journalist Barbara Reynolds yields a thrilling portrait of a woman who helped transform American morality while fighting both racial injustice and the men of the civil rights movement who consistently ignored, underestimated and undermined her. This memoir is a tonic at this historical moment and a much-needed and ultimately uplifting refresher on what it means to be a principled American.

title: Nabokov's Favorite Word Is Mauve: What The Numbers Reveal About The Classics, Bestsellers, And Our Own Writing
author: Ben Blatt
description: Statistician Ben Blatt loaded thousands of books – classics and contemporary best-sellers – into various databases and let his hard drive churn through them, seeking to determine, for example, if our favorite authors follow conventional writing advice about using cliches, adverbs and exclamation points (they mostly do); if men and women write differently (yep); if an algorithm can identify a writer from his or her prose style (it can); and which authors use the shortest first sentences (Toni Morrison, Margaret Atwood, Mark Twain) versus those who use the longest (Salman Rushdie, Michael Chabon, Edith Wharton). It’s a thin slice, looking at literature in this knowingly reductive way. It doesn’t tell you everything, and of course it doesn’t give you a true sense of the feeling you get when you read for yourself. But what it often succeeds in capturing, with astonishing clarity, is your feelings about these authors.

title: New People
author: Danzy Senna
description: A lot of times, with certain hot-button political issues, it feels like we’re saying the same things over and over again. I feel this way especially about race, an issue the whole country seems to talk in circles about, over and over again. That’s why I so loved Danzy Senna’s New People. It made me think about race in a way I never have before: of race as performance, regardless of our identities; of this idea that we all “pass” for something every day, whether we’re white, black, brown or something in between. And on top of that, New People functions as a truly delectable satirical suspense thriller. You’ve gotta read it.

title: Norse Mythology
author: Neil Gaiman
description: It’s hard not to be familiar with Thor, Loki, Odin and the other characters of Nordic mythology who populate our operas, video games and TV shows. But I was a bit sketchy on the details despite my obsession with all things Icelandic. This is the book I’ve been waiting for. Let’s face it, the classic version of these tales – the Edda – is a bit impenetrable for many of us modern mortals. Gaiman’s take is a funny and accessible account of how these Norse deities succumb to greed and vanity, how they are blinded by their self-righteousness and entitlement, and how, time and time again, they persist through – yup, you guessed it – trickery and force. In the way of myths, these ancient stories speak enduring truths about our time.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Not A Sound: A Novel
author: Heather Gudenkauf
description:
When was the last time you stayed up to finish a thriller? I usually answer that question with Ken Follett’s *Eye of the Needle* or Geoffrey Household’s *Rogue Male*, two classic World War II suspense novels. This year, though, I added a different kind of novel: Heather Gudenkauf’s *Not a Sound*, a terrific “woman in trouble” tale. 
Gudenkauf’s heroine, Amelia Winn, is profoundly deaf. (Gudenkauf herself identifies as hearing-impaired.) When Amelia and her dog, Stitch, discover the corpse of a former co-worker near her isolated cabin, she begins investigating and sinister happenings escalate. The climax here – involving the intrepid Stitch – is a real knuckle-biter.

title: On Second Thought
author: Kristan Higgins
description: Sometimes love isn’t all sunshine, roses and forever, as Kristan Higgins reminds us in this contemporary romance about two sisters grappling with the sudden demise of their relationships. Kate is approaching 40 and widowed after four months of marriage; Ainsley’s longtime boyfriend has left her suddenly. The sisters’ friendship deepens as they rely on each other to get through both heartache and the sweet second love that finds them both. This is a novel about how love happens when you least expect it, with the people you don’t always anticipate – and at any age.

title: One Day We'll All Be Dead And None Of This Will Matter: Essays
author: Scaachi Koul
description: Each essay in BuzzFeed writer Scaachi Koul’s collection begins with an email exchange with her father, who is often simultaneously furious and magnanimous (you can see where Koul gets her dry humor). She examines her family in a frank yet tender way, from her parents’ beautiful and dark sides to her hopes and concerns for her niece, nicknamed Raisin. Koul is especially afraid of death and everyone in her life dying (she mentions planning her own funeral and hypothetically dealing with her parents’ estate several times) but she fights through that anxiety, even if it means facing her fears of flying, open water and spiders on a bumpy trip to Southeast Asia.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: One, Two, Three, More
author: Helen Levitt
description: Oh, to have the brilliant eye of the late photographer Helen Levitt. These photos, taken between 1934 and 1946 (and most never published before), bring a grubby, gritty New York City to life. Women sit on stoops reading the newspaper, or prop their elbows on a window ledge with a pillow for comfort, keeping watch on the action below. Children play in the streets with makeshift toys: a bag filled with flour, a nub of chalk, a piece of string. Life then – before television, before air conditioning – was lived on the street, and Levitt was right there, a sly and sharp observer. Each one of these images is a marvel.

title: Otis Redding: An Unfinished Life
author: Jonathan Gould
description: I frankly find most musician biographies more useful than compelling, but Jonathan Gould’s account of Otis Redding’s tragically short life is gripping because Gould gets at what made Redding both a fascinating individual and an emblem. This is a Southern story of desegregation and enduring white supremacy and of country values encountering city opportunities; it’s a story of hope, conveyed within Redding’s electric performances, and disaster. (Few deaths in music history are more wrenching than the singer’s demise in a plane crash at only 26.) Gould’s book is a crucial contribution to our understanding of a time that’s too often obscured by mere nostalgia.

title: Out In The Open: A Novel
author: Jesus Carrasco
description: In this haunting novel, an unnamed boy flees home to escape sexual and physical abuse. He is taken in by an old goatherd, who – without saying so – teaches the boy how to survive “out in the open.” The deepening relationship between boy and man is composed of deceptively simple encounters with the goats and with the outdoors.  Part allegory, part primer on love and caring, Out in the Open deploys beautiful, spare language to unfurl the damaging complexities of trauma, affirming that with the right kind of nurturing, a child can forge a path forward.

title: Pachinko
author: Min Jin Lee
description: Japan’s bright, noisy pachinko parlors hide a dark secret. These pinball emporiums are run mostly by Japan’s Korean minority because for decades it was the only employment available to them. In Pachinko, Min Jin Lee traces the difficult history of Koreans in Japan through one family’s story. The saga begins before World War I in a small Korean fishing village, where a young woman becomes pregnant and is saved from disgrace by a man who offers to marry her and takes her to Japan. We follow her and her two sons as they struggle against discrimination, trying to gain respect in a hostile country. Min Jin Lee brings this corner of history to life in a tale that spans three wars and the growth of a multibillion-dollar business that offered Korean-Japanese a way out of poverty.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Love & Romance"]

title: Pretty Face
author: Lucy Parker
description: A talented British actress with a too-easily-typecast face, voice and figure auditions for a role in a London debut production directed by a cranky, stressed and unwillingly impressed veteran of the stage. There are many reasons they shouldn’t be together. In fact, Lily calls Luc “all of her lifelong deal-breakers in one man.” Outstanding dialogue and world-building, emotional honesty, characters determined to be decent people, and realistic, relatable obstacles combine to make this book a delightful twist on the celebrity fairy tale. I highlighted so many memorable lines that I probably doubled the ebook file size.

title: Quicksand
author: Malin Persson Giolito
description: Maja killed her best friend, Amanda, and her boyfriend, Sebastian, at their school. We know she has confessed; we know she is on trial for the crimes. In this English-language debut from Swedish novelist Malin Persson Giolito, however, everything is quicksand, and that includes Maja’s memories of what happened on that bloody day. The book provides a razor-sharp view of modern Sweden and its criminal justice system, yet is a tonic for readers who have had enough of the brooding, often-bloody “Scandi-crime” that has been so popular in recent years.

title: Refugee
author: Alan Gratz
description: If you are the parent of a 9- to 12-year-old who shows interest in politics or injustice and isn’t prone to nightmares, Refugee is an intelligent, human,  harrowing read by Alan Gratz. It unspools three stories of children in their own (fictional) voices. Josef’s story begins in 1938 Nazi Germany on Kristallnacht (or, the Night of Broken Glass); Isabel’s story opens in 1994 Cuba; and Mahmoud is trying to survive 2015 Aleppo, Syria. Each child goes on a dangerous journey to find what most of us take for granted – a safe place to call home. My advice: Read with your kid and be around to discuss. (For ages 9 to 12)

title: Reincarnation Blues: A Novel
author: Michael Poore
description: Milo is a wise man who is making his own meandering way toward perfection, who has already lived 9,995 of his allotted 10,000 lives. If that sounds soft and New Age-y, it isn’t. Instead it’s a very heavy story about redemption and kindness cloaked in goofiness and the kind of wild imagination that you just don’t see often. Michael Poore tells the story of Milo’s final five lives here, but the real joy in Reincarnation Blues is when he digresses into discussions of some of the other 9,995 that Milo has lived. Poore can break your heart with a single line. He can do absurdity, dry humor, shark attacks, philosophical anarchy and an acceptance of the hard fact that every life is a death sentence, made valuable only by what we choose to do between the first day and the last one.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Richard Nixon: The Life
author: John A. Farrell
description: He seethes and rages at the press, which he casts as his enemy. He spends decades with his nose pressed up against the glass, looking in on the establishment, longing for its acceptance, and raging at its rejection. And then, suddenly, he taps into an undercurrent of populism that establishment didn’t quite understand, and he makes it to the White House. But investigations loom. You get the picture. Even without eye-popping parallels on seemingly every other page, John Farrell’s book would be a top-tier political biography. With Donald Trump in the White House, it’s essential political reading.

title: Righteous
author: Joe Ide
description: Joe Ide’s second book in a planned series features his hero, Isaiah Quintabe (IQ), a brilliant high school dropout who has morphed into a private eye for home folks whom the police can’t or won’t help. Righteous takes IQ to Las Vegas, where he fights off Chinese gangsters, searches for a DJ on the run from a loan shark and chips away at the mystery of who killed his adored big brother, Marcus, via a savage hit-and-run. It’s all spiked with Ide’s mordant observations on humanity’s darker impulses. Read slowly: Book 3 isn’t due till next year.

title: Rise Of The Jumbies
author: Tracey Baptiste
description: Why buy another mediocre retelling of a Greek myth for your kid, when you can introduce them to a gorgeously told fairy tale that originated outside of Europe? Jumbies are the malevolent spirits of Caribbean folklore, and Corrine is the plucky 11-year-old heroine who had to call on her own magic to fight them in Tracey Baptiste’s first book, The Jumbies. This one, Rise of the Jumbies, is one of those great sequels that really delivers on the promise of its predecessor – taking Corrine and her buddies into an expanded undersea world and connecting the mythology to the roots of the trans-Atlantic slave trade, in a way that is complex but not heavy-handed.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: River Of Teeth
author: Sarah Gailey
description: It’s like Ocean’s Eleven, set in the Wild West … but with hippos! What more could you want? Sarah Gailey’s novella takes off from a real, but justly-forgotten early 20th century government plan to ranch hippos in the Louisiana bayous. From that unlikely premise, she spins a tight, rollicking heist tale starring a gorgeously diverse crew of misfits (and their faithful hippos, who have just as much personality as their riders). Prepare to be surprised – and not just by how fast and mean hippos really are.

title: Salt Houses: A Novel
author: Hala Alyan
description: What does home mean when you no longer have a house – or a homeland? This beautiful novel traces one Palestinian family’s struggle with that question and how it can haunt generations. Hala Alyan’s own family history – for years, she felt as if she belonged nowhere – clearly informed her book, but her professional life as a clinical psychologist who has worked with refugee clients plays a part too. Along with another favorite from this year – Mohsin Hamid’s Exit West – this is an example of how fiction is often the best filter for the real world around us.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Scandalous Ever After (Romance Of The Turf Book 2)
author: Theresa Romain
description: I only discovered Theresa Romain this summer, and then I lost several weeks of my life reading everything she had ever written. Scandalous Ever After is actually Book 2 in a series set in England’s horse-racing scene, but it stands perfectly well on its own. If you like Regency romances, sensitively-written heroes and consent as one of the hottest parts of sex, you’ll definitely fall for the widowed Lady Kate and her old friend, Evan.

title: Selection Day: A Novel
author: Aravind Adiga
description: In Aravind Adiga’s charming novel of cricket and adolescence, the narrator swoops in and out of his characters’ inner voices with frightening precision and speed, laying out the paranoia, obsessions, tokens, idols and self-made prisons of each kid in a few bright, laserlike sentences. It’s sharp, sweet and never, ever, ever boring.

title: Sex And Rage: A Novel
author: Eve Babitz
description: Eve Babitz’s 1979 novel Sex and Rage – reissued this year – is that third drink you stay out for, knowing you’re going to regret it the next morning (but in the best way possible). Babitz knows the ‘70s Los Angeles scene intimately because she lived it – and lived to tell the tale. Her writing is a refreshing antithesis to Joan Didion’s bleak vision of L.A., and her experiences blow through this book like hot wind in the jacaranda trees. Sex and Rage isn’t as sharp as the books Babitz made her name on (Eve’s Hollywood; Slow Days, Fast Company), but it’s nonetheless a mesmerizing account of a young woman grappling with addiction and balance in her career and life.

title: Shattered: Inside Hillary Clinton's Doomed Campaign
author: Jonathan Allen and Amie Parnes
description: Shattered was conceived as a behind-the-scenes account of how the first female president won the White House. But Jonathan Allen and Amie Parnes, who had unusual degrees of access to the Clinton campaign, could not have foreseen the outside forces and inside failings that led to her defeat – many of which they describe here in detail. An early front-runner in the race to explain Clinton’s stunning fall, this account has held its own through a long year of self-examination among Democrats, journalists and other professional observers.

title: Shining City: A Novel
author: Tom Rosenstiel
description: Uncannily pertinent to today’s hypercharged partisan battles, Shining City is a political thriller that opens with the sudden death of a Supreme Court justice. The president calls in Washington fixer Peter Rena and his partner Randi Brooks to secretly vet his new nominee to the Court, and as Peter and Randi wade through congressional minefields and treacherous, journalistic turf wars, they stumble upon a series of murders that appear linked to the president’s pick. Shining City is an entertaining page turner with an insider’s’ view of how Washington works.

title: Signals: New And Selected Stories
author: Tim Gautreaux
description:
“Several angry looking people sat in hot cars, their faces carrying the uncomprehending disappointment of boiled crabs.” That is how Tim Gautreaux describes the denizens of a steamy Walmart parking lot in Louisiana.
Gautreaux is the son of a Cajun tugboat captain, but he has never liked being described as a “Southern writer.” His latest book of short stories presents vivid, poignant portraits of a junk collector in Ohio, a furnace repairman in Minnesota, a Latvian in Louisiana and many other working-class characters who want to do good but struggle. You root for them – you’re completely drawn into their world.  And that, for me, is the mark of a great writer.

title: Sin Bravely: A Memoir Of Spiritual Disobedience
author: Maggie Rowe
description: Everything about Sin Bravely is unexpected. It’s a deeply personal examination of what can happen when you take religion to the extreme, but it’s also hilarious. Maggie Rowe tells her own story of checking into an evangelical psychiatric facility after years of worrying she isn’t devout enough. Her fear of eternal damnation is real and at times uncomfortable to read about. But what I found so refreshing about this book was the way Rowe balanced serious religious reflection and humor without an ounce of snark or cynicism.

title: Smile: A Novel
author: Roddy Doyle
description: Irish novelist Roddy Doyle has always been known for his uproariously funny books, but his latest novel doesn’t have any laughs, and that’s by design. Smile follows a middle-aged man reeling from a breakup as he meets an old schoolmate who reminds him of a past he’d just as soon forget. It’s a shocking book, at times almost unbearable to read, and it’s by far the most serious of Doyle’s career. It’s also his bravest novel yet, and, by far, his best.

title: Sour Heart
author: Jenny Zhang
description: Jenny Zhang’s short story collection lit up my text messages with friends for weeks. We couldn’t stop dissecting Zhang’s characters, many of them Chinese immigrants or kids of Chinese immigrants, like us. Getting to know these characters and their varied flaws was like seeing parts of our lives reflected back at us – a rare experience. Zhang’s incisive, no-holds-barred style exposes the world of young Chinese-Americans in New York City as they attempt to make sense of how they and their families fit into it. Place is a crucial part of her work, as is the relationship of her young adults to their parents and siblings. Zhang deftly constructs a world in which many different types of characters co-exist and connect; I couldn’t help but feel as though the breadth of the Chinese-American experience had been rightfully acknowledged. Zhang is a writer to watch.

title: South And West: From A Notebook
author: Joan Didion
description: Full disclosure: I worship Joan Didion. She could publish a volume of her old grocery lists and I would read it. But I promise you, this book is meatier than that – it’s a fascinating glimpse at two unfinished projects. The first section contains observations and interviews from a 1970 road trip through the American South. The second is a meditation on Californian girlhood that Didion wrote during the 1976 Patty Hearst trial. Both provide a peek into Didion’s process while offering insightful crumbs with newfound relevance.

title: Spoonbenders: A Novel
author: Daryl Gregory
description: Following a singular family of dysfunctional psychics between the 1960s and 1990s, Spoonbenders is a wacky, heartfelt novel, cutting its deck across three generations and shuffling them together with the perils of washed-up secret government agents, aging mobsters and long-distance relationships. From sleazy con-man beginnings to moving accounts of trauma and intimacy, this reads like a game of 52 Pick-Up resolving into a royal flush.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Standard Deviation: A Novel
author: Katherine Heiny
description: I love a breezy domestic novel, and Standard Deviation certainly scratches that itch. Graham is married to Audra, a charming and vivacious graphic designer who lovingly dotes on their autistic son, Matthew. Things get interesting when the couple reconnects with Elspeth, Graham’s emotionally distant first wife. This book charmed me because of the two women at its center – they aren’t archetypes, but fully realized women. I understand why Graham fell in love with both of them.

title: Stanton: Lincoln's War Secretary
author: Walter Stahr
description: When Abraham Lincoln was assassinated in 1865, Secretary of War Edwin Stanton took over the immediate task of running the government in the final throes of the Civil War. Lincoln would not have been surprised, knowing as he did the complex and driven man who was the workhorse of his Cabinet. Here, biographer Walter Stahr details the grief, ambition and struggle that formed this Ohio river town lad who became perhaps the first Washington power lawyer. One Union general called him “the vilest man I ever knew,” but Lincoln gave him enormous credit for winning  the war.

title: Startup: A Novel
author: Doree Shafrir
description: Startup is a morality play for the era of sexting. Tech company TakeOff is looking for funding, but a young tech reporter is chasing a sexual harassment story that would put TakeOff’s future in jeopardy. New York City’s Silicon Alley in the 2010s finally has the satirical novel it deserves. Doree Shafrir, a writer for BuzzFeed, gets the details spot-on and keeps the pace tight.

title: Stay With Me: A Novel
author: Ayobami Adebayo
description: The desire – no, make that the need – to have a child is the driving force of this novel set in Nigeria at the turn of this century. A young couple, Akin and Yejide, are very much in love but they cannot get pregnant. When Akin’s mother insists he take a second wife so he can be a father, Yejide becomes increasingly desperate. But few readers will guess the lengths this couple will go to in order to have a family, and what should bring happiness ends in tragedy. This is a sad story but a compelling one that will keep you turning pages as the secrets and lies that underpin this marriage are revealed and the love that first brought them together begins to collapse under the weight of deception.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Dark"]

title: Steal The Stars
author: Nat Cassidy and Mac Rogers
description: I first experienced this book as an audio play written by Mac Rogers and meticulously directed by Jordana Williams for Tor Labs, with stellar performances and jaw-dropping production standards, serialized into podcast episodes that kept me looking for stuff to clean so I could binge-listen. And now it’s a book, novelized by Nat Cassidy, who is also one of the cast members. Cornered by cruel contracts and an unforgiving military-industrial complex, Dakota Prentiss and Matt Salem decide to spectacularly quit their jobs by stealing the state secret they’re meant to be guarding. Cassidy does a brilliant job of adapting the podcast’s scalpel-sharp pacing, heartbreaking characters and flawless execution of a fascinating premise into a new format.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Love & Romance"]

title: Stephen Florida
author: Gabe Habash
description: Gabe Habash’s brilliant debut novel follows Stephen Florida, a megalomaniac college wrestler in North Dakota, as he tries to win the NCAA championship that has been his goal for years. Habash manages to make his protagonist both charismatic and repelling, frequently on the same page, and the result is one of the most fascinating characters to come along in quite a while. Stephen Florida is brash and audacious; it’s not just one of the best novels of the year, it’s one of the best sports books to come along in quite a while.

title: Strange Practice (A Dr. Greta Helsing Novel)
author: Vivian Shaw
description: This year, there seems to be a mini-trend of women reworking classic horror and science fiction tropes, and Strange Practice is my favorite of the bunch. Dr. Greta Helsing (she has ditched the “van”), granddaughter to the famous doctor in Dracula, has her own medical practice in modern-day London. But rather than hunting vampires, she is curing earaches for ghouls and modeling new toes for decrepit mummies – until something starts killing off Londoners, both human and otherwise. A delightful ensemble mystery featuring some of literature’s favorite vampires (and one really great demon).
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Tales From Around The World", "Art"]

title: Sunshine State: Essays
author: Sarah Gerard
description: In Sunshine State, Sarah Gerard’s penetrating and deeply felt debut collection of essays, she draws on the experiences and environment of her own life – specifically, her formative years in Florida. Neither a glowing encomium nor a snarky takedown, it’s a drifting, psycho-geographical exploration of Gerard’s enduring link to the unique state she once called home.

title: Swimmer Among the Stars
author: Kanishk Tharoor
description: Kanishk Tharoor’s debut collection of short stories, Swimmer Among the Stars, tackles topics ranging from the trickiness of language to technology, history and myth. It’s a testament to his empathy, rich voice and immaculate craftsmanship as a storyteller that the book succeeds in illuminating all these things while assembling a global mosaic – even as it veers unnervingly into the fringes of the fantastic.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Swimming Lessons
author: Claire Fuller
description: A mystery built around a set of letters hidden in various old books – stories within stories within a story. How delicious! Claire Fuller’s Swimming Lessons is a kind of anti-cozy cozy mystery. Flora’s novelist father lost his wife, Ingrid, over a decade ago, and now he thinks he has seen her again. When he tries to follow her, he has a terrible accident, and his daughters (Flora and Nan) come to a seaside family home to nurse him back to health. Then, Flora starts finding her mother’s letters and realizes they offer an alternative narrative of her parents’ marriage that may unlock the secret of Ingrid’s disappearance.

title: Sympathy
author: Olivia Sudjic
description: Sympathy is a sneaky, subtle debut novel about intimacy, aspiration and the Internet. As the protagonist, Alice, stalks and befriends her Instagram hero in a worsening spiral of obsession, the reader falls into something like the glassy-eyed fugue state you reach after too much online scrolling: mesmerized, inert, unable to stop.

title: Teeth: The Story Of Beauty, Inequality, And The Struggle For Oral Health In America
author: Mary Otto
description: For many Americans, skipping trips to the dentist isn’t about forgoing pearly whites worthy of an actor’s headshot. It’s about being unable to get basic care. Almost <a href="https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/fastats/dental.htm”>20 percent of children in the U.S. have untreated cavities; the number’s higher for adults. Mary Otto, a former Washington Post reporter, doesn’t just dwell on the numbers – she makes what could have been a turgid health policy tome spark with outrage over the stories of people who have suffered, including <a href="https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=10591383”>Deamonte Driver, a 12-year-old Maryland boy who died from an infection caused by one neglected tooth.

title: Tell Me How It Ends: An Essay In 40 Questions
author: Valeria Luiselli
description: I didn’t expect these 128 pages to haunt me. Mexican writer Valeria Luiselli volunteered as a translator in a New York immigration court, asking undocumented Central American children the 40 official questions that will determine their future in the United States. Luiselli uses these questions as the framework for her essay, outlining a devastating portrait of how a child’s journey in search of a better life can rob them of their humanity. I read this book several months ago, and I still find myself wondering what happened to the children ensnared in our immigration bureaucracy. That is a testament to Luiselli’s deft and compelling writing.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: Tender: Stories
author: Sofia Samatar
description: Most of the 20 sumptuous tales in Sofia Samatar’s collection Tender take place on Earth – although not always the Earth we might recognize. Sprawling in subject from the supernatural power of names to the loneliness of a half-robot woman, Tender redefines the emotional power and literary heft that speculative fiction can convey. Where Samatar’s acclaimed fantasy novels exist in a strange, dreamlike world, her short stories daringly explore the overlap of familiarity and otherness.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Alice Network: A Novel
author: Kate Quinn
description: Two years after the end of World War II, American college student Charlie St. Clair is looking for a cousin who disappeared in France. Together with jaded former spy Eve Gardiner, she combs shattered French villages for clues – but that’s only part of this story. The other part recounts Eve’s history with the Alice Network, a real spy ring that gathered crucial intelligence for Allied forces during the previous war, and the challenges of being a female spy in the 1910s. (Challenges like deceiving and charming your targets, while maintaining your virtue in the eyes of male superiors.) The Alice Network has history, suspense, romance and women kicking butt. I couldn’t put it down.

title: The Animators: A Novel
author: Kayla Rae Whitaker
description: Filmmakers and animators Sharon Kisses and Mel Vaught are creative partners, best friends and platonic soulmates. Hot off a well-received indie film, they visit Sharon’s Kentucky hometown to work on an ambitious new project based on Sharon’s childhood trauma. (To reveal more than that would spoil it.) The novel made me rethink the benefits and consequences of marrying your creative work and your personal life and who you hurt when you turn shared pain into art.

title: The Answers: A Novel
author: Catherine Lacey
description: In The Answers, a woman named Mary responds to an ad for a mysterious job. It turns out to be a social experiment in which a famous actor hires a fleet of “girlfriends” to suit all of his needs – the protagonist’s title is “Emotional Girlfriend.” If it sounds unsettling, it is. It’s also psychologically and socially astute and leaves the reader with a kind of pleasant, lingering unease that lasts for days.

title: The Apparitionists: A Tale of Phantoms, Fraud, Photography, And The Man Who Captured Lincoln's Ghost
author: Peter Manseau
description: In 1869, William Mumler was brought to trial in New York City. Technically, it was for consumer fraud. But the “spirit photographs” he had claimed to have taken – in which ghostly figures hover behind the living subjects – were about more than just getting your money’s worth. Peter Manseau deftly outlines a century to explain how war, technology, religion and commerce came together in a perfect storm, in a court case that was fundamentally about the limits of faith. And he finds a clever balance between historical remove and the immediacy of suspense, making The Apparitionists a biography about why we believe.

title: The Beautiful Country And The Middle Kingdom: America And China, 1776 To The Present
author: John Pomfret
description: In the early 1800s, adventurous Americans made their fortunes by slaughtering fur seals and selling them to China. The U.S.-China relationship has a long and rich history dating to America’s earliest days. Author John Pomfret weaves a grand tale as he lays out the cooperation and the competition, the mutual fascination and the rivalry that has marked the relationship since the beginning.

title: The Bettencourt Affair: The World's Richest Woman And The Scandal That Rocked Paris
author: Tom Sancton
description: When Liliane Bettencourt, the aging heiress to the L’Oreal fortune, fell under the spell of a handsome, talented artist and began to shower him with gifts amounting to an incredible 1 billion euros, her daughter filed a criminal suit against the artist claiming that this “Rasputin” had exploited her mother’s oncoming dementia. The ensuing 10-year scandal outed all kinds of ugly secrets, including the company’s Nazi-collaborationist past. “There was no dye,” writes Tom Sancton in this riveting page-turner, “that could hide the family’s dark roots.”

title: The Betting Vow
author: K.M. Jackson
description: What happens in Vegas definitely does not stay in Vegas in The Betting Vow. Leila Darling is a supermodel with a temper and aspirations to be taken seriously as an actress. Carter Bain is a television executive in charge of launching a new network. To do that, he needs a star – someone like Leila. A late-night, high-stakes card game leads to a marriage of convenience – and that leads to sexy, satisfying romance in which the wedding is just the beginning of the happily ever after.

title: The Blood Of Emmett Till
author: Timothy B. Tyson
description: On Aug. 28, 1955, 14-year-old Emmett Till was lynched in Mississippi after he allegedly flirted with a white woman in a grocery store. Timothy B. Tyson’s shocking new book about Till’s murder reveals that the woman who accused Till now admits that he was not guilty of any inappropriate behavior. Tyson examines how the boy’s slaying galvanized the civil rights movement of the 1950s and ‘60s. It’s a beautifully written book, and its importance can’t be overstated.

title: The Book Of Joan: A Novel
author: Lidia Yuknavitch
description: In the near future, a space station called CIEL circles the dying Earth like a bloated star, peopled by mutated humans baroquely decorated with skin grafts. Below them, Joan of Dirt, a young woman who hears voices and has an ability to manipulate the elements, makes her way across the blighted landscape. The reality of The Book of Joan is just as tremendous and inexplicable as its premise. A take-no-prisoners disregard for genre boundaries, it is a searing fusion of literary fiction and reimagined history and science fiction thriller and eco-fantasy, one that treats sex and filth and decay and violence and nature and love with equal relish. It’s not for the squeamish – but then again, nothing interesting is.

title: The Bright Hour: A Memoir Of Living And Dying
author: Nina Riggs
description: Like Paul Kalanithi’s When Breath Becomes Air, Nina Riggs’ gorgeous, morbidly irreverent posthumous memoir about facing terminal cancer in her 30s has earned her a measure of immortality with a place on the shelf of classics about mortality. Heartbreaking? Yes. But with inspiration from her triple-great-grandfather Ralph Waldo Emerson “to cease for a bright hour to be a prisoner of this sickly body and to become as large as the World,” Riggs achieves a measure of transcendence. A poet, happily married wife and mother of two young sons, she uses wit, grit and lit to show us what it means to “distill what matters most to each of us in life.”

title: The Burning Girl: A Novel
author: Claire Messud
description: Julia and Cassie have been thisclose since nursery school. But when this novel opens, it’s the last summer of their friendship. And it’s a magical one, spent lying in the sun by the quarry, exploring the woods for an abandoned asylum and playing make-believe games. But as seventh grade begins, the two friends drift apart. Julia, our narrator, can’t understand why and, as Cassie begins to go down a dangerous path, neither can she help her old friend. At its heart, The Burning Girl is a vivid, true-to-life portrait of what it feels like to have an intense childhood friendship and how hurtful and confusing it can be when it’s over. Claire Messud’s beautifully written latest novel will surely resonate with anyone who has ever either had a friend or been a teenager.

title: The Butchering Art: Joseph Lister's Quest to Transform the Grisly World of Victorian Medicine
author: Lindsey Fitzharris
description: Lindsey Fitzharris’ The Butchering Art isn’t about carving meat for cooking. It’s about surgery the way it used to be done, and it’s about one surgeon in particular who changed everything. In 19th-century England, the introduction of anesthesia led to emboldened surgeons and a spike in surgical and post-surgical deaths. That’s when Joseph Lister began to champion germ theory antiseptic surgery. At first, his theories were met with opposition, but they were ultimately accepted – with help from Queen Victoria.

title: The Changeling: A Novel
author: Victor LaValle
description: I love fractured fairy tales, and 21st-century magical realism master Victor LaValle uncovers a doozy in * The Changeling.* It’s the story of a baby lost, a mother deranged and a father descending deep into his own mess of a family inheritance. LaValle has fun with the  bortbyting legend he mines; the trolls that steal babies in that Scandinavian lore first appear in LaValle’s plot as basement-dwelling computer hackers. But he’s best when showing how the current realities that afflict urban dwellers of color – above all, racism – stymie even the most heroic monster hunters. Here, as in old fairy tales, mythic threats can’t hold a candle to the volatility of actual people.

title: The Child: A Novel
author: Fiona Barton
description: Like her 2016 smash hit The Widow, Fiona Barton’s new book centers on a cold case involving a little one. This time, the child is a newborn’s skeleton, discovered at London building site. Newspaper journalist Kate Waters is back, looking for a way to impress her boss; The Child unfolds from her perspective and that of two other women, a grieving mother named Angela and an anxiety-stricken young woman, Emma. Barton moves the story along with spliced action-narration, an excellent way of reminding you that no crime affects just one person, and each person it affects has many other people in her life.

title: The Cold Eye (The Devil's West Book 2)
author: Laura Anne Gilman
description: The second book in Laura Anne Gilman’s “The Devil’s West” series follows the further adventures of Isobel, the 16-year-old girl whose job it is to be the eyes, ears, conscience and justice of the Devil in the Old West. Here, she’s chasing after disbelieving Easterners looking to co-opt the powerful magic that resides in the West. And like the book that came before, the best thing about The Cold Eye is the interactions between its characters and the grimy, rugged, merciless world that Gilman has created where the horses always need brushing, the firewood always needs gathering and there are never enough baths. It’s a Western that doesn’t forget how hard life once was, and Gilman offers no easy mercy to those who live within it.

title: The Color Of Law: A Forgotten History Of How Our Government Segregated America
author: Richard Rothstein
description: The Color of Law lays out in clear, accessible prose “A Forgotten History of How Our Government Segregated America.” Throughout the 20th century, racist policies at the local, state and federal level worsened housing segregation and its attendant ills, educational and economic deprivation. Far from being the de facto segregation that prominent commentators and courts frequently suggest, American segregation is de jure and made up of decades of government-designed and government-sanctioned policies preventing black Americans from receiving benefits for military service, purchasing and renting homes, and much more. This book could not be more timely.

title: The Color Of Our Sky: A Novel
author: Amita Trasi
description: Mukta, the daughter of a temple prostitute, and Tara, the daughter of an upper middle-class family, form an unlikely bond in Mumbai. But when Mukta is kidnapped, that bond is put into question, and years later Tara is tortured with regret as she searches for her friend. Amita Trasi’s personal, up-close telling of Mukta’s story exposes the harsh realities of prostitution in India. The friends’ narratives appear side-by-side, and as the book progresses it becomes more about the power of women’s relationships than the oppression of women in India.

title: The Complete Stories Of Leonora Carrington
author: Leonora Carrington
description: This collection of the late surrealist artist Leonora Carrington’s published and unpublished short stories – many newly translated from their original French and Spanish – is a terrific introduction to her bizarre, dreamlike worlds. These stories have the distinct affect of fairy tales – intuitive logic that carries the reader from one moment to the next without a sense of cause-and-effect, as far away from psychological realism as possible. A woman undresses down to her skeleton; a witch gives birth to a litter of boars and kills all but the one that most resembles its father – and all in Carrington’s stripped-down prose. Complete Stories is an unusual but singular delight.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Dark", "Art", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Dinner Party: And Other Stories
author: Joshua Ferris
description: The Dinner Party is a dark and delicious chocolate box of dysfunction – stories about inept, entitled, cruel or merely stupid men, often in failing relationships. In one, a sitcom writer flounders at a party. In another, a mover irritates his employer. These stories often start out mild and spiral out of control. With this collection, Joshua Ferris interrogates the demands and expectations of masculinity with black humor and a great instinct for timing.

title: The Dry: A Novel
author: Jane Harper
description: Jane Harper’s debut novel opens with three dead bodies – an apparent murder-suicide – in a small Australian town beset by drought. Federal agent Aaron Falk is lured back to his hometown for the funerals and soon ensnared in a place that is haunted by mysteries, past and present. The real horror of the story, though, is the water-starved landscape. There’s a moment when Falk is overcome by a moment of terrifying silence, but there’s no serial killer lurking in the quiet. Rather, he has discovered that the river he has known since childhood has run dry. For this reader, raised in the American West, that reality is scarier than just about anything I’ve ever read.

title: The Essex Serpent: A Novel
author: Sarah Perry
description: In The Essex Serpent, Sarah Perry’s glorious historical novel, nature is not inertly beautiful, but dangerous and alive: A sheep is sucked into the mud, boats are taken by the water, earthquakes split houses, and the residents wake in the mornings “from dreams of wet black wings.” Those wings belong to the titular Essex Serpent, a water serpent terrorizing the village of Aldwinter. A story of collective panic, fear, superstition, and chaos, The Essex Serpent is utterly, endlessly lovely.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: The Family Imprint: A Daughter's Portrait Of Love And Loss
author: Nancy Borowick
description: When Nancy Borowick learned that both her parents were facing deadly cancers while still in their 50s, she cried, then picked up her camera. Her chronicle of the last two years of their lives together is heartbreaking, compassionate and funny. I return to these photos again and again, wishing that I could have known Howie and Laurel in life – and grateful that I can know them and their family through their daughter’s eyes.

title: The Force: A Novel
author: Don Winslow
description: Before this novel was published in June, I was at a gathering of famous mystery writers; all of them were raving about The Force as the police procedural to read in 2017. It follows an elite squad of NYPD detectives as they close in on a seriously evil drug kingpin. At a time when the role of the police is under question across the country, The Force balances its respect for the tough jobs cops do with a clear-eyed acknowledgement of how easy it is for those in authority to believe their own mythology. This is a terrific suspense story, packed with twists, turns and double-crosses.

title: The Golden House: A Novel
author: Salman Rushdie
description: One of Salman Rushdie’s characters in The Golden House tells an American, “Your country is young – one thinks differently when one has millennia behind one; you have not even 250 years.” Rushdie has experience with ancient countries, having lived in and written about India and the U.K. This book follows a wealthy Bombay family that moves to New York just as Barack Obama becomes president. It’s deeply American, with themes of reinvention and immigration. At the same time, The Golden House is unmistakably Rushdie, packed with pop culture references and mythological tropes, telling an archetypical story in a way that feels new.

title: The Gulf: The Making of An American Sea
author: Jack E. Davis
description: I’ve been captivated by the emerald green Gulf of Mexico since I was a kid, and now I have more reason to be in awe. University of Florida historian Jack Davis provides a colorful, sweeping history of the Gulf with stories about its communities, industries, estuaries, wildlife, people and politics. He says it’s the story of America – from Native Americans and European explorers, to oil wildcatters and fishing guides. Also a professor of sustainability studies, Davis portrays a vulnerable Gulf Coast in ecological crisis. In a chapter about the late artist Walter Anderson (who rode out hurricanes on Mississippi’s barrier islands), Davis writes, “If anything was certain on the Gulf, it was the inevitability of change.”

title: The Hearts Of Men: A Novel
author: Nickolas Butler
description: If, like me, you grew up spending summers at Boy Scout camp, this book cuts right to your soul. But even if you’ve never built a fire or tied a square knot, Nickolas Butler’s The Hearts of Men provides an engrossing and entertaining read.  The book’s plot unfolds over the course of 60 years in and around  a Wisconsin Scout camp. It probes at what changes, and what stays pretty much the same, as the decades pass and technology, society, and cultural values shift.

title: The Heirs: A Novel
author: Susan Rieger
description: The question that Susan Rieger explores in The Heirs is a fascinating one: Does death root us in memory as the people we were while breathing, or should consequent data, gathered after the fact, be used to ret-con the recollections of the survivors? Or, put more simply, is a good man forever a good man even after death shakes loose secrets he kept hidden in life? Though never explicitly answered – or answered in different ways by different characters – the primary joy in The Heirs is in chewing over Rieger’s gorgeous prose. The entire book reads in a clipped, clean, lockjawed voice, as if the words themselves had all gone to Vassar and spent their summers boating in the Hamptons.

title: The Idiot
author: Elif Batuman
description: Elif Batuman’s novel The Idiot encapsulates those years of humiliating, but vibrant confusion that come in your late teens. A confusion that’s not even sexual, but existential and practical: Where do people get their opinions from? “How did you separate where someone was from, from who they were?” How do I “dispose of my body in space and time, every minute of every day, for the rest of my life[?]” Like those years, The Idiot is boring and strangely intense, depressing and absurd – but mostly charming.

title: The Inkblots: Hermann Rorschach, His Iconic Test, And The Power Of Seeing
author: Damion Searls
description: Who can resist a quickie quiz that promises to reveal the secrets of our psyches? Especially when the quiz includes those arresting, mysterious inkblots. For decades, courts, the military and prospective employers relied on the personality test devised in 1921 by Hermann Rorschach, a dashing young Swiss psychiatrist and artist. Thanks to author Damion Searls, I now know that the eminent Dr. Rorschach was a Ryan Gosling lookalike who died tragically young. Searls also points out that the Rorschach test has proved to be pretty much worthless, even though it’s still in use. Given the current replication crisis in social science research, it’s good to be reminded that the problem of alluring but sketchy science has deep roots.

title: The Last Kid Left: A Novel
author: Rosecrans Baldwin
description: Rosecrans Baldwin’s novel of young love, small-town desolation and bloody murder plays out like a crowded and oddly modern version of The Scarlet Letter, but one without easy nobility and where every single character is broken and walks around all day with their guts hanging out. It is a witch-hunt story for our age of witch hunts. Guilty-until-proven-innocent-by-someone-else-just-being-MORE-guilty. Like a small town news broadcast brought to vivid life using the points of view of multiple characters, each one of them less trustworthy than the last.

title: The Leavers: A Novel
author: Lisa Ko
description: Eleven-year-old Deming’s mother, Polly, is a Chinese immigrant, in the country illegally. One day she goes to her job at a nail salon and never returns. Ten years later, Deming goes by Daniel, and he is a college dropout with a gambling problem – and then someone from his past unexpectedly contacts him with information about his mother. The Leavers is plotted like a good mystery, but that is not really the point. Instead, it’s a heartbreaking account of the terrible circumstances that would cause a mother to abandon her son.

title: The Legend Of Rock, Paper, Scissors
author: Drew Daywalt, illustrated by Adam Rex
description: This is a wonderfully imagined origin story of the silly game kids – and some grown-ups – play to figure out who gets the short end of the stick. In the beginning, Rock, Paper and Scissors are all unbeatable in their own realms – until they come face to face. The book is filled with dialogue that makes you laugh, as when Scissors says, “I hope you’re wearing your battle pants, Rock Warrior,” and Rock responds, “If by ‘battle pants’ you man ‘no pants but I’m willing to fight you’, then yes … Yes, I am wearing my ‘battle pants.’ ” We all know how that ends. But then Paper arrives, and round they go again. Even if kids can’t read, they can appreciate the clever use of typefaces to distinguish Rock from Paper from Scissors. This is a great read-aloud book that gives you a chance to try out all sorts of voices. (For ages 4 to 8)
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters"]

title: The Little French Bistro: A Novel
author: Nina George
description: I started this novel in July with low expectations. The title suggested something breezy, but the book opens with the musings of a suicidal, 60-something German housewife – not exactly the summer read I had expected. I was won over by Nina George’s smart, unsentimental story, its free-spirited characters and its fierce undercurrent of hope, as the miserable Marianne Messmann changes her life by becoming part of a tight-knit Breton community. The Little French Bistro is about the transformative power of love – a hard-nosed fairy tale for grown-ups.

title: The Marrow Thieves
author: Cherie Dimaline
description: After the ecological and psychological collapse of North America, indigenous people all over the continent – the only ones who can still dream – are hunted by the government and put in second-generation versions of residential schools. When orphan Frenchie joins a group of refugees led by the experienced Miigwans, the new family moves slowly North, looking for a place safe enough to be home. With writing that is crisp, lyrical and evocative, Métis author Cherie Dimaline breathes new life into the young adult apocalypse, with a near-future story that holds up an uncompromising mirror to the present. Unsettling, poignant and satisfying.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Ministry Of Utmost Happiness: A Novel
author: Arundhati Roy
description: I confess to a weakness for sprawling , messy epics – books that embrace the evolution of history and culture, with lashings of intrigue and romance throughout. The Ministry of Utmost Happiness does all this. (It begins in a graveyard – I was hooked.) Arundhati Roy weaves many themes of contemporary India – caste divisions (still), interethnic conflict, eco wars, transgender rights – into a story that will satisfy many (but not all) of the fans who so rabidly embraced her first book, The God of Small Things, some 20 years ago. This is not that. But it is very much worth your time.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Misfortune Of Marion Palm: A Novel
author: Emily Culliton
description: Marion Palm is on the lam. She has a backpack full of money embezzled from the private school where she works. Left behind are her lazy husband and their two daughters, who begin to fray a bit around the edges (Nathan Palm becomes an obsessive lifestyle blogger). But Marion is finally free to do as she likes – even though she doesn’t really have a plan. Will she return to her life? Should she feel worse about escaping it? Do you empathize with her and her longing for financial and emotional freedom? Do you condemn or condone her abandoning her children? The Misfortune of Marion Palm is a fun caper, maybe especially because it’s hard to tell if you’re rooting for Marion Palm or against her.

title: The Mother Of All Questions
author: Rebecca Solnit
description: After decades of publishing trenchant essays on politics, feminism and the environment, Rebecca Solnit is finally getting the attention she deserves. The Mother of All Questions, a follow-up to her 2014 book Men Explain Things to Me, is a typically sharp, graceful work on the subject of misogyny, motherhood and “the reciprocal silence we call gender roles.”

title: The New Analog: Listening And Reconnecting In A Digital World
author: Damon Krukowski
description: This is not a book about why vinyl sounds better; it’s way more interesting than that. Damon Krukowski, who used to be in a band called Galaxie 500, writes about how the transition to digital sound has changed the way we interact with one another in ways we might not understand. It’s a subtle book, and it isn’t full of opinions. But it is full of things I didn’t know, like why people yell into cellphones. (It has to do with the design of the microphone.) Ultimately, it’s about how we consume sound as a society – which is, increasingly, on an individual basis.

title: The New Odyssey: The Story Of The Twenty-First Century Refugee Crisis
author: Patrick Kingsley
description: Despite paying attention to the flood of news reports, documentaries and articles about the Syrian refugee crisis, I’ve had a hard time getting a grasp on the big picture. This book put everything in perspective. Patrick Kingsley draws you into the global crisis by presenting the personal stories of migrants, along different routes and in different stages of the journey. His writing is absolutely gripping, and his book is informative without feeling like homework. He makes each person an individual, not a statistic.

title: The Ninth Hour: A Novel
author: Alice McDermott
description: The Ninth Hour begins with a suicide. The aftermath is handled with indisputable authority by a nun who works with the sick and poor, Sister St. Saviour, who arranges both a funeral for the dead man and a future for his pregnant widow, Annie. The young woman begins working in the laundry at the local convent where her daughter Sally spends much of her childhood. As Sally becomes steeped in the world of the nuns, her mother enters into a secret life beyond the convent. Alice McDermott carefully builds a world and a story that leads to an act of love so unexpected it will leave you lingering over the final page, wondering how she pulled it off.

title: The Portable Nineteenth-Century African American Women Writers
author: Hollis Robbins and Henry Louis Gates Jr. (editors)
description: In The Portable Nineteenth-Century African American Women Writers, Henry Louis Gates Jr. and Hollis Robbins have assembled an incisive collection of pieces by over 50 women, spanning nearly a century and highlighting the importance of varied narratives in connecting the past to the present. From slave testimony to satire, romance novels to cultural commentary, these pieces form a complex and compelling whole. This isn’t a book seeking tidy answers about the “right” ambitions. It’s a rewarding history and a reminder that the past is never a single story.

title: The Power
author: Naomi Alderman
description: What if women could summon electricity from their fingers? How would the world change? Naomi Alderman’s novel * The Power* begins as a kind of feminist fantasy – women with newfound power, living free from fear – and spins into a darker parable about the inevitable corruptions of power. It’s wonderful.

title: The President's Keepers: Those Keeping Zuma In Power And Out Of Prison
author: Jacques Pauw
description: Wow, this one’s a cracker. It’s dynamite. You may not be steeped in South African politics, or the whys and wherefores of the late Nelson Mandela’s African National Congress, but Jacques Pauw’s exposé of the party’s current leader, the increasingly discredited President Jacob Zuma, pulls no punches. In The President’s Keepers, Pauw, an investigative journalist, breathlessly and painstakingly documents the people he describes as maintaining Zuma and his compromised government in power – and keeping the president himself out of prison. There’s evidence and anecdote aplenty in this gripping tale of Zuma’s fight for his political life, and it’s never quite clear whether he is the puppet or the puppet master.

title: The Radium Girls: The Dark Story Of America's Shining Women
author: Kate Moore
description: After World War I, many women worked in factories painting light-up dials. Not long after, they started to get sick. The companies claimed not to know why. The women knew better; they were being poisoned by the radium in their glowing paint. This isn’t an easy read; The Radium Girls viscerally describes both the pain these women suffered and their struggle for justice in a legal system designed to favor corporations. At times, it reads like a true-crime story (radium killed these young women, but the companies murdered them), but there’s still a hint of hope at the end.

title: The Red
author: Tiffany Reisz
description: Mona will do just about anything to save her late mother’s art gallery. Enter Malcolm, a mysterious (sexy) stranger with an unusual (and sexy) proposition – permission to do whatever he wants with her body in exchange for help saving her gallery. This familiar plot gets a smart and intriguing twist: Each sexual encounter is staged like a famous work of art. They start with Manet’s Olympia and move onto William-Adolphe Bouguereau’s Nymphs and Satyr, and James Sharples’ A Portrait of a Gentleman among others. Let’s just say that these scenes make for some very interesting subway reading.

title: The Refugees
author: Viet Thanh Nguyen
description: Viet Thanh Nguyen’s short story collection was published at a critical moment at the start of 2017, as the U.S. was rife with debate about the immigration status of those fleeing conflicts abroad. With his gorgeous prose, the Pulitzer Prize-winning author of The Sympathizers continues his tradition of illuminating Vietnamese-Americans in all their humanity. Stories about ghosts, family, sexuality and place fill this collection. It’s not one to be missed.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Americana"]

title: The River Bank: A Sequel To Kenneth Grahame's The Wind In The Willows
author: Kij Johnson
description: A beautiful, pitch-perfect sequel to Kenneth Grahame’s The Wind in the Willows, with characters original to Kij Johnson and spot illustrations by Kathleen Jennings, The River Bank  is that species of fan fiction that unfolds new material from a beloved property with hardly a hint of a seam. Meandering with a river’s pace through musings on art, home and the end of summer, The River Bank  is a more than worthy successor.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Rules Do Not Apply: A Memoir
author: Ariel Levy
description: In this memoir, New Yorker writer Ariel Levy reflects on her life leading up to a breathtakingly painful period that – at the risk of sounding cliché – changed everything. Instead of being a downer, this ends up as a hopeful meditation on womanhood and a universal question: What’s the most we should expect from ourselves and our bodies? It’s also a rumination on memory, family, commitment and the frailty of those things. You don’t have to be a woman for Levy’s writing to resonate, though. It’s unfailingly raw and relentlessly human.

title: The Rules Of Magic
author: Alice Hoffman
description: This standalone prequel to Practical Magic is mostly set in the 1960s and features the lives of the Owens siblings – Frances, Bridget and Vincent. Coming of age on the Upper East Side with fashionable parents suspicious of the extraordinary, they explore and develop their powers together in secret, all the while resisting – and failing to resist – falling in love, with catastrophic consequences. Alice Hoffman’s prose is as tender, dreamy and sweet as ever, laced with the sting of vinegar and broken glass, and The Rules of Magic blends the tug of the familiar with the enchantment of the new into a lovely, bittersweet brew.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Historical Fiction"]

title: The Sarah Book
author: Scott McClanahan
description: In a just world, there will be people who, after exhausting themselves of the tired “dirty realist” establishment (Raymond Carver, Charles Bukowski, et. al), instinctively and logically reach for Scott McClanahan next. The Sarah Book is a strange, unlovely tragicomedy, charting the fall of a character we’re never really meant to root for in the first place. Each page feels heavy with a greasy, blue-collar nihilism – a classic sort of American melancholy so embedded in our blood that moments of salvation or stupidity within feel, for better or worse, uncannily ours.

title: The Scent Of Jasmine: Coming Of Age In Jerusalem And Damascus
author: Anan Ameri
description: A vibrant collection of vignettes evokes the author’s childhood in Damascus, Syria, and Jerusalem and her coming-of-age in Amman, Jordan, and Cairo in the 1950s and ‘60s. Each chapter is linked by the era’s heady political atmosphere, which Ameri’s family fully participated in, and the aroma of jasmine, which grew in abundance in the courtyard of her grandfather’s elegant Damascus home. Now a sociologist and journalist, Ameri writes with tenderness, energy and wit about her secular Muslim family, and her stories are filled with often-poignant surprises: the dramatic marriage between her wealthy Syrian mother and socialist Palestinian father, the open secret of a lesbian relative, the pleasures of visiting a Syrian ice cream shop established in 1885 and Ameri’s eventual move to the U.S. to work as an activist. More than anything, this memoir is a love letter to Damascus and Jerusalem, two cities that tug on the heart of the world, and a warm, wistful look back at a Middle East that has virtually disappeared.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "History", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture"]

title: The Secret Lives Of Color
author: Kassia St. Clair
description: In this beautifully written and thoughtfully produced book, Kassia St.Clair, who writes a column on color for Elle Decoration, examines the roots of 75 different shades, dyes and hues, from the obscure – like gamboge and celadon – to more familiar shades like saffron, magenta and emerald. These potted histories of pigments double as a history of human civilization: Why did Homer never mention the color blue? Why was purple an imperial color? Why is scarlet both regal and scandalous? And did you know puce came from the French word for flea? Full of anecdotes and fascinating research, this elegant compendium has all the answers.

title: The Seventh Function Of Language: A Novel
author: Laurent Binet, translated by Sam Taylor
description: Laurent Binet’s follow-up to HHhH is a postmodern intellectual romp and hilarious mystery for the Mensa set and fans of Umberto Eco and Tom Stoppard. With a lineup of characters plucked from the heavyweights of 20th century French linguistic and critical theory, Binet spins a wild plot that begins with the (actual) death of literary critic Roland Barthes in 1980. Between car chases, mutilations, graphic sex, multiple murders, Russian spies, Bulgarian assassins and Venetian thugs, this action-packed, fiendishly clever novel cunningly sneaks in serious discussions about the many ways language exerts power, particularly in politics and fiction.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Art", "Seriously Great Writing", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The State Of Affairs: Rethinking Infidelity
author: Esther Perel
description: Love doc Esther Perel has made a career of addressing a central question: How do we get both stability and adventure in exclusive romantic pairings? Her research led her to the fraught topic of infidelity, which she describes as the most universally condemned yet widely practiced taboo in relationships today. In The State of Affairs, Perel explores a vast landscape of the adulterous terrain – whether disclosing an affair is worth it, why people in happy relationships cheat, and all kinds of fresh ideas that challenge conventional wisdom. Written in a way that’s deeply humane and never preachy, Perel’s book is a reminder that couples – and the way they organize themselves – are as diverse as the people in them.

title: The Stone Sky
author: N.K. Jemisin
description: This thrilling conclusion to the Broken Earth trilogy more than sticks the landing – it rearranges the terrain on impact. Delving deep into the Evil Earth’s history with the inhuman Stone Eaters and their history, in turn, with those who made them and to what purpose, The Stone Sky also sees protagonist Essun’s reunion with her daughter-turned-rival, Nassun, as they wrestle for control of the Moon and with it, the fate of the Earth. A transformative cap to a series that hasn’t so much raised the bar on epic fantasy as it has fundamentally changed the genre.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Dark"]

title: The Storm Before The Storm: The Beginning Of The End Of The Roman Republic
author: Mike Duncan
description: Mike Duncan’s popular podcast, The History of Rome and Revolutions, packed facts, dry humor and historical parallels into easily digestible 20-minute episodes. His new book, The Storm Before The Storm, focuses on the decades that led up to the fall of the Republic. From income inequality to questions about who does and doesn’t deserve citizenship to the rise of populism, it’s consistently surprising how the issues we’re facing today were relevant two millennia ago. And if you’re worried about those parallels, this book provides a dose of reassurance. We’re divided, but hey, at least we’re not laying siege to our political rivals’ cities just yet!

title: The Strange Case Of The Alchemist's Daughter
author: Theodora Goss
description: The premise Theodora Goss lays out in her novel The Strange Case of the Alchemist’s Daughter is simple, elegant and rich with promise: The daughters of some of 19th-century literature’s greatest literary villains (and, in some cases, antiheroes) come together in a grand mystery. Strange Case is a lively Victorian adventure that celebrates, overhauls, and pokes gentle fun at the era’s weird-fiction tradition. But it’s also a sparkling conversation with the canon from which it sprang.

title: The Strays: A Novel
author: Emily Bitto
description: This debut novel showcases a dazzling and doomed collection of human beings assembled under one bohemian roof in 1930s Australia. One, an adolescent named Lily, sees all but says little – until she comes to narrate this book. Framed as a memoir drafted in 1985, The Strays invites readers into a world that is by turns disturbing and magical. The avant-garde art scene in Melbourne burns white hot, becoming the reefer-fueled backdrop here. In the foreground, two young girls fall in love, creating an intense private relationship amid the adult chaos that reigns around them, and Emily Bitto’s word pictures elevate the ordinary to the exquisite.

title: The Thing About Love
author: Julie James
description: John Shepherd and Jessica Harlow were rivals in their FBI training class. Now experienced agents assigned to the same undercover case, they’re both dealing with private, painful life changes and not at all pleased to be working together. John and Jessica have to trust each other immediately as professionals – but learning to trust emotionally takes more time, and fortunately for us, more dialogue. Bonus: Jessica’s sly inner monologue as she navigates a very male-dominated field with intelligent strategy will seem familiar to any reader who has had to run the workplace obstacle course of power plays and small talk.

title: The Totally Unscientific Study Of The Search For Human Happiness
author: Paula Poundstone
description: We all need some laughs these days, and Paula Poundstone provides a hootfest in this hilarious quest for the missing key to human happiness, which captures the off-the-wall skepticism we’ve come to love from her performances on Wait, Wait…Don’t Tell Me. She is willing to try anything: getting fit with taekwondo, promiscuous hugging, swing dancing, volunteering with the elderly and a usually forbidden all-day home-movie binge with her three kids and 12 cats. One high point: the charge she feels at the sight of canned goods neatly organized on tiered risers “like a can glee club.”

title: The Twelve Lives Of Samuel Hawley: A Novel
author: Hannah Tinti
description: If you love father-son adventure novels, meet the father-daughter adventure novel, starring Samuel Hawley and his 12-year-old daughter, Loo. They have a lot of guns, and Loo is learning how to shoot at least a few of them. While Samuel, a true freelance, a gun-for-hire, attempts to hide his outlaw identity from Loo, his 12 bullet wounds eventually become a dozen stories that he remembers – and readers learn, along with a great deal more. As Samuel attempts to dull his own pain, Loo passes into adolescence and her own pain awakens. Hannah Tinti’s tender, careful evocation of how memories work for and on each of them makes this a must-read.

title: The Twenty Days Of Turin: A Novel
author: Giorgio De Maria, translated by Ramon Glazov
description: Giorgio De Maria’s cult novel was published in Italy in 1977, at a time when the country was wracked by constant terror attacks and consequent police state crackdowns and violence. Neighbors distrusted each other. The social and political fabric was unraveling. They called it the “Years Of Lead,” and it was the inspiration for The Twenty Days – a spooky, strange piece of magical realism that captures, nearly note for note, the turmoil and chaos of a community whose center has shattered but is being held together by a willful, communal rejection of the breakage. It’s an odd book to be reading at this point in history, but one that is almost spookily prescient of our current time and place.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Unwomanly Face Of War: An Oral History Of Women In World War II
author: Svetlana Alexievich, translated by Richard Pevear and Larissa Volokhonsky
description: Think of Nobel laureate Svetlana Alexievich as the Studs Terkel of the former Soviet Union. Her books are compilations of oral history; she’s conducted hundreds of interviews with survivors of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster, veterans of the Soviet occupation of Afghanistan and, in this case, female veterans of what Russians call the Great Patriotic War. The Soviet Union lost an unfathomable 20 million soldiers and civilians in World War II. Alexievich spoke exclusively to women, who served on the front to a degree unmatched in any other country. They were astonished that someone asked for their stories. We’re astonished, in turn, by their first-person accounts of horror, heroism, love, patriotism, loss and disillusion. The translation is by the famed husband-wife team of Richard Pevear and Larissa Volokhonsky, who live up to their reputation.

title: The Unyielding
author: Shelly Laurenston
description: Campy, rage-drenched hilarity with a side order of Norse mythology, retold through a cast of diverse characters, The Unyielding is Part 3 of a trilogy wherein the Norse gods have human clans of warriors on Earth. The Crows are loyal to the goddess Skuld; their motto is “Let rage be your guide.” This book, and the whole series, is the superhero television show you wish you could watch as well as a meditation on finding your true allies and friends. After all, when you’re heading into life’s daily battles, you should always know the answer to the question: “Who are my sister Crows?”
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Humor & Comedy", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Way Of The Hare
author: Marianne Taylor
description: Sometimes, you just want a book about something someone loves. The Way of the Hare isn’t particularly interested in poetic turns of phrase; it’s interested in lagomorphs, and that’s enough. Marianne Taylor fondly follows her subjects from their introduction to Britain through to their occasionally-grim futures, gathering information about witch hunts and rabbit hearts as she goes. The history is absorbing, the science full of interesting trivia, and the author’s passion evident – for Taylor, treatment of the hare is a symptom of our greater relationship with the natural world. As goes the genus Lepus, so go we.

title: The Wide Circumference Of Love
author: Marita Golden
description: Let the title of this beautiful novel entice you. Set in a Washington, D.C., readers don’t often see, The Wide Circumference of Love is a generous rendering of a black family’s efforts to adapt and cope with its architect patriarch’s descent into Alzheimer’s disease. Gregory Tate’s progressive condition provides an opportunity for family members to revisit parts of their own, painful past. Meanwhile, Diane Tate, a devoted and supremely pragmatic wife and mother, transforms the loss of her husband and their cherished life together into a journey of optimism and love.

title: The Woman Next Door: A Novel
author: Yewande Omotoso
description: Ever had a “hate-ship”? That’s how author Yewande Omotoso <a href https://www.npr.org/2017/02/12/514495267/next-door-neighbors-gradually-learn-to-get-along-in-post-apartheid-cape-town" target="_blank" >characterizes the relationship between Hortensia and Marion, the neighbors in her novel, The Woman Next Door. Hortensia, who’s black, and Marion, who’s white (and racist), live in an affluent part of Cape Town, South Africa. They have a lot in common: Both women were creative professionals with their own businesses, and both are widows. But they’re enemies until Hortensia breaks her leg in an accident that also leaves Marion temporarily homeless. Hortensia invites Marion in, and slowly their relationship begins to thaw. There’s no tidy Golden Girls ending , but you do have a sense that these two women will continue to talk and, bit by bit, bridge their ideological divide.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]
