For Tara Westover the answer is education. Raised in a family that was isolated from mainstream society, Tara never goes to school, never sees a doctor. Her father’s increasing religious extremism mirrors a descent into madness, leaving his daughter vulnerable to violence and emotional abuse. And yet Tara escapes, and powered by a thirst for knowledge, she achieves the heights of academic success — Harvard and Oxford. The price she pays is severing ties with her home and the family she loves. Educated is a powerful memoir, painful, inspirational and impossible to put down.

title: These Truths: A History Of The United States
author: Jill Lepore
description: These Truths is a compelling, page-turning journey through U.S. history. From 1492 to the 2016 election, Lepore tells the nation’s story — the glory and the guts. The United States was founded on a set of truths that were supposed to be self-evident, but from the beginning, there have been bitter divisions over what those truths really mean. This vivid history is a must-read for anyone wrestling with today’s toxic political environment. “The past is an inheritance, a gift and a burden,” she writes. “It can’t be shirked. You carry it everywhere. There’s nothing for it but to get to know it.” Lepore offers us the keys to the past, so we can unlock the present.

title: 84K
author: Claire North
description: The grimy, meticulously constructed and worrisomely believable dystopia Claire North has created for 84k is our world, just a few years down the path. A near-future England gone bonkers with fiscal rationalism and austerity panic — a place where every vital service has been privatized and every life assigned a monetary value. There’s nothing particularly original about the notion of such an actuarial society, but North comes to it with eyes round in terror, bringing a kind of slinking, resentful humanity to every street corner and slum. There are no heroes in this story. No glorious moments of blood and fire. There’s just a man, his past, the daughter he’s trying to find and a slow-burning revolution that sparks across 450-some pages and leaves nearly everyone we meet broken and buried in its path.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Florida
author: Lauren Groff
description: The stories in Lauren Groff’s Florida are visions of women at the ends of things, in trouble, at their breaking points. Her language is, as always, gorgeous and precise. Her ability to map the inner contours of characters who seem to exist entirely in extremis — and, almost entirely, within a fragile shell of feigned competence and normalcy — is remarkable. Her Florida is a frightening place that bends (solely through the eyes and experiences of her characters) into a discomfortingly modern Southern Gothic tradition, offering up stories that are all haunted by fear, by violence (either natural or man-made) and by the idea that life is measured in the ration of misery that you take — and the grace with which you suffer it.

title: Gnomon: A Novel
author: Nick Harkaway
description: This is a big, ambitious book that sometimes trips over its own bigness but reads like a game of literary telephone played by Phillip K. Dick, Arthur Conan Doyle and William Gibson. It begins (and is centered, kinda) in a near-future, AI-governed, digitally interconnected London with a murder investigation but spreads out to cover the lives of a Greek banker and mathematician who is almost eaten by a shark, an ancient alchemist, an Ethiopian painter, a video-game designer, a distributed future consciousness, and more and more until the resulting story covers 20 centuries, a dozen lives and topics as varied as love, death and traffic flow patterns.

title: Grist Mill Road: A Novel
author: Christopher J. Yates
description: It is summer, 1982, and in a clearing in the woods outside a fictional small town, a 14-year-old boy shoots a 13-year-old girl over and over and over again with a BB gun while, close by, a 12-year-old boy stands and watches. This is the setup for Grist Mill Road, all of it contained within the first few pages. And the rest of it is a slow-burn nightmare, building to a conclusion 26 years later that is as terrible as it is inevitable.

title: Iron Gold
author: Pierce Brown
description: In Pierce Brown’s Red Rising universe, the rebellion begun by slaves three books ago has nearly been won. But rather than simply rolling on into more pew-pew laser gun battles between murderous teenagers turned into gods and legends, Brown does something daring in his fourth book. He makes it largely about forgotten minor characters in his war – refugees, old soldiers, the enemy. The result is surprisingly mature science fiction within the frame of blazing space opera so dramatic it borders on Shakespearean and yet still full of some of the most cinematically brilliant pulp sci-fi you’ll ever read.

title: Only To Sleep: A Philip Marlowe Novel
author: Lawrence Osborne
description: The story is simple the way all gumshoe novels ought to be: Rich white guy dies, leaves his wife millions in an insurance policy, Philip Marlowe is called to investigate and does. But like all great gumshoe novels, there are cavernous depths here that only look shallow from the surface. Lawrence Osborne does a remarkable impersonation of Raymond Chandler’s voice here but an even better job getting inside the character of Marlowe, making him the dogged, everyman, knight-errant that Chandler always thought he was and writing some of the most beautiful, sad and lonely sentences of the year in the process.

title: Record Of A Spaceborn Few
author: Becky Chambers
description: The “Wayfarers” series has always had a utopian heart – a series of novels that exist to put human beings in bad situations and then watch as they think, debate, sulk, problem-solve and innovate their way out of them. Record is the third in the series, and sees the human fleet of generation ships becoming part of a larger community of space-faring species. It is brainy, tinkery sci-fi that excels at both character development and complex world-building, imagining a gritty, nuts-and-bolts working environment where everything, from orbital mechanics to the disposal of dead bodies, becomes integral parts of the plot.

title: Scribe: A Novel
author: Alyson Hagy
description: Scribe is a lean, hard wolf of a book. Vicious, merciless and with blood in its teeth. It is the story of a woman who writes letters for people in a post-apocalyptic future where war and disease have taken almost everything away and made everything that remains dangerous, untrustworthy or both. In brief, brutal passages, Alyson Hagy sketches a world that’s like an Appalachian fairy tale from the future, complete with boggy magic, mysterious men, powerful women and a quiet, mythic furiousness unlike anything else you’ll read this year.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: Space Opera
author: Catherynne M. Valente
description: In any normal year, if you were looking for a book in which a has-been, gender-fluid Brit-pop glam-rock band called Decibel Jones and the Absolute Zeroes has to compete for its collective life in an intergalactic version of the Eurovision Song Contest in order to prove that humans are, in fact, sentient and not just horrible sticky monsters that might (someday) constitute a threat to the greater universal neighborhood, you’d be SOL. But this being 2018 (and Catherynne Valente being the particularly brilliant weirdo that she is), you’re in luck because that happens to be EXACTLY what Space Opera is all about. Reading it is kinda like dropping four hits of acid, breaking into your local department store and rolling around in everything displayed behind the MAC cosmetics counter while Queen’s soundtrack to Flash Gordon plays at full volume. So, you know, be prepared.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Music", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Tell The Machine Goodnight: A Novel
author: Katie Williams
description: Tell The Machine Goodnight is sci-fi in its most perfect expression – no robots, no explosions, no car chases, just a future indelibly altered by the application of a single unfamiliar technology: the Apricity machine. In Katie Williams’ near future, this machine can tell you what you need to do to be happy. Learn a foreign language. Get a dog. Cut off the tip of your index finger. The machine is never wrong. It works 100 percent of the time. And reading about the world in which this machine exists is like having a lucid dream of six years from next week, filled with people you don’t know yet, but will.

title: The Cabin At The End Of The World: A Novel
author: Paul Tremblay
description: Read this book and you might not sleep for a week. Maybe longer. Its deceptively simple, psychologically deep tale of the (possible) end of the world and the sacrifice required to prevent it will shape your nightmares for months because that’s what this book is built for. Like the best horror, it is soft (until it isn’t), quiet (until it isn’t) and rooted firmly in the real.

title: The Sky Is Yours: A Novel
author: Chandler Klang Smith
description: A dystopian metropolis, two kids in love, an arranged marriage and … dragons. Those are the basic building blocks that Chandler Klang Smith is playing with in her bonkers sci-fi mashup-slash-satire-cum-mythological-allegory. It is an insane, genre-defying world, imagined with a bizarre clarity and sense of solidity. It feels like it could be our future. It seems like it might be an alternate universe. It reads like nothing else out there. If you love your sci-fi weird, your love stories tragic and your skies full of fire-breathing lizards, this one was made for you.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters"]

title: The View From Flyover Country: Dispatches From The Forgotten America
author: Sarah Kendzior
description: In this series of essays (originally written between 2012 and 2014 while she was writing for Al-Jazeera), Sarah Kendzior looks at a variety of issues – from student debt to unpaid internships, social media and freedom of speech – which became vital talking points in the 2016 election. Now, in 2018, the collection feels both prescient and honest in the way it discusses race and gender issues, class disparity, the abject lack of concern by the wealthy for those who have less, and the failure of the so-called “elites” to understand the Midwest. In a year when all of these things have taken on such terrible importance, seeing the roots of the arguments that now dominate cable news is both fascinating and a little bit haunting in retrospect.

title: Beneath A Ruthless Sun: A True Story Of Violence, Race, And Justice Lost And Found
author: Gilbert King
description: In December 1957, an arctic freeze descends upon the groves of Okahumpka, Fla., and the town’s human hearts prove just as icy as the stone-cold oranges. A well-off woman has been raped, and the black community finds itself under siege by the bigots of the sheriff’s department. The biggest mystery concerns Jesse Daniels, a developmentally disabled white boy who sleeps with a teddy bear every night, and why he gets railroaded for the crime. A ripping, real-life tale, Beneath a Ruthless Sun will plunge the reader into the legal practices, civil rights battles and stubborn sexual inequalities of the mid-20th century.

title: Bitter Orange
author: Claire Fuller
description: In this finely crafted psychological thriller, the devilish novelist gives us a sunny, summery, open backdrop that nonetheless becomes a vise tightening around the throats of both the main character and the reader. Mousy, 39-year-old Frances Jellico falls in with two trendy figures at an English country house. She develops a crush, then sees ominous signs all around. Things go bump in the night. What could be tired tropes carry their psychological weight here, the writing percolates insidiously, and you may miss some sleep as you stay glued to the page.

title: The Court Dancer: A Novel
author: Kyung-Sook Shin, translated by Anton Hur
description: It is 1891. Yi Jin is the most accomplished dancer at the court of Korea’s Joseon Dynasty, at a time when it is still largely sequestered from the rest of the world. The novel follows this orphan girl’s adventures as she moves to Paris with her Western husband and becomes celebrated as the only Korean woman in Paris. Identity is the central, defining conundrum: If Yi Jin is not a Korean lady or a French madame, what is she? Sorrow threads itself through these pages, yet there is a richness both to the period and the narrative as beautiful as any silk fan.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture"]

title: The Sparsholt Affair: A Novel
author: Alan Hollinghurst
description: Who, or what, is a Sparsholt? A student, a pilot, an engineer, a sex object, the focus of a seamy scandal? David Sparsholt inhabits all these roles and more in Alan Hollinghurst’s novel, a luscious, epic work that spans seven decades and several generations, beginning at Oxford University during the hair-raising blackout years of the Blitz. In the ‘60s pop hit “To Sir With Love,” Lulu sings of going “from crayons to perfume,” and similar transformations occur all the time in this ripe slice of British life in a bohemian, intellectual, artistic milieu, a Brideshead Revisited revisited.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Who Is Vera Kelly?
author: Rosalie Knecht
description: In this sly, intelligent novel, the author has fun with the idea that the mindset necessary as a closeted queer woman transfers seamlessly to high-stakes espionage work – both lesbian and spy are accustomed to subverting identity, using coded language and seeking out covert action. It is the late 1960s, and the confused and lonely Vera Kelly finds herself recruited by the CIA and assigned to keep watch over a student group in lush Buenos Aires. The book smartly juxtaposes Vera’s rocky coming of age with her thrilling adventures among the glamorous young Marxists. She discovers her true self by going undercover.

title: Calypso
author: David Sedaris
description: I always love to visit David Sedaris’ world. Some of the stories in Calypso are wonderfully absurd, like the one in which he attempts to feed his own fatty tumor to a snapping turtle. Other stories are bleak, hilarious and moving. His ruminations on his sister’s suicide, his ailing father and his mother’s alcoholism draw on a pathos we haven’t seen from Sedaris before; it’s a new depth that makes this particular collection essential.

title: Freshwater
author: Akwaeke Emezi
description: This coming-of-age story follows Ada, a young Nigerian woman, who moves to the United States for college. It’s a character study with an unusual structure — the story is told by the many “selves” that live inside her — and the writing is stunning. I love recommending this book to my friends who, without fail, always text me their favorite passages. Yes, it’s that good.

title: Come West And See: Stories
author: Maxim Loskutoff
description: Come West and See by Maxim Loskutoff is a new kind of American Western. The interconnected short stories shirk the tropes of destinies made manifest in favor of dark, interior tales of the angry, the wild and the socially impotent. This book climbs into the heads of its characters, pokes at their insecurities with a sharpened stick, then reaches out a hand to invite you in.

title: In Pieces
author: Sally Field
description: Award-winning actress Sally Field could have written a typically dishy Hollywood memoir. But her book, In Pieces, is an intensely personal, vulnerable accounting of her life and career. Field’s meditations on memory, fear and love will leave you shattered. Her lyrical prose and sly humor will glue you back together again.

title: Dactyl Hill Squad
author: Daniel José Older
description: Daniel José Older’s middle-grade debut follows Cuban-born Magdalys Roca and her friends from the Colored Orphan Asylum as they navigate Civil War-era New York City … on dinosaurs. As the city descends into chaos, the gang finds themselves on the run from an evil official who has been abducting children, claiming they’re fugitive slaves. Dactyl Hill Squad is a fast-paced adventure, an enjoyable blend of fantasy and history perfect for dino-loving adults and kids alike.

title: Ghosts In The Schoolyard: Racism And School Closings On Chicago's South Side
author: Eve L. Ewing
description: In Ghosts in the Schoolyard, poet and sociologist Eve L. Ewing delves into the history and politics of the 2013 Chicago school closings. This superbly written and researched account is at once poignant and deeply troubling, blending the personal and the academic in a way that makes the heavy subject matter accessible. Ghosts is essential reading for anyone trying to better understand the intersection of segregation and education — as well as the importance of preserving the public institutions that help shape communities.

title: Heart Berries: A Memoir
author: Terese Marie Mailhot
description: Written in part while the author was living in a mental institution, Heart Berries is a fierce and poetic memoir that grips you from the start and never lets go. Each page, paragraph and sentence is more gut-wrenching than the one before it. An illuminating account of grief, abuse and the complex nature of the Native experience, it is at once raw and achingly beautiful. Terese Mailhot is a truly fearless writer, and this little book is nothing short of a gift.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters"]

title: Night Moves
author: Jessica Hopper
description: Music critic Jessica Hopper plays all the right notes in this poetic memoir. Chronicling her years as a writer and DJ on Chicago’s independent music scene in the ‘00s, the book transports readers with vivid and at times hilarious descriptions of Chicago nightlife and its characters. The meandering chapters take on a cinematic tone, and Hopper’s rhythmic prose keeps the pages turning. Night Moves is a charming ode to friendship, aging and what it means to truly love the place you call home.

title: Contact High: A Visual History Of Hip-Hop
author: Vikki Tobak
description: Writer Vikki Tobak delivers a coffee table book for the ages, a feast of words and iconic hip-hop portraits spanning four decades. Juxtaposed with illuminating essays from people like Fab Five Freddy, Questlove, and RZA, Contact High spotlights an array of photographers and culture makers to get the backstory on some of hip-hop’s most famous images. Oh, and the fashion! This book is not only a trip down memory lane, but a testament to the enduring power of hip-hop, a culture that’s constantly growing, constantly changing and continues to inform the world we live in.

title: Listen To The Marriage: A Novel
author: John Jay Osborn
description: Can a marriage survive affairs and distrust? What is left to even try to salvage? That’s what husband and wife Steve and Gretchen are facing in their therapist’s office when Listen to the Marriage opens. But here’s the thing: The whole book takes place in that office, and it’s written from the point of view of the therapist. This book should be claustrophobic and could be cloying, but instead it’s a mind-expanding examination of the alchemy of two people joining in marriage. And that marriage needs tending to, just as much as — if not more than — each of the individuals who created it. Why get married? This novel is an argument in favor.

title: Arthur Ashe: A Life
author: Raymond Arsenault
description: Raymond Arsenault spent almost a decade researching and writing his biography of Arthur Ashe. The result, Arthur Ashe: A Life, takes in the entirety of that life, from the skinny, motherless boy who grew up in segregated Richmond, Va., to the svelte athlete who triumphed on tennis courts around the globe while advocating for global human rights. Arsenault shows us how Ashe evolved into the revered figure he was when he died, far too young, at age 49. “I think we still don’t understand exactly how much we lost with his death,” Arsenault sighs. His excellent biography gives some indication of the depth of that loss.

title: Washington Black: A Novel
author: Esi Edugyan
description: George Washington Black – Wash to one and all – is an enslaved 11-year-old who lives and toils on a Barbados sugar plantation. When the plantation master’s brother visits, he “borrows” Wash as ballast for a hot-air balloon he has constructed, but he also becomes Wash’s mentor and protector. Something happens that forces the two to flee in the balloon, first to the United States, then to the Article Circle; Wash eventually travels the globe. As he grows into a talented naturalist, he learns that freedom is not a one-time thing and that there are many ways to be enslaved – by fear, by anger, even by love. Edugyan’s prose mesmerizes.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Science", "Americana"]

title: A Place For Us: A Novel
author: Fatima Farheen Mirza
description: In Fatima Farheen Mirza’s debut novel, we first meet Amar at his sister’s wedding and discover that he has long been estranged from his family and that for much of his life, he has struggled to figure out his place within his family, the U.S. and his religion. It’s Amar’s story that helps drive the complicated plot of A Place For Us, and it’s his story — his downfall and his redemption — that keeps us reading. If you’re searching for an ambitious look at how multiple generations of an Indian-American Muslim family navigate their insider-outsider status and immigration to this country, this is the book for you.

title: Crux: A Cross-Border Memoir
author: Jean Guerrero
description: In Jean Guerrero’s gorgeous memoir, the KPBS reporter investigates her father’s life — and the stories he has spent his life telling — with a thoughtful eye. Guerrero’s father claims the U.S. government is testing out its mind control techniques on him as he battles an addiction to crack cocaine. While others say her father is mentally ill, Guerrero instead traces his journey across three continents to better understand him — and what has shaped his state of mind. In trying to illuminate her father’s story with reporting, cultural analysis and dives into neuroscience, Guerrero tells her own. The result is a moving, complicated look at the human psyche.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Americana", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Last Cowboys: A Pioneer Family In The New West
author: John Branch
description: I don’t read a lot of nonfiction because, well, I’m a journalist who works in it full time and I like the escapism of fiction. But John Branch’s portrait of a longtime Mormon ranching and champion rodeo family has all the trappings of a can’t-put-it-down modern Western. Don’t be turned away if you don’t like cows or ranching or rodeos. This is a smart portrayal of a way of life that’s slipping away even in the rural West. The Last Cowboys conveys an almost mythical area that has long been woven tightly in American identity, and there’s a lot in these pages that could help us better understand what’s behind this country’s deepening urban-rural divisions.

title: The Mirage Factory: Illusion, Imagination, And The Invention Of Los Angeles
author: Gary Krist
description: As I type this, I’m coming up on my sixth year living in Los Angeles, which is to say about four years longer than I ever expected to be here. I doubt I’m alone among many transplants to this confusing and exciting city with an, erm, complicated past. Gary Krist gets at that complicated past by expertly telling the stories of three iconic turn-of-the-century Angelenos, themselves once transplants: William Mulholland, the water engineer; the Canadian evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson; and the filmmaker D.W. Griffith. They’re all visionaries and leaders sharing another more dubious thing in common, their lives end in disgrace. Exported pop culture is just one layer of a much bigger symbol of American identity and free spirit that has long been wrapped up in the illusion — and invention — of LA. After reading this, I was torn between putting down roots — and driving to the airport and leaving on the next available flight.

title: Like A Mother: A Feminist Journey Through The Science And Culture Of Pregnancy
author: Angela Garbes
description: After reading Angela Garbes’ thorough, scientific investigation into the biology and business of pregnancy, you may find yourself picking up the phone to call your mom … just to say thanks. Nonpregnant and a nonmother, I still found this book fascinating. My favorite part? Learning that the placenta is an entirely new, roughly 2-pound organ that the female body creates during pregnancy to feed itself and the baby. Just – wow.

title: Happiness: A Novel
author: Aminatta Forna
description: In Aminatta Forna’s masterful Happiness, an American scientist and a Ghanaian psychiatrist meet by chance on Waterloo Bridge. Both have devoted their lives to the study of how people and animals survive trauma and displacement. Now they, too, find themselves uprooted in London, but they come together to help a woman whose son may have been swept up in an immigration raid. With beautiful prose and unsparing honesty, Forna explores the repercussions of war and trauma, the power of love and friendship, and ultimately the ties that bind people and animals together.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: A Lucky Man: Stories
author: Jamel Brinkley
description: Jamel Brinkley’s debut, A Lucky Man, is a masterful exploration of love and masculinity. The nine stories in this collection are set in New York and focus on black men struggling to save their relationships with their partners, families or friends. Often this means having to revisit painful moments in their past, even as every instinct tells them to turn away from it. Brinkley portrays not only their expectations, hopes and disappointments with great honesty, but also with great tenderness. His characters are indelible. A Lucky Man is a book that will be read for years to come.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Love & Romance"]

title: Those Who Knew: A Novel
author: Idra Novey
description: Idra Novey’s Those Who Knew is a timely and timeless novel about sex and power. The story begins in an unnamed country 10 years after the fall of a dictatorial regime supported by foreign powers. When a young woman who is involved with a powerful senator is killed, Lena is immediately suspicious. Years earlier, when she was herself a young activist involved with the same man, she remained silent about his abuse. What if she had spoken then? What if she speaks out now? The result is a completely riveting and elegantly constructed story.

title: Achtung Baby: An American Mom On The German Art Of Raising Self-Reliant Children
author: Sara Zaske
description: Sara Zaske is an American mom who spent several years raising her kids in Germany. She explains, with incredulity, that German kids walk to school alone in the first grade – and explains why she eventually let her kids do the same. She describes kindergarten classes where children learn to play safely with matches and a week when toys are removed from the class so kids are forced to use their imaginations. Zaske describes German childhoods as freewheeling and playful, in stark contrast to American kindergartens, where so much emphasis is placed on safety and academic achievements.

title: A Duke By Default: Reluctant Royals
author: Alyssa Cole
description: Alyssa Cole’s Reluctant Royals series is peak romance for 2018: multicultural, socially conscious, consent-driven and as sexy as it gets. In A Duke By Default, her heroine, Portia Hobbs, flees her glamorous but chaotic Manhattan life for a sword-making apprenticeship in Scotland – as one does – and discovers that her new boss, Tavish McKenzie, has a lot more to offer than blacksmithing lessons. But even better than watching Portia fall for Tavish is watching her discover her own intelligence. Portia might not have royal blood, but she’s the real catch in this story.

title: The House Of Broken Angels
author: Luis Alberto Urrea
description: The House of Broken Angels is a party in book form. The novel opens with Big Angel de la Cruz, the patriarch of a sprawling Mexican-American family, preparing to bury his mother. But Big Angel is dying, too. He has late-stage cancer, and so he gathers his relatives for one last birthday party, which turns into a raucous, joyful, irrepressibly high-spirited celebration of a novel. Even when it breaks your heart, reading about Big Angel and his family is a delight.

title: Your Duck Is My Duck: Stories
author: Deborah Eisenberg
description: There is no keener observer of American strangeness than Deborah Eisenberg. The stories in Your Duck Is My Duck, her first new story collection since 2007’s Twilight of the Superheroes, spin our infinite sorrows and failings into gorgeous meditations on love, responsibility and the many ways we fail to take care of one another. Eisenberg’s got a sly sense of humor and perfect dialogue-writing pitch, and her stories shine with intelligence and rueful, against-the-odds joy.

title: A Stitch In Time
author: Daphne Kalmar
description: There’s nothing ordinary about a high-spirited 11-year-old orphan named Donut who adores taxidermied mice. However, her inconsolable grief over the recent loss of her father is compounded when disagreeable Aunt Agnes shows up, intent on dragging Donut back to Boston with her. Fearing losing even more — her friends, community and the rural Vermont life she has come to love — Donut runs away into the woods. Set in 1927, Daphne Kalmar’s * A Stitch In Time* introduces you to a heroine who will find a way into your heart as she searches for home.  (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Packing My Library: An Elegy And Ten Digressions
author: Alberto Manguel
description: I bet in his dreams Alberto Manguel never leaves his beloved library; the disassembling and packing of which gives title to this brilliant book. The author describes his mind as capricious. I call it capacious. He references Al-Mutanabbi, Du Fu, Heisenberg, Homer, Pushkin and Verne — all in one paragraph. “I feel so deeply the loss of the books that my hands knew so well,” Manguel writes. “I’m like Thomas, wanting to touch in order to believe.” I doubt the loss of books has inspired a more beautiful work. Read this book — and hold onto it.

title: Unsheltered: A Novel
author: Barbara Kingsolver
description: In Unsheltered, Barbara Kingsolver flits back and forth between two turbulent times in America, right after the Civil War and during the 2016 campaign. The strong female protagonists in both epochs struggle with issues of race and class and making sense of a world that increasingly looks to strongmen to guide it. In an interview with NPR, she said, “I wanted to write about how people behave when their world seems to be coming apart. And why do we keep trying old solutions to new problems when it looks like they’re not working anymore? “She provides no answers but allows us to look at how our past can guide us in our present.

title: Asymmetry: A Novel
author: Lisa Halliday
description: Asymmetry is a novel that requires the reader to fill in some blanks. Like, what exactly do its three sections have in common? The first is about a May-December romance between a famous novelist and young woman who wants to be a writer. The second is the story of an Iraqi-American who is detained at Heathrow airport while en route to see his brother in Iraq. The third reveals the link between the two, though you can miss it if you blink. Taken as a whole, the book is at once entertaining and provocative, forcing you think about dichotomies that often dominate our lives: old vs. young, East vs. West, the powerful vs. the powerless.

title: The Overstory: A Novel
author: Richard Powers
description: *The Overstory *begins with the nine characters whose lives will intersect as the novel unfolds. Each has some kind of connection with a tree. These stories, so beautifully told and intricately crafted, perfectly set the stage for this saga about a small band of radical environmentalists who are determined to save an old-growth forest in the Pacific Northwest. They pay a heavy price for their determination. As Richard Powers pulls you into their lives and fills you with astonishing facts about trees, he deftly pits that price against the cost of allowing these magnificent forests to be destroyed.

title: The Winter Soldier
author: Daniel Mason
description: In this novel set during WWI, a young medical student named Lucius joins the military and finds himself in a remote outpost where he is expected to be in charge. With virtually no medical experience, he has to treat unspeakable injuries under hideous circumstances. At his side is a nurse, Sister Margarete, who is more his mentor than assistant. As the two work together, a bond grows between them, but then Lucius decides to take a shellshocked solider under his wing — a decision that turns into a life-altering mistake. This is a great war novel, plunging you into the chaos of conflict and the unexpected consequences of love that begins amid that chaos.

title: There There: A Novel
author: Tommy Orange
description: Tommy Orange begins his debut novel There There with an essay. He takes the iconic image of the Indian head, well-known from coins and late-night TV, and transforms it into a searing symbol of the mistreatment of Native Americans. It’s a devastating and brilliant piece of writing. What follows is an equally compelling work of fiction. Orange has created a large cast of characters, all connected in some way to the Native community of Oakland, Calif., and each heading inexorably toward a powwow where the climax of the story takes place. They represent different aspects of the Native American community, but as their connections to one another and to their traditions become clear, each emerges as a fully realized human you will come to care about deeply.

title: Transcription: A Novel
author: Kate Atkinson
description: Transcription is a spy novel that at first seems pretty tame. At the start of WWII, Juliet Armstrong is recruited by MI5 to eavesdrop on a group of Nazi sympathizers in Britain and transcribe their conversations. It’s not the exciting kind of mission most spy thrillers are made of, but in a profession that has deceit at its core, even the dullest job can veer into dark corners that will haunt its practitioners for as long as they live. Kate Atkinson is a great storyteller, and while this doesn’t quite measure up to the emotional rollercoaster of her previous novel, Life After Life, it is still a jolly good read.

title: Varina: A Novel
author: Charles Frazier
description: Who has given a second thought to the wife of Jefferson Davis, the first and only president of the Confederacy? Not me, I must confess. Still, Charles Frazier centers his new Civil War novel on the story of her life, taking us from her heady days at the center of the nation’s political and social circles in Washington, D.C., to her failed attempt to escape to Havana at the end of the war. A smart woman, who might have achieved a lot in different times, Varina never had much control over her own life. But looking back on it, she knows she won’t be forgiven for the part she played on the wrong side of history.

title: A Thousand Beginnings And Endings
author: Ellen Oh and Elsie Chapman (editors)
description: Edited by Ellen Oh and Elsie Chapman, this anthology focuses on retellings of South and East Asian myths and folktales, with contributors mostly of South and East Asian heritage. An author’s note appears at the end of each story, with a summary of the original tale and the reasons the author had for choosing to retell it. It’s an easy book to pick up and put down, and the stories vary widely in tone, theme, emotion, setting. A Thousand Beginnings and Endings is a special anthology; for those of us who may have heard these legends at home but never in an academic or literary setting, or for those of us who never heard them at all, letting us discover these tales through different lenses.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Small Animals: Parenthood In The Age Of Fear
author: Kim Brooks
description: American moms and dads may find Small Animals covers some all-too-familiar territory – the unhealthy fear that parents face every day. “There’s now the expectation that to be a good parent in this country you have to have your eyes on your children every second – or you have to pay another adult to have eyes on your children every second,” Kim Brooks told NPR. Drawing from extensive research, as well as from her own experience as a mom, Brooks explores how parenting in fear can have troubling consequences for everyone in the family.

title: Dragons In A Bag
author: Zetta Elliott, illustrated by Geneva B
description: This quick caper of a novel — perfect for kids who’ve moved beyond “Easy Readers” but aren’t quite ready for Harry Potter yet — tells the story of Jaxon, a little boy from Brooklyn whose family is on the brink of being evicted. While his mom fights for their home, she must leave him with a strange old woman named Ma … who just happens to be a witch … who just happens to have three baby dragons who must escape magic-less Brooklyn … and who just happens to disappear mid-task, leaving Jax and his friends to finish the job. Delightful chaos ensues, demonstrating that there might be more magic in Brooklyn than anyone (including other authors) has ever imagined. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Front Desk
author: Kelly Yang
description: Based on Yang’s own experiences as a new immigrant to the U.S. from China in the 1990s, Front Desk is a perfectly rendered child’s-eye view of the perils and joys of that life. Sitting at the front desk of the Calvista Motel in Southern California, 10-year-old Mia Tang sees everything, from the big injustices (like how the motel owner bullies and scams her parents, who manage and clean the motel) to the small (like her mom’s conviction that Mia, as a non-native speaker, will never succeed as a writer), and often feels helpless in front of them. But by learning the value of her voice and refusing to compromise on kindness, Mia builds a community strong enough to bring change. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Intercepted (Playbook, The)
author: Alexa Martin
description: To a drama lover like me, the world of Professional Sports Wives and Girlfriends is a scintillating one. Big hair, bedazzled jerseys, secret Botox and covert bitchery – every part of that picture is something I’m here for, and every part of it is delivered by Alexa Martin’s Intercepted. Better yet, they are just the trappings for a sweet, surprisingly grounded romance between Marlee, a graphic designer/delighted EX-professional football girlfriend, and Gavin Pope, the new quarterback of her ex’s team – the one man she can’t date, and the only man she wants. Whether you read it instead of Sunday football or in addition to it, it’s sure to keep you highly engaged.

title: My Life As A Goddess: A Memoir Through (Un)Popular Culture
author: Guy Branum
description: If you know Guy Branum through his work as a stand-up comic or his TV and podcast appearances, you already know how funny he can be, and this memoir is no exception. But what makes it remarkable is Branum’s rare ability to balance lacerating insight (into himself, others, art and the world at large) with profound compassion – whether he’s writing about his father’s love for The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance or obliquely sharing his contentious history with his sister through a lesson about (no joke) Canadian history. The result is a book that’s nuanced, eye-opening and deeply moving – in addition to being very, very funny.

title: Sadie
author: Courtney Summers
description: In Sadie, Courtney Summers imitates a Serial-style true crime podcast with such pitch perfection that her publishers actually produced segments of it. The book — which alternates between transcripts of this high-brow podcast about the murder of a teen girl and the disappearance of her big sister, Sadie Hunter, and chapters narrated by Sadie herself — absolutely merits such care. It’s a dark, crushingly beautiful story about the costs of being a young woman on the margins, the way addiction ravages a family and the devastating impact of abuse. Sadie is both a book and a character you won’t soon forget.

title: American Hate: Survivors Speak Out
author: Arjun Singh Sethi
description: “Hate comes in many forms,” Arjun Singh Sethi writes in American Hate, a collection of testimonials from survivors of hate crimes – mostly women and young people. Sethi proposes that to “move past hate,” we need the counsel of marginalized people and of the survivors themselves; community responses to their suffering contain the roots of hope and action. Read American Hate for the faces Sethi puts on our national epidemic of hatred. But read it as well for the antidote: We must understand and own our history. We must speak out, for in community is power and love.

title: Deviation: A Novel
author: Luce D'Eramo, translated by Anne Milano Appel
description: In Deviation, Luce D’Eramo unravels a complicated life through a combination of autobiographical fiction and memoir, from her early life in a bourgeois Italian Fascist family to volunteering in the hell of German labor camps during World War II to wandering stateless and ending up paralyzed from the waist down at the age of 19 after a wall falls on her as she rescues people from bombed-out rubble. D’Eramo’s teenage years stand out in sharp relief even against the great conflagration. Not only a personal story, but also a ruthless quest for self-knowledge, Deviation is a literary tour de force.

title: How To Write An Autobiographical Novel: Essays
author: Alexander Chee
description: How to Write an Autobiographical Novel is not so much a writing primer as a deeply considered memoir; half-European-American and half-Korean-American, Chee writes about coming to terms with his ethnic identity and his painful teens, during which his father died of complications from a car accident. He interrogates a history of childhood sexual abuse, embraces his homosexuality, becomes an activist during the tragic early years of the AIDS epidemic and learns to thrive as a writer and a man. Chee’s language is lyrical and accessible, whimsical and sad, often all at the same time.

title: The Fighters
author: C.J. Chivers
description: C.J. Chivers is no ordinary war correspondent. Unlike most journalists covering combat, he’s also a former Marine Corps infantry officer, and as you read The Fighters, you get the sense these fighters are opening up to him in a way they would only do with one of their own. The book asks big, hard policy questions about Iraq and Afghanistan, wars that Chivers has reported on extensively for The New York Times. But he organizes his narrative around human moments, the stories of six combatants. Through them, Chivers poses one of the biggest, hardest questions of all: How many lives have these wars wrecked?

title: Barracoon: The Story Of The Last "Black Cargo"
author: Zora Neale Hurston
description: Zora Neale Hurston wrote Barracoon in 1931, but the book generated little interest from publishers back then. Known only to scholars until it was published this year, Barracoon is an extraordinary find: It’s an account of the interviews Hurston conducted in 1927 with 86-year-old Cudjo Lewis, then the last known living person who could recount firsthand the experience of having been taken captive in Africa and transported on a slave ship to the United States. Barracoon illuminates aspects of the slave trade that largely went unrecorded and also vividly dramatizes two extraordinary voices in conversation.

title: Death Of A Rainmaker: A Dust Bowl Mystery
author: Laurie Loewenstein
description: When this striking historical mystery opens, a dust storm is bearing down on the small town of Jackson, Okla. Housewives hurry to hang wet sheets over the doors and windows of their farmhouses, moviegoers huddle in the aisles of the Jewel Movie House, and, on the outskirts of town, workers with the CCC – FDR’s Civilian Conservation Corps – scramble for their bunkhouses. When the dust settles, the body of a stranger – a con artist claiming to be a “rainmaker” – is discovered in the alley outside the Jewel. Laurie Loewenstein’s Depression-era mystery is brooding and gritty and graced with authenticity.

title: Lake Success: A Novel
author: Gary Shteyngart
description: You would think The Great American Road Trip Novel might have run out of gas by now, but with Gary Shteyngart behind the wheel, this vintage vehicle still proves capable of taking readers to places far outside the range of other literary tracking systems. The antihero of this tale is a crooked Wall Street tycoon who hops aboard a Greyhound to escape the feds and his own family. Shteyngart’s take on America on the verge of a Trump presidency is satirical and incisive.

title: My Ex-Life: A Novel
author: Stephen McCauley
description: After reading the first few chapters of My Ex-Life, I texted a friend who is an independent college counselor and said, “Read this book!” Two days later, she replied, “I’m reading and I can’t stop laughing: It’s all true!” One of the main characters in McCauley’s sparkling and droll novel is also an independent counselor, which means he reads a lot of college application essays – about 99 percent of which, we’re told, begin by mentioning a grandparent and/or cancer. Part romance, part social comedy, My Ex-Life is wise about the screwball paths that people take to finding their callings and their soulmates.

title: The Death Of Mrs. Westaway
author: Ruth Ware
description: This is the mystery I’ve been raving about to everyone, but particularly to fans of Daphne du Maurier’s masterpiece, Rebecca. The novel opens on a dark and stormy night when our young heroine, Harriet “Hal” Westaway, a tarot card reader in a seedy English resort town, receives news of an unexpected inheritance. The catch is that Hal knows the inheritance can’t possibly belong to her, but she’s so desperate for money that she steels herself impersonate the rightful heir. A gloomy ancestral mansion, never-ending rainstorms, a maniacal killer and plot twists aplenty grace this standout suspense novel.

title: White Fragility: Why It's So Hard For White People To Talk About Racism
author: Robin DiAngelo
description: Robin DiAngelo wants white people to understand why it’s so hard for them to talk about racism – and to understand why it’s so vital to overcome that discomfort. In White Fragility, the sociologist and antiracism educator meticulously maps out how the interplay of core Western ideas such as individualism, meritocracy and objectivity worked to create, propagate and protect white supremacy, and insulate white people from the realities of – and their role in – racism. I came to White Fragility from a place of deep frustration, anger and grief. DiAngelo’s straightforward prose, built on decades of thinking and lecturing about racism, provided powerful inspiration: “[G]rief must lead to sustained and transformative action.” White Fragility is an important launching pad, at once an explication of white people’s complicity in racist systems and an exhortation to do the hard but necessary work of breaking down those systems.

title: Making Up
author: Lucy Parker
description: Acrobat Trix Lane and makeup artist Leo Magasiva have a long history of mutual loathing – and a definite knack for showing up at each other’s lowest moments. In the vibrant theater world of London’s West End, Trix is up for a starring role and Leo has something to prove. They bicker and spar, and every interaction sends sparks flying – leading to (spoiler alert) moments of empathy and decency and yes, hot sexy times. But it’s nothing serious, or so they tell themselves!

title: Rage Becomes Her: The Power Of Women's Anger
author: Soraya Chemaly
description: It shouldn’t be a surprise that women are angry these days: We’re underpaid, overworked, harassed and impossibly judged. The modern woman may identify her feelings as stress or fatigue – but really, they’re rage. And Soraya Chemaly makes the case that rather than something to soothe, anger is a powerful tool to fight injustice; women should feel the burn and use it for good. At turns enraging, comforting and inspiring, Rage Becomes Her is a must read.

title: The Wedding Date
author: Jasmine Guillory
description: After a classic meet-cute – they get stuck in an elevator on the way to a wedding – pediatric surgeon Drew Nichols asks Alexa Monroe to be his fake girlfriend for the big day. Drew and Alexa hit it off and hot sexy times ensue – conflicts, too, like managing long-distance dating, balancing two high-powered careers, navigating an interracial romance and, OMG, trying to figure out what he meant by that text. This novel reads like a truly contemporary romance in that the couple grapples with issues anyone dating today will relate to – but with the promise of happily ever after.

title: When Katie Met Cassidy: A Novel
author: Camille Perri
description: Katie is on her own after her fiancé, John Michael, dumps her, but she is crushing it at work negotiating a big contract – which is where she meets opposing counsel Cassidy. Things might have stayed strictly professional if not for a chance encounter that leads to a night of drinking at the coolest lesbian bar in Manhattan’s Greenwich Village. First, Katie and Cassidy become friends. Then, Katie starts to develop feelings, which Cassidy is totally into helping her explore. Camille Perri’s lively, funny voice makes for a fresh take on the beloved old story of single girl finding love in the big city.

title: The Wrong Heaven
author: Amy Bonnaffons
description: Being a woman in the world can be a surreal experience, and Amy Bonnaffons perfectly bottles that feeling in this collection of short stories about women trying to square their desires with societal restrictions. The Wrong Heaven is a compelling, sometimes bizarre read; I identified deeply with characters doing things such as voluntarily becoming horses – something I didn’t realize could stir such rich emotions.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Ninety-Nine Glimpses Of Princess Margaret
author: Craig Brown
description: She was the Princess who would not be Queen. Margaret was glamorous where her older sister, Elizabeth, was sensible; acid-tongued and downright rude, where Elizabeth was unfailingly, royally polite; scandalous and wild, where Elizabeth could never dream of it. For many years, Margaret seemed to be everywhere, enticing admirers from Pablo Picasso to Peter Sellers. She was easily caricatured, and famously unlucky in love. In this breezy series of vignettes – some real, some hilariously imagined – author and satirist Craig Brown gives us intimate glimpses of her intriguing life.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Personality Brokers: The Strange History Of Myers-Briggs And The Birth Of Personality Testing
author: Merve Emre
description: Are you an INTJ? Or maybe an ESFP? If you know what I’m talking about, you’re among the multitudes who have taken the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator. (For the uninitiated, that’s a series of questions designed to reveal your core personality type.) Author Merve Emre charts the unlikely origin of the personality test and the myriad ways it’s been put to use since (in college admissions, corporate hiring and firing, and matching spies to secret missions.) Emre spins a compelling tale about the mother-daughter team who gave the test its name, and along the way she raises fascinating questions about just what makes us who we are.

title: Housegirl: A Novel
author: Michael Donkor
description: I hate novels. This is a strong statement, I know – but here’s why I make it before telling you about Housegirl: I hate novels because too often, I know exactly where the story is heading, where the characters are heading. I loved Housegirl because Michael Donkor’s storytelling and character building were so exquisite. I wanted to see how the might-as-well-be-orphaned 17-year-old Ghana-born Belinda was going to fare in London, where she was not expected to be a domestic, but to accomplish a job far harder: figuring out sullen, withdrawn Amma, who’s not living up to the expectations of her traditional Ghanian parents. Two days after I finished the book, I found myself actually missing the characters. This is a rare accomplishment for a first-time author, which is why I recommend Housegirl – even though it’s a novel.

title: Tigers & Tea With Toppy
author: Barbara Kerley and Rhoda Knight Kalt, illustrated by Matte Stephens
description: In this delightful, informative adventure, the granddaughter of wildlife artist Charles R. Knight narrates the life story of her “Grandpa Toppy” through their visits to museums and the zoo. During these trips, Toppy explains how, despite poor eyesight, he spent hours examining animals so that he could render them lifelike in drawings – and how he went on to imagine animals that are no longer living, bringing mastodons and dinosaurs back to life through art. The book manages to wrap natural history, family and lovely art into one inspiring package. After reading Tigers & Tea With Toppy, my 6-year-old son grabbed his paper, paint and brush and started creating his own “never-before-seen-extinct animals.”

title: American Prison: A Reporter's Undercover Journey Into The Business Of Punishment
author: Shane Bauer
description: Mother Jones reporter Shane Bauer spent four months working as a corrections officer at a private prison in Louisiana, and his new book tells the shocking story of what he observed. Bauer contends that the prison companies have no interest in trying to rehabilitate the incarcerated, or even treat them like real people. It’s an enraging, necessary look at the private prison system, and a convincing clarion call for prison reform.

title: God Save Texas: A Journey Into The Soul Of The Lone Star State
author: Lawrence Wright
description: You don’t have to be a Texan to enjoy Lawrence Wright’s witty, sharp look at the state he has called home for decades. Wright considers the Lone Star State through its past and present, its politics and culture, and explores what makes Texas so unique and divisive. Wright is one of Texas’ most talented journalists, and his book is essential reading or anyone who wants to understand how one state changed the trajectory of the country, for better and for worse.

title: How Are You Going To Save Yourself
author: JM Holmes
description: JM Holmes’ powerful short story collection follows four young friends in Pawtucket, R.I.: Gio, Rolls, Dub and Rye, who are forced to grapple with issues including race, sex, families and what happens when communications suddenly break down. The book is a stunning accomplishment, a debut that reads like the work of a writer with decades of experience. It’s an unflinching look at themes that not too many authors are eager to tackle, and a book that asks important questions and challenges the reader to answer them honestly, as uncomfortable as those answers might be.

title: My Own Devices: True Stories From The Road On Music, Science, And Senseless Love
author: Dessa
description: Minneapolis/New York rapper Dessa has a reputation for original, incisive and often heartbreaking music, and with her new memoir-in-essays, she proves that her writing talent isn’t confined to songs. Dessa writes beautifully about a wide range of topics, including science, music and the pain that comes with being in love; it’s a surprising and generous memoir by a singular voice.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Music", "Love & Romance", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Science", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Ohio
author: Stephen Markley
description: The sprawling, angry debut novel from Stephen Markley follows four high school acquaintances who return to their Midwestern hometown of New Canaan on the same day for very different reasons. Markley deftly addresses issues such as war, PTSD and sexual assault in this book that races toward a horrifying conclusion – it’s a beautiful novel that explores the aftermath of the Great Recession and the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, and the tenuous bonds that hold people together at their best and at their worst.

title: Sketchtasy
author: Mattilda Bernstein Sycamore
description: The third novel from author and activist Mattilda Bernstein Sycamore follows 21-year-old Alexa and her group of young queer friends as they use drugs, alcohol and sex to escape from their difficult lives in 1990s Boston. Sycamore paints an unsparing and unsentimental portrait of survival in a homophobic era, and her writing is beyond beautiful. Sketchtasy is a powerful firecracker of a novel; it’s not just one of the best books of the year, it’s an instant classic of queer literature.

title: The Largesse Of The Sea Maiden: Stories
author: Denis Johnson
description: Nobody ever wrote like Denis Johnson, the hardcore minimalist author who died last May at 67. His last book might be the best one he ever wrote – it’s an unceasingly bleak but beautiful collection of short stories, focusing on the lost, the hurting and the downtrodden. Johnson always embraced the dark, but reluctantly; he refused to shy away from the brutal, the violent and the desperate. He was the last of his breed, and it was a breed of one. His final collection is stunning, brilliant and as unique as the author himself.

title: The Order Of The Day
author: Éric Vuillard, translated by Mark Polizzotti
description: A novel told as a series of short vignettes, Éric Vuillard’s book examines Hitler’s rise to power in Germany through the people who enabled it through their greed, apathy or cowardice. Vuillard’s writing is spare, angry and powerful, and it suffuses the novel with an effective sense of impending doom. It’s a chilling, brilliant look at the rise of fascism in the 1930s that also works as a warning for today.
tags: ["History", "Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: There Will Be No Miracles Here: A Memoir
author: Casey Gerald
description: There Will Be No Miracles Here*, from MBAs Across America (MBAx) co-founder Casey Gerald, isn’t your typical book by a CEO (inspirational, packed with motivational aphorisms). Rather it’s a stunningly original literary memoir from a young man who’s just as good a writer as he is an entrepreneur. The book details Gerald’s life growing up in an economically depressed Dallas neighborhood to his education at Yale and Harvard, after which he became a sought-after businessman and public speaker. Gerald is a natural storyteller, and the book is beautiful and heartbreaking on every level.

title: Big Game: The NFL In Dangerous Times
author: Mark Leibovich
description: Even if you didn’t love This Town, Mark Leibovich’s takedown of Washington, D.C. (I didn’t), then you might love this book (I did). Football is America’s most-watched sport – but the NFL is remarkably opaque. Leibovich gets behind the curtain, and his way in is through his beloved New England Patriots (I hate them). Somehow, he manages to describe the inner workings of the league in a way that a fan of any team or even a non-fan can appreciate and understand. The picture is both hilarious and depressing. His sendups of league bigwigs make for a hilarious read, and his analysis of the racial and other dynamics help illuminate the stories now on the sports pages.

title: Heartland: A Memoir Of Working Hard And Being Broke In The Richest Country On Earth
author: Sarah Smarsh
description: Whether you’ve loved or hated her newspaper columns filled with diatribes about what she says “liberal elites” get wrong about the white working class, this book is worth a read. Part memoir, part socio-economic analysis, Sarah Smarsh sets her own story and that of her family against the backdrop of the major economic forces that have shaped the Midwest in recent decades. From stories of improvised fun – “sledding” on the flat plains by hitching up to a tractor in the snow – to the devastating impact of sexual abuse and too early pregnancies – Smarsh brings you into her world with equal parts deep compassion and clear eyes. It’s a good read, funny and heartbreaking.

title: On The Other Side Of Freedom: The Case For Hope
author: DeRay Mckesson
description: DeRay Mckesson, one of the best-known leaders of the Ferguson protests and the Black Lives Matter movement, offers a theology of hope that can be understood and embraced even by people who are not religious. He describes his journey from middle-class professional striver to radical disciple of social change. Somehow he pulls this off in language so simple and clear, even a middle-schooler can understand it (as long as you are comfortable with a blunt discussion of his grappling with his sexual orientation). I would recommend this book to anyone who wants to understand why this movement resonates so deeply with so many, especially if you disagree with it.

title: Inner City Pressure: The Story Of Grime
author: Dan Hancox
description: By Y2K, people the world over were dreaming of either collapse or revolution. London, a city fairly recently besieged by Tory politics, pimpled by postindustrial docklands and newly in the grip of Tony Blair, got the latter in the form of grime music – perhaps one of the more elegant symbols of a Britain in flux. Author and grime savant Dan Hancox lovingly considers this chaotic genre (which he unforgettably describes as “a tension headache you can dance to”) as a case study on the way that music can simmer disparate cultures into one neural stew, measure socioeconomic change, or sum the heart of a nation more accurately than any anthem can.

title: Bachelor Nation: Inside The World Of America's Favorite Guilty Pleasure
author: Amy Kaufman
description: I’ll admit it: Like millions of other Americans, I watch The Bachelor. Its mass appeal is both troubling and intriguing, which is why Amy Kaufman’s unauthorized, behind-the-scenes look at the franchise is so satisfying. The book provides an almost neverending collection of crazy factoids – from how producers construct a narrative (including engaging a manipulative style of editing dubbed “Frankenbiting”), to the application process, to the wild contract contestants must sign. Bachelor Nation examines America’s fascination with “falling in love” on TV. Readers, will you accept this rose?

title: The Golden State: A Novel
author: Lydia Kiesling
description: Lydia Kiesling’s gorgeously written, deeply engaging, character-driven debut novel outlines 10 days in the life of Daphne, mother to toddler Honey. Due to a bureaucratic snafu, Daphne’s husband has been denied re-entry to the U.S. from his native Turkey, so she’s on her own, trying to balance work with caring for Honey (whom she both profoundly loves and sometimes resents), and trying not to fall apart completely. To save herself, she abandons her job in San Francisco and heads for the high desert town and the mobile home where her grandparents once lived. It’s been a very long time since my daughters were toddlers, but Daphne’s trenchant observations of her complicated feelings brought it all back. Plus, it was nice to read a California novel set in neither Los Angeles nor San Francisco.

title: The Great Believers
author: Rebecca Makkai
description: I’ve long been a fan of Rebecca Makkai’s work (don’t miss The Hundred-Year House), but nothing prepared me for her moving new novel. Set in two distinct time periods and locales (Chicago in the 1980s and Paris in 2015), the Chicago chapters revolve around a group of gay men in the early days of the AIDS epidemic, while the Paris sections involve a mother searching for her missing daughter. These two seemingly dissimilar plots gradually reflect and refract upon one another, giving additional depth and meaning to each. I’ve read a lot of novels and nonfiction dealing with AIDS, but no book since Randy Shilts’ And The Band Played On has affected me so powerfully as this one.
tags: ["Family Matters", "History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: The Infinite Blacktop: A Novel
author: Sara Gran
description: This is the third in a series of noirishly existential mysteries featuring Claire DeWitt (introduced with panache in Claire DeWitt And The City Of The Dead, but it’s not absolutely necessary to read them in order), a brilliant private eye with a complicated backstory and a conveniently flexible moral compass. Here, we meet her at three points in her life as she attempts to solve three mysteries, one in the present and two from various points in her past. Sara Gran subverts all the tropes of mystery novels to ask readers to ponder the one question that Claire’s always trying to find the answer to: What is the purpose of life? These are mysteries like you’ve never read before.

title: 1947: Where Now Begins
author: Elisabeth Åsbrink, translated by Fiona Graham
description: Elisabeth Åsbrink, a Swedish journalist, takes readers month by month through this epochal year and tells us what in the world was going on. The coverage isn’t limited to the political realm (which saw, among much else, the opening of the Nuremberg trials and HUAC’s arrival in Hollywood) but also includes such events as Nelson Algren and Simone de Beauvoir’s love affair, the fight to have genocide recognized by the U.N. as an international crime, the appearance of flying saucers over the Cascades in Oregon and much more. In Fiona Graham’s smooth translation, parallels between 1947 and 2018 are frighteningly obvious. Even though I have a history degree and already knew at least a little about many of the events Åsbrink details, I found so much that was eye-opening in every month’s account.

title: Boom Town: The Fantastical Saga Of Oklahoma City, Its Chaotic Founding, Its Apocalyptic Weather, Its Purloined Basketball Team, And The Dream Of Becoming A World-Class Metropolis
author: Sam Anderson
description: It’s hard to believe that any biography of any American city could be more consistently interesting, entertaining and informative than this one. Boom Town chronicles Oklahoma City’s unusual beginnings – in the Land Rush of 1899 white settlers ran into the state at noon on April 22 to “claim” areas that had been home to many American Indian tribes. The book goes on to describe the events and characteristics that shaped the city over the next century plus, including the 1995 bombing of the Murrah Federal Building, the tempestuous weather in the epicenter of Tornado Alley and the drama surrounding the Oklahoma City Thunder basketball team. This wildly engaging civics lesson brings the city and its residents alive.

title: Doing Harm: The Truth About How Bad Medicine And Lazy Science Leave Women Dismissed, Misdiagnosed, And Sick
author: Maya Dusenbery
description: Sadly, too many women who read this book will be nodding to themselves the whole way through. If your pain has ever been dismissed by a doctor, if you’ve ever been told that your problems are simply anxiety or stress-related, this book will resonate. Through anecdotes and study results, Maya Dusenbery reveals all the ways that the medical system has ignored or dismissed women’s health issues for decades. Like this statistic: “Young women are seven times more likely to be sent home from the hospital in the middle of having a heart attack.” Why is that? And why is there so little research into diseases such as chronic fatigue syndrome and fibromyalgia? And why are women often excluded from drug studies and clinical research? This book may not make you feel hopeful, but it will make you a better advocate for your own health care.

title: Two Sisters: A Father, His Daughters, And Their Journey Into The Syrian Jihad
author: Åsne Seierstad, translated by Seán Kinsella
description: This is a true story pulled from the headlines: Two Somali sisters living in Oslo, Norway, run away to fight with the Islamic State in Syria, and their dad tries to find them and bring them home. You understand the girls’ perspectives through essays they wrote in school, conversations with their family members and online chats they had with friends. Through exhaustive research, journalist Åsne Seierstad tries to shed light on how two well-adjusted immigrants got wrapped up in the Syrian civil war.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: Who Is Michael Ovitz?
author: Michael Ovitz
description: Talent agent Michael Ovitz sat at the pinnacle of Hollywood power for some two decades, representing many of the biggest names in entertainment. Ovitz instilled fear among executives, agents, colleagues and friends. Then he disappeared from the public eye after a brief tenure at Disney. In Who Is Michael Ovitz?, Ovitz reemerges to dish about the TV and movie businesses during a period of cultural dominance. He offers a fascinating account of how he built Creative Artists Agency into a global juggernaut. The tone is controlled and the storytelling episodic. But there are poignant flashes of a man grappling with the consequences of the ruthless persona that he created.

title: My Year Of Rest And Relaxation
author: Ottessa Moshfegh
description: Sharp wit is softened by heavy narcotics in My Year Of Rest And Relaxation. A pretty-on-the-outside 20-something blonde living in New York City in the months before Sept. 11 wants to hibernate and sleep off her psychic pain. She pops a cornucopia of pills and somehow lightly tiptoes through grief, ennui and shades of addiction and depression. The book is dark but not heavy and has the kind of narrator who stays with the reader long after the book is finished.

title: The Prison Letters Of Nelson Mandela
author: Nelson Mandela, Sahm Venter (editor)
description: The Prison Letters of Nelson Mandela is a compelling, large volume. Dip in and out at your leisure and learn much from it. This often poignant collection of Mandela’s missives gives us a deeper insight into how he struggled with being an absent and incarcerated father and husband. Edited by Sahm Venter, the book – featuring previously mainly unpublished prison letters from 1962-1990 to his family, friends and associates – sheds light on a Mandela determined to parent from behind bars. Prison Letters paints an intimate portrait of the anti-apartheid stalwart lovingly writing long, detailed letters in his distinctive, easily readable script, as well complaints to the authorities demanding rights for political prisoners under harsh apartheid rule in South Africa.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "History", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: If You See Me, Don't Say Hi: Stories
author: Neel Patel
description: Of all the words written in Neel Patel’s 11 surprising, funny short stories, these three, from the dedication page, struck me as particularly brave: “For my parents.” In his debut collection, Patel introduces readers to brown Americans – mostly first-generation Indian Americans – whose stories of love and yearning are defined by the secrets they keep. As an Indian American, when I was growing up, the two people I kept secrets from the most were my mom and dad. If You See Me, Don’t Say Hi is a reminder that messiness makes the human experience beautiful.

title: Playing Changes: Jazz For The New Century
author: Nate Chinen
description: Jazz music: It’s as interesting now as ever! Who knew? If you have any opinion about that assessment, you may have read writing by Nate Chinen, perhaps in The New York Times or through NPR Music and WBGO. Playing Changes is better: an ever-broader contemporary scene portrayed through its prime movers and movements (Brad Mehldau, Jason Moran, Esperanza Spalding, etc.). It’s music criticism with suggested listening but considers emergent forces – moneyed institutions, Big Jazz Education, hip-hop – which have sneakily shaped the sounds. So it’s not only a first stab at a playlist for our times; it’s also a first draft of our times’ history.

title: The Field Of Blood: Violence In Congress And The Road To Civil War
author: Joanne B. Freeman
description: If you look at our nation’s dysfunctional capital and wonder where things might be headed, noted historian Joanne Freeman chronicles the path once taken. Early regional rivalries (see her earlier Affairs of Honor) hardened into a 30-year debate, often physical, over slavery. Southerners were quick to defend it; Northerners slowly came to oppose. In the House of Representatives (Freeman’s focus), bullying and fistfights disrupted legislative business. Congressmen came to work packing pistols or even Bowie knives. A Maine congressman was shot dead in a duel. Freeman doesn’t suggest that history repeats itself, but there are plenty of echoes across The Field of Blood.

title: Bloody Rose
author: Nicholas Eames
description: The idea of mapping the roles of a Dungeons & Dragons-style adventuring party onto the roles in a rock band is so intensely perfect, it’s a marvel that no one came up with it before Nicholas Eames. (It’s less perfect for the bards, though, who keep meeting Spinal Tap drummer-esque fates.) Bloody Rose follows bored barkeep Tam Hashford as she chucks her job and takes off for a life of monster-slaying glory. It’s the second volume in a series, but I have no compunctions about telling you to go read the first book, Kings of the Wyld, too – Eames blends both a love of music and fantasy adventure with surprisingly deep, serious storytelling for a thoroughly satisfying read. And, of course, catching all the music in-jokes is just gravy.

title: Chesapeake Requiem: A Year With The Watermen Of Vanishing Tangier Island
author: Earl Swift
description: Journalist Earl Swift spent more than a year living on Tangier Island, an isolated – and swiftly eroding – scrap of land in the Chesapeake Bay. He went out with the watermen searching for crabs, watched the summer tourists descend and leave again, and dug into the island’s unique history of religious revivalism to produce this deep, nuanced portrait of a place that’s already profoundly affected by stronger storms and rising seas, but where the islanders went solidly for Donald Trump, resist the idea of climate change, and trust in God to keep them safe.

title: How Long 'Til Black Future Month?: Stories
author: N.K. Jemisin
description: Let’s say you’ve read everything N.K. Jemisin ever wrote (ME) and you can’t face the wait for her next series (ALSO ME). Luckily, there’s How Long ‘Til Black Future Month?, which collects her powerful short fiction. Special notice goes to the Hugo Award-nominated story “The City Born Great,” which imagines a young homeless man in Manhattan who becomes the living avatar of his city and does battle against eldritch horrors. And if you, like me, have raged and shaken your fist at the false choice presented in Ursula K. Le Guin’s famed “The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas,” Jemisin’s “The Ones Who Stay And Fight” is a welcome retort.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Girl In The Green Silk Gown
author: Seanan McGuire
description: Seanan McGuire takes hoary old urban legends, midnight neon diners and weepy 1950s dead-teenager ballads and warps them into something magic, dark and strange. The Girl in the Green Silk Gown is Rose Marshall, the Phantom Prom Date, doomed forever to hitchhike along America’s highways, one step ahead of the supernatural villain who ran her off the road. If you’re the kind of person who pats your car on the dashboard and encourages it on long trips – and even if you’re not – this is the book for you. (For added enjoyment, track down and listen to the music McGuire writes and performs about these stories.)

title: Underbug: An Obsessive Tale Of Termites And Technology
author: Lisa Margonelli
description: Despite their homewrecking reputation, most termites are ingenious builders, constructing mounds that are strong, utilitarian and even air-conditioned. They’re just dumb insects, right? So they should have simple rules that guide their palatial building projects. Underbug makes a convincing case that’s not so. Robotics experts who thought they could co-opt termite tricks for their own ends are humbled by these remarkable insects. Lisa Margonelli’s story toggles back and forth between two quirky social worlds: that of the insects, and that of the scientists and technologists who are seduced by them.

title: Fear: Trump In The White House
author: Bob Woodward
description: Many readers will find veteran investigator and author Bob Woodward’s depiction of President Trump and his presidency so devastating that it can only be described as an indictment. Woodward’s Trump is uncouth, uninformed and unprepared for the demands of his office. Moreover, he appears convinced that the same braggadocio that made him rich and made him president will make the world conform to his own view of it. And, while Fear has been a runaway bestseller, it has produced no direct political ramifications and little shift in public opinion – which may be a measure of our times and the uniqueness of Trump’s political appeal. This book focuses on the 2016 campaign and Trump’s first year in office, and it carries a strong scent of a sequel in the works. Many of Woodward’s apparent sources for this work have left Trump’s ambit, but others surely remain.

title: The Word Is Murder: A Novel
author: Anthony Horowitz
description: One of my coworkers refers to books she knows she should read (but doesn’t want to) as “nutritious,” which makes Anthony Horowitz’s detective novels roughly equivalent to a deeply satisfying piece of dark chocolate – candy, but with a hint of real depth. Horowitz was the creator of the cult-favorite British TV show Foyle’s War, and the novels he’s put out since deliver similar gumshoe pleasures, updated with new twists on the formula. In The Word Is Murder, a fictional Anthony Horowitz is ghostwriting a book in real time for a private eye on the case of a woman who seems to have predicted her own murder. What could be cozier?

title: The Immortalists
author: Chloe Benjamin
description: If you knew the exact date of your death, how would you live your life? In 1969, four siblings visit a psychic, and it’s either a catalyst for everything that happens next, or it was destiny all along. The youngest moves to San Francisco to find love.
