He lives an expansive life and dies young at the dawn of the AIDS crisis. The oldest becomes a scientist obsessed with researching longevity. She lives a long time, cautious and alone. This book might not surprise you, but it does raise a lot of questions: Is what happens to the siblings a self-fulfilling prophecy or predetermined? Is knowledge freeing or limiting? If you knew your fate, would you pursue your wildest dreams? Would you want to know?

title: A River Of Stars: A Novel
author: Vanessa Hua
description: A River of Stars is the best of all worlds: part buddy cop adventure, part coming-of-age story and part ode to female friendship. Scarlett Chen is pregnant with Boss Yeung’s baby. He desperately wants U.S. citizenship for his future son, so he sends Scarlett from her home in China to wait out her pregnancy in a secret maternity center in Los Angeles. But Scarlett escapes – along with a teenage stowaway named Daisy. Now they’re on the lam, and they’ll have to figure out how to raise their kids and achieve the American dream on their own, with Boss Yeung hot on their heels. How will it all work out? The (funny, moving) twist at the end is perfection.

title: Census
author: Jesse Ball
description: A widower learns he doesn’t have long to live. But he wants to show some of the world to his son, who has Down syndrome. So he signs up as a census taker (for a dystopian government in an unnamed land), and they head north. As they get closer to the edge of civilization, the father has to learn how to say goodbye to his son and consider the world he will be leaving behind. Jesse Ball wrote Census in honor of his late brother, who had Down syndrome, in order to help people “see what it is like to know and love a Down syndrome boy or girl.” Through his spare yet beautiful writing, Ball does just that.

title: Social Creature: A Novel
author: Tara Isabella Burton
description: Lavinia is exceptionally wealthy. Louise is not. Lavinia has a glittering circle of friends and endless parties to attend. Louise is a failed writer working three jobs – until she meets Lavinia. By New Year’s Eve, Lavinia and Louise have known each other for 10 days. Louise moves into Lavinia’s apartment. They take a lot of selfies, they wear each other’s clothes. (This becomes important later.) It’s all Louise has ever wanted. Until Lavinia becomes obsessed with Louise. Or is it the other way around? Soon, Lavinia will be dead. But Louise can’t let everything fall apart now. Social Creature is Tara Isabella Burton’s unputdownable debut novel. (Seriously, I read this book in one day.)

title: The Merry Spinster: Tales Of Everyday Horror
author: Daniel Mallory Ortberg
description: In The Merry Spinster, the stories might be a mix of classic fairy tales and Old Testament, but they’re not the bedtime stories you remember. They’re darker, bloodier and messier. They also subvert traditional roles – princess, daughter, king and husband are all untied completely from gender. The frog in “The Frog’s Princess” falls in love with a daughter, who is male. It’s liberating. Can you see those tropes more clearly now? And author Daniel Mallory Ortberg has said it wasn’t just an interesting thought experiment; Ortberg was in the process of coming out as transgender while writing these stories.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark"]

title: The Night Diary
author: Veera Hiranandani
description: Nisha is a 12-year-old girl growing up in India. But it’s 1947, the year of Partition. India is being torn in two. And Nisha, who is half Hindu and half Muslim, is forced to flee newly created Pakistan with her family. Told in diary entries to her late mother, The Night Diary is everything you want in a young adult novel. There’s history and religion. An empathetic heroine who is very brave (but also sometimes scared) and loves her family (but still fights with her brother). And lessons, about how it feels to be displaced and what it means to be a refugee, unable to be safe in your own homeland – themes that, though the author started writing this novel years ago, are all the more relevant today. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: All You Can Ever Know: A Memoir
author: Nicole Chung
description: Nicole Chung was born into a Korean immigrant family and adopted by a white couple when she was an infant. All You Can Ever Know is partially about Chung’s search as an adult for her birth family, and who she found. But it’s also a thoughtful look at transracial adoption and a meditation on identity and culture. What did it mean, for example, for Chung to grow up in rural Oregon as the only nonwhite kid in her school, and without the language to recognize racism for what it was? Her memoir is a sometimes heartbreaking, always unflinching look at what it means to feel rootless.

title: A Gift From Darkness
author: Patience Ibrahim and Andrea C. Hoffmann
description: Patience Ibrahim was kidnapped twice by Boko Haram in Nigeria – and this is her story, as told to journalist Andrea C. Hoffmann. The atrocities Ibrahim endured are almost unimaginable, and, as one might expect, her story is hard to read, though told well. But it’s important, not only to understand the horrific reign of Boko Haram, but also as an act of support for women like Ibrahim, who Hoffmann says are never asked about what they have witnessed.

title: The Hollow Of Fear
author: Sherry Thomas
description: The third book in Sherry Thomas’ Charlotte Holmes series, The Hollow Of Fear delivers a thoughtful subversion of gender identity in Victorian England, housed within a cracking good mystery. Do you need to read the first two? Yes. Is that a hardship? Nope. In this installment, Charlotte must go undercover in plain sight to unearth a killer who is trying to frame Lord Ingram, her closest and yet most forbidden friend. Thomas matches her mystery elements with deliberate revelations about each character for a story that is personally engrossing and difficult to put down. Block off time and lay in snacks, because once you start this series, you won’t stop.

title: The Kiss Quotient
author: Helen Hoang
description: This book is every magical element of your favorite romantic comedy (don’t pretend like you don’t have one) enhanced with genuine contemporary complications and exquisite emotional detail. After too many terrible dates, Stella, a brilliant and successful econometrician, hires Michael, an escort, to teach her about sex and relationships. Upon this familiar foundation, Helen Hoang builds a romance which centers autism and neurodivergence, sex work, the intricacies of the Asian-American cultural experience and the diplomacy required to manage family expectations. This is the kind of story that romance readers will wordlessly hand to someone who asks why people love this genre.

title: Into The Raging Sea: Thirty-Three Mariners, One Megastorm, And The Sinking Of El Faro
author: Rachel Slade
description: Rachel Slade’s book sucked me in. There’s no other way to put it. I devoured it in three days, engrossed by her dramatic re-creation of the worst American maritime disaster in 40 years. In addition to a gripping narrative of a cargo ship’s tragic voyage into the eye of a hurricane, Slade explains the fascinating world of commercial shipping and the essential – but often hidden – role it plays in our economy.

title: Leadership: In Turbulent Times
author: Doris Kearns Goodwin
description: Transformational and inspirational political leadership is in short supply these days – especially the kind of leadership that builds broad coalitions across parties and ideological divides. Doris Kearns Goodwin’s book reminds us that it is possible, and that when it happens, it can change American history. She recaps the lives of four transformational presidents and closely analyzes one key moment where each one’s leadership moved the country forward. The book is uplifting and informative – and a relatively quick read compared with other similarly smart history books.

title: The Red And The Blue: The 1990s And The Birth Of Political Tribalism
author: Steve Kornacki
description: Even before the Brett Kavanaugh confirmation hearings, I’ve had a growing suspicion that the United States is not only reliving 1990s politics, but unexpectedly reaping political seeds sown two decades ago. Steve Kornacki confirms this in his entertaining history of the rise of Bill Clinton, Newt Gingrich and tribal partisan politics as we know it today. The book combines blow-by-blow recaps of political battles such as the 1992 presidential election with smart analysis about how confrontational and oppositional politics became the norm.

title: Inseparable: The Original Siamese Twins And Their Rendezvous With American History
author: Yunte Huang
description: Chang and Eng were brought to America in 1829 at the age of 17 and were forced to perform in a freak show. They eventually negotiated their independence, bought land, married sisters, fathered 21 children and became slave owners. Take a deep breath because, on top of that, author Yunte Huang uses this bonkers narrative to broach issues related to otherness, capitalism and hypocrisy. The result is a sensitive and affecting – if not slightly overwhelming – portrait.

title: City Of Ash And Red: A Novel
author: Hye-young Pyun, translated by Sora Kim-Russell
description: A man working for a pest control company is transferred to a dystopian nation ravaged by a mysterious virus, beginning a descent into a waking nightmare. Grim, disturbing and telegraphing a constant sense of anxiety, City of Ash and Red straddles the categories of thriller and speculative fiction novel, and succeeds in keeping us interested in a protagonist who becomes increasingly more loathsome as he scrabbles for his survival. Murder, rats, disease, rape and literal trash fires festoon this book. Not a pleasant read, but definitely a novel that deserves attention.

title: Unholy Land
author: Lavie Tidhar
description: The always daring Lavie Tidhar wades into the waters of alternate history once more. Unholy Land takes off from a real, early 20th-century British plan to settle Jews in East Africa. It was unsuccessful in our world, but there are many other parallel worlds where things turned out differently – and the walls between those realities are collapsing. The key to this erosion of worlds might lie with a pulp fiction writer turned amateur detective who is simply trying to find his missing niece. It’s the kind of smart, intertextual science fiction that would make Philip K. Dick giddy.

title: A Carnival Of Losses: Notes Nearing Ninety
author: Donald Hall
description: Big, bearded, blowsy Donald Hall died in June, at age 89. In New Hampshire, the former U.S. poet laureate spent decades writing of the earth, his farm, his beloved poet wife Jane Kenyon and getting old. This book collects short, personal observations and essays on the theme of aging. Simple and sincere, they’re also, often, witty. For instance: “When I was young, my language wore coats and shirts and trousers, neckties, bespoke shoes. In my lifetime as a writer I have cast off layer after layer of clothing in pursuit of nudity.” Right there, in a single sentence, he reflects some of his themes. Hall is funny, sad, rueful, melancholic and, most important, a practiced observer and writer of truth. It’s a wonderful book to pick up and put down at random. I suggest you do it!
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Biography & Memoir"]

title: Chasing Hillary: Ten Years, Two Presidential Campaigns, And One Intact Glass Ceiling
author: Amy Chozick
description: New York Times reporter Amy Chozick’s memoir is less an insider’s account of presidential campaign drama and more a dramedy about covering what turned out to be a pretty joyless campaign led by a candidate who had no use for the press corps following her around. (Full disclosure: I was part of that press corps in 2016 and make a couple small cameos in the book.) With humor, humility and charm, Chozick processes the decade of her life she spent covering the woman who hoped to become the first female president. I was fully engrossed reading Chozick’s accurate and engaging account – even though I knew what was coming next.

title: Famous Father Girl: A Memoir Of Growing Up Bernstein
author: Jamie Bernstein
description: Jamie Bernstein’s courageous and frank account of family life under the massive shadow of her father, Leonard Bernstein, is a bulk load of dirty laundry, lovingly unfolded. Yet amid the scandals – from Black Panther fundraisers to Lenny’s emerging homosexuality – we discover a surprisingly standard family, struggling with careers, fears, affairs, unfulfilled dreams and a mother’s ghastly battle lost to cancer. Through Jamie’s eyes and her fluid writing style, the memoir offers an insider’s view of the legendary conductor-composer and his first-born daughter who labored, sometimes bitterly, but finally found herself.

title: The Line Becomes A River: Dispatches From The Border
author: Francisco Cantú
description: At a time of overheated rhetoric about the threat along the border, Francisco Cantú relates what it means to police it. He is an empathetic witness to the desperation felt by those driven to cross it. But as a Border Patrol agent, he also knows the fear of those charged with guarding it. A sad tale, beautifully written.

title: Text Me When You Get Home: The Evolution And Triumph Of Modern Female Friendship
author: Kayleen Schaefer
description: “Text me when you get home.” Those six words aren’t just about getting home safely at the end of the night – they’re really a story about how special and fierce friendships are between women. Through interviews with experts and friends, Kayleen Schaefer dissects how our friendships provide support, and how that support changes throughout our lives. She debunks the idea that girls can’t be friends with other girls, but she’s frank about the fact that not every girlfriend you have will be there for a lifetime. This book – while not without its flaws – celebrates the love we have for each other and the friends who make sure we get home safely.

title: Becoming
author: Michelle Obama
description: Michelle Obama’s book is, first and foremost, a story about how she became who she is – a highly accomplished lawyer-turned-public-servant-turned-hospital administrator, a fiercely protective mother, a devoted wife – and then the story about the struggle to hold onto that identity, to maintain a semi-sane life, even after she agrees to let that life be hijacked by politics. Obama often gets didactic, even social science-y, but her excellent writing shines through in sections about her childhood and college years. Obama describes the profound frustration of being misunderstood – of being pegged as an “angry black woman” – and the discomfort of being a hyperaccomplished woman only recognized through her connection to a powerful man. Becoming is about the power in telling one’s own story, on one’s own terms.

title: Milkman: A Novel
author: Anna Burns
description: Anna Burns just won the Man Booker Prize for this novel, set in 1970s Northern Ireland, and it’s easy to see why. Take the first sentence: “The day Somebody McSomebody put a gun to my breast and called me a cat and threatened to shoot me was the same day the milkman died.” The book is interested in bigger questions than just: What happened to the milkman? Burns explores what happens when the divide between us and them becomes too wide to cross. She asks whether we believe women, and why. And she digs into these heavy, timely themes with humor and light. The young woman who narrates the story buries her head in books as she walks home each night. With a book like this in hand, I’d do the same.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Winners Take All: The Elite Charade of Changing the World
author: Anand Giridharadas
description: The past years have seen some outstanding books on how philanthropists and their dollars have shaped public policy. On the heels of last year’s The Givers by David Callahan, we now have Anand Giridharadas’ Winners Take All. The author zeros in on what he sees as a glaring hypocrisy among affluent elites: that while many well-meaning (and well-off) Americans claim to want to improve society’s inequalities, they don’t challenge the structures that preserve that inequality, not wanting to jeopardize their own privileged positions. Giridharadas even numbers today’s elites “among the more predatory in history.”

title: Soulless: The Case Against R. Kelly
author: Jim DeRogatis
description: Veteran music journalist and critic Jim DeRogatis has been making a public case against the R&B singer R. Kelly for two decades: uncovering, investigating and chronicling the many accusations of sexual assault and abuse against the R&B superstar. (Kelly has denied all the allegations.) Soulless is a harrowing and deeply absorbing account of DeRogatis’ slog toward justice – and a damnation of all the forces that have seemed to cosset Kelly for all these years. But DeRogatis doesn’t put himself in the center of the action. Instead, the book’s heart is the stories of the women and the girls.

title: God Save The Queens: The Essential History Of Women In Hip-Hop
author: Kathy Iandoli
description: God Save the Queens lives up to its subtitle: It is an “essential history.” Men have long dominated hip-hop’s origin stories, and music journalist Kathy Iandoli answers that imbalance with a vital history of the genre’s founding women. Hip-hop fans will be delighted to hear from Missy Elliott, Queen Latifah, Nicki Minaj, Monie Mon and more but may find themselves asking: “Why did we have to wait so long for this?”

title: Spotify Teardown: Inside The Black Box Of Streaming Music
author: Maria Eriksson, Rasmus Fleischer, Anna Johansson, Pelle Snickars, Patrick Vonderau
description: Five researchers set out to analyze the massive streaming platform, and they broke a few rules along the way. Trying to holistically understand how Spotify’s ecosystem works is difficult, given the proprietary nature of its technology and agreements with the music industry. So Maria Eriksson et al. set up their own projects within the platform, using them to get a look at the leviathan from the inside.

title: Year Of The Monkey
author: Patti Smith
description: Patti Smith calmly, sadly traces the foggy outline of a liminal and pernicious year, 2016, which delivered changes both personal – the declining health of playwright and actor Sam Shepard, the death of music producer Sandy Pearlman – and global. In this slim volume, Smith glides through her dreamlike world as a famous spirit might, performing, writing, reading, drinking coffee and a very occasional sip of alcohol. “I tried to shrug off unease, do my work, say my prayers, bide my time,” she writes near the dead-middle of the book, in which she does exactly that in her way, exactly.

title: How We Fight For Our Lives: A Memoir
author: Saeed Jones
description: In powerful poetry and prose, Saeed Jones recounts his experiences growing up as a young, black, gay man in rural Texas. Jones told NPR’s Sam Sanders he wanted “to allow readers to be in my body” through these formative years: He describes grappling with his attraction to other boys at a young age and surviving a violent sexual encounter. The book is also an ode of sorts to Jones’ mother, who died following a heart attack in 2011. Jones writes about fighting for his life – and in doing so, offers lessons in how we can all fight for our own. “Our culture is dominated by and obsessed with straight, rich, cisgender people,” Jones says. “I think those people will never fully understand how much work literally everyone else does to imagine ourselves into existence.”

title: No Hard Feelings: The Secret Power Of Embracing Emotions At Work
author: Liz Fosslien and Mollie West Duffy
description: People aren’t robots! Liz Fosslien and Mollie West Duffy push back on the myth that emotions and the workplace don’t mix. Full of fun illustrations and anecdotes, No Hard Feelings covers emotional decision-making, why “feelings aren’t facts,” and fostering positive culture on your team. I came away feeling more capable at communicating and managing up – and I even brought one of the book’s worksheets (yes, there are worksheets!) to a recent team retreat. The focus is what’s best for you – whether that means improving culture at a company you’ve been at for years, or recognizing when it’s time to leave. Women and nonbinary folks may find this book particularly helpful, but I think it’s a must-read for anyone who has ever felt feelings (aka been human) at a job.

title: Wayward Lives, Beautiful Experiments: Intimate Histories Of Social Upheaval
author: Saidiya Hartman
description: Social history poses a crucial question: Who is forgotten? Columbia University’s Saidiya Hartman has created a method – she calls it “critical fabulation” – that melds archival research with critical inquiry and imaginative writing to bring silenced people’s voices and images to life. Her latest work considers how, at the turn of the 20th century, African American women on the margins of society negotiated a freedom that white supremacy always kept fragile. Hartman uncovers court citations like one branding a teenage Eleanora Fagan – later called Billie Holiday – a juvenile delinquent; letters from soldiers to common-law wives left behind; and snapshots like one showing two young women in slacks outside a boarding house, embracing in a gleeful gesture of romantic love. Gorgeously written and intellectually rigorous, Hartman’s work shows us how these women built a free America inside the one that would keep them in bondage. In doing so, she challenges us to think differently about the official stories that have kept them from our view.

title: Blood: A Memoir
author: Allison Moorer
description: “One shot. 1-2-3-4. Next shot.” Allison Moorer uses this stark phrase to evoke the rupture at the center of her family’s story: When she was 14 and her sister Shelby was 17, her father killed her mother and then turned the gun on himself. This disaster was never a secret. Fans of the prolific singer-songwriter and her sister, who records as Shelby Lynne, have long known its outline. In this wrenching, careful excavation of all that has flowed through and around that morning, Moorer transforms it into a mystery – a memory she can never quite see clearly and a paradox with which she must reconcile, because she loved her Daddy and sometimes pitied her Mama and still lives, intimately, with their ghosts.

title: It Would Be Night In Caracas
author: Karina Sainz Borgo, translated by Elizabeth Bryer
description: Karina Sainz Borgo has spent her life writing about Venezuela as a journalist, but this is her first time writing about it as a novelist. The narrative jumps back and forth from Venezuela’s bleak and violent present – when cash is worthless, shelves are empty and gangs roam the streets – to the recent prosperous past, with scenes of abundance and joy. Sainz Borgo describes it as a love letter to her country, but it also reads like an obituary telling the tragic story of a place that has become unrecognizable to a woman who once called it home. “I have this strong feeling of loneliness…” she told me. “The country I want to go back to does not exist anymore.”
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Quichotte: A Novel
author: Salman Rushdie
description: Hosting All Things Considered leaves me almost no time to read for pleasure. I usually manage to squeeze in about one book a year that isn’t for an author interview. So my standards are high. Quichotte was my pleasure reading in 2019, and it cleared the bar with ease. Salman Rushdie’s retelling of the Don Quixote story is a sprawling, messy, multilayered, overflowing heap of a thing. It also shows the skills of a master at the peak of his abilities.

title: The Book Of Dust: The Secret Commonwealth
author: Philip Pullman
description: While Lyra Belacqua fans have been buzzing over Dafne Keen’s performance in HBO’s His Dark Materials, I’ve been dusting off Philip Pullman’s Book of Dust to get ready for this gloriously engrossing sequel. Lyra’s in her 20s now, and when a diabolical character suggests the best way to overcome institutions and ingrained habits of thought is to “delicately and subtly undermine the idea that truth and facts are possible,” I knew Pullman was writing about today. A vivid page turner and a great setup for Volume 3.

title: Jerome Robbins, By Himself: Selections From His Letters, Journals, Drawings, Photographs, And An Unfinished Memoir
author: Jerome Robbins, edited by Amanda Vaill
description: Jerome Robbins was a singular figure in 20th century American artistic history – he was phenomenally successful as both a director and choreographer for Broadway shows and, concurrently, as a choreographer for ballet. Amanda Vaill, who authored the Robbins biography, Somewhere, has edited this substantial volume of the artist’s letters, journal entries, photos, drawings, fictional work and excerpts from an unpublished memoir to give a deeper insight into the man, his creativity, his neuroses and his lifelong loneliness. Of particular interest are letters to his collaborators on West Side Story, to get them to trim and streamline the show, and Fiddler on the Roof, asking for better songs. There are long, revealing letters to friends and lovers (both men and women). And excerpts from Robbins’ unproduced Poppa Piece explore his Jewish identity, his guilt over naming names when he was called before HUAC, and his difficult relationship with his father. This is an essential volume, for lovers of both Broadway and ballet.

title: Maybe You Should Talk To Someone: A Therapist, HER Therapist, And Our Lives Revealed
author: Lori Gottlieb
description: How is psychotherapy like pornography? “Both involve a kind of nudity. Both have the potential to thrill,” writes psychotherapist Lori Gottlieb. Gottlieb speaks as both patient and practitioner in this book. A few years ago, she found herself in need of a good therapist after the man she thought she would marry broke up with her unexpectedly. In this wry and insightful memoir, she lays bare her experiences on both sides of the couch, offering a candid view of the hard work — and vast rewards — of therapy.

title: The Bride Test
author: Helen Hoang
description: While working at a hotel in Ho Chi Minh City, Esme gets an offer that’s tough to pass up: The mother of a handsome Silicon Valley accountant wants her to come to California and marry her son. The only catch? Esme has just a few months to make Khai fall in love with her – and expressing emotions (never mind being in a relationship) has never come easily for him. Helen Hoang, author of The Kiss Quotient, has written another complex, swoon-worthy love story.

title: What You Have Heard Is True: A Memoir Of Witness And Resistance
author: Carolyn Forché
description: When poet Carolyn Forché was in her late 20s, a friend’s cousin showed up at her door. The charismatic stranger had driven from El Salvador to persuade her to visit and, perhaps, write about his country, which, in the late 1970s, was on the verge of a violent civil war. Forché decides to go and witnesses the poverty of the country’s citizens and the immorality of the military government. This is an adventure story – together they dodge death squads and find refuge in remote villages – but it’s also about radical empathy and the awakening of social conscience.
tags: ["History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Biography & Memoir"]

title: The Topeka School: A Novel
author: Ben Lerner
description: This is one of those books that feel impossible to describe. In one sense, it’s pretty mundane: a family in Kansas, the teen on the cusp of college and his psychologist parents. In another sense, it’s about ideas too big to wrap your arms around: love, speech and the countless ways the mind makes and undermines a sense of self. But the truth rests somewhere in between – and, maybe, between is the best description of all. Living on the uneasy boundary between fiction and memoir, humor and dread, affection and hatred, The Topeka School will also claim some valuable real estate between your ears.

title: Black Leopard, Red Wolf
author: Marlon James
description: Full disclosure: This is not an easy read. Marlon James’ fantasy is full to brimming with mutilations and murders, witches and shape-shifters. It’s a paradox: at once distressingly claustrophobic in tone and thrillingly expansive in vision; as deft in its language as it is unsparing in what it presents; blunt but deceptive. I put this book down for others several times before, finally, it grabbed me in its jaws and refused to let me go. Now I can’t get it out of my head.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Song For The Unraveling Of The World
author: Brian Evenson
description: Here be monsters. At least, they look like monsters from this angle. But you had to be just seeing things … right? Brian Evenson’s Song for the Unraveling of the World is a collection of 22 taut, troubling short stories in which the danger seems to always lurk just out of view or beyond definition. Here are indistinct terrors that grow more real (and more amused) the more you try to discount them and a horde of questions that, if we’re being honest, you would probably rather not know the answers to. It’s an unsettling experience all around – and a worthy introduction to a prolific writer who deserves many more readers.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Horizon
author: Barry Lopez
description: Barry Lopez writes about traveling to the Arctic, the Galapagos and other remote corners of the globe in Horizon, but “travel writer” isn’t really the right way to categorize him. His books don’t inspire you to book a flight so much as they inspire you to see your own familiar environment in a new light. Throughout Horizon, he offers dense, transcendent lessons about humanity’s relationship to nature, reminding us how much we take for granted and how little time we have left to correct our course with the natural world.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Sea Level Rise: A Slow Tsunami On America's Shores
author: Orrin H. Pilkey and Keith C. Pilkey
description: Duke Geology Professor Emeritus Orrin Pilkey has long written about how to live with changing shorelines, but his latest collaboration with his son Keith Pilkey sounds the alarm on the coastal crisis looming for the country. They identify the legal, political and financial decisions required to cope with sea level rise as it threatens nearly every aspect of American life, including commerce and shipping, the military, tourism and the design and functioning of major cities. The sober assessment questions whether the recent trend toward building resilient coastal communities is even possible.

title: The Broken Road: George Wallace And A Daughter’s Journey To Reconciliation
author: Peggy Wallace Kennedy
description: This is a fascinating and unflinching memoir from the daughter of former Alabama governors George and Lurleen Wallace. The book explores Peggy Wallace Kennedy’s journey from a little girl defending her father as a “segregationist” and not a “racist” to an adult grappling with his civil rights legacy and seeking racial justice. She dubs her father “the Confederacy’s very own Napoleon.” We get a stark look at a difficult home life and a glaring assessment of George Wallace’s blinding ambition, launched with his infamous declaration of “Segregation now, segregation tomorrow and segregation forever.”

title: A Good American Family: The Red Scare And My Father
author: David Maraniss
description: Books about the so-called Red Scare of the 1950s often focus on the big names involved in that tragic drama – Sen. Joseph McCarthy and lawyer Roy Cohn, journalist Edward R. Murrow, or those caught up in the Hollywood blacklist. But Pulitzer Prize-winner David Maraniss instead examines how the anti-communist purge touched middle America. The author’s father, Elliott Maraniss, was a family man, a former World War II Army officer and a newspaperman whose life was upended when an FBI informant gave his name to the U.S. House Un-American Activities Committee. The elder Maraniss had indeed been involved in radical politics starting as a college student in the late 1930s, and that activity led to him being called to testify at a congressional hearing held in Detroit in 1952. By examining such a personal story, Maraniss brings a dark period in American history to life.

title: Madame Fourcade's Secret War: The Daring Young Woman Who Led France's Largest Spy Network Against Hitler
author: Lynne Olson
description: Outside the World War II spy world, few knew that Marie-Madeleine Fourcade was the brains and ingenuity behind the French Resistance, which was so crucial to the Allied victory. She built and rebuilt her network using the names of animals as aliases for herself and her agents. The Hedgehog, as she was known, endured tremendous family sacrifice. But she earned the trust of British and American intelligence, ultimately providing them with a detailed 55-foot-long map of the Normandy coastline where the D-Day invasion would take place.

title: Midnight In Chernobyl: The Untold Story Of The World's Greatest Nuclear Disaster
author: Adam Higginbotham
description: Don’t let the depressing nature of the world’s worst nuclear disaster put you off from reading this outstanding piece of reporting. The book tells about much more than the official denials by recounting the stories of the engineers, scientists and citizens whose lives were changed forever. The accident becomes the beginning of the downfall of the Soviet Union – and more than just one lesson for the history books. Unprecedented access to documents makes the details of the storytelling gripping and unforgettable.

title: The Mastermind: Drugs. Empire. Murder. Betrayal.
author: Evan Ratliff
description: This is the story of the drug lord you likely have never heard of but who, with his online drug websites, probably has had a lot greater impact on your life than those from Latin America making bigger headlines. The hunt to finally arrest Paul Le Roux reads like a mercenary thriller at times, but it also shines a light on hardworking U.S. government agents who don’t give up, despite Le Roux’s slippery nature as he hopscotches around the globe. It is a fascinating window into modern organized crime and the stories of people who paid the price to allow him to get away with it.

title: Last Boat Out Of Shanghai: The Epic Story Of The Chinese Who Fled Mao's Revolution
author: Helen Zia
description: This has been a decade of refugees, so it is a great time to reflect on an earlier wave – those fleeing the rise of Mao Zedong’s communist revolution in China. Helen Zia brings us into the lives of four exiles who reflect the diversity of the socioeconomic experiences of the Shanghai diaspora. Each is a story of endurance, persistence and perseverance topped with significant doses of luck. Theirs are stories of connections, community and family. Perhaps most amazing is that China does not acknowledge the Shanghai exodus, much less the enormous brain drain it triggered. Each of these stories is captivating, but together they are a powerful narrative and a reminder of the hardships of uprooting and starting again, often repeatedly, before finding a new home.

title: The Privileged Poor: How Elite Colleges Are Failing Disadvantaged Students
author: Anthony Abraham Jack
description: Navigating college is hard for many young people, and for low-income students or kids whose parents didn’t go to college, it can be even trickier. Anthony Abraham Jack talked with low-income students at an unnamed elite college and found that making college more inclusive requires creative and thoughtful solutions, such as keeping dining halls and dorms open during holiday breaks – because not every student can afford a ski trip, or even a bus ticket home. So many professors have told me this book made them rethink their own classrooms.

title: The National Team: The Inside Story Of The Women Who Changed Soccer
author: Caitlin Murray
description: For anyone who found themselves jumping up and down and screaming “Equal Pay!” along with the Women’s World Cup stadium crowd last summer (sorry, downstairs neighbors), this book is for you. Caitlin Murray puts you right there on the pitch with the game-changing athletes of the U.S. Women’s National Soccer Team as they fight for recognition and respect from the sport’s governing bodies and from the public. Interviews with several generations of soccer superstars – Mia Hamm, Julie Foudy, Brandi Chastain, Alex Morgan and more – reveal the constant behind-the-scenes work these athletes put in (a late-night sewing session to hem hand-me-down men’s uniforms will both infuriate and inspire) and give soccer fans a better appreciation for each of the four World Cup victories.

title: The Outlaw Ocean: Journeys Across The Last Untamed Frontier
author: Ian Urbina
description: I have been completely obsessed with the story of Sealand after there was speculation that the tiny ocean-platform-turned-principality could become home to Julian Assange’s WikiLeaks servers. (You can’t make this stuff up – Google it yourself!) Or better yet, grab a copy of Ian Urbina’s riveting book to read about some of the eccentric characters, pirate enterprises and human rights violations that thrive in the murky legal brine of international waters. Urbana takes readers onboard a floating abortion clinic, scofflaw fishing trawlers, a vigilante environmentalist ship and more in his extensive reporting that puts you right in the middle of action you probably never realized was happening.

title: We’re Not From Here
author: Geoff Rodkey
description: Funny story: Remember when the humans destroyed Earth and found a replacement planet containing four alien species who were willing to take us in? Well, after awakening from bio-suspension after 20 years, the remaining humans have found the aliens no longer want anything do with us. The solution? Lan’s family is going to live on Planet Choom for a while to show everyone that humans are A-OK. Geoff Rodkey manages to balance humor and good, old-fashioned talking space marshmallows with serious issues of immigration and fake news. This isn’t a space opera; this is a space mockumentary. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: I Like To Watch: Arguing My Way Through The TV Revolution
author: Emily Nussbaum
description: Emily Nussbaum doesn’t just “like to watch” TV, she’s been on something of a campaign to give the boob-tube, as many parents once called it, the cred she believes it deserves: “a critical stance less hobbled by shame – a language that treated television as its own viable force, not the weak sibling to superior mediums,” she writes. This collection of new and previously published essays includes profiles of showrunners Kenya Barris and Jenji Kohan and deep dives into such shows as Buffy the Vampire Slayer, The Americans, Black-ish and Hannah Gadsby’s Nanette. In the new essay “Confessions of the Human Shield,” she grapples with a conundrum of the #MeToo movement: Is it possible to separate the art from the artist?

title: The Ride Of A Lifetime: Lessons Learned From 15 Years As CEO Of The Walt Disney Company
author: Robert Iger
description: In difficult moments, Robert Iger turns to a Teddy Roosevelt speech for inspiration: “It is not the critic who counts … The credit belongs to the man who is actually in the arena.” From dealing with angry board members to acquiring 21st Century Fox to firing Roseanne Barr, Iger’s Ride is equal parts management guide and personal journey, told with brisk pacing, wit and sensitivity by the man largely credited with turning the once-embattled Mouse House into a respected powerhouse. Particularly moving is Iger’s retelling of the grand opening of the multi-billion-dollar Shanghai Disney while responding to tragedies at home – the Pulse nightclub shooting in Orlando and the death of a little boy at a Disney resort. “It was a happy day. It was also the saddest of my career.”

title: In Pain: A Bioethicist's Personal Struggle With Opioids
author: Travis Rieder
description: This book is a wake-up call for anyone who thinks the opioid crisis can’t touch them. After a motorcycle accident that nearly took off his foot, Johns Hopkins bioethicist Travis Rieder was prescribed large doses of painkillers. But when he tried to wean himself off, he experienced severe withdrawal and found that his doctors didn’t have answers for him. Though Rieder eventually managed to stop taking the meds, the experience prompted him to write In Pain. It’s part memoir, part indictment of the doctors and pharmaceutical companies that push painkillers without addressing the addiction issues that come with them.

title: The Starless Sea: A Novel
author: Erin Morgenstern
description: Anyone who’s read Erin Morgenstern’s wildly successful fiction debut, The Night Circus, knows how meticulously she crafts her imaginary worlds. Her new novel, The Starless Sea, revels in them. Graduate student Zachary Ezra Rawlins picks a book off the shelf in his college library and finds a description of himself and an incident from his childhood that he never told anyone. As he tries to solve the mystery of the book, both he and the reader are immersed in a multitude of stories, the threads of which gradually weave together to a haunting conclusion.

title: Let's Go (So We Can Get Back): A Memoir Of Recording And Discording With Wilco, Etc.
author: Jeff Tweedy
description: This memoir feels like a gift from the often enigmatic Wilco frontman to longtime fans. Jeff Tweedy opens up about his opioid addiction and recovery, his father’s drinking issues, and the mood disorders that affected them both. He tells us how a childhood bike accident made him a guitar player, and he gives his take on the breakup of Uncle Tupelo and Jay Bennett’s departure from Wilco. It’s all written with a wry humor that will be familiar to anyone who has ever gone to a Wilco show.

title: The Collected Schizophrenias: Essays
author: Esmé Weijun Wang
description: Esmé Weijun Wang artfully weaves clinical and cultural analysis into her 13 essays on mental health and schizophrenia. The collection includes excerpts from the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders and reactions to movies and art. Wang’s writing is deeply personal as she chronicles her family history of trauma and mental illness, her own diagnosis journey and her considerations about whether to have children. Throughout she asks: What is real, and how do we define that for ourselves?

title: Audience Of One: Donald Trump, Television, And The Fracturing of America
author: James Poniewozik
description: Only a critic with James Poniewozik’s skill can show how the evolution of modern TV mirrored the rise of the celebrity who would become the U.S. commander in chief. Poniewozik, the chief TV critic for The New York Times, documents how Donald Trump used media coverage and appearances in TV and film to become the embodiment of many Americans’ idea of a businessman, just as TV’s penchant for antiheroes and thin stereotypes of reality TV primed an audience. Toss in the anger, racist dog-whistles and sexism of Fox News Channel, and you have a recipe for Trump’s message, artfully explained by one of the best critics in the business.

title: Unexampled Courage: The Blinding Of Sgt. Isaac Woodard And The Awakening Of President Harry S. Truman And Judge J. Waties Waring
author: Richard Gergel
description: This is a book so packed with overlooked history that you will forgive that it has one of the most unwieldy titles of the year. Richard Gergel — a judge who presided over the trial of white supremacist Charleston, S.C., church shooter Dylann Roof — introduces readers to a man they should already know but likely don’t. Isaac Woodard was an African American sergeant blinded by a beating from a police chief in South Carolina while returning home from service in World War II. Gergel writes that this case inspired President Harry Truman to establish a civil rights commission and desegregate the armed forces, which then helped in arguments for the Supreme Court case that desegregated U.S. schools. The acquittal of the police chief who blinded Woodard by an all-white jury also inspired the judge on the case, J. Waties Waring, to become more sensitive to racism and issue many civil rights decisions. Gergel recounts all this with compelling prose, revealing how a single injustice led to some of the nation’s most important civil rights victories.

title: Parkland: Birth Of A Movement
author: Dave Cullen
description: By now, I have a sense of how the coverage around mass shootings tends to unfold; I’ve been at NPR less than four years and have covered nearly a dozen of them. Parkland was different – and Dave Cullen’s book shows why. The author of the definitive book on Columbine flew down and embedded with the kids behind March For Our Lives within a week of the shooting at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School and stayed with them for nearly a year. The book is a strikingly beautiful look at the origins, evolution and people of a movement that has touched the nation.

title: The Uninhabitable Earth: Life After Warming
author: David Wallace-Wells
description: Like the parable of the blind men and the elephant, my understanding of the future of our planet and the things that live on it used to be cobbled together from 100 well-intentioned half-truths and a handful of lies from people who have an interest in denying that the elephant is even in the room. Reading David Wallace-Wells’ book was, simply put, like going to meet the elephant myself. There is no better, more readable look at the consequences of the climate change that will unfold in the span of the next human lifetime. You owe it to yourself to actually understand what’s happening and what can be done to try to mitigate the outcome.

title: Normal People: A Novel
author: Sally Rooney
description: The beauty of novels is that they can briefly reveal to us a path inward, to show us our hurts, loves and fears. By that measure, Sally Rooney has written a remarkable one. Her book Normal People is the story of a young couple in Ireland who want only and desperately to understand each other, even though, like all of us, they’re unable to understand even themselves.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: The Parade: A Novel
author: Dave Eggers
description: Modern life is horrible and also opaque. By which I mean, the nature of the global economy means that I don’t know where my clothes or food or tech come from or the conditions under which they’re made. But I do know that I’d probably find the answers horrifying – the same emotion I felt after finishing Dave Eggers’ novella The Parade, a parable about global development, megacorporations and human connection. Read it and rest assured there are few unambiguously ethical choices under capitalism.

title: The Mosquito: A Human History Of Our Deadliest Predator
author: Timothy C. Winegard
description: At once a deep dive and a view from 30,000 feet. Timothy C. Winegard traces the history of civilization (Western, largely) through the lens of that most hated scourge of cocktail hour on your back deck. In friendly, discursive prose, he walks you through the many ways that the mosquito – more specifically, the diseases she carries – has turned the tide in major wars and has overthrown governments. When you learn, for example, that these miserable little bloodsuckers are responsible for the deaths of half the humans who have ever lived on the planet, it will lend a distinct tang of righteous retribution to the next time you manage to splat one before it’s done feasting on your ankle.

title: Permanent Record
author: Edward Snowden
description: Six years after he spilled valuable secrets from the National Security Agency, Edward Snowden remains a deeply polarizing figure. In his memoir, Permanent Record, Snowden says he went public because the NSA went “from the targeted surveillance of individuals to the mass surveillance of entire populations.” His revelations started a national conversation and have led to reforms. However, critics in the national security community still regard him as a traitor. He still faces criminal charges in the U.S. and remains in limbo in Moscow. From his apartment there, he says, “I beam myself onto stages around the world, speaking about the protection of civil liberties in the digital age.”

title: The Other End Of The Line
author: Andrea Camilleri, translated by Stephen Sartarelli
description: I read The Other End of the Line, Andrea Camilleri’s 24th Inspector Montalbano mystery novel, shortly before the prolific and beloved Sicilian author died in July at age 93. I’ve long been a fan of Camilleri’s crime series, but this novel – set against the backdrop of Europe’s migration crisis – struck me with its compassion and a strong moral force that couldn’t be more timely. After Camilleri died, I was heartened to learn that this story isn’t quite the end of the line. Camilleri wrote 27 Montalbano novels in Italian – so fans like me can hope for three more in translation.

title: Spying On The South: An Odyssey Across The American Divide
author: Tony Horwitz
description: In the 1850s, as the nation edged closer to civil war, an undercover reporter set off on a journey south. He was Frederick Law Olmsted, celebrated today as the visionary architect of Central Park. Olmsted was an avowed abolitionist, and for more than a year he traveled across the nation’s fault line – between free and slave states – trying to understand the angry debate consuming the country. Fast-forward to 2016, and another reporter – Tony Horwitz – re-creates Olmsted’s journey. Echoes of the fault line remain, this time among red states and blue: extreme polarization, racial strife and anger drowning out reasoned debate. It’s a delightful book for anyone interested in history, politics, landscape architecture and places far off the beaten track. And it turned out to be Horwitz’s last gift to his readers – he died the month it was published.

title: The Art Of Statistics: How To Learn From Data
author: David Spiegelhalter
description: We live in a world of big data: Numbers are thrown at us all the time, predicting who’s going to win an election, showing the state of the economy or claiming bacon sandwiches cause cancer. But as David Spiegelhalter points out, more data mean we need to be aware of what the evidence is actually worth. The book starts with a compelling tale of statistics and murder – a great way to draw in non-mathematicians like myself. Then comes the hard stuff: statistical significance, causation and correlation, uncertainty intervals, probability theory and systematic bias. Spiegelhalter carefully explains everything you need to know: Are numbers bringing clarity and insight, or are they being used to promote an agenda or attract attention? In an era of “alternative facts” and “fake news,” this is the right book at the right time. Learn how to evaluate the evidence yourself and believe your own eyes and ears.

title: The Editor
author: Steven Rowley
description: In fiction and nonfiction, editors help writers find paths through worlds, and worlds within worlds. In Steven Rowley’s novel, James Smale has sold his semi-autobiographical novel about a complicated mother-son relationship to a publisher – and the editor who acquired his book turns out to be Jacqueline Onassis. She loves the story but tells James that the ending needs work. Over many months, Mrs. Onassis helps James craft an ending to his story, on the page and in real life. The editor makes James a better writer, and The Editor will make you want to be a better son.

title: Trust Exercise: A Novel
author: Susan Choi
description: Reading and dissecting Trust Exercise was one of my greatest literary pleasures this year. But I’m hesitant to say more – revealing too much will ruin the joy of discovering the twists and turns on your own. So I will say this: The story centers on a group of students at a performing arts high school in the 1980s. But that’s not what the novel is about. It’s about the myths we create to reconcile ourselves to our memories. It’s about how there’s no single truth. It’s about who gets to own a person’s story. And it’s a satisfying read.

title: Republic Of Shame: Stories From Ireland's Institutions For 'Fallen Women'
author: Caelainn Hogan
description: A few years ago, when I read about the horrors inflicted by Roman Catholic nuns on single moms and their children at the Bon Secours Mother and Baby Home in Tuam, Ireland, I wondered what drove the shame that enforced this cruelty. Caelainn Hogan’s sensitive, can’t-look-away book, Republic of Shame, explores that question through the stories of the women who were irrevocably damaged by it. Ireland today is progressive – abortion and same-sex marriage are legal – but those milestones came only within the past five years. Through moving stories, Hogan shows how the past is still present.

title: They Will Have To Die Now: Mosul And The Fall Of The Caliphate
author: James Verini
description: I’m generally not into books about war. The epic, testosterone-laden stories of (mostly) male trials and triumphs do nothing for me. But journalist James Verini focuses on the quiet moments between battles in his book about the fight to take Mosul from ISIS. He documents the troughs between adrenaline waves, the boredom that turns fighters into philosophers and the irony of everyday indignities. War is hell, and in Verini’s prose it is also utterly absurd. But is it inevitable? Verini takes you to the front lines and forces you to confront that question over and over again.

title: The New Me
author: Halle Butler
description: Halle Butler’s tale of an embittered millennial temp worker is full of abandoned self-care rituals and existential rage. But the real surprise of this book is how tenderly the author writes the character of Millie, who at first glance seems to be a gremlin in a human suit. Unzip the shabby dress though, and you’ll find a wounded generation supped on promises of endless growth and Instagram dreams. Pick up this book for the devastating analysis of what utter self-awareness hath wrought and stay for the office-related petty revenge fantasies.

title: Five Days Gone: The Mystery Of My Mother's Disappearance As A Child
author: Laura Cumming
description: In 1929, Laura Cumming’s mother was kidnapped. Five days later, she was returned to the people she thought were her mother and father. This crime makes up the central mystery of Cumming’s gorgeously written memoir. But what could have been a satisfying whodunit is instead a close examination of collective silence and suppressed love. You will weep, you will mourn and you will wonder what it is you’ve never known – and never will – about your own family.

title: Acid For The Children: A Memoir
author: Flea
description: Flea has written a memoir not about music, but about a feral child growing up in Hollywood who assembles a family out of the other lost souls around him. The prose is like his bass technique – wild, energetic, indelible. And in the final moments of the book, as the Red Hot Chili Peppers are about to take the stage for the first time, you can’t wait to find out what’s next.

title: A Dream About Lightning Bugs: A Life Of Music And Cheap Lessons
author: Ben Folds
description: Reading Ben Folds’ memoir feels like sitting down with an old friend who’s seen it all. Folds dropped out of college, tossed his drums into a lake on campus and paid the bills playing music at a German restaurant wearing lederhosen. Even if you’re not a huge fan, it’s interesting to read the stories behind familiar tunes like “Army” (which was inspired by a conversation he had with his dad about becoming a college dropout). Like his music, Folds’ writing isn’t always graceful, but it’s full of passion and unpredictability. His talent for banging out jams on the piano and transforming personal stories into songs is epitomized in this Tiny Desk concert, which I gleefully recorded and mixed back in 2016.

title: Just Like Beverly: A Biography Of Beverly Cleary
author: Vicki Conrad, illustrated by David Hohn
description: Confession: I hugged this book before I even opened it – just like young Beverly Cleary on the cover, clutching her reading in her arms. Just Like Beverly is a picture book biography of my favorite writer. Cleary was a frustrated reader at first but learned to love reading and writing. She’s drawn so much like Ramona, with lovely touches such as Cleary cutting rose bushes (Portland, Ore.’s beloved flower) while waiting to hear if her first book will get published. I love a book that humanizes the writing process for kids and lets them know that the world awaits their stories too. (For ages 5 to 9)

title: Ayesha At Last
author: Uzma Jalaluddin
description: It is a truth universally acknowledged that a Pride and Prejudice retelling will emerge ever few years. Few, dear reader, will be as good as this one. Uzma Jalaluddin, a Toronto teacher and journalist, wanted to write about “ordinary Muslims” and chose to do it through a fetching romance. How spirited, independent Ayesha Shamsi meets, dismisses and finally clicks with shy, conservative Khalid Mirza had me from Page 1. In addition to being a thoroughly modern love story, Jalaluddin weaves in issues such as substance abuse and colorism, seldom discussed outside her community. If there is justice in the world, there will be a sequel.

title: In The Country Of Women: A Memoir
author: Susan Straight
description: There are at least two kinds of families: the ones we are born into and the ones we choose, regardless of blood connection. In this memoir, Susan Straight tells the story of the women in her Swiss-descended blood family and the African-descended women who became family when she married into their clan. This gorgeously written memoir is a gift to her three daughters, but it’s also a heartfelt refutation of so much of the nationalism and classism that have shredded our national fabric. Straight’s book reminds us that inclusion and generosity are among the values that made America great.

title: Supreme Glamour
author: Mary Wilson with Mark Bego
description: I first saw The Supremes (Mary Wilson, Diana Ross, Florence Ballard) in concert around 1964 – I wasn’t even a teen then – and remember them actually glittering from the stage. Part of it was their undeniable star power, sure, and that was enhanced by their clothes. Founding Supreme Mary Wilson gives us a luscious look at the original group’s fashion evolution, from the promlike chiffons of the early years through the slinky sequined numbers in the late ‘60s and early ‘70s. In addition to each gown’s provenance and composition (was that silk velvet or rayon velvet?), Wilson has generous inclusions of candid and offstage photos. Gift this coffee-table book to your fashionista friend, die-hard fans of the OG Supremes and anyone who misses the elegance embodied by this Motown trio.

title: Women Rowing North: Navigating Life’s Currents And Flourishing As We Age
author: Mary Pipher
description: Mary Pipher’s famous 1990s book Reviving Ophelia explores the often hidden, traumatic lives of teenage girls. With Women Rowing North, Pipher dives into stories of women older than 50 – who may have been Ophelia readers – who, after years of giving and nurturing, now look over the landscape of their lives with calm contentment, good humor and a sense of wisdom. This comes despite misogyny, ageism and the loss of so much, including sometimes themselves. Women of all ages will find much to reflect on, and respond to, in this collection of lives lived and lost.

title: The Stationery Shop
author: Marjan Kamali
description: I happened to pick up The Stationery Shop last summer just as tensions between Iran and the U.S. were hitting a peak – and what a timely, insightful look into that country’s past and America’s role in it this book turned out to be. But it’s also a gut-wrenching love story about family secrets and misunderstandings. (On the lighter side, the vivid descriptions of traditional feasts had me running hungrily to a strip of Iranian restaurants near my Los Angeles apartment.)

title: You Will Be Safe Here
author: Damian Barr
description: I love a good story where I also learn something new. And Damian Barr’s historical novel – partly set during the Second Boer War in southern Africa – checks all the boxes. Barr deftly points out how the British scorched-earth policies and development of concentration camps helped inspire the atrocities of Nazi Germany – as well as laid the groundwork for apartheid in South Africa. At times, this book felt like two completely different stories – it’s set in 1900, and also in 2010 at an apparent gay conversion therapy camp. But stick with it and you’ll see how it’s all related.

title: The Education Of An Idealist: A Memoir
author: Samantha Power
description: Turns out, facing down warlords as a 20-something reporter is pretty good training for a future diplomat. Samantha Power’s The Education of an Idealist is that rare political memoir: startlingly honest, funny and beautifully written. This Pulitzer Prize-winning author and former journalist in Bosnia spent eight years in the Obama administration, ending as U.S. ambassador to the UN. Power knows how improbable her own life story is: An Irish immigrant as a child, she eventually comes to speak for the entire United States – still, facing down warlords while raising her two very young kids. It’s a story she tells with relish and bracing candor. I couldn’t put it down.

title: Weird Parenting Wins: Bathtub Dining, Family Screams, And Other Hacks From The Parenting Trenches
author: Hillary Frank
description: You can treat this one like a favorite cookbook – pulling it down off the shelf for consultation in moments of need. Weird Parenting Wins is the brainchild of Hillary Frank, creator of The Longest Shortest Time parenting podcast. Frank crowdsourced hundreds of hard-won parenting tips from her listeners, and the result is a book filled with chapters like “The Art of Keeping Your Kids from Strangling Each Other.” It’s a laugh-out-loud parenting companion filled with tiny victories. Want your kid to go hiking? Toss ‘em a tube of lipstick and let ‘em keep applying it as long as they keep walking. Want your 2-year-old to eat peas? Load them into a PEZ dispenser!

title: The Gifted School: A Novel
author: Bruce Holsinger
description: This tale of scandalous admissions doesn’t have Desperate Housewives or celebrities’ daughters on yachts, but it’s no less salacious for it. A school for gifted kids is opening in a wealthy Colorado suburb, and parents are tripping over themselves to secure spots. The children – an equestrian bookworm, a social butterfly of average intelligence (and above-average income), a trilingual, origami-folding immigrant boy and a brilliant but socially stunted child – become pawns in their parents’ wildly inappropriate admissions antics. It’s a page-turning meditation on what it means to be gifted – and how far parents will go to prove it.

title: What My Mother And I Don't Talk About: Fifteen Writers Break The Silence
author: Michele Filgate (editor)
description: “Our mothers are our first homes, and that’s why we’re always trying to return to them,” editor Michele Filgate writes in this book’s first essay. Our mothers are also our first heroes – and therefore it feels like a deep betrayal when we learn they are mere mortals. Each essay – from writers such as Carmen Maria Machado, André Aciman, Kiese Laymon and Leslie Jamison – is a portrait of a fracture. For some, it’s a hairline – the fissure undetectable to an outsider’s eye. For others, there’s a wide gulf where the two sides used to touch. The wounds in these stories are painfully detailed, but so is, in equal measure, the love. If you’re feeling bold, the book is a potent conversation starter. But at the very least, it’s 15 voices saying: “You’re not alone.”
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Short Stories & Essays", "Biography & Memoir"]

title: The Testaments: The Sequel To The Handmaid's Tale
author: Margaret Atwood
description: Would you recognize evil if you saw it? What about resistance? In The Testaments (Margaret Atwood’s long-awaited sequel to The Handmaid’s Tale), readers get a deeper look into the conditions that created Gilead – a fundamentalist Christian dystopia that’s not quite as unimaginable as one might hope. In this society, where everyone’s options are some gradation of awful, a handful of women find ways to undermine the system. But even that resistance is fraught in a book that makes heroism look like the least glamorous choice available. The revolution is not televised, the revolutionaries don’t look like revolutionaries and the impeccably mannered bad guys just want everyone to be more civil.

title: Catch And Kill: Lies, Spies, And A Conspiracy To Protect Predators
author: Ronan Farrow
description: Ronan Farrow is perhaps the most important chronicler of powerful men’s misdeeds in this era, using the privilege of his upbringing as the son of Mia Farrow and Woody Allen to shine the light on the very community he grew up around. While the book reads like a thriller and shocks with the layers of duplicity and intimidation that were wielded to protect Harvey Weinstein, what shines through are the touching and terrible testimonies of the women who were victims of sexual assault and harassment. This book will confirm your worst suspicions about the way the world has worked up until now, with the rich and mighty protecting each other at the expense of everyone else – and yet it leaves you hopeful that things can change.

title: The Heavens
author: Sandra Newman
description: The Heavens is a novel that almost defies description. It is a time traveling fantasy, historical fiction, political allegory, social realism and love story all rolled into one. A young woman named Kate has a recurring dream that takes her to an alternate life in Elizabethan England. Kate believes her dreams are changing the “real” world, where cataclysmic events like climate change and the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks are taking place. Kate’s boyfriend, Ben, fears she is going insane, and the young couple must confront the pain that comes with mental illness. This book is a wildly imaginative ride where the reader, like Kate, is never quite sure what is a dream and what is reality.

title: The Nickel Boys: A Novel
author: Colson Whitehead
description: I almost didn’t read this book. I knew it was based on a real reform school for boys in Florida that was both violent and racist, and I was afraid of what I would find in it. Colson Whitehead does not hold back on the cruelties suffered by the inmates of the Nickel Academy. One of them, Elwood Curtis, ends up at Nickel through a twist of fate. He forges a friendship with a boy named Turner who guides the idealistic Elwood through this brutal new world with a clear-eyed reality. It is in this friendship that Whitehead finds a way to give this story a turn that takes it to an unexpected place – where a new life is possible even as the pain of the past is never forgotten.

title: A Particular Kind Of Black Man: A Novel
author: Tope Folarin
description: I picked this book up about 7:30 p.m. one Tuesday and was transported – taken on a riveting journey into self, cultural identity and the challenges of leaving a life behind to start over in a new country. This book particularly appealed to me as a black woman who too has traversed the immigrant and academic landscape of this country, and who too had a traumatic relationship with a parent. The moments of confusion and reflection, the search for answers, validation, acceptance, hope and ultimately lessons of love were beyond palpable when I turned that last page – four hours after I first entered Tope Folarin’s world.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana", "Family Matters"]

title: A Good Provider Is One Who Leaves: One Family and Migration In The 21st Century
author: Jason DeParle
description: Jason DeParle tells a multigenerational migration saga by focusing on one Filipino family. DeParle first met Tita and Emet Comodas in the late 1980s when he was reporting on slum life in Manila. Emet migrates to Saudi Arabia to work as a pool keeper, and eventually all five of his children also leave home to work overseas. DeParle documents the ongoing cycle of sacrifice and success over decades. “The fact that so many people are willing to leave their families and take such risks and be apart for such a length of time is just another measure of their poverty and desperation,” he told All Things Considered. The current discourse on immigration in the U.S. makes this book a must-read – and DeParle’s nuanced history and beautiful narrative make this gripping, satisfying read difficult to put down – no matter your political persuasion.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: Places And Names: On War, Revolution, And Returning
author: Elliot Ackerman
description: Elliot Ackerman was a Marine – five tours in Iraq and Afghanistan – and years afterward, he decides to sit down with the enemy. So he invites Abu Hassar, who fought for al-Qaida in Iraq, to lunch. They don’t share a common language. During one particularly awkward silence, Ackerman opens his notebook to a clean page. He draws a ribbon – the Euphrates River. Abu Hassar recognizes it and scribbles in the name of a town where he fought. “Our hands now chase each other around the map, mimicking the way we’d once chased each other around this country,” Ackerman writes, as it dawns on both men that they fought the same war, just different sides. Beautiful writing about combat and humanity and what it means to “win” a war.
tags: ["History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: She Said: Breaking The Sexual Harassment Story That Helped Ignite A Movement
author: Jodi Kantor and Megan Twohey
description: I know what you’re thinking: “A book about Harvey Weinstein! Detailing allegations of sexual harassment! That’s just what I wanted for the holidays!” Bear with me here, because this book is in fact such a great and empowering read. You will come away with profound respect for Jodi Kantor and Megan Twohey, and the lengths they go to in order to land their story. (How do you track down Gwyneth Paltrow’s phone number and then persuade her to talk to you?) And if you’re like me, you will also come away grateful for the many ways women have, and continue, to come together to support one another.

title: On Earth, We're Briefly Gorgeous: A Novel
author: Ocean Vuong
description: When Ocean Vuong’s mother – a Vietnamese immigrant – gave him his distinctive first name, she called into being a writer whose language will carry readers away. Vuong’s sentences are liquid, flowing, teasing and overpowering. Written in the form of a letter from a son to his illiterate mother, Vuong’s debut novel ranges from the legacy of the Vietnam War, to a gay teenager’s sexual awakening, to the wry observation that “sorry” is the most-used English word spoken by Asian manicurists. Like so many immigrant writers before him, Vuong has not only made the English language his own, he’s made it better.

title: The Beneficiary: Fortune, Misfortune, And The Story Of My Father
author: Janny Scott
description: To give you a quick sense of Janny Scott’s pedigree, her great-great-grandfather was a railroad baron and mentor to Andrew Carnegie, and her glamorous grandmother was said to be the inspiration for the Hepburn character in The Philadelphia Story. Growing up, Scott (along with her family and four generations of relatives) lived in houses scattered over an 800-acre Main Line estate. The Beneficiary is a wise and poignant memoir about the emotional weight of inheritance. Scott surveys all the things money can buy – and all the things it can’t.

title: Conviction
author: Denise Mina
description: Scottish mystery master Denise Mina departs from the gritty crime novels that have made her reputation with an eerie story that mashes up a Gothic ghost tale with a classic woman-in-trouble plot. The opening pages of Conviction set its deliciously hectic pace: Our heroine, a mother of two named Anna, pours herself morning coffee as she listens to one of the true crime podcasts she loves. Before Anna finishes that coffee, her marriage has crumbled and she learns – via that podcast – that an old friend has died aboard a cursed yacht. Conviction is the ultimate un-put-downable mystery.

title: The Salt Path
author: Raynor Winn
description: In The Salt Path, Raynor Winn takes us on both a physical and philosophical journey. What, to me, sets this trek along England’s South West Coast Path apart from several other memoirs involving hiking is that it doesn’t feel contrived: Raynor and her husband, Moth, are not young adults trying to find themselves. Instead, after 32 years of building a solid family life, a business deal gone bad leaves them homeless and searching for a future. I honestly felt like I was on this trip with them, experiencing the awe of nature and new places, worrying about all that was threatening – and finding kinship along the way.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: I Miss You When I Blink: Essays
author: Mary Laura Philpott
description: There aren’t many books that have made me tear up from both laughing and crying – on the same page. Mary Laura Philpott’s essays truly capture the emotional pull of mothering beyond the baby stage – coexisting, for some, with a midlife crisis-esque period. It’s the drive to devote yourself fully to your kids, knowing that in two seconds they’ll be adults themselves, battling the constant fear of personal stagnation. She establishes that there can be many “yous,” among them the childhood you, the single you, the married you, the parent you, the career you. Through sometimes painful, often funny and always heartwarming self-reflective stories, she shows readers that one of you doesn’t have to give way for another – that your many “yous” can coexist as “a crowd of selves.”

title: Ask Again, Yes: A Novel
author: Mary Beth Keane
description:
Two young Irish American cops, Francis and Brian, meet at the police academy, work the streets together briefly and, by coincidence, buy houses next to each other in a small town. Francis’ daughter and Brian’s son quickly become inseparable. But all is not well behind closed doors: Brian’s wife is mentally ill, but no one seems to be able to recognize or address it until it’s too late. That illness and the denial of it set in motion events that mark both families in profound ways.
This book reminded me of the people I grew up with and around, even though my family is neither Irish nor suburban. They were people who wore uniforms and a badge; some because they wanted to, some because they thought it was the best they could do. These days, police officers are either heroes or villains; in this novel, they are just human. It’s not a tidy story and there is no tidy ending, but it’s deeply felt and feels real and true.

title: How To Do Nothing: Resisting The Attention Economy
author: Jenny Odell
description: Like Thoreau’s essay on “Walking,” Jenny Odell’s big point is not that elaborate, inventive or novel. It’s in the title. How to Do Nothing: Resisting the Attention Economy is a big, thick dialectic about resisting the world of digital nonsense so that we can genuinely “want what we want to want.” Despite the distracting “How to Do” portion of the title, though, Odell’s work is less a how-to than a what-I-did. She wanders around, pointing at soothing ephemera (bird-watching, etc.), inviting us to amble alongside and consider the degree to which we’ve all succumbed to 2019’s myriad consciousness-suckers. Halfway between a park ranger and a spiritual guru, Odell gives us carte blanche to really consider the blanche.

title: The Undefeated
author: Kwame Alexander, illustrated by Kadir Nelson
description: Kwame Alexander’s unflinching verse combines with Kadir Nelson’s unparalleled artistry to take a powerful dive into the pain and the pride of black history and current events, emerging with a hopeful and resolute eye toward the future. In making sure that we remember the past, Alexander and Nelson have left us with something truly unforgettable. (For ages 6 to 9)

title: Bowlaway: A Novel
author: Elizabeth McCracken
description: I suspect there’s never been (or ever will be again) another work of fiction whose main character is arguably a family-owned candlepin bowling alley. From the moment Bertha Truitt, matriarch-to-be, is discovered unconscious in a cemetery, events follow their own idiosyncratic (and sometimes outlandish) course, but Elizabeth McCracken, a writer’s writer, brings this charming and quirky novel to a most satisfying conclusion.

title: Out Of Darkness, Shining Light: A Novel
author: Petina Gappah
description: When African explorer David Livingstone died in 1873 in what is now Zambia, the remaining members of his expedition carried his body for 1,500 miles so he could be sent back to Britain for burial. This brilliantly conceived novel is told by two narrators: Livingstone’s cook Halima, a young slave whom Livingstone bought as a “road wife” for his expedition leader, and Jacob Wainwright, an educated former slave and devout Christian, who reads the explorer’s journals and writes his own on the 285-day journey east. Their voices show us the complexity and contradictory beliefs of a man whom most of us know only by the phrase “Dr. Livingstone, I presume?”

title: Epic Continent: Adventures In The Great Stories Of Europe
author: Nicholas Jubber
description: In this fascinating mixture of armchair travel and close reading of European epic literature, we journey throughout Europe accompanied by works such as The Odyssey (Greece); the Nibelungenlied (Germany); Beowulf (Scandinavia) and – most interesting for me, since I was totally unfamiliar with it – the Kosovo cycle (the Balkans). Not only is it a painless, entertaining way to brush up on the classics, but you’ll want to use this book to plan your next European jaunt.

title: Rabbits For Food
author: Binnie Kirshenbaum
description: A simple plot description – depressed woman checks herself into a psych ward, refuses to take the prescribed meds and starts writing about her fellow inmates – doesn’t do justice to this marvelous novel, because it leaves out everything that makes it so special.
