The writing is splendid; against all odds it’s frequently explosively funny, and it offers readers a memorable picture of an often wittily acerbic woman trying to find herself in the gray fog of sadness. Oh, and there’s a cat named Jeffrey and a therapy dog who never shows up, as well.

title: Super Pumped: The Battle For Uber
author: Mike Isaac
description: In 2009, when the ride-hailing app Uber first started, it was called UberCab. The company was a basic black car service in San Francisco. Since then, the company has upended transportation around the world – at Uber’s highest valuation it was worth more than $60 billion. New York Times technology reporter Mike Isaac takes us through the fascinating rise of Uber, and the fall of its founder Travis Kalanick, who was forced to resign. Isaac brings his expertise and energy to Super Pumped, and helps us better understand one of the most important companies on earth.

title: The Empire And The Five Kings: America's Abdication And The Fate Of The World
author: Bernard-Henri Lévy
description: In his book,The Empire and the Five Kings, renowned French philosopher Bernard-Henri Lévy argues that America’s influence is declining, and five powers – China, Russia, Iran, Turkey and Sunni radical Islam – are ready to take its place in the world. Levy argues that the United States, while built on idealism and laudable principles, is now focused only on the short term and lacks a strategic aim. His book was written before the U.S. abandoned the Kurds at the request of Turkey in Northern Syria, an event that only strengthens his argument.

title: The Witches Are Coming
author: Lindy West
description: Lindy West’s follow-up to her 2016 memoir, Shrill, proves that she remains one of the funniest, sharpest cultural critics of our time. Over 18 essays, the feminist writer and New York Times op-ed contributor eviscerates the current sociopolitical reality – from Grumpy Cat memes to the president’s cries of “WITCH HUNT!” on Twitter – all while making you laugh. This is an essential book for anyone who has felt the need to let out a primal scream into a pillow (or in public) in the last few years.

title: Beirut Hellfire Society: A Novel
author: Rawi Hage
description: “Everyone loves Beirut and everyone is scared of Beirut,” muses a character in this playfully scabrous novel that draws nearly as much from Nabokov as from Lebanon’s grisly civil war in the 1970s. Beirut Hellfire Society follows the nihilistic son of a Christian undertaker who falls in with a carnivalesque assortment of gleeful scofflaws and kinky debauchees who take it upon themselves to bury the remains of gay people, heretics and other undesirables. The writing is bravura, the humor, stygian and the thrill of expression, triumphant – even over the blasted, blasphemous horror show of war.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Hollywood's Eve: Eve Babitz And The Secret History Of L.A.
author: Lili Anolik
description: One golden tale unravels into another in Lili Anolik’s stylish portrait of writer and bon vivant Eve Babitz. Immortalized in a photo depicting her playing chess – nude – with Marcel Duchamp, Babitz has a life full of boldfaced names and equally bold tales (beginning with her name, given to her by her godfather, Igor Stravinsky). A native Angeleno, Babitz was “discovered” as a writer by Joan Didion, though Anolik’s inventive biography, which began as a magazine profile, prompted the reissue of all of Babitz’s books, ushering in a Babitz revival.

title: The Castle On Sunset: Life, Death, Love, Art, And Scandal At Hollywood’s Chateau Marmont
author: Shawn Levy
description: In a town where buildings get more face-lifts than movie stars do, the Chateau Marmont stands defiantly, getting better with age as it holds down the Beverly Hills end of Sunset Boulevard. The castlelike hotel became infamous after John Belushi overdosed there. But the pages of this book reveal many other intriguing stories in the decades both before and since, like that Mabel Walker Willebrandt, LA’s first “public defender of women” and at one time the highest-ranking woman in the federal government, was one of the Chateau’s original investors.

title: The Anarchy: The East India Company, Corporate Violence, And The Pillage Of An Empire
author: William Dalrymple
description: William Dalrymple has long chronicled, with great fealty, the British Raj and the collapse of the Mughal Empire in India. With The Anarchy, he tries to answer a central question: How did the East India Company evolve from a trading corporation into a colonial power that eventually controlled much of the Indian subcontinent, looting much of its riches in the process? It’s a great story told in fabulous detail with interesting, if at times utterly rapacious or incompetent, characters populating it.

title: The Wall: A Novel
author: John Lanchester
description: An island nation decides to barricade itself from the invading hordes after some kind of climate collapse and needs to rely on its citizens to defend “The Wall” it has built. Our protagonist is one such Defender, and it’s all going well until it’s not. This is not a fun read but a thought-provoking story for our times that’s also compulsively readable.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: First: Sandra Day O'Connor
author: Evan Thomas
description: Author Evan Thomas breaks new ground with First. With extraordinary access to the justice, her papers, her personal journals – and even 20 years of her husband’s diary – the book is, in a sense, an authorized biography. But it is considerably more. It is an unvarnished and psychologically intuitive look at the nation’s first female Supreme Court justice and some of her contradictory characteristics. First is unlike every other volume written about Sandra Day O’Connor – even the books the justice wrote about herself.

title: Moneyland: The Inside Story Of The Crooks And Kleptocrats Who Rule The World
author: Oliver Bullough
description: Give Oliver Bullough points for prescience. He wrote a book about corruption in Ukraine before so very many people were concerned about corruption in Ukraine. After president Viktor Yanukovych fled his country in 2014, Bullough walked through Yanukovych’s opulent estate and wondered how the leader of a poor country could afford such luxury. The answer is “dark money”: untaxed, unregulated, untraceable and uncountable, stolen by kleptocrats and dictators and funneled into shell companies, secret bank accounts and real estate in London and New York. Bullough gamely travels the world in search of the money and mostly doesn’t find it, but that’s the point. Along the way he examines the legal loopholes that allow it to vanish and makes two persuasive arguments. First, theft on this scale is eroding democracies. Second, if we better understood how this kind of semi-criminality works, we’d be very angry. Give him points as well for writing a book that’s both furious and funny.

title: The Impeachers: The Trial Of Andrew Johnson And The Dream Of A Just Nation
author: Brenda Wineapple
description: When Donald Trump became president and I began tracking his ethics problems, I went looking for a solid, comprehensive account of Andrew Johnson’s impeachment. Historian Brenda Wineapple was on it. The Impeachers, which she started in 2013, tells a big, mostly damning tale. Draw your parallels at will, as Johnson catered to the South’s just-defeated white supremacists and fought with what he called “this radical Congress.” As for the senator who cast the decisive vote to save Johnson (he’s in Profiles in Courage), Wineapple cites lingering questions that he was bought. So swampy.

title: Mrs. Everything: A Novel
author: Jennifer Weiner
description: The women who came of age in the 1960s and ‘70s are often known to literature only as muses or objects of desire in the many stories of men of the Age of Aquarius. But Mrs. Everything is a deeply felt, moving history of two members of this “lost” generation. The novel alternates between two sisters, Jo and Bethie Kaufman, whose lives are shaped by forces beyond their control, often in the form of men and male violence. They endure but suffer terrible losses along the way – the revealing of what the title means is one of the most casually tragic moments I can remember reading. Jennifer Weiner follows these two women for decades as they navigate a world that was not nearly as “liberated” as it liked to think.

title: Gods Of Jade And Shadow
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: Silvia Moreno-Garcia takes readers on a myth-dazzled road trip through 1920s Mexico in Gods of Jade and Shadow. It starts out as a simple Cinderella-esque tale: Casiopeia is little more than a drudge in the house of her wealthy grandfather, until she finds a locked chest containing the dismembered body of Hun-Kamé, the Maya god of death. But Moreno-Garcia spins her story out to stranger places, rejecting the easy arc of a fairy tale. You’ll be left wondering where Casiopeia will go next — and longing for more of her magical world.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Deep
author: Rivers Solomon, Daveed Diggs, William Hutson and Jonathan Snipes
description: Yetu is a historian – she holds the collective memories of her people, a peaceful undersea race called the wajinru. And she does it alone, because her people would be shattered if they knew the truth: They descend from pregnant slaves thrown overboard during the trans-Atlantic slave trade. Rivers Solomon’s lyrical but uncompromising novella – inspired by a track from the experimental hip-hop group clipping. – builds a rich, gorgeous underwater world on the foundations of dry land’s horrors.

title: Jade War
author: Fonda Lee
description: Sam Briger

title: Steel Crow Saga
author: Paul Krueger
description: Every year there’s a book that falls through the cracks, that I read too late to fit into our regular coverage and then smack myself and yell “ARRRGH!” loud enough to scare the cats. This year, it’s Paul Krueger’s delightful, swaggering, multilayered Southeast Asian-inflected fantasy Steel Crow Saga. If you’re getting tired of waiting for the final volume in Ken Liu’s Dandelion Dynasty series, you should spend some time with Krueger’s band of misfits as they navigate war, rebellion, postcolonialism, diplomacy, love, family ties – and magical animal companions.

title: Rat Rule 79: An Adventure
author: Rivka Galchen, illustrated by Elena Megalos
description: On the night before her 13th birthday, Fred can’t sleep. She gets out of bed and sees her mother walk through a lantern. Fred follows her into a magical land filled with highly rational, unreasonable rules (or is it highly reasonable, irrational rules?) In the ensuing puzzles and conundrums, Rivka Galchen delivers joy and cleverness reminiscent of The Phantom Tollbooth, Alice in Wonderland and Hayao Miyazaki movies as she playfully exposes the holes in logic as presented through language. From the quirky chapter headings to the deadpan illustrations by Elena Megalos, it is a wonderful read (and re-read) for kids and adults. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Family Matters", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: The Elephant In The Room: One Fat Man's Quest To Get Smaller In A Growing America
author: Tommy Tomlinson
description: “I’ve never been not fat,” writes Tommy Tomlinson. In this deeply personal memoir, Tomlinson traces his relationship with food from childhood to adulthood and describes how, around age 50, he made the pivotal decision to change the trajectory of his body and his life. He writes candidly about what it’s like to be fat – from the health risks to the humiliations of existing in a world not designed for his body. Readers will feel the emotional pull of the small-town Georgia of Tomlinson’s youth and find thoughtful observations about the nation’s approach to food and obesity.

title: Charged: The New Movement To Transform American Prosecution And End Mass Incarceration
author: Emily Bazelon
description: Mass incarceration is now widely accepted as a major social problem in the United States, but the causes and possible solutions to the problem are intensely debated. Emily Bazelon enters that conversation with her assertion that American prosecutors are too often left out of the national discourse on mass incarceration. In her richly layered storytelling style, Bazelon gives voice to people whose lives have been profoundly impacted by a system that grants many prosecutors unfettered power. By breaking down abstract legal arguments and using the stories of real people, Bazelon brings us closer to understanding the inequities of America’s legal system.

title: How To Hide An Empire: A History Of The Greater United States
author: Daniel Immerwahr
description: How to Hide an Empire takes you on a whirlwind tour of the islands and territories the U.S. has governed from the 19th century on. It draws you in with smartly weaved, gripping stories and constructs an impressively expansive tale of America’s global conquests. Manifest destiny takes on a whole new meaning. Simmering beneath all these stories is a powerful throughline: As classic colonialism was being fazed out in the 20th century, a new, more covert form of empire-building set in – with the U.S. at the forefront. It’s not a stretch to say that this book will make you think about American history in a new way.

title: This Land Is Our Land: An Immigrant's Manifesto
author: Suketu Mehta
description: Someone made the mistake of telling Suketu Mehta that immigrants were a problem. Mehta’s response, This Land Is Our Land, begins with an anecdote about his grandfather – an immigrant well-versed in Britain’s colonial experiments – who, when confronted in London in the ‘80s by a fellow Briton asking “Why are you in my country?” responded: “Because we are the creditors. You took all our wealth. Now we have come to collect.” It’s the thesis of the book but just the barest hint of the argument that Mehta will marshal by the time he’s finished presenting his sweeping history of how solidly our world has been built by immigrants. This might sound like a tiresome polemic but, in this case, anger has just sharpened Mehta’s focus and put his eloquence to full use. Beware of scapegoats who know the truth, write this well and decide to fight back.

title: Underland: A Deep Time Journey
author: Robert Macfarlane
description: Underland takes you deep underground to stare down the Anthropocene. Robert Macfarlane brutally confronts the realities of climate change by exploring some of the most inaccessible corners of the world – and takes you along for the ride. There’s not a whole lot of optimism in his examination of humanity’s cumulative impact on nature, and yet he’s still able to see so much beauty. This work often reads more like poetry – and taps into some of the most ancient human tendencies to both bury and extract.

title: 10 Minutes, 38 Seconds In This Strange World
author: Elif Shafak
description: The clock starts ticking the moment you begin Elif Shafak’s gripping novel. That’s because there are only 10 minutes and 38 seconds left before the protagonist’s brain shuts down. Leila, a sex worker, has been murdered and left in a dumpster. Through flashbacks to her life in modern-day Turkey, minute by minute, you’ll feel her wonder, her joy, her pain. You’ll feel empathy for a girl whose life is upended from the day she is born. It’s companionship with other Istanbul transplants that saves Leila from complete despair. And as you get to know Leila’s other friends on the margins of society, you find yourself rooting for them in the unlikeliest of endeavors.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Good To Go: What The Athlete In All Of Us Can Learn From The Strange Science Of Recovery
author: Christie Aschwanden
description: I hadn’t realized that an entire industry had sprung up to help people recover rapidly from the aches and pains of serious exercise until I read Christie Aschwanden’s book. But I knew that this accomplished science reporter would lead me on a thoughtful journey to flag the hype (such as celebrity-branded underwear), while highlighting what’s likely to be true. This is by no means just a book for jocks – it tells us how to use evidence to understand the claims that swirl all around. Aschwanden devises a controlled trial using beer as a recovery aid. She floats in the dark, struggles into constricting garments and generally dishes about everything from ice baths (dubious), hydration (oversold), lactic acid (misunderstood) to sleep – which is not only effective but free!

title: How To Build A Dragon Or Die Trying: A Satirical Look At Cutting-Edge Science
author: Paul Knoepfler and Julie Knoepfler
description: Scientist Paul Knoepfler has a reputation for sorting through some of the outrageous claims around cell therapy, which are too often purported cures that end instead in tragedy. He teamed up with his high school daughter, Julie, to imagine how they would set about to design and build an actual flying, fire-breathing dragon. The subtitle calls this work a satire, but it’s really more a flight of fancy. It imagines how a not-so-mad scientist might use the latest tools, such as gene editing technology and stem cells, to create something that’s firmly rooted in fiction. It’s an amusing journey to explore the power and ethics of these technologies.

title: The Optimist's Telescope: Thinking Ahead In A Reckless Age
author: Bina Venkataraman
description: I covered climate change for NPR for many years and gradually concluded that our greatest challenge isn’t the science, the technology or even the economics, but how to change human behavior. Bina Venkataraman zeroes in on the heart of this problem in The Optimist’s Telescope. We, individually and collectively, rarely look ahead more than a few steps, whether it’s investors focused on quarterly profit reports or farming practices that increase yields today but deplete resources for the future. After framing these problems with vivid anecdotes, Venkataraman musters all the optimism she can manage to suggest how we might be able to overcome something that feels like plain old human nature.

title: Nobody's Looking At You: Essays
author: Janet Malcolm
description: Each of the essays in Janet Malcolm’s Nobody’s Looking at You turn up small, beautiful details that get caught in your brain like dandelion seeds get caught on a sweater. There’s pianist Yuja Wang, on a world tour with Beethoven’s most difficult sonatas – but all anyone talks about are her short, tight stage dresses. There’s an earnest review of the TLC docuseries Sarah Palin’s Alaska. My favorite of the lot is her essay “Three Sisters,” about the owners of a Manhattan bookstore so cool that singer and writer Patti Smith once lied about her book-restoring credentials to get a job there – and was quickly let go after she accidentally spilled rabbit glue all over a 19th century Bible, a detail I discovered while reading Nobody’s Looking at You.

title: Trick Mirror: Reflections On Self-Delusion
author: Jia Tolentino
description: Increasingly, I feel like the Internet grew up way too quickly and matured into something our society still hasn’t made sense of yet. The World Wide Web sometimes seems as if it controls us now, not the other way around. It makes us angry, exhausted, tells us what we should be thinking or doing and often knows what we want before we do. Jia Tolentino’s Trick Mirror arrives in the midst of my existential angst over all this. Pitched as personal essays, reflections on Tolentino’s own self-delusion, it’s so much more. It’s a guidebook for dealing with an Internet gone wild. Though she often has just as many questions about the corrosive nature of Internet and its intersection with late-stage capitalism, Trick Mirror let me know I’m not alone in my feelings. And that feels pretty good.

title: Queenie
author: Candice Carty-Williams
description: Queenie has been pitched as Black Bridget Jones, a label one could find a bit offensive. But author Candice Carty-Williams has no such qualms, as long as it gets people to read her book – in fact, she came up with the phrase. Carty-Williams’ desire to handle race bluntly and clearly, yet refuse to be dogmatic about it, just as she was with the label Black Bridget Jones, is what makes her debut novel sing. Her heroine – a young black woman in the U.K. struggling to make it as a journalist while also figuring out money, family and love – brings you into her world as if she’s known you forever, right from the start; the opening scene of the book has our heroine enduring an OB-GYN visit from hell, narrating the whole ordeal while in stirrups. This book is equal parts millennial comedy and treatise on modern race relations, and a work of fiction that leaps from the page as all truth, no filler.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: One Day: The Extraordinary Story Of An Ordinary 24 Hours In America
author: Gene Weingarten
description: Gene Weingarten asked strangers at a bar to pick a day at random out of a green fedora. They chose Dec. 28, 1986. So, Weingarten researched and wrote beautiful, compelling stories about the events of that otherwise average, sleepy Sunday. They are by turns heartfelt, profound, tragic, ironic, funny, cruel and heroic. One even circuitously involves Weingarten’s barber. But above all, each is a deeply human portrait, and a glimpse into an interesting and complicated 24 hours in American history. Weingarten proves, again and again, that he can turn anything and anyone into a story that I would want to read.

title: In The Dream House: A Memoir
author: Carmen Maria Machado
description: Like many women I know, I’m guilty of having said something like “Life would be so much easier if I were a lesbian” at least once. But in this memoir, Carmen Maria Machado recounts how the stereotype of the lesbian relationship as safe and utopian, and the lack of existing narratives about queer domestic abuse, kept her from recognizing her own psychologically abusive relationship with her ex-girlfriend for what it was. In The Dream House is a stunning, brave, inventive work, yes, but it’s also a historical document that can’t be erased, that says: This happened. It’s not an easy book to read, but it’s a necessary one.

title: Guest House For Young Widows: Among The Women Of ISIS
author: Azadeh Moaveni
description: The role of women in ISIS has been fetishized and sensationalized. But who are these women – and what happened before they gave up lives in their home countries for Syria and the caliphate? Some of the 13 women Azadeh Moaveni follows in this book were fleeing political and financial insecurity, some were true believers in the religious ideals that ISIS promoted and some were in love with the idea of having a warrior husband. Moaveni is clear that the women she writes about had agency when they joined ISIS, but it’s also clear that she still sees them as human; she works hard to provide much-needed context for what drives them. For me, this book’s greatest gift, other than exceptional reporting, is empathy.

title: Horror Stories: A Memoir
author: Liz Phair
description: Liz Phair’s new memoir is the installment of “Stars – They’re Just Like Us!” you never knew you needed. If you’ve ever felt a bad memory eat away at you, Phair can relate. Her Horror Stories are tales of misunderstandings, misgivings and decisions she’s still questioning all these years later. There’s enough rock ’n’ roll memoir stuff for fans of her music, but it’s also a generous peek inside the artist’s head. Turns out our rock goddess is human after all.

title: Wild Game: My Mother, Her Lover, And Me
author: Adrienne Brodeur
description: Adrienne Brodeur’s mom, Malabar, is a force of nature. A rare beauty, an accomplished chef, a consummate entertainer. She’s the kind of woman to whom everyone is in thrall, and her daughter is no different. So when Malabar creeps into 14-year-old Adrienne’s room and reveals she’s just shared an extramarital kiss with a family friend – and she’s thrilled about it – Adrienne becomes Malabar’s mostly willing accomplice in the epic affair that follows. Wild Game is Brodeur’s telling of the story of that affair, and a reckoning with her own complicity. It’s a delicious page-turner, vivid and emotionally intense. I couldn’t put it down.

title: Say Nothing: A True Story Of Murder And Memory In Northern Ireland
author: Patrick Radden Keefe
description: A history of Northern Ireland’s “troubles,” but focused in on the choices and consequences faced by a handful of families. The book is written and paced like a novel, with surprise revelations and twists. Reading Say Nothing against the backdrop of current events, I couldn’t help thinking that it shows just how quickly a neighborhood, city or country can descend into deadly violence that can then take years, even generations, to heal.

title: Our Man: Richard Holbrooke And The End Of The American Century
author: George Packer
description: Our Man is many things: a history of Democratic politics from John F. Kennedy to Barack Obama; a study of the rapidly ending era of American foreign policy experts single-handedly shaping world events; and, most compellingly, a Wolf Hall or Shakespeare-level story about a man desperately grabbing for power and prestige in Washington. The biography of Richard Holbrooke also includes one of the most readable histories of the Vietnam War that I’ve come across, and an inside look at why the Obama administration failed on its promise to wind down the American military presence in Afghanistan.

title: Agent Running In The Field: A Novel
author: John le Carré
description: My first thought when I saw John le Carré had written a new novel: Oh no. His last book, A Legacy of Spies, had been a perfect cap to a literary career spanning half a century, with its return to George Smiley and other iconic characters from his early books. It only took a few pages of reading Agent Running in the Field to change that view. The novel has all the hallmarks of a classic le Carré story: surprise plot twists, cryptic meetings in exotic locales, verbal jousting among high-brow British socialites, etc, etc. It drops all those ingredients into a plot touching on current events, including Brexit and, yes, President Trump. It all adds up to a charming and fun read.

title: Kochland: The Secret History Of Koch Industries And Corporate Power In America
author: Christopher Leonard
description: A richly reported tale of how Charles Koch and his late brother David amassed the fortune they used to bankroll a network of conservative think tanks, lobbying shops and “Astroturf” advocacy groups. For Democrats and environmentalists, the Koch brothers are among the darkest of dark-money villains. But Charles Koch is also a savvy businessman who quietly built his family’s Wichita company into a multibillion-dollar conglomerate. Christopher Leonard tells how, in this riveting story of modern capitalism full of insider accounts from refinery operators, warehouse workers, options traders and whistleblowers.

title: Aloha Rodeo: Three Hawaiian Cowboys, The World's Greatest Rodeo, And A Hidden History Of The American West
author: David Wolman and Julian Smith
description: Let’s get this out of the way: I highly recommend reading Aloha Rodeo during a Hawaiian vacation. You’ll find yourself noticing things you may have otherwise overlooked, such as the 130-year-old cattle ranch you pass on your way up Haleakala Volcano and the rocky terrain that paniolos (Hawaiian cowboys) navigated on horseback just across from where you take a surf lesson. But even if you find yourself reading it from the mainland, when it comes time for the paniolos to face off against the top American rodeo stars in Wyoming, you won’t be able to help but root for the underdogs.

title: If You Lived Here You'd Be Home By Now: Why We Traded The Commuting Life For A Little House On The Prairie
author: Christopher Ingraham
description: Christopher Ingraham did what so many of us have fantasized about: After visiting Red Lake Falls, Minn. – the least attractive place to live in the United States, per an obscure government ranking he wrote about for The Washington Post – he said goodbye to big-city life and moved his family to the heartland. Of course, his spot in the heartland features temperatures that shatter thermometers in the winter then thaw frozen dog poop in the spring, turning his front lawn into a stinky mess. But after reading his book, which weaves high-level datasets with hilarious stories from on the ground in Ingraham’s new life, I could see the possibility of Red Lake Falls, and other small towns like it, making way for more coastal migrants soon.

title: Cribsheet: A Data-Driven Guide To Better, More Relaxed Parenting, From Birth To Preschool
author: Emily Oster
description: If you (like me) have a small baby at home, you’ll be hungry for a book like this. Emily Oster spent years reading through mountains of data to be able to answer questions like: How long are you supposed to breastfeed? And why? And what if you don’t? She’s an economist, so her approach is pragmatic, even as she takes on charged topics like vaccination and co-sleeping. (She also devotes a chapter to vagina recovery titled “Trust Me, Take the Mesh Underwear.”) The book is jampacked with information, but it’s also a delightful read because Oster is such a good writer. The overall message: Consider the data, then figure out what works for your family, and quit all the second-guessing.

title: The Water Dancer: A Novel
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
description: The Water Dancer is beautiful and captivating. It starts as a story about Hiram, an enslaved man with a mysterious gift who is forced to serve his white half-brother. It becomes an expansive adventure story about organizing and traversing the Underground Railroad through a series of supernatural search-and-rescue missions. In a Fresh Air interview, Ta-Nehisi Coates explains that, in his research for the book, he found “magic is often very much a part” of the way African Americans described their escape from slavery. The Water Dancer, he says, “tries to take a somewhat forgotten tradition in African American resistance and render it seriously.”
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana", "Historical Fiction"]

title: Dark Age
author: Pierce Brown
description: In the first trilogy in Pierce Brown’s Red Rising universe, there was an epic, planet-hopping war started by slaves rebelling against their masters – the living gods known as the Golds. Last year’s Iron Gold kicked off a second trilogy and explored the aftermath of that war through characters on the periphery as well as continued the story of Darrow – the former slave who became a hero to the revolution. With Dark Age, Brown begins to weave his threads together in violent and surprising ways that avoid feeling contrived. The collisions make sense. The losses are fitting. The gains are earned. If the goal of a penultimate book in a series is to break down characters (and the reader) and put everyone through the emotional wringer, mission accomplished.

title: The City In The Middle Of The Night
author: Charlie Jane Anders
description: In a city bathed in a persistent daylight, on a planet with never-changing zones of light and dark, blistering hot and deathly cold, the hours of the day are regulated by systems and governments and strict protocols. People wake and work and sleep on the timetable set by the ruling class. The story Charlie Jane Anders chooses to tell, with imaginative characters to match this vividly realized setting, is one of love and loss, wavering friendships, loyalty, privilege, rebellion – and crocodiles. Giant, telepathic crocodiles. It is thoroughly engrossing and adventurous, and you won’t want to leave this world, harsh and unforgiving as it is.

title: Recursion: A Novel
author: Blake Crouch
description: Have you ever had a memory or recalled an event that feels like it came from another person’s life? Blake Crouch (Dark Matter, the Wayward Pines trilogy) takes that premise and just runs with it. What begins as a detective thriller about a case of false memory syndrome takes a hard turn that will leave your head spinning in the most wonderful ways. Crouch’s writing is fast, packed with smash cuts, quick dialogue and very distinct visuals. There’s almost no fat, and it feels intended to become a bingeable TV series. (And whaddya know – it’s headed to Netflix.)

title: The Raven Tower
author: Ann Leckie
description: Inspired by Hamlet, Ann Leckie’s foray into fantasy turns the story on its head: Told to the protagonist, Eolo, by an ancient and vengeful god, The Raven Tower spins out its mysteries before gleefully setting fire to them in the last few pages. Leckie has a gift for using language both as worldbuilding and as plot device, and here the use of second-person creates an effect that’s both intimate and distancing — Eolo’s motivations and inner state remain opaque to us, even as we realize that the narrator’s close interest in him is not entirely trustworthy or benign.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Daisy Jones & The Six: A Novel
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
description: Daisy Jones & The Six tells the story of how one of the most iconic (but sadly fictional) rock bands of the ‘70s came together – and how it mysteriously fell apart. Daisy Jones is a Los Angeles wild child; Billy Dunne has moved to the city in hopes of making it big with his band. Through mostly oral histories, the tumultuous relationship between the two singer-songwriters becomes clear, along with their individual struggles with drugs, sex and rock ‘n’ roll. You can’t actually play the soundtrack to this novel, but you’ll want to, just as much as you’ll find yourself wishing that Daisy Jones & The Six were a real band.

title: The Ten Thousand Doors Of January
author: Alix E. Harrow
description: The Ten Thousand Doors of January is a book to lose yourself in. In it, you’ll find January Scaller, a young girl living in New England with her father’s employer, Mr. Locke. As her father travels across the world searching for treasures for Locke, January finds a book full of adventures like the ones she longs to go on herself. This is a book about books, doors to other worlds, magic, the power of written words and love. The escape that Alix E. Harrow offers is one that you’ll still think about long after you turn the last page.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters"]

title: Furious Hours: Murder, Fraud, And The Last Trial Of Harper Lee
author: Casey Cep
description: Long after Harper Lee’s success with To Kill a Mockingbird, the author found a true crime story that she would spend years trying to make her second act. An Alabama preacher accused of killing five of his family members for insurance money is himself killed at one of the funerals – and the lawyer who helped the preacher collect the insurance money ends up representing his killer. Lee chronicles the murder trial in the hope of telling a tale to rival her friend Truman Capote’s In Cold Blood, which she had helped research. In three parts, Casey Cep presents the facts of the story as well as Lee likely wished she herself could, along with the best look at what happened to Lee after fame found her.

title: The Heartbeat Of Wounded Knee: Native America From 1890 To The Present
author: David Treuer
description: In Dee Brown’s 1970 book, Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee, the author – not Native American, and with seemingly little connection to any current tribes – declared that “the culture and civilization of the American Indian was destroyed” during the late 1800s. Not so fast, says David Treuer. He calls his book a “counternarrative” to Brown’s bestseller, with its story of U.S. government betrayal, forced relocation and massacres. In this fine and timely work, Treuer, a member of the Ojibwe Tribe, says Native Americans have not only maintained their culture and civilization through some dark years, but in some parts of the country today are thriving. The heartbeat is strong.

title: Range: Why Generalists Triumph In A Specialized World
author: David J. Epstein
description: In Range, former ProPublica investigative and science reporter David Epstein argues that the theory of specialization – that one needs 10,000 hours to master a skill – popularized by Malcolm Gladwell, is the wrong advice. What benefits people in most circumstances is not extremely high competence in one area but a diversity of experience and ability, he writes. In other words, range. Epstein stories range (get it?) from an appropriately wide berth of fields – from athletics to the sciences to chess – to make his point. Range is a blueprint for a more thoughtful, collaborative world – and it’s also really fun to read.

title: The Heartland: An American History
author: Kristin L. Hoganson
description: The Midwest, in the center of America, occupies a central place in the American political discourse. How can politicians win Midwesterners’ votes? What do they think? Everyone, allegedly, wants to know. The Heartland: An American History argues, by way of example, that those are the wrong questions. Author Kristin Hoganson writes an engaging, thorough history of the region as a big player in all the things people suggest it’s not, including international trade and fast-paced change. She also shares archival newspaper articles, old-timey livestock names and other colorful bits that make it (perhaps unexpectedly) an exciting read.

title: Underground: A Human History Of The Worlds Beneath Our Feet
author: Will Hunt
description: Space, the unknown above, is a subject of fascination for millions of people. In Underground, Will Hunt demonstrates that a world nearly as exciting is just below our feet. He takes readers through tunnels under Paris, into the world’s oldest mine on aboriginal land in Australia – and beyond. Perhaps the book’s best feature is how seriously Hunt takes his responsibility to reckon with the history of each place he visits. It’s also full of beautiful pictures. There’s plenty for those who are brave enough to adventure on their own and those, like me, who would prefer to read about someone else doing it instead.

title: How To Be An Antiracist
author: Ibram X. Kendi
description: Maybe it’s the contrarian in me, but I’ve rarely embraced “how to” books. Ibram X. Kendi’s How to Be an Antiracist is such an exception that I may have to reconsider. It’s a lofty title and an equally ambitious goal, but Kendi really does guide the reader through the process of becoming an antiracist. His success may come partly from the book also being a memoir that draws from his own missteps down the path of antiracism, from his earliest years through adulthood. Certain to both challenge and reinforce the views of many, this book is one to read.

title: Survival Math: Notes On An All-American Family
author: Mitchell S. Jackson
description: Easily the most compelling memoir I’ve read in a decade, Mitchell S. Jackson’s Survival Math: Notes on an All-American Family is a brilliant exploration of growing up black in one of the whitest cities in the country – and the strategies the author’s family and community have employed to survive. Jackson takes an unflinching look at himself and those around him, while remaining attentive to the forces that have shaped his community since its founding. While it is at times heart-wrenching and at others heartwarming, Survival Math, throughout, is marked by its abundance of beauty and grace.

title: The Source Of Self-Regard: Selected Essays, Speeches, And Meditations
author: Toni Morrison
description: Her last published work before her death, Toni Morrison’s The Source of Self-Regard gathers selected essays, speeches and meditations from the last four decades. Morrison may have been best known for her fiction, but this collection makes clear why she was also a highly regarded public intellectual. Despite the span over which the pieces were written, the book is almost uncomfortably timely, with Morrison turning her penetrating analysis on issues such as migration, racism, sexism, classism and fascism. Her final gift to the reading public, in which her trademark lyricism is on full display, is undoubtedly one to treasure.

title: Hollow Kingdom
author: Kira Jane Buxton
description: Kira Jane Buxton takes a joyfully original approach to apocalyptic fiction in this debut: Instead of us humans being the focal point in the story of our own extinction, it’s the plethora of life that we leave behind that takes center stage. Narrated by S.T., a fast-food-loving domesticated crow, the novel follows him and his droopy dog companion, Dennis, as they try to survive a world where humans have gone berserk and begin to die out. Slowly, S.T. learns that wild animals are just as creative, resourceful and lively as the humans he so loves.

title: The Archive Of Alternate Endings
author: Lindsey Dragger
description: Halley’s comet passes Earth once every 75 years or so, and its appearance serves as the organizing principle in this gorgeously written and haunting novel that spans several centuries of sightings. With characters like Johannes Gutenberg, the Brothers Grimm and Edmond Halley himself making occasional appearances, this circuitous, atmospheric book is all about the telling of tales, the passing-on of knowledge, stories’ shifting and mutating nature and how they can disappear into the black hole of unrecorded history, if allowed to. Both tragic and hopeful, it speaks to how human storytelling really is. Read it, read it, read it.
tags: ["History", "Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Go Ahead In The Rain: Notes To A Tribe Called Quest
author: Hanif Abdurraqib
description: Hanif Abdurraqib’s Go Ahead in the Rain is a brilliant meditation on race, music and writing. It’s also a history of hip-hop and an extended reflection on one of hip-hop’s most legendary groups: A Tribe Called Quest. Abdurraqib synthesizes the personal and the critical as he writes bracingly about the ambitious aesthetic of Tribe’s music and his personal affinity with the contrasting personas of the group’s principal members: the vulnerable, dynamic Phife Dawg and the cool, visionary Q-Tip. I loved Go Ahead in the Rain because it’s about what it means to take refuge in music and also what it means to break out of that refuge through music.

title: The Undying: Pain, Vulnerability, Mortality, Medicine, Art, Time, Dreams, Data, Exhaustion, Cancer, And Care
author: Anne Boyer
description: The Undying chronicles poet Anne Boyer’s experience in treatment for aggressive breast cancer as a 41-year-old single working mother. In a lyrical style that blends memoir, mythology and philosophy, Boyer argues that suffering from cancer is largely man-made, resulting not from the disease process itself but instead from the gendered social rituals surrounding it – and the deeply broken, inequitable health care system in America. This is not your mother’s breast cancer narrative; it refuses peppy “pinkwashed” optimism, yet still offers a sense of comfort to readers who have experienced illness by reminding us we aren’t alone.

title: Camgirl
author: Isa Mazzei
description: From the screenwriter of Netflix’s surreal horror flick Cam comes this candid, juicy memoir of Isa Mazzei’s experience working in the online sex industry. As her seductive alter ego “Una,” the author staged erotic digital performances to earn money and explore her own sexual longings. Camgirl culminates with revelations about childhood trauma and the challenges of sustaining a bifurcated identity. The book offers insights into the formidable physical and emotional labor demanded of adult entertainers, as well as the ways that power and desire are intertwined for young women – all recounted in Mazzei’s raw, surprisingly comedic voice.

title: A Memory Called Empire
author: Arkady Martine
description: Do you like political intrigue, high stakes and the constant self-interrogation of your love for a beautiful thing that’s destroying you even as it defines you? This is the book for you. Ambassador Mahit Dzmare has long been fascinated by the Teixcalaanli Empire, which is threatening to consume her home, Lsel Station — and now she gets to see it in person, armed only with her wits and the faulty mental copy of the previous ambassador embedded in her brain stem. (The previous ambassador died under mysterious circumstances, but that’s probably fine.) Gorgeous, supple prose and stunning world-building make this Byzantium-in-Space novel shine.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Bird King
author: G. Willow Wilson
description: Fatima, a royal concubine in the last days of Muslim Granada, and Hassan, a queer map-maker with strange powers, escape the palace together before it surrenders to Christian Castile — but an Inquisitor named Luz pursues them as they try to find their way to the fabled island of the Bird King. The Bird King is the story of a journey, but it’s also a journey into story — and faith and refuge, the family we choose and the friends we find. It’s deeply beautiful and wondrously sad, and one of the most beautifully written books I read this year.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: The Haunting Of Tram Car 015
author: P. Djèlí Clark
description: A charming romp through a lovingly realized setting. In 1879, Egypt overthrew British rule with the help of a sudden influx of djinn; in 1912, Cairo’s modernity is a mixture of magic and technology. Against this backdrop, agents Hamed Nasr and Onsi Youssef represent the Ministry of Alchemy, Enchantments, and Supernatural Entities, investigating untoward magical occurrences and abuses. When the Superintendent of Tram Safety and Maintenance calls them in to deal with the titular haunted tram car, they’re both bemused – but taking the case leads them on a merry chase through their city and alternate approaches to magic and philosophy. Delightful!
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Palestine +100: Stories from a Century After the Nakba
author: Basma Ghalayini (editor)
description: Memory and imagination are contested territories in this deeply moving collection. Twelve Palestinian authors write stories set 100 years after the Nakba — Arabic for “catastrophe” – during which, as editor Basma Ghalayini writes in her introduction, “Israel declared itself a new-born state on the rubble of Palestinian lives.” Beyond their shared premise, the stories are inventive and dextrous, as brilliant as they are painful — and playful. As often as I wept, I laughed at witty turns of phrase, surprising goofiness, shrugging resilience. There’s also a remarkable compassion for Israelis, for the Israeli experience of being settler-colonists, the psychological damage that entails. Warm and powerful.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Disappearing Earth: A Novel
author: Julia Phillips
description: Kamchatka, Russia – and its main city of Petropavlovsk – forms the backdrop to a stunning debut novel from Julia Phillips, Disappearing Earth. When two young sisters are lured into a strange man’s car and disappear, their community wants to find out why; through voices of the women who make up that community, we discover why they may never do so. A fierce indictment of a world – not just a region – that continues to devalue all things female.

title: Heaven, My Home
author: Attica Locke
description: Caddo Lake, a sprawling, irregular inland sea divided between Texas and Louisiana, has long been home to people looking to “hide in plain sight,” as one character in Attica Locke’s new Highway 59 novel, Heaven, My Home, remarks. African American Texas Ranger Darren Matthews is ambivalent about the East Texas home he has returned to after being educated in the North and Midwest. When he’s called to investigate a boy’s disappearance, he discovers that class can blind you are surely as racism can.

title: The Sentence Is Death: A Novel
author: Anthony Horowitz
description: Anthony Horowitz – well-known for, among other things, his Sherlock Holmes follow-on novels The House of Silk and Moriarty – returns with his second Inspector Hawthorne mystery, The Sentence Is Death. The narrator, “Anthony Horowitz,” a writer whose CV is exactly analogous to his nonfictional creator’s, teams up reluctantly with the somewhat shady Daniel Hawthorne to solve a murder involving a bottle of wine and a long marital feud. With just enough intrigue about Hawthorne’s past and present connections and loads of British place names, this novel satisfies like a bracing cup of tea.

title: Fumbled
author: Alexa Martin
description: Fiercely independent Poppy Patterson is a waitress at the Emerald Cabaret, a Denver nightclub with a steakhouse on one floor and aerial acts on another. But Poppy’s most important job is that of a single mom to 9-year-old son Ace. And Ace’s father is Denver Mustangs wide receiver Trevor Kyle “TK” Moore – a secret neither Ace nor TK knows, until a chance encounter changes everything. Alexa Martin – who’s married to a former NFL player – offers an authentic look into the world of pro ball, from the paparazzi to the agents to the dangers of concussion. Whether you love or hate the sport, Fumbled is a second-chance romance that delivers as many laugh-out-loud moments as heartfelt sighs – all in the name of love.

title: Rebel
author: Beverly Jenkins
description: Valinda Lacy is a Northerner who has come to New Orleans in the aftermath of the Civil War to help the city’s newly emancipated community. Captain Drake LeVeq is a physically formidable hulk of a man from an influential family with a long history in the area. Valinda has a fiancé and Drake a mistress, but their attraction to each other is immediate and unstoppable, challenging them both to make bold choices. Rebel is the first novel in Beverly Jenkins’ Women Who Dare series, and it blends history and romance for a gripping tale of a strong-willed, determined woman and a champion of a man.

title: The Shanghai Free Taxi: Journeys With The Hustlers And Rebels Of The New China
author: Frank Langfitt
description: NPR correspondent Frank Langfitt has covered China and other countries for nearly two decades. Everything he learned about China – and about building compelling narratives – can be found in The Shanghai Free Taxi. It’s a nonfiction gem that offers readers a mixture of politics, history, action, crime and humor, all adding up to a superb chronicle of China as it adapts to changes both internal and global.

title: An American Summer: Love And Death In Chicago
author: Alex Kotlowitz
description: The gun debate in this country tends to focus on what needs to be done about guns, instead of on those who have lost their lives or been affected by gun violence. Writer and filmmaker Alex Kotlowitz’s An American Summer reintroduces the human elements into the debate. Instead of discussing politics, Kotlowitz deftly writes about the brutal, blood-soaked streets of Chicago and digs into the lives of those touched by violence, both as victims and culprits. Maddening, heartbreaking and strangely full of hope, An American Summer rehumanizes the gun debate by putting names and faces to the violence we see on TV.

title: Juliet The Maniac: A Novel
author: Juliet Escoria
description: In Juliet the Maniac, the apocalypse is an internal problem. Juliet is a young woman suffering from depression; she feels alienated and has twice attempted suicide. In the aftermath, she’s stuck in a psychiatric institution, and her life keeps deteriorating as she struggles with unhelpful medication and her need to understand herself. Walking a fine line between autobiography and fiction, this is a gloomy narrative about mental illness that slowly transforms into a story about discovering hope when there is none, allowing ourselves to believe in redemption and finding ways to heal.

title: Night Boat To Tangier: A Novel
author: Kevin Barry
description: The plot of Kevin Barry’s Night Boat to Tangier is simple: Two old Irish drug smugglers, Maurice and Charlie, spend a night in the dark waiting room of a ferry terminal in the Spanish port of Algeciras, waiting for Maurice’s estranged daughter. And waiting leads to talking – about their lives of crime, lost loves, living in exile and the violence they’ve experienced. Told with incredible economy of language and a healthy dose of black humor, Night Boat to Tangier is a deliciously dark comedy about two unforgettable thugs who are also philosophers and longtime friends — and who are funny as hell.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Dark"]

title: Wanderers: A Novel
author: Chuck Wendig
description: The post-apocalyptic genre is thriving, but only a few books in every decade become part of the post-apoc canon. Chuck Wendig’s Wanderers is one of those special ones that join the likes of Cormac McCarthy’s The Road and Stephen King’s The Stand. One day, people just start walking, mindlessly, unstoppably – is it an illness? A terrorist attack? The end of the world? No one knows, and the uncertainty sends the country spinning into bigotry, mayhem and murder. Powerful and political without being preachy, Wanderers is a must-read for fans of the genre.

title: Solitary: Unbroken By Four Decades In Solitary Confinement
author: Albert Woodfox
description: Solitary made me angry. Albert Woodfox spent four decades in prison, many of those years in solitary confinement. He suffered racist abuse, deprivation, neglect and extreme isolation – and now he’s telling the world about it. While it works as a biography, Solitary is also a book about how racism has corrupted our legal system and how prisons are places where bigotry is sometimes allowed to flourish. This book should be read by everyone, and everyone should be angry about what Woodfox went through. Angry enough to make sure it never happens again.

title: What Doesn't Kill You Makes You Blacker: A Memoir In Essays
author: Damon Young
description: Damon Young, co-founder and editor-in-chief of Very Smart Brothas, isn’t afraid to tell the truth about his experiences as a black man living in the United States. In the essays that make up What Doesn’t Kill You Makes You Blacker, Young discusses everything from growing up waiting to be called the N-word to black masculinity, barbershop drama and his financial struggles. With his straightforward, witty, heartfelt prose, Young has created a special book that offers a look into a unique set of experiences that have universal resonance.

title: A Friend Is A Gift You Give Yourself: A Novel
author: William Boyle
description: Noir is alive and well, and William Boyle is one of the new masters of the genre. A Friend Is a Gift You Give Yourself is a quintessential New York City noir novel that subverts the tropes of the genre by putting women at the center of the narrative and ignoring guns in favor of focusing on feelings and the ways the past influences the present. Told with superb economy of language and packed with New York trivia, this is a deep, funny story about running away and finding yourself that features a Brooklyn mob widow, an estranged daughter and a Golden Age porn star.

title: Black Souls
author: Gioacchino Criaco, translated by Hillary Gulley
description: Black Souls tells the story of three childhood friends in Calabria’s Aspromonte mountains who turn to crime to climb out of the poverty they were born into. Like many other Mafia novels, this one is packed with violence and crime. But Gioacchino Criaco has a great understanding of the psychogeography of crime and treats his characters – who aren’t far removed from his own experiences – with humanity. The novel is also deeply immersed in Calabrian culture, showing readers a ruthless world in which people with good hearts know that crime is the only path to a better life.

title: Floating Coast: An Environmental History Of The Bering Strait
author: Bathsheba Demuth
description: Nature is inextricable from politics. People are inextricable from nature. The Arctic is inextricable from everything. In Floating Coast: An Environmental History of the Bering Strait, the Arctic comes alive with detail amid dramatic shifts – in seasons, in nature, in politics. The triumph of this lyrical history is how Bathsheba Demuth pulls numerous threads into a net from which neither whales nor our children can escape. Complex and devastating, Floating Coast can read as a eulogy, but it’s also a deeply studied book that traces a complex history, notes what faces us now and dares us to imagine better.
tags: ["History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Science", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Plaza: The Secret Life Of America's Most Famous Hotel
author: Julie Satow
description: It all comes back to The Plaza. No other New York edifice so directly reflects the city itself – the shift from old money to new business, the strained relationship between developers and unions, globalization, and every generation’s interpretations of the past. Through personal interviews and historical accounts, Julie Satow embraces the chaos of its history. The details are always dramatic, though it’s impossible to ignore the distance between backroom billionaires and expendable employees. Still, nostalgia about the Plaza always manages to turn its excess into excitement, and the fact that it survived hard times endears the hotel even to those who can only dream of staying there.

title: Elizabethan Globalism: England, China And The Rainbow Portrait
author: Matthew Dimmock
description: The Rainbow Portrait of Queen Elizabeth I has enough mythmaking character design to make a comic book superhero jealous, and yet it still retains its mystery. Part history, part art book, Elizabethan Globalism: England, China and the Rainbow Portrait offers up in-depth scholarship and a museum exhibit’s worth of historical artifacts, illuminating the world behind the painting: the queen’s growing power, England’s relationship with the Ottoman Empire, its fascination with China and issues of fashion, culture and trade. It’s a historical treasure hunt that shows how a single artifact contains underpinnings of an empire and that offers fascinating insight into how political myths are made.

title: A Woman Of No Importance: The Untold Story Of The American Spy Who Helped Win World War II
author: Sonia Purnell
description: As an American woman with a disability, Virginia Hall was an unlikely choice for British spy work. But by 1940 she was in France, with only the barest brief – build a resistance, no matter what. Sonia Purnell’s A Woman of No Importance is a gripping account of how Hall defied all expectations as she built her network and how she fought obstacles at home after the war. The book smooths a staggering cast and a complex timeline into a brisk narrative, balancing the story of the resistance that eventually made victory possible with the life of a remarkable spy who risked it all.
tags: ["History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Season: A Social History Of The Debutante
author: Kristen Richardson
description: Don’t let the evening gloves fool you – The Season is a tense history of social change. Much of debutante tradition arose from attempts to preserve narratives of power and culture and deliberately excluded families on the basis of religion, race and class. Luckily, Kristen Richardson has a wry touch with even the most mercenary moments. Though the practice of social debuts is clearly loaded, the book offers a humanizing glimpse behind the very careful performances being given at very fraught balls. Debuting is a tug of war among money, race, class, culture and tradition, and The Season makes sparkling work of it.

title: Black Card: A Novel
author: Chris L. Terry
description: The premise of Chris L. Terry’s Black Card is simple: It’s a novel that draws from the author’s experiences as a mixed-race youth existing in two overlapping and not always welcoming worlds – the punk rock scene and the South. From there, though, this wildly entertaining look at racism veers into satire, poignancy and plenty of insight into all the ways America finds to divide itself.

title: Supernova Era
author: Cixin Liu, translated by Joel Martinsen
description: Hugo Award winner Cixin Liu had the science fiction world spellbound with his Remembrance of Earth’s Past trilogy, but Supernova Era is something different. A standalone novel from early in the Chinese author’s career, it imagines a world where adults have gone extinct and preadolescents are forced to grow up overnight and save civilization from disappearing. The concept is profound, and Liu executes it with agile twists and a heart full of hope for humanity
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: The Grand Dark
author: Richard Kadrey
description: “For the patrons,” writes Richard Kadrey, speaking of the eponymous, decadent theater at the heart of his latest fantasy novel, “entering the Grand Dark was a gleeful surrender, a leap down the gullet of an alluring monster.” The same can be said of The Grand Dark itself. It’s a sumptuous tale of street-level intrigue and sprawling socioeconomic drama set in a fictional, Weimar Republic-like city that takes on a life of its own – as do most Kadrey creations.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: The Ruin Of Kings
author: Jenn Lyons
description: One of the most immersive new fantasy series of 2019 is Jenn Lyons’ A Chorus of Dragons. The first book in the series, The Ruin of Kings, establishes the empire of Quuros, a place where the magical Academy helps protect the citizenry from the threat of monsters that abound in the land – up to and including dragons. From that basic layout, Lyons constructs an epic architecture of plot, characters and world building that’s given plenty of fans distraction in the absence of Game of Thrones.

title: A Spectral Hue
author: Craig Laurance Gidney
description: Craig Laurance Gidney’s previous fiction and poetry have shown a daring openness when it comes to genre, and A Spectral Hue is cut from similar cloth. In one sense, it’s a quiet, confidently told novel of identity – gay, black, artistic and ancestral – that resonates on a wholly realistic level. In another, it’s a hybrid of horror, folklore, dark fantasy and magical realism that whispers and twists through the lush, imaginary city of Shimmer, Md., a place where the supernatural seems woven into the air itself.

title: Magic For Liars: A Novel
author: Sarah Gailey
description: Academies of magic have been fertile ground for many authors since before Harry Potter seized the collective imagination. Few authors, however, have pulled off a magic-school story with the flair of Sarah Gailey. Magic for Liars is a novel rife with mystery, rich emotion and, of course, academic magic that never fails to dazzle with its inventiveness and quirky verve. Even if you feel this kind of setting has been done to death, you’ll find something fresh and fantastic in Gailey’s lively brainchild.

title: A Song For A New Day
author: Sarah Pinsker
description: Rock music and speculative fiction have a long history together – Emma Bull’s classic fantasy novel War for the Oaks springs to mind. But Sarah Pinsker has upped the volume with A Song for a New Day. The Nebula Award-winning author delivers a dystopian tale of political suppression, virtual reality and outlaw musicians that examines why we create and how vital that is in a world gone mad.

title: We Went To The Woods: A Novel
author: Caite Dolan-Leach
description: Twenty-something Mackenzie – calling herself Mack – flees her suburban upbringing to join a kind of millennial experiment, a cloud-cuckoo-land commune in the Finger Lakes region of upstate New York. She sees herself and the other 20-something communards as “survivalists,” determined to live off the grid. “We were abuzz,” she enthuses – and variations on ecstatic togetherness recur, forming delightful tableaus or ominous danger signs, depending on the reader’s level of cynicism. A sharp, spellbinding cautionary tale, the novel reminds us that even those who do remember the past might also wind up repeating it.

title: Home In The Woods
author: Eliza Wheeler
description: It took one page of Eliza Wheeler’s Home in the Woods to make me cry. After her father dies, 6-year-old Marvel, her seven young siblings and their mother face the Great Depression on their own. Evicted from their home, they find a dilapidated tar paper shack in the woods and set about to make it a home. Following Marvel through a year of deprivation, hard work and ingenuity, Home in the Woods is a heartfelt love letter to our families who came before us and to putting family before ourselves. Wheeler’s illustrations are a revelation, using tint and saturation to evoke seasons of plenty and want. A poignant and personal story that will resound with any of us whose family stories include almost unimaginable hardship. (For ages 5 to 8)

title: Sunny Rolls The Dice
author: Jennifer L. Holm, illustrated by Matthew Holm
description: For any parent who wants to impart the magic of the 1970s to their children (I do, with a vengeance), the Sunny series is a great way to start. Summer is over, and Sunny has finally reached middle school: 1970s middle school. Into the world of Gloria Vanderbilt jeans (not Sunny’s style) and teen magazines (spoiler – Sunny fails the quizzes), comes Dungeons and Dragons in the wood-paneled basement, and Sunny is hooked. While the ‘70s references may be a bit hard for some kids to navigate, middle school struggles are universal. And so is Lip Smackers lip gloss. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Curse Of The Werepenguin
author: Allan Woodrow, ilustrated by Scott Brown
description: Imagine Mary Shelley had a preoccupation with zoos, a sense of humor and bad allergies, then commiserated with Roald Dahl and Mel Brooks to write a story for upper elementary school kids, and you would have The Curse of the Werepenquin. Bolt Wattle’s trek from his dismal orphanage to mysterious Brugaria to meet (he thinks) his mother and father leads him straight to … 12-year-old Baron Chordata? And the prospect of becoming a werepenguin himself. It’s funny, it’s ridiculous, it’s solidly spooky without being too scary and it leaves me with the impression that stale French bread may be a pretty good weapon against the Greatest Bandit in the World. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: The Last Last-Day-Of-Summer
author: Lamar Giles, illustrated by Dapo Adeola
description: Move over, Encyclopedia Brown, and make way for Otto and Sheed Alston. Lamar Giles, master of young adult mysteries and thrillers, finally brings us a book for middle-grade readers. Already legendary detectives in their small Virginia town, the Alston boys dismally face their last day of summer vacation, which, considering they have been inadvertently tricked into freezing time, ends up being the longest day of their lives. A gumshoe adventure with a sci-fi twist, The Last Last-Day-of-Summer is a fast-moving, highly entertaining read. (For ages 10 to 12)

title: Tight
author: Torrey Maldonado
description: Sixth-grader Bryan makes me nervous. Normally quiet and a bit introverted, Bryan has a new friend, Mike, and he’s bad news. With a home life that leaves him vulnerable to Mike’s bad influence, Bryan has a real struggle on his hands. Oh, Bryan, please don’t jump that turnstile! Tight is written so realistically that I could not help but wring my hands over Bryan’s choices. Relevant and relatable but not at all preachy, Tight is a good reminder of how hard it is to grow up – and makes me want to hug all the middle schoolers out there (and give them all after-school jobs so they don’t get into trouble). (For ages 10 and up)

title: Space Invaders: A Novel
author: Nona Fernández, translated by Natasha Wimmer
description: The Chilean novelist and playwright Nona Fernández’s Space Invaders, translated by Natasha Wimmer, is as addictive as the video game whose name it borrows. Space Invaders blinks by in short, spare chapters, each narrated communally by a group of primary school classmates who grew up during Gen. Augusto Pinochet’s right-wing dictatorship. Their valiant, doomed efforts to make sense of the political violence they witnessed in childhood are moving and haunting and will linger long after the book is done.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Lady In The Lake: A Novel
author: Laura Lippman
description: Laura Lippman has created something special with this novel: a mystery that manages to be deliciously discursive without ever losing its driving narrative momentum. Lippman accomplishes this by telling the story through a clever mix of voices – a third-person window on her protagonist Madeline “Maddie” Schwartz, a Jewish housewife who abandons her purportedly idyllic existence in suburban 1960s Baltimore to become a reporter; first-person interludes from Cleo Sherman, the sardonic ghost of the young black woman whose mysterious but ignored death Maddie decides to look into; and, in Lippman’s coup de grace, one-off first-person chapters from the perspective of the wide array of characters Maddie encounters throughout her investigation. The result is a richly nuanced portrait of Baltimore in a time of upheaval that also happens to be a page-turning mystery with a deeply satisfying conclusion.

title: When We Were Arabs: A Jewish Family’s Forgotten History
author: Massoud Hayoun
description: When We Were Arabs is an absorbing family history that spans continents and epochs. Author Massoud Hayoun is a member of the Arab diaspora – and along with his Moroccan, Egyptian and Tunisian heritage, he’s also Jewish. Using his grandparents’ stories, he illuminates the fading history of a once thriving Arab Jewish community, in the process delivering a scathing indictment of colonialism. When We Were Arabs is a nostalgic celebration of a rich, diverse tradition, wrapped around a prayer for a time and place where Jews and Muslims can rejoice in their commonalities.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "History", "Biography & Memoir"]

title: Here We Are: American Dreams, American Nightmares
author: Aaarti Namdev Shahani
description: Aarti Namdev Shahani, who until recently reported on Silicon Valley for NPR, has written a riveting memoir of her personal success, set against the backdrop of her close-knit family’s contorted, painful path to citizenship. After her father’s arrest and imprisonment, the family discovered that American justice is neither just nor colorblind. Even as Shahani watches her father get crushed beneath the system, she grows and evolves as a daughter. If you’re moved by frequent calls to deport criminal or undocumented immigrants and refugees, please read Here We Are. For to “migrate to America … is the boldest act of one’s life.”

title: The Yellow House: A Memoir
author: Sarah M. Broom
description: The titular yellow house anchors Sarah M. Broom’s memoir about her large and complex family. Broom maps her parents’ lives, the forgotten neighborhood off Chef Menteur Highway where she grew up and Hurricane Katrina’s onslaught. She writes with intellect, breadth and feeling about the relentless divestment of wealth from African Americans, our government’s failure to protect our poor and the papering over of systemic problems by politicians. With a mother who never flags and a young woman who circumnavigates the world in search of self and identity, The Yellow House is also about grit, love and family.

title: Thick: And Other Essays
author: Tressie McMillan Cottom
description: In well-crafted, succinct language, Tressie McMillan Cottom delivers wide-ranging essays that rip the lid off commonly held racist tropes. She interrogates subjects that absorb us, but that we don’t often question, such as the meaning of beauty, the steep price of status symbols for black people and the slippery wiliness of whiteness. Cottom proves her independence as a scholar, public intellectual and creative thinker. What she unveils isn’t pretty and isn’t meant to be, but her sense of humor and lack of pretension brings grace and clarity to her message.

title: Any Old Diamonds
author: KJ Charles
description: In this historical heist romance, London-based illustrator Alec Pyne hires two notorious thieves to relieve the Duke and Duchess of Ilvar of a diamond parure at an upcoming house party for … reasons. In order to pull off the heist, Alec and one of the thieves, Jerry Crozier, must bond so they can present themselves as friends. Friendship, seduction, romance and a devilish mystery ensue. It seems impossible that this pair should find their happily ever after along with that diamond parure, but of course they do.

title: Mrs. Martin's Incomparable Adventure
author: Courtney Milan
description: Mrs. Bertrice Martin is “some seventy-three years young,” incredibly wealthy and plagued by her Terrible Nephew. Violetta Beauchamps is 69, impoverished and just the ally Bertrice needs to evict Terrible Nephew from her rooming house. They embark on one delightfully outrageous and hysterical scheme after another – and in the process find love, pleasure and a deep connection with each other. There’s no doubt these two old women will live happily ever after together. Terrible Nephew? Not so much!

title: Evvie Drake Starts Over: A Novel
author: Linda Holmes
description: Linda Holmes, host of NPR’s Pop Culture Happy Hour, brings all of her formidable talent to this sparkling fiction debut. A year after her emotionally abusive husband died, Evvie Drake still hasn’t told anyone that she was about to leave him. Meanwhile, Dean Tenney’s pitching career with the Yankees has been derailed by a case of the yips (a fascinating condition in which athletes lose fine motor skills). When Dean moves into Evvie’s big, empty house, sparks fly – and so does witty banter. This hugely satisfying romantic comedy thoroughly delivers on both the romance and the comedy – and doesn’t shy away from difficult personal issues. It’s pure enjoyment from start to finish.

title: The Wolf And The Watchman: A Novel
author: Niklas Natt och Dag
description: Niklas Natt och Dag’s gritty, historical mystery follows two men trying to find the person who committed a sadistic murder in 18th century Stockholm. The book excels at sensory description – you’ll be able to smell the stench of the tenements as you read this book – and keeps you enthralled as it switches points of view and intertwines the characters. Perfect for fans of Perfume: The Story of a Murderer.

title: Fleishman Is In Trouble: A Novel
author: Taffy Brodesser-Akner
description: Fleishman Is in Trouble is ostensibly the story of embittered divorce-to-be, Toby, but through a magic trick of narration, Toby’s story is presented by Libby, his old friend who begins as a shadow on the page but gradually asserts her own perspective while laying out his. Through these layered narratives we begin to see Rachel, Toby’s absent, much maligned wife, take shape. Taffy Brodesser-Akner’s writing is sharp-eyed and attentive – each page is littered with precise observations of modern life that are funny and sometimes chilling in their accuracy. It’s a book to be gobbled and enjoyed, certainly, but its insights are profound. In the end, though Toby Fleishman is the center and title of the book, he is not its subject – a thought experiment in empathy that every person in a relationship ought to try.

title: Lost Children Archive: A Novel
author: Valeria Luiselli
description: There is an impressive and moving body of storytelling that has accumulated in the wake of the crisis of migrant children – lost, separated, incarcerated – at the U.S.-Mexico border. But if you’re interested in the role that fiction can play, you must read Valeria Luiselli’s marvelous book Lost Children Archive. It’s structured as a road trip novel. A journalist, her husband and two children head toward the border to document the ongoing crisis. Her own family is in crisis as well – the marriage is unhappy. With her children sleeping in the back seat, a resonant corollary to the lost children she is chronicling, the narrator is preoccupied with how she will tell the story. Can an author make something out of the horror of these childrens’ experiences, or is it a kind of theft from children that have already had so much stolen from them? And as readers, can we read about this cruelty without indulging in sanctimony before we shut the book and go back to our own lives and children?

title: The Dutch House: A Novel
author: Ann Patchett
description: Over the holidays, it’s a special pleasure to read about families with different dysfunctions than the people serving the mashed potatoes at your own table. For this season, I submit that Ann Patchett’s novel, The Dutch House, will happily fulfill your requirements. The book contains all of the best elements of the family drama – a house too gorgeous not to be haunted, resourceful orphans, a wicked stepmother. Told by one of the orphaned siblings expelled from their childhood home, it’s a book with many of the elements of a fairy tale, but with real suspense and characters that live on the page. Patchett executes the story with so much surety that you hardly notice that her writing is virtually flawless.

title: Cantoras: A Novel
author: Carolina De Robertis
description: 2019 was a rich year for characters who have often been erased from literature – because of race, gender, ethnicity or sexual orientation. One of the best of these novels is Carolina de Robertis’ gorgeous novel Cantoras, which tells the story of five queer Uruguayan women who find each other in a small village at the height of the military dictatorship there in the mid-70s. The words gay, lesbian and queer are not even in their vocabulary in the early days – they’ve been erased, but they can still be punished for who they are, an essential tyranny that hangs over the novel. But Cantoras is, at its heart, a close character study of the women themselves – they are precisely drawn in all their virtues and flaws as they fall in and out of love and try to live their lives under the constant threat of possible violence. It’s that sharply observed character study that carries this novel, and is a fitting answer to years of erasure.
tags: ["History", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture"]

title: The Old Drift: A Novel
author: Namwali Serpell
