description: Namwali Serpell’s lush speculative novel The Old Drift tells the history of Zambia from the early days of colonialism through Zambian independence, touching on the Kariba Dam, the AIDS epidemic, the ill-fated Zambian space program, and forward into the age of mass surveillance and drone warfare. It’s also partly narrated by mosquitoes!
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "History", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Water Cure: A Novel
author: Sophie Mackintosh
description: It seems like lately we’ve been calling everything “toxic.” The Water Cure, a tart, uncanny debut novel by Sophie Mackintosh, tests the limits of this metaphor. A group of women live on a remote island to protect themselves from men, whom they believe to be literally toxic. It’s never clear if women on the “mainland” are really getting sick, or if the protagonists are living in a cultish separatist movement with extreme beliefs, but the novel elegantly interrogates a suddenly ubiquitous concept.

title: Know My Name: A Memoir
author: Chanel Miller
description: For four years, she has been known publicly as Emily Doe, “an unconscious woman” or simply “Brock Turner’s victim.” In her moving, sharply uncompromising new memoir Know My Name, Chanel Miller wants to set the record straight: “I am a victim, I have no qualms with this word, only with the idea that it is all that I am,” she writes. “However, I am not Brock Turner’s victim. I am not his anything.”

title: Mitz: The Marmoset Of Bloomsbury
author: Sigrid Nunez
description: We have the success of Sigrid Nunez’s National Book Award-winning novel The Friend to thank for the reissue of her rich little 1998 gem about Leonard and Virginia Woolf’s rescued pet marmoset, Mitz. Set in England during the ominous buildup to WWII, this charming biographical novel is an ode to the Bloomsbury power couple’s devotion and commitment – to each other, their work, their friends and family, their freedom and their pets, including the tiny, impossible-to-housetrain, rodent-tailed monkey who rides around in Leonard’s waistcoat pocket. All threatened by dark times to come.

title: Olive, Again: A Novel
author: Elizabeth Strout
description: Ten years after she won a Pulitzer Prize for her linked stories about Olive Kitteridge, Elizabeth Strout returns to coastal Crosby, Maine, with this worthy update about her retired but not retiring schoolteacher, a tetchy, blunt busybody who’s also capable of kindness – and self-doubt. At this point in her life, ornery Olive struggles with widowhood, loneliness, regrets over behavior that alienated her only child, snobbery and the affronts of aging – but she’s resilient, and more importantly, open to learning a lesson we should all take to heart: Empathy can radically change your outlook on the world.

title: Spring: A Novel
author: Ali Smith
description: The third volume of Ali Smith’s audacious seasonal quartet about troubling current issues focuses on the global migrant crisis and the abhorrent, dehumanizing treatment of refugees. Once again, it showcases this brilliant Scottish writer’s clever wordplay, outrage at the world’s injustices, genuine affection for people at either end of the age spectrum and insistence on the heartwarming, life-enhancing possibilities of love, decency and art.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Art"]

title: The Grammarians: A Novel
author: Cathleen Schine
description: Cathleen Schine’s latest smart, comic novel is about two language-obsessed sisters, freakishly precocious identical twins with a passion for abstruse words (such as oxters, meaning armpits) and the nitty-gritty of English usage. One grows up to become a language “scold,” the other a proponent of informal, mutating English. Like the twin advice columnists Ann Landers and Abigail Van Buren, they feud publicly in their competing columns and suffer a lengthy estrangement. Filled with fun zingers, this is a buoyant read for people who can appreciate the absurdity of phrases like “nearly ten women.” (Is that 9.5 or 9.75?)

title: The Pursuit Of William Abbey
author: Claire North
description: In colonial South Africa, in 1884, Dr. William Abbey stood by as a boy was killed at the hands of a white mob. For doing nothing to intervene, the boy’s mother cursed him, and now no matter where he goes, the spirit of the dead boy will follow him – slowly but inexorably, never stopping. And when it catches him, it will kill someone he loves. Claire North uses this brilliant setup to tell a chameleon story that is a different thing chapter to chapter, sometimes page to page. It is a horror story, a thriller, a supernatural mystery, a spy story, a heavily political indictment of capitalism and empire, a war story, a love story, a confession. But every piece of it is remarkable and comes together to make for one of the most moving, horrifying and gut-wrenching novels of the year.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Do You Dream Of Terra-Two?
author: Temi Oh
description: There’s a pulse that runs through Temi Oh’s debut novel. A living, beating heart that races and stalls, stutters and recovers. It is the conjoined pulse of six living teenagers (plus one dead one) who have been chosen from among hundreds to leave the spoiled Earth and fly away to another fresh, perfect planet far, far away. The voyage will take them 23 years. When it is done, their childhoods will be long over, and they will be the first humans to set foot on a virgin world. That’s the promise that has been made to them. But things happen between here and there that no one could’ve expected and that not everyone will survive.

title: Exhalation: Stories
author: Ted Chiang
description: A warning letter from the future. A museum exhibit card describing the mechanization of nurturing. An exploration of emergent artificial intelligence. The stories in Ted Chiang’s newest short story collection are tightly focused dramas of individuals whom the future has struck like a wave, or a bomb. And whether the “future” is tomorrow or 100 years ago (as seen from someone witnessing it from 101 years ago), it has the weight of inexorability to it. Tomorrow comes whether we want it to or not. The world changes no matter how tightly we cling to yesterday. And Chiang writes often (almost always) with an understanding that nothing we do, nothing he does, nothing any of his characters do, can change that.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Future Of Another Timeline
author: Annalee Newitz
description: The Future of Another Timeline is about a girl, Beth, who’s involved in some terrible things and a woman, Tess, who is a professional time traveler secretly trying to protect the future from the organized misogyny of a group that wants queer women dead, trans women dead and all women cowed, quiet and consigned to the kitchen while straight white men take their rightful place as rulers of all blah blah blah. Things get nasty. Get bloody. Get complicated. It’s a time travel story – there’s no other way they go. And in the end, the question becomes how much of your past are you willing to sacrifice in the name of a better future?

title: Gideon The Ninth
author: Tamsyn Muir
description: The first of a proposed trilogy, Gideon the Ninth is a freaky science-fantasy-horror-romance mashup that is as deliriously all over the map as it is wickedly smart and propulsive. The setup is genius: In some (possibly distant, possibly ancient) corner of the universe exist nine ancient houses each adept in different forms of gooey, death-obsessed magic. Representatives from each have been called together in a competition, so The Lady Harrowhark Nonagesimus, heir to the Ninth House, and her unwilling bodyguard, Gideon Nav (a smart, mean and badass orphaned girl), arrive and find that someone (or something) is killing heirs of the nine houses and that Gideon and Harrow are in terrible danger. What follows is a goofy, bloody, foul-mouthed Scooby-Doo episode full of ghosts, monsters, sword fights and bitter, terrible friendships that will gut you before you turn the last page.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Middlegame
author: Seanan McGuire
description: There is a version of Middlegame that is just the story of two childhood friends (and separated twins), Roger and Dodger, navigating youth and young adulthood, high school and college, as preternatural geniuses with a hinky psychic connection. There is another version that is about James Reed, the powerful alchemist who created them and set them on a collision course meant to change the world. And Seanan McGuire manages to fit both these stories into one skin, jumping back and forth from conspiracy to friendship and back again in a way that beautifully ratchets up the tension on both sides until, finally, everyone comes together in a bloody conclusion that both offers answers and leaves bodies on the floor.

title: All Systems Red: The Murderbot Diaries
author: Martha Wells
description: This isn’t just one book, but the first in a series of novellas (all finally released in hardcover as of this year) that follow Murderbot – a rogue SecUnit that has hacked its own governor module and given itself free will. And while the novellas present themselves as just garden-variety killer-robot sci fi, Martha Wells has hidden a remarkably human story inside her cheesy pulp architecture. There are subtexts to be read into Murderbot – that its experience is a coming-out narrative, that it mirrors the lives of trans people, immigrants, those on the autism spectrum or anyone else who feels the need to hide some essential part of themselves from a population that either threatens or can’t possibly understand them. It’s powerful to see that on the page and to recognize that we are all a little bit Murderbot too.

title: Ninth House
author: Leigh Bardugo
description: You know all those secret societies at Yale? Skull & Bones and all the rest? OK, so imagine that they can all, to a certain extent, do actual magic. That’s the hook for Leigh Bardugo’s first foray into adult fiction (after a wildly successful run in YA), and the story follows Galaxy “Alex” Stern, small-time drug dealer from California, recruited by Yale simply because she can see and talk to ghosts. She’s a girl with a past who meets a boy with a future, both of them responsible for overseeing the grimy, bloody, powerful rituals of the societies to make sure that nothing goes wrong. And guess what? Something does go very, very wrong, and it’s up to Alex to make it all right again.

title: The Violent Century
author: Lavie Tidhar
description: The world right now is full of superhero stories. In movies and on TV, in comics and graphic novels. But Lavie Tidhar, with his sharp eye for absurdity (and stellar world-building powers), gives his heroes a literary moment, bringing them to the page in a world where American, British, Russian and Nazi supermen fought in World War II and shaped everything we know of 20th and 21st century history. It begins like a classic John le Carré story, with an old, retired hero called Fogg being brought in to discuss an old case file. But where it ends up is like nothing that anyone but Tidhar could imagine.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: This Is How You Lose The Time War
author: Amal El-Mohtar and Max Gladstone
description: Seriously, what’s better than a love story between two time ninjas on opposite sides of an epic, genocidal time war? Nothing, that’s what. Unless that love story played out largely via secret messages each leaves for the other on the alien battlefields they have inhabited. Amal El-Mohtar and Max Gladstone lean way into the weird here, making an almost experimental back-and-forth narrative between their two soldiers, Red and Blue, and the result is brilliant, mind-bending and approachable all at the same time.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Growing Things And Other Stories
author: Paul Tremblay
description: Paul Tremblay writes horror stories. Weird stories. Effed the eff up stories that seem benign (sometimes) until you close your eyes or shut off the lights and feel them moving around in the dark. In his new collection, Growing Things, he writes about monsters and ghosts and monsters and nightmares and memory and monsters and the end of the world. Apocalypses large and small haunt the space between the covers. The kind that could be happening, that have happened, are just about to happen right now. They’re the kind of stories that make you want to check your phone just to make sure what you’re reading about isn’t already on the news. That make you want to look over your shoulder to make sure the monster isn’t right behind you.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Dark"]

title: The Border: A Novel
author: Don Winslow
description: The third and final piece of Don Winslow’s drug war trilogy is a massive doorstopper of a book that brings to a close the stories of Art Keller and Adán Barrera – the cop and the kingpin who’ve been at the center of this extended tale for 15 years. Operatic, visceral, full of clipped sentences and quick action, The Border is politics made into spectacle. There’s something absolutely Shakespearean about Winslow’s work – the sense of complicated, ungainly problems presented with full lights and fireworks, costumed in gold chains and polo shirts, writ hugely in the way that only fiction can be. So 300 years from now, when our children’s children’s children want to understand the defining conflict of the late 20th and early 21st centuries, there’s a fair chance that this is what they’ll read.

title: Drive Your Plow Over The Bones Of The Dead: A Novel
author: Olga Tokarczuk, translated by Antonia Lloyd-Jones
description: I originally picked up this book just because of the title. Later, I heard that Olga Tokarczuk had won the (delayed) 2018 Nobel Prize for Literature, and it made total sense because this book (originally published in 2009, released in English translation in 2019) is both deeply strange and deeply personal – a kind of noir murder mystery with feminist, leftist, vegetarian and academic undertones. Gorgeously written and immediately engaging, it is a complicated story about deer, hunters, age, infirmity, chaos and William Blake, driven by the rigorously no-BS practicality of its elderly narrator, Janina Duszejko, who is trying to figure out who is murdering all the hunters in her small, snowbound Polish village, all while contemplating the greater mysteries of life, Poland and the universe in general.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: The Shadow King: A Novel
author: Maaza Mengiste
description: Set during the Italian invasion of Ethiopia, The Shadow King does what few war stories do: It centers around the experiences of female fighters. Maaza Mengiste introduces the reader to Hirut and Aster – one a servant, the other a mistress – both constrained by the rules of their society. When they join the struggle for freedom, their personal conflicts don’t disappear; instead, they’re recast against a larger canvas, and interwoven with the perspectives of other characters on either side of the battle, including a Jewish Italian photographer who documents the fighting. Epic in scope yet intimate in detail, this novel is a gem.
tags: ["History", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Historical Fiction"]

title: Big Wonderful Thing: A History Of Texas
author: Stephen Harrigan
description: Journalist and author Stephen Harrigan is essentially to Texas literature what Willie Nelson is to Texas music, and his latest book might just be the one he was born to write. Big Wonderful Thing, a sprawling history of the Lone Star State, showcases Harrigan’s enthusiasm for Texas – it’s an endlessly fascinating look at how the state has evolved over the years. Harrigan covers the state’s political and cultural history, paying special attention to Texans who have been overlooked in the past. Endlessly readable and written with great care, Big Wonderful Thing is just that.

title: Norco '80: The True Story Of The Most Spectacular Bank Robbery In American History
author: Peter Houlahan
description: On May 9, 1980, five robbers hit a branch of Security Pacific Bank in Norco, Calif., but failed to flee the scene before police showed up. The resulting shootout, escape and manhunt form the basis of Peter Houlahan’s gripping debut book, Norco ‘80, a nonfiction account of one of the most violent bank robberies in U.S. history, in which three people were killed and almost 20 others injured. Houlahan provides fascinating history and context of the robbery, which still reverberates in Southern California today. It’s not only a gripping true-crime account; it’s also one of the best nonfiction books of the year.

title: American Spy: A Novel
author: Lauren Wilkinson
description: Lauren Wilkinson’s book follows Marie Mitchell, an ex-FBI agent who has fled to Martinique after being attacked by someone connected to her past – while serving in the Bureau, she was recruited by the CIA to cozy up, and possibly seduce, the president of Burkina Faso. The book is a beautifully paced novel and a promising debut from a writer who’s not content to rely on the settled tropes of any literary genre – it’s an excellent thriller as well as a deeply intelligent literary novel that tackles issues of politics, race and gender in a way that’s never didactic.

title: Patsy: A Novel
author: Nicole Dennis-Benn
description: The title character of Nicole Dennis-Benn’s second novel is a young single mother in Jamaica who abandons her family when given the chance to move to New York, where the woman she has been in love with for years lives. The novel is an unflinching look at the experience of undocumented immigrants in America, who are forced to deal with racism and poverty in the land they’ve always dreamed about. Dennis-Benn is a compassionate, courageous writer, and Patsy is a brave, brilliant triumph of a book.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: Where Reasons End: A Novel
author: Yiyun Li
description: Yiyun Li’s heartbreaking autobiographical novel takes the form of an extended conversation between the narrator – an author and teacher – and her son, Nikolai, who has recently died from suicide. (Li herself lost a son to suicide.) The narrator and Nikolai challenge each other throughout the course of the novel, each straining to find words to express their emotions. Devastating and true, Where Reasons End is a masterpiece of American fiction that proves beyond a doubt that Li is one of this country’s greatest writers.

title: Gingerbread: A Novel
author: Helen Oyeyemi
description: As far as British novelist Helen Oyeyemi is concerned, you’re never too old for fairy tales. Her latest novel is a spin on the Hansel and Gretel story – it follows Harriet, a would-be social climber with a magical gingerbread recipe, and her daughter, Perdita, a troubled young woman who takes a bizarre trip to a country that may or may not exist. It’s a life-affirming, charming and just plain fun novel from an author who’s refreshingly unafraid to be joyful.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Lot: Stories
author: Bryan Washington
description: In his gorgeous debut short story collection, young author Bryan Washington paints an unforgettable picture of Houston and the people who call it home – all struggling to find a place for themselves in a quickly changing city. About half the stories, most of which are named after Houston neighborhoods, feature the same protagonist – a young, gay biracial man growing up in the city’s East End. Washington’s writing is beautiful, and he does a brilliant job making the city come to life in all its imperfect glory. It’s a stunning work of art and instant classic of Texas literature.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: Marilou Is Everywhere: A Novel
author: Sarah Elaine Smith
description: Cindy, the teenage narrator of Sarah Elaine Smith’s debut novel, longs to disappear from her lonely, poverty-stricken existence in rural southwestern Pennsylvania. She gets the chance when a girl from her county goes missing, and she’s able to slide into the life she’s always wanted for herself. Marilou is Everywhere is a novel of stunning emotional intelligence, and Cindy an unforgettable character, but it’s Smith’s writing that’s the real star of the book – her voice is nothing short of angelic, and this novel reads like a miracle.

title: Orange World And Other Stories
author: Karen Russell
description: Karen Russell’s latest short story collection features a boy who falls in love with a 2,000-year-old dead girl, a young woman possessed by a Joshua tree and a mother coerced into breastfeeding the devil – needless to say, literary realism this is not. Russell’s stories aren’t like anything you’ve ever read before; there’s no doubt at all that she is one of the most original American authors working today. She’s also one of the best.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The World Doesn't Require You: Stories
author: Rion Amilcar Scott
description: Cross River, Md. – the home of America’s “only successful slave uprising” – is one of the country’s most fascinating cities. And it exists only in the imagination of author Rion Amilcar Scott, who returns to the fictional town in his second short story collection. The book is less a collection of short, genre-spanning stories than it is an ethereal atlas of a world that’s both wholly original and disturbingly familiar; it’s one of the most innovative, and best, books of the year.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Dancing Man: A Broadway Choreographer's Journey
author: Bob Avian with Tom Santopietro
description: Remember the documentary film 20 Feet From Stardom, which told the story of backup singers for famous rock stars? Well, Dancing Man is kind of a Broadway version. In this fun, gossipy memoir, Bob Avian talks about being a Broadway chorus member, dancing in Funny Girl with Barbra Streisand and Hello Dolly! with Mary Martin, and choreographing such shows as Miss Saigon and Sunset Boulevard. But, by far, the most fascinating part of the book is the time that Avian served as assistant to brilliant director-choreographer Michael Bennett, so we get intimate glimpses of the creation of such classic musicals as A Chorus Line, Dreamgirls and Follies, among others, as well as famous bombs, such as Coco, which starred Katharine Hepburn, who could neither sing nor dance.

title: American Oligarchs: The Kushners, The Trumps, And The Marriage Of Money And Power
author: Andrea Bernstein
description: Grasping our own time while living through it is the work of the best journalists. Andrea Bernstein is one of them. Her book looks at the many untruths on which the Trump administration has been built. American Oligarchs takes on the layers of lies one by one, with stories that stretch from prewar Poland to suburban New Jersey to the White House. It’s a riveting family drama that would be hard to believe – if in fact we weren’t living it.

title: Wagnerism: Art And Politics In The Shadow Of Music
author: Alex Ross
description: This book is emphatically not a biography of one of music’s most notable – and notorious – figures. Rather, it’s a sweeping and often surprising history of just how pivotal composer Richard Wagner’s work has been across many realms, including the visual arts, literature and politics. (Among Wagner’s ardent fans: W.E.B Du Bois, Willa Cather, Luis Buñuel, Philip K. Dick, Theodor Herzl and, infamously, Hitler.) Even if you feel no particular affinity for Wagner’s music or for opera more generally, Ross will draw you into a world of modernism that he persuasively argues was actively shaped by Wagner and those influenced by his music, ideas and aesthetics.

title: The Jakarta Method: Washington's Anticommunist Crusade And The Mass Murder Program That Shaped Our World
author: Vincent Bevins
description: The CIA at one point discussed just straight up assassinating the Indonesian President in order to squash the country’s growing political left. They decided against it in favor of hiring a lookalike to film a fake sex tape. That plan didn’t work out either. Turns out the most effective strategy was a lot less outlandish – propaganda, disinformation and demonization. In The Jakarta Method, journalist Vincent Bevins uses declassified documents and one-on-one interviews to lay out in fascinating and frustrating detail how the U.S. fueled the anti-left rhetoric that led to the mass killings of anywhere between 500,000 to 1 million people in Indonesia – and then used that same playbook elsewhere in the world.

title: Luster: A Novel
author: Raven Leilani
description: This fearless debut novel hurtles into some uncomfortable areas, centering on an erotic relationship that is interracial, intergenerational, cross-class, kinky and extramarital. The prose is sleek and perpetually surprising, as when the protagonist takes refuge in the bathroom at a party: “I have come to the part of the night where I am incapable of any uppercase emotion, and every circuit responsible for my cellular regeneration has begun to smoke.”

title: Caste: The Origins Of Our Discontents
author: Isabel Wilkerson
description: I’ve recommended this book to many friends because it begs careful unpacking. Isabel Wilkerson, whose The Warmth of Other Suns is one of the towering achievements of 21st century nonfiction, draws parallels between the American racial hierarchy, the anti-Semitic regime of the Nazis and India’s highly complex caste system. She moves between her own experiences with casteism as a highly educated and upwardly mobile Black woman, and vignettes about the likes of Martin Luther King Jr. and Albert Einstein.

title: A Children's Bible: A Novel
author: Lydia Millet
description: A group of families is vacationing when bad weather hits. Actually, it’s a full-fledged natural disaster; in fact, maybe it’s the end of the world? The parents are lost in hedonistic oblivion, leaving the teens in charge. This is not like any climate change novel you’ve ever read. Allegorical but never pat, it pivots on young people’s anger about the world they’ve inherited. And you know what? We deserve it.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Dark"]

title: Echo Mountain
author: Lauren Wolk
description: Everyone blames Ellie for her father’s accident, but this city girl turned mountain expert is determined to find him a cure for his coma. Lauren Wolk’s basic writing mode is what I like to call “long and gripping,” and her storytelling makes this Depression-era tale of fortitude scintillating. There’s not a dull word on any of these pages (and I hope you don’t mind maggots!) (For ages 10 to 13)

title: The Lights And Types Of Ships At Night
author: Dave Eggers, illustrated by Annie Dills
description: “Did you realize that of all the world’s most beautiful sights, there is nothing more beautiful than a ship and its lights on the sea at night?” Dave Eggers takes young readers on a tour of his favorite seafaring vessels, as you’ve never seen them before. Here hyperbole is a force for good rather than evil! Each ship is described lovingly, yet the star of the show is the art of Annie Dills. Imagine Yayoi Kusama’s Infinity Mirrors turned into a boat book for preschoolers. Void and light and the blur of water melt into the ultimate bedtime fare. (For ages 6 to 10)

title: The Most Beautiful Thing
author: Kao Kalia Yang, illustrated by Khoa Le
description: Kalia’s grandmother has only one tooth, but her smile is the most beautiful her granddaughter has ever seen. In this moving picture book memoir about growing up with little money in a Hmong American home, financial insecurity isn’t seen as cute or charming. These immigrant kids just want the same lives as their peers. Still, it’s intergenerational love that is the heartbeat of the story, accompanied by visuals designed to engulf readers into the heart and heat of the tale. Gorgeous and searing. (For ages 5 to 9)
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Americana", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Invisible Life Of Addie LaRue
author: V.E. Schwab
description: Addie LaRue was born in France at the very end of the 17th century – but no one remembers that. Addie has traded her soul for immortality, but she didn’t realize that the hidden price is her legacy – for she is doomed to be instantly forgotten by everyone she meets as she slips through the centuries. It’s a bit cheeky to call this book Faust for romantic bisexual goths, but it’s not wrong. Meta and elegant, it reveals itself like the prestige of a particularly elegant magic trick, leaving us with the feeling that we, too, have been part of Addie’s long and invisible life.

title: Burnt Sugar: A Novel
author: Avni Doshi
description: How do you take care of a parent who failed to take care of you? Burnt Sugar (also published as Girl in White Cotton) is, at its core, a love story: Mother Tara chooses a life that comes at an appallingly neglectful cost to her daughter Antara. Thirty years later, Tara has developed dementia, and adult Antara takes her in and grapples with whether and how to forgive. I read Burnt Sugar with awe, fury and compassion, electrified by the question of whether time does heal all wounds, and whether it even should.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: No Filter: The Inside Story Of Instagram
author: Sarah Frier
description: Bloomberg tech reporter Sarah Frier captures the birth and growth of Instagram in her well-reported book No Filter – the story of the rise of a little photo-sharing app that would become arguably the world’s most influencial social media platform. Frier’s narrative writing brings the often-complicated world of tech and startups to a human level and is a real joy to read. She brings her reader along on Instagram’s journey while keeping a critical eye on the business of social media – and the mark Instagram has had on society. No Filter has made me consider Instagram’s place in my own life as well.

title: Race For Profit: How Banks And The Real Estate Industry Undermined Black Homeownership
author: Keeanga-Yamahtta Taylor
description: In Race for Profit, Keeanga-Yamahtta Taylor disrupts the notion that housing discrimination in the United States exists in some distant past. Following the Fair Housing Act of 1968 and the outlawing of redlining, Taylor shows how the exclusion of Black Americans from the real-estate market continued within a state-sanctioned system of discriminatory homeownership – what Taylor calls “predatory inclusion.” You’ll come away from this book with a new understanding of space and race in America, how the past is inextricable from our present and, ultimately, how the American Dream and ideals of property, homeownership and citizenship are fraught with violence.

title: 13th Balloon
author: Mark Bibbins
description: Mark Bibbins takes a leap back in time to the late ‘80s and early ‘90s, the heart of the AIDS epidemic that took the life of the poet’s first serious partner. Bibbins is guided by memory and longing and the true wish to have back some of what’s gone. This speaker, who is as vulnerable as language allows, knows these poems have arrived too late: “I have only language for you now/ a language/ that morphs like a virus/ to elude to survive to connect,” Alas, Bibbins seems to have anticipated this year’s seasons of grief, and he is kind company.

title: Postcolonial Love Poem
author: Natalie Diaz
description: Natalie Diaz makes the love poem into a frame for contemplating the politics and injustices that have long oppressed Native Americans. There’s no separation between personal and political when every circumstance is inflected by the wars “which started me.” Diaz locates battles everywhere, but her most important muse is the figure of her brother, whose failed struggle against addiction wrecked his own life and drew his family into a swirling storm from which they can’t break free: “he’d keep calling, hour after hour, day after day,/ lifetime after miserable lifetime, until I answered.” This is a book we’ll keep talking about.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Seeing The Body
author: Rachel Eliza Griffiths
description: This is a searing elegy for a mother who died after years of illness that held her family in its thrall. With astonishing frankness and detail, Griffiths anticipates, experiences, reexperiences and works to meaningfully incorporate her mother’s memory and death into the everyday fabric of life. This grief goes far beyond the mourner’s typical self-questioning (“Could I have ever saved her?”) into more complex realms of inheritance: “I remember/ her voice like a thorn I never want/ to pull out of my heart.” It’s one of the most empathic and transcendent books of poetry in years.

title: Mary And The Trail Of Tears: A Cherokee Removal Survival Story
author: Andrea L. Rogers, illustrated by Matt Forsyth
description: When kids need reassurance in tough times (like now, for instance), we often point them to examples from the past, to moments of resilience and courage. Mary and the Trail of Tears is ideal for that. The book was lovingly crafted by Cherokee author, mama and educator Andrea L. Rogers. It’s a window to the forced relocation of Cherokee people from what’s currently called Georgia to Indian Territory. But more than that, the story offers an opportunity for kids to really get to know and care about Mary and her family, to relate to the Cherokees of their era as human beings. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Barren Grounds: The Misewa Saga
author: David A. Robertson
description: Here’s the perfect seasonal read for curling up at home with the kids. We’re talking about a wintry portal story with echoes of Narnia’s charm but fully grounded in Indigenous belief systems. The Barren Grounds: The Misewa Saga (Book 1) kicks off a middle grade series by David A. Robertson, who’s a member of the Norway House Cree Nation. Young readers will join his heroes Morgan and Eli – in the company of the otherworldly Ochek and a sassy Squirrel named Arik – as they struggle to save themselves and the village of Misewa from an icy fate. (For ages 10 to 13)

title: Utopia Avenue: A Novel
author: David Mitchell
description: Turn off your mind, relax and float downstream. David Mitchell’s Utopia Avenue, the eighth novel from the English novelist who has spent his career building an interconnected cast of characters across continents and centuries, may be his most human work. We follow a literal band of misfits in London’s psychedelic 1960s as they struggle to find success as a musical act and learn about each other. It’s so much fun to read a book brimming with reverence for music on both sides of the Atlantic: The Beatles, Pink Floyd, Janis Joplin and the Grateful Dead.

title: Remembrance
author: Rita Woods
description: Rita Woods’ debut Remembrance is an ambitious and absorbing novel. Gaelle, a Haitian refugee, is a nurse’s aide at the Stillwater Care Facility in present-day Cleveland. Back in 1857, Margot and her younger sister Veronique are slaves, and slaveowner Mistress Catherine promises to free the two girls. And in 1791 Haiti – then called Saint-Domingue – Abigail is a devoted mother and wife owned by Monsieur Rousse. Remembrance is a complex but memorable story of loss and survival told across two centuries by four women, united by skin color and the supernatural powers they command.
tags: ["History", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Night Letters: Gulbuddin Hekmatyar And The Afghan Islamists Who Changed The World
author: Chris Sands with Fazelminallah Qazizai
description: Islamic militancy has many fathers, and Chris Sands, with Fazelminallah Qazizai, tell a cinematic story of one of them: Gulbuddin Hekmatyar. The restless black-turbaned fanatic smashed Kabul into rubble and killed untold numbers of Afghans in his failed quest to rule Afghanistan. And the rest of the world must also bear the burden of his other legacy: his adoption of foreign fighters who flocked to Afghanistan, including Osama bin Laden – and the ideological framework for jihad that he helped pollinate within extremists across the world, which finds justification for the deaths – even murder – of all those who do not follow their fiery creed.

title: God-Level Knowledge Darts: Life Lessons From The Bronx
author: Desus & Mero
description: Somehow this book is the loudest thing in whatever room you open it. It reads like an argument on a Bronx street corner, or shouty podcast, mainly because that’s what it’s based on. It’s also authentic, disarmingly frank and really, really funny. Daniel “Desus Nice” Baker and Joel “The Kid Mero” Martinez are two guys from the Bronx who made it good (a smash-hit podcast, a new show on Showtime) but haven’t moved on, and God-Level Knowledge Darts: Life Lessons From The Bronx is full of their advice on everything from finding a life partner based on shared gastrointestinal issues to being washed (read: “unhip”). They said they gave it short chapters so it could be read during visits to the toilet – so here’s hoping it’s loud enough to muffle the sounds of your laughter.

title: Barry Sonnenfeld, Call Your Mother: Memoirs Of A Neurotic Filmmaker
author: Barry Sonnenfeld
description: Barry Sonnenfeld has directed entertaining (though not always critically acclaimed) films including “The Addams Family” and the first three “Men in Black” movies.  But as listeners of “Wait, Wait, Don’t Tell Me” know, he’s also an engaging storyteller.  In his memoir, Sonnenfeld is both hilarious and tragic as he talks about working with the Coen brothers, surviving a plane crash and growing up with a cousin who sexually abused him. Somehow the combination works: Sonnenfeld’s breezy style engages us and makes us believe, as Will Smith has joked, that if Barry Sonnenfeld can be a director, anyone can.

title: Here For It: Or, How To Save Your Soul In America
author: R. Eric Thomas
description: R. Eric Thomas, who grew up with “Al Gore’s” Internet, outlines how he went viral in various digital eras, from college newspaper satire gone awry to a thirsty Facebook post that eventually landed him a job as a columnist for Elle.com. In this essay collection, Thomas examines growing up in a dangerous area but feeling more unsafe in the suburbs’ wide expanses filled with possible Babadooks. He also discusses navigating his sexuality and spirituality while dating his husband, a reverend; chronicling early adulthood haze where he finds himself with a work family at the Hard Rock Cafe; and moving to a different city on a whim. Thomas finds hope in a chaotic world and encourages readers to laugh and acknowledge Beyoncé, for all that she does.

title: Lifting As We Climb: Black Women's Battle For The Ballot Box
author: Evette Dionne
description: Evette Dionne’s book spotlights women we don’t hear enough about in the suffrage movement. Many Black women were instrumental in national and local pushes to guarantee voting for all Americans. The book is designed to look like a textbook by showcasing photographs, original documents and breakout sections with short biographies. It appeals to readers 10 and older, as well as adults who want to discover more about the lives of Ida B. Wells-Barnett, Mary McLeod Bethune and Anna Julia Cooper, among others. Reading this book can help add more context to political movements of the past, and today, in the U.S.

title: Hood Feminism: Notes From The Women That A Movement Forgot
author: Mikki Kendall
description: Anyone who identifies as a feminist would be well-served by reading Mikki Kendall’s brilliant debut essay collection, Hood Feminism: Notes from the Women That a Movement Forgot. In this searing indictment of mainstream feminism in the United States, Kendall examines how a movement that purports to represent over half the planet’s population has, in fact, consistently overlooked women of color, trans women, differently abled women and other marginalized people. She elevates the experiences of those who are too often excluded, while also giving voice to how violence, hunger, poverty, education, housing, reproductive justice and more are all feminist issues.

title: Intimations: Six Essays
author: Zadie Smith
description: Of all the books published this year, I’m betting that few speak to the current moment as directly as Zadie Smith’s most recent essay collection, Intimations. Drafted soon after “the global humbling” of the pandemic began, and completed in the days after the killing of George Floyd, these six essays feel as intimate as a long walk with an old friend. Smith deftly tackles an array of questions brought to the forefront in recent months, from the nature of isolation and what it means to be confined with the people we love to addressing police brutality and economic injustice. It’s worth a read.

title: Black Sunday: A Novel
author: Tola Rotimi Abraham
description: Some novels dig into your soul and stay there. This is one of them. With a deceptively simple plot about four siblings coming of age in Nigeria, Black Sunday lulls you into a false sense of security before dragging you through a world of poverty, death, abuse and heartbreak. Luckily, if you survive, there is a lot of love and hope waiting for you on the other side. Black Sunday is so believable it often reads like nonfiction, and that makes it even more powerful. Tola Rotimi Abraham is a great new voice, and this novel will hopefully help her land on many readers’ radars.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Blackwood
author: Michael Farris Smith
description: Some of the most exciting and unique stories in contemporary fiction are growing out of the interstitial space between crime and horror, and Blackwood is one of the finest examples. Overrun by kudzu and smelling of wet earth, this haunting novel of ghosts, broken people and lost childhood is packed with nightmarish imagery, great writing and a noir atmosphere that puts the darkest realities of small town America on full display.

title: Children Of The Land: A Memoir
author: Marcelo Hernandez Castillo
description: Migration and the U.S.-Mexico border have been so politicized that it’s easy to forget people are at the heart of the conversation. Marcelo Hernández Castillo does a superb job of rehumanizing the discussion by sharing his story of undocumented migration to the U.S. and eventual journey to citizenship. Along the way, he exposes the flaws in the immigration system, discusses the realities of undocumented immigrants, and shares the story of his absent father, caught in a long battle for citizenship after being deported. This is nonfiction with a lot of heart that shines a light on the human side of an ongoing political discussion.

title: I Don't Want To Die Poor: Essays
author: Michael Arceneaux
description: There’s nothing funny about student loan debt (trust me, I know!), but Michael Arceneaux finds a way to talk about it with honesty and humor in this collection of biographical essays that touch on everything from poverty and sexuality to Blackness and popular culture. Touching and hilarious, this book of essays can be taken as a whole because the topics and Arceneaux’s voice give it a great sense of cohesion. If you like to laugh while you cry, don’t miss this one.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Biography & Memoir", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: I Hold A Wolf By The Ears: Stories
author: Laura van den Berg
description: This is the best short story collection I read in 2020. Laura van den Berg has a talent for reinventing herself, and here she does it with each story. Magical, creepy, strange, heartbreaking and globetrotting, this collection challenges readers with every page, submerging them in modern fairy tales that shatter reality while also, at times, feeling a little too real. The best literature makes you feel things, and this collection will unsettle you. Profoundly.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Short Stories & Essays", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: MS-13: The Making Of America's Most Notorious Gang
author: Steven Dudley
description: A lot has been said about the Mara Salvatrucha, the gang known as MS-13. Most of it has been wrong. For this book, perhaps the most complete look at MS-13, Steven Dudley looked at the historical, political, cultural and financial elements that came together to create one of the world’s most notorious criminal organizations. Both a scholar with research expertise and a journalist who’s reported for NPR, The Washington Post, The Economist and the BBC, Dudley has written a book that engages fully with the darkest, most violent side of the gang – while also recognizing that some of its members are victims of circumstances beyond their control. An incredibly informative, harrowing read.

title: The Boatman's Daughter: A Novel
author: Andy Davidson
description: Creepy and dark, this book was pulled from the mud of some nameless bayou in the middle of the night. Yes, there’s plenty of weirdness, violence and gore here, but also memorable characters and a lot of heart. This is a supernatural Southern Gothic in which the darkness of the past and the bloodshed of the present dance together beautifully via superb prose and the kind of pacing you seldom find in this subgenre. It’s rare to find novels that give you profound familial love and severed heads in Styrofoam coolers, but this one does that and more.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: The Book Of Atlantis Black: The Search For A Sister Gone Missing
author: Betsy Bonner
description: Be warned: This is a sad book that asks more questions than it answers. Losing a sibling is hard, but losing one under mysterious circumstances might be worse. Betsy Bonner applies the tools of a great fiction writer to the real story of her childhood with her sister, and the events that preceded her sister’s untimely death. More than a straightforward biography, this book is an exploration of a sister’s disappearance that digs deep into the messy past that shaped her life as well as Bonner’s. Part true crime and part literary exorcism, this is a story that will haunt you long after the last page.

title: The Dead Are Arising: The Life Of Malcolm X
author: Les Payne and Tamara Payne
description: The Dead Are Arising is the definitive Malcolm X biography. 28 years of meticulous research and hundreds of hours of interviews with friends, family and scholars went into creating a deep, nuanced picture of not only Malcolm X, but the country he was born into, the racism he lived with and the way his assassination changed the United States forever. This isn’t a book about Malcolm X being a hero; it’s a biography that shows him as a flawed, complex individual who did heroic things.

title: The Socrates Express: In Search Of Life Lessons From Dead Philosophers
author: Eric Weiner
description: “Philosophy for Starbucks people” probably doesn’t sound too flattering, but that’s exactly what this is, and I mean that in a good way. Eric Weiner, a New York Times bestselling author and a former foreign correspondent for NPR, explores all the ways the teachings of philosophers like Socrates, Nietzsche, Rousseau and Confucius might improve his own life. With a lot of humor and plenty of coffee, Weiner takes readers on a literary/philosophical journey to a better life. Part travelogue and part soul-searching memoir, this is philosophy simplified – but also made more useful for the layman.
tags: ["History", "Short Stories & Essays", "Biography & Memoir", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Winter Counts: A Novel
author: David Heska Wanbli Weiden
description: On the surface, Winter Counts is a novel about a man making a living dishing out justice with his fists, in the name of people who can’t rely on the law to do so. However, right below its surface, it’s also a story about how the law has neglected all Native American reservations for decades, allowing awful crimes to go unpunished and marginalizing the people who live there. It also explores the way the opioid epidemic has affected Native American communities – in this case, the Sicangu Lakota living in the Rosebud Indian Reservation in South Dakota. Timely and smart, this is a powerful narrative about identity and heritage in a shifting world.

title: Magnetized: Conversations With A Serial Killer
author: Carlos Busqued, translated by Samuel Rutter
description: Over the course of one week in September of 1982, Ricardo Melogno killed four taxi drivers in Buenos Aires. Science has been grappling with him ever since. A mellow, intelligent man, Melogno has vivid recollections of the killings, but shows none of the behaviors psychologists, lawyers and psychiatrists expect from him. Journalist Carlos Busqued tried to get to the bottom of Melogno’s complex psyche during a series of interviews, which eventually became this book. Haunting and unsettling, Magnetized is a narrative that reminds us how little we know about the inner workings of serial killers.

title: Unworthy Republic: The Dispossession Of Native Americans And The Road To Indian Territory
author: Claudio Saunt
description: In this grim history, Claudio Saunt traces the forced deportation of Native American nations by an administration whose greed, bolstered by a growing rhetoric of white supremacy, robbed sovereign land and condemned thousands to die while publicizing a narrative of Native Americans as inevitably doomed. They were not – and, as Saunt documents, ably defended themselves by word and deed against the looming removal – but white America’s avarice bent the law at will. Unworthy Republic traces the government hypocrisy, civil uproar, petty despotism, chilling bureaucracy and human cost of an attempted genocide America has yet to reckon with.

title: The New Map: Energy, Climate And The Clash Of Nations
author: Daniel Yergin
description: For decades, no one has written in more sweeping terms about the international energy industry and how it shapes the world than Daniel Yergin. He has done it again with The New Map. His eloquent narratives take you from the first successful fracking operation in tiny Dish, Texas, in 1998, to China’s obsession with dominating the South China Sea, to the global reckoning on energy and climate change. He offers a 360-degree analysis on how nations are moving fitfully from a dependence on oil toward renewables. Spoiler alert: Whatever your preferred energy source, it’s going to be a bumpy ride.

title: Memorial Drive: A Daughter's Memoir
author: Natasha Trethewey
description: In Memorial Drive, Natasha Trethewey’s achingly beautiful memoir, the Pulitzer Prize-winning poet reflects upon the trauma of her youth while unearthing the legacy of women in her family. Here is the full range of Trethewey’s brilliance: her soft nuance, depth of looking and her shimmering constructions of language and imagery. “All my adult life I have lived with the guilt that I am implicated in my mother’s death – or, more precisely that she is dead because I am not,” writes Tretheway, whose life turns upon this moment when her abusive stepfather came to the teenage Tretheway’s school to kill her and then decided to kill her mother instead.

title: Survivor Song: A Novel
author: Paul Tremblay
description: The entire book takes place over just a few hours; the main character is an angry, scared pregnant woman in the middle of a viral zombie outbreak. And even though we know – absolutely know – how Survivor Song must end from almost the very first page, Paul Tremblay uses our own bone-deep understanding of the zombie story format and our own trope-exhaustion against us by telling a story where the narrative beats feel almost foreordained, leaving the details of character, place and response to an immediate, ticking-clock disaster as the one true focus. There are entire histories hidden inside here, and whole, sprawling architectures of need and anger and redemption. But no one inside the book (and none of us reading it) get to escape the lunacy of panic or the unforgiving countdown Tremblay hangs over everything.

title: Black Sun
author: Rebecca Roanhorse
description: Just when you thought prophecy in epic fantasy was passé – enough with the chosen ones already – Rebecca Roanhorse gives us Black Sun, first in a trilogy set in a world inspired by the empires of pre-Columbian America. A young man who’s the vessel of a god travels to reach the holy city Tova by the day of Convergence, escorted by a captain able to sing the sea under control; meanwhile, the young Sun Priest is determined to atone for old wrongs, but generational trauma and fear are difficult to ease. Here destiny and fanaticism drive unforgettable characters forward, without losing a sense of agency, and the worldbuilding is perfection. This is superlative work: inventive, gripping, rich. Best I’ve read in a while.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Flyaway
author: Kathleen Jennings
description: Prepare to be unsettled, to catch dread welling up in your bones, to pause often on a turn of phrase or image so spare and vivid and lovely it will draw blood. I want to bite Kathleen Jennings’ prose and feel it snap between my teeth it’s so damn good. Flyaway braids together folk horror and fairy tale, things older than the hills, and wild, uncanny and awful politeness. Bettina’s dad and brothers disappeared; she doesn’t think about it. “Once you start forgetting, it’s easy.” Then a letter sets her and two friends on a quest to uncover the truth in this atmospheric Australian gothic.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Family Matters"]

title: Hench
author: Natalie Zina Walschots
description: Hench counts the human cost of superheroes. And Anna, traumatized and injured by the hero Supercollider while henching for a minor villain, wants to make them pay. Anna is funny, the rage of collateral damage, one of us – where “us” is survivor, is anybody who’s ever worried about making rent or worked for a company that does some not-so-great things. Hench is both high-concept (BAM!) campy (POW!) Silver and Golden Age superhero stuff, and the mundane minutiae of a world where superpowers are real. It’s also the horror of a world with little privacy and information as weapon. Hench is thought-provoking – and a lot of fun!

title: The Empress Of Salt And Fortune
author: Nghi Vo
description: This subtle fantasy novella gives us an exquisitely crafted puzzle box of a tale about a young cleric who discovers 60-year-old intrigues eddying around an exiled empress. It’s a masterclass in storytelling, elegantly unspooled; the kind of story you want to gnaw, sucking on the marrow. The Empress of Salt and Fortune reminds us of the importance of women’s and queer voices in spite and in the face of erasure. It shows us deftly and with understated grace how the rage of marginalized people is raw and powerful and might change history. Even if history doesn’t know it.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Once And Future Witches
author: Alix E. Harrow
description: Three wayward sisters find each other and their hope for a better future – one where witching is legal and women have the vote – in Alix Harrow’s gorgeous, passionate fantasy, set in an alternate United States during the suffrage movement. There’s old magic in these pages, a spell to catch a heart and keep it. I love The Once and Future Witches unabashedly. This is a book I want to press into the hands of all my friends and say, see here, this is us. It doesn’t hurt that all the clever allusions to fairy tales and American labor history are just my kind of thing, or that Harrow’s writing is as beguiling and crisp as autumn itself.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: So We Can Glow: Stories
author: Leesa Cross-Smith
description: If you are in the mood for damn good writing but don’t have time to commit to a long novel, consider reading Leesa Cross-Smith’s latest short story collection. She is a gifted storyteller with an economical approach, and has written the most well-observed slices of girlhood and womanhood that I have read in years. She also plays with form – for example, “Girlheart Cake With Glitter Frosting” reads like a recipe. I am a picky short story reader, but this collection floored me.

title: The Roommate
author: Rosie Danan
description: I love when a romance novel flawlessly executes a far-fetched premise. Clara Wheaton is a button-downed woman who heads to Los Angeles on a whim to move in with her crush. He flakes, and she’s left with a random Craigslist roommate. She soon learns that she is cohabitating with porn star Josh Darling. It’s common for romance novels to be sex-positive with an emphasis on women’s pleasure, but The Roommate takes this notion a step further and is unabashedly porn-positive. This is Rosie Danan’s debut, and I can’t wait to read what she writes next.

title: Transcendent Kingdom: A Novel
author: Yaa Gyasi
description: Yaa Gyasi’s debut Homegoing is one of my favorite books, and I wondered if her latest novel could possibly live up to my unreasonably high expectations. I shouldn’t have worried; I absolutely loved Transcendent Kingdom. Gifty is a Ghanian American neuroscientist at Stanford, studying the behavior of mice, but she yearns to understand the behavior of humans. As Gifty looks to science to shed light on the addiction and mental health struggles within her own family, she feels drawn to the evangelical faith in which she was raised. Gyasi’s works are often framed as “important” – and they are – but they are also a profound pleasure to read.

title: The Death Of Vivek Oji: A Novel
author: Akwaeke Emezi
description: “If nobody sees you, are you still there?” wonders the titular character early in The Death of Vivek Oji. The answer to that question plays out over the course of this tragic and somehow hopeful novel about what it means to live as one’s true self. The rich, gleaming narrative comes courtesy Akwaeke Emezi, a Nigerian author now based in the U.S., who paints their country of birth with vivid color, brutal honesty and fierce love.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: A Good Neighborhood: A Novel
author: Therese Anne Fowler
description: Therese Anne Fowler’s latest novel takes place in Oak Knoll, an imaginary, mildly integrated community in North Carolina. Fowler’s protagonist is Valerie Alston-Holt, a widowed Black university professor, a botanist with a brilliant biracial son, Xavier. Their lives are turned upside down when the Whitmans, a new white family, build a McMansion next door to the Alston-Holts’ modest home, upsetting the ecological balance of Valerie’s carefully maintained land, and killing an ancient, much-beloved tree. The families clash and the result is Shakespearian. An unseen narrator, a neighbor, serves as the Greek Chorus to this story of race, class, region and, yes, tragic love. I hope HBO or Netflix or somebody is paying attention.

title: Take A Hint, Dani Brown: A Novel
author: Talia Hibbert
description: In 2019, Talia Hibbert introduced us to the Brown sisters, three winsome, Jamaican-descended Londoners from a loving, affluent family. Her first book focused on eldest sister Chloe. Now, our protagonist is middle sister Danika, a crispy PhD candidate who loves beautiful shoes and sex with no complications – and definitely no romance. But things get plenty complicated when Dani agrees to publicize a fake romance with Zafir, the hunky security guard at her university, to boost interest in the nonprofit he runs. Zaf is a former rugby star (good!) and a hopeless romantic (uh-oh!). The pair’s mutual attraction snaps and sizzles, all set against modern, multicultural London. The perfect antidote to the dumpster fire that is 2020.

title: When No One Is Watching: A Thriller
author: Alyssa Cole
description: A neighborhood in Brooklyn is gentrifying at breakneck speed – many of its longtime residents, elderly and Black, are moving suddenly, just … disappearing. Sydney Green, born and raised in Brooklyn, has escaped an abusive marriage on the West Coast and returned to her childhood brownstone to nurse her ill mother. The more Sydney pushes to discover what’s happening to her neighborhood, the stronger the story’s underlying vein of menace gets. (There’s even a website that sounds an awful lot like Nextdoor where the white gentrifiers make secret plans to make sure they will soon be in the majority.) Alyssa Cole, well-known for her romances, manages to combine elements of race, class, conspiracy and history (and a little love) into one satisfyingly creepy book.

title: Mexican Gothic
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: This gothic tale set in 1950s Mexico follows Noemí, a posh Mexican socialite, who receives a distressing letter from her recently married cousin Catalina. When Noemí goes to check on her, she finds there is something very strange happening at High Place, Catalina’s countryside estate, and sets out to understand what’s haunting her cousin. Silvia Moreno-Garcia skillfully weaves themes of class, race and eugenics into a tale of horror that truly creeped me out.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Identity & Culture", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: You Never Forget Your First: A Biography Of George Washington
author: Alexis Coe
description: Alexis Coe doesn’t succumb to larger-than-life caricatures or gloss over flaws in her biography of George Washington – the first written by a woman in 40 years, she says. The resulting narrative of Washington’s life feels refreshingly human. Every anecdote is enhanced with details from the lives of the people around him – friends, foes, family, people he enslaved – and lists like “Frenemies” and “Pettiest Acts” add plenty of personality to the historical figure you thought you knew. Coe’s take on Washington is fun to read, and you’ll soon wonder why you assumed Washington biographies were only meant to be Father’s Day gifts.

title: Big Friendship: How We Keep Each Other Close
author: Aminatou Sow and Ann Friedman
description: Big Friendship – the name Ann Friedman and Aminatou Sow gave to intentional, committed, long-term friendships – is part memoir, part thesis for elevating how we view platonic relationships. Backing up their experiences with sociological research, these besties (who co-host the Call Your Girlfriend podcast) write in the collective “we” about the ups and downs of their own friendship, ultimately inventing a new framework for relationship maintenance. We read Big Friendship as a two-person book club and an exercise in understanding our own friendship – and we came away with a new sense of what it means to invest in the Big Friendships in our lives.

title: Hidden Valley Road: Inside The Mind Of An American Family
author: Robert Kolker
description: Robert Kolker tells the true story of the Galvin family. They had 12 children, and six of their 10 boys were diagnosed with schizophrenia in the 1960s and 1970s, when far less was known about the disease. From the problems with institutions and ineffective treatments to the family’s work with researchers who wanted to study them, it’s a sobering look at the lives the brothers lived and the way the family as a whole was changed.

title: One To Watch: A Novel
author: Kate Stayman-London
description: In Kate Stayman-London’s novel, Bea is a plus-size fashion blogger who writes a piece about her frustration with a dating show that is very similar, let’s say, to The Bachelorette. Before she knows it, she finds herself invited to become the next season’s star. The book is sharp-eyed about media, about reality television and about the bodies we live in. But it’s also funny and hopeful and terribly charming.

title: This Is One Way To Dance: Essays
author: Sejal Shah
description: The essays in Sejal Shah’s finely wrought debut explore congruent realities: to be born and bred in America while simultaneously embedded in the Gujarati diaspora. Shah brings refreshing and depressing observations about having dark skin and an “exotic” name when the only country you’ve lived in is America – and reminds us that India is a giant place with multitudinous cultures. Shah also goes beyond cultural commentary, exploring both her development as a writer and the meaningful relationships in her life, including those she’s lost.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Biography & Memoir", "Americana"]

title: Leave The World Behind: A Novel
author: Rumaan Alam
description: If I had to choose one novel for readers of the future to help them understand what it was like to live through 2020, I’d choose Leave the World Behind. A slippery and duplicitous marvel, Rumaan Alam’s story opens on the domestic bickering of a family vacation car trip. It quickly takes on aspects of both a suspense story and a witty comedy of manners about race. Very slowly, it morphs into a vision of disaster that both the characters and we readers keep denying. Atmospheric and prescient, Alam’s novel captures the rhythms of how we live right now.

title: You Deserve Each Other
author: Sarah Hogle
description: This small-town romance has a deliciously wicked premise: Naomi and Nicholas are engaged, but they utterly loathe each other. Alas, neither can afford to call off the wedding – so obviously, they must each make the other person mad enough to call it quits. Thus ensues a series of pranks and deliberate attempts to push one another’s buttons in the way that only former lovers can. And in trying to go out in a blaze of glory, Naomi and Nicholas find they might want to stay together after all.

title: Life Of A Klansman: A Family History In White Supremacy
author: Edward Ball
description: In Life of a Klansman, Edward Ball does not shirk from his ancestor’s role in preserving white supremacy, and neither should you. Ball’s great-great-grandfather was a “petit-blanc” or “little white” who belonged to a Ku Klux Klan-affiliated militia in postbellum New Orleans that terrorized Black Americans and tried to overthrow the government. In 363 pages, Ball details the events and attitudes that normalized violence in pursuit of a cruel cause, checking his own assumptions and that of his readers that history is only about the past.

title: The Cactus League: A Novel
author: Emily Nemens
description: You don’t absolutely have to love baseball to enjoy this, but it’s a bonus if you do. However, any fan of character-driven fiction will find much to appreciate in this smart, crisply written novel-in-stories of a baseball team during spring training in Arizona. But Emily Nemens isn’t interested in just the players: In nine chapters (one for each inning, of course), she vividly portrays a diverse group of people associated with the Lions, including players, concession stand workers, owners, coaches, agents and hangers-on (think Annie Savoy in Bull Durham) and even the team’s organist.

title: I Want You to Know We're Still Here: A Post-Holocaust Memoir
author: Esther Safran Foer
description: Growing up, Esther Safran Foer didn’t know much about her family’s history. The child of two Holocaust survivors, she was used to the silence. “My parents were reluctant to speak of the past, and I learned to maneuver around difficult subjects” she writes in I Want You to Know We’re Still Here. Eventually she wanted to know more, and so she set out with just a faded photograph and some historical records to try to find the family that hid her father from the Nazis. Part mystery, part memoir, she takes you on a journey to a small Ukrainian town to try to fill in the gaps of her history.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: No Rules Rules: Netflix And The Culture Of Reinvention
author: Reed Hastings and Erin Meyer
description: We all know Netflix has been a disruptive force in business – first in video rental and then in film/TV production. Less well-known is Netflix’s unusual corporate culture, shaped by co-founder and CEO Reed Hastings. Hastings has evangelized an ethos of “freedom and responsibility.” Managers are given great latitude to make major decisions – but they are subjected to candid feedback that’s withering at times. In a twist on a traditional management book, Hastings doesn’t just ask us to take his word for the wisdom of his approach. His co-author, business school professor Erin Meyer, gets equal billing – and provides some skeptical balance – in a fascinating dissection of what makes Netflix such a dominant company.

title: Trouble The Saints: A Novel
author: Alaya Dawn Johnson
description: Phyllis LeBlanc has saint’s hands. Her knives never miss; it’s a terrible gift that’s made her into a Manhattan mob boss’ most feared enforcer. She has another troublesome gift: She can pass for white. Downtown, she’s Phyllis, but uptown, she’s Pea – a fraught and dangerous balance to hold. And both sides of her are tired of life as a killer, even though refusing to use her saint’s hands puts her own life in danger. Phyllis has been telling herself she only kills people who deserve it, but how much good does that really do in a world broken by racism and hatred? This is a knotty, painful, gorgeously told historical fantasy in which nobody’s hands are clean, nobody escapes the consequences of their own actions and the past will not stay buried.

title: Ring Shout
author: P. Djèlí Clark
description: In P. Djéli Clark’s version of the Jim Crow South, The Birth of a Nation wasn’t just a movie – it was a summoning spell that brought the pointy headed, pale-skinned demons called Ku Kluxes to Earth to feed off the hatred of human Klansmen. Standing against the Klan are demon-hunters Maryse, Sadie and Chef, backed by the power of the ring shouts, chants led by the old Gullah woman Nana Jean. But hatred goes both ways, and Maryse must learn how to use her own before the demons do. Ring Shout is a novella, but it packs cinematic excitement, shuddering body horror, real history and wrenching emotion into its short length.

title: Wow, No Thank You.: Essays
author: Samantha Irby
description: Buckle in for TMI hilarity with Samantha Irby’s essay collection. No topic is too intimate or too gross for this always-frank writer, a woman who has depression, Crohn’s disease and a history of passing “gerbil-size” period clots. With raw honesty, Irby makes the mundanity of living in Kalamazoo, Mich., laugh-out-loud hilarious – everything from homeownership (“must I really learn things about grass”) to buying a car (which her wife immediately “defiled” with “MOM S***” like reusable grocery bags and granola bars). Irby offers desperately needed comic relief from pandemic stress.

title: Sisters: A Novel
author: Daisy Johnson
description: Looking for a creepy read to take your mind away from the horrors of now? July and September, the titular sisters born 10 months apart, are completely enmeshed with one another, living just this side of wild in a mouldering house on the English coast. Daisy Johnson dials up the dread notch by notch as you slowly learn the story of how the teen sisters arrived at their decrepit destination, after being bullied at their Oxford school. So good I mailed copies to three friends.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Whistleblower: My Journey To Silicon Valley And Fight For Justice At Uber
author: Susan Fowler
description: In 2017, Susan Fowler, a software engineer at Uber, wrote a blog post that detailed a toxic culture of sexism, sexual harassment and chaos at the rapidly growing ride-share company. The post forced a reckoning at the company and put a spotlight on Silicon Valley culture. What Fowler’s memoir does so well is show how these stories, and the characters in them (meaning her), did not just spring up from the earth. In Fowler’s recounting of her life and the story we get an understanding of all of the factors and complexities that led to the moment she decided to hit publish on her post – and in turn what life was like afterward. In the end, she is much more than “the woman who wrote the blog post,” and in telling that story she sends the reader away with a deeper understanding of the story behind the headlines.

title: Clean: The New Science Of Skin
author: James Hamblin
description: James Hamblin knows his timing is awkward. He published a book that questions our cleansing rituals during a pandemic. There’s a line, though, between how we lather up to avoid disease and how we scrub down to feel clean. Where you draw it may help or harm the microscopic creatures living on your body’s largest organ. Who better to investigate than a doctor and journalist who hasn’t showered in years? I myself read Clean and tweaked my soap habits. The results were, at times, pungent, but at least social distancing gave me cover.

title: Where Things Touch: A Meditation On Beauty
author: Bahar Orang
description: This is not a collection so much as a lyric essay, poetry as conversation, as correspondence, as exploration, intimate as hands brushing while passing a note between them. Bahar Orang’s musing on the nature, parameters and accessibility of beauty is precise as a scalpel and diffuse as a bouquet, thoughtful and intense, thoroughly embodied and restlessly questing. I scribbled in the margins of almost every page; once I finished it, I put it in a care package and posted it to dear friends.

title: Recipe For Persuasion: A Novel
author: Sonali Dev
description: Struggling chef Ashna Raje and former soccer star Rico Silva are torn apart at a young age, only to be reunited later in life in an unideal-but-fun rom-com situation. The book chronicles the backstory of Ashna’s estranged relationship with her mother, who married into a wealthy Indian family, upending her life and, in turn, her daughter’s. I love Sonali Dev’s flair for character descriptions and her descriptions of South Asian culture – without the explanatory comma. In the vein of Jasmine Guillory and Helen Hoang, Dev weaves her characters’ lives together in each of her books, bringing background characters into the foreground in subsequent stories. The family dynamics are stronger and more realized as you, the reader, come across a favorite character in another book. This book provided some much-needed escapism for me this summer.

title: The Mountains Sing
author: Nguyễn Phan Quế Mai
description: Informed by Nguyễn Phan Quế Mai’s family history, The Mountains Sing is a fictionalized remembrance of a turbulent 20th-century Việt Nam. Told alternately through the eyes of Diệu Lan and her granddaughter Hương, we follow four generations of the Trần family as they experience the French colonial period, the Great Hunger of 1945, Land Reform and the Vietnam War (or the American War, as it’s known in Việt Nam). Quế Mai’s first novel in English is lyrical and at once heart-wrenching and hopeful. Quế Mai has described the book as her “desperate call for peace and for humans to love other human beings more.” After a year like 2020, I think that call will resonate with many.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Vesper Flights
author: Helen Macdonald
description: Did you know that a starling flock is called a murmuration? Or that a queen ant can live up to 30 years? In this collection of essays on the natural world, Helen Macdonald serves as a thoughtful tour guide, revealing myriad everyday miracles with encyclopedic knowledge. Her curiosity doesn’t stop at studying other creatures, though. Her observations of nature, sprinkled with personal anecdotes and history lessons, often seamlessly pivot to reflections on humanity – as so many of our fellow creatures’ flourishing depends on us taking a closer look at our behaviors, communities and values.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Loathe At First Sight: A Novel
author: Suzanne Park
description: Melody Joo, a Korean American, is a new production assistant at Seventeen Studios, her first job in the very male-dominated gaming industry. She doesn’t expect it to be easy – but still, she’s shocked and angered by the blatant sexism and racism that flows widely and directly from the CEO’s desk. A slow-burning romance is at the center of Loathe at First Sight, a delightful, eye-opening story about a young woman’s quest to gain her stripes in an industry she loves – and a man who doesn’t mind taking a back seat and letting his heroine shine.

title: The Boyfriend Project
author: Farrah Rochon
description: The Boyfriend Project is the first novel in Farrah Rochon’s new rom-com series, and it uses a clever premise to introduce a memorable hero and heroine – and an equally captivating group of female friends. After being catfished, Samiah Brooks puts her career first and her boyfriend prospects on the back burner. But then she meets her new officemate, Daniel Collins. It’s a romp with plenty of laughs and dialogue that pops, and much more: Rochon also layers in a sweet dose of intrigue to fuel the sexy fun.

title: Breath: The New Science Of A Lost Art
author: James Nestor
description: Between COVID-19 and wildfire smoke exposure, the health of my lungs has been top of mind this year. James Nestor’s perfectly timed book looks at what is known through research and what is known by experience – often his own painful experience – as he submits to experiments and breathwork techniques to learn more about something we typically take for granted. Reading this book, I gained both a greater appreciation for my respiratory system and a personal-best long-distance run (breathing through the nose = greater endurance)!

title: Conjure Women: A Novel
author: Afia Atakora
description: This extraordinary novel made me realize that I haven’t really challenged myself with complex novels since graduating from high school.
