Because that’s what I was feeling – challenged – in the first chapters following secretive Rue, a young Black woman enslaved on a plantation during the Civil War. Rue’s story gradually unfolds with a series of jaw-dropping plot twists that blur superstition with reality and transform the way the reader sees both Rue and her world. The result is one of the most imaginative and intricate stories I’ve read in a long time – one that almost made me wish I was back in English lit class.

title: Roadside Americans: The Rise And Fall Of Hitchhiking In A Changing Nation
author: Jack Reid
description: Why read a book about hitchhiking? Because Jack Reid uses the practice to examine shifts in American culture during the 20th century. From being an economic necessity during the Great Depression to becoming a part of the counterculture in the 1960s, by the late ‘70s and ‘80s hitchhiking was seen as dangerous: The hitchhiker was often depicted in TV and film as a deranged psychopath. Roadside Americans carries the reader back to a time when Americans gave people rides without being paid for it.

title: Up All Night: Ted Turner, CNN, And The Birth Of 24-Hour News
author: Lisa Napoli
description: Now that “the 24-hour news cycle” is a catchphrase, it’s hard to remember a time when there was no news between 11:30 p.m. and 7 a.m. Lisa Napoli’s book is an engrossing reminder. She takes us into the thrown-together beginnings of CNN, introduces us to the players (including a pre-NPR Daniel Schorr) and gives us a fascinating portrait of founder Ted Turner, the man who wasn’t a fan of the news but did more to make it ubiquitous than almost anyone. It’s a fun and instructive read that will give you more respect for what the network has become.

title: Weather: A Novel
author: Jenny Offill
description: I think ahead to my death the way it must feel to experience it: a time when the present is finally over. Though I also worry, as we all must now, about a death one order higher: the end, as the world warms, of the future. Weather is Jenny Offill’s surreal and oddly buoyant novel about a woman going about the soft tedium of life – her work as a librarian, her responsibilities to her young son and brother (recovering from addiction) – while staring down inevitable demise, of her present and all of our futures.

title: Blacktop Wasteland: A Novel
author: S.A. Cosby
description: Blacktop Wasteland is a powerful read full of racial commentary that looks at what it means to be Black in Appalachia – Black people are a big part of Appalachian culture, but rural noir hasn’t done a great job of presenting that. This novel does, and that makes it a necessary, timely read. It’s also a galloping thriller that explores the ways in which crime is often inevitable – and sometimes just too good to pass up. With great dialogue and enough horsepower to make Evel Knievel smile, Blacktop Wasteland moves forward at breakneck speed and establishes S.A. Cosby as a crime writer to watch.

title: Summer: A Novel
author: Ali Smith
description: With Summer, Ali Smith wraps up her amazing seasonal quartet, written and set over the last four years, from the 2016 Brexit vote to the COVID-19 pandemic. This final volume, like the others, is fueled by dismay, hope, heart and flat-out brilliance and reveals overarching connections between recurrent characters and themes. What will keep these books fresh long after the news cycle has moved on is their passionate engagement with the life-enhancing powers of love, art, decency and human connection.

title: Beowulf: A New Translation
author: Maria Dahvana Headley
description: This might be the best thing you read all year. It will absolutely be the weirdest. The most exuberant. The bro-iest literary sensation of the age. No matter how you think you feel about the source material – if you loved it/hated it, if it traumatized you, if it ruined and/or energized the English language for you or whatever – Headley’s Beowulf is something different entirely. Yes, it tells the story of Beowulf and Hrothgar and Grendel and Grendel’s mom and the dragon and Wiglaf and everything. Only here, in Headley’s version, Beowulf becomes its truest self: an epic poem made to be shouted over the howls of mead-drunk Spear-Danes as they toast the fallen and lovingly punch each other to sleep. It is thousand-year-old slam poetry full of blood and honor, rap battles, sea monsters and treasure hoards. And if you don’t love it, you’re gonna absolutely despise it, because with Headley’s Beowulf, there is no middle ground.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: The Hidden Girl And Other Stories
author: Ken Liu
description: Ken Liu’s new collection of shorts spools out less like a series of discrete stories and more like a series of tightly interlinked, self-referential chapters of a distributed novel, broken up by diversions, digressions, thought experiments and a couple of pieces that read like intellectual exercises in imaginary brand PR. But there’s beauty to be found in them. Occasionally a deep strangeness. And always an intelligence that feels sharply forward-looking, if sometimes a little bit cold.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: The Missing American
author: Kwei Quartey
description: A lonely widower from Washington, D.C., goes missing in Ghana after he’s duped in a trans-Atlantic love scam blessed by an influential fetish priest. Emma Djan, a 26-year-old newbie private investigator in Accra, immerses herself in the world of sakawa Internet fraud and finds that it reaches the upper echelons of power in the West African country, putting her in mortal danger. The Missing American is a mystery and thriller that kept me spellbound – and rooting for Emma.

title: Fauja Singh Keeps Going: The True Story Of The Oldest Person To Ever Run A Marathon
author: Simran Jeet Singh, illustrated by Baljinder Kaur
description: Lace up your running shoes! Young Fauja’s legs are too weak to carry him to school, yet he’s determined to become strong and healthy. A lifetime later, after leaving Punjab to join his family in London, he’s in his 80s when he discovers the joy of running marathons. When fame follows, Fauja is happy his fans are learning about his Sikh background. With lively art by Baljinder Kaur and fast-moving text by Simran Jeet Singh, Fauja Singh Keeps Going will have you cheering as he becomes the first 100-year-old in history to run all 26.2 miles of the marathon. (For ages 4 to 8)

title: Prairie Lotus
author: Linda Sue Park
description: This is not your mother’s Little House on the Prairie. Though the era, area and some of the characters in Prairie Lotus may feel familiar, the circumstances are wildly different. Upon the death of her Chinese mother, Hanna, who is half white, is uprooted from California to the Dakota Territory, where her father plans to open a dry goods store. A talented seamstress, the 14-year-old is immediately subject to suspicion and prejudice. In Linda Sue Park’s triumphant novel, Hanna must rely on her humor, determination and wits to succeed in this refreshingly different viewpoint of 1880s prairie life. (For ages 10 to 12)

title: Once I Was You: A Memoir Of Love And Hate In A Torn America
author: Maria Hinojosa
description: Maria Hinojosa traces her path to becoming an award-winning journalist, starting with her roots in Mexico City and her upbringing in Chicago. Hinojosa worked for NPR, CNN and others before launching her own media company dedicated largely to telling the stories of immigrants. Hinojosa reveals challenges in her marriage and her struggles with impostor syndrome – doubting her skills and accomplishments. She recounts being treated as “other” – female and Latina – in mostly white newsrooms. As Hinojosa describes her own career, Once I Was You also weaves in the history of a broken U.S. immigration system – creating a powerful chronicle of the country’s complicated relationship with immigrants.

title: Born To Be Public
author: Greg Mania
description: After this year, we could all use a laugh – or, better yet, a lot of them. Enter Greg Mania (not a pen name!) and his hilarious debut memoir about coming out as gay, spending time in New York clubs and becoming a comedy writer. Mania is charming, honest and, above all, laugh-out-loud funny, and while he doesn’t take himself too seriously, he writes elegantly about both the low and high points of his life. This is a gift of a book from a young writer who seems likely to become a comedy star.

title: His Very Best: Jimmy Carter, A Life
author: Jonathan Alter
description: You’ve probably heard the cliché about Jimmy Carter: His presidency was largely a failure, but he’s the best ex-president the country has ever had. But in this fascinating biography of the man from Plains, Jonathan Alter paints a much more complex portrait of the president and his legacy. Alter obviously admires Carter, but the book is fair-minded, portraying Carter as a man with his own flaws and not a saint. Anyone who’s interested in modern American history will find a lot to love in this captivating book.

title: The Glass Hotel: A Novel
author: Emily St. John Mandel
description: Emily St. John Mandel’s 2014 pandemic-themed novel, Station Eleven, proved to be especially timely this year, but her newest book, about two half siblings haunted by their pasts, is just as good – if not better – than its predecessor. It’s a stunning look at how people react to disasters, both small and large, and the temptation that some have to give up when faced with tragedy. As she writes, “The problem with dropping out of the world is that the world moves on without you.”

title: The Redshirt: A Novel
author: Corey Sobel
description: Corey Sobel’s stunning debut novel follows two college football players – Miles, a closeted gay man, and Reshawn, a quiet prodigy – as they navigate their lives at a Southern college. Sobel, who played football for Duke University, looks at the toxic masculinity that has defined the sport since its inception, with a gimlet eye and a rare sensitivity. The Redshirt is an understated yet seething novel about what it means to be a man and is one of the best football novels to come along in recent years.

title: The Regrets
author: Amy Bonnaffons
description: You don’t get to choose whom you fall in love with. That’s the lesson that Rachel, a young librarian living in Brooklyn, learns in this enchanting debut novel – she develops a big crush on Thomas, a handsome young man who, inconveniently, happens to be a ghost. Bonnaffons follows the couple as the two navigate their fiery relationship, and she handles the conceit beautifully. It’s a miracle of a love story, asking perceptive questions about the line between love and (literal) possession.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Run Me To Earth
author: Paul Yoon
description: When I was reading this tragic and beautiful novel, I thought of Wilfred Owen’s lines: “My subject is War, and the pity of War. / The Poetry is in the pity.” Paul Yoon tells the story of teenage orphans Alisak, Prany and Noi, who in the 1960s have come together in the Laotian countryside to make a family united by loss as the U.S. bombing raids on their small country (less than half the size of Texas) continue unabated. The stunning prose – like poetry, really – is spare, subtle and unsentimental, creating a novel that will stay with the reader long after the last page is turned.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Voyage Of The Morning Light: A Novel
author: Marina Endicott
description: Twelve-year-old orphan Kay Ward accompanies her older half sister, Thea, aboard a merchant ship in 1912, traveling from Nova Scotia to Micronesia and home again, under the command of Kay’s new brother-in-law. On one of their stops, Thea trades some tobacco for a little boy who is then raised as Kay’s brother. One might fear that the big issues raised by this beautiful novel – questions of colonialism, what “goodness” means, cultural differences – might sink it into didacticism, but the amazing descriptions of the voyages themselves and the three-dimensionality of the characters, especially Kay, make this a journey worth taking.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Shuggie Bain: A Novel
author: Douglas Stuart
description: A bravura roar of a first novel plunges readers into the heart of one boy’s life in a Scottish housing project in the 1980s. Shuggie Bain, a “wee poofter” by community consensus, holds himself together amid pounding abuse – his own at the hands of other boys, his father’s savagery toward his mother, his mother’s hopeless addiction to alcohol and the wrenching experience of Glasgow’s industrial class during Thatcher-era privatization. But Shuggie Bain is rapturously resilient. Douglas Stuart’s exultant pleasure in recounting the small, lovely details of existence make this powerful novel a work of absolute joy.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Dance On Saturday: Stories
author: Elwin Cotman
description: Elwin Cotman’s third collection of short fiction is only six stories long, but each story is packed with rich depth, like jeweled fruits glinting in wet loam. Mixing mythic and contemporary, humor and horror, melancholy and optimism, Cotman’s stories range from present-day Pittsburgh to fantasy Africa, with a beautifully flexible polyvocal prose. My two favorite stories make up about half the book: “Seven Watsons” and the title story both deliver all the sophistication and complexity of a novel at a third of the length, and center Black joy and endurance.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Americana", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: A Silenced Voice: The Life Of Journalist Kim Wall
author: Ingrid Wall and Joachim Wall
description: The reporter Kim Wall was 30 when she was murdered on assignment. This book by her parents, the journalists Ingrid and Joachim Wall, is an intimate portrait of their daughter. A Silenced Voice is also intended to raise awareness for the Kim Wall Memorial Fund, an organization they set up to send women on reporting projects around the world. With it, the Walls are working to make sure that Kim Wall’s name will not be a warning, but a tag under ambitious investigative pieces, a line on resumes, a ticket and a calling card. What an act of love.

title: Funny Weather: Art In An Emergency
author: Olivia Laing
description: Olivia Laing’s new nonfiction collection, Funny Weather: Art in an Emergency, is a group of essays loosely constellated around the question “What use is art – and the people who think and talk about it – in a moment of crisis?” The pieces, which jump from Freddie Mercury to gardening to migration to Ali Smith, showcase Laing as an imaginative and empathetic critic. She gets at texture, technique, feeling and politics all at once. And even better, her question is never “Is this good?” but rather “What might this work of art do for someone?”

title: Notes From An Apocalypse: A Personal Journey To The End Of The World And Back
author: Mark O'Connell
description: Mark O’Connell’s new book about the end of the world is not called Notes from the Apocalypse, but rather Notes from an Apocalypse – a gesture of articular modesty that points to a larger truth: Despite the climate crisis, despite a global pandemic, it has always been “the end of the world for someone, somewhere.” In a chain of charming, anxious and tender essays, O’Connell examines his own apocalyptic frame of mind by taking “a series of perverse pilgrimages” to subcultures devoted to preparing for the end. It’s a morbid delight.

title: Piranesi
author: Susanna Clarke
description: Susanna Clarke’s debut novel, Jonathan Strange & Mr. Norrell, was an epic blockbuster about the return of ancient magic to England during the Napoleonic wars. After it was published, Clarke struggled with an illness that left her disoriented and housebound. Now, 16 years later, she has returned with this mythic story about a man who is himself disoriented and trapped in a mysterious sort of house. His labyrinth is a mansion with oceans roaring through the basement and endless halls of marble statues. Piranesi is smaller than its predecessor in every way, but it is no less bewitching.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Swimming In The Dark: A Novel
author: Tomasz Jedrowski
description: Remember the feeling of the last day of summer camp? Nostalgia for something you haven’t quite lost yet? Tomasz Jedrowski captures that wistfulness in his debut novel, set in 1980s communist Poland. Two young men meet and fall in love. One chafes against the restrictions of society; the other finds ways to thrive within the confines of the regime. Jedrowski’s writing reminds us that even in the face of oppression, life continues. As he told me, “People still fall in love. People still go skinny-dipping. People still smoke cigarettes. And people still dream.”
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: Why Fish Don't Exist: A Story Of Loss, Love, And The Hidden Order Of Life
author: Lulu Miller
description: This is the book I’ve recommended to people more than any other in the past year. Lulu Miller, founder and former host of NPR’s Invisibilia podcast, weaves together personal memoir and scientific history to ask questions about how we maintain optimism in the face of adversity and find order amid chaos. If that sounds weighty, let me reassure you – the book is surprising and delightful, with one unexpected turn after another. There’s a murder mystery … and also fish. Actually, there aren’t fish. Y’know what? Just read the book.

title: Honeybee: The Busy Life Of Apis Mellifera
author: Candace Fleming, illustrated by Eric Rohmann
description: A lusciously illustrated Apis mellifera rendered in velvety oil paints cycles through multiple chores in a hive. Young readers get a front row seat to the natural world while jaunty narration ratchets up the suspense leading to the bee’s first flight. Think each bee has just one job? Think again. Bees hold a variety of occupations during their short, lovely lives – and you thought you were busy! (For ages 6 to 9)

title: Antkind: A Novel
author: Charlie Kaufman
description: In screenwriter and director Charlie Kaufman’s debut novel Antkind, the film critic protagonist is the only person to see what he believes to be the greatest film of all time – and then the lone copy is destroyed in a fire that also injures him and erases much of his memory. He tries to re-create the film based on a lone surviving frame. The quest takes readers through the darkest corners of the protagonist’s mind and to the front of an apocalyptic war. This chaotic novel is a remarkable experience, especially for fans of Kaufman’s films such as Adaptation.

title: How Much Of These Hills Is Gold: A Novel
author: C Pam Zhang
description: The Western gold rush is in full swing. Ma’s long gone, and when Ba dies, siblings Lucy and Sam are suddenly on their own – Chinese American orphans in a scrappy mining town. They set out for the hills with Ba’s body in a chest, determined to give him a proper burial. But nothing comes easy in a place that is as cruel as it is beautiful. C Pam Zhang’s stunning debut novel wends forward and backward in time, asking in stark terms what it takes to claim a piece of the American dream.

title: Ghost Squad
author: Claribel A. Ortega
description: For as long as she can remember, loving ghosts of Lucely Luna’s extended family have been there to help her and keep her company. When a dark force threatens them, she teams up with her best friend, Syd, and accidentally unleashes a terrible magic spell. Together, they must try to save their town from the curse – or else Lucely may lose her family ghosts for good. This middle grade adventure tale has all the best elements of an old-timey girl detective story combined with spooky supernatural high jinks and a loveable cast. And there’s a chubby tabby cat named Chunk. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Girl Gone Viral: A Novel
author: Alisha Rai
description: Going viral can be stressful for anyone, but investor Katrina King is dealing with past trauma, and she really values her privacy. So when Katrina ends up in the public eye, her bodyguard Jas whisks her away to his family’s peach farm. As Katrina learns more about Jas’ upbringing, she starts to see him as more than just her quiet, dashing security detail. This is the second novel in Alisha Rai’s “Modern Love” series, which deftly explores technology and dating app culture without being cheesy or overdone.

title: Party Of Two
author: Jasmine Guillory
description: Jasmine Guillory’s fantastic fifth novel follows Olivia, a lawyer who strikes out on her own and moves to Los Angeles to start her own firm. While sitting alone at a bar, she meets Max, a mysterious man who turns out to be a U.S. senator. As she falls for Max, Olivia grapples with the complications of dating a public figure, and with the complexities of an interracial relationship. It kind of made me think: Meghan Markle and Prince Harry … the American version.

title: A Shadow Intelligence
author: Oliver Harris
description: Modern spy thrillers are increasingly high-tech and cyber-focused. Agent Elliot Kane receives a text message with a video attachment that shows him in a room he is certain he has never been in. It all launches a personal mission to Kazakhstan where the lines between love, truth, fiction and geopolitical interests blur. The vivid description of the less familiar landscape makes the race to get answers even more compelling because the terrain turns out to be the only thing that is what it appears to be. This is a spy novel that will leave you thinking about what cyberthreats each of us could face – and it’s still good entertainment despite the disturbing questions it poses.

title: Between Two Fires: Truth, Ambition, And Compromise In Putin's Russia
author: Joshua Yaffa
description: If you want to understand life in Russia under Vladimir Putin, this book is a must-read. Joshua Yaffa unspools a series of portraits, each of which reveals the choices, accommodations and compromises facing modern Russians who want to get ahead and get along in a state system that doesn’t leave much room for doing your own thing your own way. Each character portrayed is comfortable figuring out how to work within and occasionally gently around the system. The people, their stories and their decisions are utterly fascinating looks into an authoritarian mind game.

title: Djinn Patrol On The Purple Line
author: Deepa Anappara
description: This is an empathetic story of disappearing children in India told from a child’s point of view. This fictional account of a serious problem is told with humor, grace and tension – giving a sense of the overcrowded slums and the friendship, suspicion and love found within them. It is amazing that this is a debut novel.

title: Rust: A Memoir Of Steel And Grit
author: Eliese Goldbach
description: Eliese Goldbach is not your typical steelworker. But in the difficult, dangerous work, she finds camaraderie with her fellow laborers and the perspective necessary to face past trauma and find her place in the world. This is a journey of discovery for both the reader and the author.

title: Why We Swim
author: Bonnie Tsui
description: Why We Swim is a cultural history of humankind’s relationship to bodies of water, an exploration of the benefits and dangers of submerging one’s own body in it, a highlight reel of athletic feats of swimming and diving – and so much more. Author Bonnie Tsui creates space for readers to meditate on their own experiences in the water. As I read it I found an escape, but also a connection to the water and to fellow humans who are called to it.

title: World Of Wonders: In Praise Of Fireflies, Whale Sharks, And Other Astonishments
author: Aimee Nezhukumatathil, illustrated by Fumi Nakamura
description: From its gorgeous illustrations to its unusual combination of lyrical nature writing and memoir, World of Wonders is hands-down one of the most beautiful books of the year. It’s about identity – both cultural and horticultural – and finding one’s place in the world, in part by keeping one’s eyes wide open to natural wonders. A catalpa tree on the Mississippi campus where Aimee Nezhukumatathil teaches creative writing stirs memories of the state mental institution in Kansas where her Filipina mother was a doctor. Peacocks, the national bird of her father’s native India, evoke unpleasant memories of a narrow-minded teacher in Arizona. The man she ends up marrying passed the corpse flower test: He doesn’t disparage her enthusiasm for them or “wince when I said the word inflorescence.”
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana", "Art"]

title: Writers & Lovers: A Novel
author: Lily King
description: Writers & Lovers, Lily King’s fifth novel, won’t transport you to an exotic locale like Euphoria, her most recent, but it’s a wonderful read. Set in 1997, it’s narrated by utterly sympathetic Casey Peabody, who’s struggling to finish her  first novel – as King was at that time – under the weight of college loan debts and grief over her mother’s sudden death. Things aren’t going so great in her love life, either, but soon Casey has two very different men to choose from – an older, established author and a high school teacher with a rusted-out car. This is a winning, beautifully written novel about passion, determination, finding one’s way, and the choices involved in holding onto a dream.

title: Miss Iceland
author: Auður Ava Ólafsdóttir
description: This book is a find. Set mostly in 1963 in windy, overcast Reykjavik, Miss Iceland is an atmospheric portrait of insidious sexism and homophobia in a society that had yet to open up to either women or gay men. Its wry narrator, Hekla, named for a volcano, is a copiously talented but thwarted young writer, constantly harassed by male patrons at her waitressing job and belittled by her boyfriend, a preening poet jealous of her clearly superior work. Hekla’s queer, persecuted best friend, Jón John, dreams of becoming a theatrical costume designer. The two plot their escape to Denmark, where they hope to live more fulfilled lives, but even that comes with unsettling tradeoffs.

title: Owls Of The Eastern Ice: A Quest To Find And Save The World's Largest Owl
author: Jonathan C. Slaght
description: Like the best nature writing, this is a book fueled by obsession – with both Russia’s remote, forested Primorye Province and with the endangered, oversized, salmon-eating raptor called Blakiston’s fish owl that lives there. Part wilderness adventure, part patient scientific research, part doctoral fieldwork and wholly engaging narrative nonfiction, Owls of the Eastern Ice is filled with daunting, frigid challenges and the rare birds and colorful characters who brave them.
tags: ["Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Wintering: The Power Of Rest And Retreat In Difficult Times
author: Katherine May
description: English writer Katherine May’s beautiful and unintentionally but uncannily timely book is about what she calls “wintering,” a way to weather tough periods when you feel cut off, sidelined or overwhelmed. Brought low by a perfect storm of personal challenges, May learns to slow down, hibernate and regroup. She becomes convinced that the cold has healing powers and explores how other creatures and cultures cope with the dark, frigid season. She takes up ice swimming, cradles an amazingly soft hibernating dormouse and considers the profusion of wolves and snow in fairy tales. May finds solace in her explorations, and readers, especially in these trying times of social distancing, will too.

title: Collective Gravities
author: Chloe N. Clark
description: Two of the best stories in Collective Gravities, the debut collection by Chloe N. Clark, deal with disease. “Bound” is the tale of a mysterious pandemic and the tangled lives of the scientists searching for a cure for it; “Thematic Cartography” traces the progress of a strange disease that leaves graphs and text across the skin of the afflicted. Collective Gravities was written before COVID-19, but its speculation about illness, disconnection and the future of love is uncannily prescient. At the same time, Clark veers from science fiction and magical realism in a way that’s both mind-blowing and tender.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Short Stories & Essays", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Dark Blood Comes From The Feet
author: Emma J. Gibbon
description: While the prospect of wallowing in the murkier end of the emotional spectrum isn’t exactly high on anyone’s list right now, there’s a lot to be said for confronting our demons on the printed page as well as in real life. Emma J. Gibbon gets it. In Dark Blood Comes From the Feet, her debut story collection, Gibbon serves up 17 acidic tales that eat away at society’s thin veneer of normalcy, convention and even reality. At the same time, these horrific confections – like those of Shirley Jackson – leave a bittersweet aftertaste of humanity
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Short Stories & Essays", "Americana"]

title: Hearts Of Oak
author: Eddie Robson
description: Today’s speculative fiction is full of astounding innovations and lavish spectacles. Within that genre-expanding sprawl, however, there’s still room for short, humble, understated works like Hearts of Oak. British author Eddie Robson packs his quirky novel – about a strange, wooden town ruled by a construction-mad king – with social satire, swift-witted dialogue and an air of absurdist sci-fi/fantasy in the tradition of Robson’s fellow Brits Douglas Adams and Terry Pratchett.

title: Headliners: An Enemies-To-Lovers Romance
author: Lucy Parker
description: If you love The Morning Show and Broadcast News like I do, you’ll likely have a soft spot for Lucky Parker’s latest book. Sabrina and Nick are rival television presenters who must team up to save a floundering London morning show and their careers. Sparks fly, of course, and Parker is one of the best romance writers in the game. The banter is top-notch, and the hosts have undeniable chemistry. This book is a wonderful distraction.

title: Barn 8: A Novel
author: Deb Olin Unferth
description: A wild romp with a sneaky message, Barn 8 wonders what it means to engage in protest – and how far you have to go to actually get anything to change. Our heroes: two USDA egg auditors, Janey and Cleveland. Their mission: Liberate a million chickens from an Iowa farm. High jinks and sly social commentary ensue. Also, there’s a chapter told from the point of view of a nonconformist chicken that should tell you everything you need to know about this sharply absurdist heist novel.

title: Thin Places: Essays From In Between
author: Jordan Kisner
description: Jordan Kisner’s essays range from circling her curiosity around a neurosurgical procedure for obsessive compulsive disorder, to reporting on a debutante ball in a Texas border town where Mexican American girls dress as Martha Washington, to shadowing a medical examiner performing autopsies in Cleveland. What unites them all is an exploration of the liminal – states of in-between, or the titular “thin places,” where potential and tension swell. Kisner’s curiosity is contagious, and as she glides smoothly between cultural commentary, reportage and personal probing, she illuminates how flimsy boundaries are when we look at them hard enough.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Midnight At The Barclay Hotel
author: Fleur Bradley, illustrated by Xavier Bonet
description: Recipients of an unexpected invitation arrive at the historic and haunted Barclay Hotel in the Rockies, looking forward to a free and relaxing weekend getaway. The catch? They’ve been unwittingly convened to solve a crime. It turns out one of them is … a murderer! Read on as kid “stragglers” JJ and Penny team up with their new friend Emma to solve the mystery. Ghost hunting, malfunctioning machinery, deadly desserts, a spring snowstorm, no Wi-Fi and a nice bit of teamwork are all in store for this trio. This book is for those kids who might have a secret or feel a little out of place, and are ready for a sweet and mild first taste of red herrings. Cheers to those meddling kids. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Hamnet
author: Maggie O'Farrell
description:
Verbatim transcript of my last book club meeting:
“So, what should we read for our next book?”
“We should read … um … Oh, hell, can’t we just read Hamnet again? And again? Like, just keep reading it? It was soooo good.”
My book club, a dozen seriously sharp ladies of all ages, never unanimously loves anything. Hamnet was the exception. It’s a novel that explores the connection between what was arguably William Shakespeare’s greatest play, Hamlet, and the death of his only son four years before. It’s a beautiful love story, and the writing is so gorgeous that I was often reading it through tears.

title: The Women In Black: A Novel
author: Madeleine St. John
description: This little novel powered me through the first dismaying weeks of the pandemic and, immediately after reading it, I ordered five more copies to distribute as “emergency mental health reading” for friends having a tough time. Written in 1993 and published in America for the first time, The Women in Black is set in a fictional Australian department store in the 1950s and focuses on four saleswomen who work in the “better dresses” department. Tart, beguiling, witty and compassionate, Madeleine St. John’s novel is a literary boost for the spirits.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction"]

title: The Care and Feeding Of Waspish Widows
author: Olivia Waite
description: In Regency England, the widowed owner of a London printshop, Agatha Griffin, finds more than she bargained for in her warehouse in a small town outside the city – a swarm of bees and a very enticing beekeeper, Penelope Flood. First friendship, then attraction, blossoms – though plenty of complications ensue, including the return of Penelope’s husband and the rise of an anti-vice society. This is a gorgeously written novel with beautiful worldbuilding and vividly composed characters that leaves the reader with a feeling that a happy ever after is possible at any age, in any age.

title: Interior Chinatown: A Novel
author: Charles Yu
description: Interior Chinatown is written partly like a screenplay and takes the idea that we’re all at the center of our own movie literally. You’re never quite sure whether Willis Wu really works as an actor playing “Generic Asian man,” but it ultimately doesn’t matter. Charles Yu plays with how we internalize stereotypes in the movies in this sometimes devastating, sometimes hilarious, but always captivating novel.

title: The Wax Pack: On The Open Road In Search Of Baseball's Afterlife
author: Brad Balukjian
description: The Wax Pack begins with one unopened pack of baseball cards from 1986 – and an ancient slice of chalky gum. Curious to know what happened to more than a dozen former baseball players after they hung up their caps and gloves, writer Brad Balukjian embarks on a cross-country quest to find out. It’s part baseball history, part personal travelogue, part rumination on the passage of time. But what makes The Wax Pack so compelling is that it’s not particularly interested in the heroic rise of a champion, nor the tragic decline of a baseball career, but rather the everyday lives that unfold after the fleeting spotlight of the big leagues dims.

title: One Life
author: Megan Rapinoe and Emma Brockes
description: I would give anything to go back in time and relive the pure joy and electricity of the 2019 Women’s World Cup. Luckily, with Megan Rapinoe’s memoir, I (sort of) can. The pink-haired co-captain has always been a fierce voice for social justice. She was one of the first high-profile white athletes to take a knee during the national anthem in solidarity with Black Americans protesting police brutality. The team famously sued the U.S. Soccer Federation for pay discrimination and became a symbol of gender inequality. Rapinoe’s memoir brings you onto the field for key moments in her soccer career – but also shows the making of an intersectional activist.

title: All The Way To The Tigers: A Memoir
author: Mary Morris
description: When Mary Morris was badly injured in an ice skating accident, it took a quote from Thomas Mann’s Death in Venice to get her out of her funk: “He would go on a journey. Not far. Not all the way to the tigers.” Morris, a longtime traveler and memoirist, decided that when she could walk again she would go all the way to the tigers, to India. The best travel memoirs offer readers three pleasures woven together: accounts of “what I saw,” “how I came to understand myself better” and “what I learned about the world” – and Morris’ memoir doesn’t skimp on any of them.

title: The Autobiography Of Alice B. Toklas Illustrated
author: Gertrude Stein, illustrated by Maira Kalman
description: Originally published in 1933, Stein’s “autobiography” of her longtime lover Toklas and their life together in literary and artistic Paris in the first half of the 20th century remains as fascinatingly quirky now as it did when it was first published. Was there a writer or artist who didn’t make their way to Toklas and Stein’s salon? Picasso, Matisse, Hemingway, Ford Madox Ford … the list goes on. What makes this new edition so special are the terrific illustrations by Maira Kalman, whose colorful paintings capture the spirit of Stein’s idiosyncratic text (and prose) perfectly.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Art", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture"]

title: Here We Are: A Novel
author: Graham Swift
description: What characterizes every novel by Graham Swift are its brilliant, spare prose and beautifully conceived characters whose seemingly quietly ordinary lives contain multitudes. During the summer of 1959, in a vaudeville show on the pier in Brighton, England, the intertwined lives of three performers – Jack Robbins, the show’s master of ceremonies, Ronnie Deane, magician extraordinaire, and his assistant Evie White – are related in flashbacks and flashforwards. In 200 pages Swift, a magician with words, shows us life’s mysteries, its rewards and its tragedies.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Inventing Latinos: A New Story Of American Racism
author: Laura E. Gómez
description: Latinos are the second-largest demographic in the United States, but is “Latino” an ethnic identity or a racial one? How can Latino be a race if Latinos themselves can be various races? In her book, Inventing Latinos, UCLA Law professor Laura E. Gómez makes the case that Latinos are both. She does so by examining the ways that American colonialism in Latin America and laws in the United States have led to the racialization of Latinos by denying them “full and equal participation in American life.”

title: Tender Is The Flesh
author: Agustina Bazterrica, translated by Sarah Moses
description: In a near-future in which cannibalism is legal, humans are bred to be eaten and graves are ransacked for tasty cadavers. A man who works in a meat processing facility suddenly finds himself with a costly luxury gift: a human who should be consumed for her flesh. This is a bleak, relentless dystopia that astounds with its precise, cutting prose. It’s also a delight to read fiction that does not fit into the magical realist mold that the publishing industry demands of most Latin American writers.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Amnesty: A Novel
author: Aravind Adiga
description: Dhananjaya “Danny” Rajaratnam is living illegally in Sydney, Australia, cleaning houses and working in a grocery store (the stockroom doubles as his living quarters). Danny needs to be seen as legal but also invisible. He offers wry observations of the people, politics and attitudes around him and reflects on the life he fled in Sri Lanka. Oh, also, there’s a murder and Danny believes he knows who did it. But who can he tell? He will be caught if he goes to the police. Despite the heavy premise, author Aravind Adiga knows how to add humor and absurdity where it counts. His well-timed comedic beats keep the pages turning, making this an easy weekend read that tackles a serious subject with wit and humanity.

title: The Journeys Of Trees: A Story About Forests, People, And The Future
author: Zach St. George
description: Spoiler alert: forests migrate. They are on the move away from danger and always have been. Now, these are not the dour-faced Ents of J.R.R. Tolkien’s Lord of the Rings, who are downright speedy compared to the agonizingly slow march of Earth’s trees and forests. But the journeys of trees, and the forces that drive that titular phrase, are an engrossing tale of nature’s survival, the increasing speed of climate change and of the people working to preserve these most ancient creatures. Author and reporter Zach St. George presents a thoroughly researched story about what we can learn from some of Earth’s oldest residents if we just stop and listen.

title: A Burning: A Novel
author: Megha Majumdar
description: A Burning begins with some tragic news: There’s been a terrorist attack at an Indian train station; some 100 people have died in a train station bombing. A woman named Jivan posts about the attack on Facebook without a second thought. Days later, she’s arrested and blamed for the attack. When I started reading A Burning, I had no idea what to expect. I got angrier as I read, anticipating how it would end and frustrated at the injustice the Muslim main character faces. It’s interesting to note that while the book was a bestseller in America, it was met with mixed reviews in India. All I can say is that I wasn’t able to put this book down thanks to the power of Megha Majumdar’s writing. This book is a must-read.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Heart Of Junk: A Novel
author: Luke Geddes
description: Heart of Junk might just be the most fun I’ve ever had with kidnapping and sleazy salespeople. Set in a failing antique mall in Kansas, Luke Geddes’ debut novel follows a fantastically colorful cast of characters, each with their own emotional – and literal – baggage: Neurotic Delores is out for every Barbie ever made (she enjoys holding conversations with each one in her sizable collection), and Ronald, a recent widower, is the resident deltiologist (that’s postcard collector for us laypeople). Follow along as they work to save the mall and find a missing local celebrity. It’s a story full of heart, packed with wit and, yes, overflowing with (lovable) junk.

title: Beach Read
author: Emily Henry
description: Beach Read lives up to its name. In 2020, I sought out romantic stories that allowed me to escape this dumpster fire of a year, and this book fit the bill. Two writers find themselves reunited in the same town one summer and – surprise! – they may or may not fall in love. There is a meet-cute (reunion) moment, a little connecting over mutual writer’s block and road trips, some witty banter with a bit of miscommunication thrown in and a ton of chemistry. Some dark elements in both writers’ pasts keep them from giving their full selves over to the relationship. If you’re looking to lose yourself in a fun romance, Beach Read is for you.

title: Uncanny Valley: A Memoir
author: Anna Wiener
description: There’s a certain haze one feels following a day spent bouncing among social media platforms, group chat apps and the various other products of Silicon Valley that our work and personal lives revolve around. You’re never quite sure what you’ve done or how much time has passed. Anna Wiener’s first-person account of her journey into the world of startups and tech companies captures that fog through the eyes of someone living and working right in the thick of it. If you’re a normal reader, you’ll love this book for its unflinching honesty about the Big Tech world we all now live in, for better or for worse. And if you, like me, spend your days immersed in the Internet, you’ll finish reading with a clearer view – like some of that haze has faded away.

title: To Calais, In Ordinary Time
author: James Meek
description: Some lowlife archers, a cleric, a gender-fluid pigkeeper, a dumb-but-hot peasant and a wronged noblewoman head to France and smack into the Black Death. James Meek gives both merciless pandemic Real Talk and a sort of Lonely Planet guide to medieval England. Master local lingo like gnof (lout), freke (brave man) and how to ask for the nearest confessor! Visit the hippest reliqueries, jousts and sickhouses! Learn which villages to avoid but blunder into anyway because you believe the plague is a hoax perpetrated by priests! And think deep thoughts about human responses to incoming doom, no matter the era.

title: The City We Became: A Novel
author: N.K. Jemisin
description: If your hometown was a person, what would she look like? What kind of shoes would they wear? How would he smell? What would you do if you came face to literal face with New York City? This is the world that N.K. Jemisin imagines in her fantasy novel The City We Became. This year, we need all the fantasy we can get, and Jemisin offers a multidimensional version of the world just outside your front door. New York’s five boroughs each become human avatars and walk through a world that is recognizable as our own yet wholly different, as they are under attack from a supernatural – and betentacled – enemy.

title: Legendary Children: The First Decade Of RuPaul’s Drag Race And The Last Century Of Queer Life
author: Tom Fitzgerald and Lorenzo Marquez
description: This book cleverly weaves aspects of RuPaul’s Drag Race – lip syncing, the Snatch Game, serving lewks – with the queer cultural history behind the show. In the introduction, Tom Fitzgerald and Lorenzo Marquez style bloggers Tom + Lorenzo, encourage readers to Google as they go, diving into Internet rabbit holes on various aspects of queer culture outlined in the book. Reading this book led me to discover a treasure trove of YouTube videos, documentaries and Wikipedia pages that I never would have found on my own.

title: New Waves: A Novel
author: Kevin Nguyen
description: I don’t want to read any more stories about incompetent white tech bros. Thankfully, Kevin Nguyen has written a refreshing satire of startup culture. Lucas and Margo are close friends and coworkers who often commiserate about the racism they face at work. Lucas, a low-level customer service rep, is Asian American, and Margo is a programmer and the lone Black employee at their company. When Margo dies in a car accident, Lucas decides to excavate Margo’s online persona and virtual life in order to process his grief and better get to know his friend. The novel unpacks varying types of loneliness, whether caused by grief or online interaction or because you are a POC facing racism in the workplace.

title: Having And Being Had
author: Eula Biss
description: Having and Being Had is a handbook, told in vignettes, on how to reckon with a life that bends toward owning things. Through Biss’ inquiry, one is reminded that capitalism is not inevitable and that currency goes well beyond the money in one’s pocket. This collection is curious, sharp, funny (truly) and full of questions we, as a society, have forgotten how to ask about how we spend, what we buy, why we work, what labor is and how we calculate worth.

title: We Ride Upon Sticks: A Novel
author: Quan Barry
description: It’s 1989 and the Danvers, Mass., field hockey team is looking for a win. And so, hundreds of years after a coven of teen girls kicked off the Salem witch trials nearby, the Lady Falcons give themselves over to a dark force known as Emilio (they seal the deal by signing their names in a notebook with Emilio Estevez on the cover). What follows is the story of teen girls figuring out where power comes from – who has it, who doesn’t and what it means to bump up against gender norms and social conventions. We Ride Upon Sticks is an absolute gift – a genuinely funny page turner with enough heart to win any championship.

title: Just Us: An American Conversation
author: Claudia Rankine
description: The title, says it all. Just Us is a collection of essays, photos, poems and conversations that poet Claudia Rankine has been having with friends and strangers about race. While Americans gather in demographic silos with a wink (“it’s just us, we can speak honestly here”) Rankine, who’s Black, invites us all to join in “An American Conversation.” It’s a tough, heartfelt and worthy exercise.

title: Sexual Citizens: A Landmark Study Of Sex, Power, And Assault On Campus
author: Jennifer S. Hirsch and Shamus Khan
description: Over five years, professors Jennifer Hirsch and Shamus Khan interviewed more than 150 Columbia and Barnard College undergrads about their sex lives. The research explored what students wanted out of sex, how troubling encounters unfolded and how layers of misunderstandings led to assault. This is the book to read to help lead conversations about sex. It reframes the dialogue and helps prepare young people for sexual relationships. Read it, share it. Empower the young folks in your life.

title: The Other Bennet Sister: A Novel
author: Janice Hadlow
description: Oh, what joy this year to be transported to Jane Austen’s England by revisiting and reconsidering her characters in Pride and Prejudice. Janice Hadlow explores whatever did happen to Mary, the middle Bennet sister? How does the oft-forgotten Bennet daughter use the city of London to define her life and allow her to grow into herself? This is an engaging coming-of-age story for Austenites and non-Austenites alike.

title: The Midnight Bargain
author: C.L. Polk
description: Set in a fantasy world with echoes of British Regency, The Midnight Bargain is a smart, compassionate book about the costs of making deals, who pays them and who profits. Beatrice Clayborn’s family counts on her to make a good marriage and save them from debt. She would much rather study magic in secret and use her considerable sorcerous powers on her family’s behalf. But that’s not something that is done in Chasland, where women must give up magic in favor of marriage or risk their children being born possessed. A sharp examination of birth control and parental leave delivered in gorgeous, velvety prose, this was an absolute pleasure to read.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Space Between Worlds
author: Micaiah Johnson
description: The multiverse is real, and travel between worlds is possible – but landing in a world where a version of you is alive will kill you on arrival. Consequently, the wealthy and powerful who want to plunder alternate worlds of resources and information need the most marginal people to do their dirty work for them: Only those whose existence is a small miracle can “traverse” effectively. Enter Cara, one of the best traversers – because in over 300 worlds, versions of her are only alive in eight. An absolutely stunning thriller, queer and fierce and smart: think Fury Road meets Orphan Black.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters"]

title: Anxious People: A Novel
author: Fredrik Backman, translated by Niel Smith
description: This is a story “about idiots.” It’s about a failed bank robbery and a highly unusual hostage situation. But what should be a nightmare scenario – a bunch of strangers held at gunpoint at an apartment open house – turns into a surprisingly sweet tale. It’s the perfect balance of heartwarming and heart-wrenching, and Fredrik Backman, author of A Man Called Ove, has a way of simply yet elegantly describing relationships. We can all be idiots, but we’re still all human beings worthy of connection and love.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Ghosting The News: Local Journalism And The Crisis Of American Democracy
author: Margaret Sullivan
description: News flash: Local news is disappearing. Margaret Sullivan, media columnist for The Washington Post, offers a stark warning about what could happen to our democracy if that trend continues. She writes that the health of a community is directly linked to the health of its local news and that good local reporting requires good resources. Readers know it – they just don’t always want to pay for it. With news deserts on the rise, people in power will remain unchecked. Sullivan explains why we should all be worried and start working to keep local news alive.

title: The Night Watchman
author: Louise Erdrich
description: When I find a new Louise Erdrich novel, I grab it with a grin and open it immediately. In The Night Watchman, we join Chippewa tribal councilman Thomas Wazhushk on his overnight shift at a factory near his band’s North Dakota reservation. It’s 1953, and Wazhushk is fighting a brutal federal plan to terminate his tribe’s sovereign status and rights to its land. He and other tribal members have an aching love for this place as they watch the turning of “the great wheel of the year,” “the leaves gold on green.” But the tribe is desperately poor, and its existence is under dire threat from a faraway bureaucracy. Wazhushk is based on Erdrich’s own grandfather, who waged a real-life struggle for his tribe’s survival. Reflecting on this, Erdrich writes, “… if you should be of the conviction that we are powerless to change … let this book give you heart.” I know it does me.

title: We Are Water Protectors
author: Carole Lindstrom, illustrated by Michaela Goade
description: Water is life! The Water Protectors at Standing Rock and beyond have been battling to defend Mother Earth’s sacred water to honor our ancestors, to provide for our peoples and for future generations. This exquisite picture book – seriously, it’s breathtaking – was written by Anishinabe/Métis Carole Lindstrom of the Turtle Mountain Band of Ojibwe and illustrated by Tlingit Michaela Goade. It’s not only one of the all-time best Native children’s books, but it’s one of the most beautiful and important picture books ever published – period. (For ages 3 to 6)

title: Sisters In Hate: American Women On The Front Lines Of White Nationalism
author: Seyward Darby
description: Sisters in Hate profiles three women who are, or have been, prominent figures in America’s white nationalist movement. That means it’s an intense read, but also a consistently compelling one – and journalist and author Seyward Darby’s unforced, artful writing keeps you turning the pages through the disturbing details of their beliefs and actions. The tales in Sisters are together a sort of dark counterpart to books like How to Be an Anti-Racist, which encourage white Americans to do the hard work of undoing a lifetime of racist programming. Darby presents becoming a white nationalist as the opposite of hard work. These women chose to go down a repulsive path, yes, but the path is well-paved.

title: Capital And Ideology
author: Thomas Piketty, translated by Arthur Goldhammer
description: Thomas Piketty’s Capital and Ideology surveys global inequality over the last few hundred years. Its first half is history backed up by inequality accounting. We’re taken through medieval Europe, slave and colonial societies, through Asia to reach the present, where the top 1% acquired 27% of global income growth between 1980 and 2018. Piketty’s later chapters describe policies and shifting political coalitions that could explain this rise in inequality. Charts show left-wing parties in the U.S. and Europe becoming parties for people with higher education and, increasingly, higher income. This reversal of postwar class coalitions, Piketty argues, is to blame.

title: Hanging Tree Guitars
author: Freeman Vines with Zoe van Buren, photography by Timothy Duffy
description: Part memoir, part photo essay, Hanging Tree Guitars is the story of North Carolina luthier Freeman Vines. He crafts guitars from found materials and hunks of old wood, including some from a tree once used for a lynching. Vines, 78, explains how the wood talks to him, and he reflects on growing up in a sharecropper’s family under Jim Crow. “I had learned that the white man was what controlled the code,” he says. Timothy Duffy’s tintype images of Vines and his guitars are stunning, including one where Vines is sitting in a field with his guitars strung on a frame overhead.

title: Race Against Time: A Reporter Reopens The Unsolved Murder Cases Of The Civil Rights Era
author: Jerry Mitchell
description: I’m a longtime fan of Mississippi investigative journalist Jerry Mitchell, and his memoir doesn’t disappoint. It’s a page-turner that reads like a mystery novel. But it’s frighteningly real – chronicling reporting that ultimately resulted in justice for some of the most notorious murders carried out by the Ku Klux Klan. Mitchell takes readers inside the investigations and prosecutions that, decades later, held Klansmen to account for killing civil rights leaders and workers in Mississippi, and four Black girls in a Birmingham church. I covered most of these trials for NPR, and Jerry’s memoir re-creates those historic events in riveting detail.

title: The Deepest South Of All: True Stories From Natchez, Mississippi
author: Richard Grant
description: This book is a delicious profile of a city that both glorifies and grapples with its Old South legacy. The Mississippi River town is known for elaborate antebellum homes, a raucous port culture and the lasting impact of being a center for the slave trade. The real-life characters are quirky and unflinchingly honest. There are dueling garden clubs, bourbon-soaked conversations and the first Black gay mayor in Mississippi.

title: Broken Faith: Inside The Word Of Faith Fellowship, One Of America's Most Dangerous Cults
author: Mitch Weiss and Holbrook Mohr
description: Cults are fascinating. How do people get sucked in? And when they realize their mistake, what makes them stay? This book is the result of dogged work by Mitch Weiss and Holbrook Mohr, two journalists who investigated allegations of abuse in the evangelical Word of Faith Fellowship based in North Carolina. What they discovered – in hundreds of interviews, secret recordings and documents – is as shocking as it is heartbreaking.

title: Hoax: Donald Trump, Fox News, And The Dangerous Distortion Of Truth
author: Brian Stelter
description: It’s the revelation that launched a thousand late-night TV punchlines: President Trump gets policy ideas from watching Fox News. But this book, by CNN chief media correspondent and Reliable Sources host Brian Stelter, goes deeper, unveiling just how insidious connections are between the right-wing cable news channel and the 45th president. From Fox News star Sean Hannity serving as a “shadow” chief of staff to a stable of stars who downplayed the expanding coronavirus pandemic as a Democratic Party plot, Stelter expertly details how Trump’s need for a supportive media outlet and Fox News’ thirst for ratings-driven profits led to an alliance unprecedented in modern media or politics.

title: The Searcher: A Novel
author: Tana French
description: Cal Hooper – a disillusioned, retired Chicago cop – moves to rural Ireland for a simpler life, but then he gets dragged into searching for a missing local teenager. The boy has possibly been murdered, though possibly he has just fled for the city. Of course Cal jumps in, despite his yearning for solitude. Tana French beautifully and slowly unspools the story, all the while carrying tension in every sentence and scene. Cal and the reader take the journey together, exploring both the opportunity and the danger that a city has to offer and the peace and complications of a village that is increasingly depleted of young people. (I listened to the audiobook, and Roger Clark was a wonderful reader – I highly recommend.)

title: The Age Of Phillis
author: Honorée Fanonne Jeffers
description: “Morning shards, and a mother wondered/ if her daughter forgot her real name,” writes Honorée Fanonne Jeffers in the poem “An Issue of Mercy #1” in her latest collection. In this vast, imaginative opus on Black female genius, Fanonne Jeffers excavates the figure of Phillis Wheatley Peters, the first Black woman to publish a book in America. Here, too, is a virtuosic deconstruction of the figure of Phillis, stolen from her home in the Gambia at age 7 and enslaved in America during the 18th century – and what she means as a representation of this time in our history that’s still influencing American social institutions and life today in relation to power, agency and the histories that are told.

title: The Shadow King
author: Maaza Mengiste
description: “Her hair is neatly braided and sleek and she has been careful to hide the long scar that puckers at the base of her neck and trails over her shoulder like a necklace,” writes Maaza Mengiste of her main character Hirut in the opening of her Booker Prize-longlisted novel. Hirut’s scar marks her as a survivor – of war, of capture, of learning to defend herself against war and other violences of men. In this luminous exploration of the lives of Ethiopian women who fought back against the fascist regime of Mussolini’s invading army, Mengiste both un-erases history and crafts a stunning novel that resonates with imagistic lyricism and depth.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "History", "Dark", "Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Bestiary: A Novel
author: K-Ming Chang
description: K-Ming Chang’s debut is a kind of compendium of real and mythical beasts — some human, some animal, most a bit of both — that roam through a family’s lineage and the stories its members tell each other from one generation to another. Bestiary’s primary narrator is Daughter, who is trying to understand the gulf between Mother and Grandmother as well as the new tiger tail she’s grown. Everything is alive in this book: The ground grows mouths, rivers become women, roads become rivers, crabs give birth to girlchildren, trees get up and march off in search of lost loves.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Crooked Hallelujah
author: Kelli Jo Ford
description: Justine is a 15-year-old living in the Cherokee Nation of Oklahoma with her mother, Lula, when she learns she’s pregnant. In chapters voiced by different narrators, debut novelist Kelli Jo Ford shows how Lula, Justine and Justine’s daughter, Reney, are both alike and different, each woman stubborn and acting on convictions all her own. With love binding them together even through historical and present trauma, Lula, Justine and Reney carve unexpected paths and try to find a way to feel at home and safe in a world of unpredictability.

title: High As The Waters Rise: A Novel
author: Anja Kampmann, translated by Anne Posten
description: This gorgeously written novel by German poet Anja Kampmann, translated superbly by Anne Posten, focuses on Waclaw, a middle-aged oil rig worker. In the opening pages, Waclaw learns that his dear friend, Mátyás, has gone missing from the rig and is presumed dead. Waclaw, who has already been injured once in this dangerous line of work, begins an unintentional pilgrimage to places he and Mátyás made meaningful. Eventually, as he sinks deeper into grief, Waclaw returns also to the places that shaped him and that he fled in search of a better life.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Homeland Elegies: A Novel
author: Ayad Akhtar
description: If you’re looking around and wondering how on earth we got here, check out Ayad Akhtar’s latest tour de force. The narrator in Homeland Elegies is a first-generation American born to Pakistani immigrants to the U.S. His father treated Donald Trump for a heart condition once and has decided, on that basis alone, that he’s trustworthy, which is deeply frustrating to the narrator. Meanwhile, the narrator gets involved in a scheme run by a charming capitalist whose consequences are more far-reaching than he knows. A romp, a tragedy and a serious look at the American obsession with money: You don’t want to miss this.

title: Lakewood: A Novel
author: Megan Giddings
description: Following her grandmother’s death, Lena Johnson is left carrying grief, debt and too much responsibility. Luckily – is it? – a letter arrives inviting her to become a medical test subject in a top-secret project. The pay and the benefits are too good to pass up, so Lena signs the dotted line, drops out of college and lies to everyone about her new job. For a while, things are weird but OK – until they’re weird and distinctly not OK. Haunted by eugenicist theories and the real conspiracies of the American scientific establishment, this chilling and startling debut is unputdownable.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: The Freedom Artist
author: Ben Okri
description: Ben Okri’s new novel begins with an ominous tone, telling readers that “civilizations became so successful that [people] forgot they were in prison. They began to think they were free.” This prison is both literal and metaphorical, and the novel’s characters puzzle over what it is, where it is, who its prisoners are and how it can be escaped or evaded or transcended. A modern fable in which humans walk around while sleeping and lay awake at night screaming, this unsettling read is still cautiously hopeful, pointing to storytelling as a way of understanding how to exist.

title: Tomboyland: Essays
author: Melissa Faliveno
description: Melissa Faliveno has lived in Brooklyn for more than a decade now and has witnessed how “especially after the 2016 election … people on the coast began to talk about Midwesterners as if they were nothing more than uneducated, gun-toting rednecks.” She complicates and nuances this narrative in Tomboyland, a collection of essays that are a love letter to, reckoning with and examination of her Midwestern upbringing in Wisconsin. To have roots, Faliveno implies, is to have the ability to grow upward, out and sometimes away, but to always have a context to return to.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Yellow Bird: Oil, Murder, And A Woman's Search For Justice In Indian Country
author: Sierra Crane Murdoch
description: When Sierra Crane Murdoch and Lissa Yellow Bird first met at Fort Berthold Indian Reservation in North Dakota, Lissa was knee deep into an investigation of a missing man, an oil worker. Oil had changed the reservation while Lissa had been away from it, and it was the oil boom’s effect that brought Murdoch, a journalist for High Country News, to Fort Berthold. Soon, though, Murdoch turned her own investigation to Lissa herself, and the two became allies, each intent on finding the truth and exposing it, as expansive, complicated, nuanced and multi-layered as it might be.

title: The New Wilderness
author: Diane Cook
description: It’s entirely understandable that in trying times you might want to avoid reading stories about people trying to survive dystopia, but Diane Cook’s The New Wilderness is my best argument for giving it a shot. In this beautiful novel, the United States has become largely unlivable and so Bea takes her ailing daughter Agnes to live in one of the last patches of wilderness. Life there is unremittingly hard but possible, and in that possibility Cook finds humor, incredible heart and something like hope. Buried deep inside is a reckoning with our place in nature and what wilderness and wildness look like when we don’t turn away in fear.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Father Of Lions: One Man's Remarkable Quest To Save Mosul's Zoo
author: Louise Callaghan
description: Who could resist a book that starts, “Abu Laith was not the kind of man to let another man insult his lion”? This book sparkles. Louise Callaghan writes about Iraq and the bonds that transcend war to tell the story of a Mosul zookeeper and his struggle to keep his family and his beloved lions safe. Against the backdrop of tragedy, it’s an ultimately uplifting story.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Mutations: The Many Strange Faces Of Hardcore Punk
author: Sam McPheeters
description: As the incendiary singer of the ’90s band Born Against, Sam McPheeters is a legendary figure in hardcore punk. It’s a status he hates – and he spends much of Mutations deconstructing the entire notion of icons and subcultures. Through personal essays, record reviews and features on other musicians, he dissects his own cracked heart and teeming brain with cynical insight and disarming self-deprecation. For those familiar with McPheeters, Mutations is a catch-up session with a wise, witty cousin; for those who aren’t, it’s a brilliant look at how creativity, social forces, mental illness and growing older can make for a potent literary cocktail, Molotov or otherwise.

title: Rust Belt Femme
author: Raechel Anne Jolie
description: Coming-of-age memoirs work best when the author is unafraid to dig deep into the context of their childhood, as well as the actual events. In her debut memoir, Rust Belt Femme, Raechel Anne Jolie gets it: On the surface, Femme is an exploration of trauma, queer identity and determination in the life of a Midwest kid trapped in a cycle of poverty and tragedy. But Jolie also stages this achingly personal story against the backdrop of the 1990s and early 2000s, with punk, emo and indie music playing as much of a role in her young life as the working-class environs that she rebelled against – and that she eventually came to grips with.

title: The Heap: A Novel
author: Sean Adams
description: Sean Adams’ debut novel The Heap takes place in the ruins of Los Verticalés, a leviathan 500-story building in the American desert that collapsed mysteriously years ago. The vast pile of rubble is called The Heap, and a loose-knit community has sprung up in order to salvage those remains. Trapped somewhere inside The Heap is a lone survivor, Bernard Anders, who broadcasts a radio show from the depths of the ruins – and when his brother Orville decides to find him, he exhumes devastating truths about not only The Heap, but the entire, teetering social experiment that is America.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: The Human Cosmos: Civilization And The Stars
author: Jo Marchant
description: The gravity that the Earth exerts on humanity is obvious. But in The Human Cosmos: Civilization and the Stars, Jo Marchant compellingly argues that the stars have always pulled at us just as strongly. With whirlwind curiosity and gripping storytelling, Marchant takes us on a trip through time and space, pointing out how our perceptions of the heavens have informed every step of our evolution as a civilization. Dazzling and profound, The Human Cosmos is a skyward gaze at the void we can never stop trying to fathom.

title: The Lost Book Of Adana Moreau: A Novel
author: Michael Zapata
description: Books within books: That’s the main motif of Michael Zapata’s debut novel, The Lost Book of Adana Moreau. Ping-ponging through time, between cultures and across geography, the book traces the fictional life of a forgotten author named Adana Moreau whose work has nonetheless reverberated – and sparks a life-changing mystery for a man named Saul Drower who has his own links to lost works of literature. Where does one story end and another begin? Lost Book poses this heady question, and in unpacking his answer, Zapata illuminates the reality-inventing power of storytelling itself.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters"]

title: Upright Women Wanted
author: Sarah Gailey
description: Taking place in an indeterminate future where the U.S. has devolved into a patriarchal society that resembles the Old West, Sarah Gailey’s novella Upright Women Wanted centers on Esther Augustus, a young Arizona woman who runs away to join the Librarians, an ostensibly nunlike order of women who travel in wagon trains disseminating printed information – which is strictly controlled by the government. Gailey populates the book with forces we know all too well today: homophobia, transphobia, misogyny, patriarchy, fascism and the fraught, post-truth minefield we find ourselves navigating. But it’s also a breezy, rousing and unrelentingly fun gallop.

title: Empire Of Wild: A Novel
author: Cherie Dimaline
description: This is a small book. Not in pages, but in the scope and reach of the story it tells. It is about Joan, who lost her husband and who means to get him back. And that’s all. But in this tiny thing lives the fiercely beating heart of a family, a town, a community – the Métis indigenous community living around Georgian Bay, Ontario. Joan’s entire family gets drawn into the hunt for her missing husband – whether they believe he simply walked out or whether something … darker might’ve befallen him. Cherie Dimaline uses the legend of the rogarou to square her narrative architecture and give weight (and nightmares) to Joan’s search and private hurt. The rogarou is the bogeyman that scares children home before dark. It makes Métis girls walk in pairs. It keeps men from doing wrong by women. The rogarou is part man, part dog. Intelligent. Vicious. It comes for you if you deserve it. Or sometimes, if you’re just in the wrong place at the wrong time.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Harrow The Ninth
author: Tamsyn Muir
description: The actual plot of Tamsyn Muir’s follow-up to her excellent lesbian-necromancers-in-space novel, Gideon the Ninth, is one-sentence simple: Harrow must overcome her damaging past to fight off a bunch of gross space monsters and save God. But the book is so deliciously bonkers and heartbreakingly twisted that narrative barely matters. Muir puts you in the head of an unfathomably powerful teenaged necromancer trying (and failing) to recover from the trauma she suffered in the first book, and though the book is deeply disorienting and very challenging, there’s just something so gorgeously Baroque about it all that you can’t help sliding through, rolling around blood-drunk in the mess of it all, and waiting, waiting to see how Muir brings it all home. There will come a moment when you think you can’t take another sentence and right then – right at that last moment – that’s when it all comes together. You’ll know the place when you get there and you’ll thank me for counseling patience.

title: Little Eyes: A Novel
author: Samanta Schweblin
description: Little Eyes supposes a world that is our world, 5 minutes from now. It is a place with all our recognizable horrors, all our comforts and sweetnesses, as familiar (as if anything could be familiar these days) as yesterday’s shoes. It then introduces one small thing — one little change, one product, one tweaked application of a totally familiar technology — and tracks the ripples of chaos that it creates. In this case, that single application is the kentucki: basically a Furby with a webcam and a single link to another human being somewhere in the world. One person owns the toy/pet, the other person controls it and sees through its eyes. Think for just a moment the kind of joy and the kind of horror something like that would create. Then read Little Eyes and see how whatever it was that you imagined was just the beginning of how awful it could be.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Shiner: A Novel
author: Amy Jo Burns
description: Wren Bird, 15-year-old daughter of a lightning-struck preacher called White Eye, may live isolated on the outskirts of a dying Appalachian coal town, but she understands myths and secrets. It is local myths that anchor her snake-handling father’s dwindling gas station congregation, secrets that keep her mother and her mother’s best friend, Ivy, so close, lies that bind family and community together. Amy Jo Burns writes a version of Appalachia that is one step removed from magic – all strychnine and moonshine and powerful wonder. There are pieces of Shiner that read like dreams and others that come on like nightmares, creating the push-pull tension in Wren’s heart: the desire to escape her Appalachian home and the sure knowledge that she’ll carry it with her no matter how far she runs.

title: The Worst Best Man
author: Mia Sosa
description: I often find romance novels set in the wedding industry too treacly, but this book is a salty-sweet delight. Carolina Santos is a brilliant wedding planner who was left at the altar. She’s offered a major career opportunity that unfortunately reunites her with Max Hartley, her ex-fiancé’s brother and the so-called worst best man. Mia Sosa deftly uses this unexpected reunion as a way for the leads to unpack their emotional baggage, work through their pain and eventually fall in love. Sosa sets herself apart with a story focusing on two well-written characters who have a lot of depth and heart.

title: Cleanness
author: Garth Greenwell
description: The world Garth Greenwell writes about is a lonely place of anonymous sexual encounters and attempts at real intimacy that inevitably fall short. Cleanness tells the story of a rootless gay American teaching at a university in Bulgaria whose life is briefly transformed by a love affair with a young Portuguese man referred to only as “R.” A follow-up to Greenwell’s What Belongs to You, the book is not so much a novel as a series of linked episodes bound together by the narrator’s search for connection in a world he knows can’t really provide it. Greenwell is a relentless truth-teller with a poet’s eye for detail and a shimmering prose style that’s reason enough to read the book.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Godshot: A Novel
author: Chelsea Bieker
description: Chelsea Bieker draws us in with a scarily plausible premise: Peaches, a fictional town in California’s Central Valley besieged by a years-long drought, falls under the sway of a charismatic pastor who promises to bring back rain.
