But at its heart, Godshot is the story of Lacey May, a teenager whose mother abandons her in a culture that represses women’s ambition. As Lacey May grapples with needing to find her way alone, she gradually comes to terms with the lies the pastor has fed Peaches. Come for the cult; stay for the meditations on grief and belief.

title: Unspeakable Acts: True Tales Of Crime, Murder, Deceit, And Obsession
author: Sarah Weinman (editor)
description: Born of the post-Serial boom of investigative and reflective true crime, Unspeakable Acts brings together 13 articles and essays that scratch our itch for grisly puzzles while interrogating our obsession with them. Sarah Weinman’s picks include propulsive narrative features like Pamela Colloff’s “The Reckoning,” following one victim for 50 years after the University of Texas tower shooting, and essays like Sarah Marshall’s “The End of Evil,” which reconsiders our tendency to view Ted Bundy as a monstrous genius. These sharp pieces challenge true-crime tropes, looking critically at the stories we want to tell ourselves about criminals and victims.

title: Want: A Novel
author: Lynn Steger Strong
description: The narrator of Want is in a relatable rut, exhausted by the grind of late-stage capitalism. She has a Ph.D. but can’t get a tenure-track job; she and her husband sleep in the closet of the tiny Brooklyn apartment they share with two young daughters; and they are filing for bankruptcy to get out from under their debt. Where another kind of book would see its narrator fold into herself, Want explores the ramifications of pushing for more than what we’ve been relegated to. I’ll keep returning to Lynn Steger Strong’s gut-punch lines on the repercussions of our desires.

title: Cool Town: How Athens, Georgia, Launched Alternative Music And Changed American Culture
author: Grace Elizabeth Hale
description: Take it from someone who lived there: Athens exists in its own space and out of time. You already know R.E.M., the B-52s and maybe even Pylon – their origin stories are told in this deeply researched (but personally written) history of the ‘80s music scene with a lived-in attention to detail. But there are so many musicians, professors, painters, poets and indescribable personalities who have made and still make Athens a Southern bohemia. Grace Elizabeth Hale weaves together a history of the university’s art school, punk’s do-it-yourself ethos and queer communities (both out and not) as the South’s ever-evolving identity sprawled and crawled like kudzu.

title: Catherine House: A Novel
author: Elisabeth Thomas
description: What would it look like to fall in love with a place? And what would happen if that place turned out to be all wrong for you? In Catherine House, the protagonist, Ines, starts school at a gorgeous, mysterious, cloistered college in the Pennsylvania woods. But the more she becomes invested in her surroundings, the more readers get the sense that she needs, desperately, to get out. A beautiful, chilling read for those of us who might also be in need of a change in scenery.

title: Days Of Distraction: A Novel
author: Alexandra Chang
description: Does anyone else hate their job, partner, friends and city? OK, maybe that’s the pandemic talking. But this book is a slow walk through the realization that it might be worth reconsidering literally every part of your life. As the narrator rethinks her dead-end job as a low-level tech reporter, she also imagines what it would be like to ask for more from the people around her and, eventually, herself.

title: Followers: A Novel
author: Megan Angelo
description: You ready for a book that hits a little too close to home? In this one, a massive digital leak means that everyone’s private online presence – emails, DMs, snapchats – becomes public. The narration bounces between the “before” and the “after,” two equally dystopian (and, well, familiar) worlds in which people’s lives are almost entirely dictated by how they’re perceived on social media. The book is funny and engrossing, but warning: It may leave you wanting to crawl into a hole and delete every record that you’ve ever existed.

title: If I Had Your Face: A Novel
author: Frances Cha
description: “I would live your life so much better than you, if I had your face.” In this book, set in modern-day Seoul, beauty is a means to an end. The women at its center are bluntly aware of this fact and have no qualms about using and acquiring beauty as a means to achieve their dreams. As their lives and friendships unfold, readers become increasingly attuned to how utterly weird it is that something so arbitrary governs so many of our relationships, aspirations and opportunities.

title: The Only Good Indians
author: Stephen Graham Jones
description: Though it’s a horror novel, The Only Good Indians may have you laughing despite yourself. Equal parts biting sarcasm and impending dread, the book plays with our ideas of who we are and what our lives are meant to look like. The characters face dramatic consequences for trying to live outside the expectations people have for them, and yet, in some ways, sticking to tradition turns out to be just as dangerous.

title: Larger Than Life: A History Of Boy Bands From NKOTB To BTS
author: Maria Sherman
description: It’s quite likely that Maria Sherman is the first writer who’s ever compared the Backstreet Boys’ Brian Littrell and Nick Carter to Friedrich Engels and Karl Marx, respectively. But as a cultural critic possessing both sharp wit and a true fan’s reverence, Sherman just may be the preeminent scholar of boy bands. With a clever layout that pays tribute to the teen magazines of yesterday, Larger Than Life offers a historical account of the format across history and genre – from Lisztomania to New Edition to One Direction – with humor and heart.

title: What Are You Going Through: A Novel
author: Sigrid Nunez
description: This book feels sort of like a sequel to The Friend, Sigrid Nunez’s novel about a writer who inherits a dog after a friend dies by suicide. In What Are You Going Through, the narrator, a writer and teacher, allows everyone she encounters to tell their own stories – even a cat she meets at an Airbnb. Unmarried and childless, the narrator muses about growing older, having never met “the one.” She brilliantly describes one friend’s fading beauty and her ex’s pomposity and despair. This novel is smart, witty, empathetic and so relatable – offering observations about companionship, connection and the powerful act of listening.

title: Something To Talk About
author: Meryl Wilsner
description: Assistant-boss romance blooms despite a power imbalance. Rumors of a relationship are so deafening they almost prevent the real thing from growing. One half of the couple is tough and inscrutable where the other is warmly expressive. These are all tropes you’ve encountered before – but here, in Meryl Wilsner’s Something to Talk About, they are realized with such freshness, specificity and nuance that even before you factor in both halves of the main couple being women, it’s evident you’re dealing with an unusually delightful story. Am I only recommending this novel on such a large platform to make a Lucy Liu/Merritt Wever film adaptation more likely? PERHAPS. But once you’ve read it, when you’ve read it, I cannot imagine that you’ll blame me.

title: Glitter Up the Dark: How Pop Music Broke The Binary
author: Sasha Geffen
description: Popular music has long provided a space for challenging gender norms and essentialism; think Little Richard, David Bowie, Wendy Carlos or Janelle Monáe. In Glitter Up the Dark, critic Sasha Geffen traces this history of gender fluidity in popular music and argues that these transgressions aren’t incidental. From disco to techno, punk rock to hip-hop, Geffen digs deep to explore the way popular music can facilitate exchange between listeners and performers, loosening the grip of gender roles and providing fertile ground for asking questions, seeking liberation and creating change.

title: My Autobiography Of Carson McCullers: A Memoir
author: Jenn Shapland
description: While interning at a writers’ archive as a graduate student, Jenn Shapland stumbled upon love letters between American novelist Carson McCullers and a woman named Annemarie. The discovery, Shapland says, was a turning point for her; less than a year later, Shapland was comfortable identifying as a lesbian for the first time. The letters also inspired Shapland to engage in a years-long process of research into aspects of McCullers’ life that traditional biographies overlooked; namely, her gender transgressions and her relationships with other women. My Autobiography Of Carson McCullers asks sharp questions not just about the details of McCullers’ life but, more broadly, how we understand historical figures who confound the social expectations of their time (and our own) and how, in turn, they can help us understand ourselves.

title: At Night All Blood Is Black: A Novel
author: David Diop, translated by Anna Moschovakis
description: This novel brings readers into the trenches of World War I via Alfa Ndiaye, a Senegalese soldier who has enlisted in the French army as a “Chocolat.” Alfa declines to slit his wounded compatriot’s throat in what would be a mercy killing and descends into madness. At Night All Blood is Black takes readers on a vertiginous tour of war and home, in writing that feels at once accurate and delusional. The result: A stark warning about the savage consequences of war, a masterful dive into colonial domination and the invocation of canonical texts against a world that is anarchic, violent and surreal.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Historical Fiction", "Dark"]

title: Blood On The River: A Chronicle Of Mutiny And Freedom On The Wild Coast
author: Marjoleine Kars
description: A fascinating, hitherto untold account of the 1763-64 slave rebellion in Dutch Berbice (modern Guyana), predating the better-known Haitian slave rebellion by nearly three decades. Blood on the River comes alive with period illustrations, as well as meticulous attention to primary sources. Kars recounts a tale of oppression, bloodshed and some triumph; rebelling slaves held off their masters for nearly a year. Her book is a gripping tale about the human need for freedom. It is also a story of shifting loyalties among slaves, between slaves and Amerindians, and among the Europeans themselves.

title: Counterpoint: A Memoir Of Bach And Mourning
author: Philip Kennicott
description: How to mourn a mother whose harsh judgments framed your childhood? This question haunts Philip Kennicott’s gorgeous memoir. Kennicott, the Washington Post’s senior art and architecture critic, is at pains to empathize with his mother, while also asking, “What does it mean to truly know a piece of music?” He braids his fraught maternal relationship with his fraught relationship to music, rendering a story that operates masterfully on several levels. In the end, Counterpoint meets Kennicott’s own criterion that “every good book or great piece of music carries with it the possibility of redemption.”

title: Later: My Life At The Edge Of The World
author: Paul Lisicky
description: Later is a deeply affecting memoir recalling life in Provincetown, Mass., at a time when there was no treatment or prevention for AIDS. Paul Lisicky luxuriates in Provincetown’s accessible and public sex, its embrace of queerness, and an atmosphere that allows him to be the man he seeks to be. He courts risk, flirts with unsafe sex – which is to say, flirts with death – but, too, chooses life. Writing with meticulous emotional nuance, Lisicky not only captures his day-to-day but manages to translate lessons from that day-to-day into a manual for living.

title: The Book Collectors: A Band Of Syrian Rebels And The Stories That Carried Them Through A War
author: Delphine Minoui, translated by Lara Vergnaud
description: The Book Collectors reminds us that heroes emerge in unpredictable places. Author Delphine Minoui, Middle East correspondent for Le Figaro, homes in on Daraya, a suburb of Damascus where the Syrian civil war began. Despite constant bombardment and near starvation, a small group of people manages to set up an underground library in Daraya. Within two months, the library houses 15,000 volumes collected from the rubble. It’s an oasis of calm and a gathering place for surviving neighbors to enjoy that lifesaving marker of civilization: reading.

title: The Book Of V.: A Novel
author: Anna Solomon
description: In a literary scavenger hunt, Anna Solomon creates a trio of stories that riff on forsaken Queen Vashti, who preceded the biblical Queen Esther as Ahasuerus’ wife. Solomon’s triptych includes a re-creation of Esther’s journey to wed the king in ancient times, but now the characters are Vee, a beleaguered 1970s political wife, and Lily, a contemporary woman who is her husband’s second wife. Each woman struggles to forge her own destiny while navigating the costs and challenges of marriage. Their unique stories, and how they ultimately collide with one another, make for a compelling read.

title: Black Bottom Saints: A Novel
author: Alice Randall
description: I can’t think of a more sparkling way to learn about the entertainment history of Black Detroit beyond Motown than to read Alice Randall’s novel about the section of that city known as “Black Bottom,” which was once lined with nightclubs. As its short chapters whiz by, you get a taste of being in the audience of one of those magical nightclubs where, maybe, Moms Mabley was onstage while Dinah Washington and her husband, the NFL superstar Dick “Night Train” Lane, were arriving. Black Bottom Saints is a gorgeous swirl of fiction, history, motor oil and cocktail recipes.

title: Monogamy: A Novel
author: Sue Miller
description: I’ve been an admirer of Sue Miller’s novels ever since The Good Mother came out in 1986. But throughout all these years, I’ve always found her to be a challenging writer to review: that’s because, in summary, her plots tend to sound contrived, and this story is no exception. A married couple – a bookseller and a photographer in Cambridge, Mass. – are about to celebrate their 30th anniversary when the husband suddenly dies and the wife subsequently learns of an affair. In Miller’s hands, this melodramatic premise becomes transformed into felt life. She continues to be one of our most emotionally profound and nuanced writers.

title: The Rakess
author: Scarlett Peckham
description: Seraphina Arden, lady rake, is supposed to be working on her memoirs, which will expose the powerful man who ruined her. But she is distracted by an impossible attraction to Adam Anderson, an architect with two children to protect and an aversion to scandal. And of course, their no-strings affair becomes so much more complicated than they anticipated. Adam and Seraphina are haunted by their pasts – but will the past determine their futures? This an especially sexy, feminist, smart and luscious historical romance.

title: Like A Bird
author: Fariha Róisín
description: Fariha Róisín is troubled by ghosts. They’re on her mind a lot. And writing poetry wasn’t enough, so she explores her obsession with ghosts – and the trauma they represent – in her debut novel, Like a Bird. Protagonist Taylia Chatterjee is trying to find her way on her own; her parents have disowned her after a sexual assault, and her life is full of ghosts. Like a Bird is a story about survival and healing. Like a melancholy song, this novel is both intimate and sincere.

title: Something That May Shock And Discredit You
author: Daniel M. Lavery
description: If you were a devotee of The Toast, you know that Daniel Lavery has long been one of the funniest writers to grace the Internet, thanks to his brand of extremely niche but oddly relatable humor. So it stands to reason that his memoir, Something That May Shock and Discredit You, is exactly that: a collection of essays that wrestle with gender and Lavery’s decision to transition but that are also about the Rapture, Gomez Addams and why Star Trek’s Capt. James T. Kirk is actually a “beautiful lesbian.” It’s the kind of book that only could have sprung out of Lavery’s mind palace, and the world is better for it.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Desert Notebooks: A Road Map For The End Of Time
author: Ben Ehrenreich
description: Ben Ehrenreich’s Desert Notebooks is a uniquely unsettled book. It takes on a lot at once – history, climate change, myth, politics – but at its heart is a fascinating look at the question of time. Time here is charged with a sense of apocalypse, or time’s own end. And throughout, Ehrenreich channels his thoughts in a way that feels alternately visionary and polemical. Desert Notebooks is a work of fervent humility. It’s great because it contemplates the wages of injustice, the mysteriousness of the world and how the present feels when a sense of an ending relentlessly permeates it.

title: Red Pill: A Novel
author: Hari Kunzru
description: You want to read Hari Kunzru’s Red Pill to understand the madness of the past few years. It’s about a writer who’s trying and failing to write. He starts spiraling and, among other things, can’t resist watching a TV show about police. Eventually he meets the show’s creator, Anton: a demagoguing-bigot type robbing the world of its sanity-giving oxygen. Hopelessly compelled, the writer peers deep into Anton’s hard-right vision, where raw power declares what reality is, where dominance is truth. It feels dream-like, all-consuming. Then Election Day 2016 arrives – and his paranoia becomes reality.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: The Sprawl: Reconsidering The Weird American Suburbs
author: Jason Diamond
description: The Sprawl is something you should read whether you grew up in the suburbs or not. Jason Diamond intersperses historical accounts with personal anecdotes from his own suburban Chicago childhood, and he finds the right balance. He doesn’t look to idealize suburbs, given their socially dubious origins. Instead, he expertly explores a common suburban sentiment – that something seems to be missing. And that desire to fill that missing something is what potentially makes suburban life the last thing its original advocates conceived it could be: dynamic.

title: The Look Of The Book: Jackets, Covers, And Art At The Edges Of Literature
author: Peter Mendelsund and David J. Alworth
description: A good primer for people interesting in understanding something as complex as transforming a book into an image, The Look of the Book presents a wide historical range of visual storytelling through the art and design of the book cover. A sort of survey of graphic design history, it offers graphic wit and cleverness in its own construction. Of course, you can judge a book by its cover – a good cover will catch your eye and capture the essence of the story inside.

title: Gabrielle Chanel: Fashion Manifesto
author: Miren Arzalluz
description: While many books have been written about Gabrielle Chanel’s style and complicated personal history (especially recent examinations of her actions during World War II), this book accompanies the first ever retrospective of the couturiere’s work in Paris. Dazzling photographs of Chanel’s archives allow a peek at dresses that would look chic and modern today but date back to the 1920s – a midnight-blue crepe georgette evening dress with silk fringing dyed in graduating shades of blue, or a monochromatic drop-waist silvery silk crepe number. To see the silhouettes and materials up close is to see the evidence of Chanel’s mastery as both a modernist and a couturier. Essays add depth and context to the book, and fashion lovers who can’t make it to Paris may find it to be a great escape.

title: The Vanishing Half: A Novel
author: Brit Bennett
description: To be American is to self-invent, and if you think you’re not part of that, talk to a grandparent. In The Vanishing Half, Brit Bennett’s novel about Black identical twins who can pass as white, nearly everyone is self-invented. Stella Vignes leaves her Louisiana town and crosses the color line; she marries a white man, has a white daughter, then mistreats them to keep her secret. (It’s a testament to Bennett’s writing that Stella is not unlikeable.) Stella’s niece and foil, Jude, is unwilling and unable to pass. Instead, she reinvents herself as something deeply satisfying: a happy, successful adult. The past always catches up, though, and when the family reunion comes, it’s spectacular. To reinvent yourself is to add and subtract, as Bennett makes clear. This book adds.

title: Missionaries: A Novel
author: Phil Klay
description: Phil Klay was a Marine and you can feel it. Missionaries takes us around the world, from the chaos in Iraq and Afghanistan to turmoil in rural Colombia. Along the way, we meet a cascade of fascinating characters whose allegiances change quickly from narcos to rebels to militias. This is a gray universe where it’s difficult to distinguish the good guys and gals from the bad, and it rings true to those of us who have spent time in these places. The interconnected worlds of the military medic, the Colombian army officer, the burned-out reporter and the death squad recruit will stick with you long after you close this book.

title: Alien Oceans: The Search For Life In The Depths Of Space
author: Kevin Hand
description: Many of us seem to remember learning as kids that water is scarce in our universe. That understanding has changed a lot as Kevin Hand also describes in his engrossing study. A number of planets and moons in our solar system have water, and some of them have entire oceans sloshing around, in the depths of which could be, maybe, life. If you enjoy imagining a mission in which humans launch a rocket that contains a spacecraft that releases a lander that launches a space boat – or even a space submarine – Hand’s book will help you grasp the full picture.

title: I Got A Monster: The Rise And Fall Of America's Most Corrupt Police Squad
author: Baynard Woods and Brandon Soderberg
description: This book made me want to cry and scream at the same time. It’s the story of an elite group of police officers in Baltimore who spent years stealing, lying and physically attacking people in the city. The FBI prosecuted the officers with the same statutes that it uses to take down organized crime, and the book reads like a mob story. Except it’s not the mob – it’s the police.

title: X+Y: A Mathematician's Manifesto For Rethinking Gender
author: Eugenia Cheng
description: I get it: A book that tears down gender constructs through high-level math doesn’t necessarily sound like a fun, engaging read. But this book changed my life in the most beautiful way. Eugenia Cheng doesn’t just explain why the way our society conceptualizes gender is all wrong – she proposes a new way of thinking about – and beyond – gender. Through accessible graphs and brilliant metaphor, Cheng pushes her readers instead to think about a person’s behavior. For example, is an action “ingressive,” single-track thinking or “congressive,” focused on the collective good? With plenty of references to relevant literature, Cheng gives readers the sense they’ve been in an in-depth conversation with her. Stunning.

title: Members Only
author: Sameer Pandya
description: Who hasn’t felt excluded from a group or regretted a remark? In Sameer Pandya’s heart-pounding novel, every word Raj Bhatt says, every action he takes, is consequential. Raj is an Indian American anthropology professor, and in separate incidents, his words are used against him at his private tennis club and at the liberal arts college where he teaches. He is constantly “otherized,” and he’s desperate to find connections in historically white spaces. This is a nail-biting read because Raj’s fate – a comfortable life unraveled by misinterpretations and words taken out of context – is all too familiar in today’s world.

title: Ace: What Asexuality Reveals About Desire, Society, And The Meaning Of Sex
author: Angela Chen
description: This book affirms the experiences of people who are asexual (aces) – people who are, at best, misunderstood and, at worst, believed to be in need of a “cure.” Ace has a second audience in mind: people who could benefit from deeper reflection on intimacy, romance and sex – which is to say, pretty much all of us. Angela Chen points out that aces, because they subvert norms related to sex, can help everyone else notice societal pressures that they’re too submerged in to see. The book allows us to imagine how much more we could get from our relationships if we were able to free ourselves from restrictive ideas of what we’re supposed to feel and do.

title: What Can A Body Do?: How We Meet The Built World
author: Sara Hendren
description: If you’ve ever sat in a chair that’s uncomfortable because it doesn’t conform to your body, you know what it’s like when the built world fails to meet you where you are. People with disabilities encounter this reality frequently – that furniture and technology and buildings weren’t designed with them in mind. Sara Hendren, a design researcher, presents case after case of ingenious design that helps bodies of all shapes and needs adapt to the world. In prose infused with tenderness, Hendren tosses away the idea that disability is a problem to be solved and instead shows how humans’ adaptation to the built environment is a wonder to behold.

title: Moonflower Murders: A Novel
author: Anthony Horowitz
description: Thank God for Anthony Horowitz, who has reliably churned out cozy detective fiction for the past several years. If the pandemic has you craving the comfort and structure of a whodunit, look no further. I won’t spoil the plot of this detective novel within a detective novel except to say it’s set, deliciously, at a small family-run hotel somewhere in the English countryside complete with a wedding ruined by the discovery of a body, jealous siblings and feuding staff. Honestly, what else do you need?

title: Eat The Buddha: Life And Death In A Tibetan Town
author: Barbara Demick
description: Barbara Demick is a master at bringing faraway places to life. Her 2010 book, Nothing to Envy, was about North Korea and her latest is about Tibet, which has been ruled by the Chinese Communist Party since the 1950s and is, in many ways, hard to reach. For Eat the Buddha, Demick brings to life one town, Ngaba, through profiles of a handful of people: a nearsighted nomad who becomes a monk, a schoolgirl faced with the choice between family and money, and a princess named Gonpo, the daughter of the last king of Ngaba. They’re beautifully wrought portraits that Demick paints with depth, complexity and uncommon insight. A fully absorbing and compelling book that goes way beyond the headlines.

title: Ikenga
author: Nnedi Okorafor
description: When Nnamdi’s father, the chief of police in their Nigerian town, is killed, Nnamdi vows revenge. But he’s just a 12-year-old kid! Then he’s given an ikenga, a magical object that turns Nnamdi into a superhero known as “The Man.” How will Nnamdi wield his new power? Young readers will be drawn in by magic, adventure, depictions of vibrant West African culture and fun villain names like Mama Go-Slow and Bad Market. But Ikenga also offers subtle yet important lessons about truth, power and the justice system. (For ages 10 to 12)
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Memorial: A Novel
author: Bryan Washington
description: Mike and Benson have been together for a while, but their relationship is rocky. When Mike learns his father is dying, he flies to Osaka to be with him – leaving Benson living in a one-bedroom apartment with Mike’s mother, a woman he’d never met before, now in town for a long visit. This is a novel that builds on 1,000 small moments between the characters as they cook, fight, co-exist and leave most things unsaid. It’s a quiet kind of book, with sparing language, but Memorial still says a lot: about who we consider family, where we call home, how we love, how we forgive and how sometimes forgiveness is the purest form of love. (There are also mouth-watering descriptions of food that will make you wish for a companion cookbook!)

title: Pizza Girl: A Novel
author: Jean Kyoung Frazier
description: Pizza Girl is a pregnant teenager who works at a pizza shop, lives with her mom and boyfriend, and drinks beer after beer in the shed at night while wondering if she’s turning into her alcoholic father. When a woman named Jenny orders a pizza with pickles, Pizza Girl slowly becomes a part of Jenny’s life. And then she becomes a little bit more than that. Is it friendship? Attraction? Obsession? This slim novel about the often confusing feelings we can have for other people is a little scary, a little sad and a little funny. A perfect read for when you want to escape deep into someone else’s head.

title: Sex And Vanity: A Novel
author: Kevin Kwan
description: The settings: A lavish Capri wedding, Italian villas, mansions in the Hamptons and a mega yacht. The drama: Wall Street fortune-bearing Lucie Churchill (of yes, those Churchills) is stuck in a love triangle. As a teenager she falls for George Zao, a Chinese Australian surfer who is “not the right kind of rich.” But disaster strikes, and Lucie doesn’t see George again until years later when she’s engaged to status-obsessed, nouveau riche Cecil Pike, who has somehow been named “The Most Desired Dude On The Planet.” What’s a girl to do? This book could not possibly be written by anyone other than Kevin Kwan, author of Crazy Rich Asians, who writes about the (relatively) subdued glitz of the East Coast with as much color and verve as he does the decadent worlds of Hong Kong and Singapore. This book has almost no relationship to (the average person’s) reality, making it the perfect escape from it.

title: Neon Girls: A Stripper's Education In Protest And Power
author: Jennifer Worley
description: This sex worker memoir begins as a typical babe in the woods tale about a young woman drawn to the adult industry for extra cash, but it quickly turns into something more galvanizing and urgent. A graduate student who danced at San Francisco’s storied Lusty Lady peep show during its 1990s heyday, Jennifer Worley confronted the controlling, exploitative rules of management by organizing her fellow strippers – many of them radical lesbians – into the world’s first unionized strip club and, later, worker-owned co-op. Neon Girls is lively and crucial – a slice of queer urban history and a necessary rethinking of sex work as a site of collective labor struggle.

title: Political Sign (Object Lessons)
author: Tobias Carroll
description: During a moment in which Americans are besieged by an onslaught of political messaging from the sublime to the ridiculous, this slim, thoughtful volume helps make sense of what we’re seeing. Tobias Carroll has written a timely meditation on the political sign, an object that telegraphs our deeply held beliefs and exists in “the space between poetry and prose.” It’s not only billboards, bumper stickers and lawn signs that count here, but also Banksy’s street art, Roger Stone’s Richard Nixon tattoo and David Wojnarowicz’s leather jacket – tools used to curate our identities, proto-memes that marry art and protest. You won’t look at a MAGA hat the same way again.

title: The Chiffon Trenches: A Memoir
author: André Leon Talley
description: For months after the longtime Vogue editor was on Fresh Air, we’d quote him to each other around the office: “I wanted to look like a pirate! And I dressed like a pirate!” So I was delighted that André Leon Talley’s memoir captures his voice in all its glorious melodrama and encyclopedic use of proper nouns. (He loves to drop a name!) Talley narrates his decades-long career in fashion – the people who took him in, and those who spit him out – and, as he writes, “how a black man survived and thrived in the chiffon trenches.”

title: The Undocumented Americans
author: Karla Cornejo Villavicencio
description: An undocumented immigrant who graduated from Harvard and is pursuing a Ph.D. from Yale refused to write another “DREAMer” memoir. Instead, Karla Cornejo Villavicencio wrote about the immigrants who don’t “inspire hashtags or t-shirts”; undocumented immigrants who, if they’re acknowledged at all, are just seen as workers. In The Undocumented Americans, Villavicencio writes about the rich, complex lives of those so-called workers in a style so unique it defies description. She was nominated for a National Book Award.

title: Scorpionfish
author: Natalie Bakopoulos
description: Late into the night, Mira and her former sea captain neighbor have deep conversations, separated by a balcony wall. The characters share their recent losses of loved ones, their past relationships and their sense of belonging. The city of Athens echoes the turbulence of their personal lives – altered by the economic crisis and the refugee crisis, and haunted by the violent legacy of the Greek junta. Natalie Bakopoulos shines at capturing friendships between men and women – and this quiet novel speaks loudly about the power of friendship, art and place to help us shape who we are despite wounds from our pasts.

title: Family In Six Tones: A Refugee Mother, An American Daughter
author: Lan Cao and Harlan Margaret Van Cao
description: As a mother, Vietnamese American novelist Lan Cao struggles to provide emotional security for Harlan – her teenage daughter – while suffering from the “hybrid, chaotic, unstable” legacy of her refugee experience. As a daughter, Harlan intuitively accepts that she and Lan are exiled in their own separate ways but at the same time “very attached to each other.” As a mother-and-daughter memoir, Family in Six Tones poetically captures fragmentation and the search for wholeness through a full reckoning with the past.

title: Kim Jiyoung, Born 1982: A Novel
author: Cho Nam-Joo, translated by Jamie Chang
description: The offspring of Virginia Woolf and Franz Kafka, Kim Jiyoung, Born 1982 imagines the modern day Korean woman as a “mom roach” – a creature gradually stripped of all human agency, metaphorically turned into vermin by the patriarchy’s crushing prerogatives. Endowed with a generic Korean female name, Jiyoung is an educated, 33-year-old woman who gives up her job at a marketing firm to care for her infant daughter. Soon she begins to experience psychotic episodes where she channels the voices of other women – both living and dead – to assert herself in public situations. In her dis/integration, Jiyoung represents the collective fate of all modern Korean women whose inexorable oppression – perpetuated by seemingly supportive fathers and husbands – becomes doubly devastating. The novel also asks a salient question: “Do laws and institutions change values, or do values drive laws and institutions?”
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Splendid And The Vile: A Saga Of Churchill, Family, And Defiance During The Blitz
author: Erik Larson
description: Erik Larson is one of America’s greatest writers of nonfiction, and with his latest book, he turns his sharp eye to Winston Churchill’s first year as prime minister of the United Kingdom. Larson delves into Churchill and his family and also provides a gripping account of the Blitz, paying attention to its effects on ordinary Britons. You don’t have to be a history buff to love this bravura performance by one of America’s greatest storytellers.
tags: ["History", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Family Matters"]

title: Tokyo Ueno Station: A Novel
author: Yu Miri, translated by Morgan Giles
description: The latest book by Korean Japanese author Yu Miri to be published in English is a gorgeous but dark read. The novel tells the story of Kazu, a homeless man who comes back as a ghost to the park where he used to live. Yu’s writing is unsparing, never letting the reader forget the enormities of poverty and loss, but it’s also quite beautiful – a stunning novel, as well as a harsh, uncompromising look at the unfairness of poverty and the pain of existential despair.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Where The Wild Ladies Are
author: Aoko Matsuda, translated by Polly Barton
description: The ghost stories in Aoko Matsuda’s collection aren’t your average spooky tales – they’re based on traditional Japanese stories of yokai, ghosts and monsters that figure prominently in Japan’s folklore. But Matsuda puts her own clever spin on them, making them feel contemporary, unique and unapologetically feminist. Matsuda writes with a subtle self-assuredness mixed with a sly, unexpected sense of humor, and the results are downright addictive.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Network Effect: A Murderbot Novel
author: Martha Wells
description: Everyone’s favorite grumpy, TV-obsessed security robot is back, in its first full-length adventure. Over the course of four novellas, we’ve watched Murderbot grow closer to its human companions, figure out its place among them and even make a real friend (all while insisting it just wants to be left alone to rewatch its favorite series, Rise and Fall of Sanctuary Moon). Now, all those relationships are in danger, so what’s a Murderbot to do? Well, probably some murdering at the very least. Lots of stories don’t stand up when stretched to novel length – luckily for us, Network Effect not only stands up, it soars.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Slippery Creatures
author: KJ Charles
description: KJ Charles is my go-to for queer historical romance; Slippery Creatures is the first book in her Will Darling Adventures, and luckily for you the second one came out this summer. Will Darling, back from the Great War, is making his way in the civilian world as a humble bookstore owner – but things don’t stay calm or humble for long, because both the War Office and a zodiac-themed criminal gang are convinced he’s holding some vital information. Enter the aptly named Kim Secretan, who’s hiding vital information of his own but who may be the only person who can help Will. Mayhem (and hot sex, of course) ensue!

title: Snapped
author: Alexa Martin
description: The fourth book in Alexa Martin’s football-inspired Playbook series has everything I’ve come to expect from Martin: smart, competent women, hunky, intelligent football stars, and a supportive cast of female friends who all have their own fully realized stories. But Snapped goes deeper; Elle, the heroine, is figuring out what it means to be biracial after being raised “colorblind” by her white single father, now dead. Quinton, our hero, is dealing with the repercussions of taking a knee on the field during the first game of the season – and taking care of his father, a former player now incapacitated by CTE. Snapped never feels didactic or heavy-handed, though. Martin brings it all together with skill and care, for a romance that – OK, I’m gonna say it – SCORES.

title: The Faithless Hawk
author: Margaret Owen
description: I don’t generally recommend books that aren’t the first in a series; it’s hard to tell readers to pick up somewhere in the middle of the story. But I’m making an exception for The Faithless Hawk – go pick up the first volume, The Merciful Crow, and dive face-first into Margaret Owen’s alien – yet sadly recognizable – world, ruled by a bird-themed caste system in which the lowly Crows are the only people immune to a terrible plague that ravages the land. Fie, future chieftain of her band of Crows, is one of my favorite recent fantasy discoveries; straight-talking, swift-acting, fierce and confident in her skills. Watch her extract payment from upper-caste Phoenixes for carting their plague dead away, and you’ll be willing to follow her anywhere.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Last Emperox
author: John Scalzi
description: John Scalzi’s Interdependency series gives us a vast galactic empire on the verge of chaos as the interstellar pathways it depends on have begun to collapse. In this last installment, Emperox Grayland II is trying desperately to save as many people as she can from a death of interstellar isolation, while dodging coup attempts from self-interested corporate goons. Scalzi has said that he didn’t intend the series to be quite as topical as it turned out to be: “Some of that was completely unintentional, it was just the pace of the story. But some of it was. I live in the world.”

title: Me And White Supremacy: Combat Racism, Change The World, And Become A Good Ancestor
author: Layla F. Saad
description: When George Floyd died beneath a white policeman’s knee, legions of people shocked by the video rushed to understand how systemic racism works in America and their lives. So it’s a good thing Layla F. Saad had already created this workbook; a detailed plan walking readers through definitions, questions and concepts needed to understand how racial stereotypes are embedded in American society and how we reflexively reference those stereotypes in our own thinking. Readers go on a journey of discovery – which Saad calls “the work” – with masterful guidance on how to examine your own ideas, how to talk about these ideas across race and gender lines, and how to turn your own revelations into action aimed at unwinding systemic racism wherever you can.

title: Everything Man: The Form And Function Of Paul Robeson
author: Shana L. Redmond
description: In her deeply innovative exploration of the cultural resonances of the titanic – yet often now overlooked African American song interpreter, actor, athlete and Freedom Fighter Paul Robeson  – scholar Shana L. Redmond writes: “The story that unfolds over these chapters is more than a praise song for a singular man.” Going beyond conventional biography or criticism, Redmond pioneers a new form as she explores how Robeson, his movements suppressed by the U.S. government because of his leftist politics, still managed to leave his mark on the culture. Considering Robeson as a hologram, a recorded voice, the subject of multiple artworks and a vivid presence within sites that become more than memorials, Redmond returns Robeson to his rightful place at the center of the American conversation.

title: The Meaning Of Mariah Carey
author: Mariah Carey with Michaela Angela Davis
description: Supreme diva Mariah Carey doesn’t reveal every detail of her tumultuous, self-made life in this deeply felt and fun-to-read remembrance, but she does reflect on how her childhood as a biracial social outcast on Long Island and her bad marriage to music-biz executive Tommy Mottola, among other events, gave her primary material from which she shaped her stealth R&B sound and her butter-and-Teflon persona. Carey and veteran editor Michaela Angela Davis also make sure the world knows she is a serious musician who thinks as seriously about her song structures and sampled beats as her perfume lines.

title: Looking To Get Lost: Adventures In Music And Writing
author: Peter Guralnick
description: In the introduction to this collection of profiles from throughout his long and venerable career (plus some new ones), much-loved American roots music chronicler Peter Guralnick plays that old game of imagining his favorite dinner party, composed of the subjects of his book. And what a gathering! Guralnick writes with deep empathy and respect about legends like Johnny Cash and Willie Dixon, lesser-known key figures like bluesman Lonnie Mack and music-adjacent kindred spirits like the novelist Lee Smith. This book will make you feel nostalgic for up-close conversations as Guralnick gently reaches the heart and soul of his subjects.

title: Jack: A Novel
author: Marilynne Robinson
description: Jack is the fourth of Marilynne Robinson’s novels set in Gilead, Iowa, and it is the story of John Ames Boughton, the son of Gilead’s Presbyterian minister. The Jack we have met in previous books is a ne’er do well, a prodigal son whose sins are large and small, who has encountered tragedy as well as created it. This book tells the story of his romance with Della Miles, a black woman who is also the daughter of a preacher. The first 80 pages we watch them falling in love while trapped in a graveyard – but the rest of the book they are trapped in the racism of America in the late 1940s. It is quite a time for Robinson to be explicitly addressing our racist past as we confront our racist present. In typical Robinson fashion, she is concerned with the question of whether or not this wayward man can be loved, forgiven, changed.

title: Long Bright River: A Novel
author: Liz Moore
description: Liz Moore’s thriller Long Bright River is a book that a lot of different readers can enjoy: It’s a contemporary novel about the opioid epidemic, it’s a novel about sisters and families, it’s a book about the police and how they fall short of the communities they serve, and it’s a really well-plotted crime novel. Its central story revolves around Mickey, a patrol officer raising a young son in a working-class neighborhood in Northeast Philadelphia, and her missing sister, who’s addicted to drugs. Both women are the children of addicts, raised by a tough grandmother. The central mystery is the hunt for a killer targeting sex workers, but truly, this is a great literary novel about a city in the age of opioids and two sisters navigating their past. And in the tradition of many great literary thrillers, I promise you, you will not see the end coming.

title: Real Life: A Novel
author: Brandon Taylor
description: Before Brandon Taylor’s excellent novel Real Life was published, I knew him mostly through his thriving Twitter feed, but his debut is a pleasure to sink into: a campus novel about a queer Black grad student from the South coming to terms with the whiteness of his environment. Wallace, the main character, has the kind of interactions with his peers that novels are made for – kinetic, uncomfortable, honest – and that are so well written I felt like I was watching the events, rather than just reading the prose. This book operates on many levels – and I assure you there is one for you here – whether you are in it for the portrait of academic life, the precision of the characters or Real Life’s lingering descriptions of what it is like to be Black in a place that insists, wrongly, that it is a bastion of progressive values.

title: Such A Fun Age
author: Kiley Reid
description: It’s tempting to call Kiley Reid’s debut, Such A Fun Age, a novel about race and privilege – but that might not capture how smooth and funny a read it is. It’s the story of a young black babysitter, Emira, and her white employer, Alix. The two women are both sophisticated and well drawn – Alix becoming borderline obsessed with the younger, cooler woman who babysits her daughter, Emira dealing with the complex power dynamic between her and her employer. It’s funny and contemporary and will ring true for anyone living in the year of our Lord 2020. At a time when race and class are topics that have hopefully become a part of our lives in a more honest way, Such A Fun Age is a kind of contemporary novel of manners, and it’s also a reminder that those manners exact a higher toll on some Americans than others.

title: The Mirror & The Light
author: Hilary Mantel
description: It is not an exaggeration to say that The Mirror and the Light, the last book in Hilary Mantel’s trilogy about Thomas Cromwell, chief minister to Henry VIII, is the book I have most looked forward to in my entire adult life. (Remember too, I am old enough to remember when the last Harry Potter book came out.) Yes, you must start with Wolf Hall – but I can tell you that if you make it here, you will be greatly rewarded. There are no possible spoilers for a book about a historical figure – we’ve known all along that Cromwell’s head will end up in a basket – but I also can’t possibly spoil for you, or even describe honestly, the pleasure of living inside his intricate brain, watching him rise to power through intellect and compromise and sometimes brute emotion. And know also, inhabitant of this particular universe, this is a book about pandemics and marriage and influence and sycophancy and men that wield power in increasingly wild and terrifying ways.

title: Minor Feelings: An Asian American Reckoning
author: Cathy Park Hong
description: Cathy Park Hong defines minor feelings as “when American optimism is enforced upon you, which contradicts your own racialized reality, thereby creating a static of cognitive dissonance.” In this memoir, Hong examines her own conflicting ideas of being an individual versus the idea of the model minority myth that American society forced on to Asian Americans. She explores the trauma she felt growing up in America after immigrating to the U.S. from South Korea; her complex relationship with the English language; and her identity as a mother, a writer, an artist, a friend and a citizen in the U.S.

title: The Things They Fancied
author: Molly Young
description: The only thing that might’ve been more fun than breezing through this NSFW zine is researching it. In the early days of the pandemic, Molly Young spent two voyeuristic weeks immersed in the ridiculous obsessions of the rich. Mini chapters include countryside manors, French teeth, pineapples, pubic mist, pet squirrels, luxury fragrance. With the right social marketing, she suggests, we’ll fall for anything. Young diagnoses us as the gluttons we are: “People spend money on the dumbest s*** … rich people just spend a lot more of it.” While I – like many – was stuck inside during the bleakest of summers, Young’s wry romp into how the other half lives, today and throughout history, was a fizzy tonic. I emerged grateful for modern amenities like fresh produce, dentistry, bleach and toilets. Recommended if you fancy: Airbnb cottage getaways, JSTOR rabbitholes, Marie Antoinette, The Favourite, sneering at the 1%.

title: The Lying Life Of Adults
author: Elena Ferrante, translated by Ann Goldstein
description: Is there any contemporary international novelist better than Elena Ferrante at plumbing the depths of adolescent girls’ souls? I loved Ferrante’s Neapolitan quartet and found The Lying Life of Adults a satisfying return to form. Ferrante tells the story of Giovanna, a young teenager who feels “like a cake made with the wrong ingredients.” Disillusioned with her all-too-human parents, Giovanna takes her first steps toward independence and seeks understanding, inspiration and love – from her father’s estranged sister, lonely and profane, living in a rough Naples neighborhood, and later from a friend’s fiancé. The lies that Giovanna tells herself, her parents and her friends are how she creates a private life and her own story – she’s determined to become an adult “as no one ever had before.”
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Pull Of The Stars: A Novel
author: Emma Donoghue
description: A pandemic so virulent the world has become “a machine grinding to a halt.” Shops shuttered, coffins stacked and the public admonished to “refrain from shaking hands, laughing, or chatting closely together.” This is Dublin, 1918, at the height of the so-called Spanish Flu pandemic in Emma Donoghue’s eerily timely novel (written before COVID-19). Donoghue, author of the bestselling Room, sets her new book almost entirely in a tiny hospital supply room that’s been converted into a maternity ward for women suffering from the flu. These women, Donoghue writes, have been “worn down to the bone” by poverty and the deadly trauma of far too many pregnancies: “She doesn’t love him unless she gives him twelve,” the ghastly saying goes. At the novel’s center is narrator Julia Power, a nurse-midwife struggling valiantly to save her patients and their babies, with unexpected volunteer Bridie Sweeney at her side. It’s Sweeney who opens Power’s eyes – and ours – to Ireland’s brutal treatment of unwed mothers and their children. A warning: Donoghue’s graphic descriptions of early 20th century childbirth are not for the faint of heart.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Deacon King Kong: A Novel
author: James McBride
description: Who might like this? Brooklynites, music lovers, baseball fans, housing project dwellers (or expats), students of urban decay, people in need of a laugh – have I left anybody out? This insane jazzy riff from musician/author/National Book Award winner James McBride brings all those folks in and then some. Set in a (fictional) Brooklyn housing project in 1969, it follows the doings of the hard-drinking Sportcoat and his one-time baseball prospect-turned-heroin dealer Deems Clemens. McBride opens the book with Sportcoat shooting Clemens’ ear off – for reasons that only become more or less clear in its final pages (no fair peeking ahead!) after twists and turns that bring in mobsters, white beat cops, church folks – you get the idea. Journalists are often frustrated and frustrating in the way the work flattens people out – especially marginalized people. McBride fills people back up and out again and makes them whole.

title: The Riches Of This Land
author: Jim Tankersley
description: Why are so many people, especially middle-class people, so angry? Why do so many people feel they can’t get ahead no matter what they do? Jim Tankersley has answers to these simple but powerful questions. Spoiler alert: No, it’s not immigrants, and, no, the aggrieved are not wrong to feel they’re getting screwed. Tankersley is a veteran New York Times reporter, but he didn’t produce a conventional economics tome. His reporting has revealed an alternate narrative where fighting discrimination and unchaining the doors of opportunity to women, so-called minorities and immigrants is actually what produced the great post-WWII boom, while two big economic shocks eroded it – along with economic policies that really do favor the rich and richer. Most important, he charts a path to rebuilding a solid middle class. Sound like a dream? He says the alternative is a nightmare.

title: A Promised Land
author: Barack Obama
description: Barack Obama began his literary career with Dreams From My Father, recalling the struggles of his youth while still in his early 30s. Now in A Promised Land, approaching 60, he recalls how his most audacious dreams came true in 2008 and details his first 30 months as U.S. president – from the Great Recession through Obamacare to the killing of Osama bin Laden. Obama continues the story he began telling before the world was listening. Whatever one’s deepest feelings about this man, they are likely to be brought to the surface by this book: We hear his voice in every sentence.

title: Notes On A Silencing: A Memoir
author: Lacy Crawford
description: Notes on a Silencing is a difficult, often enraging memoir to read. It’s the kind of book that left me visibly angry every time I put it down. But it’s an important and honest look at sexual assault and the institutional cover-ups that often immediately snap into place to protect not only perpetrators, but the organizations in and around where the assaults take place. In this memoir, Lacy Crawford recounts her time as a high school student at one of America’s most prestigious boarding schools. Re-creating moments in dorm hallways, cafeterias and ice rinks with detailed, engaging prose, she documents life on campus both before and after she was assaulted. She describes how students, teachers, administrators and even doctors all singled out and attacked her, and protected the two boys who assaulted her. When I interviewed Crawford, she said, “I want all of the girls and women who have ever been made to carry the blame for the aggression of men, the aggression of others – I wanted them to see that they were intended to carry that blame and that it was never theirs.” This book did just that, and did it with vivid and unforgettable writing.

title: Rodham: A Novel
author: Curtis Sittenfeld
description: Alternate histories, to put it mildly, have had a lot of mental appeal of late. But the genre can be difficult to pull off, and even more difficult to build an engaging plot around once you arrive at the initial “WHAT IF…” premise. With Rodham, a novel based around the premise that Hillary Clinton never becomes Hillary Clinton, and instead dumps Bill after law school and doesn’t marry him and move to Arkansas, Curtis Sittenfeld pulls it off. Her novel is an engaging read that at times perfectly parallels recent political history, and at other times veers off in a wild and surprising direction. (Her most interesting plot points do both at once.) I tore through this novel in a matter of days. In addition to everything else, it made me rethink some of my assumptions about how much that happened in 2016 and since had to do with the individual people on the political stage, and how much was instead tied to sentiments that are baked into American public opinion.

title: One Night Two Souls Went Walking
author: Ellen Cooney
description: Ellen Cooney’s shimmering, remarkable novel follows an unnamed chaplain as she works the night shift at a hospital. The woman is haunted by her patients, as well as by what appears to be the ghost of a therapy dog that used to be a fixture at the medical center. This is a quiet book by design; it meanders gently, eschewing big plot twists or showy dialogue. Thanks to Cooney’s gorgeous writing and generous compassion, it’s also a literary triumph.

title: Creatures: A Novel
author: Crissy Van Meter
description: Crissy Van Meter’s debut novel follows Evie, a California woman preparing for her wedding to a fisherman (who’s gone missing) while trying to come to terms with her unusual childhood: Abandoned by her mother, she was raised by her pot-dealing father. She’s a realistic, memorable character, and Creatures, filled with Van Meter’s understated but powerful writing, is a beautiful look at how we navigate the pain and heartbreak that come with being human.

title: Processed Cheese: A Novel
author: Stephen Wright
description: Stephen Wright might not be a household name, but he should be. He’s an unpredictable author with an unwavering commitment to the surreal, and his newest book is more proof of his bizarre genius. Processed Cheese follows a couple who come across a huge bag of money and go on a long spending spree. Unfortunately for them, the rightful owner wants the cash back. It’s a novel as darkly funny as it is unsettling, and one of the sharpest critiques of American consumerism to come along in years.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Humor & Comedy", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: Agency
author: William Gibson
description: William Gibson builds bombs – beautiful clockwork architectures of plot and pressure disguised as science fiction novels. In Agency, he introduces us to Verity Jane, just coming off her 15 minutes of fame as the (now ex-) girlfriend of a venture capitalist billionaire, who finds a gig beta-testing a new artificially intelligent digital assistant named Eunice. At the same time, she is noticed by a cast of near-future Russians and Londoners who vie for control over various historical “stubs,” of which our current tomorrow is one. Agency oscillates between future London and Verity Jane’s present, ratcheting up the tension. Verity and Eunice are the rabbit. The hounds are contract assassins, military contractors, tech-bros in over their heads. And Gibson just keeps winding the wire around his explosive core, looping in gig-economy surveillance applications, a barista, a time-traveling robot and a housewarming/launch party complete with helicopter assault until Agency’s timer finally ticks down to zero.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Mysteries", "Thrillers"]

title: Riot Baby
author: Tochi Onyebuchi
description: Riot Baby is a lot of things: A dystopian nightmare that looks startlingly similar to real life, a street-side shot of magical realism, a brilliant work of political speculative fiction that uses rocketing language and the supernatural as means to interrogate American racism and the carceral state, and a story of black people with superpowers. Kevin is the “riot baby” of the title – born in Los Angeles in 1992, just as the cops who beat Rodney King are being let off. The story follows him and his sister Ella (touched with powers that allow her to see into multiple futures) through schools and streets and prison and beyond; through decades of worsening police violence and strict order that, from all the wrong angles, looks like peace. It is sharp, cruel, occasionally brutal, surprisingly hopeful, and Ella’s visions – her “Thing,” her power – offers a vision of an end to oppression that feels like true revolution.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: Conditional Citizens: On Belonging In America
author: Laila Lalami
description: This is the first nonfiction book by Laila Lalami, a Moroccan-born novelist who writes searingly about outsiders. It comes at a fraught time for immigrants in America. Lalami movingly chronicles her own journey from optimistic, naturalized American to post-Sept. 11 “conditional citizen” repeatedly scrutinized as an immigrant, an Arab, a Muslim. In one scene, she describes a “white woman in a blue pantsuit” at one of her book readings pressing her on ISIS, as if “I was a specimen, culled from a group of people these readers found mysterious and perhaps dangerous.” Conditional citizens, Lalami writes, are those who cannot dissent without their patriotism being questioned, who cannot move freely, who are jailed without cause. Though the U.S. prides itself on being a country of immigrants, it has often treated new Americans with exclusion and mistrust.

title: The Last Million: Europe's Displaced Persons From World War To Cold War
author: David Nasaw
description: After WWII, more than a million Eastern Europeans — concentration camp survivors, forced laborers and even Nazi collaborators — ended up in American and British-run refugee camps in post-fascist Germany. They stayed for as many as five years, as the former Allied powers turned their backs on the most vulnerable. The U.S. let in thousands of war criminals but often refused Jewish refugees due to blatant anti-Semitism. David Nasaw’s engrossing, infuriating and meticulously researched book shows the heartlessness with which the West dealt with refugees, a dehumanizing pattern that continues today in the U.S. and the EU. Nasaw also acknowledges, though incompletely, that some of those refugees went on to force out hundreds of thousands of Palestinians from their homes, creating another refugee crisis.

title: Perestroika In Paris: A Novel
author: Jane Smiley
description: Horses have trotted through the pages of plenty of Jane Smiley’s novels, but this is the first time one has actually talked. Perestroika in Paris, a delightfully whimsical tale about creatures living in Paris’ Champs de Mars, is a balm for harsh times. Drawn by curiosity, Smiley’s titular racehorse wanders from her stall and connects with an elegant German shorthaired pointer, a pontificating raven, a squabbling pair of mallards and a rat pining for a mate. They overcome their prejudices to band together to help each other and, eventually, a lonely, orphaned 8-year-old boy. This warmhearted fable, with plenty of pathos but no villains, extols the advantages of domesticity, the joys of freedom and exploration, and the power of friendship.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Creatures of Passage
author: Morowa Yejidé
description: Morowa Yejidé transports us to 1977 Anacostia – a historic neighborhood of D.C. too often overlooked by tourists, pundits and fiction writers – and reimagines the city as a mystical realm nestled between the “kingdoms” of Virginia and Maryland. With a murdered girl’s ghost in the trunk of a Plymouth Belvedere, an aging woman’s mystical connection to her dead twin brother and the death prophecies of an estranged niece, the novel is worthy of every Toni Morrison comparison it receives, effortlessly blending the brutalities of D.C.’s history with the mythical and supernatural. Creatures of Passage is a lyrical journey that will stick with you.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: White Magic: Essays
author: Elissa Washuta
description: White Magic explores iterations of magic and witchiness including everything from the Native rituals that shape Elissa Washuta’s Cowlitz heritage to smudge sticks packaged in plastic. The book unfolds in three mesmerizing acts, like a magic show; across the intertwining essays, Washuta is drawn to magic as a method of healing from a decade of abusive relationships, alcoholism and PTSD, looking for meaning and control. Standouts include an essay about playing the classic computer game Oregon Trail as a coping mechanism, and one on her home state of New Jersey as a “land of genocidal fairy tales.”

title: A Most Remarkable Creature: The Hidden Life and Epic Journey of the World's Smartest Birds of Prey
author: Jonathan Meiburg
description: Even if you’re an avid birder, you might never have heard of the striated caracara, a charismatic but endangered scavenger that makes its home in and around Tierra del Fuego and the Falkland Islands. In his fascinating debut book, ornithologist and indie rocker Jonathan Meiburg writes about his journeys in search of the elusive bird, and the changes that threaten its future. You don’t have to know the first thing about birds to be drawn into this beautifully written, enchanting book.
tags: ["Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: A Singing Army: Zilphia Horton and the Highlander Folk School
author: Kim Ruehl
description: Most people haven’t heard of Zilphia Horton, but the musician and activist’s influence has lasted to this day. Horton was an influential figure in the Highlander Folk School, which trained civil rights activists including John Lewis and Martin Luther King Jr., and she introduced hymns like “We Shall Overcome” to the mid-20th century social justice movement. Ruehl, the former editor-in-chief of alt-country magazine No Depression, tells Horton’s life story in this exhaustively researched book – beautifully written and endlessly fascinating, it’s one of the best biographies of the year.

title: A Thousand Ships
author: Natalie Haynes
description: In school, we read about the ancient Greeks: the fierce and clever Spartans who vanquished the Trojans. What brilliance to construct a horse – a GIFT – inside which brave warriors hid, waiting to ambush a kingdom and reclaim the beautiful Helen, whose face had launched a thousand ships – and thousands of sentences from mostly male writers ever since. In Natalie Haynes’ own brilliant construction, the 10-year tale of the Trojan War is retold by the many women ignored, widowed, enslaved, heartbroken and snuffed out by the pride and stupidity of warring men. Cassandra, Hecabe, Iphigenia, Penelope: They speak unfiltered. Muses and goddesses give their derisive takes as well. It is a fresh and utterly satisfying feminist take on one of the oldest stories in Western literature.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: All the Feels: A Novel
author: Olivia Dade
description: All the Feels is Olivia Dade’s latest installment in a loosely linked series of stories about the cast of a fantasy TV show that TOTALLY isn’t Game of Thrones (even though everyone, including the cast, hates the last season). Bad boy actor Alexander can’t stop getting into trouble; he despises his character arc and he’s got plenty of off-screen demons, so after one bar fight too many, the showrunners stick him with a minder – short, stubborn former emergency room therapist Lauren. Do sparks fly? You bet they do. Is there angst? Oh so much. Alex and Lauren have chemistry like Mentos and Diet Coke — and Dade’s spot-on depiction of fan culture is just chef’s kiss.

title: Book of the Other: Small in Comparison
author: Truong Tran
description: Mixing cadenced prose, vernacular speech and legal grievance, Vietnamese American poet Truong Tran invokes the three questions that serve as framework for his classroom teaching and cultural identity, “what haunts you. what hunts you. what are you hunting.” Tran’s answers to these questions resist both the traditional poetic form and the performance of trauma. Repeatedly denied promotion at the college where he teaches, Tran bluntly asserts, “my living. cannot be literary. it does not have. the elevation of poetry … there is no creative. to this nonfiction.” Audacious and inspiring, Tran’s latest work shows how artistic expression can be consciously reinvented as an act of protest.

title: Dial A for Aunties
author: Jesse Q. Sutanto
description: A crime novel wrapped up in a romance? Just what the doctor ordered for readers during the pandemic! There’s a lot to love here, especially the criminal high jinks that Meddelin, her mother and her aunts find themselves immersed in as she rekindles a lost flame. Above and beyond, though, this is a love letter to the bonds of family and culture.

title: Driving While Brown: Sheriff Joe Arpaio Versus the Latino Resistance
author: Terry Greene Sterling and Jude Joffe-Block
description: Joe Arpaio, the right-wing Republican ex-sheriff of Maricopa County, Ariz., has been one of the most controversial political figures of the past few decades, accused of racial profiling and mistreating prisoners, and convicted of criminal contempt of court in 2017. (President Donald Trump pardoned him weeks later.) In their new book, journalists Terry Greene Sterling and Jude Joffe-Block tell the story of the Latinx activists who brought about his downfall – it’s a smart, well-documented book about a group of people determined to hold the powerful to account.

title: Facing the Mountain: A True Story of Japanese American Heroes in World War II
author: Daniel James Brown
description: Daniel James Brown’s absorbing book tells the story of the 442nd Regimental Combat Team of the U.S. Army, which was composed of Nisei, second-generation Japanese American soldiers in World War II. He chronicles the valor of the young American men who fought in Europe, but whose families were imprisoned in internment camps, the victims of the vile racism that choked the country at the time. It’s a fascinating, expertly written look at selfless heroes who emerged from one of the darkest periods of American history.

title: Four Lost Cities: A Secret History of the Urban Age
author: Annalee Newitz
description: What does the ancient city of Pompeii have in common with the even more ancient metropolis known as Cahokia, located alongside the Mississippi River near present-day St. Louis? Or Angkor, a medieval capital of the Khmer Empire? Or the Neolithic settlement of Çatalhöyük, the first known city in the world? All of these cities were eventually abandoned. Journalist Annalee Newitz sifts through the mystique around their demises in this graceful, engrossing urban history, unearthing not only fascinating narratives but poignant warnings about why some places slide into the past.

title: Fulfillment: Winning and Losing in One-Click America
author: Alec MacGillis
description: America has been sorted into “winner-take-all” places and “left-behind” places – and the two are increasingly isolated, struggling to comprehend the divide. That’s the story that unfolds in Alec MacGillis’ Fulfillment: Winning and Losing in One-Click America. MacGillis, a reporter for ProPublica, had set off to write about U.S. regional disparities and chose Amazon as connective tissue. The narrative does not directly criticize or offer solutions. It lays out an economic history of the U.S. through hyper-detailed portraits of people and companies in Seattle, Washington, D.C., Baltimore, Ohio, Pennsylvania and other parts of the country – told with the flair of an investigative magazine writer.

title: Girlhood
author: Melissa Febos
description: Peering back at one’s childhood and adolescence can be a daunting task, one that Melissa Febos takes seriously. Although most of the essays in Girlhood dip into the author’s adult life as well, they keep trying to find the child and teenager that she was – how she learned to be, feel, believe and react. In her trademark lush prose, Febos questions the ways girls’ behaviors are controlled, and how we learn to cage and control ourselves well into adulthood. But there is hope: She also shares her own attempts to unlearn the lessons of girlhood and find pockets of liberation.

title: Harlem Shuffle: A Novel
author: Colson Whitehead
description: Colson Whitehead’s Harlem Shuffle is about a heist in Harlem and the ramifications of the crime for those who did the deed. The novel kicks off in 1959 and storms into the 1960s with Ray Carney, furniture store owner, at the helm. Whitehead’s characters live in Harlem (thus the title), and his book brings the Black community of the 1960s to life with insight, grace, research, an outstanding cast of characters, and humor that is by turns gritty, culturally observant and wickedly funny. Harlem Shuffle is a powerful must-read – if it isn’t already on your bookshelf.

title: House of Sticks: A Memoir
author: Ly Tran
description: Ly Tran’s memoir House of Sticks is unique among Vietnamese American narratives in the sense that the author’s identity crisis is not about liberating herself from her parents’ past, but her unwillingness to remove herself from their suffering. As an unsentimental yet deeply moving examination of filial bonds, displacement, war trauma, and poverty, the impact of House of Sticks lies in Tran’s nuanced celebration of a family love that withstands the enormous cost of the American dream.

title: Last Best Hope: America in Crisis and Renewal
author: George Packer
description: It’s no exaggeration to say I’ve thought about the essay at the core of this book – about how America has sorted itself into four tribes that increasingly view each other with suspicion and contempt – just about every day since I first read it. I think I’ve sent a link to the Atlantic excerpt to nearly everyone I know. George Packer’s analysis of how the pandemic and Trump-era hypercharged division in the country is bleak, despite the positive spin that the title’s framing and the book’s final chapter attempt to put on things. But it’s essential reading to understand what has gone wrong in our politics and civic institutions.

title: Let the Record Show: A Political History of ACT UP New York, 1987-1993
author: Sarah Schulman
description: This sprawling history of AIDS activism, based on hundreds of interviews conducted over a 20-year period, offers a stirring retrospective of the social movement responsible for tremendous advances in the ways people with HIV are treated in America – medically, politically and culturally. A veteran queer activist herself, Sarah Schulman aims to properly historicize both the accomplishments and the limitations of the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power. Let the Record Show is invaluable as both an archive and a blueprint for contemporary organizers of all stripes.

title: Martita, I Remember You / Martita, Te Recuerdo: A Story in English and Spanish
author: Sandra Cisneros
description: Every time that Sandra Cisneros has a new book out, I race to the library. I’ve never been disappointed, and this book is no exception. It’s a short story that Cisneros wrote in English, then had translated into Spanish with an unconventional tête-bêche binding: there’s a front cover for the English version, and when you flip the book over, there’s the Spanish one. The story is structured around letters between three women – Corina, Martita and Paola – who became friends in Paris and are now trying to revive the embers of that bond. Fans of Cisneros will recognize fictionalized elements of her own life: a young Latina woman abroad trying to kick-start her writing career while waiting on an arts grant she desperately needs, all the while making friends and getting her heart broken. But Corina’s story takes turns quite unlike Cisneros’ life and makes for an ending that I’ll be thinking about for a while.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Matrix: A Novel
author: Lauren Groff
description: It’s 1158 and an unlucky but well-educated girl of 17 is sent from her home to become a nun. She proves savvy and sharp-elbowed: In a mere lifetime, she transforms the decrepit abbey into a women-only, lightly queer Vatican. Fans of Fates and Furies will recognize Lauren Groff’s penchant for leads with dark psychologies – and not much else. It’s a challenging read, but deeply rewarding.

title: My Broken Language: A Memoir
author: Quiara Alegría Hudes
description: Yes, Quiara Alegría Hudes wrote the book to the Lin Manuel Miranda musical In the Heights. But she’s also a Pulitzer Prize-winning playwright in her own right. This memoir shows why. Hudes writes about growing up half white, half Latinx, and struggling to find her artistic voice in language that, despite the title, is lyrical and powerful.

title: Nicky & Vera: A Quiet Hero of the Holocaust and the Children He Rescued
author: Peter Sis
description: Nicky had a secret. An unassuming businessman, he saved hundreds of children from the Nazis – and never told anyone. This brilliant picture book by author-illustrator Peter Sis is also the story of Vera, a young Czech girl Nicky helped rescue. Their lives come together 50 years later when he is surprised on a television show and honored by those he saved. In addition to the moving text, the exquisite art is at once intimate and expansive, as is this incredibly moving story. (For ages 6 to 8)
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Night Rooms: Essays
author: Gina Nutt
description: In her trenchant essay collection Night Rooms, Gina Nutt blends images from horror films with personal experience, revealing how horror tropes both echo and affect real life. An essay on being a child beauty queen, “Whatever Happened to Baby Jane,” flashes between shopping malls and JonBenét Ramsey; a current of fear and desire pulses underneath. Nutt punctuates her meditations on anxiety, depression, suicide and grief with scenes from campy films like Texas Chainsaw Massacre and quotes from poets like Paul Celan. Reading Night Rooms feels like wandering the rooms of a haunted house as a final girl, trapped and forever reckoning with her survival.

title: Oh William!: A Novel
author: Elizabeth Strout
