On Día de los Muertos, Julieta and Ramón are masked strangers who share a kiss and assume they’ll never see each other again. That’s until Julieta finds out Ramón is her family’s new landlord and he plans to shut down their restaurant for good. This novel’s got a little Shakespeare and a lot of tacos, with a very steamy haters-to-lovers relationship at its core.

title: Portrait of a Thief: A Novel
author: Grace D. Li
description: At its core, Portrait of a Thief is a fun, frenetic heist novel. Readers follow five young Chinese Americans who are forcibly taking back what colonialism stole – specifically, five Chinese sculptures – from Western museums. Amid the heists, though, we watch these characters grapple with the weight of expectation specific to being part of immigrant families, and the unmoored loneliness of not feeling Chinese enough or American enough. Although the genre is a familiar one, Li establishes the stakes in a way that feels more relatable than stealing a necklace at the Met Gala ever could.

title: Camera Man: Buster Keaton, the Dawn of Cinema, and the Invention of the Twentieth Century
author: Dana Stevens
description: Whether or not you’re a fan of the silent film star Buster Keaton (and, my goodness, if you are not a fan, rectify that immediately), Camera Man is an essential guide. Dana Stevens guides us through films like Sherlock Jr. and The General and places Keaton in his proper temporal context. The book creatively uses his life to explore various eddies along the river of the 20th century: the early days of cinema, women’s rights, addiction and the development of television.

title: The Power Law: Venture Capital and the Making of the New Future
author: Sebastian Mallaby
description: Sebastian Mallaby is the master of unspooling human drama from financial systems. Here, the venture capitalists are the protagonists. Whether it’s financiers scrambling to court a pajama-wearing young Mark Zuckerberg or venture capitalist Bill Gurley’s efforts to oust Uber founder Travis Kalanick from the company, the stories are almost Shakespearean in their depictions of ambition, jealousy and ego.

title: The Power of Regret: How Looking Backward Moves Us Forward
author: Daniel Pink
description: This book not only reframed regret for me, it also showed me how the kinds of things we regret point us to what makes a life well-lived. It was really clarifying and transformative, and it informed the “big picture” types of decisions I’ve made since. It’s well-written and well-paced, too.

title: The Latinist: A Novel
author: Mark Prins
description: Oxford University graduate student Tessa Templeton trusts her dissertation adviser, Christopher Eccles — but should she? When she learns that her mentor has betrayed her, she tries to undo the damage he has done to her career, having learned that the only person she can trust is herself. Along the way, she unearths the truth about a little-known Latin lyric poet famous for a limping meter and discovers that, in the dark world of grad school, so much depends on the right footnote. The Latinist, which twists around the Daphne and Apollo myth from Ovid’s Metamorphoses, culminates with a deeply satisfying blow to the treachery of academia.

title: Our Last Days in Barcelona
author: Chanel Cleeton
description: Chanel Cleeton has a cinematic way of making multigenerational stories tangible, weaving lives together like threads in a tapestry. In her recent novel Our Last Days in Barcelona, she alternates timelines about three women whose lives are irreparably affected by the injustice they see in their homelands. Cleeton shows the butterfly effects of oppression and rebellion in Cuba and Spain and what happens when you’re forced to choose among loyalty to your country, family and love. Our Last Days in Barcelona is a master class in family and political drama, in star-crossed love stories and in capturing the enormity of what home is.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Mecca: A Novel
author: Susan Straight
description: I think of Susan Straight as the anti-Joan Didion. Both write about California, but Straight always felt her experience of California – filled with working-class refugees from Texas, Mississippi and Oaxaca – was overlooked, even dismissed, by Didion and her literary peers. Straight’s newest novel, Mecca, is a fine set of interwoven tales from her California. It’s set in a Southern California filled with desert highways, strip malls and small suburban houses where everyday people are sometimes faced with choices that are anything but. Her writing is both luminous and sharp and told, as usual, through richly layered family histories.

title: When We Were Birds: A Novel
author: Ayanna Lloyd Banwo
description: Over the past few years, we have been immersed in news about death. It often forces us to question our roles in this world and ask, why are we here? Ayanna Lloyd Banwo’s When We Were Birds gives us a look at what it means to live surrounded by death on a daily basis. This is a novel about inheritance, loss and healing that follows the love story between a man who buries the dead and a woman whose ancestral destiny is to guide their souls after death. The book’s various threads deliver insight into life and death, written entirely in Trinidadian dialect.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters"]

title: Chilean Poet: A Novel
author: Alejandro Zambra, translated by Megan McDowell
description: This book paints a beautiful, hilarious, sometimes excruciating portrait of what it means to be a cliche at different stages in your life. There’s the teenager who can’t get over a heartbreak, the young adult trying to outrun their nerdy youth, the overwhelmed new dad, the dad who leaves, the dad who doesn’t know how to come back, the son who pretends not to care. And of course, the tortured poet. Alejandro Zambra makes all of these stages feel rich and human, and the characters never become too old – or too stuck in their ways – to outgrow themselves.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Hacienda
author: Isabel Cañas
description: I’m a sucker for Gothic novels, and I’ve been loving the trend of Gothics that take place somewhere unexpected (i.e., not Europe). The Hacienda is a story that takes us to, well, a hacienda – in a remote Mexican town in the 1820s, not long after the War of Independence. The protagonist, Beatriz, moves to her new husband’s large estate, eager to escape the rejection, poverty and tragedy that she’s suffered in Mexico City. What she finds instead is a ridiculously haunted house inhabited by some equally haunted-seeming people – including those meant to be closest to her.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Historical Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Dark", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: A Warbler's Journey
author: Scott Weidensaul, illustrated by Nancy Lane
description: If tragedy is the grim excavator of beauty, the era of our pandemic shutdown exemplified that. Bird song rose when noise levels from traffic, airplanes and even barking dogs plummeted. Bird-watching boomed.The world stopped, and birds and people noticed each other. A new book for children and adults by Scott Weidensaul, with gorgeous illustrations by Nancy Lane, is an epic story of a warbler’s resilient migration told through the eyes of two little girls. The story of its adventure is smart, beautiful and a reminder of what’s possible when we notice what we have in common with a tiny bird. (For ages 5 to 8)

title: Making Videogames: The Art of Creating Digital Worlds
author: Duncan Harris and Alex Wiltshire
description: Visual beauty may not be the main explanation for video games’ hold on our culture, but this remarkable coffee table volume makes a compelling case for how  the exquisite collusion of math and design contributes to their power. Non-gamers may be startled to learn how the heightened naturalism of the Hudson River School inspired the look of Red Dead Redemption 2 or how Control’s atmospheric lighting is drawn from the films of Nichols Winding Refn. Making Videogames is as immersive and impressionistic as the games it celebrates; a reminder that, like anything else, they’re an interface empty without art.

title: Under The Skin: The Hidden Toll of Racism on American Lives and on the Health of Our Nation
author: Linda Villarosa
description: In this sobering, page-turning account, Linda Villarosa identifies racism as the recessive gene in the nation’s DNA that adversely and devastatingly affects the health of Black people and continues to rob them of years of life expectancy. The systemic factors that were exposed by the COVID-19 pandemic have roots that go back centuries. As she writes, “Something about being Black is creating a health crisis, and that something is racism. It is the American problem in need of an American solution.”

title: Blood and Ruins: The Last Imperial War, 1931-1945
author: Richard Overy
description: Do we need another doorstop history of the best chronicled war of all time? When the book tries to do something wholly new – urges us to think of World War II in a completely new way – the answer is an emphatic yes. Only rarely do such books appear, and this is one of those rarest of books – and one that future histories of that conflict will likely be measured against for a while (and also one that was unexpectedly a perfect companion to the on-going conflict in Ukraine; I can’t remember the number of times I scribbled in the margins, “Just like today!”)

title: The Return of Faraz Ali: A Novel
author: Aamina Ahmad
description: It starts out as a crime novel. A murder. A cop. An investigation. And then evolves into so much more. This assured debut novel evokes an older Pakistan, with all the many complications of class, society, entitlement, entangled history. Anyone looking for a pat ending should leave this one alone; instead come for the evocative writing, the subtle characters, and plot – some of which veered in territory completely unexpected territory.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: How High We Go in the Dark: A Novel
author: Sequoia Nagamatsu
description: Sequoia Nagamatsu’s debut is beautiful and unsparing in its depiction of a world reeling from a climate catastrophe-driven plague. From the earliest days of a pandemic, to the impacts that linger centuries into the future, the plague forces humans to reckon with immeasurable grief and loss. And the commercialization of death is inescapable: There’s a euthanasia amusement park for terminally ill children, a hotel for the dead. But despite the doom and gloom, these stories are endlessly imaginative and rich with meaning. Though the universe these stories are unfolding within is undeniably bleak, Nagamatsu imbues his characters with a sense of cosmic hope and humanity.

title: The Paradox Hotel: A Novel
author: Rob Hart
description: Mixing elements of mystery, thrillers and science fiction, this is a riotously imaginative story that has everything from corpses that aren’t quite there to dinosaur-egg contraband. A fresh take on the dark possibilities of time traveling that also manages to discuss global warming. As funny as it is tense and engaging.

title: Scattered All Over the Earth
author: Yoko Tawada, translated by Margaret Mitsutani
description: This dystopian novel is one of those books I’m terrified enough people won’t read. It’s riveting, bizarre as can be, and like nothing else I’ve ever read. A refugee from a Japan that no longer exists, Hiruko is a teacher who travels through Europe speaking in her invented language that somehow just makes sense to everyone. The book’s told in episodes and strange little spells of romance and precise world-building, which is incredibly fun in itself. But the real pull is Hiruko, one of the most charming and memorable characters I’ve ever encountered. Forget Wordle: Hiroko has us doing word games in a dystopian world! What more could one ask for?
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Nightcrawling: A Novel
author: Leila Mottley
description: High school dropout Kiara Johnson has a lot on her plate – she has rent and bills to pay while caring for her brother and her young neighbor who lives next door. So she turns to something she never expected she would do to make ends meet: nightcrawling, or in other words, sex work. Through her work, she sees her city from new perspectives – ones that hold the joy, pain and resilience of its people despite failing infrastructure and corrupt police. Leila Mottley was just 17 when she began writing this debut novel, inspired by true events in the Oakland Police Department.

title: Good Inside: A Guide to Becoming the Parent You Want to Be
author: Becky Kennedy
description: Good Inside is not just about sleep, or food, or sibling rivalry, though “Dr. Becky” does share concrete advice on all those things in this comprehensive parenting guide. (Personal shoutout to the whining section!) It’s about how to build a relationship with your kid, especially when they’re struggling and acting out. How do you set boundaries and reassure a child that you’re unconditionally on their team? Becky Kennedy’s simple, empowering connection-first approach to parenting prompted many aha moments for me. And my almost totally underlined copy has become a trusted map on the miraculous and maddening journey that is parenting.

title: Undercover Latina
author: Aya de León
description: Seamless code-switching becomes a spy technique in this tale of a Latinx teen who must pose as a white girl to expose a white supremacist terrorist. Aya de León cleverly touches on questions of authenticity and “passing” while at the same time filling her book with spy gear, hidden bombs, undercover work and even the occasional cute boy. (For ages 10 to 14)

title: American Murderer: The Parasite That Haunted the South
author: Gail Jarrow
description: Gail Jarrow’s latest entry in the “Medical Fiascoes” series for kids focuses on the history of the hookworm and how it sucked the blood and energy out of the American South in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Good luck prying this book – terrifying and fascinating by turns – out of the hands of young readers who love getting grossed out. (For ages 10 to 17)

title: The Invisible Kingdom: Reimagining Chronic Illness
author: Meghan O’Rourke
description: Shortly after graduating from college, Meghan O’Rourke began experiencing sharp, stabbing pains – almost like electric shocks or tiny beestings – up and down her arms and legs. In the decades that followed, the stings would mysteriously flare up or fade away, as would a host of other debilitating symptoms, including extreme fatigue, brain fog and chronic pain. O’Rourke shuffled between specialists and spent thousands of dollars on treatment before finally getting a diagnosis. The Invisible Kingdom is O’Rourke’s lyrical account reckoning with her own body – and with a health care system that overlooks and gaslights Americans living with autoimmune disorders. A finalist for the National Book Award, this is a particularly resonant read as the nation untangles the lingering symptoms of long COVID.

title: The Star That Always Stays
author: Anna Rose Johnson
description: The Star That Always Stays is set in the early 1900s and lovingly woven from the threads left for debut author Anna Rose Johnson by her ancestors from the Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians. Johnson’s writing is gentle, unhurried and reflective – with touches of humor and heartbreak. She showcases Norvia, a courageous young hero navigating complex family dynamics, the pressure to pass and universal daily-life challenges, too. Supplemental materials provide a bridge for middle graders interested in learning more. This new release could be paired well with “The Birchbark House” series by Louise Erdrich. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Latecomer
author: Jean Hanff Korelitz
description: Desperate to become a mother, Johanna Oppenheimer is an early adopter of IVF — but she never imagined that her triplets would grow up to despise one another. With her husband still distant after a tragedy in his youth, Johanna decides to have a fourth child from an embryo frozen nearly 20 years prior. Are shared genes and one woman’s sheer will enough to constitute a family, let alone sustain it? The Oppenheimers may scatter at the first opportunity, but what binds them together is at the heart of this sharp, decades-spanning saga.

title: Essential Labor: Mothering as Social Change
author: Angela Garbes
description: Working from home – amid unwashed dishes and unswept floors – has been a powerful reminder for me of how our economy relies on unpaid or underpaid domestic labor. Angela Garbes argues that the often unglamorous tasks of home maintenance, care work and mothering  – a verb inclusive of anyone involved in raising children – are critical to society, yet they’ve been rendered invisible by capitalism and colonialism. Garbes characterizes parenting as a reminder of our bodily needs, and an avenue for social change as valuable as protesting and voting.

title: Bad Sex: Truth, Pleasure, and an Unfinished Revolution
author: Nona Willis Aronowitz
description: When Nona Willis Aronowitz finally ends her marriage because of lackluster sex, she’s left with a question: Why, more than a half-century after the sexual revolution, are we still having bad sex, and what is standing in the way of our satisfaction? The daughter of prominent feminist Ellen Willis, Aronowitz is well situated for the messy work of understanding how cultural forces shape intimate desires. She deftly weaves her own story with her late mother’s writings. This spirited history paints feminism not as a fixed set of passed-down beliefs, but as an invitation to question the status quo.

title: Scenes from My Life: A Memoir
author: Michael K. Williams and Jon Sternfeld
description: It seemed an impossible task: finishing the deeply personal memoir of groundbreaking actor Michael K. Williams months after his death from a drug overdose. But co-author Jon Sternfeld artfully completed the searingly honest story of how Williams survived a childhood filled with trauma in a Brooklyn housing project to become one of Hollywood’s most impactful actors. Known for playing tough characters who subvert traditional ideals of Black masculinity – including his breakout role as The Wire’s gay robber of drug dealers, Omar Little – Williams was the opposite of a tough guy in his neighborhood. “Way before I was anything or anyone, I was an addict,” Williams writes in the book’s introduction, previewing a hopeful tone on overcoming his addictions that lends more poignancy for readers, who already know how his story ends.

title: It's Not TV: The Spectacular Rise, Revolution, and Future of HBO
author: Felix Gillette and John Koblin
description: Published days before the 50th anniversary of HBO, this book from high-level media reporters Felix Gillette (Bloomberg News) and John Koblin (The New York Times) does more than trace the background of a programming service that has survived multiple bouts of dysfunctional ownership to define quality television. It also outlines the company’s problematic history with how female executives have been treated, further exploring previously reported allegations that former HBO Chairman Chris Albrecht choked a female staffer with whom he had had a consensual affair and then disparaged her after she took a settlement and left the network. By recounting such stories and the service’s fitful shift from male-centered programming to more diverse offerings, Gillette and Koblin present an incisive account that is more than a rosy victory lap for one of TV’s most influential channels.

title: The Trayvon Generation
author: Elizabeth Alexander
description: A meditation on the relationship between Black joy and Black pain, Elizabeth Alexander’s book of essays focuses on “The Trayvon Generation.” How did pervasive experiences and stories of anti-Black violence shape these young people and their views of the world and of themselves? Art by Black artists is interspersed throughout, but Alexander explains her work “is not a celebration, nor is it an elegy.” The Trayvon Generation is a reminder that humanity and freedom must be constantly reasserted, danced, sung, painted and proclaimed.

title: Nona the Ninth
author: Tamsyn Muir
description: Nona can’t really remember who she is. Or why her planet is on the verge of destruction. She just wants a birthday party – and to pet all the dogs she can find. (Highly relatable.) Nona the Ninth, the third book in “The Locked Tomb” series, is a thrilling, mind-boggling and surprisingly heartwarming story, answering pertinent questions from the first two books and leaving you with more. It hurtles us through time and space, has scientists with God complexes (literally) and features many bouts of necromancy. Be prepared to find yourself yelling, “What? WHAT?” several times over.

title: Lessons: A Novel
author: Ian McEwan
description: In Lessons, Ian McEwan’s hapless hero combs his life trajectory from childhood to old age, seeking an overarching thread that makes sense of it all. Despite the novel’s many autobiographical elements, Roland Blaine, unlike McEwan, feels he never lived up to his potential – careerwise or otherwise. Set against a backdrop of 70 years of global cataclysms, his focus is on damaging wrong turns, including an aberrant adolescent affair with a predatory boarding school teacher, and abandonment by his wife, who left him and their infant son without notice in order to give her all to her writing. There are lessons aplenty here, including how to read literature that borrows and bends details from real lives, including the author’s.

title: Fellowship Point: A Novel
author: Alice Elliott Dark
description: This deliciously immersive novel is about the lifelong friendship between two women who share a love for the coastal peninsula in Maine where their families have summered for generations. But they’ve made very different life choices – one a never-married, stridently independent author who has preserved her privacy with a pseudonym, the other a deferential wife and mother. As they enter their 80s, their divergent paths color their stance regarding the future of Fellowship Point, which is threatened by developers. Dark’s beautifully written novel is rich with reflections on aging, writing and responsibility for the people and places we love.

title: Lucy by the Sea: A Novel
author: Elizabeth Strout
description: Elizabeth Strout’s fourth novel about Lucy Barton finds the recently widowed, hypersensitive, supremely sympathetic writer at sea, awash in confusion as she agrees to flee Manhattan and ride out the pandemic in Maine with her philandering ex, William. Lucy’s plain-spoken, painfully honest narrative reads as if she were baring her heart and soul to a new friend. This chronicle of the first year of the pandemic captures its disruptions, uncertainties, anxieties and changing relationships better than any novel I’ve read on the subject. It also demonstrates once again that Strout’s warmth and understanding of the human condition are capacious.

title: The Hero of This Book: A Novel
author: Elizabeth McCracken
description: Decades ago, Elizabeth McCracken promised her fiercely private mother that she would never write about her. So this posthumous tribute is a lightly fictionalized memoir that interrogates genre and the act of writing as it conjures up her mother – the hero of this book – in all her splendid idiosyncrasy. It features the snappy prose and oddball sensibility we’ve come to love in McCracken’s novels and stories. “Why do I write?” her barely disguised narrator asks. “To try to get human beings on a page without the use of vivisection or preservatives or a spiritualist’s props.” Mission accomplished, delightfully.

title: Foster
author: Claire Keegan
description: Foster is spare, heart-rending – and perfect. This is the first release of its full text in the U.S., which, since its original publication in Claire Keegan’s native Ireland in 2010, has become part of the school curriculum. The nameless narrator is a little girl whose parents, impoverished Catholic farmers already overwhelmed by too many children and the father’s bad habits, farm her out to distant relatives before the next baby’s arrival. She has no idea what to expect – whether she’ll be worked hard or treated kindly, and for how long. The experience turns out to be life changing, as the deprivations of her former life reveal themselves through the things she notices, like the sparkling kitchen and calm efficiency of her temporary guardians.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Man Who Could Move Clouds: A Memoir
author: Ingrid Rojas Contreras
description: When you can’t return to your home country, there’s something very cathartic about reading someone else’s chance to do it. That was my feeling following Ingrid Rojas Contreras on her journey back to Colombia with her mother – a trip meant to wrap up some of her late grandfather’s unfinished business. Not any regular business: He was a curandero, a healer, and he comes to them in their dreams for this last request. In a fascinating exploration of magic, gender, family and memory, Rojas Contreras writes in such a gripping narrative that it’s easy to forget it’s about her real life.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Biography & Memoir", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Less Is Lost
author: Andrew Sean Greer
description: Arthur Less’ losses are legion: his former lover, his current partner, his house, his self-confidence. So – in this follow-up to the Pulitzer Prize-winning Less – he takes to the road in a rented van (with a borrowed pug) to make some money and figure out if he still loves who (and what) he thinks he loves. He’s accused of being a “bad gay” and wonders if he really might be guilty as charged. Less is a hapless Walloon: a possibly European man of letters and privilege who stumbles his way out of impending tragedies, through mounting humiliations, and into surprising successes. Yet his inability to recognize his luck as luck is why we love Less more and more.
tags: ["Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Attack of the Black Rectangles
author: Amy Sarig King
description: Ms. Sett does not appreciate Mac’s questions about slavery, stolen lands or early explorers in her sixth-grade history class. When Mac opens his classroom copy of the Holocaust novel The Devil’s Arithmetic and finds that many of the words are blacked out, he and his friends discover Ms. Sett is behind the black rectangles. With no help from the principal or most adults in the town, Mac and his friends are determined to find out what the blacked-out words are and take on their teacher, many parents and the school board in a battle against injustice and censorship. It’s a novel about having the freedom to read and learn – and trusting kids with hard things. (For ages 9 to 12)

title: The Last Beekeeper
author: Pablo Cartaya
description: In a future where technology is king, the environment has collapsed, and most people subsist in desperate attempts to farm the ruined Earth, 12-year-old Yoly is determined to get out of her desolate valley and into the elite city of Silo. But when she learns that the desolation, poverty and subjugation are manufactured by the ruling class, Yoly takes matters into her own hands. Discovering that her family has managed to keep one last hive of the long-extinct honeybee alive, Yoly takes on the task of saving the valley – and maybe the world. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The First Kennedys: The Humble Roots of an American Dynasty
author: Neal Thompson
description: The Kennedy mystique is well-trodden ground. But for the First Kennedys, it was anything but Camelot: escaping Ireland’s Great Famine aboard a coffin ship, surviving Boston’s disease-ridden tenements and searing anti-Catholic bigotry. Bridget Murphy Kennedy, John F. Kennedy’s great-grandmother, leaps from the page, an indomitable force in her newly adopted country, a grocer who stocks liquor behind the counter and wrenches her penniless family from poverty. The Irishness of this story gripped me: the sociability, the raw intelligence and, yes, a bit of larceny – all conspiring in the improbable rise of not only the Kennedys but a whole new class of immigrants.

title: The Newlyweds: Rearranging Marriage in Modern India
author: Mansi Choksi
description: In Bollywood films, boy meets girl, they fall in love – and everyone dances in unison. In real life, more than 90% of Indian marriages are arranged by families. Caste and religion matter more than chemistry. This book is about the less than 10% who defy tradition and follow their hearts. Bookending The Newlyweds with her own parents’ story, Mansi Choksi braids the love stories of three couples who break boundaries of caste, religion and gender to be together. Choksi observes them like an anthropologist, documents their courtships like the investigative journalist she is – and writes like a poet. The result is a meditation on love, and what we sacrifice for it.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: A Merry Little Meet Cute
author: Julie Murphy and Sierra Simone
description: Welcome to Christmas Notch, Vt., the gingerbread-scented backdrop for the Hope Channel’s wholesome holiday movies. When adult-film star Bee Hobbes (aka Bianca Von Honey) is accidentally cast in this year’s movie, she knows she’s got to keep her porn identity under wraps – and get used to performing with her clothes on. Meanwhile, Bee’s co-star (and childhood boyband crush), Nolan Shaw, is hoping to use the Lifetime-esque movie to rehab his own bad-boy image. He’s just gotta keep his, ahem, candy cane in his pants. This holiday romance is naughty and nice and everything in between.

title: How to Keep House While Drowning: A Gentle Approach to Cleaning and Organizing
author: KC Davis
description: Have you recently uttered the phrase “things will calm down after this week”? Does the thought of tackling your mountain of laundry send you into a deep abyss? This is the kindest, gentlest guide to the Sisyphean tasks of cleaning, grooming and generally keeping oneself alive. Part pep talk, part guidebook, KC Davis’ tips are aloe vera for a burned-out brain. Her take on tidying? There are only five things in any room: trash, dishes, laundry, things that have a place and things that do not have a place. Toss everything into those five piles and you’ve already made this monumental task smaller. Davis digs into the emotional relationship we have with chores – reminding us that mess is not a moral failure.

title: America Made Me a Black Man: A Memoir
author: Boyah J. Farah
description: When Boyah J. Farah arrived in the United States as a teenager, he expected the country to be paradise. And for a while it was; when he rode his bike down the quiet streets of his Boston suburb, past smiling neighbors with their perfectly manicured lawns, he felt like he was in a sort of heaven. But try as he might to hold on to that image, the reality of American racism eventually began to surface cracks in Farah’s fantasy. This memoir tells the story of what Blackness has meant to Farah, from his childhood in Somalia to his coming-of-age in the Northeast, to the moment as an adult he decided to return to Somalia after decades spent away.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana", "Identity & Culture"]

title: Fruit Punch: A Memoir
author: Kendra Allen
description: This book is called Fruit Punch, but “sangria” might be the closer metaphor for what it felt like to read. The memoir is funny and painful and sweet and angry and scared and hopeful – not at different moments, but all at once. The writing is spare and the chapters are short, but each vignette is dense: learning to slow dance in the laundromat, driving to visit her dad for the last time, losing your religion while singing in the church choir. Kendra Allen contrasts the simplicity of childhood language with the dizzying complexity of childhood emotion and experience. It’s a book best sipped on, in my experience. Drink too fast and you might lose your head.

title: Mother of Strangers: A Novel
author: Suad Amiry
description: Toward the beginning of this novel, Subhi – “the best mechanic in town,” despite being only 15 – is called to fix the well of a local citrus magnate. When he arrives at the grove, he is taken aback by how different the city of Jaffa looks from way up high; how seeing it from this vantage point must affect one’s hopes, fears, politics and loyalties. That sense of perspective-shifting carries throughout the book. The characters force readers to think about how our desires change when our settings do and how expansive (or limited) our sense of reality might be.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture"]

title: House of Hunger: A Novel
author: Alexis Henderson
description: It’s a cliché, of course, to talk about a job that requires blood, sweat and tears. But in this book, author Alexis Henderson imagines a world in which certain young women are asked to devote literal blood – drained from the arm at needle-point – to their employers to drink. It’s a Gothic horror that blurs the lines between work and love, coercion and choice, freedom and servitude. And though the book’s setting involves a bit of the paranormal, the questions it asks about our relationship to work are all too real: What are you willing to give of yourself? Will you know your limits before it’s too late? Or will you gladly surrender the last and best parts of you to the person who holds your purse strings?

title: The Daughter of Doctor Moreau
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: Where, exactly, is the line between human and animal? Deep in the tropical forests of Silvia Moreno-Garcia’s Yucatán, the answer is intentionally unclear. A girl, Carlota, grows up helping her father with his important, but mysterious research. But it is only when their small enclave is visited by outsiders that Carlota realizes just how earth-shattering this research really is and how deeply she’s implicated in it. Part science fiction, part adventure, part social critique, this book – like many of the characters who inhabit it – is never just one thing.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Thistlefoot: A Novel
author: GennaRose Nethercott
description: For centuries, the crone Baba Yaga has been a figure in Slavic folklore – the kind of character who might lend you a magical candle or kill you and use your skull to decorate her house on chicken legs. In her debut novel, Thistlefoot, author and folklorist GennaRose Nethercott reimagines Baba Yaga as a Jewish woman living in an Eastern European shtetl in 1919, during a time of civil war and pogroms. Through the crone and her story, Nethercott explores the idea of folklore as a retelling of a memory too painful to talk about plainly.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture"]

title: The Revolutionary: Samuel Adams
author: Stacy Schiff
description: “More than just a beer!” Perhaps that’s what Stacy Schiff should have subtitled her biography of Samuel (never “Sam”) Adams, since his popular image as a brewer has overshadowed his role as “chief incendiary” of the American Revolution. Schiff’s biography of Adams is un-put-downable. It opens on Paul Revere’s ride and charges forward at breakneck pace. Adams was an activist, tirelessly writing and organizing to whip up early support for independence; he also opposed slavery and championed universal free education. In this thrilling biography, Schiff gives us a rough-and-ready Founding Father who fought against the odds to realize democracy.

title: The Light Pirate
author: Lily Brooks-Dalton
description: A near-future Florida is abandoned by the government after extreme heat and a series of devastating hurricanes that tear and soak the state. But some people don’t leave, even when electricity and communication are no longer supported. The Light Pirate is about Wanda, a girl burdened with the name of the storm that fractured her family and led to her community’s slow death. She learns to thrive as Florida re-wilds in a story that’s surprisingly beautiful and uplifting, given that it’s built on the rubble of civilization’s self-inflicted collapse.

title: The Pallbearers Club
author: Paul Tremblay
description: Creepy, funny and Gen X all the way: Art Barbara’s memoir is about his odd friendship with Mercy Brown, whom he meets in the late 1980s when he’s 17. She’s older. Cooler. She turns him on to punk music. Oh – and she feeds on his life force, or does she? While Art builds his case that she’s a New England vampire, Mercy (writing in the margins), firmly and hilariously disputes his memoir. By the end of The Pallbearers Club, you don’t know for sure if Art’s delusional or if Mercy is a gaslighter: It’s a deliciously nagging confusion that makes the book a masterful psychological thriller.

title: The Devil Takes You Home
author: Gabino Iglesias
description: This story is about one man’s descent into hell, with violence so relentless I repeatedly grabbed my bookmark and took a breather. But I kept coming back, unable to resist Gabino Iglesias’ crisp, propulsive writing. His narrator, Mario, quits an honest job and becomes a hit man. Mario’s wife has left him following his young daughter’s death. He’s empty inside (so he thinks), plus there’s crushing medical debt to pay off. Mario thinks he’ll get a fresh start if he teams up with two other men to successfully hijack a drug cartel’s cash shipment before it gets to Mexico. Instead, he’s on a dark odyssey made even darker by the growing realization that he’s not as morally bankrupt as he thinks he is, but will it be too late?

title: Stories from the Tenants Downstairs
author: Sidik Fofana
description: Among the eight, interconnected short stories set in a Harlem high rise is one that will break you: a middle schooler named Najee who’s struggling to write a remorseful letter to a friend’s mother. There’s also a would-be hairdresser who’s mortified when he goes viral and a young woman wrestling with animosity and vengeance toward one of her neighbors-in-need. Sidik Fofana’s characters tell their stories in such vivid voices, rendered so completely, that you swear you can see their facial expressions and hear their breathing as you laugh at their wit and hurt at their pain: a brilliant debut.

title: Breathless: The Scientific Race to Defeat a Deadly Virus
author: David Quammen
description: I’m normally not interested in reading about the coronavirus pandemic in my free time. This book is one of the few exceptions. David Quammen, whose book Spillover predicted that a novel coronavirus might cause the next pandemic, writes about the virus that causes the disease COVID-19 by introducing us to the scientists who raced against the clock to uncover the microbe’s origins and how it was spreading so quickly. Breathless was a National Book Award finalist for good reason: The quality of the writing succeeded in helping me understand how exactly the coronavirus jumped from animal to human, while keeping me eagerly turning the pages.

title: Didn't Nobody Give a Shit What Happened to Carlotta
author: James Hannaham
description: When we first meet Carlotta, she’s freshly released from federal prison, or as she calls it, an “incorrectual facility” where there were “never no guards to guard the guards.” Terrible timing put her behind bars for decades, and her bad luck gives no quarter. Wearing homemade makeup and prison khakis, Carlotta swirls back to Brooklyn into a family largely indifferent to her gender transition and through a gentrified New York that leaves her – and the reader – breathless with nostalgia. Riotous and resilient, she is a supercharged survivor. Author James Hannaham’s picaresque novel, written with hedonistic verve, does Carlotta justice.

title: Sweet, Soft, Plenty Rhythm
author: Laura Warrell
description: In this stunning novel – longlisted for the Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence in Fiction – Laura Warrell turns the “womanizer” trope inside out by centering the stories, struggles, and, yes, joys, of the diverse cast of women who populate his life. Her “womanizer” – 40-year-old jazz musician Circus Palmer – learns the woman he loves most is unexpectedly pregnant with his child. Meanwhile, his teenage daughter Koko (my favorite character in the book) struggles to understand her own blossoming sexuality, cope with her father’s rejection and deal with her mother’s deteriorating mental health. I love the way Sweet, Soft, Plenty Rhythm’s musical, staccato prose enlivens its subject matter. This is a book for anyone who has experienced heartbreak, love and everything in between.

title: Dark Goals: How History’s Worst Tyrants Have Used and Abused the Game of Soccer
author: Luciano Wernicke
description: All manner of scoundrels, strongmen (and strongwomen) have sought to use “The Beautiful Game” as a canvas for their philosophies. Adolf Hitler’s propaganda minister, Joseph Goebbels, tried to use soccer to elevate Nazism. In Argentina, Evita Perón made it clear which team should be ascendant – and what would befall those who didn’t see to it. And the list goes on. Dark Goals chronicles the ways that the world’s most popular and democratic sport has laundered the reputations of nations and their leaders. As you watch the World Cup in Qatar, listen carefully for the echoes of a complicated past that is prologue.

title: An American Martyr in Persia: The Epic Life and Tragic Death of Howard Baskerville
author: Reza Aslan
description: I picked up An American Martyr in Persia not knowing or, frankly, caring much about early 20th-century Iran or the American missionaries who spread the Gospel there, but Reza Aslan does a remarkable job proving the importance of that time and place. Howard Baskerville was an evangelist of both American religious virtue and democracy when his own Jim Crow America was little more than an apartheid state. Still, the people of Persia – now Iran – believed in his ideals and risked their lives to win their own freedom. What then happened to them – and to Baskerville himself – was a tragedy that echoes down to the present day, as Iranians of a new generation attempt to claim for themselves some measure of the freedoms Americans take for granted.

title: Mercury Pictures Presents: A Novel
author: Anthony Marra
description: Nearly a decade after his debut, A Constellation of Vital Phenomena, Anthony Marra returns with a sprawling story that ushers readers through the streets of pre-World War II Rome, the backlots and front offices of a B-movie studio in Hollywood and the halls of Congress. The novel centers on Italian émigré Maria, teasing through the tangled, complex relationships she has with the men who shaped her past, empowered her career, and inhibit or expand her dreams. But as compelling as the story and characters are, the real star is the writing. Marra’s ability to capture and render both the joy and pain of life, and depict the web of fate that encompasses and shapes our lives, remains unmatched.

title: Our Wives Under the Sea: A Novel
author: Julia Armfield
description: This is a deeply strange and haunting novel in the best possible way. A married couple – Leah and Miri – try to reacclimate to life together after Leah, a marine biologist, returns from an expedition gone wrong in the deep sea. Miri is desperate to help the woman she loves, but it becomes increasingly clear Leah is slipping from her grasp. An impressive and exciting debut novel that may leave you thinking about your own relationships in a new light.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Love & Romance"]

title: His Name Is George Floyd: One Man’s Life and the Struggle for Racial Justice
author: Robert Samuels and Toluse Olorunnipa
description: Most of the world knows George Floyd as the symbol of a new movement for racial justice. Journalists Robert Samuels and Toluse Olorunnipa tell the story of George Floyd the man, before he was killed under a white police officer’s knee. He liked telling people he loved them. He suffered lifelong claustrophobia. A large Black man, he knew he made people nervous, so he went out of his way to put them at ease. This deeply reported biography reaches into history to help us understand how the forces of racism shaped every aspect of Floyd’s life, up to his tragic death.

title: I'm Glad My Mom Died
author: Jennette McCurdy
description: Former iCarly star Jennette McCurdy’s account of her tumultuous relationship with show business, disordered eating and abuse by her mother is the heart of her debut memoir, I’m Glad My Mom Died. McCurdy’s storytelling is not only fantastic but intimate and raw, full of both pain and humor. While it can be hard to read, learning about how much she was going through privately while in the public eye, I’m Glad My Mom Died is also hard to put down – and hard to forget.

title: Lapvona: A Novel
author: Ottessa Moshfegh
description: Lapvona is gross. Ottessa Moshfegh writes about bodies with specificity – all the orifices and odors and juices come alive so the whole book kind of smells like toe jam, in the best possible way. A young boy in the small, famine-stricken medieval town of Lapvona finds himself adopted into a powerful noble family, exposing a class discrepancy that serves as a deeper meditation on the pointlessness of life and the cruelty of God or god or “God.” Is there salvation to be found in Lapvona? I’m not sure, but if there is, it’s probably a little stinky.

title: Luda: A Novel
author: Grant Morrison
description: Aging drag artist Luci LaBang gets goaded into mentoring a young protégée in the ways of her magic. This is, of course, a horrible idea. The world Grant Morrison creates in Luda is mystical and mysterious: Is Luci actually imbued with magical abilities, or is she just that good at makeup? Does it matter? Recommended if you like going, “Wait, what the heck is going on?” – not in a confusing way, but more in bewilderment of the adventure Morrison is taking you on.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance"]

title: If I Survive You
author: Jonathan Escoffery
description: I could say something buttoned-up, like “Jonathan Escoffery’s debut collection of short stories examines race, identity and class in incisive ways.” And that’d be true, but phrasing it that way betrays the lack of didacticism in his writing. Instead, the book – which follows generations of one Jamaican American family – focuses on the hunger (literal and figurative), heartbreak, horniness and (maybe?) hope that often come hand-in-hand with trying to make it in this country.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Partners in Crime: A Novel
author: Alisha Rai
description: Mira is an accountant who wants to settle down in a boring, stable relationship using an Indian matchmaker. But when her aunt dies and Mira needs to settle the estate, she runs into ex-boyfriend Naveen. If it isn’t awkward enough to run into the gorgeous guy you ghosted, try being kidnapped – and then escaping – with him. Mira and Naveen must race to find a missing diamond necklace in Vegas, with a crime syndicate on their trail. Fast-paced Partners in Crime is Ocean’s Eleven meets The Lost City, full of high jinks and sexy capers. It’s a fun, and at times tender, read.

title: Carrie Soto Is Back: A Novel
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
description: Taylor Jenkins Reid has done it again! Carrie Soto Is Back follows a legendary but unpopular tennis player as she comes out of retirement to attempt a comeback. Unable to stomach her Grand Slam record being beaten, she brings her dad back as her coach and trains with a former rival. Alternating between flashbacks and present day, readers discover the cost of fame, the struggles of being a female athlete, and that it’s OK to fail sometimes. Its release coincided with Serena Williams’ final matches, which made it even more interesting to read about another late-career female tennis star showing the world what she can do.

title: A Map for the Missing: A Novel
author: Belinda Huijuan Tang
description: I love fiction that takes me somewhere I’ve never been, and I’ve certainly never been to rural China in the 1970s. Belinda Huijuan Tang’s debut novel has the building blocks of a page turner. There’s romance, family drama and societal upheaval. It centers on an adjunct professor in the U.S. who returns home to his family’s small village in China for the first time in years. But it’s more than just a great story. Tang’s deep research into the waning days of the Cultural Revolution allows this novel to rest on a foundation of a fascinating moment in Chinese history.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Chaos Machine: The Inside Story of How Social Media Rewired Our Minds and Our World
author: Max Fisher
description: This book has actually changed the way I use social media on a daily basis. It has also changed the way I think about the role of platforms like Facebook and Twitter in politics, global affairs and my own neighborhood. At dinner parties, I have to stop myself from sharing too many jaw-dropping anecdotes from the book, like the one about what YouTube did to reach its goal of a billion hours of watch time per day. In short, if you use social media or know people who do, this one is worth your time.

title: The Family Outing: A Memoir
author: Jessi Hempel
description: Every family has secrets. In a span of five years, everyone in Jessi Hempel’s family revealed theirs – some coming out as queer, one as trans, and one as a survivor of a traumatic experience. But as the author says, “Revealing our secrets is not the same as living our truths.” With rich detail and deep empathy, Hempel chronicles the highs and lows of their journey to healing, through marriages and divorces, personal growth and grief. Along the way, they began creating families of their own and grew stronger together.

title: Anon Pls.: A Novel
author: DeuxMoi
description: The story of DeuxMoi – an anonymously run Instagram account that posts celebrity gossip items – is now a novel, written by its still-anonymous creator. In Anon Pls., DeuxMoi is Cricket Lopez, an overworked and underpromoted assistant. Her boss is a publicly lauded but privately heinous stylist, whose tyrannical behavior has grown intolerable. This burnout leads Lopez to start DeuxMoi as a release valve that gains steam overnight. The novel does a good job of exploring the motivations and land mines that come with running an account as popular as DeuxMoi. While holding A-list celebrities accountable is fulfilling, it’s a great power that comes with great responsibility.

title: The World's Worst Assistant
author: Sona Movsesian
description: If you know Conan O’Brien, you know Sona Movsesian. Movsesian has been the late-night host’s assistant for years, but she’s always been a star. Her extremely funny memoir spans her childhood growing up in Hacienda Heights, Calif., and travels through the highs and lows of her comedy-adjacent career. Her observations are spunky and sweet, undercutting every scene with an incisive humor that makes you laugh out loud. Whether she’s flying by private jet or printing notecards for the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, Movsesian remains blisteringly honest, relatable and altogether lovable.

title: Honey & Spice
author: Bolu Babalola
description: Dear reader, I loved it. Bolu Babalola’s aptly titled Honey & Spice is a smartly observed mashup of sweetness and fire – Beyoncé meets Jane Austen on a London campus. Kiki is an influential college DJ spinning hip-hop-inflected feminist insight. Malakai is a filmmaker and the slick new guy in town. Despite sparks, she very publicly writes him off as a player. Then boom: An involuntary collaboration breaks through the static, and rivals become more. Babalola first made her mark with the myth-inspired story collection Love in Color (also an NPR favorite). With this swoony coming-of-age story, she cements her status as “romcomoisseur” and chronicler of Black British life.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Music", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Shutter: A Novel
author: Ramona Emerson
description: As a thriller, Shutter has a killer premise. What if victims could help solve their own murders? When she was growing up, seeing and hearing dead people made Rita Todacheene an outcast from her Navajo community. But in her life as a forensic photographer, these gifts enable her to capture things that others overlook. The scenario turns ugly when a dead woman delivers an ultimatum: Find her killers, or she’ll wreak unholy havoc. Split between Rita’s past and present, this mystery is elevated by the depth of the characterization and expressive, propulsive writing. This combination earned Ramona Emerson a spot on the National Book Awards longlist.
tags: ["Dark", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Americana", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Winter Work: A Novel
author: Dan Fesperman
description: What happens to spies when the world shakes? As a coda to the Cold War, the fall of the Berlin Wall signaled staggering geopolitical and social shifts. It also put the notorious East German spy apparatus known as the Stasi out of business, leaving a trove of sensitive secrets up for grabs and impacting the lives of millions. This political thriller-murder mystery beautifully intertwines the story of a crumbling nation and the people caught in its wake. At the center is an unusual yet sympathetic leading man, a disillusioned former spy with a complicated home life and a former boss who is found dead in the woods.

title: Lady Justice: Women, the Law, and the Battle to Save America
author: Dahlia Lithwick
description: Slate senior correspondent Dahlia Lithwick’s chronicle of the Trump years is about the best kind of defiance. In late 2016, when most progressives were still shellshocked over Donald Trump’s triumph, a diverse group of women attorneys took action to safeguard democracy and protect the vulnerable. They included now-legendary figures like Sally Yates, the acting U.S. attorney general who refused to sign off on the Muslim travel ban, and Georgia voting rights advocate and gubernatorial candidate Stacey Abrams, as well as other lesser-known figures. The Supreme Court’s Dobbs decision, which ended federal protection for abortion, only magnifies the significance of their fight.

title: Jollof Rice and Other Revolutions: A Novel in Interlocking Stories
author: Omolola Ijeoma Ogunyemi
description: Told in interlocking stories, this bittersweet debut novel follows four girls who meet in a punishing Nigerian boarding school. The paths of Remi, Nonso, Aisha and Solape take radically different directions, but their friendship is an anchor. Omolola Ijeoma Ogunyemi charts their roots and trajectories across decades and continents: from West Africa in the late 1800s and the 1980s to Europe, North America and back, deep into the 21st century. Through a series of individual stories, we gain insight into the ebb and flow of relationships and the social and political landscapes they inhabit.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Short Stories & Essays", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: In the Mouth of the Wolf: A Murder, a Cover-Up, and the True Cost of Silencing the Press
author: Katherine Corcoran
description: This deeply reported and riveting account of the murder of Mexican journalist Regina Martinez – who wrote about corruption and organized crime – is an epic and essential investigation. Some may use Katherine Corcoran’s findings to validate a claim of Mexico’s broken society. But In the Mouth of the Wolf paradoxically reveals the best of Mexico – the courage of Mexican journalists determined to uphold reportorial integrity, the resilience of citizens determined to find justice where the rule of law is itself hostage, and the love of family and country that has unified the Mexican people for generations.

title: Diary of a Void: A Novel
author: Emi Yagi, translated by David Boyd and Lucy North
description: On its surface, Diary of a Void is a placid book. It meanders through the details of a seemingly mundane life with its endless flow of coffee cups and trips to the gym. But at heart, Emi Yagi’s narrative – about a 34-year-old office worker in Tokyo whose life changes radically for the better when she pretends to be pregnant – is explosive. There’s something anarchic about the gentle way in which Yagi so seamlessly blends the boring with the beautiful. I binge-read this book in two days and am hungry for more from this debut novelist.

title: The Angel of Rome: And Other Stories
author: Jess Walter
description: As good as Jess Walter’s novels are (2012’s Beautiful Ruins spent 69 weeks on The New York Times bestseller list), I think his short stories are even better. What I love most is his ability to blend laugh-out-loud humor with throat-catching heart. The characters in this collection – from the middle school teacher with a crush on his worst student’s mom, to the climate professors vying for a job during a freak Mississippi snowstorm, to the insecure college kid adrift in Rome in the title story – will surprise you, break your heart and ultimately leave you with a sense of hope for humanity.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Dear Denise: Letters to the Sister I Never Knew
author: Lisa McNair
description: Lisa McNair recounts growing up in newly integrated Birmingham, Ala., after the notorious 1963 Ku Klux Klan church bombing that killed her sister Denise and three other Black girls. Lisa was born a year later, and her memoir imagines what it would have been like to have had a relationship with her big sister. The book is a series of letters to Denise describing how the McNair family endured, the ongoing fight against racism and Lisa’s own struggle to find her identity in a country changed by her family’s tragedy. This intimate historical reflection proves to be a cautionary tale about racial violence.

title: The Confessions of Matthew Strong: A Novel
author: Ousmane K. Power-Greene
description: This novel is a terrifying page turner. Back home in Alabama for her grandmother’s funeral, philosophy professor Allie Douglass is kidnapped by diabolical white supremacist and Christian nationalist Matthew Strong. Strong is abducting Black girls and women as part of a delusional plot to turn society back to the antebellum South. And he has the help of local law officers and powerful state players. The racial violence in the story is raw and unsettling, but it underscores the message here – that radical ideology is an enemy to be taken seriously.

title: By Hands Now Known: Jim Crow's Legal Executioners
author: Margaret A. Burnham
description: The detailed accounts of racial terror in this book are hard to stomach, but necessary to understand the national legacy of slavery and the Jim Crow system that emerged after emancipation. Story after story reveals how white authorities used violence, fear, kidnapping and murder to perpetuate white supremacy and deny the basic human dignity of Black people. Margaret Burnham’s rich historical analysis documents the longstanding failure of federal laws and institutions to prevent racial violence and police brutality. The book also shines a light on the resourcefulness of African Americans who organized to help one another and fight for justice.

title: Reputation: A Novel
author: Sarah Vaughan
description: Reputation is half psychological thriller, half courtroom drama – think Law & Order: UK but with a constant sense of impending peril. Like her previous book, Anatomy of a Scandal, Sarah Vaughn’s latest is set in the world of British politics. Emma Webster is a member of Parliament with a rising profile. After a scandal involving her daughter erupts, the journalist who broke the story (with whom Emma had a one-night stand) is found dead in her apartment. The subsequent murder trial raises questions about reputations: what they mean to men versus women; what we’ll do to protect them; who we’d be willing to sacrifice them for.

title: I'm Not Broken: A Memoir
author: Jesse Leon
description: Jesse Leon’s memoir about being a “poor, sexually abused, drug-addicted Chicano kid” is at once brutal and full of hope. I’m Not Broken is the kind of narrative that vividly brings to the page the realities of someone ignored by the system and trapped by the streets, but who still manages to make it out and build a new life. As devastating as it is inspiring, this memoir delves deep into the importance of education, growth and helping others.

title: The Passenger
author: Cormac McCarthy
description: One of the best living writers is back after 16 years with two intertwined novels about troubled siblings who love each other in an improper way. The Passenger follows a salvage diver who’s haunted by both his past and his sister as he gets involved in a bizarre case of a missing body. Stella Maris, the second novel, tells the sister’s story, but entirely through her conversations with a doctor at a mental institution. The two narratives are dense, immersive and unique, and they inhabit a bizarre interstitial space between thrillers, literary fiction, love stories, philosophy, quantum mechanics and that particular brand of weird darkness that Cormac McCarthy is known for.

title: Stella Maris
author: Cormac McCarthy
description: One of the best living writers is back after 16 years with two intertwined novels about troubled siblings who love each other in an improper way. The Passenger follows a salvage diver who’s haunted by both his past and his sister as he gets involved in a bizarre case of a missing body. Stella Maris, the second novel, tells the sister’s story, but entirely through her conversations with a doctor at a mental institution. The two narratives are dense, immersive and unique, and they inhabit a bizarre interstitial space between thrillers, literary fiction, love stories, philosophy, quantum mechanics and that particular brand of weird darkness that Cormac McCarthy is known for.

title: Lute
author: Jennifer Thorne
description: Would you accept a calm life full of health and happiness if you knew that every seven years people would die because of it? That’s the question at the core of Lute, and the story’s characters answer with a resounding yes. Every seven years, the inhabitants of a remote English island pay a tribute of seven lives in exchange for seven more years of health and safety. A creepy, atmospheric tale of familial drama, bizarre history and supernatural events, this entertaining story explores the nature of sacrifices and the way we sometimes become prisoners of our decisions.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Mysteries", "Thrillers"]

title: Anywhere You Run: A Novel
author: Wanda M. Morris
description: In 1964 (and still true today), the color of your skin played a major role in the kind of “justice” you received. In Anywhere You Run, Wanda M. Morris drops readers into the racial turmoil of early 1960s Mississippi to follow the story of two sisters forced to run away from everything they know. One runs because of an unwanted pregnancy and the stigma associated with it; the other because she knows she’ll be blamed for a crime and understands that not being guilty of anything matters less than being Black and having others think you are guilty of something.

title: We Spread: A Novel
author: Iain Reid
description: Creepy and unnerving, Iain Reid’s We Spread reads like two novels fused together by some mad scientist. One half of this novel is a tale about getting older, loneliness, the growing importance of memories as we age and exploration of art. The other half is a narrative about an assisted living residence run by a strange woman, losing track of time, being a powerless prisoner and a strange fungus that grows on people. The wild mix works well in Reid’s capable hands, and the result is a fast, profoundly unsettling novel that leaves readers with more questions than answers.

title: Cruz: A Novel
author: Nicolás Ferraro, translated by Mallory N. Craig-Kuhn
description: A slice of Argentine noir brilliantly translated from the Spanish by Mallory N. Craig-Kuhn, Cruz is a grimy, fast, violent narrative about debts, loyalty, bad decisions and two brothers who follow in their father’s criminal footsteps. With one brother in prison, the other takes on a difficult smuggling job for a drug lord to pay his brother’s debt – and fails. With his entire family in danger, he realizes the only way to keep violence at bay is more violence. Awash in drugs, cartels and guns, this propulsive read is not to be missed by fans of gritty crime fiction.

title: The Other Side of Night: A Novel
author: Adam Hamdy
description: The phrase “a series of mysterious deaths” is so overused in crime and horror fiction that it’s often enough to push readers away. The Other Side of Night actually delivers on the darkness that this phrase promises, and it does so while elegantly blending the lives of three characters: David, who is writing about the turmoil in his life; Harriet, a police officer who is working on clearing her name; and Ben, a mysterious man who enters Harriet’s life – and who also might be a murderer. This carefully constructed tense yarn is full of doubts, beautiful passages and as many twists as a mountain road.

title: Men in Blazers Present Gods of Soccer: The Pantheon of the 100 Greatest Soccer Players (According to Us)
author: Roger Bennett, Michael Davies and Miranda Davis, illustrated by Nate Kitch
description: Whether you’re a super fan or someone who just enjoys the World Cup every four years, Gods of Soccer is a fun and highly opinionated compendium of stories about the sport’s all-time great players. The hilarious soccer-obsessed commentators of the Men in Blazers podcast and TV show run down early stars like Brazilian legend Pelé to the women making a splash right now like Megan Rapinoe. The entries are accompanied by vibrant photo illustrations of each superstar. My favorites are of France’s speedy goal scorer Thierry Henry and U.S. shot stopper Briana Scurry. Gods of Soccer would definitely look nice on any coffee table.

title: Which Side Are You On: A Novel
author: Ryan Lee Wong
description: A stunning debut novel from Ryan Lee Wong, Which Side Are You On follows a young Korean American activist who grapples with police brutality and racism. Through intimate conversations with his mother, who was once the leader of a Korean-Black coalition in Los Angeles, Reed seeks new ways of healing, to understand and act on his outrage. It’s an intense read – funny, too – but you still may close the book in anger. It will leave you with a lot to process, from your feelings to the Korean American and Los Angeles history it touches on.

title: Adam Smith’s America: How a Scottish Philosopher Became an Icon of American Capitalism
author: Glory M. Liu
description: Once and for all, let’s bury the myth of Adam Smith. The Scottish economist who first wrote of “the invisible hand” was no free market fundamentalist. In fact, Smith also authored some of the keenest critiques of capitalism. How did we so badly garble his legacy? In Adam Smith’s America, Glory M. Liu reveals the American mythmaking machine at work. She traces the brazen misreadings that flattened this Enlightenment intellectual into a neoliberal folk hero. It’s a history of a fantasy – a parable about America’s talents for inventing heroes and dodging inconvenient truths.

title: The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida
author: Shehan Karunatilaka
description: Set in 1990 Colombo, Sri Lanka, Shehan Karunatilaka’s Booker Prize-winning novel is everything all at once: magical realism, satire, political fiction and thriller. It’s also bold storytelling from a Sri Lankan author for how it includes everything – colonialism, ethnic identity, politics, religion – that factored into the country’s civil war and its aftermath. Shady and brash photojournalist Maali Almeida is dead and in a limbo state. He has seven moons to deal with unfinished business before he can move on. He (re)encounters mythical and folkloric characters, war victims, friends and enemies. Karunatilaka’s irreverent humor keeps the pace breezy and the mood whimsical, but this novel holds nothing back.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Historical Fiction"]

title: The Book of Everlasting Things: A Novel
author: Aanchal Malhotra
description: Aanchal Malhotra’s groundbreaking oral histories of the India-Pakistan Partition have already filled two door-stopper nonfiction books and won her much acclaim. With this first novel, she shapes some of her monumental research material into a far-reaching love story about two people who practice the ancient arts of Indian perfumery and Urdu calligraphy. Malhotra’s detailed world-building gives the narrative sensory layers and textured depths. Moving from the early 1900s to the present day, she explores how Partition continues to be a living, breathing catastrophe and how the violence and trauma have been inherited by later generations, no matter where they live now.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters", "Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance"]

title: Roses, in the Mouth of a Lion: A Novel
author: Bushra Rehman
description: With her second novel, Bushra Rehman returns to the time, setting and protagonist of her first: 1980s Corona, Queens, in New York City, and Razia Mirza, a Pakistani American girl. As before, this narrative is structured as fragmentary vignettes. Razia’s growing pains with her cultural heritage, Muslim faith, parental pressures, queer sexuality, and more lead to self-discoveries that help her reshape complex family and friendship ties. These pages are filled with plenty of 1980s Pakistani and American markers – music, movies, books, clothing – to evoke a singular worldview of the Pakistani immigrant community of that time and place.

title: When We Were Sisters: A Novel
author: Fatimah Asghar
description: This first novel by Fatimah Asghar – one of the finest poets in the South Asian American diaspora – tells the story of three orphaned immigrant sisters and is narrated in the lyrical voice of the youngest, Kausar. Asghar pulls no punches: In addition to dealing with grief over their parents’ deaths and neglect by their guardian, the girls also navigate cultural dissonance, sexuality, Muslim Americanness, physical and sexual abuse, racism and more. Rendered in fragments and collages, Asghar’s narrative is an ever-shifting kaleidoscope, revealing how each sister works to find her individual place in a hostile, indifferent world. A heartbreaking and elegantly told story.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: Meet Us by the Roaring Sea: A Novel
author: Akil Kumarasamy
description: What happens when you braid a dystopic future and a conflict-ridden past together and then let it unfurl across multiple continents? You get Akil Kumarasamy’s second book. Exploring memory and language, reality and imagination, loss and grief, climate change and personal privacy, agency and accountability, this novel demands the kind of close reading that its unnamed protagonist, a Tamil-to-English translator, does with the historical text she’s translating. Employing the uncommon second-person and first-person plural points of view, Kumarasamy complicates our sense of intimacy and distance with her characters. An ambitious scope, but such capaciousness also allows for dynamic characters, ideas, histories and possibilities.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing", "Identity & Culture", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Walking the Bowl: A True Story of Murder and Survival Among the Street Children of Lusaka
author: Chris Lockhart and Daniel Mulilo Chama
description: This extraordinary book may be the most honest portrayal of street children ever written. It took years of research and the help of formerly homeless children to penetrate the culture of the streets in Lusaka, Zambia. It reads like a thriller, beginning with the body of a boy discovered at the city dump. As we travel into a dystopian world where adults play almost no role, we meet a cast of kids – from the scavenger Lusabilo to Kapula, who worked in a brothel to survive – who maintain their humanity despite immense cruelty. Ultimately, this is a tale about how small acts of kindness can transform lives.

title: The Marriage Portrait: A Novel
author: Maggie O'Farrell
description: This is one of those books you finish, then immediately reread for sheer pleasure. Maggie O’Farrell’s magnificent novel The Marriage Portrait is set during the Italian Renaissance. After Lucrezia de’ Medici marries Alfonso II, duke of Ferrara and real-life inspiration for Robert Browning’s poem “My Last Duchess,” she begins to think her life is in danger. Is it? The author’s historical note tells us yes, definitely. I cannot emphasize enough how gorgeous this novel is: how sharp its characterizations, how skillfully O’Farrell makes us question what we’re told will happen, how masterfully Lucrezia is brought to life.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Historical Fiction", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Wild Hunt: A Novel
author: Emma Seckel
description: Emma Seckel’s novel takes place after World War II on a Scottish island where the sluagh, lost souls in the shape of crows, gather each October. Down-on-her-luck Leigh returns to the island for her father’s funeral to find that, like so much else, the sluagh have changed after the war. They’ve become unpredictable, more dangerous, and when a boy goes missing, Leigh delves into the island’s secrets. This debut novel is haunting; it deals in the liminal, asking questions about homecoming, perception, communal grief, memory – and hope. The Wild Hunt is perfect for a wuthery, gray day.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction"]

title: High Times in the Low Parliament
author: Kelly Robson
description: High Times in the Low Parliament by Kelly Robson is witty, warmhearted, frequently gorgeous and an awful lot of fun. Robson’s alternate 18th century England (“Angland”) sings. When a favor goes awry, Lana – scribe, flirt and “family despair” – is sent to work in the fairy-policed Low Parliament. Good news is Lana might have a shot at friendship and romance. Bad news: Parliament’s hung. If the politicians can’t come to an agreement, fairies will drown Parliament and everybody inside. High Times in the Low Parliament is an enchanting comedy of law and clever Lana, a character I’d love to have a drink with.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Booth: A Novel
author: Karen Joy Fowler
description: How did the spawn of an abolitionist, vegetarian father become the rabid secessionist who assassinated Abraham Lincoln? That question underpins this gripping novel, but Karen Joy Fowler also centers John Wilkes Booth’s family members: among them, father Junius, a wildly famous Shakespearean actor as well as a manic drunk, and sister Rosalie, retrieved from obscurity and given a story of her own. Booth is historical fiction, but it’s impossible to ignore the resonance with present-day America. In her author’s note, Fowler writes: “The more I read of Lincoln’s warnings concerning the tyrant and the mob … the more brightly lit the road from there to here became.”

title: Faith, Hope and Carnage
author: Nick Cave and Seán O’Hagan
description: Adapted from a series of conversations between post-punk legend Nick Cave and journalist Seán O’Hagan, this book sees the Australian rocker open up about music, art, belief and the loss of his teenage son Arthur, who died in 2015 after falling from a cliff. It’s quite dark in parts, of course, but beautiful throughout; Cave is one of music’s most brilliant thinkers. Anyone who has ever been moved by art – or lost a loved one – will find a lot to admire in this powerful book.

title: Runaway: Notes on the Myths That Made Me
author: Erin Keane
description: How much do we really know about where we came from, and how we are affected by the popular culture that we’re exposed to? Salon Editor-in-Chief Erin Keane explores those questions in her fascinating memoir about her mother, who ran away from home as a teenager. It’s a compelling story enriched by Keane’s original takes on everything from Gilmore Girls to Return of the Jedi. Memoirs this smart and absorbing don’t come around all the time.

title: Maybe We'll Make It: A Memoir
author: Margo Price
description: If you haven’t heard the songs of Margo Price, you’re missing out on one of country music’s brightest stars. Her new memoir, which covers her childhood and her years struggling to start her career in Nashville, proves that she’s just as talented at penning prose as she is at writing songs – which is to say, really damn good. Reading this book feels like hanging out with one of your smartest, liveliest friends. Check it out along with her album That’s How Rumors Get Started, and thank me later.

title: The Book of Goose: A Novel
author: Yiyun Li
description: There’s no American author more consistently surprising than Yiyun Li. One of the country’s most brilliant novelists, Li seems incapable of repeating herself. Her latest book follows a pair of poverty-stricken teenage girls in post-World War II France who decide to pull off a literary hoax; the results come as a surprise not just to them, but to the reader. Witty, dark and beautifully written, this is a great place to start if you’ve never read Li before. (Honestly, though, there’s no bad place to start.)
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Aesthetica: A Novel
author: Allie Rowbottom
