description: This debut novel follows a 35-year-old woman undergoing a surgery to reverse the plastic surgeries she underwent while she was a teenage Instagram influencer living under the thrall of an abusive manager and lover. Allie Rowbottom’s book is as dark as it sounds, but it’s also vital, written with real anger and compassion. One of the best American novels so far about social media, it’s a chilling look at the state of today’s world – both real and virtual, although those boundaries keep blurring.

title: Dinosaurs: A Novel
author: Lydia Millet
description: Here’s a unicorn: a literary novel that addresses urgent social issues – climate change, economic inequality, hatred – that won’t make you give up what hope you have left. Lydia Millet’s beautiful novel follows an unhappy oil heir who moves from New York to Phoenix, befriends the family living next door and tries to figure out how to do good in the world. Millet is no Pollyanna; she knows how urgent our present problems are. But this novel emphasizes hope, making it a welcome tonic for our … interesting times.

title: Dr. No: A Novel
author: Percival Everett
description: OK, stay with us. The latest novel from the always brilliant Percival Everett is about a Black professor who studies the concept of nothing and is recruited by a wannabe James Bond villain to rob Fort Knox, which contains not gold, but nothing. But the nothing is actually something. Kind of. This book is (obviously) hard to explain, but at its heart, it’s a sharp-witted satire about racism, violence and academia – and it proves why Everett is one of the most unpredictable and original novelists working today.

title: The Grimkes: The Legacy of Slavery in an American Family
author: Kerri K. Greenidge
description: Sisters Sarah and Angelina Grimke were two of America’s most well-known abolitionists, inspired to speak out against slavery by their Quaker faith. But the story of their family goes even deeper – their brother was a cruel sadist who fathered three children with an enslaved woman. Historian Kerri K. Greenidge digs deep into the history of the family, both its white and Black members, and the result is a fascinating examination of the legacy of slavery in America. This beautifully written book isn’t just important; it’s actually essential.

title: Singer Distance: A Novel
author: Ethan Chatagnier
description: Is there life on Mars? David Bowie never answered the question one way or the other, but the characters in Ethan Chatagnier’s entrancing debut novel have no doubt. The book follows a group of graduate students seeking to make contact with the red planet; when one disappears after the project, her boyfriend goes in search of her. Chatagnier writes with a real compassion and sensitivity — this is a love story at its heart, one that’s both beautiful and unforgettable.

title: Sinking Bell: Stories
author: Bojan Louis
description: This debut short-story collection from Diné writer Bojan Louis is slim in length, but it packs a serious emotional punch. The stories collected here, which take place in and around Flagstaff, Ariz., deal with working-class characters who long for lives free of poverty, addiction and racism; their hopes and desires often go unmet. Louis’ writing is unfailingly gorgeous, and his characters unforgettable. This is a collection filled with compassion and a gritty, sharp-edged beauty.

title: The Consequences: Stories
author: Manuel Muñoz
description: It’s hard to imagine a more gorgeous collection of short fiction than the latest book from Arizona-based author Manuel Muñoz. The stories here, set in California’s Central Valley, focus on the lives of immigrants, queer men and others in the 1970s and ‘80s; an underlying theme is the obligations that we have toward our friends and families. Muñoz’s prose is shining and hypnotic, and suffused with care and tenderness. This is one of the best short story collections to come around in recent years.

title: Reward System: Stories
author: Jem Calder
description: After the first coronavirus lockdown, a lot of us developed love-hate relationships with our smartphones. That’s the case for many of the characters in Jem Calder’s excellent debut book, a collection of short stories about millennials whose lives have been reduced to bad jobs, uncertain identities and constantly checking their apps. Calder can be funny and wrenching, often on the same page; he does a wonderful job depicting millennial anomie and rootlessness. This book is beyond promising – but maybe don’t read it on your smartphone.

title: Rogues: True Stories of Grifters, Killers, Rebels and Crooks
author: Patrick Radden Keefe
description: Patrick Radden Keefe’s last two books were among my favorite peak-pandemic reads, and Rogues did not disappoint. It’s a collection of a dozen articles Keefe wrote over the years, focused on a cast of shady, scandalous, sympathetic and generally complicated characters in fields ranging from finance to entertainment to vintage wines. Some of the stories and subjects are better known than others, but all make for page-turners in their own way. I love that Keefe closes each chapter with a short epilogue describing what has happened since each story was originally published – linking past to present and reportage to reality.

title: You Gotta Be You: How to Embrace This Messy Life and Step Into Who You Really Are
author: Brandon Kyle Goodman
description: What if fitting in wasn’t worth the energy, the pain? Who would you be if you could shove off other people’s expectations? Brandon Kyle Goodman, who is Black, queer and nonbinary, has done a lot of work trying to figure that out for themself, and they’re sharing all they’ve learned – the highs, the lows, the mess – in this book. How can we show up better to love others, while respecting our own boundaries so we can better love ourselves? We laughed, we cried, we cry-laughed — and by the end, felt better about bringing our full selves to everything we do.

title: Imagine a City: A Pilot's Journey Across the Urban World
author: Mark Vanhoenacker
description: Part travelogue, part memoir, Mark Vanhoenacker’s latest book takes you on a guided tour of far-flung destinations like Nairobi, Brasília and Kuwait City, while never losing sight of his hometown, Pittsfield, Mass. Growing up, Vanhoenacker built model airplanes, and while studying a globe and sketching imaginary skylines, he dreamed of the places those planes might carry him. Today, he pilots real jetliners on long-distance routes linking continents. While his earlier book Skyfaring described the exhilaration of flying, this book is about what Vanhoenacker finds when he lands: cities rich in poetry, birdsong and stories.

title: The Last White Man: A Novel
author: Mohsin Hamid
description: Anders wakes up one day to find he no longer recognizes himself. His white skin has turned brown. Soon, he’s not alone. The Last White Man’s opening chapter suits a novel that grew from a Kafkaesque era of pandemic and protest. The device feels familiar to readers of author Mohsin Hamid’s Exit West. The laws of reality mutate. What was once surreal becomes normal, but not before exposing the societal fissures that many felt, but not all could see. Some react with suspicion, others with tenderness. The Last White Man insists: When you feel the earth shift, who will you offer kindness to?
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Bronze Drum: A Novel of Sisters and War
author: Phong Nguyen
description: Thrillingly cinematic, Bronze Drum recounts the legend of Trung Trac and Trung Nhi, two sisters barely out of their teens who liberated Vietnam from colonial rule in A.D. 40. Combining meticulous historical research with emotional immediacy, Phong Nguyen vividly evokes the cultural chasm between Chinese and Vietnamese worldviews, in which the former’s repressive patriarchal system directly conflicts with the natives’ matriarchal model, where women can inherit property, have multiple romantic partners and form flexible family arrangements. As syncopated weapon and storytelling instrument, the bronze drum in the novel symbolizes Vietnam’s unyielding spirit and rich cultural heritage.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Fortunes of Jaded Women: A Novel
author: Carolyn Huynh
description: The Fortunes of Jaded Women is a multigenerational saga about Vietnamese women cursed to give birth only to female offspring who in turn cannot find true love. These jade-adorned, loungewear matriarchs regularly consult gnomic herbalists and fortunetellers to revise their daughters’ errant fates. Other life forms in Southern California’s Little Saigon include smooth-talking Kevin Nguyens, bikini-clad baristas and nail salon workers who are employed illegally and well-versed in post-Marxist arguments. Despite its satiric content, the novel poignantly addresses the after-effects of war and diaspora, showing how the characters’ bulldozing quest for stability manifests a precarious hold on their adopted land.

title: Even Though I Knew the End
author: C.L. Polk
description: A decade ago, magical detective Elena made a deal with the devil, trading her soul for a gift. Now in 1940s Chicago, her side of the bargain is coming due. Luckily, a client offers her a way out – by finding the White City Vampire who is leaving the remains of human sacrifices all over town. Elena has three days to solve the case so she can secure her soul and spend the rest of her life with her beloved, Edith. This is an entrancing mystery novella of queer romance, hard-boiled detectives and Faustian bargains.

title: Just Like Home
author: Sarah Gailey
description: I love a good, old-fashioned haunted house story, and this one is chilling and very unsettling. With her mother’s impending death looming over her, Vera finally returns to her childhood home. That house was a source of pain and darkness for many, but for Vera her father was the light of her life. When he’s tossed in jail for being a serial killer, everything falls apart. Now Vera is grown, and something bloody and terrible is waiting for her back home. Evil lurks, but it’s not what Vera is expecting.

title: Babel, or the Necessity of Violence: An Arcane History of the Oxford Translators' Revolution
author: R.F. Kuang
description: This new doorstopper of a historical fantasy novel reimagines the rise of the British empire as intrinsically intertwined with the colonization and consolidation of magic. Robin, stolen from his home in Canton and brought to Oxford’s Royal Institute of Translation in the mid-19th century, finds himself at the center of a plot to bring down the imperial machine. Although the stories are very different, Babel gave me serious Jonathan Strange and Mr Norrell vibes, from the fascinating footnotes to the lush prose to the creeping sensation that there is far more going on than what appears on the surface.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "History", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Tread of Angels
author: Rebecca Roanhorse
description: Looking for a little mystery with a weird-west genre hook? Look no further! In a world of mechanical wonders powered by the divine magic of a dead angel live sisters Celeste and Mariel, descendents of the Fallen angels. Celeste will do anything to save Mariel from execution after she’s accused of killing one of the righteous Virtues – including making deals with demons, scamming her fellow Fallen, and trying to outsmart the enemy Virtues.

title: Africa Risen: A New Era of Speculative Fiction
author: edited by Sheree Renée Thomas, Oghenechovwe Donald Ekpeki, Zelda Knight
description: This was one of my most anticipated 2022 anthologies, and for good reason. The stories range all over the speculative realm, from past to present to future, from the continent to the diaspora, from authors with bibliographies a mile long to those who are making a name for themselves. With nearly three dozen writers represented, there’s a little something for everyone here. If you like short stories, are looking for an entry point to short speculative fiction, or need something to break a reading slump, you can’t go wrong with Africa Risen.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Before I Let Go
author: Kennedy Ryan
description: USA Today bestselling romance author Kennedy Ryan delivers another scorching tale of love, desire, heartbreak and heartache in her new second-chance romance Before I Let Go.  Ryan is a fantastic storyteller and superb writer who expertly explores complex and realistic situations from depression and divorce to success and failure – to the reincarnation of love. With her witty, insightful dialogue and a cast of remarkable, believable characters, Ryan’s immersive storytelling leaves no corner of the heart untouched. You will be hard-pressed to let go of Yasmen, Josiah, Deja, Kassim and Otis, to name a few of her unforgettable characters.

title: Killers of a Certain Age
author: Deanna Raybourn
description: New York Times bestselling author Deanna Raybourn turns in a delightful, edge-of-your-seat action-adventure-thriller in Killers of a Certain Age. A retirement vacation turns into a fight for survival when four 60-year-old female assassins realize they are being targeted by the Museum, the organization they’ve spent 40 years working for. This is Raybourn’s first contemporary novel, and she knocks it out of the park with excellent storytelling, relatable characters and fun (and believable) dialogue in a highly effective thriller. Billie, Mary Alice, Helen and Natalie are fully developed characters who are physical, smart and excellent at what they do. This is a ride you don’t want to miss.

title: The Last Dreamwalker
author: Rita Woods
description: This stunning novel by the Hurston/Wright Legacy Award-winning author Rita Woods tells the tale of Layla Hurley and her connection to the Sea Islands off the coast of South Carolina. The islands are the home of the Gullah/Geechee people, who have retained the ethnic traditions of West Africa since the mid-1700s. Layla is a complicated, emotional protagonist who learns she has a unique gift, passed from generation to generation, following her mother’s death. The book contains imaginative mythology, exquisite prose and wonderfully paced storytelling. It’s a beautifully written, gripping contemporary story and a must-read work of fiction.
tags: ["Family Matters", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: The Monsters We Defy
author: Leslye Penelope
description: Leslye Penelope’s latest is a genre-bending mashup that delights as much as it intrigues. A fantasy novel set in 1925 Washington, D.C., the book features a girl who can talk to spirits, a heist, an awesome crew of magical misfits and a heart-pounding romance. All this and more in a well-researched journey through African American culture in the nation’s capital in the 1920s. Clara Johnson, the novel’s protagonist, is as “ornery as a red hornet,” but beneath the hard-boiled exterior is a determined young woman – and one of the most inventive characters in fantasy.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "History", "Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Americana"]

title: Lessons From the Edge: A Memoir
author: Marie Yovanovitch
description: Marie Yovanovitch, the former U.S. ambassador to Ukraine, was vilified by then-President Donald Trump and chose to fight when her reputation was questioned. But this book is about much more than the political headlines. Her 33-year career in the Foreign Service was a testament to good judgment and initiative, proving that values and interests equal the diplomatic equation. This is a real-life textbook of a career under pressure, even in low-profile assignments that take unexpected twists and turns.

title: The Violin Conspiracy: A Novel
author: Brendan Slocumb
description: Ray McMillian is practicing for the Tchaikovsky competition in Russia when his family violin is stolen from his New York hotel room and replaced with a new sneaker and a ransom note. The whodunit becomes a tour through the pressure of competition and family feuds and an exposé on the prejudice faced by Black performers. This is a fast-paced, crisp read infused with a love of music, determination and perseverance.

title: River of the Gods: Genius, Courage, and Betrayal in the Search for the Source of the Nile
author: Candice Millard
description: The 19th-century race to definitively establish the source of the Nile was full of adventure and motivation for great achievement. Richard Burton and John Hanning Speke are not household names today, but their quest for glory makes for an enjoyable read about characters who, while deeply flawed, persevered through personal grudges, disease and dangerous elements in their journey for the source of the Nile. Ultimately, neither could have survived without Sidi Mubarak Bombay, arguably the real hero in the group.

title: Last Call at the Hotel Imperial: The Reporters Who Took On a World at War
author: Deborah Cohen
description: The American foreign correspondent burst on the scene in the 1920s and 1930s, urging Americans to abandon their isolationist preferences for a full-on engagement in world leadership. Four of the highest-profile correspondents, John Gunther, H.R. Knickerbocker, Vincent Sheean and Dorothy Thompson, chased each other across Europe, mixing friendships and professional rivalries as well as advocacy and reporting. This is an insightful look at early pack journalism trying to make sense of the dictators who would bring the U.S. fully into the global chess game.

title: Year of the Tiger: An Activist's Life
author: Alice Wong
description: “Ableism conscripted me into activism,” Alice Wong writes in this collection of funny, fierce, original and precise essays. But every activist is also other things, and Wong explores friendships, community, food and joy, as well as the acrid scent of discrimination. In addition to her own writing, Wong includes interviews, art, recipes and other formats, making the book a mosaic of a thoroughly lived (and living) life rather than a neatly linear narrative. Year of the Tiger invites readers to witness the beauty in imagining and creating a better world for one another.

title: Diary of a Misfit: A Memoir and a Mystery
author: Casey Parks
description: When Casey Parks first learned of Roy Hudgins, a “woman who lived like a man” during her grandmother’s childhood in Louisiana, her interest was piqued. Newly out to her family and recently condemned to hell by their preacher, Parks begins a yearslong scavenger hunt of sorts. Finding all the clues to Roy’s story proves difficult, but in the process, Parks beautifully portrays her interview subjects in the South and what she both loves and finds painful about home. A blend of reportage, research and memoir, Diary of a Misfit is a riveting read.

title: Patricia Wants to Cuddle: A Novel
author: Samantha Allen
description: When the last four contestants on The Catch, a reality TV dating show, arrive on Otters Island, their most pressing concern is surviving the upcoming Elimination Event. But survival takes on a whole new meaning as Renee, Vanessa, Amanda and Lilah-Mae’s clashes become heated, secrets begin to spill and doubts become blaring alarm bells. Plus, there seems to be something in the woods on Otters Island. Will they make it out in love, alive, both or neither? Journalist Samantha Allen, author of Real Queer America, has written a debut novel that’s a hilarious sendup of and love letter to The Bachelor franchise.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance"]

title: On the Rooftop: A Novel
author: Margaret Wilkerson Sexton
description: It’s 1953 in San Francisco’s Fillmore district, and Vivian just knows that her daughters – Ruth, Esther and Chloe – are on the brink of stardom. They’ve been singing together for years, but it’s Vivian who drives the dream. While the sisters love singing together and are the pride and joy of their local community, they have other desires too, as yet unvocalized to their mama. When Ruth drops a bombshell on the family and white men come sniffing around the neighborhood that they’re hoping to clear and “develop,” Vivian’s dream begins to crumble. Margaret Wilkerson Sexton’s newest is a moving, warm and beautifully rendered family portrait.

title: Before All the World: A Novel
author: Moriel Rothman-Zecher
description: “I do not believe that all the world is darkness,” Gittl, a Jewish teenager, thinks one day upon waking from a strange dream. Soon, she is faced with the darkest of darknesses, a pogrom that leaves her and one child, Leyb, the sole survivors of their village. Years later, Leyb, living in Philadelphia, meets Charles, a Black man who speaks Yiddish, at a gay bar. In startling language filled with the flavor of Yiddish’s combination words, Moriel Rothman-Zecher’s book moves forward and back and forward again in a dreamlike trance that acknowledges how the worst suffering exists side by side with the tender beauty of memory, friendship, words and the silences of recognition.

title: Body Language: Writers on Identity, Physicality, and Making Space for Ourselves
author: Nicole Chung and Matt Ortile (editors)
description: The collected essays in Body Language reckon with the many ways we do and do not occupy the silly, unruly, beautiful, strong, disabled, uncomfortable, painful, joyful fleshiness of our humanity. With meditations on race, weight, ability and more, these writers give voice to the complexity of having a body in all its multifaceted needs, desires and disconnects. As a person who dwells in a genetically disordered and chronically pained body, I tend to feel entirely separate from it, a mind trapped in a meat suit, and in these essays I find both kinship and inspiration to strive toward being at peace with my physicality.

title: Dead-End Memories: Stories
author: Banana Yoshimoto, translated by Asa Yoneda
description: Banana Yoshimoto’s 11th book to be translated into English, Dead-End Memories (originally published in 2003) is a collection of stories, each of which, while specific and distinct, has at its center a woman both losing and finding something of herself. In Asa Yoneda’s elegant translation of this collection – whose title story Yoshimoto herself considers her best – the soothing rhythm of the everyday and the mundane is broken by equally quiet moments of profundity. “I know now,” the narrator of the title story tells us, “that just then, under the worst possible conditions, I was in the midst of my greatest happiness.”
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: How to Read Now: Essays
author: Elaine Castillo
description: On the opening page of this collection of essays, Elaine Castillo writes: “it feels like first I became a reader; then I became a person.” It is with this deep, abiding love of reading – books, yes, but also other media – that Castillo explores how we read and what we bring with us to the text. What assumptions, beliefs and status quos have we inherited, and how do they affect our reading practice? What do we embrace, and what should we strip away? How might we face ourselves in the practice of reading as well as embrace the interconnectedness of our stories?

title: Gods of Want: Stories
author: K-Ming Chang
description: K-Ming Chang, author of Bestiary, brings her distinctive style and imagery to her first collection of stories. The 16 stories in the collection explore themes of family, inherited truths and myths, queer desire and the stark and sometimes violent beauty of the natural world. In one story, a woman accompanies her wife to photograph a hurricane, her many dead cousins in tow. In another, the narrator’s endless aunts are listed one after another in a poetic kind of litany. A lifeblood of desire courses through the bodies of the girls and women peopling the stories – a desire to love, lust, live and thrive.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Nomad Century: How Climate Migration Will Reshape Our World
author: Gaia Vince
description: Political leaders almost always frame migration as a threat. In her book, British environmental writer Gaia Vince makes the case that migration might actually be the best way to adapt to a real existential threat: climate change. As rising temperatures make large swaths of the globe uninhabitable, she urges countries to form a global group that would plan mass migration, from ravaged areas to livable ones, and work on geoengineering solutions. She offers a confidence in humankind that it hasn’t earned – consider how nationalists have weaponized migration. But it’s refreshing to read someone asking us to reexamine our prejudices as we consider life- and planet-saving options.

title: Are You Sara? A Novel
author: S.C. Lalli
description: It starts off with a nightmarishly riveting conceit: Two women with the same name accidentally switch cabs and one of them gets murdered at her wrong drop-off location. That’s what happens to Saraswati “Sara” Bhaduri and the rich college student Sarah Ellis after meeting at a bar one night. Now Sarah is gone, the police have no leads, and Sara is paranoid that she was supposed to be the target because of her past. Are You Sara? cogently navigates the duality of identity, what it says about social hierarchy, and the things people will do to survive. Perhaps one of the most striking moments in the book is when Sara wonders if the police would’ve paid her any attention had she gone missing. S.C. Lalli’s debut thriller is punchy and an introspective reminder that money and power always come with dangerous strings attached.

title: You're Invited
author: Amanda Jayatissa
description: You know serious drama is about to go down when an ex-best friend invites you to her wedding to your ex-boyfriend in Sri Lanka. And by drama, I mean the bride goes missing on the day of the wedding and all eyes are on Amaya, the jilted friend who had a falling out with the rest of the family. But in Sri Lanka’s high society, nothing is what it seems, and behind the Louis Vuitton bags and the extravagant saris sits a crowd willing to protect their own even as they throw them to the vultures. Amanda Jayatissa’s atmospheric novel careens toward a sinister ending that has readers questioning everything and reminds us to keep our enemies close – but our friends and family closer.

title: Mademoiselle Revolution
author: Zoe Sivak
description: Zoe Sivak’s debut novel Mademoiselle Revolution truly accomplishes what this author set out to do with her work – “explore famous male figures through the lens of the women beside them.” As a fan of historical fiction, this novel captured and kept my attention as it follows a privileged biracial woman caught in the middle of political and social revolutions in Haiti and France. What I love about this book is the author’s ability to take a well-known event in history and give it a fresh take through the eyes of a fictional female character, who might as well have been a part of these famous men’s real lives.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture"]

title: Acceptance: A Memoir
author: Emi Nietfeld
description: Throughout her traumatic adolescence, author Emi Nietfeld clung to the dream of getting into an Ivy League university as a means of escape. She grew up with a mother who was a hoarder, endured stints in a residential treatment facility and foster care, and was homeless while on breaks from boarding school. She did get into Harvard University and had a job offer in hand from Google upon graduation. But Acceptance isn’t a phoenix-rising-from-the-ashes tale, and for precisely this reason it’s a must-read. Instead, the memoir refuses silver linings and serves as a necessary corrective to the gospel of “grit.”

title: Life's Work
author: David Milch
description: If, like me, you’re a dedicated fan of David Milch’s transformative television – from Hill Street Blues and NYPD Blue to Deadwood and John from Cincinnati – you’ll come to this memoir for its behind-the-scenes stories. But the real gifts of Life’s Work are Milch’s meditations on writing and how to live. Milch, who has Alzheimer’s disease, collaborated on this book with his wife and adult daughters, who helped him piece together his recollections. What results is an exigent reflection on a truly remarkable life, one that holds lessons about humanity and the power of art to make those lessons visible.

title: Stay True: A Memoir
author: Hua Hsu
description: Stay True is an ode to a time and place – UC Berkeley, the mid ‘90s – and to the stuff that forges friendships: mixtapes and zines, late-night talks and long drives. It’s also an ode to a friendship cut short. Ken initially seems to embody the mainstream that Hsu defined himself against. But soon their conversations would shape his worldview – and then Ken was gone, killed in a carjacking. After, Hsu writes, “I became obsessed with the possibility of a sentence that could wend its way backward.” Stay True marks a wrenching, reverent attempt at that sentence.

title: Tell Me I'm an Artist: A Novel
author: Chelsea Martin
description: Joey, a sophomore from a lower-class family, feels out of her depth at art school, where wealth seems necessary for self-realization. As she tries to avoid getting roped into fixing her sister’s and mother’s problems, she procrastinates on an experimental-film assignment – hilariously, she has decided to remake the movie Rushmore without ever having seen it. Told through fragmented scenes interspliced with handwritten notes, Google searches (“how do you know what you like,” “cheap dinner that isn’t beans”) and Rushmore rewrites, Tell Me I’m an Artist perfectly probes the collision of financial insecurity and artistic self-doubt.

title: Somewhere Sisters: A Story of Adoption, Identity, and the Meaning of Family
author: Erika Hayasaki
description: In 1998, identical twin sisters were born in Vietnam, only to be separated soon after. Isabella was adopted by a wealthy white American family, while Hà remained in Vietnam. As teenagers, they reunited. On the surface, this sounds like a fairy tale about long-lost family. But Somewhere Sisters is not a fairy tale – it’s the product of intensive reporting that distills the five years that journalist Erika Hayasaki spent with Isabella, Hà and their families, tracing how they came to live such divergent lives. Crucially, Hayasaki contextualizes their stories in the larger history of transracial and transnational adoption, as well as nature-nurture science, making for a nuanced portrait.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: Strangers to Ourselves: Unsettled Minds and the Stories That Make Us
author: Rachel Aviv
description: For years, I have devoured Rachel Aviv’s nuanced New Yorker features on challenging topics like brain death, the “troubled teen” industry, and the mutability of memory. Her first book is just as gripping and subtle in its exploration of how psychiatric explanations of behavior can both limit and fail to capture who we are. Starting with her own story of being hospitalized for anorexia at age 6, Aviv explores the “psychic hinterlands,” profiling how five others journeyed through “the outer edges of human experience.” This deeply empathetic book raises probing questions about our psychiatric system and the very nature of self-identity.

title: Night of the Living Rez: Stories
author: Morgan Talty
description: Hair frozen in snow. A jar of teeth. A highway carpeted with caterpillars. These are just a handful of the indelible images that Morgan Talty conjures in his debut linked short-story collection set on the Penobscot reservation in Maine. Night of the Living Rez jumps back and forth in time, tracing the decisions that have shaped a boy named David, from scheming to steal root clubs to climbing out a window with his sister’s baby in his arms. Talty’s book haunted and thrilled me in its raw explorations of inheritance, grief and survival, imbued with humor and warmth.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: A Woman's Battles and Transformations
author: Édouard Louis, translated by Tash Aw
description: Current enfant terrible of French letters Édouard Louis is known for exposing the underbelly of France’s undervalued, underserved, struggling working class, as well as what it feels like to be gay outside a major metropolis. Now Louis turns his autofiction to his mother, a woman who has suffered from poor education, lack of financial resources and abusive men. The result is a searing and meaningful exploration of a mother-son relationship, against the challenges and cruelties of men and within France’s rigid class structures.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Mr. B: George Balanchine's 20th Century
author: Jennifer Homans
description: New Yorker dance critic Jennifer Homans, known for her encyclopedic study of ballet titled Apollo’s Angels, has written another indispensable volume for ballet lovers. Her study of the 20th century’s most celebrated and elusive choreographer, George Balanchine, takes readers into fascinating, dusty corners of history – from Russian iconography and its influence on Balanchine to the impact of the violence on St. Petersburg streets during the Russian Revolution, and from Balanchine’s complex and codependent relationship with impresario Lincoln Kirstein to the dancers Balanchine trained and loved and abandoned and worshipped. Homans’ writing is exquisite and her research awe inspiring.

title: Uncommon Measure: A Journey Through Music, Performance, and the Science of Time
author: Natalie Hodges
description: In spare, carefully considered prose, recent Harvard University graduate Natalie Hodges recalls a lifetime of playing the violin – and what a long-term, professional career in music might mean for her. She doesn’t stint on describing the hardships of a musician’s life, including those of one in training. Hodges undertakes a captivating study of the science of time – physics and music – in this very special memoir that defies categorization.

title: The Book Eaters
author: Sunyi Dean
description: Imagine learning Morse code from eating a manual or literally growing up on a diet of Jane Eyre in the Yorkshire moors. Sunyi Dean’s contemporary fantasy debut features extrahuman creatures who consume more books than we could ever attempt to read, yet possess a stunning lack of imagination for stepping outside the pages of their own toxic fairy-tale narrative. Princesses, knights, dragons, weddings – these words lose their meanings when queer mother Devon must protect her monstrous, beloved mind-eater son, Cai, from the intergenerational abuse in their family tree. Unlike her book eaters, Dean pulls from a nigh-infinite well of inspiration.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: Best of Friends
author: Kamila Shamsie
description: Best of Friends follows Zahra and Maryam’s friendship through their teenage years in Karachi and mid-40s in London, from being inseparable in school to recognizing important differences in their politics and ideas of justice later in life. On the face of it, they couldn’t be more different – sensible but impulsive Zahra and privileged but street-smart Maryam – and over time, their shared experiences are no longer enough. The novel explores how lifelong friendships follow different rules: We are tied to our childhood friends, not because they share our values or interests but because they are a part of our history.

title: The Last Folk Hero: The Life and Myth of Bo Jackson
author: Jeff Pearlman
description: Bo Jackson awed fans with the impossible. The sensational, two-sport, baseball and football superstar has a highlights reel packed with moonshot homers, crazy throws, unstoppable touchdown runs and iconic Nike ads. If you grew up in the 1980s and ‘90s, you probably wanted to be like Bo. In The Last Folk Hero, Jeff Pearlman shows us how Vincent Jackson became “Bo” with engrossing reporting that takes us from his early childhood to his athletic apex to that fluke, career-changing injury. Along the way, Pearlman unpacks the real story from the embellishments and myths that seem to grow in each retelling.

title: How to Be Eaten: A Novel
author: Maria Adelmann
description: If not for the title (its innuendo), if not for the cover (its brilliant and naughty heightening of the innuendo), if not for the premise (fairy-tale heroines in group therapy for their traumas), then read it for the question at the heart of the whole thing: Can telling your story free you from reliving your story? Maria Adelmann’s book reads quick and popcorny, like a reality show come to life. But it deepens as it goes, peeking at not just the wolves behind the camera, but those sitting, with popcorn on their laps, in front of the screen.

title: Transforming Space Over Time: Set Design and Visual Storytelling with Broadway’s Legendary Directors
author: Beowulf Boritt
description: Theatrical set designers are both chameleons and problem solvers – depending on the script and budget, their work can be seemingly simple and abstract, or elaborate and highly detailed. Tony Award-winning set designer Beowulf Boritt raises the curtain on the design process in Transforming Space Over Time, detailing his work on six shows, as well as engaging in conversation with directors like James Lapine, Susan Stroman and the late Hal Prince about their long careers, along with the late composer/lyricist Stephen Sondheim. Lavishly illustrated, the book is a fascinating glimpse into a theatrical world that’s seldom explored.

title: Mothercare: On Obligation, Love, Death, and Ambivalence
author: Lynne Tillman
description: “Not everything can be put away,” Lynne Tillman concludes in the opening paragraph of this spare, saturated memoir of her mother’s long decline and its effects on her sisters and herself. What reappears like dust, in Tillman’s telling, is both emotional and practical: the medical industry’s failures, the tensions between professional caregivers and family, the roil of emotions continually exposed as a difficult parent becomes more and more like a problem child. A great formal experimenter in her many other books, Tillman aims here for absolute, unsentimental clarity. She spares no one, including herself, facing death’s trap and the equally inevitable human urge to avoid it. A must-read for anyone with an ailing elder, or really anyone who is mortal.

title: Shine Bright: A Very Personal History of Black Women in Pop
author: Danyel Smith
description: Danyel Smith takes a unique look at some female pop music greats by considering them in moments between the flashbulbs – and interweaving their stories with her own. As the pages recount how Gladys Knight, Aretha Franklin, Janet Jackson and more battled industry double standards, interpersonal strife and even their own demons, Smith gives context and credit where it’s due with a care that Black women’s stories are rarely granted. Along the way, the heralded music and culture journalist shares tricks of the trade (like how to convince a gatekeeping publicist that all she needs is 20 minutes with Whitney Houston) and coming-of-age lessons about love, compromise, failure and success that only music could teach her.

title: Dilla Time: The Life and Afterlife of J Dilla, the Hip-Hop Producer Who Reinvented Rhythm
author: Dan Charnas
description: James DeWitt Yancey – the Detroit producer who, both as Jay Dee and J Dilla, created an irresistibly destabilized pulse in pop music – was not a man who gave up his secrets easily. So Dilla Time, this magnificent book by veteran hip-hop journalist Dan Charnas, feels a bit like a miracle. It’s not just a deeply revealing portrait of Dilla, who died in 2006, or an exploration of the neo-soul and hip-hop movements that coalesced around him. It’s also a lucid study of the new rhythm science he invented, in language more tactile than technical: an approachable head-nod sort of read.

title: Demon Copperhead: A Novel
author: Barbara Kingsolver
description: This reimagining of Charles Dickens’ classic coming-of-age novel David Copperfield swaps Victorian England for southern Appalachia, as the eponymous orphan hurtles through a wasteland of post-coal poverty and opioid addiction in search of love and belonging. If that sounds like a downer, it’s anything but. The miracle of Barbara Kingsolver’s menagerie here, led by the indomitable Demon – and the playfulness of her prose – is that they animate every page with an unrelenting hopefulness.

title: The Rabbit Hutch: A Novel
author: Tess Gunty
description: Tess Gunty’s debut novel, The Rabbit Hutch, takes place in a dying Midwest town that industry left behind and focuses mainly on the inhabitants of an affordable-housing building there. Their lives are varied – there’s the new mother afraid of her newborn’s eyes, the single woman who moderates an obituary website and a group of four teens who have aged out of foster care. Their ways of coping with their lives are also varied: One man rubs himself in glow-stick liquid, while one girl is obsessed with female Catholic saints. The book, which won the National Book Award for fiction, is at times darkly funny, at times absurd and at times heavy, but it is creative and fascinating throughout.

title: Lone Women
author: Victor LaValle
description: Lone Women opens with Adelaide Henry burning down her family home in early 20th century California with her parents’ corpses still inside. She makes her way to the Montana wilderness (with a steamer trunk that she guards fiercely and never unlocks) to begin a new life as a homesteader. It’s a propulsive start to a novel that claims a fast-paced plot – though I found myself gripped just as much by Adelaide’s defiant, glorious stubbornness and guarded wit. Lone Women combines elements of Western fiction, horror and magical realism, while featuring queer, POC characters inventing lives for themselves in the last years of the American frontier. It’s impossible to categorize and impossible to put down.

title: Black River Orchard
author: Chuck Wendig
description: A demon, a farmer, a cat, a teen wannabe influencer, a cheating wife, a dom, a Richie Rich type, and a rare fruit hunter walk into an apple orchard. At 640 pages, this horror novel might feel daunting to some. But it’s so good that, if you’re anything like me, you’ll end up bingeing it over a couple of days. The story crisscrosses through time and characters as author Chuck Wendig shows readers the consequences of power. It’s intense, frightening, and a little bit gross, but also wonderfully diverse. Also: evil apples.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Sordidez
author: E.G. Condé
description: Taínofuturism (futuristic science fiction rooted in Taíno culture and customs with an Indigenous-centric decolonial framework) is likely a new genre term to many readers, but that makes this novella all the more worth diving into. In this compelling near-future story, Vero, a trans journalist from Puerto Rico, ventures into the jungles of the Yucatán to a refuge for the chronically ill run by Doña Margarita. In the shadows, a rebel leader enacts her revenge. Fiercely decolonial, this will appeal to anyone eager to read about resisting an empire and reclaiming a culture.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Secret Lives of Country Gentlemen
author: KJ Charles
description: This romance novel set in Regency-era Kent will make you swoon. Joss Doomsday is a scoundrel to some and a leader to others. His livelihood as a smuggler is put at risk with the arrival of Sir Gareth Inglis. Gareth grew up feeling abandoned and unloved, but now has a baronetcy to manage. Tensions rise and sparks fly. If you’re an audiobook person, Martyn Swain’s narration is a balm for the soul. And if you want more Doomsday romance, the sequel, A Nobleman’s Guide to Seducing a Scoundrel, came out in September.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Witch King
author: Martha Wells
description: Kai, a demon, survives a murder attempt, only to discover his close friend is missing and the new world he helped forge out of the ruins of a brutal empire is on the verge of collapse. With old friends and new allies, Kai takes on powerful mages and past traumas. This motley crew of mages, demons and otherworldly beings crosses oceans and continents, past and present, to save the world and people they love. It’s the kind of novel you want to sink into, with a narrative style that hooks you in and makes it impossible to put down.

title: He Who Drowned the World
author: Shelley Parker-Chan
description: The sequel to 2021’s magnificent historical fantasy She Who Became the Sun continues its reimagining of Zhu Yuanzhang’s rise to power and the founding of the Ming Dynasty. Zhu, the Radiant King, teams up with her former enemy General Ouyang as their goals of toppling the emperor temporarily align. Also scheming for power are the new Prince of Henan, Wang Baoxiang, and the ambitious Madam Zhang. Gender, power, queerness, sex and empire are explored in complex and sometimes shocking ways. This duology will leave you breathless.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Study for Obedience
author: Sarah Bernstein
description: A young woman moves from her remote hometown to take over domestic responsibilities for her brother after his wife leaves. After she arrives, she senses hostility toward newcomers as unusual events begin to unfold among the livestock. Although the plot is a bit of an enigma, the book delves into the lasting impact of trauma stemming from the Holocaust and the power of truths, both spoken and unspoken. Not the easiest read, but worth the work.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: The Adventures of Amina al-Sirafi: A new fantasy series set a thousand years before The City of Brass
author: Shannon Chakraborty
description: Amina al-Sirafi, a once-foul-mouthed, cunning, ambitious seafarer, has retired to live a quiet life with her mother and daughter. Amina is determined to stay out of trouble – that is until the mother of one of her crewmates, who died under mysterious circumstances, seeks her help. She offers Amina one last mission, untold riches and a chance at redemption. But all is not what it seems. I loved everything about this book: Amina and her crew, the way she navigates the world of the jinn and other beings – and how the book centers people who are often erased or vilified in much of the high fantasy I grew up reading.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Time's Echo: The Second World War, the Holocaust, and the Music of Remembrance
author: Jeremy Eichler
description: Culture and memory live on in music. That’s the premise of this deeply absorbing and moving narrative of how four composers – Richard Strauss, Arnold Schoenberg, Dmitri Shostakovich and Benjamin Britten – metabolized the horrors of World War II and the Holocaust and how each man’s own personal circumstances and choices shaped their artistic responses. Jeremy Eichler, the chief classical music critic of The Boston Globe, is a sensitive and keenly perceptive documentarian, and this richly drawn history places each of the four composers into a broad historical narrative.

title: You Could Make This Place Beautiful: A Memoir
author: Maggie Smith
description: In 2016, poet Maggie Smith went viral with her poem “Good Bones.” She wrote: “The world is at least / fifty percent terrible,” noting that “Any decent realtor” would try to sell you a place with good bones with the claim: “You could make this place beautiful.” Smith comes back to that line as the launching point of this lyrical, bittersweet memoir about her divorce – making something beautiful out of a horrible situation. (As her career skyrocketed, Smith says, her marriage crumbled.) If you’re looking for a bitchy tell-all about her marriage fracturing, this isn’t it. Instead, Smith is working through her pain, page by page, in a heartbreaking and exquisitely rendered collection of vignettes, poems and brief musings.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Madonna: A Rebel Life
author: Mary Gabriel
description: The pop megastar Madonna turned 65 this year, not long after she became the first female artist to earn a Billboard Top 10 album across five separate decades. (Five. Decades.) Whether you adore her or despise what she represents, she’s certainly earned a scholarly biography – and it’s finally arrived in the form of Mary Gabriel’s seriously hefty, earnest book. (It’s 858 pages! 858!) If you have a major Madonna fan in your life, this is the perfect deep dive into an endlessly complicated celebrity. Gabriel traverses Madonna’s many public personas, including Material Girl, Lady of the Manor and beatific mother of many, with grace and skill – and reflects on what she means as a pop culture doyenne.

title: Ringmaster: Vince McMahon and the Unmaking of America
author: Abraham Josephine Riesman
description: If you’ve ever seen some attention-hoarding loudmouth spout their nonsense into a microphone and wondered “wait, is this a bit?” then this book is for you. Abraham Josephine Riesman’s biography of WWE mastermind Vince McMahon isn’t just a deep dive into the history of pro wrestling, it’s also an explanation and exploration of what’s dubbed “neokayfabe” – that perfect mixture of half-truths and semi-fictions concocted to exploit our attention economy. And once you see the pattern laid by Riesman out in the world of “sports entertainment,” you can see it all throughout our everyday lives, because ultimately the answer to “is this a bit?” is “yes, but it’s real.”

title: Poverty, by America
author: Matthew Desmond
description: In 2022, the noise rock band Chat Pile put out a song that repeatedly asks “Why? Why do people have to live outside?” In Poverty, by America, sociologist Matthew Desmond plainly delivers an answer: because we benefit from it. Not a vague, squishy, ethereal we, but a more direct we – him, me, and, likely, you. The book is a tightly written, research-backed and uncomfortable argument that those of us lucky enough to live above the poverty line directly profit by keeping the people on the other side beneath it. Or, as Chat Pile puts it, “real American horror story.”

title: Emily Wilde's Encyclopaedia of Faeries
author: Heather Fawcett
description: Emily Wilde – an anti-social, curmudgeonly Cambridge professor – is an expert in the study of faeries. This book is her journal, complete with faerie-lore-filled footnotes, on her northern research journey to study the fae. Her observation takes a turn when she gets far too involved in the world of the fae and begins chronicling her increasingly dangerous adventures, not just her research. Throw in a handsome, and mysterious, academic rival, Wendell Bambleby, who shows up uninvited to join the expedition, and you get an incredibly fun journey through fae lands and dark magic.

title: Chain Gang All Stars
author: Nana Kwame Adjei-Brenyah
description: Chain-Gang All-Stars takes place in the near future, where imprisoned people are made to fight to the death gladiator-style in a multimillion-dollar sporting league with America’s rapt attention and viewership. While the pages are futuristic with hovering cameras following the convicts’ every move, the footnotes cite real facts about imprisonment in America. A dystopian sci-fi with a message about for-profit prisons, the American prison industrial complex, and America’s love of violent sport, this book will have you feeling uncomfortable but also have you racing to the next page.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: Hit Girls: Women of Punk in the USA, 1975-1983
author: Jen B. Larson
description: Every artistic movement has its legends, but in punk many of the best were unknown, underground and, most of all, local. And the most overlooked involve women. This book, designed to be as vivid and personal-feeling as a classic photopied punk zine, is both a travelogue and a compendium of the women-led bands that burned up the clubs in punk’s forming days, not only in New York and Los Angeles, but in vibrant scenes from South Palm Beach to Milwaukee to San Francisco. Mixing mini-bios with interviews, essays and raggedly cool visutals, Hit Girls shows how unhinged female energy gave punk life.

title: But Will You Love Me Tomorrow: An Oral History of the '60s Girl Groups
author: Laura Flam and Emily Sieu Liebowitz
description: As much as the Beatles, and more than Elvis, girl groups shaped the sound of classic rock. In this exhaustive and exciting oral history – think Meet Me in the Bathroom in taffeta and kitten heels – girl group devotees Laura Flam and Emily Sieu Liebowitz gather more than 100 voices to reveal nearly every aspect of the early-1960s girl group moment – from the artistry of the Brill Building songwriters and the mostly Black teen singers who brought their compositions to life, to the role girl groups played in the fight for civil rights and the sad stories of some who were more exploited than celebrated. If you love great pop and underheard stories, in this book you’ll hear a symphony.

title: Astor: The Rise and Fall of an American Fortune
author: Anderson Cooper and Katherine Howe
description: The Astor family made its money in beaver trapping, but the family is perhaps most known for its political and cultural influence. The prominent American family helped develop New York City with multiple real estate holdings, and the Astors were mainstays of Gilded Age society and had the most famous death on the Titanic. As with most other ultrawealthy Gilded Age families, the money that was made by one or two generations dwindled over the years and is now mostly gone (see Anderson Cooper’s 2021 book on his ancestors, the Vanderbilts). The book details the often ruthless ways members of the family made and spent their fortune and how it led at times to unhappiness and greed. Money can’t buy you everything.

title: Once More With Feeling
author: Elissa Sussman
description: Katee Rose was a child pop star who dated the lead singer of a boy band. A love triangle developed with another band member, Cal Kirby, ultimately leading to the destruction of Katee’s career and public life. But as an adult, Katee (now Kathleen) gets an opportunity to star in a Broadway show written by another childhood friend – something she’d always dreamed about. Except Cal is directing it, and they haven’t spoken in 10 years, each blaming the other for their downfall. Old flames start to ignite, and they have to decide how much they’re willing to risk for a second chance.

title: The Ugly History of Beautiful Things: Essays on Desire and Consumption
author: Katy Kelleher
description: Katy Kelleher’s collection of essays is part history, part memoir of the precious and pretty things we consume, from gemstones and porcelain to makeup and perfume. Her lush descriptions of these objects are reason enough to recommend the book, though she writes with equal care of their painful, extractive and deadly origins. Throughout, she weaves in memories of, and musings on, her own fraught relationship with mascara and turquoise jewelry, silk clothes and gleaming countertops. Kelleher never lectures or guilt-trips, nor does she shy away from ambivalence. She assures us that to seek and covet beauty is natural and ancient, even life affirming – and that there are ways to do it that can enrich our understanding, even enhance our pleasure and maybe leave less of a mark.

title: Blackouts
author: Justin Torres
description: Blackouts is an immersive experience. This National Book Award winner contains photographs, illustrations and handwritten letters – ephemera that help tell the story of an early 20th-century lesbian researcher and activist named Jan Gay. Justin Torres embeds her real-life story (complete with cameos from Andy Warhol, Emma Goldman and Martin Scorsese) in a fictional (or is it?) dialogue between a dying man named Juan Gay and a young unnamed narrator. The book is mysterious, sexy and layered, and as soon as I finished it, I wanted to go back and read it again.

title: The Lonely Hearts Book Club
author: Lucy Gilmore
description: It’s a classic trope: Sweet woman meets grumpy man, and after a series of twists and turns, they realize how much they need each other. You probably have an idea where this is going, but you’re probably only halfway right. Sloane is a young librarian who gets into daily arguments about books with an old curmudgeon named Arthur. When Arthur stops coming around the library for a few days, Sloane risks her job by looking up his address to track him down. When she gets there, she finds him bedridden and alone. Wanting to keep a close eye on him, she starts a book club with him and a few other misfits, much to his chagrin. It turns out they all need each other, but for different reasons. And if you think you know what happens, that doesn’t take away from the journey of this sweet story.

title: Thicker Than Water: A Memoir
author: Kerry Washington
description: Celebrity memoirs can be inherently tantalizing, especially when written by a “famously private” famous person. But I’m not sure many contain revelations as shocking as what Kerry Washington learned and subsequently shared about her own family history. Very mild spoiler warning here: In uncanny examples of life imitating art, the actor lays bare some lived experiences that mirror the plotlines of her on-screen roles in Scandal, She Hate Me and Little Fires Everywhere. Even if you haven’t avoided full spoilers for this book, Washington’s candor about forgiveness and building resilience after multiple traumas is deeply compelling. Insight and wisdom about the craft of acting is also sprinkled in, to boot.

title: The Covenant of Water
author: Abraham Verghese
description: The doctor and novelist (how can you really be that multitalented?!) Abraham Verghese returns to the literary scene with an epic family drama that has been years in the making. The result – one of the best books I’ve read about South Asia in years. On its surface, this story set in South India  chronicles three generations of a family that suffers from a medical mystery of drownings. But the storyline is much deeper – a complex plot with lessons about love, pain and human understanding. Don’t be discouraged by the 700+ pages: The book is a page-turner that miraculously gives you a way to find hope in the face of repeated tragedy (though I still found myself crying).
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: The End of Drum-Time
author: Hanna Pylväinen
description: Set in the cold, dark and often eerie world of Finland’s Sámi people, Hanna Pylväinen’s second novel – a finalist for the 2023 National Book Award – derived from her investigation of family roots and her discovery of fundamentalist Christian ancestors who once interacted with Indigenous groups in the far north. When Willa, a young 19th century woman whose strict minister father disapproves of her love for reindeer herder Ivvár, decides to take a chance and follows the herders to the sea, readers will see that Pylväinen cares more about the culture clash than about the crimes committed in the name of anyone’s gods. It’s an unusual, and unusually elegant, perspective on what happens when groups of humans cannot possibly agree on the best use of land, time and strength.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: Glitter Everywhere! Where It Came From, Where It's Found & Where It's Going
author: Chris Barton, illustrated by Chaaya Prabhat
description: Why did the European Union officially ban loose glitter in 2023? We think we’ve found the answer. Everyone’s favorite microplastic gets a book of its own as Chris Barton digs deep to show everything about the sparkly substance, from its invention and ties to the LGBTQ+ community to its devastating effect on the environment. Original research digs into who created the first glitter and what we can do to create a sustainable version in the future. Love glitter? Great! Hate glitter? Even better! Here’s a book that can truly appeal to both sides of this eternal debate. (For ages 5 to 9)

title: The Many Assassinations of Samir, the Seller of Dreams
author: Daniel Nayeri, illustrated by Daniel Miyares
description: Any book that begins with a kid running hell-for-leather away from a group of monks attempting to stone him to death is clearly a book worth reading! A kid referred to only as Monkey is saved from certain death by a fast-talking merchant named Samir on the Silk Road. Trouble is, six different assassins have been hired to kill Samir, and Monkey is on the hook to save him each time. A book by turns shocking and hilarious. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: There Was a Party for Langston, King of Letters
author: Jason Reynolds, illustrated by Jerome Pumphrey and Jarrett Pumphrey
description: In 1991 the New York Times photographer Chester Higgins Jr. went to a party at the Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture and got a shot of Maya Angelou dancing with Amiri Baraka above the scattered ashes of Langston Hughes himself. Now kids can join this poetry party of the page as it cheers on America’s Black wordsmiths one and all, from W.E.B. Du Bois to Octavia Butler. Come for the celebration, stay for the jaw-dropping art and killer typography. You want a book topped up with Black joy? Come and get it! (For ages 4 to 8)

title: An American Story
author: Kwame Alexander, illustrated by Dare Coulter
description: “How do you tell a story that starts in Africa and ends in horror?” Around the U.S. right now, some towns and states are challenging children’s books that dare to show a more complicated vision of our American past. Fortunately, our kids have brave histories like this one to double down and show just how much complexity can be packed into a single picture book. It’s slavery, but explained with the honesty our children deserve. And as the book says, our children will deal with this information “by being brave enough to lift your voice, by holding history in one hand and clenching hope in the other.” (For ages 4 to 8)

title: Ordinary Notes
author: Christina Sharpe
description: There are 248 “notes” contained in Christina Sharpe’s collection. Among them are personal and historical photographs, letters, reading lists, observations and definitions. Taken together, they create a portrait of Black life – both Sharpe’s singular life and the collective lives of Black people – that is honest and thought provoking and heartbreaking and, above all, exquisite. Though Sharpe calls this collection Ordinary Notes, there is nothing at all ordinary about it.

title: When Crack Was King: A People's History of a Misunderstood Era
author: Donovan X. Ramsey
description: As a Black man who grew up during the crack epidemic, author Donovan X. Ramsey watched firsthand as the drug lay waste to his community – but he never understood why it happened. In When Crack Was King, Ramsey reevaluates the era by focusing on the stories of four people who lived through it. Along the way, he makes a convincing case that government policies criminalized what was essentially a public health crisis, and he busts some of the most pernicious media-generated myths of the epidemic – including the much ballyhooed threat of the “crack baby.”

title: We Could Have Been Friends, My Father and I: A Palestinian Memoir
author: Raja Shehadeh
description: There’s something so simple, so heartbreaking about the title of Raja Shehadeh’s memoir, We Could Have Been Friends, My Father and I. In it, the Palestinian lawyer and human rights activist revisits his strained relationship with his father, a brilliant lawyer displaced from Jaffa by the Israelis in 1948, then later banished and imprisoned by the Jordanian regime for advocating on behalf of Palestinians. Shehadeh describes his father, Aziz, as a “pioneer, ahead of his peers,” a man whose advocacy for a two-state solution put him at odds with both Arabs and Israelis – and may have been the impetus for his assassination in 1985. Writing now, decades later, Shehadeh finds himself sorting through his father’s papers, surprised and saddened by how much of himself he recognizes in Aziz’s work.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Rediscovery of America: Native Peoples and the Unmaking of U.S. History
author: Ned Blackhawk
description: Historian Ned Blackhawk opens The Rediscovery of America with a map of the contiguous United States, overlain with the names of the Indigenous tribes that called each area home. Millions of Native people were living in North America prior to Columbus’ landing in 1492 – but they’ve often been left out of history books and official archives. This engrossing and thought-provoking retelling of five centuries of history won the National Book Award for nonfiction, and it highlights the myriad ways Indigenous people have shaped the United States’ politics, Constitution, diplomacy and culture.

title: The Great Escape: A True Story of Forced Labor and Immigrant Dreams in America
author: Saket Soni
description: Saket Soni’s The Great Escape begins in 2006, when Soni, a labor organizer, receives a midnight phone call from a Mississippi number. Speaking in Hindi, the caller pleads, “You need to help us, sir. We are growing desperate.” The man is one of 500 Indian welders and pipefitters who paid $20,000 to a firm that promised green cards if they would help rebuild after Hurricane Katrina. But the green cards were a lie, and the men are living in squalid work camps. What follows reads like a movie script: There’s a daring middle-of-the-night escape from camp, then a march to Washington, D.C., where the workers file a human trafficking complaint with the Department of Justice. Throughout it all, Soni humanizes the men, reminding us that exploitation is all too common among marginalized workers.

title: Lesbian Love Story: A Memoir In Archives
author: Amelia Possanza
description: Amelia Possanza tells the story of seven lesbians and their loves, including Mabel Hampton, co-founder of the Lesbian Herstory Archives; Rusty Brown, a male impersonator who performed at Coney Island in the McCarthy era; and Gloria Anzaldúa, who refused to choose between her lesbian and Chicana identities. The stories are deeply researched and imaginatively told through a mix of historical record, primary sources, invented scenes and the author’s own wrestling with the meaning of what she finds. Possanza challenges the history we’re taught, one that codifies and reproduces heteronormativity. “Scholars … will construct arguments out of what little evidence remains, arguments that suit their needs, further their own careers. I have defiantly joined them, arguing for the lesbian ancestor I need.”

title: The Woman in Me
author: Britney Spears
description: How did one of the most beloved, visible and successful women in the world end up in a 13-year conservatorship that revoked her access to her wealth, her privacy and even her food preferences? The Woman In Me, the bombshell memoir from pop star Britney Spears, attempts to answer just that. We find the expected – opportunistic family, dastardly exes, and a labyrinthine family court system – but always in the periphery, fueling the paparazzi’s chase, is the public, who lapped up every one of Spears’ missteps on gossip blogs and tabloid pages. In showing us her demons, Spears also hands us a mirror.

title: My Name Is Barbra
author: Barbra Streisand
description: At nearly 1,000 pages long, Barbra Streisand’s memoir is the ultimate exercise in directorial control. Over the course of 59 chapters, Streisand recounts her life as a series of odysseys, and she the ever-triumphant victor. Frankly, she has earned it. Our culture loves to press pause on Streisand, seeing her only as Babs – the consummate prima donna, our forever leading lady. But in doing so, audiences, critics and biographers have failed to acknowledge the shrewd image-maker she has always been: the architect of her persona and performance.

title: Ink Blood Sister Scribe
author: Emma Törzs
description: As a book person, is there anything more alluring than a book about people who are obsessed with books? I was immediately intrigued by the premise of Ink Blood Sister Scribe: Estranged sisters are reunited when mysterious forces threaten their family’s library of magic books. It took only a few pages for this stellar debut to put me completely under its spell. Genres entwine to form the cleverly paced narrative as we travel from thriller to murder mystery to romance, while always keeping a foot in the deliciously fantastical.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Love & Romance"]

title: The Magician's Daughter
author: H.G. Parry
description: A girl raised by a magician on the hidden island of Hy-Brasil must leave the safety of her secluded world to help her guardian save the existence of magic. This book is rooted in appreciation for the classics, but it never feels derivative or awkwardly nostalgic. I ripped through it with a sort of manic glee, repeatedly forgetting I was supposed to be reading it critically rather then just enjoying myself. In short, this is a charming romp of an old-school coming of age fantasy about family and magic that will take your heart for a wild ride.

title: To Shape a Dragon's Breath: The First Book of Nampeshiweisit
author: Moniquill Blackgoose
description: An Indigenous girl bonds with a dragon and is forced to attend a school where the colonizing Anglish train their youth to control dragons. She rapidly learns that conforming to Anglish values threatens everything she cares about. These days, I find that it’s hard to enjoy a fantasy boarding school setting that doesn’t consider the inherent imperialist undertones of such institutions. This book cuts right to the chase and offers a scathing rejection of the idea that there is one right way for a person to be educated. I tore through it, caught up in an enthusiasm for dragons that I hadn’t experienced since I was a teenager obsessed with Ursula Le Guin’s Earthsea and Anne McCaffrey’s Pern.

title: The Queen of Dirt Island
author: Donal Ryan
description: This story is about four generations of women in rural Ireland, beginning in the 1980s with the birth of Saoirse (and the death, the same day, of her father). We see this family through Saoirse’s eyes (and what perceptive eyes!) as she grows up under the care of her mother, Eileen, and grandmother Mary – Eileen’s mother-in-law turned best friend. Men both dead and alive linger at the periphery, but these women run the show, with fierce opinions and even fiercer love that are tested when Saoirse becomes pregnant. Their lives unfold in short, lyrical chapters that sneak quietly into your gut and grab you by the heart.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Last Devil to Die: A Thursday Murder Club Mystery
author: Richard Osman
description: Richard Osman’s books are a slyly sophisticated bunch, boasting emotional development equal to the memorable mysteries. Every Thursday, members of a wealthy retirement community apply their considerable talents to unsolved local crime. They begin with cold cases, then move on to active investigations, to the frustration of the local authorities. The core four – diarist and social butterfly Joyce, retired psychiatrist Ibrahim, Ron, the bombastic political activist, and Elizabeth, a former spy and natural leader – grow tighter with each installment, making The Last Devil to Die especially poignant. While ex-spy Elizabeth reaches an unmistakable turning point with her husband, whose dementia has grown acutely painful, danger lurks close to home as the crew tries to solve the recent murder of a friend. Thursday Murder Club mystery stands up well on its own, but given the richness of character and relationships, as a set, they’re bloody brilliant.

title: Something Wild & Wonderful
author: Anita Kelly
description: Some of the best modern romances are as much about growing into oneself as about finding the one to create a life with. With a literal quest at its center, Anita Kelly’s latest melds those two arcs particularly well. Alexei Lebedev and Ben Caravalho have their minds fixed on their personal hurdles when they meet cute, just barely avoiding disaster on the challenging Pacific Crest Trail. Completing the 2,500-mile hike is a trial. It takes months. But it’s a great way to exhaust your body while seeking change or tending to emotional and mental bruises. Though they have very different experiences and outlooks, Ben and Alexei are undeniably drawn to each other after repeatedly crossing paths. Their romantic relationship grows organically and gorgeously as they press forward into the wild.

title: Symphony of Secrets
author: Brendan Slocumb
description: This haunting and heartaching second novel is a rarity – a suspenseful dual-timeline thriller about icons and hidden figures in music. Like the star of Brendan Slocumb’s debut, The Violin Conspiracy, Josephine Reed defies expectations. In the early 20th century, her options are severely limited; she’s a young Black woman and, though it wouldn’t have been called this then, neurodivergent. She’s also enormously talented, and her art is everything. She followed her passion to New York, writing music in secret, doing odd jobs, and sharing an apartment with a new friend, the ambitious but struggling composer Frederic Delaney. While Fred gets famous, Josephine fades away like so many new arrivals. Decades later, musicology professor Bern Hendricks receives an enticing call from an institution inviting him to restore Fred’s long-lost handwritten opera score for performance. What the young music professor and his high-tech partner Eboni discover could shake the foundation to the ground.

title: We Could Be So Good
author: Cat Sebastian
description: When Cat Sebastian is creating romance, every serve is an ace. Little surprise that this sweet and sizzling 1950s story is my favorite of the year. Sebastian draws the challenges of queer life in the period with insight and a light touch. The real stars, though, are her characters and their chemistry. “Nick Russo could fill the Sunday paper with reasons why he shouldn’t be able to stand Andy Fleming.” On top of being the boss’s son, Andy is a gadfly and a diffident heir who’s only “slumming it” on the city desk at The Chronicle, the New York newspaper where Nick is sweating out his dreams. Nick clocks a litany of reasons to loathe this upstart, yet the hostile pose lasts barely an hour. He becomes Andy’s guide, then best friend. Their slow-burning sparks are organic, joy inducing and earned.

title: Crook Manifesto
author: Colson Whitehead
description: In this enticing follow-up to the heightened heist novel Harlem Shuffle, after 15 years in business, uptown anti-hero Ray Carney, the industrious son of a notorious career criminal, has earned the trappings of respectability. A property owner and merchant, he’s expanded his landmark store and ensconced his family in a Strivers’ Row brownstone that even his persnickety in-laws should envy. And yet, Ray remains both prosperous and twitchy – just itching for a reason to stray. So when his daughter begs for sold-out Jackson 5 concert tix, down the illicit rabbit hole he goes. Who knew trading favors with a dirty cop would create complications? In this twisty, three-part tale, Colson Whitehead’s evocation of a fraying 1970s New York and blending of social history and crime make the journey exquisite.

title: Age of Vice
author: Deepti Kapoor
description: With its focus on a family dynasty rooted in crime, this meaty Indian crime saga is frequently compared to mafia drama, but I read Deepti Kapoor’s creation more as a page-turning social novel than pulpy thriller. It stirs the pulse while digging into the entrenched and evolving structures and contradictions of modern India through three primary figures – a playboy with ambition beyond his shady, blood-soaked family business, a lonely servant, and an ambitious journalist. The most memorable of the trio, the one that stuck with me long after I put the book down, is a young man of low caste whose life began spiraling out of control with one tiny mistake at the age of eight.

title: The Enchanters
author: James Ellroy
description: This jazzy 1960s carnival ride of a thriller by the preeminent chronicler of Old Hollywood noir imagines that Marilyn Monroe’s demise may have stemmed from an obsession with the most powerful man in the free world. Matching the mood of its sensational subjects, the writing is sexy, nervy and drug fueled. Alongside disgraced detective Freddy Otash and the doomed star, the players include President Kennedy, his brother Robert and mobbed-up labor boss Jimmy Hoffa, who’s out to destroy the crusading pols. The plot that brings them together is sprawling and twisty: A kidnapping and the suspicious death of one of the most famous women ever to grace the silver screen brings Freddy’s special skill set back in the bosom of the LAPD to suss out the facts.

title: Better the Blood
author: Michael Bennett
description: I read this thriller in January 2023 and haven’t stopped thinking about it since. Taking a knife to a nation’s idyllic image, this debut puts a tragic fact in the center of a murder mystery: “Aotearoa/New Zealand is a paradise with an awful and bloody colonial history.” That past propels a series of shocking contemporary crimes 160 years after a brutal act against the nation’s Indigenous people. While some caution that “fire ignites more fires,” or “violence begets violence,” others demand no less than retribution. The senior officer running the investigation, Maori detective Hana Westerman, has long been caught up in these conflicts. Her white ex is the police chief, her daughter is an activist and her own family has been rocked by the underlying rifts.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Secret Hours
author: Mick Herron
