description: Though Mick Herron’s latest thriller connects the dots between the sins of the post-Cold War era and the messy, contemporary misadventures of the popular Slough House spy series, it’s also fantastic as a standalone. That said, if you’ve read the previous books, the breadcrumbs are a delight to follow. After years of dead ends and low-level testimony, two career MI5 civil servants are grappling with a new development in a career-killing assignment investigating whether (or how badly) the UK’s elite intelligence service has overreached their authority in covert operations. When a bombshell case file mysteriously lands in their laps, they’re told to shut it all down. Instead, Griselda and Malcolm call the crucial party to testify. Deadly consequences, riveting flashbacks, and revelations ensue.

title: The Happy Couple
author: Naoise Dolan
description: As a fragile romantic, I was shocked by how much I enjoyed this wry tale of modern love gone off the rails. But like reality television, unholy messes are riveting. Luke and Celine are enviable, newly engaged, 20-something Dubliners with great careers and fashionable friends, but their relationship is hobbled by doubt. That sounds like Sally Rooney territory, but this is darker – reading it feels like a twisty and suspenseful thriller with wounded hearts in place of dead bodies. Part of that is due to the ingenious structure. Naoise Dolan tells this story from multiple perspectives, saving one of the central players for last.

title: A Haunting in Hialeah Gardens
author: Raul Palma
description: It’s a rare book that scares you and makes you think in equal measures. In this viscerally affecting debut, Raul Palma reimagines the manifestations and consequences of debt in terrifying and thought-provoking ways. Debt is more than what you owe the bank. It’s the sins you commit that come back to haunt you just when you think you’ve made an escape. For widower and Santeria priest Hugo Contreras, grief-stricken and hellbent on revenge, the devil is a dangerous debt collector to challenge. Hugo is haunted and tormented by the myriad debts he has accumulated since his brother helped him escape the mines of Bolivia as a child. He’s a widower who feels implicated in his wife’s suffering and death, and the mistakes he has piled up aren’t easy to shake.

title: The Fraud
author: Zadie Smith
description: When an Australian butcher with a very unaristocratic mien and accent comes forward claiming to be the long-lost missing heir to a British title and estate, the resulting court case becomes a divisive cause célèbre in Victorian England. And when, more than a hundred years later, one of our most revered literary icons makes the case the center of her first historical novel, it’s an event. There are myriad reasons to love Zadie Smith’s grand novel – precise period detail, the sensational case at the center and how Smith captures the social and political landscape of an era. Smith delivers on all those counts. What I didn’t expect was the emotionality and how well it would reveal the lives of, and relationships between, those watching and those on the periphery of the case, including those who were once considered property and part of that disputed estate.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: In Memoriam
author: Alice Winn
description: Any reader picking up a World War I book will have some sense, at least intellectually, of the historic horrors that await. But this propulsive and poetic debut thrust me deep into the muddy maws of industrialized trench warfare in a way that felt emotionally convincing and new. But In Memoriam is more than a searing war novel; it’s also a suspenseful story of forbidden queer love. It follows best friends Henry Gaunt and Sidney Ellwood from their English boarding school, into the officer ranks and to the battlefields of Ypres, Loos and the Somme. As expected, their paths are strewn with misery, death and escape, but I was left most awed by Alice Winn’s brilliant probing of the invisible injuries – those left by war, yes, but also by the repressive civilization that preceded and precipitated it.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Berlin
author: Bea Setton
description: You may think you’ve read this story before: Girl gets fed up with her hometown, girl moves to another country to find herself, girl finds success. But what if it doesn’t quite work out that way? This book is a wry, slinky exploration of something that many of us have dreamed of doing, but it’s coupled with an existential crisis that comes from the dream being less than what was expected. Written in punchy, self-effacing prose, Berlin is one of those novels whose jokes and terrors alike will stick with you for a while – in the best of ways.

title: The Wolves of Eternity
author: Karl Ove Knausgaard, translated by Martin Aitken
description: What does a moody, confused 19-year-old in 1980s Norway have to do with a middle-aged scientist in present day Russia? If your answer is “not a lot,” think again. In his signature style of having seemingly disparate narratives weave together in unlikely ways, Karl Ove Knausgaard brings us another story exploring the bonds we make, those we break, and those foisted upon us through circumstance in a wry, captivating story that will make you think about those you’re close to in new and different ways.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Beijing Sprawl
author: Xu Zechen, translated by Jeremy Tiang and Eric Abrahamsen
description: Seventeen-year-old Muyu leaves his rural village to make a life in Beijing, where things aren’t always as great as they seem from afar. He lives with three friends in a dusty apartment, and in Xu’s stories detailing their travails, we learn about how a changing city can change its people, too, both for the good and for the bad. In a place that’s constantly evolving, can one ever truly feel at home? Muyu and his friends aren’t quite sure, but they’re going to try their best despite all odds.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: Family Lore
author: Elizabeth Acevedo
description: Flor can tell when someone’s going to die. It’s her gift, her little bit of magic. Her three sisters have their own gifts, as do her two nieces – a magical ability to tell when someone’s lying. But when Flor announces she’s having a living wake, the family wonders whether she has foretold her own demise. This multigenerational story of secrets, survival and self-discovery artfully weaves together touches of magic with the reality of what it is to be a woman – daughter, sister, mother, tía. Whether you come for the chisme, the magic or the desire to read something real, you’ll leave having devoured a lyrical family saga that touches so many real, tender parts of what it means to be human.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Guest
author: Emma Cline
description: A romp of epic proportions – Alex, our adrift protagonist, swims and eats her way through the private pools and stainless steel kitchens of the Hamptons’ elite. Broke and without a home to return to, she extends her summer stay by weaseling her way into the lives of various seasonal residents. After puzzling through all the actually difficult parts of infiltrating the world, her demise is her own once she’s in. Alex’s self-destructive impulses land her in increasingly anxiety-inducing, thriller-like scenarios and interactions that become harder to unravel as she leaves a trail of mess with every exit. She simply must scratch the itch – steal the watch, kiss the boy, jump in the pool.

title: The Making of Another Major Motion Picture Masterpiece
author: Tom Hanks, illustrated by R.Sikoryak
description: Yes, this is a love letter to the movies. More to the point, it’s a love letter to the people who make movies. Tom Hanks is at his best when he’s breathing life into characters who, with a few exceptions, toil behind the camera: producers and assistants, drivers and hair stylists. There’s a humanity to this book that has always been Hanks’ greatest gift as an actor. Remember that scene in “The Money Pit” when a baby-faced Hanks is trapped in the carpet of his collapsing life? This book is like that: hilarious and tragic, ridiculous and heartbreakingly sincere all at once.

title: Mascot
author: Charles Waters and Traci Sorell
description: At a time when grown-ups struggle to thoughtfully tackle culture issues, a diverse group of middle schoolers debates their stereotypical “Indian mascot.” This multiple-viewpoint novel in verse reflects opinions of those with anti-racism, pro-Indigenous perspectives and of those who prioritize school tradition and “fun.” The conversation, which begins in the classroom, tests the tweens and their relationships. Respectively Black and Cherokee co-authors deftly interweave nuances of identity and impact with noteworthy attention to socioeconomic class. Their engaging, accessible narrative effectively utilizes white space on the page, inviting young readers to reflect on the controversy.  (For ages 10 to 13)

title: Eagle Drums
author: Nasuġraq Rainey Hopson
description: In a brilliantly timeless middle-grade debut, this authentic retelling blends faith and folklore centered on the origin of the Iñupiaq Messenger Feast. The art is gorgeously layered in meaning and tradition, elevating an engaging, adventurous text with imaginative, evocative visual storytelling. Young readers will be drawn in by Pinja’s high-stakes, epic adventure – rendered with immersive sensory detail and interwoven with the ways of Alaska Native life. A soaring story of family and courage, culture and community, wonder and resilience. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Americana", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction"]

title: Losing Our Religion: An Altar Call for Evangelical America
author: Russell Moore
description: The last decade in the U.S. has been fraught as it relates to politics and Christianity. Scandals, Trump, more scandals. In Losing Our Religion, former Southern Baptist pastor and Christianity Today editor-in-chief Russell Moore offers a roadmap for Christians trying to make sense of it all. There are no quick solutions here; he points, instead, to the teachings of Jesus in expressing how humans should be: rooted, generous and patient. Loving to our neighbor. Obsessed not with influence but with integrity and healing. If you, like me, have grown increasingly wary of the labels of Evangelical or even Christian, this book may be a good place to find your footing.

title: Recoding America: Why Government Is Failing in the Digital Age and How We Can Do Better
author: Jennifer Pahlka
description: Why are government websites and digital services so bad? Jennifer Pahlka’s experience behind the scenes tells her it’s not primarily an issue caused by outdated technology or the average government worker’s tech skills. Instead, Pahlka details how years of built-up policy “cruft” and top-down cultures conspire to leave us frustrated. The book offers vignettes explaining why the unemployment system was crushed under COVID-19, why many people in poverty don’t use food stamps and even why satellite maps could be made better. Despite tales that would frustrate even Franz Kafka, Pahlka’s optimism and problem-solving clarity are an inspiration for fixing pain points in any organization.

title: A Slow, Calculated Lynching: The Story of Clyde Kennard
author: Devery S. Anderson
description: Americans likely know how James Meredith made history in 1962 as the first Black student admitted to the all-white University of Mississippi. But before Meredith, another Black Mississippi veteran, Clyde Kennard, tried repeatedly to integrate Mississippi Southern College in Hattiesburg (now the University of Southern Mississippi). And he paid a great price for it. This meticulously researched book reveals how Kennard was wrongly imprisoned in Mississippi’s infamous Parchman prison farm, where he was denied proper medical care despite a cancer diagnosis. It reveals the chilling tactics the state of Mississippi employed to preserve white supremacy.

title: All You Have to Do Is Call
author: Kerri Maher
description: Powerful. Dramatic. Insightful. Kerri Maher’s All You Have to Do Is Call centers on the Jane Collective, an underground women’s health network in Chicago during the years of 1969 to 1973 – a time when abortion was illegal in most of the United States. Maher writes vividly and compassionately about the complexities and courage of her three main characters as they face a turbulent time, battling the limitations of health care. It’s not only a timely novel, but storytelling at its finest – a must-read.

title: The Cuban Heiress
author: Chanel Cleeton
description: The Cuban Heiress by Chanel Cleeton is an absorbing page-turner in a genre mashup of mystery, suspense, romance and thrills. It’s 1934, and during a voyage from New York to Havana on a luxury cruise liner, two fascinating women find themselves intertwined in a tale of love, larceny, loss and revenge. Inspired by the true story of the SS Morro Castle, Cleeton’s book is an unforgettable read that should be at the top of every TBR list.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: All the Sinners Bleed
author: S.A. Cosby
description: New York Times bestselling author S.A. Cosby’s All the Sinners Bleed is crime fiction at its best. Titus Crown, the first Black sheriff in Charon County, Va., is a former FBI agent who returned home to care for his sickly father, but solving crime is in his blood. A school shooting and a suspect shot to death by deputies lead Titus to the discovery of a series of gruesome murders – and the chase is on for a serial killer. Cosby’s powerful writing makes this an unforgettable tale that expertly weaves a taut criminal investigation with heart-pounding action and a heartwarming family. S.A. Cosby’s novel is another must-read from a crime fiction master.

title: The House of Eve
author: Sadeqa Johnson
description: New York Times bestselling author Sadeqa Johnson’s The House of Eve is a beautifully written and wonderfully crafted tale of two young Black women, their families and their love stories. However, that’s only the beginning of this immersive, captivating story. Johnson’s two main characters are strikingly different but stunningly similar. Readers will never forget Ruby or Eleanor, or the choices they make and the sacrifices they endure in this timely, relatable tale of women, children, skin color, haves and have-nots with an ending that will stay with you for a long, long time. A Reese’s Book Club pick, The House of Eve is an instant classic.

title: Romantic Comedy
author: Curtis Sittenfeld
description: If your love language is snark, this book is for you. Known for two kinds of books – historical fiction where the history is 20 minutes ago (Rodham, American Wife) and social X-rays in the lineage of Jane Austen (Eligible, Prep) – Curtis Sittenfeld checks both boxes with her latest. The oh-so-recent history here is the COVID-19 pandemic, and the historical figures are characters based on Saturday Night Live writers, producers and actors. It’s a very satisfying plot that Sittenfeld pulls together from the strands of our familiar lives and from sources documenting SNL. And my favorite scene: The main character’s first significant one-on-one interaction is the give-and-take of an edit!  You don’t have to be an editor, though, to be charmed by this romance.

title: A Fever in the Heartland: The Ku Klux Klan's Plot to Take Over America, and the Woman Who Stopped Them
author: Timothy Egan
description: This fascinating and timely book about the second rise of the Ku Klux Klan in America reads like a legal thriller. The story follows the rise and fall of Indiana’s con-man-ish grand dragon, D.C. Stephenson, whose qualities may remind some readers of a certain prominent political figure of today. When a woman brings an unlikely case against him, it challenges the status quo in 1920s Indiana, where belonging to the KKK became as common and as socially acceptable as belonging to the Rotary Club.

title: Four Battlegrounds: Power in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
author: Paul Scharre
description: What are the implications of the use of artificial intelligence in democratic and authoritarian states? Paul Scharre’s clear primer on AI addresses the race to lead AI in determining the rules of the next century. He raises the possibility that AI in war accelerates past human control, undermining democracy and freedom. He urges democratic nations to shape the future of AI, arguing that the U.S. has struggled to develop an identifiable strategy for AI, unlike China, which views it as a key to national success.

title: The Ghost at the Feast: America and the Collapse of World Order, 1900-1941
author: Robert Kagan
description: When the U.S. awakened to its status as a world power, it had to learn how to exercise power and shape the world without losing its founding principles. It had no strategy to do so. Robert Kagan challenges old tropes with economic data and a keen look at the domestic situation, arguing that World War II was a result of poor decisions in the 1920s. Underestimating U.S. power at home and abroad in 1919 had huge consequences. Once unleashed, there was no going back on how or when to use it.

title: The Right Call: What Sports Teach Us About Work and Life
author: Sally Jenkins
description: Sally Jenkins has had a superb career chronicling the highest achievements in sports, both by individuals and by teams. With The Right Call, she looks back through her reporter’s notebooks to capture what makes some athletes and coaches reach the peak of their game. Character and judgment prevail, but she explores how they succeed without pithy shortcuts. Whatever part of life you want to excel in – family, friendship or the workplace – this book will have you rethinking what you do and how you might do it differently.

title: Bright Young Women
author: Jessica Knoll
description: Written from the perspective of the young women whose lives were forever changed by the serial killer Ted Bundy, this book is part historical fiction – an old story told from a refreshingly new perspective – and part mystery. There are multiple voices and multiple time periods, but it all gets woven together with a great payoff at the end. Read it for the great characters, lots of plot and enough unanswered questions that you’ll have trouble putting it down.

title: Cassandra in Reverse
author: Holly Smale
description: This book both touched me and taught me about autism. The title character is autistic (as is the author, who prefers that terminology) and remembers – and conveys – her past in painstaking detail. This ability, though, comes in handy when Cassandra discovers she can go back in time and change that past. Of course, her first efforts are inept. But if you stay in Cassandra’s world, I imagine you, like me, will end up rooting for her – and remembering her long after you’ve finished reading.

title: Egg: A Dozen Ovatures
author: Lizzie Stark
description: I never thought all that much about eggs. But Lizzie Stark dives into their mythology, their uses in scientific experiments and the food industry, and the many ways that they’ve been decorated. And, no yolk, there is a lot to egg-splore. Egg puns aside (and she has a lot of them!), Stark also has a difficult relationship with her own eggs: She has a genetic link to cancer. The combination of facts and whimsy makes this an egg-cellent read.

title: The Museum of Failures
author: Thrity Umrigar
description: OK, no plot spoilers because I want you to read this novel! The central character, Remy, is an Indian American ad executive who returns to Bombay to adopt a child. He learns that his mother, from whom he’s estranged, is ill and has stopped speaking. Why she has and the nature of the estrangement are the basis of this profound story about love and forgiveness, parents and children. I sped through the book and was invested to the last page and beyond. Please discover it for yourself.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Brotherless Night
author: V.V. Ganeshananthan
description: What makes a person become a terrorist? This book explores that question through the eyes of Sashi, a young Tamil woman growing up in a Sri Lanka on the edge of civil war. She wants to be a doctor but cannot ignore her loved ones who want to fight for a Tamil homeland. I was completely engrossed in this novel; reading it made the story of the Tamil Tigers and the bloody conflict in Sri Lanka real to me in a way they hadn’t been before.

title: Unreliable Narrator: Me, Myself, and Impostor Syndrome
author: Aparna Nancherla
description: Comedian Aparna Nancherla has long explored mental health in her stand-up. Her book allows her to dig deeper into how psychology has become more mainstream in popular culture. Her essays reflect on her own experiences with anxiety and depression, along with the rise in mental health being normalized and monetized on social media – with self-deprecating jokes along the way. In one instance, Nancherla offers a peek into her brain hour by hour as she deals with performance anxiety before a stand-up set, an art form she loves dearly but that sparks internal conflict. Her writing reveals how she has adjusted to anxiety and depression as present, yet evolving, forces in her life, which is relatable for anyone who is navigating life with these experiences.

title: Congratulations, The Best Is Over!: Essays
author: R. Eric Thomas
description: R. Eric Thomas’ latest essay collection focuses on the absurdity of midlife. He returns to his hometown of Baltimore after his husband takes a new job, despite Thomas having a “toxic relationship” with the city. Thomas chronicles how he and his husband work hard to establish friendships, while he also feels out of place in his own hometown. At one point, Thomas is not recognized while standing next to his family at church and is then mistaken for a local bartender who left the area. On the plus side? He gets free wine out of the interactions. Thomas’ incisive and hilarious essays are about the pains of starting over and making friends as an adult. In one of my favorite essays in the collection, he weaves together his love of Oprah’s Favorite Things with his experiences with depression and seeking therapy.

title: Quietly Hostile: Essays
author: Samantha Irby
description: Samantha Irby’s latest essay collection includes some familiar topics, like her love of Dave Matthews Band and her frequent references to diarrhea. She also weaves together new stories about navigating COVID-19 isolation, in part by adopting a poorly behaved pandemic dog, which I deeply relate to. Irby’s voice is distinct and hilarious, whether she is discussing Red Lobster or ridiculous reactions people online had when it was announced she was a writer on the Sex and the City reboot, And Just Like That …. I appreciate that she shares her disappointments, like spending years pitching a television show about her life that never aired. Hopefully Hollywood will reconsider, but in the meantime, we have Irby’s essays to enjoy.

title: The Centre
author: Ayesha Manazir Siddiqi
description: At first, The Centre seems like so many other thrillers – a young woman finds herself in a secluded environment with a shocking secret at its core. In this case, the young woman is Anisa, a frustrated translator, and the secluded environment is an elite training program that can make anyone fluent in any language in just 10 days. Where this book starts to turn things on its head is when Anisa discovers the Centre’s shocking secret – and fails to be all that shocked (in fact, she kind of vibes with it). To that trope subversion add smart critiques on the intersections of race, class and gender – as well as a few laugh-out-loud moments – and what you have is either a thriller with a literary theory degree or literary fiction wrapped in a thriller’s (skull-and-flower-encrusted) cover.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Vaster Wilds
author: Lauren Groff
description: In 17th-century colonial Virginia, a young servant girl escapes a famine-torn settlement to flee into the sprawling, punishing wilderness beyond. In her fifth novel, Lauren Groff, an author whose prose imbues even the most mundane with magic, crafts a tale that transcends genre. Part historical, part horror, part breathless thriller, part wilderness survival tale, The Vaster Wilds is a story about the lengths to which we will go to stay alive.

title: A Perfect Vintage
author: Chelsea Fagan
description: A Perfect Vintage has it all: a picturesque French countryside château and a complex web of relationships that could come only from mixing family, work and pleasure. We follow Lea Mortimer, who is meticulous about her job remodeling French country estates into hotels. It’s business as usual until her best friend (and cousin) Stephanie, in the midst of a messy divorce and in need of an escape, convinces Lea to take her and her daughter to Lea’s next project in the Loire Valley. At the château, they step into the complex dynamics of the Lévesque family, owners of the property. Trysts blossom, relationships are tested and a tangled web is woven. Pairs well with sitting poolside, a glass of French wine in hand!
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Unraveling: What I Learned About Life While Shearing Sheep, Dyeing Wool, and Making the World's Ugliest Sweater
author: Peggy Orenstein
description: Like many other people, I picked up knitting again during the throes of the COVID-19 pandemic. Peggy Orenstein did too – in a more thorough way than most. She documents my (maybe somewhat specific) dream: making a sweater truly from scratch, starting with shearing a sheep, then spinning the yarn, dyeing the wool and finally knitting it up. As Orenstein goes through each step, she interrogates our historical and current relationship to the making of clothes. At the same time, Unraveling is a memoir of a life during COVID: the tedious stress of lockdown, all while facing big shifts in her relationships with a parent and a child. The breadth is wide, and it all comes together cohesively in Orenstein’s conversational, engaging prose.

title: Without Children: The Long History of Not Being a Mother
author: Peggy O'Donnell Heffington
description: My mother never pressured me to become a mother, which may be part of the reason why I never felt it was an imperative. And also why I was somewhat surprised to discover so many people felt it was – for themselves, sure, but also for me and every other woman, regardless of desire, ability or inclination. The often untold, hushed, pitied, even vilified (but rarely celebrated) history of women without children is a worthwhile read for anyone who wants to consider how women build their lives and how we can support them whether they are mothers or not.

title: Nineteen Claws and a Black Bird: Stories
author: Agustina Bazterrica, translated by Sarah Moses
description: This is a relatively short collection of stories, but it opens with a bang and never relents. A girl says she has a rabbit between her legs. A man commits suicide to spite his neighbor. A woman offers a guide to overcoming a breakup. A man falls for an alien. Wild, hilarious, violent, unexpected; this collection has it all, and Agustina Bazterrica deftly changes tone and atmosphere with each story.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Whalefall
author: Daniel Kraus
description: When you read a novel about a man who is accidentally swallowed by a whale – yes, a modern version of Jonah’s tale – you probably expect strangeness and gore, and Daniel Kraus’ narrative delivers plenty of those. However, it’s the book’s wealth of scientific facts and emotional content that makes this a must-read. An enthralling, claustrophobic, action-packed thriller that is also a love letter to the ocean and a beautiful tale that explores the broken relationship between a man and his dead father, this is Kraus’ best novel to date.

title: Monstrilio
author: Gerardo Sámano Córdova
description: To say Gerardo Sámano Córdova exploded onto the literary scene with this debut would be an understatement. The story of a mother who raises a sentient chunk of lung she cut from her dead son – and also the story of the being that grew from that piece of lung – Monstrilio is a wonderfully bizarre and unexpectedly touching narrative about queer love and the things that grief makes people do.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: The God of Endings
author: Jacqueline Holland
description: Zombies, haunted houses and vampires are a few horror tropes that must feel entirely new and unique in order to work, and that’s exactly what Jacqueline Holland pulls off here. Sure, this is a vampire novel, but it’s also much more than that. Gorgeously written even when it gets dark and violent and epic in terms of breadth, scope and the years it covers, The God of Endings follows one woman’s journey for two centuries, while also exploring the transformative power of art, the effects of solitude, the power of love and the beauty of brevity.

title: Brooklyn Crime Novel
author: Jonathan Lethem
description: Part crime novel, part literary mosaic that follows a plethora of lives in Brooklyn through time, and a love letter to the borough, Brooklyn Crime Novel sees Jonathan Lethem in the same spectacular form he showed in novels like Motherless Brooklyn and The Fortress of Solitude. Fast, funny, gritty and full of nostalgia, this mosaic novel is wildly entertaining while also delivering a history of Brooklyn and its residents that doesn’t shy away from tackling difficult topics like injustice, racism and gentrification.

title: Our Share of Night
author: Mariana Enriquez, translated by Megan McDowell
description: Mariana Enriquez’s Our Share of Night is a novel about black magic, family, the tumultuous years of Argentina’s military dictatorship and the strange years that followed. As poetic as it is gory and as complex as it is violent, this novel is one of the richest multilayered narratives of the last half-decade. It’s also a novel that will surely turn Enriquez into a household name, just like she is in her native Argentina.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Beast You Are: Stories
author: Paul Tremblay
description: Paul Tremblay is one of the leading voices of contemporary fiction, and he pushes the short story in new directions in each of his collections. From a ghost story to a narrative that uses letters to tell a tale, and from a mysterious building that just pops into existence one day to a long poem about animals fighting monsters, The Beast You Are is smart, creepy, funny and full of unique tales that show why Tremblay is widely recognized as a modern maestro of dark fiction.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: The House Is on Fire
author: Rachel Beanland
description: Rachel Beanland’s The House Is on Fire is the kind of historical novel that makes you forget that a lot of what you’re reading really happened. The novel chronicles the burning of a theater during a performance and the tumultuous aftermath of it in Richmond, Va., in 1811. While following several characters, the novel also looks at the rampant racism and misogyny of the times and explores how these two elements helped shape the narrative of the event and distort the truth of what happened during the ensuing investigation. This is a historical novel that is also very timely.

title: Victory City
author: Salman Rushdie
description: With nothing left to prove in his stellar career as a writer, it’s amazing to see Salman Rushdie deliver one of his best novels to date. Epic in both breadth and scope, Victory City tells the story of a young woman who becomes a vessel for a deity, while also chronicling the history of a city as it changes through 250 years. Presented to readers as the translation of an ancient epic, Victory City is a superb tale of love, grief, war, sibling rivalry, myth, growth and adventure that could’ve come only from one of the best living writers.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Holly
author: Stephen King
description: Fans of Holly Gibney have been eagerly waiting for a novel in which she is the main character since the moment she first appeared in Chapter 24 of Stephen King’s Mr. Mercedes, the first novel in the Bill Hodges trilogy. Holly appeared again in the two subsequent books and then in other King narratives, but Holly puts her front and center, and the wait was worth it. Dark, gripping, political and very of its time, Holly is a great read and hopefully the launch of Holly Gibney’s own series.

title: Pockets: An Intimate History of How We Keep Things Close
author: Hannah Carlson
description: In Pockets: An Intimate History of How We Keep Things Close, Hannah Carlson traces the cultural impact of pockets, from medieval Europe to Twitter hashtags. They might sound like a historical footnote, but to Carlson, pockets function as markers of class, industry, leisure and even gender. Above all, she argues, they offer privacy – increasingly precious in the surveillance age – and a deeply personal glimpse of what you carry with you. This book will give you fresh appreciation for that pocket in your coat (or convince you to make one).

title: Comedy Book: How Comedy Conquered Culture – and the Magic That Makes It Work
author: Jesse David Fox
description: Jesse David Fox is a shrewd and incisive critic of a vibrant and mutable art form that historically rejects criticism. Each chapter examines stand-up comedy from a different angle – “Context,” “Truth,” “Politics,” “Timing” and, not to be overlooked, “Funny.” But Fox brings the same level of inquiry and passion to them all, making an argument for comedy’s role in society even as he’s grappling with the shifting alchemy of the thing itself. It’s fascinating to read him making a very good case that, when it comes to stand-up, laziness is and has always been a far worse problem than not being funny. His insights go beyond pat notions of “punching up” and “punching down,” and he writes movingly about the craft of comedy – both its deft, hilarious uses and its hack, cruel abuses.

title: The Queer Film Guide: 100 great movies that tell LGBTQIA+ stories
author: Kyle Turner, illustrated by Andy Warren
description: Film critic Kyle Turner gives us a whistle-stop tour through the history of queer cinema that offers fresh insight into familiar chestnuts while spotlighting overlooked films that deserve a place in the canon. Turner’s analyses are steeped in queer academic theory, but – happily – his prose isn’t; it’s crisp and engaging and comes from a place of curatorial passion. The essays on each film are brief but substantive and are accompanied by cleverly evocative illustrations by Andy Warren, which make it all a great coffee-table book. Or, as it’s queer, an iced-coffee table book, I suppose.

title: The Male Gazed: On Hunks, Heartthrobs, and What Pop Culture Taught Me About (Desiring) Men
author: Manuel Betancourt
description: In this collection of essays, writer/critic Manuel Betancourt explores queer representation in media in an intensely personal, and uniquely engaging, manner. He recalls a childhood in Colombia as a “soft and skinny” kid whose ideas about masculinity were shaped by a host of disparate influences – including Ricky Martin, Saved by the Bell, Calvin Klein ads, Disney films, Walter Mercado and Pedro Almodóvar – that will resonate with his many readers. Betancourt finds the through line of forbidden desire connecting them all and unpacks how his life as a writer and a gay man exists in an ever-evolving conversation with their notions of manhood.

title: Sure, I'll Join Your Cult: A Memoir of Mental Illness and the Quest to Belong Anywhere
author: Maria Bamford
description: Maria Bamford is the best stand-up comic working today. And everything that makes her so jumps from every page of her memoir about her struggles with mental health and the various support groups, talk therapies and medications that have helped – or very, very haven’t. Her definition of “cult” is broader than yours (read: it includes things like AA, showbiz and her family), but her artisanal blend of unflinching self-awareness, searing honesty and compulsion for hilarious self-disclosure makes for an unforgettable read (or listen, because the audiobook is fantastic).

title: Hi Honey, I'm Homo!: Sitcoms, Specials, and the Queering of American Culture
author: Matt Baume
description: If you’re interested in the modern history of queer folk, what better lens through which to examine it than the American sitcom – that omnipresent cultural object that so diligently reflects the prevailing sensibilities of its time? Hidebound by narrative formula, fueled by tropes and conventional wisdom, the sitcom itself is anything but queer, but Matt Baume reveals how sitcom creators have always found ways to engage with queer content in good ways and bad, directly and (until relatively recently) indirectly. He shows that queer representation in media – even the most cringeworthy variety, on the hackiest sitcom – can challenge stereotypes by making them part of the joke.

title: Opposable Thumbs: How Siskel & Ebert Changed Movies Forever
author: Matt Singer
description: That subtitle promises a great deal, but anyone who grew up during Gene Siskel & Roger Ebert’s reign can only nod sagely at its wisdom. Strange that a TV show would nurture a love of film in an entire generation of viewers and critics (hi!), and Matt Singer lays out an ironclad case that not just any TV show could have done so. He unpacks how the two men’s bitter rivalry drove the show’s engine and remained the key to its uncanny appeal. I now host an unscripted roundtable discussion show that’s marked by less acrimony, but (hopefully) just as much passion and curiosity and eagerness to champion new voices. That’s the aim, anyway, and Siskel and Ebert were the template.

title: Outrageous: A History of Showbiz and the Culture Wars
author: Kliph Nesteroff
description: Kliph Nesteroff’s chosen subject is a tricky one. After all, pop culture comes in for criticism from all sides. Or does it? As you’d expect, you’ll find in these pages many self-appointed public scolds who, affronted by depictions of sex, politics, or religion, have convinced governments to censor artists for decades. But today, some of these very same individuals and organizations can be found making bad-faith complaints about “wokeness.” The through line, of course, is a regressive resistance to social change, and Nesteroff’s meticulously researched book chronicles the many battles that have been waged on that front in the culture wars since the birth of show business. It expertly provides the one thing lacking in too many discussions of popular culture: deep, substantive, meaningful context.

title: Künstlers in Paradise
author: Cathleen Schine
description: Cathleen Schine’s latest social comedy, Künstlers in Paradise,  involves “glamorous exile,” as multiple generations of a family shelter in Los Angeles from the Holocaust and, decades later, the COVID-19 pandemic. When 24-year-old Julian heads west in February 2020 to help his 93-year-old grandmother, “Mamie” Künstler, after a fall, he has no idea he’ll be trapped in her Venice Beach bungalow for months. Mamie seizes the opportunity to open the eyes of her whiny, floundering grandson with stories of her family’s harrowing flight from Vienna in 1939, their luck in landing in Hollywood and their adaptation to American life. Schine’s moving tale about how shared memories forge connections is lightened by witty repartee, a wry, breezy tone and fun cameos by Greta Garbo and Arnold Schoenberg.

title: Dayswork
author: Chris Bachelder and Jennifer Habel
description: Dayswork is charmingly unclassifiable. A collaboration between a husband and wife – one a novelist, the other a poet – it is part portrait of a literary marriage weathering the COVID-19 pandemic, part fictionalized memoir, part poetry and part biographical and critical dive into the often unhappy and not-altogether-successful life of Herman Melville. It all adds up to a clever and utterly enjoyable exploration of what it means to say that a life “works” – or doesn’t.

title: Roman Stories
author: Jhumpa Lahiri, translated by Jhumpa Lahiri and Todd Portnowitz
description: The nine stories in Jhumpa Lahiri’s first collection since she switched to writing in Italian powerfully convey her characters’ struggles to find their place in the world and overcome their painful sense of being outsiders. This pervasive sense of foreignness and being caught between two worlds strikes not just the refugees and immigrants Lahiri writes about with such compassion as they strive to gain a toehold in a xenophobic society, but also lifelong Romans and privileged American expats too. Elegantly written and largely translated by the author – who herself straddles several cultures and languages – Roman Stories presents an affecting view of an often cruelly unwelcoming world.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The House of Doors
author: Tan Twan Eng
description: Tan Twan Eng’s captivating, lushly atmospheric novel, set largely in 1921 Penang, imagines a two-week stay by the writer William Somerset Maugham at the home of an old friend. “Willie,” ever on the prowl for new material, finds plenty to spark his interest in his host’s apparently lovelorn wife, Lesley Hamlyn. The House of Doors encompasses at-the-time risky interracial and same-sex love stories, a scandalous (actual) murder trial and – through the discrepancies between what Lesley confides in Willie and the stories she later reads in Maugham’s 1926 collection, The Casuarina Tree – a clever exploration of the art of transforming life experiences into literature.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Women of Myth: From Deer Woman and Mami Wata to Amaterasu and Athena, Your Guide to the Amazing and Diverse Women from World Mythology
author: Jenny Williamson and Genn McMenemy, illustrated by Sara Richard
description: The goddesses, heroines and monsters of this collection share a trait: absolute badassery. The authors, who created the Ancient History Fangirl podcast, balance eyebrow-raising details with historical and cultural context. You’ll learn that ancient Vietnamese warrior Triệu Thị Trinh would sling her bosoms over her shoulder in battle, that she was likely a real-life rebel against Chinese imperialism, and also that she’s represented in some video games today. The full-page portraits depict people of action, who are unearthly yet relatable. (These Barbies have realistic body types!) Their personalities shine, ooze and/or scream through; even the corpselike Mexican ghost La Llorona has a certain je ne sais quoi.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "History", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Secret Diaries of Charles Ignatius Sancho
author: Paterson Joseph
description: Charles Ignatius Sancho was a real person: abolitionist, merchant, composer, husband, father, freed slave and the first known Black man to vote in a British general election. His fictionalized diaries imagine his youthful antics and, in an epistolary interlude, his long-distance romance; these are interleaved with brief detours into his respectable, gout-plagued middle age. The vibe is rollicking, but Charles’ bone-deep fear of slave catchers underlines the story with anxiety and sadness. Our hero’s bouncy resilience despite an unjust society and his personal demons (gambling, alcoholism, indigence) makes for a thrilling read that’s neither trauma porn nor superficial picaresque. A note for book-design nerds: The endpapers on this baby are to die for.

title: Choosing Family: A Memoir of Queer Motherhood and Black Resistance
author: Francesca T. Royster
description: Francesca T. Royster and her wife, Annie, decide they want to raise a child when they’re in their 40s and 50s respectively. Overwhelmed and awed by this desire, Royster, who is Black, and Annie, who is white, consider the most ethical way they can do so. In what is both a memoir and a work of accessible queer theory, Royster explores how Black families have historically needed to reinvent the institution. Indeed, Royster finds in her own family history, and its many and varied matriarchs, a kind of roadmap for her own coming parenthood.

title: People Collide
author: Isle McElroy
description: Eli is married to Elizabeth and is living with her in Bulgaria, where she’s completing a fellowship. One day, Eli visits Elizabeth’s workplace and discovers that people are addressing him as if he is his wife; when he realizes that he is, indeed, inside Elizabeth’s body, he understandably freaks out and spends several days at home trying to figure out what’s going on. Shenanigans – both hilarious and profound – ensue. People Collide is a deep exploration of marriage, love, the ways we know one another (and don’t), and how slippery a sense of self can be.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Love & Romance"]

title: Sidle Creek
author: Jolene McIlwain
description: The 22 stories in Jolene McIlwain’s debut collection all take place in the Appalachian plateau of western Pennsylvania, where the author grew up and lives now, and all convey the deep love of the people and place McIlwain has long called home. One of the collection’s recurring motifs is a masculine softness that isn’t always easy to express in words but that is nevertheless deeply felt. Another theme is community care; not in the Hollywoodized small town “aw shucks” kind of way, but rather in the difficult, intentional work of maintaining contact with one’s neighbors and trying to provide what people need.

title: Rootless
author: Krystle Zara Appiah
description: Sam gets home one evening to find that his wife, Efe, has left him and their toddler. Confused, angry and worried, Sam tracks her down – only to learn that she’s in Ghana, thousands of miles away from their London home. The book then takes us back in time to Efe’s youth and her arrival in the U.K., bound for high school and then university. Efe has always struggled with conflict between who she’s expected to be and who she is – and in this tender debut, we follow her as she navigates her friendship and later her romance with Sam and balances her own desires alongside her family’s.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Today Tonight Forever
author: Madeline Kay Sneed
description: Sometimes, you’re just tired of going to weddings. That’s the case for Athena, recently divorced from her wife and, once again, serving as a bridesmaid in a friend’s wedding. Exhausted with the grief of losing her father as well as splitting with her wife, she’s arrived at the beaches of Watercolor, Fla., unenthusiastic but ready to put her best face on. What follows is a whirlwind of a weekend, the novel following a wide cast of characters – from Athena to her ex-wife to the bride to assorted family members and old friends – as they confront secrets, old flames, betrayals and more.

title: Sunshine Nails
author: Mai Nguyen
description: Jessica Tran returns home to Toronto after living in Los Angeles for eight years. In all that time, she never came to visit, and her parents never felt they had the time to fly out to California. Having just lost her job, she’s insulted when her father offers her work at the family business, Sunshine Nails, a salon the Trans have been operating for years. Eventually, though, she doesn’t have much choice – and Jessica is surprised to find herself getting competitive with Take Ten, the trendy chain salon opening across the street that’s endangering Sunshine Nails’ business. This swift and pleasing read also simmers with questions about work, class and generational divides.

title: Bellies
author: Nicola Dinan
description: Ming and Tom meet while they’re both at university, and even though they couldn’t be more different, they hit it off immediately. The future seems certain for these lovebirds, but when they move to London after graduating, Ming shares with Tom that she’s a woman and is planning to transition. Tom loves Ming and at first is intent on staying with her; but Tom is gay, and what does it mean for him to be with a woman? Tom and Ming are far from perfect, but they’re complex and loving and navigate the following years with attempts at caring for both each other and themselves.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: Glassworks
author: Olivia Wolfgang-Smith
description: This era-spanning debut novel begins in 1910 with Agnes, a philanthropist and naturalist, who marries a cad. To cope, she loses herself in a friendship with Ignace Novak, a glassblower she has hired to make pristine models of plants and animals. In the next generation, Agnes’ son, Edward, a failed priest candidate, becomes enamored with a spirited young woman, who’s not what she seems. Their eventual child, Novak, a window washer in New York City, gets entangled with a Broadway ingénue, Cecily; Cecily’s child, Flip – also working with glass like those in generations past – has to untangle her family’s history as she tries to find her own way in life.

title: The Last Animal
author: Ramona Ausubel
description: It is the Age of Extinction, with the most recent death being that of Jane’s beloved husband, dead in a car accident. Jane, a graduate student in paleobiology, takes her daughters to Siberia on a research trip, and while there, the girls discover something miraculous: an entire baby mammoth frozen in the ice. Back in the U.S., trying to return to their regular lives, Jane and her daughters continue to grapple with their grief – both personal and ecological. When an opportunity to do some experimental science comes up, they jump on it, throwing caution and proper procedure to the wind.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Science", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Road to Dalton
author: Shannon Bowring
description: The fictional Dalton, a town in northern Maine’s Aroostook County, is the setting for this tender novel and its quiet and complicated characters. Richard and Trudy Haskell are married, and while Richard, the town doctor, knows everyone’s secrets, he cannot share those or much else with Trudy, who is preoccupied with her librarian job and her romance with Bev, who is married to Bill. The respective couples’ children are each struggling in different ways, as are the town’s other inhabitants. Via an intricate web of familial and neighborly relationships, Shannon Bowring brings to life the dramas of this politely distant yet loving community.

title: The Postcard
author: Anne Berest, translated by Tina Kover
description: An unsigned postcard with the names of relatives killed in the Holocaust arrives at her mother’s home; finding out who sent it and why leads the author to learn about her family history and her own identity. Anne Berest describes this as a “true novel” because all of the events in it are true – a result of her meticulous investigation into her family history – but she wrote it like fiction, changing the names of people who harmed her family so their descendants don’t suffer today and imagining characters’ thoughts. Berest’s story is specific, but there are themes in this book anyone can identify with – that history and bias are ever-present when we dig just a little, that how people see us may be so different from how we see ourselves, and that even relatives we never met influence the way we are.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Pineapple Street
author: Jenny Jackson
description: Jenny Jackson’s Pineapple Street is a helpful antidote to the grief and anxiety wrought by today’s pressing issues. Sasha, who marries into wealth, is the reader’s ambassador to a world of Brooklyn Heights brownstones and prenuptial agreements. Among the more compelling family members is Georgiana, who learns that her trust fund is far more staggering than she had thought. An ill-fated romance helps her realize that she needs to do something more with her money than join the world of the ladies who lunch. Sometimes, a story of high-end real estate and mistrustful sisters-in-law is just the thing you need.

title: Broadway Bodies: A Critical History of Conformity
author: Ryan Donovan
description: So much of the casting for Broadway musicals depends not on voice range or mastery of dance styles, but on the height, thinness, age, color, ethnicity and lack of disability of certain bodies. Ryan Donovan, an assistant professor of theater studies at Duke, examines the history of the “Broadway Body” over the last 50 years, looking at questions like: Why do some shows use fat suits instead of casting heavier actors? Why are there so many disabled characters in musical theater and so few disabled actors? And most crucially: Is casting based on body shape/size/ethnicity/color actually crucial to the work onstage, or is it just a form of entrenched bias and legalized discrimination?

title: The Porcelain Moon: A Novel of France, the Great War, and Forbidden Love
author: Janie Chang
description: Set against the backdrop of World War I in France, Janie Chang’s latest novel, The Porcelain Moon, tells the story of two women, one French, one Chinese, who break generational expectations and restrictions to find the love – and the lives – that they want. Camille, raised outside Paris in genteel poverty in the shadow of family money long gone, is trying to escape a violent marriage; and Pauline, who works for her uncle at his Paris shop, is trying to avoid an arranged marriage in Shanghai. Their lives intersect in a heart-pounding way amid the war. I love Chang’s richly researched historical fiction books, and this one doesn’t disappoint. She draws from Chinese legends and her family’s history to weave tales of love, familial obligations and freedom that will have you hooked.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: Asian Americans in an Anti-Black World
author: Claire Jean Kim
description: The latest book from political scholar Claire Jean Kim is a razor-sharp retelling of Asian American history, one in which Asian Americans have not merely struggled against white supremacy but have often perpetrated – and benefited from – anti-Blackness. Kim traces these reciprocal strains of racism across 200 years of Asians in America, from Chinese exclusion to Japanese American incarceration during World War II to our present-day clashes over affirmative action. The story of race and rights in the United States cannot be told without Asian Americans. This book will show you why.

title: The Great White Bard: How to Love Shakespeare While Talking About Race
author: Farah Karim-Cooper
description: This blend of scholarly research and accessible writing helps readers approach Shakespeare with new insight. Farah Karim-Cooper, a professor at King’s College London and education director at Shakespeare’s Globe Theatre, unpacks some of the more problematic elements of race, misogyny and otherness in plays like Othello, Antony and Cleopatra and Merchant of Venice, as well as in the very language Shakespeare uses in his comedies and sonnets: white versus Black, fair versus brown. Using a contemporary lens on a playwright over 400 years after his death, the book unlocks, as Karim-Cooper told me in an interview, “Shakespeare’s capacity to make sense in the world today.” Highly recommended.

title: Every Rising Sun
author: Jamila Ahmed
description: Jamila Ahmed’s well-crafted debut is a revisionist retelling of One Thousand and One Nights, foregrounding Scheherazade in a real historical period and even allowing her a different ending. It’s one thing to boldly take on this much-debated, well-known literary classic. To then turn its conventional – and, let’s be honest, tired – tropes upside down and inside out is a literary feat deserving of our recognition and respect. Ahmed’s Scheherazade subverts the patriarchal codes of her time, wielding stories as beautifully forged weapons to fashion her own path. Following her pioneering journey, we rediscover the many gemlike truths and pleasures of those quintessential stories.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Kantika
author: Elizabeth Graver
description: Rebecca Cohen grows up in a well-to-do family, sheltered from political turmoil in interbellum Constantinople/Istanbul, but after her father’s business is requisitioned, they fall on hard times and try their luck in Barcelona, Spain. In a land that expelled her Sephardic ancestors, a pig’s head is thrown into their garden and she adopts a Catholic name to work as a seamstress. When she moves to Queens, N.Y., for an arranged marriage, society is more welcoming, but she yearns for an idealized version of her birthplace. Kantika was inspired by the experiences of the author’s grandmother, and it’s a highly readable and poignant tale of dislocation, refuge and resilience as a Jewish woman struggles to build a new life in two foreign lands.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: America the Beautiful?: One Woman in a Borrowed Prius on the Road Most Traveled
author: Blythe Roberson
description: Blythe Roberson quits her day job to go on the classic American rite of passage: the cross-country road trip. She structures her solitary trip around national parks where she can complete junior ranger badges (an aspiration I now hold near, as far too many of my friends are probably willing to attest). Roberson discusses ecotourism, conservation, class, politics, sex, gender and the beautiful, varied nature of the national parks. Roberson’s travelogue had me cracking up. She’s funny, engaging and opinionated, and I’ve bought a second copy because so many of my friends borrowed my first.

title: The Salt Grows Heavy
author: Cassandra Khaw
description: A voiceless mermaid flees her prince’s kingdom, with a masked plague doctor, after the kingdom is devoured and destroyed by her monstrous progeny. Instead of safety, they find a violent cult of children presided over by three surgeon-saints who claim control over life itself. Here is hunger, violence and beauty. Here is prose that gnaws at you; you feel it in your gut. Cassandra Khaw neatly vivisects fairy tales to create something as gorgeous as it is gory – and it is gory. There’s meat to sink your teeth into in this fierce, elegant abattoir of a story. But dig through the viscera, and you’ll find something surprisingly tender and hopeful.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Nestlings
author: Nat Cassidy
description: Ana and Reid are dealing with a lot – first-time parenthood, Ana’s paralysis, postpartum depression, loss of income, the recent death of Reid’s mother, an antisemitic landlord and more – when they win a life-changing housing lottery. It seems like their luck is turning. But it isn’t. The home – the Deptford – with its secret histories, gargoyles, labyrinthine hallways, green-in-winter courtyard, is as memorable as literature’s well-known Overlook Hotel and Hill House. Nat Cassidy is good at revealing just enough to disquiet, steadily ratcheting up dread and “too late?” anxiety until you don’t know what the right choices are. This unusual vampire novel asks what is the price of belonging; how much is survival worth. No easy answers here.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Dark"]

title: Thornhedge
author: T. Kingfisher
description: Prepare to be delighted and enchanted with T. Kingfisher’s revisiting of “Sleeping Beauty.” Toadling, stolen and raised in fairyland by water fairies, was given a task in the human world. Centuries later, she is still determined to keep her world and the human world separate. Enter Halim, a curious knight after the truth of the tale. What follows is a moving story of connection, responsibility, belonging and unexpected friendship. Kingfisher’s fairyland is full of teeth, wonder and horror; what’s dark is very dark, but what’s good will give you warmth. Toadling is so charming a heroine I won’t soon forget her; by the last page, my heart was mended.

title: Burn It Down: Power, Complicity, and a Call for Change in Hollywood
author: Maureen Ryan
description: I read a ton of nonfiction about Hollywood, and this book is essential reading for all viewers of TV and film. It would be trite to boil down Maureen Ryan’s thesis to: All your faves are problematic. But her meticulous and essential reporting on shows I love, including Saturday Night Live and Lost, helped me better understand how Hollywood institutions reinforce a system complicit in abuse, at the expense of women and people of color. But this book isn’t a slog. Ryan’s writing is passionate and personal, and I found Burn It Down to be a life-changing read.

title: We Were Once a Family: A Story of Love, Death, and Child Removal in America
author: Roxanna Asgarian
description: Start with a sensational case that grabbed our attention: Two mothers who crashed their SUV over a California cliff, killing themselves and their six adopted children. Then add a reporter who knows her subject in depth –  the inner-workings of America’s foster care system. Reporter Roxanna Asgarian spent nearly five years to reveal a truer story – about a system where a well-off white couple can dodge claims of abuse, but the children’s Black families had their kids taken quickly, leaving adults and siblings grieving for their broken family. Press coverage in 2018 puzzled over the motivation of the mothers. Asgarian more thoughtfully asks: What does this tragedy say about a foster care system where, in most cases, children are removed for issues of neglect and poverty, not for abuse?

title: Translation State
author: Ann Leckie
description: What do you do when a powerful, murderous alien race with which you have a peace treaty is so different from humanity that there’s not even a straightforward way to communicate? In the Imperial Radch universe, the alien race in question, the Presger, have found a way to bridge that gap through Translators, though the process of creating those Translators is guarded and mysterious. In this book that’s part space opera, part coming-of-age tale, part body horror delight, Ann Leckie explores themes of power, gender identity, family, destiny and AI through charming characters, dramatic diplomacy and nail-biting action.

title: Starling House
author: Alix E. Harrow
description: In Eden, Ky., the air is thick with dust. The dying coal town is the perfect setting for this gothic fantasy about a haunted house and the inevitable, inescapable pull of history. At the center of the story are Opal and Arthur – two lonely people shouldering the burden of a town that refuses to make peace with the past. And the romance that unfolds between them is so lovely in its yearning, it’ll make your bones ache.

title: Immortal Longings
author: Chloe Gong
description: Immortal Longings is the cutthroat fantasy novel of a Hunger Games girlie’s dreams. Chloe Gong seamlessly blends revenge, romance and power struggle in this story about a former princess who teams up with an exiled aristocrat in a competition to the death. The book’s claustrophobic setting of San-Er (inspired by the real-life Kowloon Walled City), lends a gritty realness to a world where anyone can switch bodies (yes, there’s body-jumping in this book, but weirdly it works). If you find yourself hungry for a story that will cut you while you read and where betrayal lurks around every corner, you’ll want to check this title out.

title: You, Again
author: Kate Goldbeck
description: As Marie Kondo once said, “I’m so excited, because I love mess.” This gender-swapped When Harry Met Sally retelling (she’s the commitment phobe, he’s the hopeless romantic) is for anyone who craves romance novels with realistic and flawed characters. Ari and Josh circle each other for years in the kind of delicious, slow-burn tension that only comes from a well-executed enemies-to-friends-to-lovers arc. And while they make many mistakes (so many mistakes) on their journey to one another, you can’t help but enjoy every second of the ride.

title: The Last Tale of the Flower Bride
author: Roshani Chokshi
description:
How well do you really know the person you married? That’s the question at the heart of Roshani Chokshi’s unsettlingly gorgeous adult fantasy. In the book, a man is forced to face uncomfortable truths about his mysterious wife, Indigo, on a trip to her childhood home. Indigo has always held the secrets of her past between tight fists and closed lips –  especially regarding her childhood friend Azure, who disappeared years ago. But the past has a way of coming loose –  no matter how hard you try to keep it hidden underneath the floorboards.
This book is for anyone who loves dark fairy tales and is obsessed with the monstrous nature of teenage girlhood.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Birnam Wood
author: Eleanor Catton
description: New Zealand is far away from my home, so I appreciated my jet lag-free trip there in the form of this thrilling novel about a group of young environmentalists. Mira Bunting is part of a “guerrilla gardening collective” – their activism revolves around farm projects, but there is nothing calm or pastoral about this suspenseful and tragic tale. None of the characters is spared from Eleanor Catton’s precise and devastating examinations of their motives, blind spots and manipulations. I found myself shouting warnings out loud to the characters, just like in a horror movie: Look behind you!
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Hang the Moon
author: Jeannette Walls
description: Picture it – the Jazz Age. Glamorous speakeasies. Illegal liquor. Brash bootleggers. Jeannette Walls’ Hang the Moon parachutes us into the middle of one of the most movie-ready eras in U.S. history, but instead of meeting Al Capone in NYC, we’re introduced to the fiery bootlegger Sallie Kincaid in rural Virginia. This effervescent, drama-filled page-turner barrels through a few storylines, touching on a fraught battle over family business succession, racial tension in a poor rural county, family secrets and land conflict. For Ms. Kincaid, car chases, shootouts and changing the status quo are all in a day’s work.

title: The Witching Tide
author: Margaret Meyer
description: Martha Hallybread is a woman of faith – somewhat. When a strange man rolls into her small fishing town and establishes himself as a witch hunter, that faith is tested. And so are Martha’s loyalty and sanity. Margaret Meyer’s debut novel, The Witching Tide, takes us into the middle of the terrifying witch trials in East Anglia in the 17th century. Martha is a healer and midwife who can’t speak. The town is paranoid, and as almost every woman in her once close-knit town is being accused of witchcraft, her inability to speak may save her from the noose as she cannot formally confess to the accusations of being a witch. But her saving grace becomes the final nail for her friends and neighbors when Martha is unable to use her voice to save them.

title: The Roaring Days of Zora Lily
author: Noelle Salazar
description: Who doesn’t love a story about blinding ambition and female perseverance – plus fashion, fame and fortune-hunting? Noelle Salazar tells the story of Zora Hough, a poor seamstress in 1920s Seattle, following her dream to become a world-famous fashion designer. Then, the book jumps ahead 100 years to the present day, where a dress once worn by Greta Garbo is being archived at the Smithsonian – and a conservator finds Zora’s name under the designer label. This is a captivating story about friendship, love and family duty – and how success isn’t always linear.

title: How to Say Babylon: A Memoir
author: Safiya Sinclair
description: Poet Safiya Sinclair’s How to Say Babylon is a masterpiece and should be required reading. Sinclair’s deeply personal story of self-love, family pain, cultural traditions and reckoning with post-colonial systems takes a look at the complexity of what it means to grow up Rasta. What makes this memoir special – beyond the exceptionally melodic writing – is the excavation of Jamaica’s brutal history and the thread that runs through an oppressed country, her oppressed parents, and then to her, the oppressed daughter. There were numerous attempts to silence her, but through poetry, Safiya Sinclair came out victorious – roaring from the hills like the lioness that she is.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Hey, Hun: Sales, Sisterhood, Supremacy, and the Other Lies Behind Multilevel Marketing
author: Emily Lynn Paulson
description: Emily Lynn Paulson’s memoir explores the world of multilevel marketing from the inside, where she was a successful MLM salesperson who eventually became disillusioned with the whole industry. She writes about how MLMs prey on women in particular, setting up barriers to keep sellers inside. While Paulson opens herself up to criticism about how she continued to benefit as she was in the process of leaving her MLM, she is very honest about how MLMs manipulate and gaslight their workforces.

title: How to Sell a Haunted House
author: Grady Hendrix
description: I always look forward to Grady Hendrix’s next book, and this one was another pleasure to read. After the death of her parents, Louise heads home to prepare their house for sale with the help – or lack of help – of her deadbeat brother, Mark. But there’s something waiting in that house for the next generation to come and it doesn’t want them to go. It takes a turn from the horror genre I thought we were in, but was still very enjoyable.

title: Night Vision: Seeing Ourselves through Dark Moods
author: Mariana Alessandri
description: Using Plato’s allegory of “The Cave,” Mariana Alessandri explores our culture’s obsession with “toxic positivity” and our fear of darker emotions like grief, anxiety and depression. She argues that instead of scrambling out of these moods, we should examine them as an opportunity to better understand ourselves and those around us who are experiencing their own struggles. Instead of encouraging our loved ones to “stay positive,” maybe it’s worth sitting with someone in their darkness.

title: Homestead
author: Melinda Moustakis
description: Homestead is a gripping tale of what it must have been like to be brave enough to build a life and survive in one of the world’s harshest environments long before it was cool to go “off grid.” But Melinda Moustakis also excellently captures the complexities around a moment in American history that too often is reduced to a line or two in schoolbooks – Alaskan statehood. It was during an era like now when American life seemed to be undergoing tectonic shifts. The characters in Homestead themselves are escaping tumultuous pasts largely tied to forces out of their control. Everything seems to come into focus for them only after a dramatic and tragic climax that’s just one of many reasons why you won’t want to put this novel down.

title: The Country of the Blind: A Memoir at the End of Sight
author: Andrew Leland
description: When Andrew Leland was growing up in New Mexico, he noticed that he had trouble seeing in the dark. That night blindness was the first sign of retinitis pigmentosa, a rare genetic condition that gradually narrows one’s sight. The Country of the Blind represents Leland’s personal, cultural and historical journey into “the world of blindness and what possibilities might exist there” as he comes to terms with the total blindness he knows awaits him. This artful blend of memoir and wide-ranging investigation generously invites the reader to “discover the largely invisible terrain of blindness” and sit together with Leland in uncertainty.

title: Cousins
author: Aurora Venturini, translated by Kit Maude
description: The late Argentine author Aurora Venturini wrote this brilliant, provocative novel at age 85, winning the newspaper Página/12’s 2007 New Novel award. Anglophone readers are lucky that Kit Maude’s translation has brought it to us for the first time. The story centers on four women in a poor family in provincial La Plata, Argentina, who face sexual assault, illegal abortions, exploitation and murder – extremely dark stuff. But even as Cousins settles in the muck of depravity, it is shot through with humor and formal risks that make it a propulsive read.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Brutes
author: Dizz Tate
description: On the first page of Dizz Tate’s debut novel, a 14-year-old girl has gone missing from Falls Landing, Florida, where screams from nearby theme parks ripple through the air. Instead of focusing on the missing girl, Tate makes the far more compelling choice to zoom in on her pack of 13-year-old friends who are used to blending into the background – and who know far more than the town’s adults. In plunging the reader into the girls’ collective perspective, Brutes makes for an original and stylistically ambitious take on the well-trodden subject matter of girls in peril.

title: Take What You Need
author: Idra Novey
description: Leah has settled into a worldly life in New York City when she learns that her estranged stepmother, Jean, has died in their hometown. Jean had an accident while welding steel sculptures in her living room and bequeathed her oeuvre to Leah. Take What You Need alternates between Leah’s Trump flag-lined journey home to the same insular slice of the Rust Belt where Idra Novey herself was raised, and Jean’s path to belatedly producing outsider art. The result is an odd and beautiful novel about the ineffable impulse to make art, wedged into an exploration of what happens to towns and people left behind.

title: The End of the World Is a Cul de Sac
author: Louise Kennedy
description: Whereas Louise Kennedy’s 2022 debut novel Trespasses, which was shortlisted for the Women’s Prize for Fiction, is set in Northern Ireland during the Troubles, this collection of stories mostly focuses on the lives of contemporary Irish women at moments of rupture, with settings up and down the island. It is both dark and beautiful, blending Kennedy’s lyrical descriptions of foreboding landscapes with the complex interiority of women who feel hemmed in by marriage, economics and Irish traditionalism.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Mobility
author: Lydia Kiesling
description: Lydia Kiesling pulls off a feat in this novel about fossil fuels and climate change – her sharp pairing of politics and the personal eschews the didactic but remains charged. A big part of why Mobility works so well is Kiesling’s choice of protagonist: a hapless, onetime foreign service brat deliciously named Bunny Glenn. As Bunny languishes in Azerbaijan as a 1990s teenager oblivious to the global scramble for oil, stumbles into an oil career in Texas in young adulthood and grapples with our climate-wrecked future, Kiesling explores individual complicity with late capitalism.

title: The Guest Lecture
author: Martin Riker
description: I stayed up too late reading this novel of ideas that enacts a sleepless night. Martin Riker takes us into the mind of Abby, an economist who has just been denied tenure, as she prepares to give a lecture on John Maynard Keynes. Abby mentally walks through her house, attempting to memorize her speech on Keynes’ pragmatic optimism by associating the speech’s different sections with familiar rooms. But she keeps getting sidetracked, pulled into self-recriminations and anxieties – and into imagined dialogues with the likes of Keynes himself. In Riker’s nimble hands, this insomniac’s dark night of the soul abounds with humor and feeling.

title: Prophet Song
author: Paul Lynch
description: Paul Lynch’s latest takes place in a near-future dystopian Dublin, where the Garda National Services Bureau – a secret police force – has been given seemingly boundless powers. Prophet Song centers not on how the liberal democracy got here, but on how authoritarian rule affects one family. We follow Eilish, a microbiologist and mother of four, as she struggles to keep her family together in the aftermath of her husband’s disappearance by the state. The premise alone is blood-chilling; Lynch’s narration, which unspools in long, breathless paragraphs, plunges the reader into the family’s nightmare. Prophet Song won the 2023 Booker Prize – the judges said “it is propulsive and unsparing, and it flinches away from nothing.”
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: To Name the Bigger Lie: A Memoir in Two Stories
author: Sarah Viren
description: To Name the Bigger Lie has the page-turning quality of a thriller, but instead of tracking down culprits and solving mysteries, Sarah Viren methodically untangles knotty philosophical tensions in pursuit of what is real. Viren was working on a book about her high school philosophy teacher – a man who trafficked in conspiracy theories – when her wife was anonymously accused of sexually harassing students at Arizona State University. The couple quickly discovered that the complaints were part of a smear campaign by a professional rival of Viren’s. She revised her book project, tracing both men’s lies and how they skewed her understanding of reality.

title: Disruptions: Stories
author: Steven Millhauser
description: Master of the short story Steven Millhauser unearths the surreal lurking under the mundane surface of the American suburbs in his 10th collection.
