Each of these 18 fabulist stories painstakingly constructs a world that looks much like our own, until something odd disrupts it: In one, a fad for climbing ever-taller ladders takes over a town; in another, a community of regular humans shares space with 2-inch-tall counterparts. A sense of strangeness lingers long after the final page.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: The True Love Experiment
author: Christina Lauren
description: This author duo (Christina Lauren is the pen name of the combined Christina Hobbs and Lauren Billings) knows how to write a dang love story, right down to the dedication page: “This one is a blatant love letter to our genre.” And it truly is. Prolific romance writer Fizzy Chen gets cast on a reality television show in the hopes of finding love – or at least the inspiration that’ll save her from an epic bout of writer’s block. When filming starts, sparks start flying – with the guy behind the camera. In casting an Asian American woman as their lead, the two (white) writers clearly put in the work to make Fizzy feel authentic. She’s no token character, and her complicated feelings about love and life reflect that.

title: Love and Other Flight Delays
author: Denise Williams
description: If a person is their best, most ideal self on vacation, surely the opposite is true for our airport selves. The airport is a place for sweat, schlepping and snapping at fellow travelers who don’t understand that coats do not belong in the overhead compartment! It is a deeply unromantic place, and that is why this trio of stories is so delightful. Air travel brings together such an eclectic mix of humans: the handsomely suited stranger getting off his flight, the passenger seated across the aisle on a packed flight – anything could happen! Don’t get me wrong – I love a whirlwind romance in a dreamy place, but if you can find love at baggage claim, that’s a connection worth keeping.

title: Forget Me Not
author: Julie Soto
description: It’s really hard not to fall for a romance novel where the main love interest is a brooding tattooed dude who runs a flower shop. Elliot Bloom (lol) hates being a florist, but he took over the family shop when his dad died. Meanwhile, Ama Torres is a wedding planner who doesn’t believe in marriage. And despite working in the same industry, the two have carefully avoided each other since their disastrous breakup years ago – until they’re both booked for the same wedding. This book is a love letter to beautiful and rare flowers that’ll have you running to your nearest florist – if not a tattoo artist.

title: MoneyZen: The Secret to Finding Your 'Enough'
author: Manisha Thakor with Lisa Sweetingham
description: I feel personally attacked by this book. I picked it up hoping for some helpful perspective on personal finance – and I got that. But what MoneyZen is really about is our deeply harmful obsession with work. In her decades in finance, Manisha Thakor worked herself into the ground – damaging relationships and becoming so physically sick from stress that she had to take months off. And health statistics suggest that she’s not the only one. Even if your work obsession is less severe, it’s nice to be reminded that life has value and meaning outside the workplace.

title: Safe and Sound: A Renter-Friendly Guide to Home Repair
author: Mercury Stardust
description: If you’ve ever felt out of place in the hardware store or felt too intimidated to ask for help while lost in an aisle filled with power tools and screws, may I please introduce Mercury Stardust, the Trans Handy Ma’am? Mercury’s book is an extension of her fantastic social media videos. She’s a sparkly mix of pep talk, practical knowledge (replacing a toilet seat!) and whimsy (um, hello, cat-shelf tutorial?!). If there were an Adulting 101 class, this would be required reading.

title: Much Ado About Nada
author: Uzma Jalaluddin
description: Nada and Baz have a secret past. A messy, layered one. One that ended so badly that they haven’t seen each other in years. A lot of things have ended badly for Nada – personally and professionally. The app she worked so hard to launch totally flopped. Now, nearly 30, she’s a shell of her former self. When Nada’s best friend drags her to a big Muslim conference in Toronto, she comes face-to-face with Baz. The story flips from past to present as Nada wrestles with who she and Baz once were and who they might be still. Come for the second-chance romance; stay for the deeply lovable (and chaotic) cast of characters who are Nada’s family and friends.

title: Our Hideous Progeny
author: C.E. McGill
description: The plot at the beginning of this book sounds almost slapstick: An ambitious but struggling scientist couple has a harebrained idea to construct a prehistoric sea creature – and bring it back to life. But beyond the somewhat fantastical premise lies a story of two people trying, with mixed success, to find meaning in their lives and their relationship after suffering a stillbirth. It’s a reconsideration of the Frankenstein story, of course – one that looks at what the impulse toward life can create and what we owe the creatures we’ve brought into this world, no matter how feared or misunderstood they may be.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Science", "Dark", "Family Matters"]

title: House of Cotton
author: Monica Brashears
description: In the first sentence of this book, the narrator, 19-year-old Magnolia, says she has never “felt as trapped and choked” as she does in that very moment, sitting at her grandmother’s funeral. That feeling of claustrophobia permeates the now-orphaned Magnolia’s every move – and to the reader, it gives logic to all the strange, contradictory and surreal things that will happen next, as she makes her way through the world. This is the story of someone trying to get free in a world where the paths toward freedom are winding, dark – and filled with tricksters and wolves. And Magnolia is a complex heroine, drawn to dark shadows even as she relentlessly seeks out the light.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Yellowface
author: R.F. Kuang
description: “Nail-biting thriller about the world of book publishing” is not a phrase I ever expected to use. But that’s what this book is, with the added dimension of a toxic friendship, an identity thief and, of course, some racebending. The story is glossy and fun but tackles some very serious ideas – like who owns history, what it means to grieve and where the line is between appreciation, appropriation and outright thievery. This one is hard to put down – as baffling and often pernicious as the narrator’s decisions sometimes are, I found myself unable to stop hoping, desperately, that she would eventually find the light.

title: I Have Some Questions for You
author: Rebecca Makkai
description: The stories we tell ourselves about our childhoods are often only partially true – tales we invent to make our youthful experiences make sense. But what happens when you revisit a foundational story and begin to unpack it? What happens when you realize that the people you once trusted were inventing tales of their own? This book follows a woman who travels back to teach a class at the boarding school she once attended – and to revisit the circumstances under which her former roommate died while they were at school. It’s an eerie look at how race and gender and youth shape our perceptions of guilt and innocence, and a story that reminds us that it takes more than one bad guy to get away with murder.

title: The Reformatory
author: Tananarive Due
description: It’s hard to dream up something more terrifying than real history, and Tananarive Due doesn’t try. Instead, she uses elements of the spiritual and supernatural to enhance a setting already dripping with horror – a reform school (based on a real place) where young boys are sent to repent for their youthful misdeeds. The book takes place in 1950s Florida, and the protagonist is Robbie, a young Black boy navigating racism and ghosts alike. But though the world he inhabits is often cruel and terrifying, Robbie’s story is also propelled by community, persistence, creativity and love.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana"]

title: We Are a Haunting
author: Tyriek White
description: We are a Haunting is written so vividly that it’s almost vibrating. In this multigenerational family epic, East New York – with all of its sounds, smells and pulsing sensations – feels almost as alive as the people who inhabit it. But it is, of course, the people who bring so much heart, pain, strangeness and beauty to this story. The family at its center is blessed with an otherworldly gift to connect with both the living and dead – an ability that brings surreal layers to a very real-world struggle: trying to hold on to home and community in a city that is continually crushing and displacing some of its longest-standing residents.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Americana", "Family Matters"]

title: Mass Supervision: Probation, Parole, and the Illusion of Safety and Freedom
author: Vincent Schiraldi
description: Mass Supervision is a hard book to categorize. It’s part policy argument, part memoir, part historical analysis. In other hands, that combination could result in something unbearably wonky or disjointed. But Vincent Schiraldi makes it work with a fascinating – sometimes devastating – argument: that people who care about mass incarceration in the U.S. should also be concerned about the way that probation and parole ensnare an enormous, vulnerable population into cycles of exploitation, surveillance and poverty.

title: American Whitelash: A Changing Nation and the Cost of Progress
author: Wesley Lowery
description: Sticks and stones may break my bones, but words are the tool used to convince people to pick up the sticks and stones. That’s the argument – girded by reporting and historical research – that Wesley Lowery makes in this book. He looks at the rhetoric that politicians and the media use to describe different groups, including immigrants, people of color, Jews and Muslims. Then, he draws direct connections between that language and some of the deadly violence that has been visited on those communities, with anecdotes from various points throughout U.S. history.

title: Anansi's Gold: The Man Who Looted the West, Outfoxed Washington, and Swindled the World
author: Yepoka Yeebo
description: Catch Me if You Can meets Coming to America in this epic tale of one of the greatest scammers of all time. It’s the (true) story of a Ghanaian con artist who traveled the world spinning tales that wove together history, desire, corruption and hope of a fabulous wealth. And even though you know, from the beginning, that these tales aren’t true, his misadventures are so compellingly catalogued that when all is said and done, you may forget whom you should be rooting for.

title: Dragon Palace
author: Hiromi Kawakami, translated by Ted Goossen
description: From the author of the bestseller Strange Weather in Tokyo, this new story collection goes further than just strange. Reading Hiromi Kawakami’s stories is a bit like riding a dragon – you’ll have to hold on tight and go with it. Here are just a few of the unforgettable characters: a great-grandmother who was a sex-loving religious leader, an office-working mole married to a human, and a housewife who eats stucco and nurses a god in her kitchen. Kawakami’s work always challenges our society’s expectations of women.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Mater 2-10
author: Hwang Sok-yong, translated by Sora Kim-Russell and Youngjae Josephine Bae
description: This nearly 500-page novel opens with a laid-off railroad worker in Seoul camped out on a platform atop a factory chimney, where he will stay for 410 consecutive days in protest. As he braves the elements, his ancestors, also railroad workers, visit to relive the murders, imprisonment and torture they endured under Japanese and U.S. occupation while fighting for better working conditions. The Nobel Prize in literature almost always goes to a European, but for the next one that’s awarded to a non-European, I’m rooting for Hwang Sok-yong, perhaps South Korea’s most renowned author.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Girlfriends
author: Emily Zhou
description: I can’t believe this is Emily Zhou’s debut – it’s one of the most well-crafted short story collections I’ve had the pleasure of reading. Girlfriends is a set of seven stories centered on trans women navigating the business of their lives. In the mundanity of odd jobs, roommate drama and new crushes, Zhou captures her characters in moments of change – subtle ones that are no less profound for their quietness. With its cutting wit and clear prose, Girlfriends is well worth a read.

title: Idlewild
author: James Frankie Thomas
description: Idlewild is a devastating debut novel about two adults looking back on the dissolution of their teenage friendship. Nell is a lesbian and deeply into her best friend, Fay; Fay is deeply into gay men but doesn’t think becoming one herself is possible. When a pair of sexually ambiguous boys join their quirky Quaker school, Nell and Fay become obsessed with them – and the four entangle into an inevitable implosion. I’m a gay trans guy; Fay is too. Watching both Nell and Fay hurt one another as they refuse to see the truth of that was painful to read. Even through the pain, I felt so protective of and tenderly for these two characters – and also for my younger self.

title: Feed Them Silence
author: Lee Mandelo
description: I love books about people who kind of suck – especially gay people who kind of suck – and this tight little speculative novella really did the trick for me. It’s about a harried lesbian academic whose research involves using technology to mind-meld with a she-wolf. As her obsession with the wolf deepens, she tries to hold off both the ethical quandaries associated with her work and the breakdown of her relationship with her wife. Feed Them Silence is a creative exploration of belonging and the burdens of humanity with an incredible amount of depth, given its short length.

title: On a Woman’s Madness
author: Astrid Roemer, translated by Lucy Scott
description: In this queer Dutch classic, a young Surinamese woman named Noenka leaves her brief marriage for a voyage of self-discovery, described in gorgeous (and often steamy!) sensory detail. Astrid Roemer’s writing, in Lucy Scott’s new translation, is lush and sinuous, turning quiet domestic scenes into dreamscapes and cranking up the volume on the many love stories this novel contains. It has taken far too long for On a Woman’s Madness to make it into English – don’t miss it now that it’s here!
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Temple Folk
author: Aaliyah Bilal
description: Temple Folk is a testament to the power of short stories to illuminate the human condition. It offers an exploration of the lives of Black Muslims in America and a nuanced and multifaceted portrayal of their experiences with faith, family and identity in a society that often seeks to marginalize and define them. In a collection of deeply personal and universally relatable stories, Aaliyah Bilal offers poignant and insightful glimpses into the lives of individuals through lyrical and evocative prose, capturing the essence of her characters’ lives with a poetic sensibility.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Evil Eye
author: Etaf Rum
description: This engrossing novel delves into the complexities of Palestinian American women’s experiences through its protagonist, Yara, whose journey of self-discovery and liberation is a powerful story of resilience and determination as she confronts societal expectations and her internal struggles. Yara struggles to feel fulfilled, while achieving success in her marriage and career. The novel sheds light on broader themes of displacement, cultural identity and the ongoing struggle for Palestinian self-determination. It’s a valuable contribution to the continuing discourse on gender, identity and the enduring spirit of a people in the face of adversity.

title: Hijab Butch Blues: A Memoir
author: Lamya H
description: Hijab Butch Blues is a raw and relatable memoir that challenges societal norms and expectations. Through personal experiences, Lamya H navigates the complexities of gender, sexuality and faith as a queer, nonbinary Muslim. The book is a manifesto for self-acceptance and a call to action for dismantling rigid binaries. It’s a powerful reminder that we are all capable of defying labels and embracing our true selves.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Starter Villain
author: John Scalzi
description:
The cover of John Scalzi’s Starter Villain features a cat wearing a suit – blue tie, white shirt, dark jacket – and yes, believe it or not, there’s a good reason.
Charlie isn’t particularly close to his uncle Jake, so he hears about Jake’s death on TV. He later learns that Jake has left him a business – a supervillain business. Jake was part of a sort of supervillain secret society, and he had enemies. Now, those enemies are Jake’s problem. Trying to explain where the cats fit in, or the dolphins’ work stoppage, or the volcano? It’s all too much. But Scalzi’s command of the balance between a genuinely compelling story and infectiously whimsical imagery means that it doesn’t really matter if it doesn’t make logical sense. It’s just a delight.

title: Prom Mom: A Thriller
author: Laura Lippman
description: A Laura Lippman book can be expected to tease and twist deliciously, even when, as Prom Mom is, it’s set during the difficult early months of COVID-19 and springs from the real stories of high school teens who don’t even know they’re pregnant until they go into labor. Amber, who once was at the center of such a story and suffered the consequences, crosses paths years later with Joe, who was the indifferent father of her baby. Now married and uninterested in revisiting the past, he is initially not eager to renew their acquaintance. But they both have secrets, and they both have fears, and they begin to talk. What unfolds is a tale of betrayal, resentment, reckoning and being haunted by someone who is absolutely sure he is a good guy.

title: Beware the Woman
author: Megan Abbott
description: Megan Abbott writes about women and the things they need, love and fear. In Beware the Woman, a pregnant woman named Jacy goes with her new husband for a vacation with his doctor father at the family cabin in the woods. She begins to realize that something is wrong, then very wrong, as the men’s concern for her safety teeters on the edge of becoming a kind of entitlement to her body. Abbott writes gripping thrillers, and a story about how pregnancy intersects with privacy, self-determination and safety – even for women in “good” situations – could not have come at a better time.

title: Oscar Wars: A History of Hollywood in Gold, Sweat, and Tears
author: Michael Schulman
description: Michael Schulman’s history of the Oscars doubles as a breezy history of the whole movie business – full of gossipy and delicious anecdotes – as well as a collection of perhaps forgotten truths about how the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences began and how it has changed. Who cared about winning and who didn’t, which surprises shook careers for good and for ill, and which campaigns majestically backfired? There is Harvey Weinstein, there are good and terrible ceremonies – including, yes, the botched Moonlight victory – and there is the counterculture that movies like Easy Rider represented. Schulman considers the awards’ terrible history with race through the stories of Hattie McDaniel, Sidney Poitier and Halle Berry, and he zooms in on the blacklist that ended careers. The book is both juicy and informative.

title: Ruby Lost and Found
author: Christina Li
description: l love intergenerational stories, and this one does not disappoint. After the loss of her beloved grandfather, Ruby feels unmoored. Trouble at school lands the 13-year-old at her grandmother’s senior center for the summer. And then when her family’s favorite Chinese bakery is in danger of closing, Ruby becomes determined to find a way to keep it open. Her search for answers leads to unexpected realizations in this lovely novel about heartbreak, resilience, and hope. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Mona Lisa Vanishes: A Legendary Painter, a Shocking Heist, and the Birth of a Global Celebrity
author: Nicholas Day, illustrated by Brett Helquist
description: Smile! This isn’t just a whodunit – it’s also a delightful, deceitful, and uproarious how-done-it?! In 1911, when Leonardo da Vinci’s Mona Lisa disappears from the Louvre, speculation about the mastermind behind the missing 16th century masterpiece runs rampant. Toggling back and forth between the heist and the life of the painting’s creator, we meet surprising suspects like Pablo Picasso, and follow wild leads and false accusations in this true-life art romp. Brett Helquist’s witty and wonderful black and white art heightens Nicholas Day’s fascinating narrative to make this thriller the real deal. (For ages 10-14)

title: Stolen
author: Ann-Helén Laestadius, translated by Rachel Willson-Broyles
description: Nine-year-old Elsa witnesses the slaughter of her reindeer – a hate crime against the Indigenous Sámi community – and is threatened into silence by the killer. But when he strikes again a decade later, she is ready to take down both the killer and the criminal justice system that allowed him to terrorize her people with impunity. This coming-of-age thriller explores themes of white male entitlement, rural despair, generational trauma, colonialism and gatekeeping with a nuance that makes you forget its Swedish setting. Scandinavia, it turns out, is not as cozy-cabin-core as Americans like to imagine. In fact, it’s just like us.

title: Landscapes
author: Christine Lai
description: In the last days of Mornington Hall, an ancient family estate in a futuristic Britain demolished by earthquakes and climate change, Penelope finishes archiving the contents of a once-grand library. Disaster refugees have joined them to make Mornington a last-ditch respite. But Penelope and her partner Aidan are out of money and time, and the estate has been sold. Working against time to catalog artists and storytellers before the building is demolished, her archive becomes a diary of solace and poignant recollection until she learns Julian – Aidan’s brother and Penelope’s rapist – is returning to see his former home one last time. As she writes an elegy of preservation, Penelope navigates a prosaic detour around her future shock.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Art", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: The Wind Knows My Name
author: Isabel Allende, translated by Frances Riddle
description: When I learned Isabel Allende’s latest novel is set in my hometown of Nogales, Ariz., I put it on my reading list. I wanted to know: Is her story as dearly held as my memory of a childhood of blended language and culture, of my grandmother’s whistled greetings to her neighbors? Children trapped by geopolitical violence and left to navigate immigration by themselves are the inspiration for this story. The novel is a powerful love letter to them. Their catharsis is both reference and refrain, evoked as a whistling in the dark of a forever song of hope. You can hear it in these pages, as I once did in the resilient neighborhoods of Nogales.

title: Transplant: A Memoir
author: Bernardine Watson
description: Bernardine Watson’s memoir about her journey through family estrangement, life-threatening kidney disease, three marriages, raising a son and an impressive career consulting for foundations and nonprofits is a page-turner. Watson insists the reader understand that kidney disease afflicts Black people and people of color in outsize numbers in the U.S. and that our health system continues to fail us. She tells a difficult story while threading affirmation and positivity through to finding her true love. Most important are her clear-eyed explanations about the complexity and challenges of kidney disease, all too ordinary in America.

title: Master Slave Husband Wife: An Epic Journey From Slavery to Freedom
author: Ilyon Woo
description: This book tells the story of Ellen and William Craft, who staged a life-threatening escape from enslavement in Georgia in 1848 and spent the rest of their lives advocating for Black freedom. Never far from the slave hunter’s maw, the Crafts joined the abolition movement and made tremendous contributions both in the United States and in England, speaking alongside great activists such as Frederick Douglass. Ilyon Woo’s ability to weave the Crafts’ personal love story and their love of family, alongside their public speaking and travel epic, makes for a gripping read. Kudos to Woo, for bringing this courageous couple to light.

title: The Boy From Kyiv: Alexei Ratmansky's Life in Ballet
author: Marina Harss
description: Dance writer Marina Harss follows famed Ukrainian Russian choreographer Alexei Ratmansky from birth to the present day in a revelatory book about how he evolved into the internationally sought-after choreographer of the moment. Illustrated with beautiful photographs, this book is a must-read for balletomanes keen to understand Ratmansky’s Russian, Canadian and Danish influences, as well as his full-bore immersion into American ballet. It is also for readers who want to learn how a world-spanning dance career that includes two sides of an ongoing war unfolds.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Laughter
author: Sonora Jha
description: Sonora Jha’s fast-paced campus drama turns the tables upside down in this well-traveled genre. Jha deploys a clueless narrator, Dr. Oliver Harding, who becomes smitten with Ruhaba Khan, a Pakistani law professor. Jha gets inside the hubristic Dr. Harding’s head to show his obliviousness to his white privilege, his antiquated views on race, his impact on others and, ultimately, his fragile position on campus. The “laughter” is in Professor Khan’s sense of humor, but more so in the author’s own dark humor.

title: Small Mercies: A Detective Mystery
author: Dennis Lehane
description: Within just a few pages, you are submerged in the prejudice, hypocrisy, violence, anger, hatred and despair of South Boston festering in the hot summer of 1974. The integration of its schools through forced busing is looming when Mary Pat’s daughter goes missing. As she searches, Mary Pat confronts painful truths about herself, her world and the people who dominate it. While this evocative, awfully contemporary story makes you wonder whether we ever truly will be capable of change, it also offers small mercies that will linger with you for a long time to come.

title: Fourth Wing
author: Rebecca Yarros
description: Rebecca Yarros’ Fourth Wing checks a lot of the boxes that readers are going cuckoo for these days – enemies to lovers, love triangles, sorting rituals, underdogs overcoming, etc. – but mix in some dragon riding and this book takes off. After training her whole life to spend the rest of her days with books and maps, Violet Sorrengail is forced (by her commanding general mother) to join the ferocious and enemy-ridden dragon riders instead. You may think you know what’s going to happen next, and then something new lands just in time to catch you off guard. Fourth Wing will have you ninth in your library’s hold line for the sequel (like I was), dying for more!

title: Absolution
author: Alice McDermott
description: In Absolution, Alice McDermott transports her signature characters – Irish American, Catholic, working-class New Yorkers – to Vietnam, circa 1963. Tricia Kelly is a shy newlywed who aspires “to be a helpmeet for my husband” – an engineer allegedly working for “navy intelligence.” Charlene is a strawberry-blond dynamo of a corporate wife who conscripts Tricia into her charity projects in hospitals and orphanages. McDermott brilliantly evokes the early buildup of American involvement in Vietnam as witnessed by some “minor characters” – expat wives mostly oblivious to the deeper machinations of their husbands and country.

title: Monsters: A Fan's Dilemma
author: Claire Dederer
description: What do we do with great art made by men (it’s mostly men) who’ve done bad things? Men like Pablo Picasso, Norman Mailer, Ernest Hemingway, Roman Polanski, Miles Davis and Woody Allen, to name but a few. Do we put blinders on and just focus on the work? Do geniuses, as Monsters asks, get a “hall pass” for their behavior? Or do we “cancel” the art of men who’ve been “monstrous” in their behavior? Claire Dederer, who started out as a film critic, writes with wit and passion in this slim, smart book.

title: The Stolen Coast
author: Dwyer Murphy
description: There’s a noir vibe to this tale of a femme fatale who returns to trouble the guy she left behind and perhaps sets up for a final fall. The Stolen Coast is set in a dingy seaside town in Massachusetts, with a harbor “shaped like a teardrop.” Our potential patsy is Jack Betancourt, nicknamed “the ferryman” because he ferries people on the run into new lives. While his clients’ false IDs are being made, Jack stows them away in vacation cottages. Whether Jack himself will end up “stowed away” is a question Dwyer Murphy keeps aloft until the final pages.

title: This Other Eden
author: Paul Harding
description: This Other Eden is inspired by the real-life events on Malaga Island, Maine, which, from roughly the Civil War era to 1912, was home to an interracial fishing community. After government officials inspected the island in 1911, Malaga’s 47 residents, including children, were forcibly removed, with some rehoused in institutions for the “feebleminded.” In 2010, the state of Maine offered an apology. You could imagine lots of ways a historical novel about these terrible events might be written, but none of them would give you a sense of the strange spell of This Other Eden – its dynamism, bravado and melancholy.

title: Young and Restless: The Girls Who Sparked America’s Revolutions
author: Mattie Kahn
description: Claudette Colvin. Anna Elizabeth Dickinson. Mabel Ping-Hua Lee. Alice de Rivera. You may not know these names, but you certainly know the revolutions they sparked – for equal rights, for abolition, for equal access to the ballot, for gender equality in schools. They are just a few of the fearless teenage girls whose defiance and determination changed the world. This riveting and well-researched book invites us to reexamine the history we’ve been taught and to view teenage girls as a powerful force for change.

title: Hestia Strikes a Match
author: Christine Grillo
description: Imagine this: It is Civil War time in the precarious border state of Maryland. A young woman is left to support herself when her husband leaves to fight. But this isn’t 1863. It’s today, and that Civil War? It’s happening in modern times. Hestia Harris is looking for love and living her life in Baltimore against a backdrop of civil unrest that has seen the U.S. divided into two separate countries, with Hestia living in the progressive north. It’s a modern girl-on-the-go story with a dark side, as frequent bombings make going out to dinner quite challenging. The work is funny, the protagonist is relatable and the concept is uncomfortably close to home – in a good way.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Humor & Comedy", "Americana"]

title: Tom Lake
author: Ann Patchett
description: In Tom Lake, a mother uses the long days of pandemic lockdown to tell her adult daughters about her showmance with an actor (who goes on to be famous) back in her summer-stock days. There’s drama; there’s summer love; there are questions about how a moment can impact your life’s trajectory. It will make younger readers question what path is in front of them and make older readers feel appreciative of the road they’ve already taken. This novel is why Ann Patchett is a master.

title: The Frozen River
author: Ariel Lawhon
description: Dig your crime novels blanketed with a late 18th-century, New England snow? How about with a capable, middle-aged midwife in the role of detective, telling the men in power things they absolutely do not want to hear? This compelling story begins in a river community in Maine with a body frozen in the ice; it unspools with the alleged assault of a minister’s wife. This is a most uncozy mystery that addresses the unbalanced power dynamics of men and women, rich and poor. Bonus: The character of the midwife and some plot points are based on a real person, Martha Ballard. Not quite true crime but true enough!

title: The God of Good Looks
author: Breanne Mc Ivor
description: Bianca Bridge had an affair with a married Trinidadian government minister, and the resulting scandal has left her blackballed. Unable to get a job in journalism, she’s had to parlay her looks into modeling gigs she finds necessary but demeaning. But an up-and-coming makeup mogul sees something in Bianca that will help advance his struggling company’s fortunes, if only the two can get along! What seems at first like a light, frothy romance goes deeper into the beauty industry, sexism and class while also being witty along the way.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Wreck: A Daughter's Memoir of Becoming a Mother
author: Cassandra Jackson
description: Fertility treatments can feel incredibly dehumanizing, as well as core-shaking. For Cassandra Jackson, they also became clarifying. Her desire for a biological child was intertwined with the incredible loss her father had suffered years before her birth: the deaths of five members of his family in a terrible car accident in rural Alabama. The tragedy haunted him for decades, but emboldened to learn more about it, she becomes closer to him. The wreck isn’t just the accident, it’s the emotional wreckage left behind and the incredible legacy of resilience that one Black father gives to his daughter.

title: North Woods
author: Daniel Mason
description: A little patch of land in Massachusetts sees a cabin built, an apple orchard grown, a rich man’s getaway: The property changes hands and generations, decades and centuries, retaining some wildness but never really belonging to anyone but itself and its ghosts. Daniel Mason’s North Woods is a profoundly good – and fun – read, especially the chapter about the sexy beetles!

title: The Librarianist
author: Patrick deWitt
description: Bob Comet has led a quiet life among the stacks of the libraries of Portland, Ore., and now he’s leading an even quieter, unremarkable retirement. A chance encounter at a 7-Eleven with a resident who cannot communicate leads him to her senior center, where he begins to find a community. Quirky, at times absurd, Patrick deWitt’s novel begs of us to consider the interior lives and needs of introverts, with the added idea that it’s never too late to have a rich life.

title: Spare
author: Prince Harry, The Duke of Sussex
description: What was behind Prince Harry’s decision to step back from royal duties? How bad did the relationship between him and his brother and father get? What was the deal with the frostbit todger? One more question: Who cares? Don’t read Harry’s memoir to answer those questions; instead, come for a well-told (kudos to ghostwriter J.R. Moehringer) coming-of-age story about a boy born in a fishbowl who has to overcome one of the world’s most famous dysfunctional family dynamics in order to become his own man.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Five Sorrowful Mysteries of Andy Africa
author: Stephen Buoro
description: Andy Aziza’s life is at an inflection point, but like most teenagers, he doesn’t recognize it. He lives in northern Nigeria with a mother he’s at odds with, writes poetry, and hangs out with his besties, dreaming of the West and lusting for blond women. Then violence erupts at a church event, and everything changes. This novel shimmers with the wit and desires of a 15-year-old who doesn’t fully grasp how complex his family and country are and who isn’t yet mature enough to make good decisions. Andy will make you laugh, and then he will break your heart.

title: Edith Holler
author: Edward Carey
description: Do not, no matter how hungry, eat the beetle spread! It’s the fictional condiment of the city of Norwich, made (supposedly) from ground deathwatch beetles. And 12-year-old Edith Holler, cursed never to leave her family’s Victorian theater lest she die, suspects there’s something ghastly ground into the spread. When she tries to communicate this to the city, everything goes sideways: A mysterious stepmother with suspiciously sharp teeth arrives, a stage collapses, uncles disappear, and beetles, beetles, beetles are everywhere! Edward Carey excels in writing – and drawing! – peculiar characters, and the cast he creates for the macabre and fun Edith Holler is no exception.

title: Someone Else's Shoes
author: Jojo Moyes
description: Nisha Cantor and Sam Kemp are women with seemingly nothing in common, apart from both being at crisis-y crossroads in their lives. Sam is struggling with her marriage and career, and Nisha’s entire lifestyle is in jeopardy. Their paths intersect only when Sam accidentally grabs Nisha’s similar-looking gym bag in a locker room mix-up. The bag, of course, contains the MacGuffin: the titular shoes, which Nisha desperately needs to get back. The ensuing journey is equal parts fun, clever caper and sweet ode to unlikely friendships, new chapters and finding out what happens when you walk in … well, you know …

title: Family Meal
author: Bryan Washington
description: Bryan Washington was still in his 20s when he published his first two critically acclaimed books, Lot and Memorial. Those were brilliant; his latest novel is somehow even better. The book follows Cam, who moves back to Houston from Los Angeles after the death of his beloved partner; he reconnects with an old friend, TJ, and takes a job at TJ’s family bakery. Washington writes about difficult subjects — mourning, sexuality, estrangement – with an intelligence and grace well beyond his years. It’s a stunning look at what it really means to be family.

title: Our Migrant Souls: A Meditation on Race and the Meanings and Myths of “Latino”
author: Héctor Tobar
description: What does it mean to be Latino? Los Angeles author Héctor Tobar considers the question in his latest, inspired by his years teaching English and Chicano/Latino studies at the University of California, Irvine. Tobar writes movingly about his own childhood, as the son of Guatemalan immigrants, and takes fascinating detours into history, immigration and popular culture. An essay collection with elements of memoir, this book is brilliantly structured and filled with Tobar’s illuminating insights – it’s a must-read for anyone interested in what makes America what it is.

title: Sing Her Down
author: Ivy Pochoda
description: Meet Florence “Florida” Baum, imprisoned in Arizona for a crime she hasn’t quite come to terms with. She makes a jailhouse enemy in Diosmary “Dios” Sandoval, who thinks Florida has a darker heart than she lets on. When the two are released from prison at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, Florida sets out for California, with Dios on her trail, determined to make her take responsibility for her actions. This is a taut, violent Western thriller that never lets up – and it’s definitely not for the squeamish.

title: The Great Displacement: Climate Change and the Next American Migration
author: Jake Bittle
description: Climate change is reshaping the world, leading to rising temperatures and sea levels, melting glaciers and increasingly severe droughts. It’s also changing the U.S. in another way, as Americans move from their hometowns to other areas, hoping to escape the meteorological threats that leave them vulnerable. Jake Bittle is an empathetic writer with a real gift for explaining the fraught issues that make the climate crisis so complex, and this is a fascinating look at how America has changed, and will continue to change, as the climate crisis wreaks havoc on the nation and the people who live there.

title: Witness: Stories
author: Jamel Brinkley
description: Jamel Brinkley made a splash in the literary world with his debut short story collection, A Lucky Man. His follow-up collection, set in a changing New York City, contains 10 stories about people who choose to speak on behalf of others – or fail to do so. Brinkley tackles several themes in the book, including community, responsibility and grief, and he does so beautifully, with assured prose and artful dialogue that rings true, sometimes painfully so. This is a brilliant collection that offers hope, but not at the expense of realism.

title: The New Earth
author: Jess Row
description: Jess Row’s timely novel follows a family that has weathered several implosions. Sandy and Naomi have recently separated, with the former impulsively abandoning his job and the latter living with her new partner. Their two surviving children, Patrick and Winter, are haunted by the loss of their sister, a peace activist who was killed by an Israeli sniper in the West Bank. This is a modern epic that takes an unsparing look at family and national dynamics that nobody really wants to confront. It’s ambitious and magnificent, the rare swing for the fences that actually connects.

title: Gone to the Wolves
author: John Wray
description: You don’t have to be a headbanger to love this novel about three friends involved in the heavy metal scene of the 1980s and ’90s. There’s Kip, a Florida misfit turned rock journalist; Leslie, a bisexual Black man whose life is nearly derailed by substance use; and Kira, a bartender who vanishes on a trip to Europe. Kip and Leslie reunite to search for Kira, but it’s not an easy task. John Wray perfectly captures the spirit of late-20th-century metal in this novel that manages to be both funny and disturbing.

title: Mr. Texas
author: Lawrence Wright
description: It ain’t bragging if it’s true: Everything’s bigger down here in Texas. (Pipe down, Alaska, we don’t want to hear it.) That includes political dysfunction, as veteran Texas author Lawrence Wright knows well. His new novel follows Sonny Lamb, a hapless cattle rancher recruited by Texas Republicans to run for the state legislature after the Democratic incumbent dies. He’s elected and soon finds himself resisting his party’s attempts to control him. It’s sharp, warmhearted and funny as hell – if only the late Molly Ivins were around to enjoy it.

title: Wellness
author: Nathan Hill
description: Nathan Hill’s brilliant second novel follows Jack and Elizabeth, a couple staring down the advent of their middle age and trying to find deliverance in upgraded living quarters, life hacks and new adventures, all hoping it will make them and their marriage better. This is a perfect novel for our age, filled with a deep awareness of the internet-poisoned, marketing-driven engineered emptiness of modern times, but also with a compassionate optimism about our ability to find and maintain love nonetheless. It’s a monumental achievement: a masterpiece by an author who has, in the space of two novels, become indispensable.

title: The Bee Sting
author: Paul Murray
description: Fans of Paul Murray’s hilarious 2010 novel, Skippy Dies, might be shocked by his latest – this is a dark story about the dissolution of four members of an Irish family contending with economic trouble, alcohol abuse and violence, among other notably non-funny subjects. The novel spans more than 650 pages and it’s frequently heartbreaking, but Murray’s beautiful prose – the best of his career so far – and pitch-black sense of humor make it fly by. It might be dark, but it’s definitely a trip worth taking.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Old God's Time
author: Sebastian Barry
description: Sebastian Barry’s latest novel follows Tom Kettle, a 66-year-old Irishman who has retired from his career as a police detective and moved to a small lean-to adjoining a castle in the town of Dalkey. He’s still haunted by an old case involving a murdered priest, as well as his own demons, and he’s not quite prepared to have to confront them all again. This is a relentlessly bleak, stunning novel about how the effects of violence and abuse can reverberate for years and across generations.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Mysteries", "Thrillers"]

title: The Vulnerables
author: Sigrid Nunez
description: After the last few years, it would be understandable if you don’t want to read another word about the COVID-19 pandemic. But it would be a shame to miss out on the latest novel from Sigrid Nunez, which follows a New York woman in the early days of lockdown who is watching an apartment and its resident macaw for an acquaintance. She ends up forming an unlikely friendship with the Gen Z college student who used to care for the bird. This one is tender without being treacly, and Nunez’s dry wit shines through.

title: This Bird Has Flown
author: Susanna Hoffs
description: This debut novel from the Bangles singer and guitarist follows Jane Start, a one-hit-wonder musician whose life is in shambles: Her boyfriend has left her for a younger woman, and at 33, she has moved back in with her parents. But her luck changes when she meets a handsome Oxford literature professor on a plane to London, and she gets an opportunity to revitalize her flagging career. Susanna Hoffs is a tremendously funny author; she writes with an amused gentleness that syncs perfectly with Jane’s frenetic, somewhat anxious personality. This novel is a winning rom-com and a charming valentine to music.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Music", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Deliver Me From Nowhere: The Making of Bruce Springsteen's Nebraska
author: Warren Zanes
description: It’s been more than 40 years since Bruce Springsteen shocked the rock music world with Nebraska, his stripped-down, mostly acoustic, folk-inspired album featuring songs about troubled people often on the wrong side of the law. Springsteen’s fellow musician, Warren Zanes, considers the iconic album in his new book, which tells the story of its history and considers the effect it had on rock music as a whole. Extensively researched and beautifully written, this book is a must for anyone who cares about American popular music, not just fans of The Boss.

title: Lou Reed: The King of New York
author: Will Hermes
description: Alternative rock pioneer Lou Reed was a notoriously slippery subject – terse and belligerent with interviewers, he jealously guarded his privacy. That’s what makes journalist Will Hermes’ biography of the Velvet Underground co-founder so remarkable: Hermes paints a vivid picture of the musician from his difficult childhood to his last days and explores Reed’s complicated relationships with drugs, music, sexuality and gender. It’s a compulsively readable and astonishingly well-researched book that’s not just for Reed’s fans, but for anyone interested in American cultural history. This is a biography with a real rock and roll heart.

title: Wednesday's Child: Stories
author: Yiyun Li
description: The short stories in Yiyun Li’s new collection focus chiefly on people trying to put themselves together after some kind of loss: dealing with anguish that takes its time, rising from its dormancy at unexpected moments. Li is a master at understanding human emotion, but her tenderness never gives way to sentimentality; her compassion, coupled with her gorgeous prose and painstaking attention to detail, is what makes these stories so beautiful, so accomplished. This is a perfect collection by a writer at the top of her game.

title: Hello Beautiful
author: Ann Napolitano
description: A tight sister posse, a neglected son who finds his purpose and his love in basketball, Chicago winters, mental health, an underachieving but lovable dad – it’s hard to figure out how Ann Napolitano packed all these ideas into this lovely, poignant story about … all of the things I just said, but she did and somehow it works. You don’t have to have a sister (or wish you did) or love basketball to enjoy this novel, but it helps.

title: The Heaven & Earth Grocery Store
author: James McBride
description: James McBride is so talented – as a jazz musician, memoirist and novelist – it’s almost annoying! But this book is so good and so special you have to forgive him. It’s set among fictionalized Black and Jewish residents in Pottstown, Pa., in the 1920s and ’30s, where a Jewish couple runs a theater and grocery store and a Black couple who works for them asks their help to care for a young deaf boy whom the authorities want to institutionalize. McBride somehow intertwines their stories in a way that is intricate and heartwarming without ever being cloying – and that still manages to be hilarious too. At a time when there is so much tension and hatred in the world, this story is a reminder of how love, respect and courage really can overcome evil.

title: The Rachel Incident
author: Caroline O'Donoghue
description: Who knew the financial crisis could be so … fun? Don’t get me wrong, The Rachel Incident gets into some heavy themes – class, sexual identity, abortion, to name a few – but this book navigates a young Irishwoman’s chaotic early 20s, during a specific economic moment, with a cozy warmth that had me laughing out loud throughout. This is one of those catch-yourself-smiling-without-realizing-it books – and one I think I’ll go back to, the next time I just want to feel like it’s all going to be OK.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Jovita Wore Pants: The Story of a Mexican Freedom Fighter
author: Aida Salazar, illustrated by Molly Mendoza
description: An inspiring true story of Jovita Valdovinos, a woman who defied the expectations of her community in order to save it. With her poetic touch, Aida Salazar shows how a rambunctious youngster (bristling against her skirts) persevered through violence and heartache to become a pants-wearing revolutionary leader standing against the Mexican government. Molly Mendoza’s artwork powerfully captures Jovita’s indomitable spirit, which feels like it is jumping feet (and pants) first off of every action-packed page. (For ages 6 to 9)

title: The Heat Will Kill You First: Life and Death on a Scorched Planet
author: Jeff Goodell
description: This was one of my more memorable reads of 2023; Jeff Goodell’s writing is so engaging. He explains the science behind climate change and its effects, taking you on a journey from the halls of power where crucial decisions are made to the front lines, through personal stories of people who have been affected by climate change, which is relatable and moving. He combines hard-hitting facts with compelling storytelling to make the consequences of our warming planet feel tangible. It’s a wake-up call to the realities we face but also a call to action, urging us to take responsibility for our planet’s future. If you want to better understand the climate crisis and to feel inspired to make a difference, and not just dwell in the doom and gloom, this book is a must-read.

title: Biography of X
author: Catherine Lacey
description: How well can anyone really know their spouse? When an enigmatic artist – who goes by the name X – dies suddenly, her wife, CM, quickly learns how little she actually knew about the woman she had spent years with. Biography of X is CM’s metafictional attempt to write, well, a biography of X, an artist with multiple personas who’d become famous for her transgressive, controversial work. Set against the backdrop of an alternate-universe United States that’s recovering from a post-World War II schism between the North and South, the book documents CM’s journey to untangle the true history of her wife’s life by interviewing people who each knew a completely different version of her. And what she uncovers, revealed in the book’s final act, left me breathless.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "History"]

title: Liliana’s Invincible Summer: A Sister’s Search for Justice
author: Cristina Rivera Garza
description: In 1990, an abusive ex-boyfriend murdered Cristina Rivera Garza’s younger sister, Liliana, in Mexico City – a crime that has tormented the author ever since. She has confronted that grief, in part, by writing this book: part memoir, part true-crime investigation, part treatise against femicide. It’s rich with details of Liliana’s history, pieced together through interviews, police reports and the archive of diary entries and letters her sister left behind. But it’s Rivera Garza’s beautiful, poetic writing that has really stayed with me; her love for her sister is so apparent it makes my heart ache – and rage – that much more.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: None of This Is True
author: Lisa Jewell
description: None of This Is True is a tale of two women born on the same day. It follows Alix, a successful podcast host, and Josie, a quirky but otherwise seemingly ordinary woman. Alix begins interviewing Josie for her show in hopes that Josie’s life story will interest her listeners. Their lives quickly become intertwined, as Josie is revealed to have a set of depraved secrets. Then Josie disappears. Soon Alix realizes she has made a dangerous mistake and has become the subject of her own true-crime podcast. The book’s unreliable narrator, dark twists and unending suspense will keep you on edge until the very last page.

title: Some Desperate Glory
author: Emily Tesh
description: Raised on militaristic Gaea Station, Kyr (short for Valkyr) is the perfect warrior to avenge Earth’s destruction by the alien federation known as the majoda – until she’s assigned to the Nursery, her body only good enough to birth the next generation of soldiers, and must reevaluate her entire worldview. Like her war-hungry namesake, Kyr is vicious, ruthless and cruel. But jumping through alternate realities cracks her open into soft-bellied vulnerability, teaching her to forgive – the majoda, her peers and even herself. Despite the toxic martyrdom of her upbringing, Kyr’s emotional growth is wholly earned. Emily Tesh unflinchingly plumbs black hole depths of bleakness, with a resolution that had me sobbing cathartically, ready to say goodbye.

title: Easily Slip into Another World: A Life in Music
author: Henry Threadgill and Brent Hayes Edwards
description: Henry Threadgill’s fiercely original musical voice – irrepressible, uncategorizable, an enlightened riot of sensation and sound – finds a close literary analog in this gripping memoir. Along with tales from the harrowing streets of Chicago and the hallucinatory jungles of Vietnam, the book opens a window onto Threadgill’s resolute artistic convictions, which set him apart from both the jazz tradition and the classical establishment (while leaving his mark on both). That apartness is a personal trademark, and in his telling – fashioned with a deft touch from his perceptive co-author, Brent Hayes Edwards – Threadgill also makes it seem a superpower.

title: Becoming Ella Fitzgerald: The Jazz Singer Who Transformed American Song
author: Judith Tick
description: Before her anointing as the “First Lady of Song,” Ella Fitzgerald was a soft-spoken yet impetuous striver who worked hard to create an impression of breezy naturalism. This revealing biography honors Fitzgerald’s effort, along with the resilience it took to prevail in an industry predisposed to view her artistry through a lens of girlish naïveté. Through conscientious research, notably including a mountain of material published in the Black press, historian Judith Tick reminds us not only of this jazz singer’s exceptional talent, but also her unwavering dignity and clear-eyed ambition – all of which she only seemed to carry as easily as a tune.

title: A Spell of Good Things
author: Ayọ̀bámi Adébáyọ̀
description: A Spell of Good Things by Ayọ̀bámi Adébáyọ̀ is the story of two families in Nigeria, one rich and one poor, as their lives come together in one fateful moment. We follow a tired young doctor with the world at her feet and a hungry teenager fighting to stay in school. Their intertwined story reflects on power, political corruption and the unexpected ties that bind us. Arresting, layered and heartbreaking, it almost reads like a thriller. I became very invested in the characters and will be thinking about this book for a long time.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Loot
author: Tania James
description: Loot by Tania James is a treasure that chronicles the journey of a wooden musical tiger and its makers from Mysore, India, to Rouen, France, to England. It tells the story of Abbas, a woodcarver who wishes to create something that outlives him. It’s based on real history and speculates about the makers of Tipu’s Tiger, which is currently exhibited at the Victoria and Albert Museum in London. Its unusual premise and unforgettable heist will completely wrap you up in its world. Masterful, hilarious and moving, it’s a story that you can easily lose yourself in.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "History", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Western Lane
author: Chetna Maroo
description: What is it like to be a child sports prodigy? We’re captivated by the real-life stories of Tiger Woods and the Williams sisters. Now, in her taut, spare debut novel, Chetna Maroo introduces us to Gopi, a fictional 11-year-old daughter of Indian immigrants living in a London suburb. Her extraordinary talents in the game of squash are revealed in the wake of a devastating tragedy that leaves Gopi, her sisters and her father emotionally frozen and fragile. In selecting this novel as a finalist for the Booker Prize, the judges especially singled out Western Lane for its language, praising its “crystalline prose that also feels warm and tender, which can be a difficult balance to strike.”
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Fire Weather: A True Story From a Hotter World
author: John Vaillant
description: How unfortunately ironic that this gripping investigation into a catastrophic 2016 Canadian wildfire was published at exactly the moment when the Eastern Seaboard found itself gasping through a yellow haze caused by record-setting fires that scorched over 70,000 square miles north of the border. John Vaillant, an U.S.-born author and journalist living in Canada, examines an earlier, comparable disaster. The Fort McMurray wildfire, known locally as “the Beast,” caused billions of dollars in damages and displaced tens of thousands of people. Vaillant takes readers back into the deep history of the boreal forests before thrusting us into the Beast’s fiery heart. Fire Weather is a report from the front lines of environmental cataclysm and a prediction of what more will surely come.
tags: ["Science", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Wager: A Tale of Shipwreck, Mutiny and Murder
author: David Grann
description: The Wager: A Tale of Shipwreck, Mutiny and Murder is an exhaustively researched and thrilling account of a long-forgotten, 18th-century calamity at sea. Unlike Herman Melville’s or Patrick O’Brian’s nautical adventures, which I also love, this historical narrative isn’t just based on a true story – it really is one, despite extremes of psychology and nature that make the recounted events almost unbelievable. That’s part of why reading it felt like getting swept away by a hurricane-force gale, as journalist David Grann laid out the stunning facts and surprises in his deceptively simple and spare writing.

title: Tress of the Emerald Sea: A Cosmere Novel
author: Brandon Sanderson
description: This book recounts the journey of Tress, an extraordinarily pragmatic young woman with a fondness for collecting cups. When her friend Charlie, the only son and heir to a duke, is kidnapped by the Sorceress, Tress devises a plan to rescue him by smuggling herself off her home island. Her adventures, told by Hoid, a recurring character in Brandon Sanderson’s expansive universe, The Cosmere, take her across oceans of dangerous pollen aboard a pirate ship, where she uncovers the captain’s nefarious plans. This book has everything you want in a fantasy story: talking animals, extraterrestrial beings, curses, dragons and a unique system of magic. It’s a standalone novel that will delight both hardcore Standersons, like myself, and new readers.

title: Road to Surrender: Three Men and the Countdown to the End of World War II
author: Evan Thomas
description: This is an enthralling account of the events leading up to the Japanese surrender in World War II. Evan Thomas has gotten access to the diaries of leading players on the American and Japanese sides of the drama. What he has produced is a riveting account of the decision to drop two atomic bombs, but even more importantly, he has shed new light on the cataclysmic power struggle behind the scenes in Japan and how the powerful military fought the idea of surrender quite literally to its death. This is a book perfectly paired with American Prometheus (which was the source material for the film Oppenheimer) and is remarkable for the way it counters modern conventional wisdom about the atomic end of the war.

title: Big Swiss
author: Jen Beagin
description: A hilarious, dark and quirky coming-of-middle-age story starring Greta, a 45-year-old aspiring writer drifting in upstate New York. She transcribes sex therapy sessions for the local amateur therapist – and starts a life-changing affair with one of his clients, a woman she calls “Big Swiss.” Jen Beagin’s third novel is strictly fiction, though it borrows details from her life. It started a bidding war in Hollywood before it was even released, and an HBO series starring Jodie Comer (Killing Eve) is in the works.

title: The Collected Regrets of Clover
author: Mikki Brammer
description: Who knew a book about death could be so uplifting? Clover Brooks is a death doula, focused on giving people their best possible final days rather than making the most of her own. It’s a delight to watch as new people come into her life and help her change that (easier said than done, as any introvert knows). This book is like a warm hug, with a lot to love — including a feisty nonagenarian, romances both budding and rediscovered, a cross-country road trip and many words of wisdom about what it means to live a beautiful life.

title: The Quickening: Creation and Community at the Ends of the Earth
author: Elizabeth Rush
description: Books about Antarctica are always about dudes. Brave dudes who went to a cold, scary place and performed feats of strength and endurance in the name of being first or, sometimes, a monarch. This book is the antidote to those. It takes place on a somewhat-claustrophobic research ship bound for Antarctica. Elizabeth Rush mixes science reporting with memoir as she ruminates on her choice to have a child, how it feels to confront climate change and how laborious it is to study Antarctica.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Science", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: An Amerikan Family: The Shakurs and the Nation They Created
author: Santi Elijah Holley
description: Twenty-seven years after Tupac Shakur’s unsolved murder, Las Vegas police are finally working to bring the case to justice. But to grasp the seismic tragedy of his death, you have to understand how unjust Pac’s life was from birth. Santi Elijah Holley doubles all the way down on that mission, detailing the legacy of struggle, self-determination and sacrifice Pac shouldered as a Shakur. From his mother Afeni to his godmother Assata to his stepfather Mutulu, his family members were freedom fighters and self-declared political prisoners who dared to dream, and wage war, in the name of Black liberation. Before Pac picked up a mic and tatted “Thug Life” on his torso, his people were branded America’s Most Wanted. Even in defeat, this revolutionary family rerouted the course of U.S. history. The book is documented proof.

title: Limitless: The Federal Reserve Takes on a New Age of Crisis
author: Jeanna Smialek
description: Since the pandemic, the economy has been on something of a roller coaster. It can feel arbitrary to most people reading the headlines, but there is one organization whose job it is to manage all of this: the Federal Reserve. New York Times reporter Jeanna Smialek covers the Fed, and in this book, she explains to the reader just how important the Fed is to the lives of ordinary people and details the changing role of the Fed amid crisis. Through human-level storytelling, she makes the Fed’s seemingly closed-door decision-making feel refreshingly transparent.

title: What the Dead Know: Learning About Life as a New York City Death Investigator
author: Barbara Butcher
description: What the Dead Know is Barbara Butcher’s memoir about her career as a death investigator in Manhattan (she was the first woman to hold the job for longer than a few months). Homicide, suicide, accident or natural – it was her job to figure out how people died but also, as she writes in the book, how they lived. The story takes an emotional turn when, after 9/11, it became Butcher’s job to identify the remains of people who died that day. While it may sound bleak, reading this memoir is like watching an episode of CSI with your dry, brassy best friend. And, yes, she has heard all the jokes about her last name.

title: Leg: The Story of a Limb and the Boy Who Grew From It
author: Greg Marshall
description: Greg Marshall grew up with a limp, thinking it was “tight tendons,” only to find out – well into adulthood – that it was actually cerebral palsy and that his high school secretary knew before he did. While Leg is a memoir about Marshall’s complicated relationship with his most interesting limb, the leg has a lot of competition for top billing among the troop of brash, loud siblings, a dad with ALS and a mother who makes it her decades-long mission to be a star cancer patient. Leg has everything you want in a memoir: plenty of body humor, a little bit of smut, family drama and tons of personality.

title: Good Night, Irene
author: Luis Alberto Urrea
description: Good Night, Irene is historical fiction based on Luis Alberto Urrea’s mother, who served as a Red Cross volunteer in an outfit called the Clubmobile Corps in World War II. These so-called Donut Dollies made doughnuts and served coffee in a 2.5-ton GMC truck, boosting morale and providing comfort to the soldiers. Oh – and they also drove to the front lines, were trapped behind enemy lines during the Battle of the Bulge and were there when Gen. Patton liberated Buchenwald. I like that this novel is a fresh take on WWII history, but I love that it’s an epic buddy comedy starring characters who model friendship, courage, good humor and wit even in the toughest of circumstances.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance", "Family Matters"]

title: All the Beauty in the World: The Metropolitan Museum of Art and Me
author: Patrick Bringley
description: What E.L. Konigsburg’s From the Mixed Up Files of Mrs. Basil E. Frankweiler is for kids (OK, I read it as an adult, too!), All the Beauty in the World is for grown-ups: a peek behind the curtain at The Metropolitan Museum of Art. That’s where, after his brother died from cancer, Patrick Bringley quit his job and donned a polyester blue uniform to become a guard. He writes that he just wanted to stand still for a while, but ended up staying for 10 years – studying the Masters, pondering the mummies, and contemplating life, art, and beauty. I found this memoir, and Bringley’s portrait of the museum, so compelling that I actually planned a trip to The Met!

title: Vampires of El Norte
author: Isabel Cañas
description: In her follow-up to The Hacienda, Isabel Cañas once again brings the creepy vibes in a page-turner of a story. Set in the 1840s on the Texas-Mexico border, Cañas’ novel tells the story of two childhood friends separated by a tragedy and the creatures they must face, both human and supernatural. Weaving together elements of horror, romance and historical fiction, this book still haunts my brain months after reading it. I envy those who get to read it for the first time.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Love & Romance"]

title: My Murder
author: Katie Williams
description: Would you investigate your own murder if you could? Lou has the opportunity to do just that after she’s brought back to life following her death at the hands of a serial killer. Once I picked this book up, I couldn’t put it down. I’ve read a lot of thriller/mystery/suspense novels and I’ve become pretty accustomed to what twists lie around the corner, but this book surprised me and made me laugh out loud more than once, while also confronting our culture’s obsession with true crime.

title: Weyward
author: Emilia Hart
description: This spellbinding tale spanning five centuries instantly caught my attention. With its seamless transitions between 1619, 1942 and 2019, it tells the stories of three generations of strong and resilient women. Each one faces unique hardships, but through their unwavering determination and the support of their loved ones, they triumph over adversity. You will uncover a cottage, secrets and immeasurable strength. This debut novel is an impressive tribute to the indomitable spirit of women, the transformative power of nature and the enduring bonds of family and ancestry.

title: Romney: A Reckoning
author: McKay Coppins
description: It is rare for a politician to prioritize moral stands over his or her electoral self-interest. It is even more rare for a politician to willingly share private journals, and honest self-assessments, with a journalist. Mitt Romney has now done both of these things, and the result is McKay Coppins’ engrossing new biography. It’s one of the best political books I’ve read in years, and it provides key insights into how drastically the Republican Party has shifted in the decade since Romney was its presidential nominee.

title: Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O’Connell’s Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People
author: Tracy Kidder
description: Dr. Jim O’Connell has been treating unhoused people in Boston for more than three decades, and he hasn’t worked himself out of a job yet. On the contrary, the job is bigger than ever – not only in Boston but in nearly every American city. Nevertheless, I found Tracy Kidder’s book about O’Connell and his Sisyphean work surprisingly uplifting. The onetime bartender is both cleareyed and compassionate toward his patients – people who are ignored by so many others. His work might be a Band-Aid on the bigger problem of homelessness, but as he says, “This is what we do while we’re waiting for the world to change.”

title: Day
author: Michael Cunningham
description: How to write a novel about the pandemic without writing a pandemic novel? Michael Cunningham manages that feat with beauty, grace and lines that linger, like this from the book’s opening: “This early, the East River takes on a thin layer of translucence, a bright steely skin that appears to float over the river itself as the water turns from its nocturnal black to the opaque deep green of the approaching day.” The novel follows a Brooklyn family – already in something of a bubble, even before COVID-19 – across a single day, April 5, across three years, 2019, 2020 and 2021. There’s a passage toward the end about a little girl in a yellow dress – too small, a difficult color, those around her think – well, I won’t spoil it.

title: The Wren, the Wren
author: Anne Enright
description:
The great (fictional) Irish poet Phil McDaragh is revered for portrayals of love, like this:
the wren the wren
was a panic
of feathered air
in my opening hand
so fierce and light
I did not feel
the push
of her ascent
away from me
A man of great appetites – for women, for drink – McDaragh’s legacy, off the page, is more complicated.
How the daughter he abandons, and in turn her daughter, contend with that legacy shapes this exquisite novel.
Throughout, Anne Enright includes the poems – which she has written – for her poet, a task she characterized as “extremely cheeky” when she spoke with NPR’s Scott Simon.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Peacock and the Sparrow
author: I.S. Berry
description: A spy thriller that is engaging and utterly believable, in large part because the author of this debut novel, I.S. Berry, has served overseas in the CIA herself. With the vividly painted backdrop of Bahrain in the time of the Arab Spring as the setting and her spy, Shane Collins, as the main character, the story that unfolds is steeped in actual knowledge of spy stuff. It’s cynical, fast moving and immensely readable.

title: Soil: The Story of a Black Mother's Garden
author: Camille T. Dungy
description: Poet Camille Dungy writes about her efforts to transform her suburban yard in predominantly white Fort Collins, Colo., into a wilder, natural garden full of native plants. By turns a memoir of family life during the pandemic, a meditation on – and contribution to – American nature writing, and an exploration of the historical effect of segregation on not just where people of color have lived but how they engage with the natural world, this book is wide – ranging. Dungy is so expressive, exploratory and engaging that you definitely want to go along for the ride with her.

title: Kala
author: Colin Walsh
description: In this novel, Katherine “Kala” Lanann goes missing from the town of Kinlough, Ireland, in 2003. Fifteen years later, her three once-close friends find themselves back – or still in – Kinlough reckoning with the state of their lives. After two more teen girls – cousins to one in the group – go missing, the pain and mystery of what happened is brought urgently back to the surface of their lives. Even after you put the pieces together, you’ll want to stay with this powerful meditation on the power of fear and the meaning of bravery.

title: Empty Theatre: A Novel: or The Lives of King Ludwig II of Bavaria and Empress Sisi of Austria (Queen of Hungary), Cousins, in Their Pursuit of Connection and Beauty...
author: Jac Jemc
description: Being royalty seems great from far away – all jewels, banquets, and balls. Closer up, the pressures on royalty make it look far less appealing. At the end of every gilded hall lies a heavy price: the pressure to marry to form an alliance, to produce an heir, to navigate a court full of rumors. This novel beautifully captures the dichotomy of the royal lives of King Ludwig II of Bavaria and Empress Elizabeth of Austria in the late 19th century. By turns amusing and heartbreaking, it’s a captivating story rooted in bizarre reality, but told in a fresh format.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Vera Wong's Unsolicited Advice for Murderers
author: Jesse Q. Sutanto
description: Vera Wong’s San Francisco tea shop isn’t as busy as it once was, but she keeps her life on track with a steady routine – a routine that’s upended when she finds a dead body on the floor of the tea shop one morning. Suddenly, she’s busier than she might have bargained for. The mysterious surprise leads Vera to investigate when it’s clear the police have it wrong. With the help of customers new and old, she tracks down the truth of the matter, building community, sparking some romance and leading us on a delightful caper to the conclusion.

title: Real Self-Care: A Transformative Program for Redefining Wellness (Crystals, Cleanses, and Bubble Baths Not Included)
author: Pooja Lakshmin
description: With meaningful roots in the social justice movement, the term “self-care” has become so ubiquitous that it’s starting to lose meaning. The phrase has been co-opted by marketers to sell a growing number of goods and services. How, then, do you – a person hanging on by your fingernails – find meaning and actual relief in caring for yourself? Enter Pooja Lakshmin, a psychiatrist dedicated to helping people put down the guilt that comes with attempting to achieve perfection within broken systems. Instead of pushing us to strive – or suggesting a cleanse – Lakshmin provides tools for boundary setting and treating yourself with compassion.

title: The Illiterate
author: Ágota Kristóf, translated by Nina Bogin
description: Sharp and precise as a surgeon’s blade, this compact memoir vividly evokes the author’s serialized displacements due to war and political oppression. A precocious reader in her native language, Ágota Kristóf was repeatedly rendered “illiterate” by historical circumstances: She had to absorb Russian as a child during the Soviet occupation of Hungary at the end of World War II, then she was forced to learn French when resettling in Switzerland after her escape from Hungary’s failed uprising against Soviet rule in 1956. Comparing her exile to “a desert,” Kristóf nevertheless survived the wilderness through hawklike observation of her social environment and a fierce survival instinct.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "History"]

title: Owner of a Lonely Heart: A Memoir
author: Beth Nguyen
description: Owner of a Lonely Heart offers indelible insights on biological and surrogate motherhood, informed by the author’s upbringing in a chaotic yet emotionally repressed family led by a Vietnamese refugee father and a second-generation Mexican stepmother in white-centric Grand Rapids, Mich. Referencing yet subverting the swaggering lyrics of a 1983 Yes song, Beth Nguyen’s memoir, a taut, contemplative follow-up to Stealing Buddha’s Dinner, reconciles the apparent gaps in her family history with the need for sufficient space and time to redefine the past.

title: Birth Canal
author: Dias Novita Wuri
