But when she learns that M. has been accused of sexual assault, she’s not entirely surprised. So begins this absorbing debut novel that movingly portrays its protagonist coming to terms with her imbalanced, difficult and sometimes harmful friendship with M. that was, at the same time, an essential and at times euphoric part of her life for years.

title: Lilith
author: Eric Rickstad
description: This is a hard read, but also a very necessary one. A truly harrowing narrative about a school shooting and its aftermath, Lilith offers an unflinching look at gun culture and the way school shootings change the lives of everyone involved in very significant ways. Honest to the core, fearless in its approach to a touchy subject, and written with impeccable pacing and economy of language, this one should be mandatory reading in all schools.

title: Listen for the Lie
author: Amy Tintera
description: Listen for the Lie is a true crime podcast hosted by Ben Owens, and its compelling Season 2 will pull you right in. Owens is investigating the unsolved murder of a young woman, Savvy, whose best friend, Lucy, was found wandering the street covered in Savvy’s blood. Lucy was clearly at the scene of the crime but can’t remember anything about what happened. Years later, after establishing a fairy-tale life following the brutal loss of her friend, Lucy is pulled into the past again, as Owens tries to untangle the mystery. Think you can catch the lie?

title: Little Rot
author: Akwaeke Emezi
description: In this twisty, poetic roller-coaster ride of a novel, a bitter breakup precipitates a harrowing descent into darkness for a wealthy young Nigerian man, his pious, long-suffering ex-girlfriend and their friends.  Aima and Kalu met as ex-pats in Houston and returned home for his business and to build a life together. They’d been happy abroad, but the move shook loose something important. Back home they revert to destructive patterns. It’s complex, but at the core, love lies and dies in this fictional, decadent yet riveting, money-loving city of “New Lagos.” If you want a book that grabs onto your brain and shakes it, I highly recommend Akwaeke Emezi’s genre-defying Little Rot. It gutted and enthralled me in equal measure.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Long Island
author: Colm Tóibín
description: A word about sequels: I don’t usually review them because not everyone has read the first book. But in this case, it would be well worth your time to catch up: Colm Tóibín’s Long Island, together with its 2009 predecessor, Brooklyn, is a devastating dual portrait of a woman in two different seasons of her life, thrashing against the constraints of fate. As readers familiar with Brooklyn know, Eilis Lacey is a restless soul. The opening shocker on the second page of Long Island is that Tony – her easygoing husband of now 20 years – has been getting restless himself.

title: Long Island Compromise
author: Taffy Brodesser-Akner
description: After the patriarch of the wealthy Fletcher family is kidnapped and then returned for a hefty ransom, everyone tries to forget anything ever happened. But as the decades pass, it becomes clear how the family never really escaped from the terror of that day. The family fortune, generated by the factory founded by their Jewish immigrant forebears, can’t save the Fletchers from themselves – in fact, it often just enables them to be the architects of their own misery. But when the money suddenly vanishes, it becomes apparent that wealth was the glue keeping everything from falling apart. Despite the dark premise, Taffy Brodesser-Akner doesn’t let readers feel too sorry for her cast of characters – often unpleasant and out of touch, they’re both unsympathetic and deeply compelling.

title: Long Live Evil
author: Sarah Rees Brennan
description: Do you often find yourself saying “if villain, why hot?” Do you mourn the early cancellation of shows like My Lady Jane and Galavant? Then you’re going to love the self-aware adventure that is Long Live Evil. The book follows Rae, a terminally ill woman, who wakes up in the world of her favorite book. Great turn of events! Except Rae’s character is the villain of the story. Oops! Nevertheless, Rae decides to persist, with villainy. She assembles a motley crew of knife-carrying and morally deficient henchmen, including the very sexy Key, who is equal parts sociopath and simp. Rae’s journey to seize control of the narrative is meta, camp and oh so much fun! If you’ve ever wanted to break the fourth wall with your bare hands, you’ll want to pick this one up.

title: Madwoman
author: Chelsea Bieker
description: I have been a fan of Chelsea Bieker’s fever-dream fiction about women struggling to break free from bad men since her 2020 debut Godshot. In her latest, Bieker explores the aftermath of a seemingly clean break. After a childhood spent fearing that her father would kill her mother, Clove has built a safe life with a husband who would never raise a hand to her, and children who have never known raised voices. But a letter from a women’s prison shatters the illusion that her past is past. Madwoman reads like a thriller, but with a deep meditation on the intergenerational effects of domestic violence.

title: Magical/Realism: Essays on Music, Memory, Fantasy, and Borders
author: Vanessa Angélica Villarreal
description: Like the Witchers, whose avatars she assumes in the video games she played to heal from her white ex-husband’s abuse and betrayal, poet and cultural critic Vanessa Angélica Villarreal leaps across seemingly insurmountable boundaries in this stunning book of interwoven essays and shattered, artfully reassembled memoir. Music and fantasy form the framework of her own bildungsroman as she exposes the bigotry she and her immigrant family survived in the Texas borderlands of her youth and the echoes of that racism throughout the popular culture she adores and remakes through her avid engagement. Villarreal deftly blends her own family’s stories with unique insights into realms like grunge and indie music, Jennifer Lopez rom-coms, the life and death of Selena and the meanings of Game of Thrones.

title: Margo's Got Money Troubles
author: Rufi Thorpe
description: The simple way to describe this is as a story of a college gal getting knocked up by her English teacher and resorting to starting an OnlyFans to make ends meet. Not sold yet? Well, then let me add that this book opens up far wider and deeper than its premise. You’ll come to care so deeply about Margo, her professional wrestler father, Jinx, and all the relationships she forms as she figures things out. A warm, heartfelt, hilarious novel that I won’t soon forget.

title: Martyr!
author: Kaveh Akbar
description: Kaveh Akbar’s Martyr! is a portrait of Cyrus Shams – a man consumed by addiction, depression and spiritual untethering. Full of sadness and righteous anger – especially when it comes to the American tendency to negate the value of certain Middle Eastern lives – it’s also one of the funniest books I’ve read in years. Martyr! is propelled by those kinds of contradictions. It breezes by while knocking you over. It strikes an optimistic note within its fatalism. It suggests death might provide the meaning of life. Ultimately, it could leave you in tears of laughter or of sorrow. Or most likely both.

title: Mean Boys: A Personal History
author: Geoffrey Mak
description: Berlin’s techno scene, evangelical Christianity, Alcoholics Anonymous and the Isla Vista mass shooter – these are just some of the unexpected threads that Geoffrey Mak explores in his first book, which blends memoir, reportage and cultural criticism to great effect. In thoughtful and far-reaching essays, Mak writes about his own psychotic breaks and addiction; the limits of empathy, as well as the transcendent possibilities of self-discovery to be found in it; and his return to Christianity, which, as the son of a minister, he had once spurned. An intellectually vivid and curious collection that refuses to find simple answers to its complex questions.

title: Memory Piece
author: Lisa Ko
description: This is a coming-of-age story about three friends growing up in and around New York City in the 1990s. Their friendship evolves over the decades as they experiment with, and push the boundaries of, art, performance and technology. I loved that the book makes art feel real and weird and kind of gross – not glamorous and sugarcoated.

title: Metal from Heaven
author: August Clarke
description: Orphaned when her parents are murdered while on strike, Marney escapes with a band of rebels. She has the uncanny ability to manipulate ichorite, a metal so versatile that Yann Industry Chauncey has made a fortune manufacturing it – even when his laborers, like Marney, suffer from life-threatening allergic reactions. As Marney ages, her rage coalesces into a demand for revenge. This is the kind of novel that makes me pillage the thesaurus looking for words to describe it. It’s dazzling and disconcerting – a revolution spiced with kink, a queer manifesto and an anarchist raising a middle finger to capitalism with one hand and lobbing a molotov cocktail into a factory with the other. An epic poem crossed with an industrial fairy tale, August Clarke’s novel is, to put it simply, stunning.

title: Mid-Air
author: Alicia D. Williams, illustrated by Danica Novgorodoff
description: Reviewers and critics typically overuse the term “lyrical” when discussing great books, but there are few better appellatives to apply to this canny verse novel. With multilayered aplomb, the story focuses on Isaiah and his friends Drew and Darius. These three love biking, skating and trying to break all kinds of world records, but when an accident claims the life of Darius, Isaiah feels responsible and hopelessly lost. This deft narrative is capable of delving into serious topics, like what happens when grief is compounded by violence, without dragging the book down or ever feeling too heavy. A tale where your redemption is inextricable from forgiving yourself. (For ages 10 and up)

title: Miss May Does Not Exist: The Life and Work of Elaine May, Hollywood's Hidden Genius
author: Carrie Courogen
description: The writer, director, actor and comedian Elaine May has pissed off a lot of people in Hollywood. Carrie Courogen chronicles everything from May’s start as a child performer in Yiddish theaters to her affinity for eating apples, core and all, in this comprehensive biography. Even if you don’t know May’s name, you know her work: In addition to her films (The Heartbreak Kid, A New Leaf) May was involved in the making of Tootsie, Labyrinth, The Birdcage and more. And those people she pissed off? Oh, they’re still pissed! But they can’t seem to help but gush about May’s brain, talent and work ethic in this book.

title: My Antarctica: True Adventures in the Land of Mummified Seals, Space Robots, and So Much More
author: G. Neri, illlustrated by Corban Wilkin
description: Take a trip to the coldest, windiest, highest and driest continent in the world. Here, young readers will find answers to every question they’ve ever had about Antarctica – not to mention ones they hadn’t even thought to ask. Who is there now? Why? What do you eat when you’re there? G. Neri’s easygoing narrative reads like a journal, full of cartoons, photos and the occasional mummified seal. Plus, he profiles the many different scientists at work at McMurdo Station with humor, candor and wonder. Just be ready for one inevitable question after reading this book: “Can we go?” (For ages 7 to 10)

title: My Black Country: A Journey Through Country Music's Black Past, Present, and Future
author: Alice Randall
description: Still wondering what all the fuss over Beyoncé’s Cowboy Carter was about? Let Alice Randall – esteemed novelist, Vanderbilt University professor and longtime Nashville songwriter – inform you. Recounting the role country music played in her Detroit childhood (The Supremes, she declares, were country) through her adulthood as a revolutionary presence on Music Row, Randall interweaves her own story with detailed histories of underacknowledged or misplaced figures like Jimmie Rodgers collaborator Lil Hardin and Hollywood’s Black cowboy Herb Jeffries, offering a unified theory of what “Black country” means. This gripping, graceful read shows that, no matter what boundaries others set around it, country is a Black art form growing from an obscured past toward a brighter future.

title: My Friends
author: Hisham Matar
description: “Our friendships are our fate,” novelist Hisham Matar told me this year. The entirety of his book My Friends unfolds during a late-night walk across London. As the story begins, the protagonist, a middle-aged Libyan man named Khaled, has just bid farewell to his old friend Hosam Zowa at St. Pancras station. On Khaled’s long walk home from Hosam’s departure, he tells the story of their relationship – everything from how they met as young men, to the loves and books they shared, and, most importantly, the changing and inevitable seasons of friendships.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: New Cold Wars: China's Rise, Russia's Invasion, and America's Struggle to Defend the West
author: David E. Sanger
description: This is the best book so far to define a new era of great power struggles between the United States, China and Russia. Consumed by the Middle East and Afghanistan, America was slow to respond to the far greater challenge of a resurgent China. Washington was also late to countering Russia as it sought to rebuild its old sphere of influence in Eastern Europe by force. Both authoritarian countries are now trying to reshape the American-led world order. U.S. leaders have a tough task ahead: building a new strategy to navigate the most complex geopolitical landscape in decades, while managing a divided electorate at home.

title: New Testaments: Stories
author: Dagoberto Gilb
description: Texas- and Mexico-based author Dagoberto Gilb doesn’t care about ivory towers, but he does know how to build them. He started writing short stories while working a construction job at the University of Texas at El Paso and has been going strong ever since. The stories in his latest collection focus on working-class Mexican Americans and are written with Gilb’s signature realism, which manages to be both gritty and beautiful. Looking for accomplished, unpretentious fiction that celebrates real people? This is your man.

title: Nicked
author: M. T. Anderson
description: This is an 11th century romp but with stolen, maybe mystical, bones: An earnest monk dreams of St. Nicholas, thinking he’s directing him to minister to those suffering from a pox in Bari. The monk’s bosses say “Nope!” and decide to use his dream to serve their goal of bringing (stealing, filching, nicking) the saint’s remains from Myra, across the Mediterranean (saints’ bones will cure a pox and boost tourism, you know). Enter the relic hunters and their boat, off goes the earnest monk to see the dubious mission through, and so begins a comic adventure that’s complete with swashbuckling, sharp banter and successful flirting.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Humor & Comedy", "Dark"]

title: Nightwatching
author: Tracy Sierra
description: Nightwatching begins with a scene straight out of a nightmare: A woman is at home with two sleeping children when she hears the footsteps of an intruder on the stairs. The story that follows is by turns suspenseful, uncomfortable and enraging. Tracy Sierra skillfully uses the home invasion to explore the terrifying responsibility of motherhood and to expose the pure horror of being a woman in a society that does not always choose to believe women.

title: No Country for Love
author: Yaroslav Trofimov
description: No Country for Love is Doctor Zhivago meets Stalingrad – a mix of romantic historical fiction and gritty, reportage-like storytelling. No surprise: Author Yaroslav Trofimov is a top foreign correspondent for The Wall Street Journal. He was born in and has reported extensively from Ukraine. The story he tells across decades of impossible love, war and survival rings painfully and beautifully true. The history is spot-on, going from pre-Communist times, through World War II, to the era of Stalin and after. And the stories it tells of the human heart, through the eyes of its heroine Deborah Rosenbaum and those who befriend or betray her, are unforgettable.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: No One Gets to Fall Apart: A Memoir
author: Sarah LaBrie
description: In 2017, when Sarah LaBrie was in her early 30s and living in Los Angeles, her mother – a nurse in Houston – was found on the side of a highway, paralyzed with paranoia that she was being targeted by assailants in white sedans. Through the lens of her mother’s schizophrenia diagnosis, LaBrie, who is Black, examines her intense and unstable upbringing, her family’s history of mental illness and her own artistic ambitions. As these threads intersect, No One Gets to Fall Apart forms a remarkable meditation on inheritance, race and mental illness, the mother-daughter relationship and the blurry line between madness and artistic brilliance.

title: Nosy Neighbors
author: Freya Sampson
description: Author Freya Sampson is the patron saint of city dwellers who form unexpected, solid-as-steel friendships. In this story, it’s cantankerous septuagenarian Dorothy, who is rooted in place by her memories, and misanthropic 20-something Kat, who is trying to outrun hers. Dorothy is a long-term resident of the once resplendent, now crumbling Shelley House, an apartment building in a British village that also happens to be the first place Kat has been able to settle in and feel at home for years. When the building is threatened by developers and a neighbor turns up dead, the two combine forces in an effort to save their community.

title: Not a Speck of Light
author: Laird Barron
description: Laird Barron’s work comes from a place in which crime and cosmic horror collide with stellar results. The haunting memories of a serial killer, an elderly couple who learn to live with the thing that inhabits their attic, and the lingering effects of sinister childhood games are just some of the things readers will find in this superbly written collection from one of the best contemporary purveyors of Vantablack-dark speculative fiction.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Short Stories & Essays"]

title: Not Quite a Ghost
author: Anne Ursu
description: Violet doesn’t much like her new house, particularly since her new attic bedroom has some seriously creepy wallpaper. When she becomes ill with a disease that leaves her exhausted, she discovers that something in the wallpaper has been waiting a long time to take advantage of her very situation. Not Quite a Ghost takes the 1892 short story “The Yellow Wall-Paper” and passes it through the wringer of the 21st century, tweaking the female hysteria narrative to one of chronic fatigue and doctors who don’t believe their female patients. Anne Ursu shows off her horror-writing chops as she draws parallels between the contemporary medical establishment and an old (but prescient) classic tale. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Not the End of the World: How We Can Be the First Generation to Build a Sustainable Planet
author: Hannah Ritchie
description: Not the End of the World sifts through the evidence on pollution, extinction threats and deforestation. Once the numbers are clinically separated from emotion, a surprising guidebook to an eco-friendly life emerges. Food miles: not likely to affect climate change much. Meatless Mondays: helpful, especially if eschewing beef. Not everyone will interpret the world’s chances of staying within 2 degrees Celsius of warming with the same cautious optimism as Hannah Ritchie (“I’m confident we can keep moving closer”). But Ritchie’s data-first perspective makes this book an invaluable chaser to climate doomscrolling.

title: Nuclear War: A Scenario
author: Annie Jacobsen
description: The bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki occurred nearly 80 years ago – long before I was born. Annie Jacobsen moves nuclear war out of the history books and into the present day. Interspersed with her reporting on the current state of preparation for nuclear war, she makes a well-educated guess about how such a situation could unfold today. Jacobsen’s writing in this scenario has the suspense of a great political thriller. The book leaves no doubt that the immediate aftermath of nuclear war would be horrific and it would only get worse from there.

title: Old King
author: Maxim Loskutoff
description: It’s 1976, and Duane Oshun has decided it’s time for a change. He moves out of Utah and finds himself in a small Montana town, where he soon discovers there’s something off about his neighbor, a recluse named Ted Kaczynski. Writing about a real-life figure, especially one as infamous as the Unabomber, can be tricky, but Maxim Loskutoff captures his menace perfectly. Readers with an interest in the American West will be thrilled by this one, but don’t start it at night – it’s the kind of book you’ll want to read cover to cover.

title: Olivetti
author: Allie Millington
description: I had a young visitor point to my old typewriter and ask, “What’s that?” In Olivetti, Ernest’s mother goes missing and – are you ready? – he and his mom’s beloved typewriter conspire to find her. So much is at stake in this unique and wholly captivating mystery. Memories are lost, then found, and new ones made along the way in this tender novel. The next time a kid asks me about my typewriter, I will offer them Allie Millington’s book and say, “Here, I’ll let the typewriter tell you all about what it is.” (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Ours
author: Phillip B. Williams
description: When an award-winning poet writes an epic saga, you want to sit up and take notice, and while Ours is lengthy and beautifully complex, it is also a page-turner. Opening on a contemporary death of a teenager and his shocking resurrection, the book then travels back to the 1830s and a conjure woman named Saint who is freeing enslaved people from their bonds and taking revenge on their masters. She founds a town for these freed people and shields it from the world – but she can’t protect its residents from their own pasts and present hurts.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Pages of Mourning
author: Diego Gerard Morrison
description: Aureliano Más may be named for 19 characters from One Hundred Years of Solitude and may hail from Comala, Mexico, the literal ghost town in Juan Rulfo’s novel Pedro Páramo, but he sees magical realism as pure fantasy that serves to soothe a country riven by forced disappearances. Pages of Mourning follows Aureliano’s attempts to dismantle the genre as he writes a novel about his mother, who vanished when he was an infant. It’s an inventive work of metafiction that grapples with the horrific realities of Mexico’s drug wars and the families left grieving without bodies to bury.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Phantom Orbit: A Thriller
author: David Ignatius
description: Phantom Orbit is an enthralling fictional account of satellite warfare – perhaps a frontier not given enough attention in headlines today. Could Russia and China cooperate to use malware to take out the Western GPS system used globally? What are the implications of a GPS failure and the use or abuse of commercial satellites for military purposes? As with all of David Ignatius’ novels, this book is unnerving, contemporary and fast-paced with compelling, relatable characters. If you like spy thrillers, this one is provocative and won’t disappoint.

title: Pink Slime
author: Fernanda Trías, translated by Heather Cleary
description: Even if you’re wary of reading a pandemic novel, give this one, by Uruguayan author Fernanda Trías, a chance. It tells the story of a woman living in the aftermath of a deadly algae bloom that has decimated her city, leaving residents dependent on “pink slime” – a meat product as unappealing as its name. Trías conveys the horror of mass illness perfectly in this enormously unsettling, but surprisingly beautiful, novel.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Playground
author: Richard Powers
description: Richard Powers won the 2019 Pulitzer Prize for fiction for The Overstory, a book that helped many readers see trees in a different way. This novel does something similar with the world’s oceans and reefs. He weaves a story spanning decades, tracing a friendship and rivalry between childhood friends whose life paths veer in different directions. And he somehow manages to balance the sweep of subjects like colonialism, ecosystems and artificial intelligence with intimate details of humans playing games and cuttlefish putting on an underwater light show.
tags: ["Science", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Pony Confidential
author: Christina Lynch
description: The pony in this mystery is such a delightfully bitter misanthrope, “bent on revenge” and singularly devoted to finding the girl who cast him aside, condemning him to a life of spoiled, bratty kids. Turns out, she’s grown up and charged with murder: a death that happened years before, the last night she and her pony were together. Did she do it? Did the pony? Can he use his wits and resist peppermints long enough for all to be revealed? A kooky page-turner that took me back to every girl-and-her-horse book I ever read, but Misty of Chincoteague was nowhere near as spicy as this pony is!

title: Poor Deer
author: Claire Oshetsky
description: Margaret, a 16-year-old girl, can’t run away from her past, because a mysterious creature that lives inside her mind won’t let her. The teenager is haunted by the memory of an incident that happened when she was 4 and caused the death of her friend in a game that went awry. Poor Deer, the creature, insists that she write a confession, throwing Margaret into an intense turmoil. It goes without saying that this is an emotionally wrenching novel, but it’s also one with an undercurrent of hope – and it proves that Claire Oshetsky is one of our most brilliant working writers.

title: Practice
author: Rosalind Brown
description: In Rosalind Brown’s beguiling short novel, an ambitious but restless and libidinous Oxford undergraduate runs into the age-old philosophical mind-body problem in her struggles to drum up a worthy topic for an essay on Shakespeare’s sonnets, due the next day. The result is a simultaneously serious and entertaining meditation on physical and intellectual appetites. Practice joins a shelf of fiction about the writerly discipline involved in filling a blank page with something original and compelling. But it is also about the art of reading and engaging with a text, body and soul.

title: Rakesfall
author: Vajra Chandrasekera
description: This one is intense. Rakesfall is a challenging, protean work of speculative fiction, dazzling and wounding by turn. At its most basic, Rakesfall is the story of two souls connected across many lives. We first meet them as Annelid and Leveret, children during the Sri Lankan civil war. If you require a straightforward narrative, this is where I tell you to go elsewhere. This story takes place across timelines, worlds, liminal spaces and selves. Characters shed lives and understanding, merging or separating, with breathless frequency. Vajra Chandrasekera’s prose unspools, sometimes fabulous, sometimes blunt – I couldn’t look away. I was fascinated.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Ready or Not
author: Cara Bastone
description: Eve is pregnant after a one-night stand, and even more surprising than her pregnancy? The reactions to it. Her best friend, struggling with infertility, emotionally ghosts her. Eve’s baby daddy is in the picture, but it’s complicated. Thankfully, Eve has Shep, who has been hanging out in the friend zone for years. He literally shows up with a bike that has a baby seat attached. If that’s not New York City for “I love you,” then what is? And, sure, this is a love story. But it’s also about all the ways love shows up. Eve’s disappointing friend and baby daddy try to show up the best ways they can. And at the end of the day, that’s the ideal family, right? Never perfect but always trying.

title: Real Americans
author: Rachel Khong
description: In the final third of this novel, a Chinese immigrant notes how she and her American-born roommate eat an apple differently. I attuned so closely to this observation as an American-born Chinese that I immediately shared it with my mother, who immigrated to the U.S. around the same time as the character does in the book. There are many such crystalline moments in this intergenerational saga, which also employs subtle science fiction elements in its portrait of one family’s attempts to obviate the pain of the past and set new narratives into motion.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture"]

title: Rebel Girl: My Life as a Feminist Punk
author: Kathleen Hanna
description: As both a singer and author, Kathleen Hanna’s iconic voice bellows, soars and dances defiantly; it splatters bright reds, pinks and violets over a gray and black world. Unsurprisingly, Hanna’s memoir is raw, clever and irreverent, with moments that are gut-wrenching, funny and poignant – much like your favorite Bikini Kill record. In Rebel Girl, Hanna finally tells the story of her life so far in her own words and double-dares the audience to come along for the entire wild and compelling journey.

title: Rednecks
author: Taylor Brown
description: During the Battle of Blair Mountain in 1921, around 10,000 miners from all races revolted against the mine owners that fought their union, the government (which sided with the owners) and the state militia. It was the largest labor uprising in American history, and it was a violent, important chapter in the fight for worker protection. Rednecks is a superb historical novel that mixes real people with fictitious characters to bring this story to the page in outstanding detail – and with vivid scenes that will stay with readers long after they finish the book.

title: Rejection: Fiction
author: Tony Tulathimutte
description: Rejection hurts – but what happens when you just can’t move on from it? This novel of connected stories explores the lives of people stuck in the downward spiral of rejection, huffing the fumes of their own shame. From a male feminist-turned-incel to a slang-slinging startup bro, the depths of each character’s solipsism turn them grotesque, like monsters of the deep sea. The book reads a bit like Reddit’s AITA page but better, sicker, freakier. It sounds punishing (and it kind of is – I had to put the book down at times just to escape the relentless cringe), but it’s also one of the funniest books I’ve read in years and a smart take on how the internet breaks our brains.

title: Relinquished: The Politics of Adoption and the Privilege of American Motherhood
author: Gretchen Sisson
description: Gretchen Sisson’s research and careful retelling of first/birth mothers’ experiences sheds light on the people who are too often ignored, dehumanized and erased within the institution of adoption. This book deepened my understanding of how adoption, while typically viewed as a noble, feel-good form of family building, actually hinges on the trauma of family separation. Relinquished reveals the structural forces behind this loss, commonly blamed on the individual failures of a mother or birth parents. These are interviews that broadened my understanding of reproductive justice and myself as an adopted person. It’s essential reading in this era of reproductive rights under threat, for anyone who has thought of adoption as “a simple alternative” to abortion, and anyone considering adoption as a family plan.

title: Reservoir Bitches: Stories
author: Dahlia de la Cerda, translated by Heather Cleary and Julia Sanches
description: It’s an undeniably rich time in Mexican literature, and we’re lucky that so much great work is being translated into English for American readers. Many will be familiar with Valeria Luiselli and Yuri Herrera, but authors like Fernanda Melchor and Dahlia de la Cerda shine a light on femicide and violence against women south of the U.S. border. De la Cerda’s debut links together 13 first-person stories about women connected to a cartel boss’s daughter who aspires to be a “superhot housewife” while ordering killings. Prepare to be disturbed.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Same as It Ever Was
author: Claire Lombardo
description: Plenty of books have messy heroines – palatable, quirky ladies who (gasp) confess that motherhood is hard or that they sometimes hate their husbands. And then there is Julia, the protagonist of Same as It Ever Was. Julia is a frayed, spiky knot of insecurity and anxiety and loneliness and self-sabotage, a woman who finds herself falling apart as a stay-at-home mother to a 3-year-old in the 1980s. When she meets Helen, a sage older woman with an impossibly dreamy life, Julia feels she has found a life preserver. Their short, intense friendship has consequences that reverberate throughout Julia’s life. Calling this a drama about family doesn’t do Claire Lombardo’s novel justice – it’s an almost uncomfortably perceptive exploration of what it feels like to not feel at home in the world … which is to say, it’s deeply relatable.

title: Sandwich
author: Catherine Newman
description: This slim volume depicts delicate balances and shifting dynamics between “Rocky” (Rachel) and her husband, Nick; their young-adult children; and her aging parents during their annual vacation to Cape Cod. Rocky’s week gives her time to reflect on marriage, parenting and a range of reproductive issues amid past secrets and anxiety about change. Throw in some septic issues and a cat named “Chicken,” and author Catherine Newman unfurls roiling emotions propelling readers from joyful and tender scenes to teary heartaches and laugh-out-loud moments. While some will find the situations realistic and others may strongly reject choices made, readers and book clubbers will find ample opportunities to dissect the characters and events.

title: Service
author: Sarah Gilmartin
description: Hannah lands a job as a server in Dublin’s hottest new restaurant, led by chef Daniel Costello. It’s a good job, with good wages and staff camaraderie. Plus, Daniel’s taken an interest in helping Hannah’s career advance. But years later, after she’s left the job, many of Hannah’s old colleagues accuse Daniel of sexual assault. As Daniel faces trial, Hannah comes to terms with her own experiences that summer. In a story narrated by Hannah, Daniel and Daniel’s wife, Julie, Sarah Gilmartin weaves a compelling story about consent, power and what we choose to forget.

title: Sex, Lies and Sensibility
author: Nikki Payne
description: Set in the heart of vacationland, Nora Dash and Ennis “Bear” Freeman are both fighting uphill battles. After her dad dies, Nora inherits some serious family drama – and a rundown cottage in Maine. Now, Nora and her sister have just months to turn the place into a successful resort. Meanwhile, Bear’s struggling with his own business of guiding visitors through his native Abenaki land. The tours take him through Nora’s backyard and the two team up. Their chemistry is off the charts as they spend hours working and finding stress relief in long runs through the Maine woods. But both are keeping secrets and have let shame work its way through their lives like an invasive species. The two have to figure out how to move forward once those secrets spill out.

title: Sidetracks
author: Bei Dao, translated by Jeffrey Yang
description: Bei Dao’s Sidetracks, translated with cinematic eloquence by Jeffrey Yang, defines the poet’s choice of the road less traveled as resistance against both totalitarianism and aesthetic orthodoxy. A leading figure of meng long shi – or “misty,” “no direction home” poetry – who galvanized the 1989 pro-democracy movement in China, Bei Dao employs paradox and parataxis to portray his misguided Red Guard youth as “prisoner of the lost road” and the time lost via exile and regained via the poetic imagination.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Slow Productivity: The Lost Art of Accomplishment Without Burnout
author: Cal Newport
description: If you’re the typical knowledge worker, your life is overwhelmed by a dizzying flurry of emails and Slack messages breaking your focus every few minutes. You breathlessly ricochet from task to task yet never get enough real work done. Stop. Take a deep breath. Then read Slow Productivity, which expounds on productivity expert Cal Newport’s tripartite philosophy of 1) do fewer things, 2) work at a natural pace and 3) obsess over quality. He provides practical hacks to implement these principles into your life, while weaving in examples of how deep thinkers such as Jane Austen embodied slow productivity. Newport writes, “The way we’re working no longer works.” But if enough knowledge workers embrace slow productivity, we can revolutionize the world of work.

title: Small Rain
author: Garth Greenwell
description: A new novel from Garth Greenwell is cause for celebration. Small Rain takes place during the terrifying, early days of the COVID-19 pandemic. The protagonist has a blindingly painful tear in his aorta that lands him in an isolated Iowa City hospital room. He narrates from his bed, where his intimate portraits of each orderly, nurse and doctor open doors to human kindness. This book is a lyrical meditation on life and death, sickness and health, the birth of love, and an examination of human frailty and strength. As always with Greenwell, Small Rain is also an erotic celebration of gay love.

title: Smoke and Ashes: Opium's Hidden Histories
author: Amitav Ghosh
description: This is a gripping tale of how the British became history’s first narco state, curiously, to help pay for the tea its people so loved to drink. Amitav Ghosh narrates how the British forced opium into China, creating a market by creating addicts. But opium did so much more. Ghosh investigates how it created many of the modern merchant families of India and the United States, including the fortunes of the Delanos (Roosevelt’s maternal grandfather) and the Forbeses. But perhaps the most important part of this book is how Ghosh looks at the history of opium through the prism of what we know now about opioid addiction, and the relatively newfound sympathy we have toward addicts – white addicts.

title: Sociopath: A Memoir
author: Patric Gagne
description: Shame. Embarrassment. Empathy. From a very young age, Patric Gagne knew she didn’t feel these emotions. She also knew it was best not to tell anyone. Gagne bluntly outlines childhood tales of lying, stealing and home invasion – her efforts to feel anything but an emotional void. She spent much of her young adulthood grappling with the idea that being a sociopath didn’t mean she was destined for violence or jail. This candid memoir is a gripping firsthand view into the “emotional learning disability” of sociopathy.

title: Someone You Can Build a Nest In
author: John Wiswell
description: A man-eating monster lurks in a rundown manor. Her name is Shesheshen and she’s tired of people always barging into her swamp pit trying to kill her and messing up her decor. This time, however, she eats the wrong person and winds up pretending to be human to keep the real humans off her back. She also happens to fall for Homily, a woman struggling to survive her abusive family. Secrets pile up as Homily and Shesheshen sort out their queerplatonic feelings toward each other, culminating in a fight that will redefine their futures. It’s a wonderfully queer and fantastically strange novel that has a lot to say about being asexual in an allosexual world.

title: Somewhere Beyond the Sea
author: TJ Klune
description: The inhabitants of Marsyas Island are back in this warm, cozy sequel to The House in the Cerulean Sea. Arthur Parnassus looks deceptively ordinary, except he can turn into a phoenix. And he’s not the only magical being around –  he and his partner, Linus Baker, are parents to six magical children in the island’s orphanage. Keeping their children safe in a world wary of their powers isn’t always easy, especially when the human-run government sends an inspector to their home. TJ Klune delivers another heartwarming read about belonging, identity and found family, with plenty of laugh out loud moments along the way.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Love & Romance"]

title: State of Paradise
author: Laura van den Berg
description: State of Paradise is an enchanting and complex study of Florida’s psychogeography that is also a creepy story about people who went missing mysteriously, ghosts, cults and technology. Written in a unique voice and dealing with things like family and the lingering effects of the pandemic, this is one of those novels that defy categorization. That said, the only thing that matters is that it is sharp and superbly written. It’s, perhaps Laura van den Berg’s most bizarre and wildly entertaining novel to date, and that’s saying a lot.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Americana"]

title: Supercommunicators: How to Unlock the Secret Language of Connection
author: Charles Duhigg
description: Self-help books often leave me feeling deflated. The checklists of shortcuts and habits to optimize your life are enticing, but who has the time for all that? Supercommunicators is different. The simple premise drew me in: Why do so many important conversations end poorly? And Charles Duhigg’s anecdote-heavy writing makes for a breezy read (or listen in my case). I came away not feeling intimidated, but encouraged. One takeaway still often crosses my mind: When a conversation begins to go south, stop defending yourself and start asking questions.

title: Swift River
author: Essie Chambers
description: After her beloved father mysteriously disappears, Diamond and her mom find themselves living hand to mouth in a faded New England mill town where Diamond is the lone Black resident. Why did a previous generation of Black families abandon it? This propulsive and poetic first novel, by an accomplished documentary film producer, grounds a tender coming-of-age narrative in a history of migration, marginalization and imagination. Threaded through every step of Diamond’s journey is her deadpan wit; of one ramshackle dwelling, she observes, “the whole house looks like it’s having a cigarette.” And she reflects, when a heartbreaking legal issue is finally resolved, “That was the thing about a racist town. It got to decide when it would be kind.”

title: Swimming Pretty: The Untold Story of Women in Water
author: Vicki Valosik
description: With a history that harks back to the “diving girls” of the early 20th century, synchronized swimming has always been a curious mix of pageantry, grace and athleticism. In Swimming Pretty, Vicki Valosik takes a deep dive into the evolution of “artistic swimming” (as the sport was rebranded in 2017) and the larger story of women in water. I usually try to put my phone away while reading, but I broke my own rule several times over for this book. How could I not pause to pull up a video of a water-skiing Esther Williams being lifted from water to sky by a helicopter, or to marvel at the Chinese team’s gold-medal performance at the Paris Olympics?

title: Sylvia's Second Act
author: Hillary Yablon
description: Sylvia is a retiree who hates Boca. So when she finds her husband cheating on her with a neighbor she also can’t stand, it’s not the end of something, but a chance to start anew. Sure, it’s not what she expected 63 would be for her, but with an octogenarian friend widow from Florida, she moves to New York to try to make it in the business she abandoned decades ago: wedding planning. Relationships are complicated, celebrating love can be fraught. But Sylvia brings pragmatism, grace and good humor to even the thorniest situations. Hilarious, heartwarming and unexpected.

title: Table for Two: Fictions
author: Amor Towles
description: The first half of this jaunty short-story collection takes place in New York. Among the memorable characters are a Russian immigrant whose chief role in life is to stand in lines; a young antiquarian bookstore employee who gets more than he bargains for in his desire for life experience; and a seemingly straight-laced family man with a big Wall Street job, whose secret pastime, once discovered, upends his and his loved ones’ lives. The second half, devoted entirely to the novella “Eve in Hollywood,” is set in Los Angeles during Tinseltown’s Golden Age. The pithy, film noir-ish thriller picks up where the author’s 2011 novel Rules of Civility left off – with the plucky, scar-faced adventuress, Evelyn Ross, deftly saving the honor of a host of Hollywood starlets.

title: Tell Me Everything
author: Elizabeth Strout
description: Elizabeth Strout loves her characters so much, whether they are chronically lonely and emotionally fragile, like Lucy Barton and Bob Burgess, or curmudgeonly and self-absorbed, like Olive Kitteridge and William Gerhardt, that she keeps checking in on them. In Tell Me Everything, she brings together several beloved protagonists from her earlier novels. They connect through storytelling and the shared gift of being truly listened to and heard. “I get it,” they keep saying to each other in this endearing ode to empathy.

title: The Abandoners: On Mothers and Monsters
author: Begoña Gómez Urzaiz, translated by Lizzie Davis
description: This year, the literary world couldn’t stop talking about the impossible tension between motherhood and artistic ambition. But nowhere have I seen mention of what remains an ultimate taboo: a mother who, as Begoña Gómez Urzaiz writes, “for a time, wants to act like she isn’t one.” The Abandoners explores the stories of real women like Muriel Spark, as well as fictional characters like Anna Karenina, who decided that they would not sacrifice themselves wholly for their children. In focusing on extreme stories, the collection illuminates how our fears of “monstrous” mothers speak to the judgments we make about all women’s choices.

title: The Antique Hunter's Guide to Murder
author: C.L. Miller
description: Years after their estrangement, the protege of an antique dealer is pulled back to her hometown to try to solve her mentor’s murder. Freya Lockwood is an expert appraiser and can see that something is clearly amiss with the items she’s valuing, while she’s filling in on a job for Arthur Crockleford after his death. Freya must make connections quickly before she becomes the next victim of a nefarious network of ne’er-do-wells with whom Arthur had become entangled. Written by the daughter of an Antiques Roadshow pro, this story pushes us to consider what the items we ascribe meaning and value to say about us in turn.

title: The Backyard Bird Chronicles
author: Amy Tan
description: In 2016, Amy Tan, author of The Joy Luck Club, despaired that the world was “turning uglier and uglier,” she told NPR in an interview this year. So she turned to nature, spending up to 10 hours a day watching and sketching birds right outside her window. The result is a delightful illustrated nature journal about the joys of bird-watching, featuring detailed drawings of the birds that visited Tan in her backyard.

title: The Bog Wife
author: Kay Chronister
description: The Haddesley siblings know this: They are bound by ancient compact to care for the bog. In return, the bog cares for them and provides a wife for the eldest Haddesley son. The rules are strict; their lives, isolated. But now their father is dying, the bog is failing, and the sibling who left must be called back. The Bog Wife builds slowly, effectively weaving claustrophobic dread with family dysfunction, myth with secrets. Eerie eco-horror meets bog gothic is a subgenre of gothic I didn’t know I needed until now. For fans of Jeff VanderMeer, T. Kingfisher, and James Frazer’s The Golden Bough.

title: The Book of Love
author: Kelly Link
description: The Book of Love is Kelly Link’s first novel, ambitious in scope and sharp of teeth, which it will set into your heart. Laura, Daniel and Mo died one year ago; they, along with another, are brought back by their teacher, who gives them fairy-tale tasks, such as learn magic and remember how they died. Hanging over their heads: Only two get to stay. Nobody else writes a sentence or renders the mundane into the magical like Kelly Link. No other book made me cry quite so much or love quite so hard. The Book of Love is a masterpiece.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: The Briar Club
author: Kate Quinn
description: At Briarwood House, women young and old rent rooms from a bitter landlord who resents the children left behind by her ex-husband. But with the arrival of widow Grace March, these disparate boarders slowly become something more than just housemates. With the novel set during the height of McCarthyism in the 1950s, discussions of the fear of communism are mixed with in-depth portraits of each resident Kate Quinn employs to weave a tale. This rare female view of the era starts with a murder and then takes us back in time to slowly unfold a mystery and a drama that are compelling, warm and, at times, heartbreaking.

title: The Brides of High Hill (The Singing Hills Cycle, Book 5)
author: Nghi Vo
description: I love a story as much about observation as it is about storytelling. I love having my expectations thoroughly upended. And I loved The Brides of High Hill, the latest addition to The Singing Hills Cycle series. Once again we follow Cleric Chih, who is accompanying a young bride to her husband-to-be’s opulent estate. Something stinks, and it isn’t just the strange, monstrous talismans hidden around buildings, or the rot-taken library. Nobody is quite what they seem. Silence is a curse or complicity. Bad things happen to good and bad people. Author Nghi Vo is so good.

title: The Bright Sword: A Novel of King Arthur
author: Lev Grossman
description: This eminently worthy successor to The Magicians series explores what happens after the death of King Arthur fractures the Round Table through the eyes of Collum, a bastard backwater nobody with dreams of knighthood. Joining up with Camelot’s castoffs, including second-tier knights and enchantress Nimue, he confers with queens and duels dark fairies in a bloody and disillusioning battle of succession as they seek “a new sword, a new stone.” This tome is sweepingly epic yet accessible, transforming legends back into flesh-and-blood creatures. Most of all, it allows us to relate to Collum’s gleeful awareness that he is inhabiting an epic narrative in the making – as well as his grim acceptance that this adventure would have been impossible in the old Camelot.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Bullet Swallower
author: Elizabeth Gonzalez James
description: The Bullet Swallower tells two stories about the same family. The first follows Antonio Sonoro in 1895 as he tries to rob a train to get out of his desperate financial situation. Instead of riches, he finds the Texas Rangers on his trail and a lot of violence. The second narrative is that of Jaime Sonoro, a renowned actor and singer in 1960s Mexico who learns about his family from a book and wants to do everything in his power to clear the Sonoro name. Part family saga, part Western adventure, and part historical fiction with some magical realism sprinkled in, this novel will appeal to readers of all genres.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Historical Fiction", "Family Matters"]

title: The Butcher of the Forest
author: Premee Mohamed
description: Lost children. A haunted forest. A rescue mission where danger lurks at all times. Premee Mohamed’s The Butcher of the Forest is a superb mix of horror and fantasy that also manages to discuss things like trust and the way power and riches don’t guarantee happiness. A short, engaging novel that almost demands to be devoured in a single sitting.

title: The Cemetery of Untold Stories
author: Julia Alvarez
description: The Cemetery of Untold Stories is a novel about being haunted by the stories we don’t write. A mix of folklore and magical realism, this is an entertaining story about a writer who uses a chunk of land to bury her characters so they can rest in peace and then must deal with the consequences of said characters developing a mind of their own. Another great innovative novel from a literary icon.

title: The City and Its Uncertain Walls
author: Haruki Murakami, translated by Philip Gabriel
description: Picture this: As a teenager, you find love and create a world of your own with your beloved. Then, suddenly, she disappears and, as an adult, you find yourself in that same world of your imagination. Is there a way back to reality, or to find the one you miss the most? And is the reality you thought you had actually real at all? Haruki Murakami’s latest novel is a profound work of love, loss and yearning that exceeds the highs of his previous works. It’s a book that further cements him as a leading figure in modern magical realism, and one of our most interesting authors today.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The City in Glass
author: Nghi Vo
description: The city of Azril is luminous and thriving – until angels come and raze it to the ground. This shatters the heart of the city’s guardian demon, Vitrine, who curses one angel in particular to haunt the city forever. A sweeping, gorgeously written narrative follows, where an angel and demon work together to build the future of a new Azril. And though Vitrine and her angel grow more bound to each other, it is Vitrine’s love for the city that is at the heart of this book. Through her eyes, we see the city rebuilt through grief, loss and hope.

title: The Cliffs
author: J. Courtney Sullivan
description: An empty house, faded to a soft purple, perches high above the sea in Awadapquit, Maine. The house calls out to Jane Flanagan, a lonely working-class teenager. Jane begins cutting classes to explore the purple house and to read outside under its trees. Thus begins J. Courtney Sullivan’s quintessential page-turner of a ghost story, eerie and atmospheric. When Jane returns to Awadapquit after her mother’s death, she’s a professional woman in her 30s, tormented by personal demons: alcoholism and guilt. The Cliffs is a novel to get lost in and to remain haunted by.

title: The Dead Cat Tail Assassins
author: P. Djèlí Clark
description: Author P. Djèlí Clark is on “auto-buy” for me. If he has a book available, I’ll purchase it. This book is a little lighter and a whole lot funnier than his other fare, but it still hits that same high-quality mark. In the city of Tal Abisi lives Eveen the Eviscerator, an unkillable assassin with a very particular set of skills. Skills she has acquired over a very long career. Skills that make her a nightmare for the people at the pointy ends of her blades. All except for one teenage girl she can’t bring herself to kill. When she fails to complete her contract, it kicks off a series of twisty, sinister events involving patriarchal jerks, edgelords getting their comeuppance, a pissed-off goddess and wibbly-wobbly, timey-wimey strangeness.

title: The Djinn Waits a Hundred Years
author: Shubnum Khan
description: The djinn of the title – I pictured a depressed Grinch – haunts this comforting dose of tropes: A girl with a deceased mom moves into an old, possibly magic house with an inaccessible area. Blocked-off rooms being irresistible to teenage main characters, Sana Malek digs her way in, uncovering a tragic family secret or two. The twists and revelations that follow aren’t exactly jaw-dropping, but are emotionally wrenching enough to clear out the old tear ducts without leaving a grief hangover.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters", "Dark"]

title: The Enigma Girls: How Ten Teenagers Broke Ciphers, Kept Secrets, and Helped Win World War II
author: Candace Fleming
description: Ah, the rare World War II history that doesn’t involve creating the bomb (apologies to Oppenheimer). Imagine you’re a teen in England during the rise of Hitler. You want to help with the war effort, so you’ve been recruited to monitor communications, break codes and catalog German intelligence information at Bletchley Park. The catch? When the war is over, you will never be able to tell anyone what you did. Candace Fleming cleverly cuts up the narratives of 10 distinct young women with additional (and fun!) spy and code-breaking information. There are pranks, dancing and romance, even alongside the difficult moments. At last, the people who helped break the German ciphers get their long-delayed day in the sun. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Extinction of Irena Rey
author: Jennifer Croft
description: This book has so many layers! Let’s start with the premise. Eight translators meet up at the home of a famous Polish novelist to translate her latest work – which is apparently so brilliant it could change the world! – into their respective home languages. But their beloved author goes missing, setting off their search for her in the nearby Białowieża forest – filled with so many layers of wilderness! The narrator is the Spanish translator, but we’re reading the story in English – it’s been translated by the English translator. Those two don’t get along. More layers! If you like language, literature – and fungi – this wild ride of a very esoteric mystery is for you.

title: The Familiar
author: Leigh Bardugo
description: It is the Spanish Golden Age, and kitchen maid Luzia has secrets to hide: her skill at magic and her Jewish heritage. When her employer discovers her spells, Luzia is entered into a tournament to find King Philip, who hopes to increase his military standing, a champion. She is trained by the strange creature Santángel, an immortal with a mysterious past. This is a gorgeously lush, vividly written book that shines with its strong cast of characters. Luzia is a hero you’ll find yourself rooting for right from the start, and the magic system in this world is a breath of fresh air. Once again, Leigh Bardugo proves she never misses the mark when it comes to intricately building fantastical worlds – leaving you thinking about them long after the last page is turned.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance"]

title: The Formula: How Rogues, Geniuses, and Speed Freaks Reengineered F1 into the World's Fastest-Growing Sport
author: Joshua Robinson and Jonathan Clegg
description: Cheating scandals, intentional crashes, rule changes, media deals, host countries with questionable human rights records … Joshua Robinson and Jonathan Clegg weave through nearly 80 years to explore how Formula One went from small garages in England to the global phenomenon it is today. You’ll be astounded at what some sponsors, executives, teams and drivers have done to keep the sport afloat or keep the world championship in reach. Whether you’ve been a fan for decades or you just discovered the sport through Netflix’s Drive to Survive, this is a fascinating and fast-paced account of the business, finance and entertainment sides of Formula One.

title: The Fox Wife
author: Yangsze Choo
description: There’s a little bit of mystery and mysticism on every page of this book. Set in China in the early 1900s, the book centers on two characters in separate but connecting narratives: a fox masquerading as a young woman that’s set out to avenge her daughter’s death and a detective with an affinity for foxes who is working a murder case. It’s clever and observant, with twists and turns, and just the perfect amount of folklore to keep you asking: What is real and what is imagined?

title: The Friday Afternoon Club: A Family Memoir
author: Griffin Dunne
description: This is the memoir for folks who think they hate celebrity memoirs. Yes, you get details about Griffin Dunne’s longtime friendship with the late Carrie Fisher, glamourous Hollywood parties and his acting career. But Dunne also writes with loving honesty about his family: his brother, who struggled with mental illness, his mother, who lived with multiple sclerosis, and his younger sister, Dominique, whose murder devastated the family but also ignited his father Dominick’s career as a true-crime writer. The book is an answer to why Dunne didn’t make it big as an actor: He was a writer all along.

title: The God of the Woods
author: Liz Moore
description: Early one morning in 1975, a summer camp counselor finds an empty bunk – a 13-year-old camper has vanished. As the search begins along the banks of a lake in the Adirondacks, this 500-page drama unfolds – and it is worth every page! Liz Moore’s storytelling captures such an authentic picture of youth, young friendship and family secrets. There are thoughtful, well-developed characters, unexpected revelations, a history of a serial killer recently escaped, captivating storylines, shocking connections and surprising answers to every single mystery along the way. The God of the Woods has become a personal favorite of the year!

title: The History of Sound: Stories
author: Ben Shattuck
description: The stories in The History of Sound deal in intimate histories, linger on the immediacy of a moment felt before it is lost. Preservation is considered from many angles, whether music preserved on wax cylinders, self-preservation in the face of grief, preservation of a relationship or a historical record. Ben Shattuck’s writing is so sure; he is so thoughtful, so reverent of life and its messes, and so very, very good at connecting his stories in unexpected ways. As a collection, The History of Sound sings; Shattuck’s voice is superb — I am definitely now a fan.

title: The Horse
author: Willy Vlautin
description: Al Ward has isolated himself from the world, living in an abandoned mining claim in the Nevada desert. The musician is working on beating his alcoholism and is fixated on memories of his past life, but his reclusive existence comes to an end when a blind, ailing horse shows up. Al knows he has to help, but he has to conquer his own demons first. This is a stunning novel, a new take on the Western that’s overflowing with soul from a writer whose books are just as accomplished as his music.

title: The Hunter
author: Tana French
description: Set in the hills of Western Ireland, this novel picks up the story of characters introduced in 2020’s The Searcher –  retired American detective Cal Hooper and Trey, a teen girl he’s taken under his wing. As Tana French revisits the seemingly bucolic landscape where trouble roils just under the surface, her writing continues to shift from mystery to meditation. While there’s still a knot of questions about crimes – including both fraud and murder – to be untangled, this novel is ultimately about belonging. It’s about the ways in which families do, and don’t, owe each other debts – and the communities we resist, alienate, or become a welcome part of. Morally shaded and complex, it will leave you thinking about who’s right, and what’s wrong, long after you turn the last page.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Husbands
author: Holly Gramazio
description: Lauren leaves her London flat for a bachelorette party one night only to discover a husband at home awaiting her return. Not only was she not married when she left for the night, she doesn’t recognize this man. Slowly she works out that he’s not a threat – and that all evidence on her phone, in conversations with friends and neighbors, and in their apartments points to him being fully integrated into her life. And there he is until he goes into the attic and a different husband emerges, slightly – or drastically – altering Lauren’s life. The pattern continues as Lauren searches for metaphysical clues to what’s going on and wrestles with how to know, if she can ever know, which life is right for her. A rare combination of the truly hilarious and profound.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Humor & Comedy", "Family Matters"]

title: The Last Fire Season: A Personal and Pyronatural History
author: Manjula Martin
description: In 2020, wildfires in California broke records, burning more than 4 million acres. Manjula Martin records what it was like to live through these conflagrations, with lyrical attention to detail, placing her experience in the context of deep ecological history. As she recounts months when the siren on her local firehouse in western Sonoma County blared almost daily, Martin reflects on what it means to make one’s home in a place that is destined to burn and to live “inside a damaged body on a damaged planet.” This meditation on the climate crisis demands attention.

title: The Last Hour Between Worlds
author: Melissa Caruso
description: All Kembral Thorne wants is a break. She’s a new mom, on leave from her day job rescuing people who slip between the layers of reality (no biggie), and she’s looking forward to a few hours at an office party/ball. But the universe has other ideas. Someone is killing the guests at the party in a sinister game for power. And it’s up to Kem to stop it. This book – the first in a series – is like if a game of Clue also took place in the multiverse. Kem’s world is surreal, brutal and deeply entertaining. Melissa Caruso keeps the wild premise in check with a smart writing style that delivers well-timed moments of levity. And if you like sapphic romance, you’ll appreciate the chemistry between Kem and her professional rival Rika, who is as prickly as she is mysterious!

title: The Letters of Emily Dickinson
author: Emily Dickinson, edited by Cristanne Miller and Domhnall Mitchell
description:
This collection is probably the closest thing we’ll ever have to an intimate autobiography of Emily Dickinson. Some 300 previously uncollected letters have been added to this volume, for a grand total of 1,304 letters. They detail a life filled with visitors, the poet’s loyal dog, Carlo, and recipes for doughnuts and coconut cakes. There’s mention of the racist minstrel stereotype Jim Crow, as well as of Florence Nightingale and Walt Whitman. Most likely, the last letter an ailing 55-year-old Dickinson wrote before falling unconscious on May 13, 1886, was to her cousins. It reads:
Little Cousins,
“Called back.” Emily.

title: The Lion Women of Tehran
author: Marjan Kamali
description: The Lion Women of Tehran by Marjan Kamali is an emotionally captivating page-turner about women’s friendship and the fight for women’s rights in Iran, beginning during the Shah’s reign and progressing to almost the present day. Kamali writes beautifully and passionately about friendship, love, youth, conflict, aging and the challenges of sustaining meaningful relationships amid high-stakes Iranian politics. The novel is full of atmosphere, history, great food and two unforgettable heroines, Ellie and Homa, who, despite the adversities they must face and sometimes overcome, will endear and intrigue readers from the opening scenes to the last page – a must-read.

title: The Lost Story
author: Meg Shaffer
description: I fell hard for this novel starring Jeremy and Rafe, young men on a quest to locate friend Emilie’s sister. She is lost in a Narnia-like world, a hidden dimension within a West Virginia forest where they themselves had gone missing as teens. Who becomes transformed into a knight, a prince, or a princess matters less in Meg Shaffer’s luminous storytelling than the slow dawning of gentle queer love between Jeremy and Rafe. That narrative arc unfolds as beautifully as any I’ve encountered, painting a world of bravery and kindness as genuine as it is magical.

title: The Mango Tree: A Memoir of Fruit, Florida, and Felony
author: Annabelle Tometich
description: This family memoir begins with a courtroom scene like no other. After a night in jail, Annabelle Tometich’s mom is charged with firing at a man who, she says, was stealing mangoes from the tree in her front yard. Tometich then hits rewind, taking readers back through her Fort Myers, Fla., childhood – with her Filipino American mom and white dad, a couple whose personality differences do not make them stronger together. The writing is both jewel-like and effortless, and Tometich’s memories – some mundane, some extraordinary – are mesmerizing.

title: The Manicurist's Daughter: A Memoir
author: Susan Lieu
description: In juxtaposing her Vietnamese American family’s refugee trajectory with her mother’s 1996 death at the hands of an uninsured plastic surgeon, Susan Lieu eloquently expresses how this loss has been both an irreparable internal rupture and an ongoing process toward public reckoning. Lieu’s initial zeal to avenge her mother’s death by publicly shaming the unscrupulous doctor (he died of Parkinson’s disease before she had the chance) becomes, over time, a way to redefine her mother’s legacy within the evolving lives of surviving family members who still struggle with the social stigma from the young matriarch’s death. By exploring public and private ways to honor her mother’s memory, Lieu’s memoir encompasses both social and familial dimensions.

title: The Mesmerist
author: Caroline Woods
description: Caroline Woods’ The Mesmerist is an immersive and compelling story inspired by the Bethany Home for Unwed Mothers and front-page news about a serial killer in 1894 Minneapolis. Deftly mixing crime fiction and historical women’s fiction in a tightly plotted, tension-filled tale, the novel focuses on three very different women who work together to save their home – and stop a killer. Abby, the staunch Quaker; May, seeking her happily-ever-after; and Faith, who arrives at the Bethany Home mute, are unforgettable female characters in a story of magic, mystery and murder. The Mesmerist will linger long after you turn the last page.

title: The Message
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
description: The Message is an urgent reevaluation of the stories we are told and tell ourselves about the world. Ta-Nehisi Coates travels to Dakar, Senegal, where he confronts “the idea of a Black civilization”; to South Carolina, where he attends a school board meeting about banning his book Between the World and Me; and to Israel and the Israeli-occupied West Bank, where he witnesses “the separate and unequal nature of Israeli rule” over Palestinians. Throughout, Coates untangles the nationalist myths that have long propped up grievous inequities and charts a new path forward for how we can use storytelling to face difficult truths.

title: The Midnight Feast
author: Lucy Foley
description: The opening weekend of an exclusive “woo-woo, but still chic” wellness resort run by a wealthy and ambitious influencer (think Lady Macbeth if she read Goop) is the setting for Lucy Foley’s latest locked-room thriller. Here, Foley’s usual techniques of unraveling the central mystery through multiple timelines and narrators are paralleled by the spiraling events of the weekend, which careen from juice shots and yoga on the lawn to a chaotic climax at a hallucinogen-fueled bacchanal where hidden identities and multiple murders are brought to light. It is an absolute bonkers delight (and I haven’t even mentioned the local bird cult yet …).

title: The Ministry of Time
author: Kaliane Bradley
description: You’d think a novel about the bureaucracy of a time-travel government agency might be kinda, boring? But from the moment you meet the book’s enigmatic protagonist – as she starts a new job in the U.K.’s top secret new time travel agency – to the introduction of the dashing Graham Gore, an 1847 Arctic explorer plucked through time, you’ll be hooked. Come for the romance, stay for the unraveling of a mystery, the nuanced, genre-bending treatises on race and identity, and the long-lingering ideas on colonialism, empires and the mutability of history.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Most
author: Jessica Anthony
description: Jessica Anthony serves an ace with this tightly constructed novel, a portrait of a marriage at its breakpoint that highlights the trade-offs people make in life. The Most spans a single, unseasonably warm November day in 1957, during which the wife, a former intercollegiate tennis champion, slips into her apartment complex’s kidney-shaped pool and refuses to get out. To break through the marital impasse with her husband, she deploys a cunning tennis strategy from which the novel takes its title.

title: The Mountains Are High
author: Alec Ash
description: For years since China’s economic reform, many ambitious young Chinese have migrated to bustling cities to get rich and seek career fulfillment. But the highly competitive rat race has also forced some to rethink their ultimate pursuit. All this happened as China’s economic growth slowed and job opportunities waned. In recent years, phrases describing those who opt for a pressure-free life, such as “tang ping” (lying flat), have stormed into China’s everyday lexicon. Some of the urban escapees – including author Alec Ash himself – ended up in smaller cities such as Dali, in Southwest China. In this beautifully written book, Ash introduces us to them. They fittingly call Dali “Dalifornia,” for its chilled vibes, California-like good weather and picturesque mountains.

title: The Murder of Mr. Ma
author: SJ Rozan and John Shen Yen Nee
description: I don’t read much in the way of historical mysteries, but the premise of this book was too tempting to pass up. In London in 1924, the scholar Lao She meets Judge Dee Ren Jie. Dee is investigating the murder of a man who worked alongside him in the Chinese Labour Corps during World War I. Their investigation takes them all over London and into the homes and businesses of Chinese immigrants fighting to find a place in a country mired in xenophobia and anti-Chinese sentiment. It’s a bit murder mystery, a bit martial arts action movie and a bit historical drama; think Warrior meets Sherlock Holmes.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The New India: The Unmaking of the World's Largest Democracy
author: Rahul Bhatia
description: An unrepentant Nazi. A movement to purify Hinduism in response to British colonialism. A dusty book on a forgotten bookshelf in Delhi. A tech evangelist creates a database of every Indian citizen, but why? A Muslim man fighting for justice, tilting at well-worn windmills. Rahul Bhatia weaves all these elements together to look at the endemic challenges to India’s democracy – and also the newer challenges – threatening it all. This book should be heavy, yet it is so readable, so swiftly sketched, that it’s a page-turner. It’s like a mystery novel, with India as the main character.

title: The Nightmare Box and Other Stories
author: Cynthia Gómez
description: This was hands down my favorite horror anthology of 2024. The stories are loudly and proudly Latinx, queer, feminist and working-class. You’ll encounter Black Panthers and vampires, witches and magical clothes, brave activists and cops who get what’s coming to them. Longtime San Francisco Bay Area residents will love picking up the references to local sights – places that never hit the tourist tours or the big bestsellers. Of the 12 stories in the anthology, the eponymous story is the one I think about the most. Set in 1970, the piece is about a woman named Serena who can create custom-made nightmares. She’s forced to work against the interests of some local activists and decides to resist in the only way she knows how.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Dark"]

title: The Other Olympians: Fascism, Queerness, and the Making of Modern Sports
author: Michael Waters
description: When Zdeněk Koubek announced in 1935 that he would live as a man after his rise to celebrity in women’s sports, it seemed that the public was poised to accept gender expression outside the binary. But a small group of international sporting officials developed a system of “sex testing” that still affects athletes today. The Other Olympians offers a retrospective of the sporting world’s attempt to understand, define and ultimately police sex in competition. Against the backdrop of the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Michael Waters weaves dense history with narrative storytelling while never failing to center the humanity of the athletes and activists of the time. Within a fraught history, Waters uncovers stories of queer joy and resilience still relevant today.

title: The Phoenix Crown
author: Kate Quinn and Janie Chang
description: This book’s gorgeous prose weaves together four diverse women – an aspiring opera singer, a visionary painter, a brilliant botanist and a talented Chinatown seamstress – in 1906 San Francisco days before an earthquake nearly destroys it. Friendly acquaintances before the quake, these women unite to find justice against a greedy, murdering hustler amid the city’s ruins, and across continents. Each character is layered with historical facts; some are even based on real people. This historical drama is so fascinating – it will transport you to a remarkable moment in U.S. history.

title: The Physics of Sorrow
author: Georgi Gospodinov, translated by Angela Rodel
description: In this brilliant book, Georgi Gospodinov considers the concept of “weight” in physics. He writes, “The past, sorrow, literature – only these three weightless whales interest me.” This book raises vexing questions about the human condition, traveling down labyrinthine digressions about topics that consume us – life, death, social woes, war, peace, old age, youth. And, above all, the creation of literature. For Gospodinov, time is an artifice and present, past and future slide around like pieces on a chessboard. This fragmented novel coheres into a remarkable whole, a winding maze through Bulgarian communism, art, literature, history, the personal past, love, sorrow and so much more.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Return of Ellie Black
author: Emiko Jean
