description: When Ellie Black, a teenager who went missing two years ago, is found alive, it turns Detective Chelsey Calhoun’s world upside down. Twenty years ago, Chelsey’s own sister went missing. Now, she must figure out what happened to Ellie during her disappearance, while also confronting complicated family memories. Emiko Jean digs into family trauma and how the past never really leaves us, in this engrossing, fast-paced book. I had a hard time putting it down.

title: The Secret History of Bigfoot: Field Notes on a North American Monster
author: John O'Connor
description: More than a nonfiction book about Bigfoot, John O’Connor’s The Secret History of Bigfoot is a witty, impeccably researched deep dive into the subculture of folks who live for the famous bipedal ape rumored to lurk in many forests across the country. Surprisingly political, unexpectedly hilarious and incredibly informative, this is a great serious book about a topic that most people quickly dismiss.

title: The Seventh Veil of Salome
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: Silvia Moreno-Garcia refuses to confine herself to one genre, and that’s great news for readers. Her latest is a historical novel set in Golden Age Hollywood; it follows Vera, a young Mexican actor who lands the role of Salome in a big-budget biblical epic, and Nancy, a racist striver who can’t stand the young newcomer. Moreno-Garcia perfectly captures the feel of 1950s movies with her expert pacing and snappy dialogue. If the thought of a Turner Classic Movies marathon puts a smile on your face, this one couldn’t be more up your alley.

title: The Showman: Inside the Invasion That Shook the World and Made a Leader of Volodymyr Zelensky
author: Simon Shuster
description: In this cinematic page-turner, Time correspondent Simon Shuster paints a vivid portrait of the Ukrainian president, who honed his powerful communication skills during decades as one of Ukraine’s most popular comedians. Shuster charts the rise from naïve political novice to steely – and unforgiving – wartime president. Deeply reported and deftly written, this book is a feat not only because it sheds light on one of today’s most consequential political figures, but also the history that shaped him and the tectonic shift in geopolitics that he’s now forced to navigate.

title: The Spamalot Diaries
author: Eric Idle
description: If making comedy is hard, making a Broadway musical is even harder, as Eric Idle describes in The Spamalot Diaries, his chronicle of the creation of the hit musical based on Monty Python and the Holy Grail. Idle fills out his breezy, sly diary entries with emails to and from the creative team; talks about all the songs written, rewritten and dropped from the show; and includes some lyrics – even a song written to the melody of Ravel’s Boléro. Idle showers heaps of praise on the late Mike Nichols, the show’s Tony-winning director, even as he describes some of his insecurities. If you love Monty Python or Broadway musicals, this is an entertaining read.

title: The Stardust Grail
author: Yume Kitasei
description: Maya Hoshimoto is caught between cultures: She was raised on a space colony that wrested independence from Earth and then spent a decade as an alien-art thief, only to give up that life for the stability of Princeton’s graduate anthropology program. But when glitches in the Interstellar Web threaten to cut Earth off from the nodal network, Maya can’t resist returning to fieldwork. Reunited with tentacled alien pal Auncle, she must steal the mythical stardust grail before Earth can. But like Indiana Jones, Maya must choose: save Auncle’s endangered people or secure Earth’s node. Author Yume Kitasei keenly establishes humans as the relative newbies (and the cultural other) among more experienced extraterrestrials, while still showing how even advanced alien species can’t resist looting other cultures’ artifacts.

title: The Stone Home
author: Crystal Hana Kim
description: In this wrenching intergenerational story of survival, Crystal Hana Kim explores a dark period of South Korean history – the military rule of the 1970s and 1980s, when the government sponsored “welfare facilities” for “vagrants” that in reality were internment camps. As the novel unfolds, Eunju Oh, now in her 40s, reveals long-buried secrets of her horrific experience at one such facility, the Stone Home, to the Korean American daughter of Sangchul, who escaped as a teenager. It makes for a compelling exploration of the intoxicating effects of power and the endurance of the human spirit.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Storm We Made
author: Vanessa Chan
description: The Storm We Made follows the Alcantara family during Japan’s invasion of Malaya (now Malaysia) during World War II. Told from multiple points of view and different timelines, it is an exploration into one family’s struggles with racism, status, poverty and war. Vanessa Chan beautifully navigates all these themes and more. By the end of the book, I was left bereft and speechless.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Swans of Harlem: Five Black Ballerinas, Fifty Years of Sisterhood, and Their Reclamation of a Groundbreaking History
author: Karen Valby
description: Journalist Karen Valby reclaims the stories of five Black ballerinas – women who were the bedrock of Arthur Mitchell’s Dance Theatre of Harlem, founded in the wake of Martin Luther King Jr.’s assassination. These dancers came from different walks of life and different geographies, but shared passion and talent for the artform. They performed around the world and opened audiences’ eyes to superlative dancing, as well as new choreography and music. This book describes the challenges and joys of working with Mitchell, a driven and exacting leader. This important story should inspire generations of girls who want to dance.

title: The Tainted Cup
author: Robert Jackson Bennett
description: In the first book of a series, detective Ana Dolabra and her assistant, Dinios Kol, are investigating the murder of a high-ranking military officer. Set in the botanical Empire of Khanum, the story is a masterful combination of rich world-building and compelling mystery. Robert Jackson Bennett – author of The Founders Trilogy – is one of the most exciting fantasy writers I’ve read recently, designing worlds that feel inclusive and expansive. The next adventure of Din and Ana can’t come soon enough.

title: The Threshold of Dissent: A History of American Jewish Critics of Zionism
author: Marjorie N. Feld
description: The world is a very confusing place right now – at least, that’s how it feels to me – so I’m always looking for books that can help me better understand where we are as a society and how we got here. The Threshold of Dissent is one of those books. In clear, careful language, the author illustrates some of the major moments over the past century that have shaped Jewish beliefs about Zionism, anti-Zionism and non-Zionism. It’s a history told with both rigor and compassion – two qualities that seem especially essential when embarking in conversation on such a fraught and contentious subject.

title: The Unclaimed: Abandonment and Hope in the City of Angels
author: Pamela Prickett and Stefan Timmermans
description: Trust me that you’ll be captivated by this book about death. The Unclaimed tells remarkably vivid, intimate stories about four people in Los Angeles whose bodies went unclaimed after they died – a more common phenomenon than you’d probably guess. The authors, both sociologists, follow neighbors and friends whose abiding care goes unrecognized by the state, and local government workers who are determined to bring dignity to people who’ve been abandoned in death. This lyrical and moving book raises profound questions about what we owe our family, our neighbors and our fellow citizens.

title: The Wide Wide Sea: Imperial Ambition, First Contact and the Fateful Final Voyage of Captain James Cook
author: Hampton Sides
description: I’m a sucker for sea stories, for tales of man against nature, for people who put themselves on the line. So I picked up this book on a whim and read it straight through. I knew a sentence or two about James Cook, but not how famous he was in the 1700s or what he accomplished. This account of his final voyage puts you right on his leaky, smelly, cockroach-infested ship. What proves most dramatic is not his struggle with nature but with other people, the Polynesian islanders who Hampton Sides does much to bring to life. This is a story of exploration but also of empire, with all its dark implications.

title: The Women
author: Kristin Hannah
description: “You’re only going to be a nurse until you get married,” her mother said. But Frankie McGrath had other ideas, ones that would lead her away from her wealthy family’s conservative outlook on how daughters should behave. Kristin Hannah’s The Women follows young Frankie’s transformation, when after working as a nurse in California and tending to a wounded soldier, and missing her soldier brother, she joins the Army as a nurse. That takes her from a comfortable life of known expectations to one of the chaos and danger of war, new career opportunities and love. Tangled love. When Frankie returns home, she finds her country still protesting the war and those who served. The Women shines a light on a then-little-known aspect of the war: the women who also served in Vietnam, as nurses.

title: The Wonderful Wisdom of Ants
author: Philip Bunting
description: Think you know ants? Think again. Good humor and surprising facts are the name of the game here. “Ants love: Family. Micronaps. Recycling. Helping others. Being caught on camera carrying stuff way bigger than they are.” Though seemingly simple, the more of this book you read, the more you come to realize how little you may actually know about these tiny workers. This book is chock full of factual hilarity that’ll engender a newfound respect for our hardworking (albeit minuscule) neighbors. And don’t hand this book to just the budding entomologists either. Give it to kids who know next to nothing about ants – it may just convert them. (For ages 4 to 8)

title: The Woods All Black
author: Lee Mandelo
description: Get ready for exceptional historical horror. It’s sometime in 1920s Appalachia and the community of Spar Creek invited a nurse, but Leslie is unwelcome from the moment he arrives. He’s too “mannish” for a proper woman (about that…), marked dangerous by a local pastor who wields religion like a closed fist, and unable to build rapport enough to help another “gay young thing,” Stevie, who has recently become the focal point of deep ugliness. This is a sublime examination of monstrousness, gruesome and graphic, queer and angry. Do you like an unapologetic revenge story? Me too. Look no further.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Dark"]

title: The Work of Art: How Something Comes from Nothing
author: Adam Moss
description: This book isn’t just about the creative process. It illustrates the creative process through scribbles, drafts, sketches and jotted notes. There are more than 40 chapters, each featuring one artist. And that word “artist” covers an enormous range, from sandcastle builders to crossword puzzle constructors to, well, Stephen Sondheim. You could start reading on any page, skip to chapters about the creators you like most, or read it cover to cover. The book might not turn you into a creative genius, but it’ll offer a load of insights into how those people make the things that have earned them the title.

title: There Are Rivers in the Sky
author: Elif Shafak
description: Turkish British writer Elif Shafak captivates with terrific storytelling, emphasizing that human arrogance and frailty can drive history. A page-turner, the novel starts with a single raindrop and follows it through centuries. Shafak involves us with a wide variety of protagonists from around the world; ancient Assyria is as transfixing as immigrant London in the 21st century. The author takes lessons from history to give us messages for the present. Iraq figures as home to vast civilizations, and as a place of conflict today. Whether categorized as Kurdish or Yazidi, one characters says, “We are the memory tribe.”

title: There's Always This Year: On Basketball and Ascension
author: Hanif Abdurraqib
description: I don’t even watch basketball all that much. And yet, there’s something alluring about Hanif Abdurraqib’s meditation on the sport. Because, sure, it’s about hoops and LeBron James and Cleveland and the funny way time works when you’re watching a Game 7. But it’s also about losing loved ones. Fans of Abdurraqib’s work will recognize his rhythms and stylistic flairs that hardly ever fail to draw a reader in and his talent at making you see the beauty in the things he finds beautiful.

title: This Great Hemisphere
author: Mateo Askaripour
description: A wonderful Russian nesting doll of a novel, This Great Hemisphere is  futuristic, dystopian speculative fiction with a wildly inventive and thoughtful allegory of race at the center. It’s also a moving saga about love and family, as well as a twisty political thriller. In the heroine Sweetmint’s world, people like her have second-class citizenship because their skin is invisible. When a political assassination sets off a manhunt for the killer, she’s determined to find her long-missing brother, the unlikely prime suspect, before the security forces can get to him. In my fantasies, I produce the inevitable film adaptation of this incredibly original story.

title: Those Beyond the Wall
author: Micaiah Johnson
description: English grammar can barely handle the premise: Humans from parallel worlds are jumping into other worlds, which kills their doppelgängers in the destination world, where this book takes place. Whew! (The preceding novel, The Space Between Worlds, will help make sense of it.) The wall of the title separates a high-tech, climate-controlled city from Ashtown, the home of protagonist Scales. A mechanic and member of Ashtown’s guardian gang, she tries to figure out what’s happening, amid environmental devastation and family dysfunction and generational trauma and structural inequality and institutional racism and more. Plus, motorcycles!

title: Thyme Travellers: An Anthology of Palestinian Speculative Fiction
author: Sonia Sulaiman
description: Science fiction, fantasy and horror are littered with anthologies, and as a book critic specializing in speculative fiction, I read a lot of them. This is one of the best to come out this year. These 14 stories skip across speculative fiction, hitting folklore, dystopian, magical realism, history and near future. Each centers Palestinian lives, from those who reside in their homeland to those scattered across the diaspora. At once mournful and hopeful, the stories collected here reshaped my perspective. I especially recommend the opening story, “Down Under” by Jumaana Abdu, a powerful piece about searching for home.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Traveling: On the Path of Joni Mitchell
author: Ann Powers
description: NPR music critic Ann Powers turns her gimlet eye to one of America’s most legendary musicians in her latest book, which is part biography and part reflection on the career of the ever-enigmatic Joni Mitchell. Powers does an excellent job putting Mitchell’s music in context and doesn’t shy away from discussing some of the singer-songwriter’s inexplicably offensive stunts, including her character Art Nouveau, whom she portrayed in blackface. This is a careful portrait of a complicated artist, and it will fascinate not just Mitchell’s listeners but anyone who cares about modern American music.

title: Unshrinking: How to Face Fatphobia
author: Kate Manne
description: This book kind of blew my mind right from the start, not only as a health reporter who (I learned) needs to be less sloppy equating people’s size with their health, but also as a person. At least once, I cried from painful realizations and memories. I couldn’t stop talking about this book with fellow parents at school drop-off or at home with my spouse – and I gave a copy to my mom when I finished reading it.

title: Us Fools
author: Nora Lange
description: “Junk kids.” That’s what Joanne and Bernadette, two girls growing up on an Illinois farm, call themselves; they’re neglected by their parents, whose lives have been thrown into turmoil by the 1980s farm crisis. Nora Lange follows the lives of the sisters as they grow up to lead very different lives, both carrying the scars of their childhoods. This novel is tender and exceptionally moving, but also mordantly funny in parts, and it’s a razor-sharp critique of American capitalism. And it’s written so beautifully that it’s hard to believe it’s a debut novel.

title: Victim
author: Andrew Boryga
description: Lying is kind of funny. The stress of someone jumping through increasingly wild hoops to avoid getting caught in a lie is hilarious. Victim is about Javi, a writer from a marginalized community who fudges his way into the kinds of rooms where people say “marginalized” and “community” a lot. The book is a charming critique of the publishing industry and its surface-level attempts at righting societal ills (which is kind of bold for a debut author), while staying empathetic toward the well-meaning individuals who give Javi a shot.

title: Vision: A Memoir of Blindness and Justice
author: David S. Tatel
description: David Tatel has written the book that his friends and admirers always hoped he would write, but expected he would not. One that deals candidly with his “vision” – his blindness, and his years of treating it as an asterisk, all while becoming one of the most prominent and thoughtful judges in the country. This book is both novelistic and introspective in its treatment of his lack of sight – from his love affair with his wife and children, to his “cane lessons,” to his later-in-life affection for his guide dog, Vixen. Along the way, it is also a book about the law, the art of judging and today’s Supreme Court. And it’s fascinating.

title: Wandering Stars
author: Tommy Orange
description: This novel follows multiple generations of a Cheyenne family from the Sand Creek Massacre of 1864 to the Carlisle Indian Industrial School to present-day Oakland, Calif., where we find some of the characters from Tommy Orange’s first book, There There. Trauma and addiction haunt every generation, but Orange gives each character a way to survive, and he creates one of my all-time-favorite matriarchs holding the family together. The resulting work combines rage and despair with wisdom, grace and love – a powerful cocktail that helped me confront the ongoing violence of settler colonialism.

title: We Solve Murders
author: Richard Osman
description: Did I read this and then immediately read every book in the Thursday Murder Club series? Yes, I did, because Richard Osman’s mystery novels are so fun. In We Solve Murders, Osman introduces a new crime-fighting trio: Amy Wheeler is a bodyguard for billionaires, her father-in-law, Steve, is a semiretired London cop obsessed with his cat, and Rosie D’Antonio is a bestselling novelist of indeterminate age currently being threatened by a Russian oligarch. There is a supervillain and there are some murders, but that’s not going to stop our detectives from having a lot of laughs as they travel around the world – or from enjoying the amenities on Rosie’s private plane. While I’m sad that I have to wait until 2025 to read another Richard Osman mystery, I’m happy to have two series to look forward to.

title: We're Alone: Essays
author: Edwidge Danticat
description: Edwidge Danticat is agile when juggling duality. It’s a core feature of We’re Alone, her essay collection that strives for a “kind of aloneness/togetherness.” The title is a nod to both the isolation that life can make us feel and intimacy between reader and writer. And that duality governs so much of what Danticat confronts – displacement, gun violence, hurricanes, political tumult. Things that can end you, or make you anew. “Part of my job as a writer is to wrestle with mortality, both my own and that of others,” she writes. And yet Danticat’s observations feel more like a guide to living – a testament to what writers can offer in difficult times.

title: Whale Fall
author: Elizabeth O’Connor
description: Elizabeth O’Connor’s spare and bracing debut opens with an isolated Welsh island on a precipice. It is September 1938, and a whale has washed ashore, transfixing the 12 families that remain and drawing the attention of a pair of ethnographers from Oxford who are eager to document the islanders’ customs. Initially, Manod Llan, the novel’s gimlet-eyed, 18-year-old narrator, is eager to translate for the ethnographers – their attention stokes her desire to lead a worldlier life. But as Whale Fall unfolds, Manod must reckon with the costs of being seen from the outside.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: What Have We Here?: Portraits of a Life
author: Billy Dee Williams
description: If your only encounter with Billy Dee Williams’ work is his turn as Lando Calrissian, you already know he possesses an inimitable charm. Trust me, then, when I say you want to spend more time getting to know the man behind roles iconic and forgotten alike. What Have We Here? is full of unexpected and interesting stories from an incredible life. His teachers expected him to be the next truly great American painter, before he turned his eye to acting. Triple the charm if you listen to the audio version, read by the author.

title: Whiskey Tender: A Memoir
author: Deborah Jackson Taffa
description: In straightforward and affecting prose, Deborah Jackson Taffa writes about being brought up by a Quechan (Yuma) and Laguna Pueblo father and a Catholic Latina mother, both on and off the Yuma reservation. Although her parents were united in their approach to maintaining a family, their attitudes toward the world diverged in other ways, and Taffa received mixed messages about her Indigeneity, her proximity to whiteness and how she was meant to carry herself. As a teenager, she began to experience anger at the injustices her people were subjected to and, at the same time, began to learn that all change is sacred.

title: Who's Afraid of Gender?
author: Judith Butler
description: Judith Butler, who wrote the groundbreaking 1990 book Gender Trouble, leans into the titular question of her latest work, asking: Why has gender become such a “phantasm” in American life, and what does it tell us about how we’re approaching some of the biggest problems facing us, like climate change and far-right extremism? Butler has a clear perspective – and spells out the dangers of an ascendant “anti-gender ideology.” But it’s also an invitation to consider how we think about gender – and what that might tell us about who we are.

title: Women and Children First
author: Alina Grabowski
description: Alina Grabowski’s kaleidoscopic debut chronicles the lead-up to and aftermath of the mysterious death of a teenage girl, but it’s not a thriller or a whodunit. Set in Nashquitten, a fictional town on the Massachusetts coast, Women and Children First passes narration among a Greek chorus of 10 women and girls linked to the tragedy. Through her pitch-perfect summoning of this intergenerational female cast, Grabowski explores the fickleness of truth, how difficult it is to really see those closest to us and how easy it is to betray one another.

title: Women's Hotel
author: Daniel M. Lavery
description: In Women’s Hotel, we get to live with the midcentury women who reside at the Biedermeier hotel. Not a whole lot happens for most of the book; we just ride around in the characters’ heads (and they are characters) as they reflect on their pasts and overthink interactions with dates or housemates. The stakes are quaintly low. The writing is so charming and clever that I kept finding myself reading passages over and over. I wouldn’t want to live at the Biedermeier, but it sure is a wonderful place to visit.

title: Woo Woo
author: Ella Baxter
description: This art world send-up that doubles as a study of fear follows Melbourne-based conceptual artist Sabine in the week leading up to the gallery opening that might just be her big break. Already vacillating between wild hubris and crushing self-doubt, Sabine unhinges further as she is visited by a stalker who watches her through windows and leaves threatening letters. Ella Baxter – who wrote Woo Woo while being stalked herself – offers a bewildering exploration of exposure and autonomy and of seeing and being seen, through art and in life.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Art", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Worry
author: Alexandra Tanner
description: Alexandra Tanner’s hilarious debut opens in 2019 Brooklyn, where 28-year-old MFA graduate Jules Gold works for a SparkNotes-esque website. Her little sister, Poppy, has come to crash indefinitely, having fled their parents’ house in South Florida. Worry revolves around the sisters’ many anxieties: Is their three-legged rescue dog, Amy Klobuchar, foaming at the mouth from rabies, or is she just dehydrated? Why does their mother have an Instagram account suspiciously similar to those of conspiracy-minded momfluencers? Underneath the absurdity, the novel explores the thorniness of sisterhood and whether we’re different people when we’re around our families.

title: You Dreamed of Empires
author: Álvaro Enrigue, translated by Natasha Wimmer
description: This salty and dark historical fantasia feistily explodes well-worn textbook narratives about the meeting of the Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés and his captains with the Aztec emperor Moctezuma and his entourage in Tenoxtitlan – now Mexico City – in 1519. Álvaro Enrigue’s depiction of the stressed-out, clumsy Cortés and the drugged-out, mercurial Moctezuma sets these near-mythical figures into earthy relief. But it’s mostly the intrigues and machinations of these leaders’ canny consorts – the Aztec princess Atotoxtli and the conquistadors’ translator Malinalli – that power the plot. Natasha Wimmer’s English translation sharply delivers the novel’s poetic and witty qualities, while at the same time reveling in its core theme: the fundamental untranslatability of human experience.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: You Should Be So Lucky
author: Cat Sebastian
description: Sports romance is all the rage right now. As a person who does not care for sports, this new trend is not my favorite. On the other hand, I love Cat Sebastian as an author so much that I will read anything she writes – which was how I ended up with her new romance about a cinnamon roll of a baseball star and a grumpy reporter, set in 1960. Eddie and Mark are forced to work together, and sparks fly. But those same circumstances might pull them apart, since Eddie can’t be out publicly and play professional sports, and Mark refuses to be trapped in another secret relationship. If you like audiobooks, one of my favorite narrators, Joel Leslie, does a fantastic job on this one.

title: Liars
author: Sarah Manguso
description: There’s something deliciously one-sided about Sarah Manguso’s Liars. It’s the story of the dissolution of a marriage, told from the eyes of a woman whose husband seems uninterested in husbanding. At first, it’s easy to brush off the slights as him just being a guy. But then the slights and put-downs and lies build, and it’s obvious to everyone outside of the relationship that this is unsustainable. It’s a story that’s been told a million times, but in Manguso’s hands, it’s terse and funny. The marriage is sour. You can wince, but you can’t look away.

title: Mina's Matchbox
author: Yoko Ogawa, translated by Stephen B. Snyder
description: There’s such an elegance to the way Mina’s Matchbox unfolds that it didn’t even stick out to me that the young, rich, asthmatic title character was riding a hippo to school. In the novel, 12-year-old Tomoko moves in with her rich cousin (the aforementioned hippo rider). They form crushes and cook up schemes as preteen girls tend to do. The world Yoko Ogawa builds is quiet, warm and it should feel comforting. But there are peculiarities about the whole thing that keep you on the tips of your toes.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Burning Earth: A History
author: Sunil Amrith
description: First, don’t be put off by the title. The Burning Earth is a bracing book, rather than a depressing one. Although it’s a history of the terrible things human beings have done to the planet, to each other and to an unfathomable number of innocent creatures, Sunil Amrith’s chronicle is as compassionate as it is riveting. A Yale professor and the recipient of a MacArthur “genius” grant, the author moves seamlessly from how global conquest and war reshaped Earth from 1218 onward, to fascinating cultural analysis, such as a look at the artistry of Charlie Chaplin, Akira Kurosawa and Diego Rivera through an environmental lens, to a resolute call for integrating our own vulnerable “creatureliness” into a road toward repair.
tags: ["Science", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Rental House
author: Weike Wang
description: When you marry a person, do you also marry their family? That’s a question central to Rental House, Weike Wang’s smart and incisive novel about a married couple, Keru and Nate, and the fissures that appear in their relationship when their families (and some unexpected guests) join them on vacation. Keru’s parents are Chinese immigrants who believe that suffering is a necessary part of life; Nate’s rural, working-class family distrusts his academic inclinations. Is it any surprise that after two weeks of vacation, Keru and Nate are definitely not getting their security deposit back?

title: Patriot: A Memoir
author: Alexei Navalny
description: Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny died in an Arctic Russian penal colony in February. But even in death, he continues his fight against President Vladimir Putin. This posthumous memoir has two sections: The first half is a traditional narrative, beginning with a true crime story when Navalny is poisoned with a nerve agent on a flight from Siberia in 2020. Halfway through, the book pivots to become his prison diary. Through even the darkest episodes, Navalny’s sunniness and humor shine through – whether he’s describing an episode of Rick and Morty that he left unfinished when he collapsed on that flight, or taking joy in the indulgence of bread and butter that he only ate on Sunday mornings behind bars.

title: This Motherless Land
author: Nikki May
description: I have a high bar for new takes on classic novels, and this book transcended it with ease. Wahala author Nikki May reimagines Jane Austen’s Mansfield Park as a cross-cultural family drama complicated by race. Nine-year-old Funke Oyenuga’s childhood in Lagos wasn’t perfect, but she was safe and deeply loved until a car accident spun Funke’s world off its axis. Suddenly, her beloved mother is dead, her Nigerian father’s family thinks she must be a witch for surviving, and her brilliant father is a wreck. So she’s shipped off to her mother’s English family, which assumes she must be a savage. The one bright side is her exuberant and loving cousin Liv. This keenly observed reimagining delivers perceptive twists and its own unique thrills.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: War
author: Bob Woodward
description: There is a certain wistful quality about reading the latest bestseller that Bob Woodward has written about a U.S. president and national security. Published in October, it conveys the hope that the electorate will agree with Woodward’s judgment that Donald Trump “is unfit to lead the country.” Woodward bases that judgment not only on the three books he previously published on Trump (Fear, Rage and Peril) but on Trump’s actions since to undermine President Biden and complicate his struggles with Afghanistan, Ukraine and Gaza. Woodward is surprisingly positive about the efforts of Biden and his team to control the damage and dangers, especially in Ukraine and Gaza. War also includes a report on Woodward’s 1989 interview with Trump, conducted along with colleague Carl Bernstein from the Watergate era. A transcript and voice recording of that interview resurfaced in 2023. It reveals a Trump who, at 42, sounds remarkably like the Trump of today.

title: Bad Therapy: Why the Kids Aren't Growing Up
author: Abigail Shrier
description: This book is a provocation, but not in the way people might think. Abigail Shrier doesn’t dismiss talk therapy; she takes it seriously as a potent treatment for real mental illness. The problem, she says, is that we’ve made it one of those “It can’t hurt!” things, like vitamins or fresh air. Citing studies and interviewing parents, she highlights therapy’s potential risks, especially when it’s applied to the normal emotional ups and downs of childhood. The book is personal (Shrier is a mother) and at times contentious, but her case against the “therapization” of American kids is worth considering.

title: The Mighty Red
author: Louise Erdrich
description: Louise Erdrich’s latest novel is set in North Dakota’s Red River Valley, and like a river, it gracefully winds through the landscape of Erdrich’s conscience: the toxic toll of large-scale farming (sugar beets, in this case), the inequities of capitalism, the sanctity of a good bookstore! Luckily, our guide is a Goth teen, Kismet, who comes of age before our eyes as she navigates tragedy and comedy, families both broken and bound by love, and, most importantly, two young men who profess to adore her. This is my favorite kind of book: one that can make you laugh and cry (and laugh again) in just a page, a paragraph or even one holy sentence.

title: Play Nice: The Rise, Fall, and Future of Blizzard Entertainment
author: Jason Schreier
description: A deft examination of a gaming titan, Play Nice isn’t just thoroughly reported – it’s also a great yarn. Even if you don’t play the Warcraft, Starcraft and Diablo games that made Blizzard billions, Jason Schreier’s surprising anecdotes dramatically illustrate the studio’s shoestring origins, its all-encompassing work culture and its riotous excesses. It’s the story of a company that went from beloved boutique to industry pariah – but it’s also deeply human. Schreier never loses sight of the people who built Blizzard and battled for its soul.

title: Soldiers and Kings: Survival and Hope in the World of Human Smuggling
author: Jason De León
description: This book, which won the National Book Award for Nonfiction, reads like a gripping novel. But it’s all fact, the product of years of embedded reporting by an anthropology professor. Jason De León’s page-turner took me deep into the lives of the maligned “coyotes” who smuggle immigrants into the U.S. – wait, could they be victims too? – and through intimate details about their lives, their jobs and their world, what I thought I knew was turned upside down. The book walks with people we just want to see as the villains and seriously complicates the narrative.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Baldwin: A Love Story
author: Nicholas Boggs
description: The book is massive at 720 pages – but how else can anyone properly summarize James Baldwin’s love life and the effects it had on his creative process? Baldwin: A Love Story is a biography organized by the four men with whom Baldwin had his most significant relationships, until his death in 1987. Each was a turbulent affair, and several took the writer across the globe. Portions of the deeply researched book are told in Baldwin’s own words through excerpts of correspondence he kept with friends, family and, of course, his lovers.

title: I'll Tell You When I'm Home: A Memoir
author: Hala Alyan
description: In I’ll Tell You When I’m Home, Palestinian American writer and psychologist Hala Alyan describes her experience with infertility and the decision to have a child via surrogacy. The process of “waiting for something to happen in someone else’s body,” as she told NPR, echoed metaphors of exile and displacement for herself and generations of women in her family who were forced to leave their homes throughout the Middle East. What the book lacks in linear structure, it makes up for in poetry, raw memories and visceral notes from a mother to her daughter.

title: Killer Potential
author: Hannah Deitch
description: Former high-achieving student and now SAT tutor Evie Gordon shows up to her student’s luxurious home one day to find the rich parents dead, and a mysterious stranger tied up in a closet. She impulsively unties the stranger, who does not speak to her. Through a series of mishaps and fateful decisions, they both end up on the run on a crime spree/road trip, wanted as serial killers. The plot details slowly unfold from both Evie’s and the stranger’s points of view. A romantic plotline and bits of social commentary add more depth to the tense thrill ride of a story.

title: Beasts of Carnaval
author: Rosália Rodrigo
description: The el Carnaval de Bestias is a fevered bacchanal, a luxurious, exotic escape for the elite, and a descent into surreal torment for Sofía. Born enslaved and now free, Sofía arrives on Isla Bestia searching for her missing brother. Instead, what she finds is a waking nightmare. The island seethes with pleasure and energy, but for her, the past and present blend together in a disorienting collision. Her Taike’ri ancestors call to her, and she dare not turn away. This may be Rosália Rodrigo’s debut, but she deftly weaves together Caribbean history, Taíno culture, and the Black diaspora into a profound experience.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Family Matters", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture"]

title: Psychopomp & Circumstance
author: Eden Royce
description: Set in an alternate, magical, post-Reconstruction South Carolina, this Southern gothic novella delves into generational secrets and self-realization. Phaedra St. Margaret and her socialite mother have never gotten along, what with Phee’s stubborn refusal to behave like a “proper” young lady. After her estranged aunt dies, Phee heads from New Charleston to the small town of Horizon in a cart pulled by a hippocamp to plan her aunt’s homegoing. Buried memories, mysteriously locked rooms and strange spirits lurk in the corners of Aunt Cleo’s home in this haunting story.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Americana", "Family Matters", "Historical Fiction"]

title: Son of the Morning
author: Akwaeke Emezi
description: This book takes well-worn tropes and imbues them with Akwaeke Emezi’s trademark gorgeous literary style and an inventive queer romance. Sweet Galilee takes her first steps outside her hometown of Salvation and immediately meets sexy, dangerous Lucifer – yes, that Lucifer. The third point in the love triangle is Leviathan, a prince of Hell. He has Gali in his crosshairs for reasons she doesn’t fully understand. Full of religious commentary, twisty action and steamy scenes, this romantasy was a wild ride.

title: Tall Is Her Body
author: Robert de la Chevotière
description: After witnessing the brutal murder of his mother and uncle, young Fidel is taken from his quiet Caribbean home on Guadeloupe to live with his mother’s family on the nearby island of Dominica. There, life goes from placid to terrifying, with new people in his life either cruel, negligent or dying before they can save him. His ability to see spirits of the dead and his family’s belief in Obeah clashes with the strict teachings of his new Catholic school and, later, his life in Canada. The lush prose takes the reader on a journey across decades and continents. A magical realism tour de force where cultural identity confronts colonization.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: The Door on the Sea
author: Caskey Russell
description: The cover copy describes this as “the Tlingit indigenous response to The Lord of the Rings,” and it lives up to that premise. This first book in the Raven and Eagle series centers on Elān, a young bookworm watching the shape-shifting Koosh invaders get closer and closer to his people, the Aaní. Elān would rather be learning than adventuring, but after an encounter with a foul-mouthed raven, he and a motley crew of warriors set off in a great canoe to find a lost weapon that will drive the Koosh away. Get ready for a new, fascinating take on a classic epic adventure and coming-of-age story.

title: The Voice of Blood
author: Gabriela Rábago Palafox, translated by M. Elizabeth Ginway and Enrique Muñoz-Mantas
description: It’s a shame that the late Gabriela Rábago Palafox is not nearly as well known in the U.S. as she was in her home country of Mexico. Lucky for us, this newly translated collection of some of her gothic short stories offers a striking introduction. In these stories, Rábago Palafox takes vampire lore and layers in feminism, fabulism and folklore to create a shockingly good body of work. At once alluring and gruesome, the collection is a must-read for fans of thrills, chills and bloodsuckers.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Dark"]

title: When They Burned the Butterfly
author: Wen-yi Lee
description: Post-independence Singapore in the 1970s was a time of great change and economic upheaval. While turmoil roils outside, 16-year-old Adeline Siow is bored and lonely at home. But life gets a lot more intense when her mother is murdered and Adeline, who like her mother has the ability to manipulate fire, is soon dragged into a dangerous world of gangsters, gods and secrets. A blossoming sapphic romance is threatened by patriarchal violence. With its heart-pounding action and vivid imagery, this book will have you hooked from the first word to the last.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction"]

title: Marble Hall Murders
author: Anthony Horowitz
description: Anthony Horowitz is back with a new mystery-within-a-mystery book featuring returning character Susan Ryeland. She has to edit a new novel by Eliot Crace centered on world famous detective Atticus Pünd. Pünd finds himself in the South of France solving the murder of his friend, wealthy Lady Margaret Chalfont. After she is poisoned, Pünd has to figure out who in her family is the killer. As Ryeland tries to figure out who the killer is in Crace’s Pünd novel, she becomes a suspect in a murder herself. Horowitz delivers another cleverly written story full of hidden anagrams, secrets and disguises that is well worth the trip back into the world of Susan Ryeland and Atticus Pünd.

title: A/S/L
author: Jeanne Thornton
description: Scrappy and bootstrappy, endlessly niche and DIY, the early internet was very different from the infinite scroll it has become today. One could disappear into assumed personas and passionate interest groups, like the amateur game-development chat rooms where Abraxa, Sash and Lilith “meet” in cyberspace as teens, each struggling with different flavors of neurodivergence and gender dysphoria. Two decades later, their lives converge again. Through IRC chat logs and unsent emails, A/S/L tells a story of delusions, obsessions and compromises, as three online childhood friends find their way and each other in a world that has changed under their feet.

title: Among Friends
author: Hal Ebbott
description: It’s your oldest friends who can really grind your gears. Hal Ebbott’s debut novel starts off as an examination of this relationship – two families spend a weekend in a house together to celebrate a birthday and, of course, old resentments and jealousies creep in and out of friendly tennis games and predinner drinks. But then something happens and everything shifts and all those resentments don’t seem so old anymore and the jealousy blooms into something else altogether.

title: Audition
author: Katie Kitamura
description: I guess I could explain the plot to you: An actress meets up with a man who is convinced she’s his mother. It turns out she’s not. I think? Maybe she is? Or, maybe she’s not but actually kind of is? What is a mother? The most impressive thing about this Booker Prize finalist is how Katie Kitamura plays with the narrative and toys with the reader without being overly clever about it all. She’s stingy with details and answers, but generous with intrigue and depth.

title: The Rest of Our Lives
author: Benjamin Markovits
description: If you’re here from the future hoping to find your next read, some quick context for you – there was a lot of ink spilled about men and literature in 2025. This year, we were very curious to answer questions such as: Are men reading? Why don’t men read literature? If they are reading, is it honest or performative? If men read more, would they stop acting like that? Which brings us to this Booker Prize finalist novel that, yes, is about and by a man. And it teasingly approaches male anxieties about aging, masculinity and sex (it’s about a guy whose wife cheated on him, and now he’s on an indefinite road trip). But Benjamin Markovits instead veers away from trying to answer the big questions plaguing men, and instead offers up a quiet story that’s funny, tender and intimate.

title: The Ten Year Affair
author: Erin Somers
description: My fellow millennials, we’ve made it. We’re now old enough to be in long, unhappy marriages and to have our fiction reflect that. Hurray! As a consolation prize, Erin Somers’ take on the slog that marriage can sometimes be doesn’t feel like a mere throwback to the types of domestic dramas that used to be literary fiction’s bread and butter. Instead, the novel takes a multiverse approach to marriage and infidelity. (Our hero doesn’t really cheat — she just thinks about it a lot. But wait — isn’t that sorta, kinda cheating?) And as any superhero fan will tell you, multiverses have a tendency to … crash into each other in interesting ways.

title: Tilt
author: Emma Pattee
description: An extremely pregnant woman has to go to Ikea – and yet her day somehow gets worse from there when an earthquake hits. In a lot of ways, Emma Pattee’s debut is a lean, action-adventure novel about a woman who has to overcome obstacles to get from Point A to Point B. But the book is tender in its approach to the singular will to survive, while also making time to poke fun at the keeping-up-with-the-Joneses of modern-day motherhood.

title: John & Paul: A Love Story in Songs
author: Ian Leslie
description: Fans of the Fab Four may have been skeptical when this meditation on the Beatles’ central creative partnership, written by a bestselling human behavior specialist, hit the already heaving shelf of books on the band. But Ian Leslie’s dual portrait reframes the Lennon-McCartney relationship in ways that made this lore-soaked Beatles fan bow in recognition and respect. From the lads’ first meeting as teen strivers to the breakup that shook the world, Leslie fleshes out a deeply affectionate, wildly competitive dynamic between two exceptional young men unable to say in words what they shared so easily in song. A love story, indeed.

title: The Harder I Fight the More I Love You: A Memoir
author: Neko Case
description: If you’ve read even one sentence about Neko Case’s memoir of growing up and into her life’s work as a singer-songwriter, you know her family story has a mind-blowing twist. No spoilers here! The narrative of her difficult, often materially deprived youth is gripping, but what makes this book exceptional is the way Case writes about a creative mind finding – in nature, among steadfast friends and fellow baby rock and rollers, through solitude and small rebellions – a growing determination to speak, not only for herself, but for all the vulnerable creatures who make up her extended chosen family. Her prose is golden and gloriously ragged and deeply alive, like her songs.

title: Cry for Me, Argentina: My Life as a Failed Child Star
author: Tamara Yajia
description: Remember that scene from Little Miss Sunshine where a young Abigail Breslin performs a striptease for a room full of horrified onlookers? That’s a story straight out of Cry for Me, Argentina. Only, it was Tamara Yajia’s real life and the crowd was full of rabbis. Her memoir is full of laugh-out-loud moments like that one as she recounts her attempts to become a child star, her moves back and forth between Argentina and California, as well as some heartfelt, difficult stories from her dysfunctional family life.

title: Night People: How to Be a DJ in '90s New York City
author: Mark Ronson
description: Much like a great night out, Mark Ronson’s account of his life in the ‘90s as an aspiring New York DJ rides the line of hedonism and transcendence, weaving smartly between the two. Before the age of Spotify, discovering new music to play required real adventure: befriending other music nerds and convincing them to share their record collections, visits to rare record dealers, putting in hours at clubs to absorb what other DJs were playing. Ronson chronicles those quests while naming song after song that shaped him, resulting in an encyclopedia of clues for anyone interested in their own musical treasure hunt. In the end, the story tips in the direction of transcendence – Ronson treats music like it’s sacred, and to him it is. It might be for you, too, by the end of the book.

title: The Emperor of Gladness
author: Ocean Vuong
description: The banal sentence, “This is the best novel I’ve read in years,” captures what I’ve told friends about The Emperor of Gladness – which is that Ocean Vuong’s gorgeous prose makes every line I’ve ever written seem wan by comparison. “Best novel I’ve read in years”? How insipid! This book tells the story of an unlikely friendship between a college dropout and an elderly woman with dementia. It paints a picture of the bond that forms among workers at a fast-food restaurant in a small New England town. And writing these sentences, all I can think is – Vuong would phrase it so much more beautifully.

title: Heartwood
author: Amity Gaige
description: Heartwood is a perfect thriller for people who don’t like thrillers (🙋‍♀). A nurse nicknamed Sparrow who is trying to move past the trauma of working during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic disappears while hiking the Appalachian Trail. The narration alternates among three women: a state game warden leading the search, a lonely retiree who becomes very invested and the missing woman herself, whose plight is told via the journal that keeps her going while lost. Heartwood is equal parts gripping and moving, filled with empathy and hope – not just for Sparrow’s safe return but for human connection overall.

title: Darkenbloom
author: Eva Menasse, translated by Charlotte Collins
description: It is 1989, and the residents of the Austrian village of Darkenbloom are uneasy. East German refugees on the village’s border with Hungary are raising the ghosts of World War II. The town has secrets; “good Nazis” and bad Nazis abound, and there are no Jews left. The book succeeds on multiple levels – it’s a gossipy, small-town satire that’s laugh-out-loud funny but also a historical mystery that lays bare the complicity of characters who have convinced themselves it is possible to see evil only in hindsight. It is this last idea that sticks with you. “There was always so much to do, we couldn’t pay attention to that,” the Greek chorus whispers. Pay attention, says the book.

title: If You Love It, Let It Kill You
author: Hannah Pittard
description: Literature about the anxieties and dissatisfactions of 40+ women is one area in which, thankfully, middle-aged women are not to be disappointed. To the recently rich genre plumbing the particular discontent of this stage in life, add Hannah Pittard’s latest. Her narrator – significantly, Hana P. – is a funny and restless English professor plagued by the impending publication of her ex’s autofictional novel. Any midlife woman who has felt a little deranged by a relentless mix of desire and irritation will recognize herself in Hana, even as her mind takes unique and surreal detours. Read it for the mix of comedy and malaise, and for dessert, you can plumb the internet for the epic literary back-and-forth between Pittard and her ex-husband about their real-life breakup. Pittard’s novel is fabulous and stands on its own, but it also serves as her very satisfying last word on that matter as well.

title: Things in Nature Merely Grow
author: Yiyun Li
description: I must begin with the facts, as the author does: Writer Yiyun Li lost both her teenage sons to suicide. It’s a tragedy we might call “unthinkable,” but Li’s book is about thinking. While the deaths of her sons are the context, the subject is Li’s thought process as she lives through the aftermath of her younger son’s death in 2024. To be in Li’s head is a rare privilege – she is a writer of elegance and clarity and is a deeply interesting thinker. And the book, surprisingly, is not sad. This National Book Award finalist is moving and even funny in places as she writes about her wonderful friends, a few awful strangers, but most of all her extraordinary sons. You may find yourself transformed after reading it. I did.

title: Vantage Point
author: Sara Sligar
description: A summer mystery with a rich, troubled family and a curse? Sign me up. Vantage Point is set on a secluded island in Maine and reads like a tech thriller with the soul of a gothic dynastic horror story. It’s told from the dual perspectives of Clara, the youngest member of the wealthy, politically connected and highly unlucky Wieland family, and Jess, Clara’s childhood best friend who’s married to Clara’s brother, Senate hopeful Teddy. When a series of deepfake videos targets Clara and then Jess, it feels as though the famous Wieland curse has come into the digital age. The book is a rich drama about friendship, class and inherited trauma – all in the package of a propulsive yarn.

title: What We Can Know
author: Ian McEwan
description: The year is 2119, and catastrophes (climatic, nuclear) have reshaped the world entirely. On the archipelago that is now Britain, literature scholar Tom Metcalfe is engaged in a 21st century pursuit: hunting for the missing masterpiece of poet Francis Blundy, a birthday poem written in 2014 for his wife, Vivien, and heard only by the six guests at her birthday dinner. In the first half, Tom narrates his search, speculating on the Blundys’ marriage and other shifting alliances. In the second half, the reader is poignantly reminded of the limits of scholarship. The book is egalitarian about genre; it’s a romance, a mystery and a post-apocalyptic domestic drama that makes the stakes of our own precarious epoch deeply personal. And it demonstrates a hopeful counterweight to anxiety about the future – perhaps our epistemic limitations are merely an opportunity for our most tender and human artistic endeavors.

title: The Pretender
author: Jo Harkin
description: Perhaps you thought the Wolf Hall trilogy was the last of historical fiction that combines Tudor history and psychological exploration so brilliantly? I present to you Jo Harkin’s novel about Tudor impostor Lambert Simnel. The book is narrated by the pretender himself: a 10-year-old, manipulated by powerful adults, robbed of his personhood again and again, a pawn in a European power struggle. It contains some of the most authentic writing in a child’s voice I’ve ever read. And while the book is a deeply engaging read, it underlines profoundly the experience of those who must try to forge and keep their identity without agency, caught in the powerful maw of history.

title: Heart the Lover
author: Lily King
description: An intimate novel about formative first love, friendship and the relationships that never leave us. This time-hopping story (of the perfect length, if you ask me!) sweeps you into the narrator’s point of view with such visceral immediacy that it’s easy to lose yourself in her memories. I canceled my plans to finish this gorgeous book, and I felt welcomely emo for a few days afterward. Heart the Lover is both a prequel and a sequel to Lily King’s beloved novel Writers & Lovers, published in 2020, but it absolutely stands on its own.

title: Raising Hare: A Memoir
author: Chloe Dalton
description: I never knew I could care so much about a hare! In the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, Chloe Dalton set out on a country stroll and crossed paths with a newborn hare too young to survive on its own, no mother in sight. Dalton takes the baby in, and this decision turns out to dominate the next years of her life (hares, which are not domesticated like rabbits, do not fare well in captivity). Part diary, part natural history, part field guide, this book is also an epic hero’s journey – where you set out on an ordinary day and stumble upon something that changes you forever.

title: The Road to Tender Hearts
author: Annie Hartnett
description: This quirky novel, which is somehow gentle, disturbing and darkly comedic, will take you for a ride – especially if you can embrace a little magical thinking. I went into it cold, and I was glad I did, propelled by the twists, turns and reveals as this ragtag cast of characters embarks on a cross-country journey, each on their own mission for answers, adventure and belonging.

title: Changeover: A Young Rivalry and a New Era of Men's Tennis
author: Giri Nathan
description: For an extraordinary 20-year stretch, men’s tennis was dominated by the legendary “Big Three” of Roger Federer, Rafael Nadal and Novak Djokovic, who collectively won more than 60 Grand Slam singles titles. Then, in 2024, a new era began when two young stars – Jannik Sinner, the willowy Italian with the mop of curly red hair, and Carlos Alcaraz, the Spaniard with the enormous boyish smile – emerged as the new twin suns of men’s tennis. Together, Sinner and Alcaraz have claimed every Grand Slam title of the past two years, and author Giri Nathan has been along for the ride. Nathan writes with both a deep knowledge of tennis and a love for it too. His proximity to the matches and the players comes through in this easy and enjoyable read that’s a must for any sports fan.

title: A World Without Summer: A Volcano Erupts, a Creature Awakens, and the Sun Goes Out
author: Nicholas Day, illustrated by Yas Imamura
description: If a volcano erupts in Indonesia, can it starve cows in Switzerland? Can it inspire the world’s first work of science fiction? Can it write the hymn “Silent Night”? Even if the answer to these questions is yes, the natural follow-up question is “How?” Take a trip back in time to 1815, when a volcanic eruption managed to upend the entire world. These days, the Tambora volcano doesn’t have the same name recognition as Krakatoa, but Nicholas Day aims to change all of that. A clever summary at the end regarding climate change rounds out a book that’s as ambitious as it is genius. (For ages 10 to 14)

title: Blood in the Water
author: Tiffany D. Jackson
description: Kaylani has been sleuthing ever since her father was imprisoned for a crime he didn’t commit. Now, against her will, she finds herself with family friends among Martha’s Vineyard’s Black elite – a world far removed from the life she knew in Brooklyn. And that’s before the blood-soaked body appears. This rip-roaring whodunit is filled with references to Jaws and Martha’s Vineyard’s Black history. And Kaylani’s detective instincts will delight budding mystery fans. (For ages 9 to 12)

title: My Presentation Today Is About the Anaconda
author: Bibi Dumon Tak, illustrated by Annemarie van Haeringen, translated by Nancy Forest-Flier
description: A group of animals, insects and water-based denizens have prepared a series of 20 short presentations. The catch? They’re making these presentations about one another, and nobody appears to be capable of seeing the world through their subjects’ point of view. As such, readers are treated to a fox’s view of geese (they’re delicious!) or a barn owl’s delight when considering the Tasmanian devil (proclaiming exultantly that “He can rip the skin off a kangaroo with the greatest of ease.”). Personal prejudices come into play, bringing into question whether animals are any more impartial and unbiased than their human counterparts. An index of delights rounds out this irreverent compendium of informational fiction and anthropomorphic animals at their finest. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Scarlet Morning
author: ND Stevenson
description: Two orphans, seemingly resigned to death by boredom on the island of Caveat, are rescued (in a sense) by a mysterious sea captain seeking a mysterious book in their possession. Once they leave, they find themselves plunged into a world of uncertainty, danger and undeniable excitement. Viola and Wilmur have only ever had one another, but after they throw in their lot with the captain and her crew, they begin to untangle the mystery of their decomposing world and whether it’s possible to set it right again. ND Stevenson puts his back into this inventive series of exploits and near escapes. Smart as a whip, with enough surprises and deeply creepy elements to keep kids guessing, this is a swashbuckling epic certain to make pirate enthusiasts out of all its young readers. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The Experiment
author: Rebecca Stead
description: A tail. Nathan has grown a tail and now nothing will ever be the same again. Raised to understand that he, like his parents, is just an alien in human form, Nathan has always been perfectly comfortable with his double life. But the minute he grows a furry tiger tail (one with a mind entirely of its own), he begins to realize that there’s more to his existence than meets the eye. Could it be that his entire existence is a lie? Rebecca Stead expertly weaves such disparate elements as Charlotte’s Web and Calvin and Hobbes into a work of science fiction that makes good trouble, questioning authority in all the best possible ways. A rebellious response to untrustworthy leadership. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: The History of We
author: Nikkolas Smith
description: All of humanity began in Africa. That’s a simple statement of fact, but in an era when books about race are regularly banned from libraries, saying it can almost feel subversive. Nikkolas Smith doesn’t just lean into that historical reality, however. He pays homage to our earliest ancestors’ humor, dignity and lives. This is a human history, full of Black faces and joy. “What does ‘the beginning’ look like?” Here it looks like thick painted brushstrokes and vibrant scenes from humankind’s earliest days. When describing what people did back then, Smith reverts time and again not to statements of what “they” did but what “we” did. An inclusive and telling reminder of our shared past. (For ages 4 to 8)

title: The Village Beyond the Mist
author: Sachiko Kashiwaba, illustrated by Miho Satake, translated by Avery Fischer Udagawa
description: It shouldn’t come as any surprise that filmmaker Hayao Miyazaki was deeply influenced by the books he read throughout the years. In 1975, a book of extraordinary imagination was released in Japan. The story featured a young girl, dropped off unceremoniously at a train station, who discovers a small magical village where all the denizens are descended from wizards. Spoiled princes, magical sweets, cowardly tigers, malevolent toys, this book has it all! Now finally published for the first time in the U.S., the book is ideal bedtime reading. Experience the originality and creativity that helped inspire Miyazaki to make Spirited Away. (For ages 8 to 13)
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Whale Eyes: A Memoir About Seeing and Being Seen
author: James Robinson, illustrated by Brian Rea
description: It’s a brave book indeed that starts off by teaching kids how to fake reading in class. Born with an eye condition called strabismus, author James Robinson had firsthand knowledge of how to find workarounds in his daily life. With the use of clever illustrations and inventive techniques, young readers are placed directly into Robinson’s shoes (eyes?) as they attempt to read words that bob and leap about the page. That he grew up to become a successful documentary filmmaker is a testament to the resilience and strength depicted on these pages. An incredible memoir, as empowering as it is empathetic. (For ages 10 and up)

title: American Kings: A Biography of the Quarterback
author: Seth Wickersham
description: Do you think of quarterbacks like Pokémon? I do now, after reading this sweeping narrative that describes how unimposing creatures (teenage athletes) manage to evolve into world-beating stars blessed with superpowers. As Seth Wickersham finds, no two origins are the same. In short vignettes that are easy on a busy reader, he explores the self-doubt, pain and fortitude that are required of great athletes – and maybe of all of us. Along the way, he charts how a country evolved along with its favorite sport. Extra point: Wickersham explains his research clearly, taking us along on an intriguing journey.

title: Good Movies as Old Books: Films Reimagined as Vintage Book Covers
author: Matt Stevens
description: Never judge a book by its cover, but, man, is it ever fun to judge a film by its Matt Stevens cover. The ingenious designer/illustrator reimagines roughly 200 of his fave flicks as vintage paperbacks – Mad Max with a cover that could be a Peter Max-on-acid portrait of Dr. Dementus, Speed with a lit, clock-faced bomb, Ghostbusters as midcentury sci-fi noir. His covers conjure the 1950s and ‘60s so effectively that I found myself thinking that maybe I’d somehow missed that Jordan Peele’s Get Out (never a novel) was originally published in 1953. I also reeeeally wanted to read it.

title: When the Moon Hits Your Eye
author: John Scalzi
description: How would you respond to a world-changing event that probably means the end of humanity as we know it? And what if that event were completely silly and absurd? That’s the premise of When the Moon Hits Your Eye. It’s the last of a loose trilogy of absurdist sci-fi books by John Scalzi. It imagines how humanity might respond to the moon suddenly being replaced by a massive chunk of “organic matrix,” aka cheese. This breezy beach read will keep you giggling as dozens of characters come to terms with the idea that the world may not end with a whimper or a bang, but rather in a layer of hot mozzarella.

title: Greyhound: A Memoir
author: Joanna Pocock
description: In 2006, grieving multiple miscarriages, Canadian writer Joanna Pocock embarked on a road trip across the U.S. via Greyhound bus. Seventeen years later, she repeated her Detroit-to-Los Angeles passage – a journey she chronicles in her new memoir. Pocock’s trip takes on what she calls a “mythical dimension.” Seatmates come and go, but delays are constant. One driver abruptly walks off the job in Indianapolis, and America seems coarsened by COVID-19 and capitalism. “Much of the hope I had seen in 2006 had evaporated,” she writes. Greyhound weaves together observations from Pocock’s two journeys, along with environmental and cultural commentary. The end result is an engaging combination of the profound and the profane – much like the country Pocock traverses.

title: First-Time Caller
author: B.K. Borison
description: Imagine hearing your daughter whispering on the phone late at night. When you ask her who she’s talking to, you discover she’s on the line with a radio show host. As you take the phone, you’re unexpectedly drawn into a conversation about your love life, which leads to your participation in a new segment on the local romance hotline aimed at helping you find love. The story takes the main characters, Aiden and Lucie, on a beautiful journey of letting love into their lives. If you are a hopeless romantic or just love a great meet-cute, this is for you.

title: Sounds Like Love
author: Ashley Poston
description: This magical romance follows a songwriter trying to make her next hit while struggling with writer’s block. She returns to her hometown and develops an undeniable connection with a musician she meets there. They have a telepathic line of communication, and they hope that by collaborating, they might break free of a haunting melody they share in their heads. This musical story explores love in many forms, from family connections to romantic bonds and reads like a ballad.

title: A Rare Find
author: Joanna Lowell
description: I adored it! Stylish and surreptitiously meaty, this historical romance – in the hands of a connoisseur of the craft – weaves together unapologetic swoon with lesser-known queer history. Elfreda Marsden liked her quiet, rustic life as everyone’s rock. Caretaker to her sisters and collaborator/uncredited ghostwriter for her archaeologist father, she even went as far as authoring a major paper in his name. Life was comfortable, if a bit tame, until her former childhood rival, Georgina Redmayne, now “Georgie,” barreled back into her life like a fashionable bull in a china shop. After ruining what could have been the find that put Elfreda on the map, the former frenemies unite to scope out long-hidden treasures and discover feelings they never imagined in the bargain.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: All of Us Murderers
author: KJ Charles
description: Like an Edwardian Succession, this gothic romp is about who gets what in the event of a patriarch’s passing. Novelist Walter Wyckham lived until nearly 80 but, rumor has it, made a pact with the devil, ensuring that he would enjoy success and long life but that none of his successors would be as lucky. Many years later, the author’s son and heir, Wynn, gathers his quarrelsome family to sort out what will happen to the extensive estate in the event that the curse leads to his untimely death. Of course there’s an outrageous stipulation. And the lengthy house party is a nest of fortune-hunting vipers. The exception is Zeb, who’s more concerned that his ex is now Wynn’s personal secretary. Shady shenanigans ensue. But this is KJ Charles, so it’s also stylish and sexy.

title: August Lane
author: Regina Black
description: Regina Black is magic. I was mad at Luke Randall and his creator before I hit Page 1 – I knew he had made his career stealing credit from the heroine the book is named after. And I was supposed to root for him, for them? Well, I fell deeply in love with that “sober drunk,” his tender aching heart and the “deep, smoky tone he’d been told gave his voice more swagger” right alongside August. Plus, like Beyoncé’s Cowboy Carter come to life, this masterful novel wraps bittersweet second-chance love and family loss around nuanced lessons about the overlooked and misappropriated legacy of Black women in country music. The result is the most snot-inducing, truly and swooningly romantic novel I’ve read in a long, long while.

title: Endling
author: Maria Reva
description: A tragicomedy about the Russian invasion of Ukraine was always going to be challenging, but Maria Reva makes her debut irresistible with spectacular writing and a bonkers plot. At the center is Yeva, a scientist dedicated to the preservation of hundreds of endangered snails, many the last of their species (endlings). When grants and academia fail, Yeva funds her lab pretending to be a prospective bride on Ukraine’s lucrative international romance tours and then gets entangled in a cross-country mass kidnapping caper meant to sabotage the trade that sustains her. But that’s not all. Endling is also a formally daring novel that breaks the barrier between fiction and commentary. One of my favorite lines: “I need to keep fact and fiction straight, but they keep blurring together.”
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Science"]

title: Great Black Hope
author: Rob Franklin
description: I love a debut that shouts its arrival and a novel that thrills while making you think. Artful and transporting, Great Black Hope is a book I couldn’t wait to get lost in. Smith is upwardly mobile but spiraling out at breakneck speed. One of his closest friends has died mysteriously, and just as he’s trying to numb his brain with illicit substances, he runs afoul of law enforcement. As a queer young Black man from an elite Atlanta family, with family money and a Stanford degree, Smith knows his identity matters. He’s privileged and protected by virtue of his class, but vulnerable on account of his race and sexuality. So when he’s arrested for cocaine possession in the Hamptons, the next moves are crucial. This compulsive coming of age novel traces the ups, downs and twists in Smith’s journeys, from grief to glam party circuit to gritty backrooms to recovery and beyond.

title: Rules for Ruin
author: Mimi Matthews
description: Like the London smog, the chemistry is thick in this Victorian romance loosely inspired by David Copperfield. Products of London’s rookery, Effie and Gabriel are outsiders hustling hard to infiltrate London’s elite, but their aims make them rivals. Effie is the star protege of the mysterious Miss Corvus, who, like a more political Miss Havisham, wreaks revenge for her plight by training young women as secret agents in the fight for women’s rights. Effie’s role is to play a debutante, flirt her way through the marriage mart and gain the proof to bring down an aristocratic predator who opposes a key piece of legislation. That plan conflicts with Gabriel’s scheme to exploit the villain’s debts and secrets for the good of his people and himself. The journey from combatants to lovers sizzles.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Librarians
author: Sherry Thomas
description: As a longtime Sherry Thomas fan, I am delighted to see her master contemporary crime after winning acclaim for international historical romances and the inventive Lady Sherlock mystery series. At the center of a plot that reads like the cozy-glam love child of Crazy Rich Asians and Agatha Christie is Hazel Lee, who relocated to Austin, Texas, after her luxe life in Singapore ended with her husband’s indictment for financial crimes and disappearance. Working at the library near her grandmother seemed like the safe second act Hazel needed, until not one, but two library patrons turned up dead. Hazel is joined by a group of intrepid librarians who investigate, trying to remove the cloud of suspicion that suddenly lingers over them. I ate this one up and can’t wait for the sequel.

title: The Secret Room
author: Jane Casey
description: I’ve read all of Jane Casey’s addictive London-based Maeve Kerrigan detective novels at least twice. All 12 are deviously smart and character-driven, blending meticulously plotted mysteries with psychological insight, like Tana French’s Dublin Murder series – but with more chemistry and snark. The Secret Room delivers two impossible scenarios. The first is that the investigation Maeve is leading is a locked-room murder with a married woman found dead in the bathroom of her swanky hotel room. The second, which Maeve shouldn’t touch but can’t resist, is that nemesis-turned-trusted-colleague (and would-be lover) Josh Derwent is suspected of beating his semi-estranged partner unconscious in their home. It’s completely out of character, yet the evidence says otherwise. The twists are diabolical.

title: The White Hot
author: Quiara Alegría Hudes
description: Maybe I shouldn’t have been surprised that a novel by the lyricist and playwright known for writing the book for the Tony Award-winning musical In the Heights would be stunning. But this slim, powerful debut shook me nonetheless. April Soto was barely holding it together, containing the white-hot rage that had plagued her since childhood, when she learned that daughter Noelle might face the same struggle. With prose that reads like poetry and unflinching honesty, the novel mostly takes the form of April’s letter to Noelle explaining why she ran and stayed away for so long. Contemplating whether the best thing she could do for her child was, in truth, to vanish from her life, she thought: “But how, God? How can love look like leaving? And tell me, will I ungunk the gutters of my bad, bad soul?”

title: Picks and Shovels
author: Cory Doctorow
description: This smart and pacey “historical techno-thriller” – the third in a series by the Anglo-Canadian writer Cory Doctorow – transports readers to the dawn of the PC era. It’s the mid-1980s in San Francisco and a young MIT dropout turned freelance tech accountant, Martin Hench, finds himself working, somewhat improbably, for a computer manufacturing company run by a Mormon bishop, a Catholic priest and an Orthodox rabbi. When it turns out to be a huge scam aimed at fleecing religious groups, Hench joins a competitor startup run by a trio of defiant women. Hench and his new colleagues risk their lives in a fight to make the PC revolution more open source.

title: The War of Art: A History of Artists' Protest In America
author: Lauren O'Neill-Butler
description: This book is about the creative – if often short-lived and not always successful – ways in which artists have fought for social change in the U.S. since the 1960s. Personal favorite: a chapter on how the scrappy video collective, Top Value Television (TVTV), changed the public’s view of political conventions. With artist-led protests once again becoming a thing – from the thousands of actors and filmmakers who recently pledged to boycott the Israeli movie industry in response to the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, to the presence of a 12-foot statue depicting President Trump and the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein frolicking on the National Mall, this book about the past provides a powerful frame for thinking about artist-led actions today.

title: So Far Gone
author: Jess Walter
description: Jess Walter has a knack for capturing the absurdity and humanity in the places and eras his novels explore, whether it’s 1960s Italy (Beautiful Ruins) or early 1900s Spokane, Wash. (The Cold Millions). With So Far Gone, it’s the back roads of the Pacific Northwest in our fractured political moment. Retired journalist Rhys Kinnick is content to live off the grid, away from the toxic politics that have strained his relationship with his daughter and conspiracy-theory-obsessed son-in-law. That is until his grandchildren show up on his front porch with news that their mom is missing.
