Rhys becomes the reluctant hero of a madcap journey that ultimately leads to a face-off with a Christian nationalist militia in rural Idaho. It’s a story that is by turns hilarious, heartbreaking and all too relevant.

title: Dear Writer: Pep Talks & Practical Advice for the Creative Life
author: Maggie Smith
description: “Your creativity is calling. It needs you,” urges poet and writer Maggie Smith. This guide on craft is an open, thoughtful gold mine of advice for writers and nonwriters alike. Filled with breakdowns of Smith’s own poems, as well as exercises and suggested outside reading, these pages invite you to reference their knowledge over and over, for a lifetime.

title: The Book of Alchemy: A Creative Practice for an Inspired Life
author: Suleika Jaouad
description: Some books can be daily companions for a more examined life. An invitation toward ritual, reminiscence and observation. In this collection of essays and journaling prompts, Suleika Jaouad offers a library of experiences and tools for written contemplation. Whether you’re a lifelong journaler or new to the practice of taking pen to paper, this creative tome is sure to inspire.

title: Too Soon
author: Betty Shamieh
description: Betty Shamieh’s page-turner of a family epic, Too Soon, tells the story of three generations of Palestinian-American women finding their places in the world, looking for love and, as Shamieh puts it, wringing “an acceptable amount of joy out of a world that is not designed for them to do so.” The story starts with granddaughter Arabella, a theater director given a chance to put on Hamlet in the West Bank. Her grandmother sets her up with an old flame’s grandson, a doctor saving lives in Gaza. But Arabella can’t stop thinking about the Israeli American she’s leaving behind in New York.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Witchcraft for Wayward Girls
author: Grady Hendrix
description: In the 1970s, young women who got pregnant before marriage were sent to homes to have their babies away from prying eyes. It was like a magic trick – a practice in concealment, disappearance and forgetting. In a state of complete powerlessness, hidden away in the stifling heat of St. Augustine, Fla., Fern – not her real name, never give your real name – meets other young girls like herself. Then a visiting librarian gives Fern a book on witchcraft, and she learns what she is willing to give up in return for that power.

title: No Fault: A Memoir of Romance and Divorce
author: Haley Mlotek
description: Haley Mlotek and her high school sweetheart separated 13 months into marriage, after a 13-year relationship. Her memoir weaves her own memories of their divorce with vignettes on the history of the subject and examples from pop culture. Mlotek resists a clean narrative about her relationship and offers no answers or advice for those who may be looking. The only question that matters, she says, is a hopeful one: “But what happened after?”

title: An Oral History of Atlantis: Stories
author: Ed Park
description: Although this is his first short-story collection, Ed Park told Lit Hub that the stories were actually written over a span of about 25 years. No surprise, then, that readers will recognize the same playful wit and eclectic, occasionally challenging style that animate his two previous novels, including the 2024 Pulitzer Prize finalist, Same Bed Different Dreams. But unlike that book, which pulls from Korea’s past for a grandly wrought alternative history, the stories in Atlantis live squarely in our own absurd present, a workaday wonderland where lives can be told in sleep-medication side effects and Internet login passwords.

title: Archipelago of the Sun
author: Yoko Tawada, translated by Margaret Mitsutani
description: Raised in Japan, now based in Berlin, Yoko Tawada reaches readers in English only via translation. Still, there’s no mistaking the shimmering strangeness of her voice. That’s partly a testament to translator Margaret Mitsutani, whose previous work with Tawada has earned one National Book Award and a spot on the shortlist for Scattered All Over the Earth. The latter book opened a trilogy that concludes with Archipelago. Consider this a recommendation for the whole series, frankly. Its three slim books craft a winningly genial kind of dystopia, bleak and earnest in equal measure, in which an intrepid band of misfits carry out their grail quest with all the logic of Calvinball, in which the only rule that really matters is the one you’re about to make up next.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Atavists: Stories
author: Lydia Millet
description: “Is there a writer more profound and less pretentious than Lydia Millet?” That question lead NPR’s review of the author’s previous short story collection and bears asking again now. After a spell that saw her publish a couple of novels and a work of nonfiction, the former Pulitzer and National Book Award finalist has returned to short fiction with her latest, a collection of 14 interconnected stories set in a Los Angeles that is teetering on the cusp of climate catastrophe. Careful though: As always with Millet, the writing here is spare, straightforward and often funny – but beware of its dark and perilous depths.

title: Dealing with the Dead
author: Alain Mabanckou
description: For years, fairly or unfairly, Alain Mabanckou has shouldered the mantle of “the African Samuel Beckett” for his philosophical bent, linguistic mischief and keen sense of the absurd. In his new novel, the Congolese author, now based in California, returns to the coastal city where he grew up, Pointe-Noire, Republic of Congo. This also happens to be the place where you died – “you,” in this case, referring to the book’s second-person point of view, a newly minted member of the deceased whose life, death and peculiar experiences after death shape this prismatic portrait of a city. Like the traditional, semiprofessional mourners who show up at your funeral – known in Helen Stevenson’s translation from the original French as “singer-dancer-weepers” – the novel nimbly dances from mood to mood in a loopy narrative arc that’s as restlessly surprising as it is convincing.

title: Flashlight
author: Susan Choi
description: Susan Choi took home the National Book Award for her previous novel, 2019’s slim, slippery Trust Exercise, a formally inventive portrait of adolescence that the prize jury described as “timely, mesmerizing, and, in the end, unsettling.” This time, Choi broadens her scope, with a story that draws on geopolitics even as it obsessively returns to a single family catastrophe that’s as formative as it is mysterious. Both books share an affinity for shocking twists, as well as the happy tendency to twist reviewers into knots just trying to come up with a spoiler-free synopsis. Flashlight was nominated for both the Booker Prize and the National Book Award.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Flesh
author: David Szalay
description: David Szalay’s first book in seven years won the 2025 Booker Prize. Spare and detached on the page, lush in resonance beyond it, Flesh reads a bit like an immigrant bildungsroman crossed with Albert Camus. At the heart of it all is István, a Hungarian teen making his first stumbling steps into an adulthood that will bring him money and privilege. Along the way he’s beset by trauma, flashes of violence and the more mundane misunderstandings that come to shape his life.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Dark", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Good and Evil and Other Stories
author: Samanta Schweblin, translated by Megan McDowell
description: It makes sense that Samanta Schweblin brought up David Lynch during her 2022 chat with NPR’s Weekend Edition. Sure, the Argentine expat (now based in Berlin) works in a different medium and language, but Schweblin’s fiction bears an undeniable resemblance to the late American director’s films. Even when they’re not outright fever dreams, her worlds tend to be inescapably off – disconcertingly akimbo and bathed in unplaceable menace. Best not to let your guard down with her new short story collection, brought into English by Megan McDowell, a veteran translator of Schweblin’s work.

title: Only Son
author: Kevin Moffett
description: Kevin Moffett’s debut novel follows the arc of his narrator – an only son, natch – from the childhood loss of his father, to an adulthood as the father of an only son himself. Of course, that plot summary does little justice to this slim suburban Buddha of a book, which is composed of a series of short vignettes that are so simple, subtle and sweetly melancholy, they almost resemble Zen koans — though no koan I’ve encountered has, like this book, gotten me giggling enough to alarm passersby.

title: Strata: Stories from Deep Time
author: Laura Poppick
description: It can be fun (or frightening) to imagine humans at the center of everything, but the truth is, we arrived awfully late to the party. Humans have been around for a vanishingly small fraction of the Earth’s roughly 4.5 billion-year life so far. Science journalist Laura Poppick’s first book offers an accessible introduction to what we know of the vast, obscure past that predates us, one layer of rock at a time, offering an entertaining answer to that eternal question of latecomers the world over: So, what did we miss, guys?

title: The Pelican Child: Stories
author: Joy Williams
description: Joy Williams is adept in the art of estrangement – in literally making the mundane strange. The short stories in her latest collection are spiny little nuggets of jamais vu. Sure, they tend to bear the distinguishing characteristics of the familiar world, with all its stressed assistants and heiresses and bewildered man-children. But they’re also moved by some alien inner logic that can be disquietingly difficult to discern. In Williams’ hands, reality is a changeling with a wicked sense of humor.

title: The Wayfinder
author: Adam Johnson
description: Adam Johnson’s back catalog doesn’t take up much shelf space – just four titles in the last 20-plus years. Of course, he won a National Book Award for one of those books, Fortune Smiles, and a Pulitzer Prize for another, The Orphan Master’s Son. Johnson’s first novel in more than a decade shows that the intervening years haven’t dimmed his ambition any. Anybody who knows a kid under 10 may hear echoes of Moana in The Wayfinder’s jacket copy, but the arc of a would-be teenage voyager is just one thread in an impressively dense weave. As he did with North Korea before this, Johnson takes a remote setting – in this case, the Tongan Empire of the Middle Ages – and eschews cliché for living detail, animating this swashbuckling adventure with vivid characters, unnerving violence and more than one literal bag of human bones.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Buffalo Hunter Hunter
author: Stephen Graham Jones
description: The Buffalo Hunter Hunter is many things: a clever nesting doll of narratives, a sanguine revenge thriller stitched inside the corpse of an old vampire yarn, and a fearsome accounting of America’s murderous past. Lucky for us, Stephen Graham Jones has bound it all together with a hero (antihero?) for the ages – a man from the Blackfeet tribe, aptly named Good Stab, who is determined to right the wrongs of the past, even if it takes him a few lifetimes.

title: The Summer of the Bone Horses
author: Virginia Driving Hawk Sneve, illustrated by Steph Littlebird
description: Did you know that upward of 80% of Native Americans live in cities? Many Indigenous kids like Eddie from The Summer of the Bone Horses by Virginia Driving Hawk Sneve (Sicangu Lakota) are partly raised in urban areas and partly by extended families back home on their reservations. While his parents are away for his father’s medical treatment, Eddie stays with Grandma and Grandpa High Elk; their dog,Fritz; their horse, Buck; and the chickens. As the days pass, Eddie grows to appreciate rural life, including his chores, the local store, church and fishing, while also navigating challenges like an injury to Grandpa and rattlesnakes. Steph Littlebird (Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde) offers bright, dynamic illustrations that add texture and emotion to this gentle page-turner. (For ages 5 to 8)

title: Who Is Government?: The Untold Story of Public Service
author: Michael Lewis (editor)
description: Thousands of unsung heroes in the government are making life better for Americans. But because of bureaucracies being made up of bureaucrats, we rarely hear those stories. This book showcases them. Like a coal-mining safety official who helped the U.S. reach zero mine-collapse deaths. Or the man who has led the National Cemetery Administration to the top of the American Customer Satisfaction Index. As the federal government is in its biggest shake-up in a generation, it’s worth learning about where the bright spots are.

title: Alabama Village: Faith, Hope, and Survival in a Southern Town
author: J. Malcolm Garcia
description: J. Malcolm Garcia’s intimate portraits of young people struggling to survive in a rough neighborhood outside Mobile, Ala., are heartbreaking and, yet, somehow hopeful at the same time. We meet them through their connection to a church established by a Christian couple who see the good at the core of people whom society has largely written off. The community is plagued by gun violence, blight, generational poverty and addiction. Too many people don’t live to be 30. The storytelling is powerful: “Death has as much of a presence in the Village as the people living here, attaches to them like a second shadow.”

title: Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church
author: Kevin Sack
description: There was great symbolism when a white supremacist targeted Charleston’s Mother Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church, killing nine Black worshippers as a means to ignite a race war. As we learn in this deeply researched history, the congregation has been involved in the struggle for racial justice ever since it was founded in an “act of bold subversion” by enslaved and free African Americans in the 1800s. I am struck by the stories of clergy and members who fought against seemingly insurmountable odds at nearly every turn of history, truly living out their faith and believing in a better America.

title: Coded Justice: A Thriller
author: Stacey Abrams
description: A well-intentioned entrepreneur harnesses artificial intelligence to help military veterans get the best health care possible. What could go wrong? Murder, for a start. Are the robots behind it? This is the third in the Avery Keene legal-thriller series. Keene is now an investigator at a law firm assigned to look into it all. The novel is ethically and morally nuanced; plus, it’s a fun way to learn more about AI. Stacey Abrams told NPR’s Michel Martin that every Avery Keene novel starts with a question Abrams has. This one began with two: How does AI work? And why are we so bad at health care? Even if you have little interest in either question, the novel is a compelling read.

title: Love Forms
author: Claire Adam
description: Don’t be put off by the dull title. This novel reads like a poignant memoir by an older woman trying to piece together fragments of her past, ostensibly to help her find the daughter she gave up for adoption decades earlier. Dawn, now divorced and living in England, grew up white and wealthy in Trinidad. At 16, she was sent on a harrowing journey by small boat to Venezuela, where nuns took in unmarried pregnant girls. Is it Dawn’s search for her daughter that finally allows her to seek answers to questions she’s never dared to ask?
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Cursed Daughters
author: Oyinkan Braithwaite
description: Cursed Daughters, by bestselling author Oyinkan Braithwaite (My Sister, the Serial Killer), is another genre-bending novel that offers family insights, multigenerational curses and surprises. Cursed Daughters follows three women of the Falodun family – Monife, Ebun and Eniiyi – who are bound by a long-standing curse that “no man will call your house his home.” Infused with Nigerian culture and magical realism, the novel showcases Braithwaite’s rich characterizations, atmospheric settings and a daring premise. It also offers a multilayered story that makes Cursed Daughters a captivating, immersive read.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Happy Land
author: Dolen Perkins-Valdez
description: New York Times bestselling author Dolen Perkins-Valdez has written a beautifully crafted novel about hope, loss, tragedy, pride and family. Happy Land, inspired by actual events, explores the post-Reconstruction Black community that purchased land on the border of North and South Carolina and governed it, naming a king and queen. The story, told in a dual timeline, also examines legacy and estranged familial relationships while offering a compassionate look at how decisions made 150 years ago impact a modern family and help rebuild the bonds that connect generations. The novel is both an informative read and a loving tribute to Black families and the ties that link the past to the present.

title: Harlem Rhapsody
author: Victoria Christopher Murray
description: Harlem Rhapsody, set in the early 1920s, is an outstanding novel that tells the fictionalized story of the real-life Jessie Redmon Fauset, the first literary editor of the NAACP’s monthly magazine, The Crisis. Victoria Christopher Murray writes a thoroughly researched and enjoyable novel about Fauset, arguably one of the most overlooked women of the Harlem Renaissance. Murray explores Fauset’s contributions as editor and mentor to literary icons such as Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston and Countee Cullen. The novel also explores Fauset’s complex relationship with W.E.B. Du Bois. New York Times bestselling author Murray (The Personal Librarian and The First Ladies, co-authored with Marie Benedict) crafts a well-paced, engaging story that is a must-read and a highlight of 2025 historical fiction.

title: I, Medusa
author: Ayana Gray
description: Ayana Gray reimagines a classic Greek myth in a compelling, emotional and unexpected coming-of-age story. The New York Times bestselling author of young adult fantasy series (Beasts of Prey, Beasts of Ruin, Beasts of War) makes her debut in adult fiction with I, Medusa. This bold novel provides an in-depth look into a villain’s origins, emphasizing themes of sisterhood, culture, identity and revenge. Medusa, or Meddy, is a mortal 17-year-old with two sisters who have immortal blood. She catches Poseidon’s attention, but jealousy leads to an unthinkable consequence. Gray’s adult novel offers a bold interpretation of Medusa’s monstrous origins, making it a must-read.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Once Upon a Time in Dollywood
author: Ashley Jordan
description: Ashley Jordan’s debut novel (and Reese’s Book Club pick) Once Upon a Time in Dollywood is an emotionally fearless story that is simultaneously heartbreaking, funny and tender, while challenging traditional expectations of a happily-ever-after. Eve, a young woman on uncertain ground, must face tough choices and heartbreaks as she rebuilds her sense of self and love. The story draws out every emotion from its main character as she searches for herself amid the ruins. Unpredictable yet deeply satisfying, Jordan’s debut delivers an emotionally resonant ending from an exciting new voice in contemporary fiction.

title: People of Means
author: Nancy Johnson
description: People of Means by Nancy Johnson, the bestselling author of The Kindest Lie, tells the story of Freda, a student in 1959 at Fisk University, a historically Black college, and of her daughter Tulip, who is climbing the corporate ladder in 1992. In this dual-timeline novel, Johnson skillfully weaves rich characterization, social history and moral stakes into a compelling narrative set during two pivotal moments in American history – when students in Nashville began organizing against Jim Crow and during the national reckoning following the Rodney King verdict. People of Means is an important, thought-provoking novel that explores Black excellence, activism and the middle-class experience.

title: The Princess and the P.I.
author: Nikki Payne
description: Nikki Payne is a powerhouse in the romance genre, and her latest novel, The Princess and the P.I., showcases her signature mix of wit, heart and social insight. The plot description: Fiona Addai leads a double life – by day, she is the obedient daughter of a Ghanaian megachurch pastor; by night, she becomes the Reddit sleuth “@Princess_PI,” solving scandals and cold cases. Payne expertly combines romance, mystery and thrills, pairing a badass heroine and a guilt-ridden hero in a story that offers substance, style and excitement in a beautifully wrapped romance package.

title: The Wilderness
author: Angela Flournoy
description: The Wilderness by Angela Flournoy was longlisted for the National Book Award and is the author’s second novel, after The Turner House, which was also a finalist. Flournoy’s new work follows five Black women – two sisters, Desiree and Danielle, and their three friends, January, Monique and Nakia –  beginning in their early 20s and spanning decades as they navigate relationships, motherhood, careers, lovers and friendship. A nonlinear, complex story that is loving, tragic, funny and thought-provoking, The Wilderness showcases Flournoy as a fearless writer who creates richly nuanced characters, revealing their raw emotional lives and the deep bonds that connect them. It’s a must-read.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Fetishized: A Reckoning with Yellow Fever, Feminism, and Beauty
author: Kaila Yu
description: Rock star and former model Kaila Yu traces the very fine line between Asian representation in mainstream media being empowering and objectifying. In this memoir-in-essays, Yu uses her personal stories as a woman who admits to “cater[ing] to the white male gaze” as a vehicle to explore the fetishization of Asian women, particularly in American pop culture. From her opening anecdote about an “Asiaphile” who wanted to start a class that would have Asian women teaching others how to be the best wives, to her closing reflection on self-objectification and internalized racism, Yu’s memoir is a true examination of the systemic factors that have shaped – and are still shaping – the exotification and fetishization of Asian women in the U.S. today.

title: Heart Lamp: Selected Stories
author: Banu Mushtaq, translated by Deepa Bhasthi
description: Heart Lamp hurls you into southwest India, where Muslim women survive in a world of casual cruelty, one of rules made for – and by – men. In one story, a woman despairs in marital abandonment but finds strength in her daughter. Another woman is ignored as she begs for help for her daughter, later describing her relief at her baby’s death. Banu Mushtaq’s book isn’t a simplistic takedown of Islam. These women are evocative and fully formed. They are inspired by Mushtaq’s own experiences as a lawyer representing women in her own Kannada-speaking community. When the book won the International Booker Prize, Mushtaq described the moment feeling “like a thousand fireflies lighting up a single sky.” That’s how I felt reading this book.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Short Stories & Essays", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Shattered Lands: Five Partitions and the Making of Modern Asia
author: Sam Dalrymple
description: Sam Dalrymple’s book does the rare thing of recasting history to help us understand it anew. He investigates the five major partitions that the British undertook as they slowly ended their imperial rule over India – an India that we no longer know, which included Burma, parts of the Middle East, Bangladesh and, yes, Pakistan. The book is sweeping in scope but evocative in the small people and moments he brings forward. You don’t have to be a South Asia nerd to be into this book – it’s so gripping that it holds its own as a work of popular fiction. But for us nerds, it is an excellent work on the broken promise of ethno-nationalism in South Asia.

title: The Technological Republic: Hard Power, Soft Belief, and the Future of the West
author: Alexander C. Karp and Nicholas W. Zamiska
description: Has the Western world lost interest in the great possibilities of science and technology? This provocative book claims giant tech firms are focusing on short-term commercial gain from consumer products that don’t – and won’t – have lasting impact, while ignoring bigger questions that could – and should – benefit society at large. The authors argue for a renewed partnership between tech companies and government, viewing it as a moral responsibility. Artificial intelligence makes the stakes high. The time is now, as it all shapes the community to which we belong. These important questions merit debate. The book is easy to read, and though it doesn’t exactly declare definitive answers, it’s worthy of your time.

title: My Friends
author: Fredrik Backman, translated by Neil Smith
description: This is Fredrik Backman at the height of his empathy and resonance. It’s the story of a painting – its artist and its subjects – and the journey of a young woman to understand how it came to be. It’s also a deeply personal exploration of art, what we sacrifice of ourselves to bring beauty into the world and what we gain when others find a home within it. I frequently paused to marvel at the way Backman captured the rebellion of joy, the ferocity of devotion and the cruelty of indifference. Every Backman book should come with the warning that your heart will be split in two but also, more importantly, with the assurance that it will be repaired with restored faith in the small miracles of being human.

title: All the Other Mothers Hate Me
author: Sarah Harman
description: This book got me out of a reading rut! It’s about a mom who is struggling to keep her life together – while simultaneously trying to solve the mystery of her son’s missing classmate. It’s got fun twists and turns and characters who surprise you. Very plot driven and definitely hard to put down.

title: The Correspondent
author: Virginia Evans
description: This is the story of a 70-year-old woman – who I’m honestly not sure I really liked! – who had a distinguished legal career, a painful family life and is on the way to going blind. The book is told through letters and emails – both sent and received. There are letters between friends, emails with customer support and even some hate mail. I was a bit hesitant about an epistolary novel, but there’s enough going on in all the various forms of contact that some parts read like a love story, others a comedy of errors, and then there are some mysteries and twists built in that keep you engaged until the end.

title: Kakigori Summer
author: Emily Itami
description: This book transported me to a hot summer in coastal Japan, where three half-Japanese sisters gather at the family home after one of them has become a national scandal. What rings so true for me is that it not only explores the shifting dynamics of sisterhood, but also probes what it’s like to be a perpetual outsider in a place that you think of as home. The book is a summer treat that doesn’t melt away but continues to resonate after you put it down.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: My Name Is Emilia del Valle
author: Isabel Allende, translated by Frances Riddle
description: I wish I could be more like Emilia del Valle! She doesn’t let the fact that she’s a woman (in late 19th century San Franciso) stop her from first becoming an author – and then a war correspondent. Deciding to explore her Chilean roots, Emilia plunges headlong into Chile’s civil war, travels with the troops and manages to fall in love. Yet she never loses her sense of who she is and what she wants. The book immersed me in history I didn’t know and gave me someone I could really root for.

title: Unfit Parent: A Disabled Mother Challenges an Inaccessible World
author: Jessica Slice
description: This book made me question assumptions I’d made about child-rearing. Author Jessica Slice became disabled, then a parent, and discovered that not only could she raise a child, but she was also better able to cope with some of the demands of parenting than those who weren’t disabled. Slice interviews other parents with disabilities and discovers a variety of creative ways they take care of their kids, even while she and they continually encounter prejudice about their fitness to do so. A mind-opening read.

title: Girl Dinner
author: Olivie Blake
description: The House is the most exclusive sorority on campus: The members are beautiful, the alumnae successful, and the traditions involve just the occasional touch of ritual cannibalism. Viewed through the eyes of Nina, an ambitious sophomore pledge trying to reinvent herself, and Sloane, a new mother and floundering adjunct professor drafted to serve as faculty adviser, The House becomes the locus for this smart and grimly funny satire. Girl Dinner is a slow burn of a book, where humor and horror work together to unpack contemporary notions of girlhood and femininity.

title: The Dream Hotel
author: Laila Lalami
description: Archivist and mother Sara T. Hussein gets detained at an airport. Her crime? A dream deemed too high risk by an AI algorithm. Writing incisively, Laila Lalami brilliantly builds a world where a pre-crime system collides with surveillance capitalism. With the novel’s compelling cast of characters and endless parallels to today, I found The Dream Hotel instructive for navigating a society beset by mass surveillance – where the only escape can be found in shouldering risk together.

title: Desi Arnaz: The Man Who Invented Television
author: Todd S. Purdum
description: Some I Love Lucy fans may have seen Cuban-born Desi Arnaz as little more than husband and co-star to comedy legend Lucille Ball on their groundbreaking 1950s-era sitcom. But Todd S. Purdum digs deep to detail how the son of Cuban aristocracy fled to America and fought racism to help redefine the shape of modern TV. As co-founder of Desilu Productions, he helped pioneer shooting I Love Lucy episodes with three cameras on film – making rebroadcast “reruns” of TV shows possible – building a studio that eventually made series like Mission: Impossible and Star Trek. It’s a detailed and compelling look at an ace performer who became the first Latino star and first Latino executive in American TV.

title: Lorne: The Man Who Invented Saturday Night Live
author: Susan Morrison
description: He’s been compared to everyone from Darth Vader to the Wizard of Oz. But Saturday Night Live creator and executive producer Lorne Michaels has remained a coolly enigmatic figure for most of the show’s 50-season tenure – ripe for a star-filled, myth-busting biography. Susan Morrison, an editor at The New Yorker, got Michaels to open up in interviews, developing a detailed, knowing look at his career, from his early days in Canadian TV to his status as major gatekeeper in American comedy. She explores it all in a 656-page tome flipping between his life story and work on one episode in 2018, revealing how Michaels leverages a detached confidence and command of the zeitgeist to keep SNL relevant.

title: Waiting on the Moon: Artists, Poets, Drifters, Grifters, and Goddesses
author: Peter Wolf
description: It makes complete sense that a memoir by a respected rock veteran would read like a favorite album, with each chapter playing like one great storytelling track after another. Although, in this case, Peter Wolf’s stories challenge the usual rock star narrative to include his experiences with art (he’s a painter), philosophy, books, short marriage to a Hollywood star and even a very brief NPR mention. But don’t fret, musician stories abound, including mentions of Keith Richards, Merle Haggard, and the most intimate, loving portrayals of blues musicians John Lee Hooker and Muddy Waters that I have ever read.

title: Immaculate Conception
author: Ling Ling Huang
description: Ling Ling Huang’s Immaculate Conception is one of those novels that are about so much that they’re hard to pin down. An exploration of art in the digital age, a narrative about the contrasts between those who have and those who don’t, a deep look at the world of art in a near future where most art is derivative or irrelevant, and a science-fiction tale of grief and friendship, this is a nuanced, complex novel that, despite its flaws, soars thanks to its author’s unflinching approach to discussing creation, originality and the volatile world of art.

title: Lay Your Armor Down
author: Michael Farris Smith
description: Every year there is a novel that comes out of left field and sticks with me for a long time. This year, it was Michael Farris Smith’s Lay Your Armor Down. A novel that shyly dips its toes into the supernatural while anchored in crime fiction, this story of desperate men caught up in a strange situation with a child who may or may not have special powers is raw and violent. But it’s also full of lyrical passages and the kind of sharp prose that has made Smith a household name.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Necessary Fiction
author: Eloghosa Osunde
description: I think everyone who read Vagabonds! is at least curious, if not downright excited, about whatever Eloghosa Osunde does next – and that next is here. Necessary Fiction takes readers into the hectic heart of Lagos and drops them into the lives of multiple queer characters looking for love, joy, freedom, and themselves while navigating their identity in a country where who they are can mean broken relationships and scorn. The lies these characters tell others are the tools that allow them to participate in everything the city has to offer, but these “necessary fictions” come at a price, and I can’t imagine anyone better to explore that price than Osunde.

title: Saint of the Narrows Street
author: William Boyle
description: William Boyle has built a devout readership by delivering novels about normal people who get caught up in bad situations, and this one is his best work yet. It’s a heartbreaking tale of loss, anger, grief, poverty, the varying strength of familial bonds and how an accidental death can haunt a family for generations. Boyle is a master at making the ordinary important, the everyday special. A dark novel with a beautiful, broken heart.

title: Secret Lives of the Dead
author: Tim Lebbon
description: Tim Lebbon is great at horror and action, and Secret Lives of the Dead has both in spades. This is a novel about a family legend that manages to be two things at all times. The first is a frenetic, gruesome noir tale of secret agendas and violence. The other is a narrative full of folk horror and supernatural violence. Over the course of a single day, the lives of everyone in this story – and their pasts – collide with brutal consequences.

title: Spread Me
author: Sarah Gailey
description: Erotic horror isn’t easy to pull off, but Sarah Gailey does so with flying colors here. A remote research outpost surrounded by sand can be either a dream or a nightmare depending on your personality, but when a researcher breaks safety protocols and brings in a dead animal full of the thing that killed it, what ensues is a tense, creepy story where everyone could be something else – and desire is only the means to an end.

title: The Night Birds
author: Christopher Golden
description: Christopher Golden consistently delivers solid horror fiction, and The Night Birds is no different. A boat that turned into an island of mangroves and is now being studied by scientists is the setting of this tale of witches in which saving a child and overcoming the past are two almost impossible things. They are also just the tip of the proverbial iceberg. Another great horror action novel from one of the best in the genre.

title: The Note
author: Alafair Burke
description: Alafair Burke is one of those authors who can turn a small note into a gripping novel, and that’s what she does here. A group of friends leaves a note on a car, and that implicates them in something – or is there more to the story? Engaging from the first page, full of complex, believable characters, and with an eye on internet and cancel culture, this is another solid addition to Burke’s already impressive oeuvre.

title: Your Steps on the Stairs
author: Antonio Muñoz Molina, translated by Curtis Bauer
description: “Read this amazing novel about a man waiting for his wife in Portugal” might not seem like the best pitch, but the fact that Antonio Muñoz Molina managed to write a captivating book that’s mostly about waiting merits attention. A man is waiting for his wife after they decided to leave New York behind, but the emptiness of the wait makes him think, and those ideas, memories, obsessions, fears, desires and opinions make up most of the novel. A perfect example of a book that shouldn’t work, but definitely does.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: Strangers and Intimates: The Rise and Fall of Private Life
author: Tiffany Jenkins
description: Don’t let the page count worry you; Tiffany Jenkins is a confident guide through several centuries of social upheaval, tracing developing concepts of personal privacy: freedom from surveillance, autonomy in work, the personhood to participate in public life. (Many specifics feel timeless; early Kodak ads touted “the thrill of capturing the likeness of a person without their knowledge.”) The scope of this history is impressive, and at a time when every app wants your location, concerning. A remarkable chronicle of the ways people have fought for our personhood and privacy – and what we stand to lose when those are gone.

title: The Feather Detective: Mystery, Mayhem, and the Magnificent Life of Roxie Laybourne
author: Chris Sweeney
description: Forensic ornithology is real, and Roxie Laybourne made it happen. This painstaking biography traces the ornithologist’s Smithsonian career from early days restoring samples to being the world’s best bird identifier, assisting with everything from poaching investigations to NASA flights. Though not always comfortable under the pressure of working with law enforcement, Laybourne was an indomitable force, and her passion for birds – through investigation, teaching and conservation – is evident on every page. For those who love niche stories of nature, science and social history, The Feather Detective offers a timely account of a fascinating – and peculiar – ecosystem: one woman, and lots and lots of birds.

title: Actress of a Certain Age: My Twenty-Year Trail to Overnight Success
author: Jeff Hiller
description: The most pleasant surprise of this year’s Emmys ceremony was the best supporting actor in a comedy award going to Jeff Hiller, for his work in the brilliant Somebody Somewhere. Hiller had been working in Hollywood for years, mostly in bit parts, and Actress of a Certain Age is his funny, dishy, self-deprecating, occasionally filthy chronicle of his years in the wilderness. Hiller is candid and clear-eyed about himself and his work and he makes for a bracingly self-aware guide through his life. He reads the audiobook, too – and he lands every joke with a charming giggle.

title: Florenzer
author: Phil Melanson
description: Florenzer focuses on a young Leonardo da Vinci as he comes to terms with his talent and what we would today call his queerness, in a way that never seems ahistorical or overdetermined, like it’s forcing modern sensibilities and perspectives onto a very unmodern world. Instead, Phil Melanson has taken great pains to capture the place and time with a bracing clarity and immediacy – not only the internecine politics of the Medicis and the papacy, but the physicality of Florence, Italy, itself. I love the vividness of Melanson’s prose, which captures the sights, sounds and smells so well you feel like you’re walking the streets of the city alongside his characters.
tags: ["Art", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction"]

title: Self-Sabotage: And Other Ways I've Spent My Time
author: Jeffery Self
description: Actor/writer/comedian Jeffery Self’s memoir Self-Sabotage is about exactly that – the many ways he’s managed to be his own worst enemy over the years. He writes with a kind of hilarious intensity about bad decisions, bad behavior and bad breakups. And he’s not just charmingly self-deprecating, he’s searingly self-lacerating, whether he’s telling stories about his life as a screamingly gay theater kid in rural Georgia, moving to New York and paying the rent with sex work or finding a mentor in Broadway actor Gary Beach – which is a hugely moving part of the book, all about what queer folk owe to our elders. Even if you don’t know Jeffery Self the actor, you should check out Jeffery Self the writer – he’s in complete command of himself and his voice.

title: Julie Chan Is Dead
author: Liann Zhang
description: Julie and her estranged twin, Chloe, may look identical, but that’s where the similarities end. Julie works at a supermarket, while her sister revels in the love of millions as a social media influencer. But when Chloe dies, Julie realizes she can pass for her twin – if people don’t look too closely. What follows is a thrilling, haunting look at the upkeep of pretending to be someone you’re not, whether on-screen or in person. As Julie goes to brow-raising lengths to keep up the farce and maintain her newfound audience’s love, you’ll find yourself asking whether she has a limit.

title: There's Something About Mira
author: Sonali Dev
description: Mira’s pre-wedding trip to New York is turning out to be interesting: Her fiancé is too busy with work to travel, her twin brother is mad at her, and she stumbles upon a mysterious ring. She’s determined to find the ring’s owner, but she needs the help of surly journalist Krish Hale. Meanwhile, in parallel, we hear through letters the story of two women in India in the 1980s – friends who are forbidden to be anything more – who are connected by, you guessed it, a ring. This is one of Sonali Dev’s most powerful works yet, going beyond just romance to explore identity, family and resilience.

title: The Three Lives of Cate Kay
author: Kate Fagan
description: Anne Callahan and her best friend, Amanda, have dreams of making it big in Hollywood, until the night that changes the course of their lives. The story follows Annie’s transformation from high school thespian to one of the world’s most famous authors – though she’s known as the pseudonym Cate Kay to her readers. But Annie also goes by other names to satisfy different ambitions. We learn what she gains and loses along the way – the friends and homes she leaves behind, the heart she breaks along the way, and the loneliness that comes with her isolated fame.

title: When the Going Was Good: An Editor's Adventures During the Last Golden Age of Magazines
author: Graydon Carter with James Fox
description: “You never know when you’re in a golden age,” Graydon Carter writes. “You only realize it was a golden age when it’s gone.” Carter is a larger-than-life figure from the magazine world’s golden age, but he can be self-deprecating: He describes himself as a “beta male” even during the time he helmed Vanity Fair, at the height of his influence. For me – someone who’s devoured magazines all my life and came of age as a reporter at Time, where Carter got his start – this memoir was essential reading of the most joyful sort, with its evocation of a more abundant, irreverent and freewheeling journalistic time. “I simply love being an editor,” Carter writes. I simply love his book.

title: A Truce That Is Not Peace
author: Miriam Toews
description: I’m always skeptical when critics say a book about heavy stuff is darkly funny, but Miriam Toews’ memoir about the deaths by suicide of her father and sister 12 years apart really is both very serious and laugh-out-loud funny. This tribute to her only sibling – and to her stalwart mother, who shares her morbid sense of humor – is a tender meditation on writing and silence that ponders the question, “If silence says more, why write?” Clearly, like laughter, writing is a powerful coping mechanism for the talented Toews.

title: Dark Renaissance: The Dangerous Times and Fatal Genius of Shakespeare's Greatest Rival
author: Stephen Greenblatt
description: Elizabethan scholar Stephen Greenblatt has written a riveting thriller about the short, daring life and work of Shakespeare’s contemporary, Christopher “Kit” Marlowe. The son of a poor Canterbury cobbler, Marlowe was an unlikely artistic genius whose innovative, popular plays reflected the brutal violence, backstabbing ambition and greed that characterized Elizabeth I’s regime. Did he spy for the queen? Was he killed at age 29 on her orders, or in a bar brawl? Was he an atheist? Homosexual? Due to lack of documentation, Greenblatt can’t say for sure, but he has his theories.

title: Notes to John
author: Joan Didion
description: In late 1999, Joan Didion started seeing a psychiatrist, hoping it would help untangle her troubled daughter Quintana’s issues. Her detailed, unguarded notes on her sessions, addressed to her husband, John Gregory Dunne, were found in a portable filing cabinet in her home office after her death in 2021. The decision by Didion’s heirs to publish them raises questions about privacy, but Notes to John offers a valuable key to the genesis of Didion’s memoirs about the devastating deaths of her husband and daughter, and it reaffirms that writing was how she processed everything.

title: The Ferryman and His Wife
author: Frode Grytten, translated by Alison McCullough
description: In its original Norwegian, the title of this short, quiet treasure is The Day That Nils Vik Died – and that’s precisely what this novel is about. On a rainy November day, we accompany the widowed ferryman on his last trip plying the fjords in his beloved boat. Along the way, the ghosts of passengers from his past “ooze out of the logbooks” and line the fjord. He reconnects with people (and a dog) he helped, as well as others whose needs he wishes he’d better understood. It’s a beautiful tribute to the power of kindness.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Paris Express
author: Emma Donoghue
description: In her latest historical novel, Emma Donoghue takes us for a ride on the Paris-bound train that left the Normandy coast on Oct. 22, 1895, and ended up crashing through the parapet of Montparnasse Station seven hours later. En route, we meet a cross-section of fin-de-siècle France, including crewmen, artists, engineers, prominent politicians, rich toffs, immigrants and an angry young radical feminist who plans to detonate a homemade explosive sometime before the train reaches Paris. The result is a propulsive read that plows ahead unstoppably to its climax.

title: The Place of Tides
author: James Rebanks
description: If you’re looking for an escape from the stresses of what English farmer and author James Rebanks calls our “dark and chaotic world,” I highly recommend his transporting account of 10 weeks on a tiny Norwegian island where he arranges to help one of the last “duck women” through the annual eiderdown harvest. Initially feeling burned out and restless, Rebanks comes to find the painstaking, repetitive work of preparing for and tending the breeding ducks both settling and calming. This lovely, meditative book is a paean to slowing down and paying attention.

title: Three Days in June
author: Anne Tyler
description: This short, funny and wise charmer features a divorced couple – one inflexible and judgmental, the other loosey-goosey and forgiving – thrown together by their daughter’s wedding (and an aged, overweight rescue cat in need of a home). Both are teachers, and neither cares about fancy food or clothes. It may be Anne Tyler-lite, but it’s the perfect setup to address one of her pet subjects: how marriages, whether successful or failed, morph through the years.

title: Little Bosses Everywhere: How the Pyramid Scheme Shaped America
author: Bridget Read
description: At the start, this book sounds like it’s overselling the evils of multilevel marketing. Surely LuLaRoe and Mary Kay cosmetics aren’t part of “a radical ideological plot to reorganize everything about how we work, survive and take care of one another”?! At the end, you’ll say, “Wow, capitalism is the biggest MLM scheme of all.” In between, Bridget Read explains why few people understand how MLM organizations function; how MLM heavyweight Amway has tentacles in the Heritage Foundation (of Project 2025 fame) and the Republican Party; and why these cult-esque operations are still allowed to flourish – all over the world.

title: The Naming of the Birds
author: Paraic O'Donnell
description: This Victorian mystery novel is Dickens meets Sherlock Holmes meets La Femme Nikita, and it wears its genre conventions proudly. The heroes: a brilliant, gruff police officer and his bumbling assistant, aided by a plucky lady journalist. The crimes: elaborate serial murders of insignificant elderly men. The killings are connected to the book’s prologue, a harrowing tale of mistreated orphans seemingly in training to be assassins. The reader knows this, but the detectives do not, giving the events a frisson of dramatic irony as the body count ticks up.

title: elseship: an unrequited affair
author: Tree Abraham
description: Falling in love with your roommate is a horrible idea, but book designer Tree Abraham didn’t plan to – it just happened. When Abraham confessed her love, she was gently but firmly rebuffed; her roommate was straight and didn’t feel the same way. In this lyrical account of the months following that rejection, Abraham documents how, instead of letting this be the end of their friendship, she and her roommate forged something deeper from the awkward space, a complex relationship that included plenty of love. Anyone who has ever found romance and friendship more alike than not will find a deep recognition in these gorgeous pages.

title: Make Sure You Die Screaming
author: Zee Carlstrom
description: You know that meme where a dog is sitting in a burning house saying “This is fine”? The narrator of this book is like that: They’re incredibly angry at the world and its ills, but they seem to be using that rage to suppress the ways their own life is on fire. They head home (ostensibly to help their mom search for their missing dad) in a stolen car with a new friend, Yivi, and spend days with her on the road making one bad decision after another. This book is for anyone who’s mad, messy and needs a good laugh to boot.

title: Meet Me at the Crossroads
author: Megan Giddings
description: I first got to know Megan Giddings’ writing through her short stories and flash fiction, and I adored her first two novels – so it’s no surprise to me that her third, Meet Me at the Crossroads, is gorgeous, heartfelt and wonderfully strange. Twins Ayanna and Olivia each live with a different parent: Olivia with their Christian mom and Ayanna with their Pathsong dad. Pathsong, the religion Ayanna comes of age in, is a belief system centered around the seven mysterious doors that have appeared around the world and which open at unpredictable intervals. Through the doors lies, maybe, the world of the dead – or, maybe, just a totally different plane of existence. When Ayanna takes part in a ritual that brings her through the doors, Olivia unexpectedly tags along and, well, you should read it and find out.

title: Old Soul
author: Susan Barker
description: Jake and Mariko meet when they both miss their flight out of Osaka and decide to share a meal. Later, drunk, Mariko tells Jake about how her twin brother died, and Jake is eerily reminded of a beloved friend’s strange death. The deceased both began behaving differently shortly before they died, after meeting an exceptionally charming woman, and both had biological oddities discovered during their autopsies. Could it have been the same woman? If so, who is she? And what is she after? If you, too, are a sucker for books that follow a central mystery through the stories of seemingly disparate but ultimately interconnected characters, this one is a must.

title: Poets Square: A Memoir in Thirty Cats
author: Courtney Gustafson
description: Courtney Gustafson was just trying to survive in the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic; she was working for a food bank but ironically struggling to make ends meet herself. She certainly didn’t expect the house she’d just signed a lease on to have dozens of feral cats hanging around. She was even less prepared for how much she began to care about those cats that were, like her, doing their best. As Gustafson and her feral cats accidentally go viral, she begins to think about the nature of care and how intertwined its different forms are. (Fellow cat lovers, make sure to keep some tissues handy.)

title: Save Me, Stranger: Stories
author: Erika Krouse
description: It’s likely that we’ve all, at one time or another, wanted to save someone or be saved – sometimes both – and in these stories Erika Krouse explores how complicated, and maybe even impossible, those desires are to fulfill. From a pair of terminally ill combat veterans who become angels of death, to the mayor of the coldest town on Earth, Krouse’s narrators are people navigating what they want, what others want for them and what they think they deserve. My favorite kind of short story doesn’t resolve neatly, but rather leaves me with a lingering image, idea or possibility, and Krouse’s Save Me, Stranger absolutely fits the bill.

title: The Expert of Subtle Revisions
author: Kirsten Menger-Anderson
description: Hase was raised by a single father who, despite shielding her from much of the real world, always loved her dearly, so when he doesn’t show up at Half Moon Bay on her birthday, she worries. Soon she’s embroiled in a mystery that involves a shady institute that believes in time travel. The book itself then travels back to 1930s Vienna, where readers meet two men who move in the same academic circles in very different ways. History, Hase knows from the hours she spends revising Wikipedia entries, can look different depending on who’s telling it and how, but she never imagined that knowledge applying to her own story.

title: The Island of Last Things
author: Emma Sloley
description: In the not-too-distant future, climate change and its attendant complications have caused masses of species to go extinct, with some rare specimens inhabiting places like the private zoo on Alcatraz Island. Camille works and lives on Alcatraz and prefers spending her time with the animals – until, that is, a new zookeeper, Sailor, arrives, and she finally feels like she’s found a kindred spirit. But when Sailor begins hatching plans to free the animals, Camille is faced with questions about the nature of freedom, survival, fantasy and friendship. This sad, sweet and still hopeful book is for the animal lovers among us.

title: The Other Wife
author: Jackie Thomas-Kennedy
description: Zuzu’s life seems perfect: Her wife is a high-powered lawyer, she lives in a beautiful house full of beautiful things, and she’s a full-time mom to her son Gideon. But she’s restless, and when a chance comes up to see her longtime friend Cash, whom she had a crush on in college, she jumps at it. Sound like a book about the one who got away? Sure, in some ways it is. But in more ways, it’s about the sometimes sustaining, sometimes destructive nature of living inside one’s fantasies of what other people are like.

title: The Payback
author: Kashana Cauley
description: Who hasn’t fantasized about getting a crew together, Ocean’s Eleven-style, and executing a heist? Well, narrator Jada probably hadn’t before her life began to fall apart and the weight of her student loan debts became unbearable. But after being fired from her dream job and then being fired from her retail job, which she mostly liked OK, Jada and her friends begin to plan a heist, of sorts, to wipe out their – and everyone else’s – student loan debts. The stakes are especially high because the chiseled, turquoise-wearing members of a newly formed police division have been brutally beating people in debt, disproportionately targeting Black women like Jada.

title: The Bewitching
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: “Could someone plateau at twenty-four? Could your brain shrink?” asks Minerva, one of three protagonists in this witchy, multigenerational tale. During a summer spent on the grounds of her beloved Stoneridge College, Minerva struggles to write her graduate thesis on the macabre literature of Beatrice Tremblay, an alum from the 1930s. But burnout isn’t quite the issue; Minerva begins to wonder whether she’s being haunted by a witch, a witch of the same ilk that crept into the life of her great-grandmother Nana Alba – and perhaps the life of Beatrice Tremblay as well. Does witchcraft somehow link these women together? There are endless delights to uncover within the novel’s enchanting orbit of historical academic spook. Dead rats abound, for one. And maybe even a touch of the sapphic.

title: Everything Is Tuberculosis: The History and Persistence of Our Deadliest Infection
author: John Green
description: Tuberculosis has preyed on the most vulnerable humans since the dawn of our species. But author John Green argues that we now have the knowledge and resources to defeat the disease – we lack only the will. Spun through surprising histories (did you know TB led to the creation of the Stetson hat?) and personal anecdotes from a modern-day survivor, Everything Is Tuberculosis makes an urgent case for the kind of global health initiatives that the U.S. has been turning against. It’s also witty, cogent and achingly beautiful – everything we’ve come to expect from the writer of The Anthropocene Reviewed and The Fault in Our Stars.

title: Katabasis
author: R.F. Kuang
description: Let’s get two things out of the way first: Katabasis is the Greek term for stories recounting journeys to the underworld, and the real hell is grad school. R.F. Kuang’s dense novel follows two rival doctoral students (of magic!) who follow their adviser into literal hell. Following the threads of tales like Dante’s Inferno, Alice Law and Peter Murdoch journey through each circle of the damned, which for them mirrors the college campus from whence they came. Though they face some genuine fire and brimstone demons, their real challenge is confronting their own notions of success and sacrifice and acknowledging the ways they’ve each been damaged by academia.
tags: ["Dark", "Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Theater Kid: A Broadway Memoir
author: Jeffrey Seller
description: Jeffrey Seller’s well-written and compulsively entertaining memoir, Theater Kid, gives insight into the circumstances that formed one of the most successful theater producers of our time. Not only does the book detail bringing Hamilton, Rent and In the Heights to life, but Seller examines his hardscrabble childhood as the adopted son of a troubled Jewish family, discovering his passion volunteering with a community theater and coming out as gay during the AIDS crisis. Anyone who loves theatrical memoirs will cherish – and reread – this book.

title: Atmosphere: A Love Story
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
description: Fans of Taylor Jenkins Reid will agree that Atmosphere is one of her best books yet. This thrilling fictional portrayal of NASA’s space shuttle program in the 1980s doesn’t miss. It opens with Joan in Mission Control managing a catastrophe on the shuttle. Then readers flash back almost five years to learn how each of the astronauts earned their place on the mission. The novel is immediately complex, compelling and high stakes. I recommend listening on audiobook so readers don’t destroy a paper copy with tears. Themes include sexism in the workplace, LGBTQ relationships and found family.

title: Joyride: A Memoir
author: Susan Orlean
description: Celebrated journalist Susan Orlean became a pop culture icon when her nonfiction book The Orchid Thief became the basis for the fanciful 2002 film Adaptation, written by Charlie Kaufman (Orlean was played by Meryl Streep). Her new book, Joyride, part writing manual and part memoir, deconstructs a handful of her best-known articles and books (including The Orchid Thief) and answers the questions most often asked at writing conferences: How do you come up with ideas? How do you report a full-length profile? How do you approach structuring a book for the first time? Though she breezes past most details of her life, the book is a master class in nonfiction writing, and Orlean instructs with humility and warmth.

title: Lin-Manuel Miranda: The Education of an Artist
author: Daniel Pollack-Pelzner
description: This authorized biography of Lin-Manuel Miranda, the composer, lyricist, director and actor best known for Hamilton, delves into the fascinating nitty-gritty of the collaborations that help his productions come together. People call Miranda a genius because he seamlessly mashes seemingly disparate ideas together (the American Revolution and hip-hop!) to make work that is sparkling with joy, emotionally resonant and blazingly new. And writer Daniel Pollack-Pelzner brings us so deeply into this work – tracing the birth of individual songs line by line – that we feel the zing of creation.

title: The Garden
author: Nick Newman
description: I normally edge away from post-apocalyptic stories, but this one drew me in with its intimate, slow-burn focus on two elderly sisters. Evelyn tends their garden sanctuary, collecting the eggs and picking slugs off the spinach. Lily … dances. When the life they have built is threatened, they approach this new test in very different ways. What haunted me afterward was the implicit question this climate fiction fable asks: Is it better to focus on the daily duties of life or on its beauties? On survival or on meaning? And can you have one without the other?

title: Culpability
author: Bruce Holsinger
description: AI, autonomous vehicles and shadowy political donors collide in Bruce Holsinger’s gripping, impossibly timely thriller. A fatal crash in a self-driving car shatters one family’s carefully constructed facade, launching a high-stakes investigation into technology, class and betrayal. As they work to determine who’s legally responsible, each family member harbors their own secrets – and the truth won’t stay hidden for long. I couldn’t put it down.

title: Hemlock & Silver
author: T. Kingfisher
description: Healer Anja’s expertise is poisons – specifically, antidotes, although her success rate is marginal. When the king “invites” Anja to save his daughter Snow, the sole survivor of a gruesome family tragedy, she can’t say no. Armed with intellect and curiosity, Anja is assisted by a talking cat and a reluctant guardsman, even as her investigation takes her to a dark mirror world. T. Kingfisher writes with such warmth and wit that it’s easy to become ensnared before you realize the story’s going to chill you to the bone. Hemlock & Silver is a masterpiece of character voice and a deeply creepy, utterly refreshing take on a familiar fairy tale.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark"]

title: Is a River Alive?
author: Robert Macfarlane
description: Robert Macfarlane gives the river a voice in this extraordinary work of nature writing. In pursuit of an answer to the titular question, he explores the lives of three very different rivers, explicating their perils and potential triumphs. What is epic in scope, Macfarlane makes feel intimate and urgent. His thorough knowledge of the subject matter is rivaled only by his mastery of language. These pages are full of passion; of clarity and poetry, fascinating and absolutely gorgeous. I think Macfarlane is one of the best writers working today. Don’t miss this one. If you are interested in life, it’s for you.
tags: ["Science", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Murder Ballads: Illustrated Lyrics & Lore
author: Katy Horan
description: In this fully illustrated book, Katy Horan digs into the history and cultural life of 20 murder ballads. The stories behind the songs are told with careful empathy, impressive research and a storyteller’s surety. Murder ballads are rooted in violence: Somebody’s voice was taken. When possible, Horan gives those who were silenced their due. You don’t need to be a folklore, true crime, or music expert to enjoy this book or appreciate how well Horan’s strikingly evocative illustrations complement the subject. I loved this. Add a list of notable recordings and an extensive bibliography, and this brief but evocative book has an afterlife.

title: The Possession of Alba Díaz
author: Isabel Cañas
description: 1765, Mexico: Alba Díaz flees the plague to the countryside around her fiancé’s family’s silver mine. Elías, her fiancé’s cousin, is a black sheep with a dark secret, only there to get rich and get out. Both are isolated, striving sans allies for security; then they discover a supernatural terror as old as the dark. Isabel Cañas writes nuanced, sympathetic characters and knows exactly what to hide and what to reveal – surprising me more than once, to my delight. Our protagonists can’t take a step without finding a secret, and I couldn’t wait to read more. This is an exemplary gothic full of dread, tension, sensuousness and yearning – a perilously good read.
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: When the Wolf Comes Home
author: Nat Cassidy
description: Jess is having a bad day. Then she winds up on the run with a scared, runaway kid, chased by his monstrous father. What follows feels like classic 1980s or ‘90s horror – gritty and fast-paced with practical effects and over-the-top gore. In the periphery there’s a big bad world, one with mysterious government agencies, dishonest parents and uneasy choices. Nat Cassidy keeps the focus tight, leaving the fleeing protagonists only to amp up the urgency, to reflect on pathos. We struggle with fear, love and perception of ourselves and others; there’s horror in that. Turns out, it’s a lot of fun to read.

title: Audition for the Fox
author: Martin Cahill
description: I’m a sucker for trickster tales, especially involving foxes, and this is a really good one. Nesi’s audition for acolyte of T’sidaan, the Fox of Tricks, is harder than she imagined: She’s thrown into the past to ensure that a specific uprising occurs. If she fails, revolution against the brutal Wolfhounds never happens; the horror of occupation never ends; history changes. Martin Cahill’s novella shows that cleverness and connection are more important than brute force or special gear. Success is finding humor or beauty even with a boot on your neck. You’ll be gladder for reading this engaging, timely fantasy, and for meeting Nesi and T’sidaan. I was. Bonus fables breaking up the chapters are a treat.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Looking at Women Looking at War: A War and Justice Diary
author: Victoria Amelina
description: A brilliant novelist and poet, Victoria Amelina faced Russia’s invasion and assault on her native Ukraine by transforming into a war crimes researcher and real-time documentarian. When I last interviewed her in June 2023, she was deep into writing this book, one focused on “extraordinary women” seeking justice. When a Russian missile killed her a few weeks later, it was left to three of her closest friends and her husband to finish her book using her notes. This book, at times anecdotal, is mesmerizing, combining short narratives, diary-like commentary, raw testimonies and disquieting detail.

title: The Teacher of Nomad Land: A World War II Story
author: Daniel Nayeri
description: In the 2025 National Book Award-winning The Teacher of Nomad Land, Daniel Nayeri again proves he operates on a level all his own. Spare, sharp and profoundly moving, the novel follows Babak and his sister, newly orphaned in World War II-era Iran, as they navigate a world closing in on all sides. When they meet Ben, a Jewish boy in hiding, their fragile hope collides with the brutal weight of history. Nayeri distills war, childhood, grief, loyalty and faith into something achingly intimate. The emotional terrain he covers in so few words is astonishing. This isn’t just a book – it’s a prayer, a lament, a master class in storytelling. Nayeri writes like someone who knows what stories are for: to carry us, to crush us and then to redeem us. (For ages 8 to 12)
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Blessings and Disasters: A Story of Alabama
author: Alexis Okeowo
description: Think of Alabama. OK – now think again. That’s what New Yorker writer Alexis Okeowo asks readers to do when they read her book Blessings and Disasters: A Story of Alabama. To the often black-and-white framing of the state’s history Okeowo adds the dimension of the Nigerian immigrant experience. So, what does that do to the story of Alabama? Of the U.S.? She explores what it means to love a state some Americans are determined to loathe.

title: The Life Cycle of the Common Octopus
author: Emma Knight
description: Oh, I love a great coming-of-age novel. And that’s one element of what Emma Knight has written in her debut, The Life Cycle of the Common Octopus. It’s set in Scotland during a Canadian student’s first year at university – it includes train rides and first loves and solving a family mystery. And there’s wisdom therein! So, grab it for yourself or anyone setting off to figure out who they are in the world.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Secret History of the Rape Kit: A True Crime Story
author: Pagan Kennedy
description: Pagan Kennedy’s book tells the story of the United States through the frame of The Secret History of the Rape Kit. The “box that holds DNA evidence collected after a sexual assault” was something that had to be invented and then disseminated. The woman who fought for it, Marty Goddard, and her untold story are the heart of this book – one of the best I read this year. As Kennedy told NPR in an interview, Goddard “created the idea that every survivor had the right to file evidence and to be treated with respect and to have that evidence taken seriously.” That should not be remarkable, but it was.

title: The Everlasting
author: Alix E. Harrow
description: First things first, The Everlasting is not a book you’re going to get over easily. It’s razor sharp and designed to cut you deeply. You’ll be moved, you’ll probably cry, and by the end you’ll say thank you for delivering my suffering so beautifully. This story follows a scholar and a mythical (lady) knight who have lived the same story countless times – caught in a historical time loop. It’s a book about storytelling, and how nationalism cannot exist without the support of a well-told myth. It’s a thrashing examination of how we choose our heroes. And, most importantly, it’s a love story – about two people who learn over and over again that they’re doomed in every possible way but still choose each other anyway. What exquisite agony, wonderfully delivered.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: The Raven Scholar
author: Antonia Hodgson
description: Let me start by saying that writing a really good epic fantasy is hard to do. But with The Raven Scholar, Antonia Hodgson makes it look like a breeze. The story takes place in Orrun, where a fierce competition to replace the emperor is about to take place. When a contender is found dead before the tournament begins, Neema Kraa, a scholar – with admittedly dubious morals – is tasked with finding the killer. This tale of murder, tragedy and familial betrayal is firing on all cylinders, layering multiple mysteries with characters who defy their own archetypes.

title: Abundance
author: Ezra Klein and Derek Thompson
description: As someone who consumes a lot of news, sometimes it is hard to remain optimistic about the future. Perhaps you feel it too: The woes of the world can be paralyzing. It was refreshing, therefore, to be called to action by Abundance. Well-known journalists and podcast hosts Ezra Klein and Derek Thompson argue that, yes, we really can build and invent our way to the better future we want and need. With a shift in our collective mindset – from one of scarcity to abundance – we can build “not just more, but more of what matters.” One question remains: When do we start?

title: Isola
author: Allegra Goodman
description: This is the story of a 16th-century French noblewoman taken against her wishes to “New France” by her guardian. Upon arrival, she is cruelly cast out as punishment for falling in love and must attempt to survive on a small, remote island. As a castaway, her entire existence hinges on her natural environment: finding food, water and shelter, and not becoming a meal for polar bears. A harrowing page-turner inspired by a true story.

title: A New New Me
author: Helen Oyeyemi
description: Helen Oyeyemi is a brilliant and deeply imaginative writer, so it is no surprise that her latest offering is an intellectually provocative story that will keep you on the edge of your seat. In A New New Me, Oyeyemi introduces us to Kinga, a woman who has a different personality for every day of the week. Can you imagine being present and conscious of your actions for only one day out of the week? Or, even worse, waking up to a man tied up in your apartment by one of your other personalities – one who might be trying to erase the rest of you? A masterful storyteller, Oyeyemi takes us on a wild ride as Kinga unravels this mystery and tries to keep her many lives from exploding. A New New Me is a clever and original story that makes you think about how one’s identity is shaped and whether it can be controlled. A must-read.

title: Vera, or Faith
author: Gary Shteyngart
description: In Vera, or Faith, Gary Shteyngart’s most recent novel, the story of a credibly dystopian America of the near future is told through the eyes of a precocious, chess-playing 10-year-old girl. The book has Shteyngart’s usual combination of breezy writing, incisive wit and poignant cultural observations about American identity, masculinity, cultural snobbery and politics. At the heart of the novel is its eponymous heroine, Vera, who weaves her way through Manhattan in an attempt to find her Korean American birth mother and ultimately learns truths too complicated even for grown-ups. I loved this little book, which despite its bleak themes, leaves you with just the right amount of hope.

title: A Flower Traveled in My Blood: The Incredible True Story of the Grandmothers Who Fought to Find a Stolen Generation of Children
author: Haley Cohen Gilliland
description: Journalist Haley Cohen Gilliland offers an extraordinary investigation into how Argentine grandmothers banded together to find their disappeared children and grandchildren. Rendered in meticulous detail via deep archival research and reporting, A Flower Traveled in My Blood traces how, during the Dirty War in Argentina, a military junta systematically disappeared about 30,000 supposed “subversives,” including more than 300 pregnant women whose newborns were then fraudulently adopted. Through the story of one woman’s decades-long participation in the Abuelas de Plaza de Mayo movement, searching for the truth about what happened to her daughter and grandchild, Gilliland weaves in a profound meditation on questions of generational bonds, identity and what makes a family.
tags: ["Family Matters", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: American Grammar: Race, Education, and the Building of a Nation
author: Jarvis R. Givens
description: In this deeply researched book, Harvard University professor of education and African American studies Jarvis R. Givens locates 1819 as a “crossroads” in the history of education in the United States. That year, Congress passed the Civilization Fund Act, providing funding for assimilative boarding schools for Native American children, and the governor of Virginia signed an anti-literacy law that made it a crime to teach enslaved people to read and write in schools. Amid the Trump administration’s effort to dismantle the Department of Education, Givens’ clear-eyed assessment of American education offers an opportunity to reflect on the long-standing relationships among race, power and schooling in the U.S.

title: Culture Creep: Notes on the Pop Apocalypse
author: Alice Bolin
description: It’s fair to say that I have been anticipating Alice Bolin’s new essay collection since I turned the last page of her dagger-sharp debut Dead Girls: Essays on Surviving an American Obsession in 2018. If in Dead Girls Bolin probed what’s underneath our appetite for true crime stories – and what our desire for such narratives reveals about our culture’s attitudes toward women – in Culture Creep she widens her lens to interrogate a seemingly disparate array of millennial pop culture obsessions, from NXIVM to nostalgia for the teen magazines of the aughts. Under Bolin’s gimlet eye, what connects these topics is the overlap between (post)feminism, late capitalism, and technology, which all inspire cultish thinking. It all makes for an ambitious and unexpected collection.

title: Daughters of the Bamboo Grove: From China to America, a True Story of Abduction, Adoption, and Separated Twins
author: Barbara Demick
description: In 2009, journalist Barbara Demick published a bombshell article on the confluence of the Chinese Communist Party’s one-child policy and international adoption. Her reporting revealed that workers from the State Family Planning Commission kidnapped “excess” babies and toddlers from extremely poor families, transformed them into paper orphans and placed them for adoption to reap profits and satisfy Western demand. In Daughters of the Bamboo Grove, Demick expands on her reporting, unraveling how this scheme impacted the lives of twin girls, one who grew up with her Chinese family in a remote village and the other wrenched away and adopted by a couple in Texas. Their stories lay bare the all-too-human costs of social engineering, family separation and corruption in the adoption industry.
