tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: Elita
author: Kirsten Sundberg Lunstrum
description: This absorbing midcentury American take on Nordic noir opens with two men apprehending a seemingly feral girl on Elita, a tiny island in the Puget Sound that for years has been home only to a federal men’s prison. Kirsten Sundberg Lunstrum elevates the moody mystery with her choice of protagonist – Bernadette Baston, a scholar of child development and single mother, who consults on the girl’s case. Bernadette is fascinated by the child’s fierce independence in a world that sets stark constraints on the lives of women and girls. She must fight for her own independence in order to uncover the girl’s origins in this slow-burn study of insular communities and working motherhood.

title: Great Disasters
author: Grady Chambers
description: This tender and thought-provoking novel of male friendship and the calamities of post-9/11 America follows Graham, a writer still trying to find his footing in his 30s, as he looks back on his coming-of-age and the relationships that once shaped him. “In those years we lived like sisters,” the novel opens, as Graham traces how his tight friend group of six high school freshman boys in 2001 Chicago came to splinter over the years and where that leaves him. Great Disasters masterfully entwines the personal with the national, examining the different ways these six friends have navigated building their lives through the long war on terror, the lost hope of the Obama era and the rise of Donald Trump.

title: Medicine River: A Story of Survival and the Legacy of Indian Boarding Schools
author: Mary Annette Pember
description: Mary Annette Pember, a citizen of the Red Cliff Band of Wisconsin Ojibwe and a national correspondent at ICT News, grew up in the 1950s and 1960s as her mother’s “secret confessor,” listening to fairy-tale-like stories of the horrors she endured at an assimilative boarding school. In Medicine River, Pember traces the repercussions of her mother’s maltreatment, situating her family’s story within the United States’ systemic use of education to eradicate Native cultures. Through an approach that is “part journalistic research, part spiritual pilgrimage,” Pember provides a cuttingly personal account of the history of federally funded Indian boarding schools and a moving look at how Indigenous traditions and rituals can light the path for healing.

title: She's Under Here: A Memoir
author: Karen Palmer
description: In 1974, Karen Palmer, then 17, fell in love with Gil, a 36-year-old in the midst of a divorce; she writes that he seemed like the answer to “How do I escape my unhappy home?” Yet over 14 years of marriage, Gil tormented Palmer with his alcohol and drug binges, illegal schemes, and violence, jealousy and control that only escalated when she filed for divorce. In a bid for safety, Palmer remarried and fled with her daughters to Colorado, where they all assumed new identities. Told with cinematic, heart-thumping detail, She’s Under Here offers haunting meditations on how fear lingers long after we reach safe shores and on what disappearing cost Palmer’s family.

title: The Killing Fields of East New York: The First Subprime Mortgage Scandal, a White-Collar Crime Spree, and the Collapse of an American Neighborhood
author: Stacy Horn
description: In the 1990s, East New York, Brooklyn, garnered an ignominious reputation as New York City’s “killing fields,” because more than 100 people a year were killed there, with children routinely getting caught in the crossfire of gang violence. The status quo explanation for the neighborhood’s violence has long been that its predominantly poor Black and Puerto Rican residents were either responsible for trafficking in drugs and guns or stood by and tolerated the chaos. Journalist Stacy Horn offers a more complex diagnosis of East New York’s decline with her searing investigation of a white-collar criminal conspiracy that lined the real estate industry’s pockets while destroying a onetime affordable enclave for working- and middle-class families.

title: The Tragedy of True Crime: Four Guilty Men and the Stories That Define Us
author: John J. Lennon
description: Over the course of the past decade, alongside the post-Serial boom of true crime, John J. Lennon has emerged as our country’s leading prison journalist, writing for outlets like The New Yorker from inside New York state prisons, where he is serving a 28-year-to-life sentence for second-degree murder. In The Tragedy of True Crime, Lennon reflects on his own crime and time spent in prison alongside the intimately reported stories of three other guilty men whom he has served alongside. This revelatory, challenging book asks readers to grapple with how true crime narratives flatten victim and perpetrator alike, and to consider what it might mean to allow admitted violent criminals to be given second chances.

title: There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America
author: Brian Goldstone
description: In this paradigm-shifting, immersive book, journalist and anthropologist Brian Goldstone follows five families in Atlanta who, despite working full time, struggle to stay housed amid gentrification, a lack of tenants’ rights and low wages. These families, all Black, fall into a “shadow realm” – they are not considered officially homeless by the federal government, but lack a fixed living place as they double up with friends and family, sleep in their cars, or pay exorbitant rates at extended-stay hotels. Woven throughout their stories is a trenchant exploration of how America’s disinvestment in public housing and relentless pursuit of free-market growth have fueled housing insecurity for poor working families.

title: We Survived the Night
author: Julian Brave NoiseCat
description: Sugarcane, the 2024 Oscar-nominated documentary co-directed by Julian Brave NoiseCat, follows NoiseCat and his father, Ed Archie NoiseCat – a renowned traditional carver and member of the Canim Lake Band of the Secwépemc – as they confront the grievous abuses perpetrated against Indigenous children, including their ancestors, at St. Joseph’s Mission in British Columbia. In this rousing book, NoiseCat deepens and expands the work of Sugarcane, exploring how Indigenous peoples have persisted despite centuries of attempted erasure by North American settlers. He weaves together lexéy’em (oral histories), tspetékwll (legends), memoir and on-the-ground reporting in a fascinating multifaceted restoration of Indigenous history and a celebration of contemporary Indigenous life.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "History", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: What Debt Demands: Family, Betrayal, and Precarity in a Broken System
author: Kristin Collier
description: Kristin Collier, like millions of other American students, funded her college education in part with loans. But she was shocked when she first saw her credit report – it listed more than $200,000 in debts, mostly for private student loans that Collier did not take out herself. In What Debt Demands, Collier braids her personal experience of familial identity theft and fraudulent debt together with a broader reckoning with how being saddled with debt impacts every aspect of a person’s life, and how banks, loan servicers and the federal government benefit from borrowers’ suffering. Student debtors especially will find sympathy and solidarity in this book, which takes the dysfunctional American student loan system to task.

title: The Compound
author: Aisling Rawle
description: When contestants are dropped in the remote desert to film a Love Island-esque reality show, they must couple up to stay in the game and complete tasks to receive everything from basic amenities to beautiful luxuries. As the tasks become more dangerous, contestants don’t know who to trust. Who is acting on their own and who is doing so because a task told them to? Even as their season spirals into dangerous chaos, they stay more interested in winning brand deals than their own well-being or in the vaguely alluded-to wars happening outside the show. With commentary on materialism, influencer culture and our ability to look the other way, you feel as trapped in the story as the contestants are in The Compound.

title: Unshrunk: A Story of Psychiatric Treatment Resistance
author: Laura Delano
description: Unshrunk issues a provocative challenge to psychiatrists across the U.S.: Maybe pharmaceutical drugs aren’t always the antidote. In this memoir, Laura Delano shares how a diagnosis of bipolar disorder in her teen years led to a life in and out of treatment. After more than a decade and 19 prescriptions, Delano made the decision to taper off her medication and find relief outside the mental health system. This is an uncomfortable read but one that will leave you wondering how we might take better care of ourselves and each other.

title: Courtroom Drama
author: Neely Tubati Alexander
description: Some people dream of escape to a white sand beach, others the Eiffel Tower, but me? My wildest fantasies are being unreachable by phone. That’s what happens when Sydney Parks gets put on a sequestered jury for a high-profile murder case involving one of her favorite reality TV stars. Sydney’s life is put on pause as she spends her days listening to dramatic courtroom testimony. The rest of the time, she’s holed up at a hotel with no access to TV, the internet or the outside world. She has only her fellow jurors for company, one of whom just happens to be her estranged childhood best friend, Damon.

title: Friends to Lovers
author: Sally Blakely
description: When they were growing up, Joni’s and Ren’s families spent every summer together at a vacation home on the Oregon coast. The two best friends passed idyllic days at the beach and nights camping out on the porch. They haven’t spoken in recent years though, and this summer, everyone is back at the nostalgia-drenched beach house for a wedding. Inwardly, Joni is yearning for Ren, sifting through years and years of memories – right up to that last one where everything imploded. Outwardly, she’s doing her best to pretend that everything is FINE. But the thing about loving (and being loved by) a best friend is that they can see through the facade because they know you to your core.

title: Promise Me Sunshine
author: Cara Bastone
description: Most nights, Lenny rides the Staten Island Ferry until sunrise. She can’t bear to go back to the apartment she once shared with Lou, her now-dead best friend. As she struggles to bear each day without Lou, a grumpy stranger named Miles falls into her life. Like some sort of guardian angel of grieving, Miles offers Lenny a balm for when the pain overwhelms: Do something good for you. Something bad for you. And, a change of scenery. Slowly, Miles coaxes Lenny back to life. On its surface, grief may seem an odd theme for a romance novel, but then again, what is grief if not the purest expression of love?

title: The Heartbreak Hotel
author: Ellen O'Clover
description: This is one for the folks who leaf through guest books, wondering at the lives of previous visitors. After a breakup, Louisa convinces her mysterious landlord to let her turn the beautiful Colorado mountain home she had rented with her ex into a bed-and-breakfast for the brokenhearted. Guests arrive and form community over cups of coffee and chats about grief and love around the kitchen table. Henry, the landlord (did I mention he’s cute and likes dogs?), keeps popping up too, fixing things, joining guests on hikes and perhaps healing a heartbreak of his own. Grief often feels like the tragic end of a story, but romance novels and this one in particular are a reminder that heartbreak is the middle of your story, not the end of it.

title: Where You're Planted
author: Melanie Sweeney
description: After natural disasters, communities often come together, pooling resources to rebuild. Well, the guy at the botanic garden where Tansy Perkins is supposed to set up her makeshift library clearly never got that message. The hurricane that swept through Houston damaged both Tansy’s library and Jack Reid’s gardens. Now the two have to share desperately needed resources to rebuild. The more time they spend together, though, it’s clear that Tansy and the, ahem, Plant Daddy have chemistry. They might even have a future if they can navigate the debris left by previous natural disasters in their personal lives.

title: Perfect Victims: And the Politics of Appeal
author: Mohammed El-Kurd
description: It’s been a while since I read a book that was simultaneously so easy and so difficult to read. Perfect Victims is beautifully written – the prose is gripping and poetic. But sitting with the meaning is a true challenge. It’s a book that forces readers to grapple with who they identify with and why; how our society lets some people be human and forces others to be either angels or monsters. Mohammed El-Kurd shows how Palestinians have been stripped of their humanity in both directions, as “perfect victims” or unredeemable villains. And in spelling out the process, he shows how, under the right circumstances, any group can be written into the same story.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Mother Mary Comes to Me
author: Arundhati Roy
description: When your mother is your anchor, your icon and your tormentor, how do you deal with her death? That’s what celebrated writer Arundhati Roy grapples with in her searing memoir. It’s a raw look at the feminist force that shaped her as an award-winning novelist and essayist. The story begins in Roy’s childhood in Kerala, India, as her mother, Mary, strikes out on her own and founds a school. As a single mother, Mary is constantly fighting for a space for women like herself and her daughter, but the fight creates resentment that she takes out on her two children. So when Roy loses her in 2022, she is surprised by the intensity of her grief over a woman she writes was “my shelter and my storm.” What shape is she without the mother she ran from at 18 to survive? What shape is she without the woman who taught her never to shrink in the face of injustice, without her “gangster”? It’s a story of turbulent love and of liberation that is beautiful, witty and at times uncomfortable to read.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Bring the House Down
author: Charlotte Runcie
description: At the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, a male theater critic savages a one-woman show – and then has a one-night stand with the star. The struggling actress learns of the review only when she wakes up at his apartment the next morning. When word gets out, readers are justly outraged. This #MeToo-adjacent novel could have gone the easy route of hating on its villain for 300 pages, but Charlotte Runcie chooses a more complicated narrative. The protagonist is the critic’s female colleague, who develops a surprising fascination with the villain while staying with him in Edinburgh to cover the festival. When a reworked version of the one-woman show – now focused on lambasting the critic – goes viral, the narrator has to console and even understand her colleague.

title: Discontent
author: Beatriz Serrano, translated by Mara Faye Lethem
description: The funniest office comedy I’ve read in years culminates in the worst company retreat ever – paintball and tranquilizers, anyone? Marisa is an ad agency’s higher-up, but one would be hard-pressed to find a less engaged employee. “I only come into the office to lower my air-conditioning bill,” she says. Discontent is slim and voicey, and Marisa’s unhinged and mercilessly witty narration turns the book’s familiar premise into something delightfully unpredictable. One of the few office novels where you really feel like anything could happen.

title: Paradise Logic
author: Sophie Kemp
description: It’s about time someone dared to write about dating in their 20s in a hyperreal fashion. If you’re a reader who has grown allergic to low-affect Dimes Square coming-of-age novels, Paradise Logic is a perfect antidote. Sophie Kemp takes big swings with her 23-year-old narrator, Reality, whose name reflects only what she’s most out of touch with. Reality only wants to be the “perfect girlfriend” to her idiot boyfriend, and the lengths she’ll go to achieve this perfection are both hilarious and horrifying. How I described it to a friend: It’s like Room by Emma Donoghue, but the room is all of Brooklyn.

title: Something Rotten
author: Andrew Lipstein
description: Andrew Lipstein nails the humorous culture clashes between an expat American and his adopted Danish community. Reuben loses his job at NPR for exposing himself on Zoom, and his wife, Cecilie, is all too ready to spend the summer in her native Copenhagen. Reminiscent of the recent Danish film Another Round starring Mads Mikkelsen, in which a group of friends decide to drink heavily to stimulate their creativity, Reuben gloms onto Mikkel, one of Cecilie’s hypermasculine, antisocial friends. Cecilie, meanwhile, becomes closer with an ex who may or may not be terminally ill.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Colony
author: Annika Norlin, translated by Alice E. Olsson
description: A well-worn literary trope in recent years has been to portray an alternative living community as a scary cult – especially prevalent in dystopian or climate fiction. Fewer books, however, portray these communities without judgment. The Colony sidesteps cliches, and while it doesn’t make every one of its characters likable, it does make this unlikely band of misfits comprehensible. Weaving together multiple storylines spanning 50 years, Annika Norlin creates an off-the-grid colony that may not have all the answers but offers its residents what modern life often can’t: connection and family.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Tokyo Suite
author: Giovana Madalosso, translated by Bruna Dantas Lobato
description: The dual narratives of The Tokyo Suite grip the reader right from the opening chapters, which alternate between the points of view of a mother distracted by her job as an executive and the nanny who kidnaps the exec’s daughter. As this two-hander unfolds, Giovana Madalosso plays with the reader’s sympathies as both protagonists entangle themselves in the consequences of their bad choices. By the end, you’re certain the only path forward is tragedy, but instead you’ll be left thinking about what happens beyond the pages long after you close the book.
tags: ["Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Minor Black Figures
author: Brandon Taylor
description: Minor Black Figures is one of the most perfect books I’ve ever read. It’s about an artist struggling with modern life (and dating a hot ex-priest), and I was so engrossed by all the questions it raised. How do you make Black art when the world so badly misreads it? How can you love when love requires vulnerability? Can we restore our faith in the world when it keeps disappointing or harming us? These threads come together deftly, beautifully, with prose I happily lost myself in.

title: Stag Dance: A Novel & Stories
author: Torrey Peters
description: Stag Dance is an incredibly engrossing collection of stories spread out among very different genres. There’s a lumberjack’s tall tale, a dystopian sci-fi, a teen romance and a horror story – but they all have a couple of things in common: One, they’re all wonderfully rendered, and two, they all explore the margins of gender. These stories explore the murky area between what we understand as cis and trans. Torrey Peters brings her signature wit and heart to get past the labels and into the core of how we all negotiate the ways we want to be seen.

title: Universality
author: Natasha Brown
description: Universality is a delicious, nasty read. A tight novel in several parts, it’s a splashy magazine piece followed by the story of its fallout. As the book unfolds and the magazine article’s truthfulness comes into question, Universality also explores the wretched state of Britain’s media ecosystem and culture at large. So riveting, so funny, perfectly satisfying. It was nominated for this year’s Booker Prize.
tags: ["Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: Fundamentally
author: Nussaibah Younis
description: This book is laugh-out-loud funny in a way you wouldn’t expect, given the heavy subject matter – ISIS brides and extremism. Nussaibah Younis somehow turns one of the darkest corners of modern life into sharp, self-aware comedy. Her humor, about an academic on a U.N. mission to Iraq, is deadpan one moment, absurd the next and always disarmingly smart. It’s a light, quick read that keeps you laughing while making you think – proof that even the most serious topics can be handled with wit and heart.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Humor & Comedy", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: One Day, Everyone Will Have Always Been Against This
author: Omar El Akkad
description: This 2025 National Book Award-winning book is a blunt, courageous, searing condemnation of what Omar El Akkad sees as Western complicity in Israel’s war in Gaza and which he has labeled genocide. El Akkad beautifully, and painfully, captures what it feels like to be an Arab immigrant in the West while watching mass killing unfold in real time, funded in part by that very place he now lives – the grief, the anger, the helplessness. He says out loud what many have struggled to put into words: the discomfort of witnessing, the weight of silence and the question of accountability. A book that refuses to let you look away.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World", "Americana"]

title: The Eyes of Gaza: A Diary of Resilience
author: Plestia Alaqad
description: This book haunts you long after you put it down. It includes the author’s diaries, as well as her photographs and reporting following the Hamas-led Oct. 7 attacks on Israel, offering a clear, intimate and deeply affecting account of the first 45 days of Israel’s deadly campaign in Gaza. It’s a deceptively easy read, but one you’ll want to savor, linger over and return to in thought. Plestia Alaqad’s writing makes the unimaginable feel immediate and impossible to ignore. This book is one of the most important books of our time – essential reading for anyone seeking to understand the suffering of Palestinians in Gaza.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The True True Story of Raja the Gullible (and His Mother)
author: Rabih Alameddine
description: This book – winner of the 2025 National Book Award for fiction – feels like sitting down with an old friend who is a brilliant storyteller. It’s an amusing and beautifully written portrait of a mother and her middle-aged son that lingers long after you finish it. Rabih Alameddine’s prose is so warm and vivid that you can almost hear him talking to you as you read. I loved his tender, nuanced portrayal of Raja’s prying mother, Zalfa, and how he captures the complicated bond between mothers and their children.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Vulture
author: Phoebe Greenwood
description: I grew up in Gaza and covered it until I left in 2017. This novel reminded me that fiction can reveal more about the people who bring us the news than reportage ever can. It shows the relentless stress of the work, the creeping numbness, and the small moments of humanity that survive in spite of everything. I was struck by how accurately it captures the younger version of myself: ambitious, chasing the biggest and riskiest stories, never stopping long enough to understand what that drive was doing to me. Vulture grasps the moral and personal cost of that pursuit with remarkable clarity. Its emotional center is the relationship between the Western correspondent and the local journalist, two people trying to report honestly in a place sealed off and constantly in danger. The novel also exposes the gendered hierarchy of war reporting, where young women are taken seriously only when catastrophe surrounds them. By the end, it reminded me what it meant to live that life and why I stepped outside it.

title: The CIA Book Club: The Secret Mission to Win the Cold War with Forbidden Literature
author: Charlie English
description: The CIA Book Club is Charlie English’s latest true story focused on official efforts to control or destroy narratives of civic culture. The book reveals an astonishing international spy effort to flood the post-World War II communist zone with The Gulag Archipelago, among other titles. His reporting of grassroots book smuggling by the Polish people in an effort to fight Cold War tyranny with literature is a gripping account of citizens risking their lives in the service of meaning. Their story is a timeless reminder of ideas unleashed.

title: Unpacking My Father's Bookstore
author: Laurence Roth
description: I opened Laurence Roth’s biography of his bookseller father anticipating an eloquent journey of memory and meaning. There could be, I thought, a window opened into Roth’s fascinating life as a writing teacher, lover of books and elucidator of history and identity. Roth’s memoir is even more – a beautiful lamentation of his father’s work, an epic family history, a timely illumination of Jewish life in modern Los Angeles. His elegy ends not in despair but instead arrives at a sort of pragmatic consolation. I know the feeling – when I recall my family during Día de Muertos, remembrance is also deliverance. Roth rescues himself and his father with a beautiful – and mindful – embrace.

title: Casualties of Truth
author: Lauren Francis-Sharma
description: Set in the relative present in greater Washington, D.C., as well as in South Africa during the Truth and Reconciliation hearings of the 1990s, this novel shines a light on the devastating impacts of government-sponsored racism. Prudence Wright is an engaging protagonist who struggles to navigate terrible truths as a young law student witnessing the Truth and Reconciliation hearings and, much later, as a mature woman with a neurodivergent son and a successful career behind her, trying to make sense of what she saw. The story may concern South Africa, but the casualties of truth could not be more relevant in today’s America.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction", "Americana"]

title: Song So Wild and Blue: A Life with the Music of Joni Mitchell
author: Paul Lisicky
description: Paul Lisicky adds a new dimension to his collection of memoirs with this elegant book. In lyrical prose, Lisicky brings us into his childhood as a budding songwriter, active in his church choir, awkward and gay. He discovers Joni Mitchell and feels her speaking to him. This volume is a love letter to the iconic folk singer, a meditation on her life and what makes her great, and a shout-out to her work as a painter. Lisicky splices his coming-of-age story in with his insightful exploration of her work, making for an absorbing read.

title: The Seers
author: Sulaiman Addonia
description: Sulaiman Addonia, born in 1974 to an Eritrean mother and Ethiopian father, lived in a Sudanese refugee camp as a child, before landing stateless in London via Saudi Arabia. After mastering English, he settled in Brussels. His third novel (the second to be published in the U.S.) pushes gender, race and national boundaries. Hannah, an Eritrean refugee sex worker, both loving and violent, fixates on men and women. She’s bereft and traumatized yet enamored of London’s wet weather. She lives moment to moment but is consumed by her past. The result is a fever dream of emotion and geopolitics, with Addonia’s command of language and structure making for a breathtaking, original read.

title: The Waterbearers: A Memoir of Mothers and Daughters
author: Sasha Bonét
description: This searing, poetic memoir covers generations of powerful Black women who raise their children singly and transmit strength by showing up. The author’s iconic grandmother may have little money and fewer words, but there’s enough to feed anyone who appears, and plenty of love as well. The author’s mother is tough and wise, purposeful if not effusive. Sasha Bonét writes her own mothering story with brute honesty. Even as she becomes a successful professional, she is dogged by racism. She’s another strong woman in her line, telling it like it is. If only this outsized strength weren’t necessary to live in your own country.

title: Chokepoints: American Power in the Age of Economic Warfare
author: Edward Fishman
description: This book is a survey of how the U.S. has increasingly used the dollar’s global dominance as a way to exert control and influence over allies and enemies. The author is a former U.S. sanctions official, so this is part history, part research and reporting, and part a firsthand account from an insider. It’s immensely readable, despite pretty incredible information density. And with the dollar’s role in the spotlight right now (and maybe in flux), it could not be timelier.

title: Dream State
author: Eric Puchner
description: Eric Puchner’s Dream State is a melodic decades-long journey on the vector of a cluster of friendships. Puchner set out to write about friendship and marriage over the course of a life, so readers get to witness these characters evolve, in their own right and in relation to each other, all the way to the end. That scope makes the mundane wildly profound. Months after I finished reading it, the characters have stuck with me, and it changed the way I think about life.

title: Bread of Angels: A Memoir
author: Patti Smith
description: Bread of Angels is a sequel and a prequel to Just Kids, Patti Smith’s 2010 memoir that’s become a classic New York story. Bread of Angels feels more intimate. Here, Smith discusses her own sexuality and describes her first glimpse of her late husband, Fred “Sonic” Smith, at a party: “He stood by a white radiator in a blue overcoat. I noticed the threads where a button was missing. … He placed the button in my hand. … I felt a gravitational force; … I knew in that moment he was the one I would marry.”

title: Buckeye
author: Patrick Ryan
description: Where else would a novel called Buckeye be set but in Ohio? Its title, however, is the only expected thing about this moving historical narrative, which follows the lives of two intertwined couples in the fictional small town of Bonhomie from pre-World War II to the close of the 20th century. Patrick Ryan, whose previous books include the standout 2016 short story collection The Dream Life of Astronauts, aims to write an American epic and he has the chops to do so, even channeling Herman Wouk to take readers into the thick of naval battles in the Pacific.

title: Last Seen: The Enduring Search by Formerly Enslaved People to Find Their Lost Families
author: Judith Giesberg
description: In 2017, historian Judith Giesberg and her team of graduate student researchers launched a website called the “Last Seen” project. It now contains over 5,000 ads placed in newspapers by formerly enslaved people hoping to find family members separated by slavery. The ads span the 1830s to the 1920s and serve as portals “into the lived experience of slavery.” In Last Seen, her book drawn from that monumental website, Giesberg closely reads 10 of those ads placed in search of lost children, mothers, wives, siblings and comrades who served in the United States Colored Troops during the Civil War.

title: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny
author: Kiran Desai
description: It took Kiran Desai almost 20 years to write this near-700-page novel – her first since The Inheritance of Loss – and, boy, was it worth the wait. The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny explores themes of exile and displacement – not only from one’s home country, family and culture, but also from one’s own sense of self. At heart, though, this Booker Prize finalist is a great love story, abundant with coincidences, misunderstandings and one bona fide art monster.

title: The Very Heart of It: New York Diaries, 1983-1994
author: Thomas Mallon
description: I’ve loved Thomas Mallon’s writing ever since I reviewed Henry and Clara, his 1994 novel about the engaged couple sharing the Lincolns’ box at Ford’s Theatre on that fateful night in 1865. Throughout a career spanning 11 novels – most of them historical – Mallon has captured the tragic and absurd lurking in even the weightiest moments of the past. Turns out that in his diaries, Mallon was doing the same for his own life and times. This volume gathers entries from the years 1983 to 1994, when he’d recently come out and moved to New York, even as the AIDS epidemic raged.

title: Claire McCardell: The Designer Who Set Women Free
author: Elizabeth Evitts Dickinson
description: Every woman has something in her wardrobe she can thank fashion designer Claire McCardell for – a dress with pockets, a pair of ballet flats, a hoodie. This fascinating biography brings McCardell to life – her early days as a sporty small-town girl, her audacious move to New York City in 1925 and her time in the fashion world. After years of working in the Seventh Avenue trenches, copying French designers or creating others’ visions, McCardell was finally able to design the clothes she wanted to wear as a modern, active, working woman – and in the process she revolutionized the American fashion industry and women’s wardrobes.

title: Glitz, Glam, and a Damn Good Time: How Mamie Fish, Queen of the Gilded Age, Partied Her Way to Power
author: Jennifer Wright
description: We don’t party like we used to, as this delightful biography of Gilded Age hostess Mamie Fish reveals. Mamie wasn’t the smartest, prettiest or even the wealthiest, but she was funny and infused her parties with a sense of humor. Consider the time she hyped up a party celebrating “Prince Del Drago,” only to reveal a monkey dressed in a tuxedo as the guest of honor. More than just Mamie’s life story, this book is also a rollicking history of partying in America. These days, it’s wild to read about such reckless, excessive spending – but it is definitely “a damn good time.”

title: The Aviator and the Showman: Amelia Earhart, George Putnam, and the Marriage that Made an American Icon
author: Laurie Gwen Shapiro
description: If you thought you knew Amelia Earhart from children’s storybooks, this biography will make you think again. Though she wasn’t the era’s only female aviator or even the best one, Amelia was the one who captured America’s imagination. The surprising reason: her husband, George Putnam, “the publishing world’s P.T. Barnum,” whose stunts and knack for publicity made Amelia a celebrity. They were quite the team – the showman and the daring aviator – until her final, tragic flight. This book gives us the lovely opportunity to know Amelia not just as an icon, but also a kindhearted and complex woman.

title: You Didn't Hear This From Me: (Mostly) True Notes on Gossip
author: Kelsey McKinney
description: Whether you think of gossip as delicious or trashy (or both), Kelsey McKinney makes the razor-sharp case that it is built into the fabric of our society, with a role in everything from Picasso to politics. McKinney, co-creator of the Normal Gossip podcast, is just as funny as she is smart, so the book reads like an excellent gab session with a friend.

title: Dwelling
author: Emily Hunt Kivel
description: The shoe is a house shaped like a boot, the old lady is really a young lady who has nowhere else to go. Evie is just maintaining when a housing crisis leads to mass evictions in New York City, and she has to seek shelter from a distant Texas relative she’s never met. People interrupt her life in the boot, popping in for shoe repair. This story takes a mad, zany, surrealist turn midway as Evie falls in love with a key master, takes up cobbling and meets talking animals on a quest to rescue her sister from an asylum. It had me gleefully chuckling all the way to the last page.

title: Fair Play
author: Louise Hegarty
description: Abigail hosts a New Year’s Eve murder mystery party at a countryside Airbnb – but in the morning her brother is dead for real. Who killed him? Can a renowned detective solve the crime? Or is it a crime? Is Abigail’s mind playing tricks on her, grasping for all the tropes of a fair play mystery as it seeks comfort and escape from this incredible loss? I cannot believe this clever and ultimately deep story about grief is Louise Hegarty’s first novel: It is a mystery within a mystery within the most existential of mysteries.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Mysteries", "Thrillers", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: How to Dodge a Cannonball
author: Dennard Dayle
description: I thought I was done with reading anything about the Civil War until this kooky satire marched into my life. Conspiracy? It’s got it. Flag twirling? Oh, it’s there,  ultimately deployed with deadly force. A white teenager flips sides – twice – before trying to pass himself off as a flag-bearing octoroon in a Black Union regiment. None of them believes his foolishness, but they tolerate him anyway.  His naivete and vainglory are delicious and hilarious, employed deftly by Dennard Dayle to skewer those who think war is da bomb and those who can’t be bothered to think too deeply about race.

title: King of Ashes
author: S.A. Cosby
description: Family secrets are something and in this Southern crime drama, they burn! When Roman Carruthers’ elderly father is incapacitated by the local drug gang, this prodigal son returns to set things right and protect his hapless younger brother and his hardworking sister. She needs help keeping the family crematorium business going. Know what’s good for getting rid of a body you don’t want around? A crematorium! Roman gets pulled in deeper and deeper as he tries to take down the gang from the inside, just as his sister thinks she’s uncovered the mystery of what really happened to their long-missing mother. This story spins and spins violently to a dark and satisfying conclusion.

title: Murderland: Crime and Bloodlust in the Time of Serial Killers
author: Caroline Fraser
description: Until I read this book, I never really thought about why there seemed to be a number of prominent serial killers in the 1970s and ‘80s, especially in the Pacific Northwest. Caroline Fraser, though, has thought a lot about it – and in Murderland she provides a detailed examination of the confluence of smelting, environmental pollution, poor highway design and murder in the region. It’s a miasma of psychopathy on the individual level and greed on the corporate one that makes you wonder whether the definition of “serial killer” ought to be expanded.

title: Patchwork: A Sewist's Diary
author: Maddie Ballard
description: I quilt. I make things. I love to chat with people who make things, to hear what they think about when they work and how what they make folds into their lives. Maddie Ballard learned to sew during the pandemic, and her memoir-through-projects is a thoughtful progression from basic pants to complex coats and from being part of a couple to being single again. A lovely read for anyone whose brain takes flight whenever they look at a bolt of fabric.

title: Sex of the Midwest: A Novel in Stories
author: Robyn Ryle
description: Loretta has a vendetta against the hot-dog-cart guy. Coach Don has a vendetta against STDs and a need for a new lung. Rachel hates making bloody marys but loves reading Virginia Woolf. There’s a pink lady with pink hair riding her pink bike through the linked short stories about life in a small Midwest town where a sex survey has scandalized some and bored others and where the pandemic has shaken all sorts of assumptions. A rich, surprising and delightful short read.

title: The Hounding
author: Xenobe Purvis
description: These sisters are real dogs. Or not. During an unusual drought in the 18th century that threatens the livelihood of an English village, a resentful boatman starts a rumor that five girls can turn into dogs at will. A quick, weird read about pernicious alternative facts, unreliable narrators and why people willingly believe things that beggar belief.

title: The Savage, Noble Death of Babs Dionne
author: Ron Currie
description: Babs is a clear-eyed, ruthless grandmother who is the de facto ruler of her small, predominately Franco-American town in Maine. The town has seen better days, and when her control of the drug trade is usurped, everything comes crashing down. One daughter goes missing, the other daughter seeks the truth, and Babs makes a defiant final choice. Ron Currie creates compelling and complex female characters operating with cool logic in a morally corrupt world. Funny at times and entirely enthralling.

title: Time Loops & Meet Cutes
author: Jackie Lau
description: Noelle is a cautious, lonely workaholic whose adventure starts when she eats a magical dumpling from a mysterious night market vendor that causes her to keep reliving the same Friday. Avery, a woman Noelle meets in the market, is caught in the same loop but a very different predicament, and together they try to figure out why this is happening and how to get unstuck. Noelle’s strategy involves trying to orchestrate the perfect meet cute with brewery owner (and walking green flag) Cam, who aaalmost seems to remember her, despite meeting her anew each time. Romance is the goal here, but learning to take risks and how to be a good friend are just as important in this book.

title: Casanova 20: Or, Hot World
author: Davey Davis
description: This third novel from Davey Davis is also their best, which is saying something – their previous books, The Earthquake Room and X, were both marvels. This one follows two best friends on opposite sides of the U.S. both dealing with loss: Adrian realizes that his legendary beauty is fading, while Mark is confronted with a grim medical diagnosis. Davis’ writing is nothing short of beautiful, and the book is unsparing but compassionate. Novels this great don’t come around very often.

title: Deep House: The Gayest Love Story Ever Told
author: Jeremy Atherton Lin
description: As he proved in his 2021 book, Gay Bar: Why We Went Out, Jeremy Atherton Lin isn’t content to stay within one genre. His latest book blends his own love story with his partner, a British painter, with a history of LGBTQ+ activists’ struggle to win the right to intimacy with same-sex partners. The fact that this is often a deeply hilarious book doesn’t detract from the seriousness of its subject matter, and Atherton Lin’s unique voice is irresistible.

title: Mercy
author: Joan Silber
description: Joan Silber is one of America’s most underappreciated literary treasures, and she just keeps getting better. Her latest novel follows Ivan, who in 1970s New York experiments with heroin with a friend and then abandons him in a hospital. Silber recounts the fallout from Ivan’s decision with compassion and clearheadedness and explores the themes of friendship and forgiveness with the self-assuredness of a master storyteller – which she undoubtedly is.

title: Origin Stories
author: Corinna Vallianatos
description: Corinna Vallianatos won the Grace Paley Prize for Short Fiction for her collection My Escapee. She returns to the form with this new volume of stories that explore the worlds of love, longing and people trying to navigate their relationships with loved ones. In each story, Vallianatos creates a new world, with characters who pine for something, even if they don’t know what it is. This is a sometimes funny, sometimes poignant collection from a master of the form.

title: The Imagined Life
author: Andrew Porter
description: Andrew Porter has quietly become one of the United States’ most reliably great fiction writers. This novel tells the story of Steven Mills, a man whose family has fallen apart and who embarks on a trip up the coast of California to try to find out what happened to his father, who disappeared long ago. Porter evokes both 1980s and contemporary California perfectly and writes with a psychological insight that calls to mind John Cheever and Richard Yates. He’s that good.

title: The Slip
author: Lucas Schaefer
description: This debut novel from Lucas Schaefer is a sprawling epic with a memorable cast of characters, including a 16-year-old boxing student in Austin, Texas, who disappears and another teenager who seeks answers to questions about his body via a phone-sex line. The book tackles themes of sex and identity beautifully, and Schaefer writes with a self-assurance that’s stylish but not showy. Fans of authors like John Irving, Paul Murray and Nathan Hill will find a whole lot to love in this knockout of a novel.

title: The Stalker
author: Paula Bomer
description: The antihero of Paula Bomer’s novel is Doughty, a liar, misogynist and dyed-in-the-wool sociopath who manages to fail upward by preying on women who fall for his deceit. The novel chronicles his time in New York City, where he hurts everyone he can, with no semblance of guilt or even basic humanity. This is, in part, a darkly funny novel, and Bomer walks a fine line brilliantly – the moments of humor don’t detract from the book’s important themes.

title: The Trouble Up North
author: Travis Mulhauser
description: Travis Mulhauser perfectly evoked northern Michigan, in all its beauty and icy menace, in his previous novel, Sweetgirl. He returns to the region in this new book, which is just as accomplished as his last. The new novel follows a family of bootleggers and smugglers whose criminal enterprise has fallen on tough times, leading one member of the clan to take matters into her own hands. This is a top-notch literary thriller that’s extremely difficult to put down.

title: Wanting
author: Claire Jia
description: How much is enough? That’s the question Claire Jia asks in her debut novel about two childhood friends who reconnect in Beijing and whose lives intersect with an architect whose marriage is falling apart. Jia tackles themes including jealousy, love and the special bonds of friendship perfectly – this is a moving novel, funny in parts and heartbreaking in others, and also a quietly wise one.

title: Wolf Bells
author: Leni Zumas
description: When the government abandons its most vulnerable citizens, others step in to protect them. That’s the hope, at any rate, and in Leni Zumas’ latest novel, it works – at least for a little while. The book follows a house where the elderly and infirm live, assisted by others who live there rent-free. But everything is thrown off-balance when two kids, who are being sought by the authorities, arrive. Zumas’ writing is arresting, and she writes with a compassion informed by a punk-rock spirit. This is a funny, bighearted novel with special resonance in our current era.

title: Words for My Comrades: A Political History of Tupac Shakur
author: Dean Van Nguyen
description: When rap legend Tupac Shakur was killed in 1996 at the age of 25, he left behind a discography that changed American music forever. While his albums and singles have been studied for nearly three decades, Dean Van Nguyen takes a different approach in this book, writing about the influence the rapper’s parents, who belonged to the Black Panther Party, had on him, as well as Shakur’s own engagement with politics. This is a beautifully written, fascinating look at the legacy of an American legend through a different lens.

title: Dream Count
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
description: What do women really want? To be respected, cherished, admired, successful? To have it “all”? Or just … something? Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie’s latest – her first novel in more than a decade – follows the interconnected stories of four women of Nigerian or Guinean heritage as they try to figure out what it is they really want and how to go about having it in a world that is not built for them no matter how privileged or educated or connected they are. It weaves in a shocking (but not surprising) real-world crime while it examines interior lives in a manner that is as real and raw as an X-ray.

title: Dream School: Finding the College That's Right for You
author: Jeffrey Selingo
description: OK, so if you come from one of those Ivy League-or-bust families, then: I feel sorry for you. But if you are the parent of a high school student, a cool aunt or uncle who always knows what to say, a high school guidance counselor or a grandparent who’s picking up part of the college tab, then this is the book for you. And if you are a student thinking about college? Read it to help you decide what you really care about before you throw big money into a prestige trap that might have nothing to do with who you really are and what you want to do in your life.

title: Helm
author: Sarah Hall
description: Helm, a wind that blows through a region of England, is fascinated with humans, the strange animals that encounter and almost immediately anthropomorphize the wind and allow it to weave in and out of their lives. Sarah Hall spins a lively series of stories starring Helm, from a girl in the 1970s who is in love with the wind, to a man hell-bent on quantifying it and, finally, a scientist in the present day who slowly realizes that the very humans who created Helm’s personality may be the ones who bring about its end. Helm’s story, and those of the humans it observes, brims with emotion and delight and manages to wrap up in its small details weighty ideas about humanity, love and climate change.
tags: ["Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "Seriously Great Writing"]

title: We the People: A History of the U.S. Constitution
author: Jill Lepore
description: As the U.S. approaches the 250th anniversary of its founding, it feels like an appropriate time to reflect on where we’re at as a country and how we got here. We the People, by Jill Lepore, a history and law professor at Harvard University, helps satisfy that impulse. It tells the story of the U.S. Constitution, which is among the world’s oldest constitutions. Lepore focuses on battles over amendments, which were fought not just by politicians but by ordinary Americans. The founders designed the Constitution to be amended, but it has become much more difficult to do so over the years. As the Constitution becomes harder to amend, Lepore writes, the risk of political violence becomes greater.

title: The Uncool: A Memoir
author: Cameron Crowe
description: As a die-hard Almost Famous fan, I couldn’t wait to get my hands on Cameron Crowe’s memoir about the years that inspired the movie. Crowe was just a teenager when he started writing for Rolling Stone. The Uncool takes you backstage and on the road with some of the biggest artists of the 1970s – The Allman Brothers Band, David Bowie, Led Zeppelin, The Who, Joni Mitchell and more. In a reversal of the usual tendency to compare the movie adaptation to the book, it’s fun to compare the book to the movie – the real stories are often much grittier and darker than the Hollywood version. I was particularly moved by Crowe’s account of his late sister Cathy, who died by suicide, and how her absence and the silence around her death in part drove him to spend a life asking questions.

title: Woodworking
author: Emily St. James
description: At first glance, the friendship between 17-year-old Abigail and her 35-year-old teacher Erica might raise some eyebrows. But the two share something in common that no one else at their school in small-town South Dakota knows: Erica is a transgender woman, closeted to everyone except Abigail, who came out as trans as a teenager. Life can be difficult in their conservative town, particularly in the lead-up to the 2016 election, in which local leaders have taken anti-trans positions. But any heaviness in this story is counteracted by how delightfully rendered its two protagonists are, with lives full of friendship, romance and chosen family.

title: El Dorado Drive
author: Megan Abbott
description: Megan Abbott’s books trap readers inside highly specific bubbles: wannabe-Olympian gymnastics, a cutthroat science lab, a honeymoon-turned-imprisonment. Her latest bubble is more literal: suburban Detroit during the 2008 financial crisis. Down-on-her-luck Harper Bishop joins sisters Pam and Debra on the Wheel – neighborhood women pooling thousands in cash to lift up one member in need. But if it sounds too good to be true, that’s because it is: The sisterhood is as false as the money cakes; one woman’s windfall is another’s murder motive. Abbott compellingly captures the endless cycles of feast or famine through these sisters whose family already lost everything with Detroit’s auto industry collapse, yet will gamble it all away again to scramble atop the Wheel.

title: The Incandescent
author: Emily Tesh
description: What if you never left the site of your greatest adolescent screwup and stubbornly insisted you could do better for the next generation? Saffy Walden is the director of magic at Chetwood School and its most infamous alumna, having risen from precocious teen to fearsome demonic incursion expert. Her unorthodox doctorate studies into containing demons within puny mortal flesh demand equally staggering brilliance and hubris. When Chetwood’s resident slumbering demon awakens, Saffy’s interventions reveal the far greater threat that lurks beneath her own skin. This eminently worthy new addition to the “magic school” canon interrogates academic burnout, arrested development and the futility of inking over past mistakes while ignoring how you branded yourself in the first place.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Isle in the Silver Sea
author: Tasha Suri
description: This alt-historical fantasy England rises and falls on stories; if an archetypal fairy tale fails to follow expected narrative beats, that part of the Isle collapses into ash and ink. The entire Isle’s survival hinges on the story of “The Knight and the Witch,” who are fated to fall in love and kill each other, lifetime after lifetime. But this generation’s incarnates – bastard lady knight Vina and immigrant witch Simran – hail from Elsewhere, which empowers them to try and finally rewrite their tragedy’s ending. It’s an enchanting critique of how colonialism blurs individual histories in favor of a nationalist narrative, with royal archivists destroying seditious primary texts while Simran and Vina struggle to hold on to themselves through revisionist reincarnations.
tags: ["Sci Fi, Fantasy & Speculative Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World", "Historical Fiction"]

title: These Summer Storms
author: Sarah MacLean
description: For her first contemporary novel, historical romance author Sarah MacLean opens with a tried-and-true trope: Alice Storm sleeps with a fiercely protective stranger the night before reuniting with her estranged (and very wealthy) family on their private island, only to discover that Jack is her late father’s fixer and the overseer of Franklin Storm’s inheritance scavenger hunt. Succession-esque stakes are masterfully anchored by Alice grieving the brilliant but stubborn father whose pride kept her a pariah, despite their being kindred spirits. The isolated setting is lightning in a bottle for the Storm siblings, as they squabble over these cruel games designed to pit reconciliation against recompense – all while Alice and Jack grapple with whether their attraction is fate or Franklin’s will.

title: Oceans of Time: The Musical Autobiography of Billy Hart
author: Billy Hart and Ethan Iverson
description: Drummer Billy Hart, who turns 85 this year, is a jazz legend of multifarious experience: He played with soul-jazz titan Jimmy Smith, with crooner Shirley Horn, and with a funk-obsessed Miles Davis, among dozens of others. Reading Oceans of Time – a slender book co-authored and organized by Ethan Iverson, pianist in the long-running Billy Hart Quartet – feels like pulling up a chair as Hart holds court, spilling stories (and just a little tea). His tone is warmly approachable, even mildly self-deprecating, but he’s clear about what he’s learned and why it matters.

title: Water Mirror Echo: Bruce Lee and the Making of Asian America
author: Jeff Chang
description: Bruce Lee was a marvel in motion and in stillness: the most consequential martial artist of the last century and a pioneer for Asian representation onscreen. This revelatory biography vividly captures his hunger and his hubris, as well as the sparkplug charisma and rigid discipline that made him an icon. But what really elevates Water Mirror Echo is the way Jeff Chang, author of the touchstone Can’t Stop Won’t Stop: A History of the Hip-Hop Generation, places Lee’s story within a concurrent bloom of Asian American identity. Chang tracks that cultural and political dimension so deftly that it feels inseparable from his biographical subject — and more than 50 years after his untimely death, so it is.

title: A Guardian and a Thief
author: Megha Majumdar
description: In near-future Kolkata, India, as the city faces blistering heat and famine, a family’s passports are stolen one week before they are meant to start a new life in the United States. The thief is no stranger; he has caught the mother of this family in her own crime. In a time of scarcity, both his family and hers take every measure to protect themselves, at the cost of each other. What would you do to save the people you love? In the novel, we see both sides of the story: the weight of lies, the humanity we lose in times of crisis, and how far we will go to survive. This National Book Award finalist was an exquisite read, short enough to consume in one go but arresting enough to stay in your thoughts.
tags: ["Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: The Inheritance
author: Trisha Sakhlecha
description: Picture this: a deserted island in Scotland, a family reunion gone wrong, sibling rivalry and a messy murder. The Agarwals are waiting for their inheritance, which will come from the sale of their father’s multimillion-dollar company. Everyone desperately needs the money and will do whatever it takes to get their cut. We know that someone has died, but who is it and who killed them? This thriller is full of foreshadowing, but it’s still unpredictable. It will shock you, even at the last minute and on the last page. With layers of deception and intrigue, it questions how far we will go to protect our secrets and interests, and who we are willing to sacrifice to get there.

title: Where Are You Really From: Stories
author: Elaine Hsieh Chou
description: If you’ve ever thought short stories are boring, this collection will change your mind. From a teenage girl in Taiwan who plans to kill and cook her neighbor into soup, to a mail-order bride who arrives at her new husband’s doorstep in a cardboard box, to an American au pair who moves to Paris to find herself but becomes obsessed with her French doppelgänger, each premise is dark and mesmerizing. In Chou’s signature razor-sharp style, these stories examine loneliness, projection, redemption and revenge – all while being masterfully funny and unexpected. This collection feels like a surreal dream sequence that isn’t afraid to make you uncomfortable, but helps you see the world differently.
tags: ["Short Stories & Essays", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Dark", "Tales From Around The World", "Humor & Comedy"]

title: The Land in Winter
author: Andrew Miller
description: There’s a storm beneath the somber surface of The Land in Winter, Andrew Miller’s 10th novel. The land is the rural West Country of England during the legendary Big Freeze of the early 1960s, when blizzards engulfed trains on their tracks and froze over rivers. The two young couples who anchor the book are, unsurprisingly, frozen too: a gentleman farmer uneasily married to a former nightclub hostess, and a posh doctor’s wife whose husband is conducting an affair with a patient. The action unfolds like a thaw, and by the end of this Booker-shortlisted novel, it feels as though the drama has soaked itself into your soul.
tags: ["Seriously Great Writing", "Historical Fiction", "Love & Romance", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Liquid: A Love Story
author: Mariam Rahmani
description: An unnamed ex-academic decides to marry rich to escape the grim university job market and her lackluster love life. She makes a spreadsheet and tracks her dates, while navigating life in LA and Tehran, her Iranian Indian heritage and a close will-they-won’t-they friendship. The novel is stylish and quick-witted, and it blends heady theory – about love, capitalism, immigration, sex and death – with the brass tacks of parties, hookups, post-9/11 surveillance in the U.S., health care in Iran and more. Mariam Rahmani includes the best details. And the cover art is gorgeous.

title: Can't Get Enough
author: Kennedy Ryan
description: I first met Hendrix Barry in Before I Let Go, the first book in Kennedy Ryan’s Skyland series. From the beginning, she was courageous and outspoken and led with her humor and her heart – especially when it came to her friends Soledad and Yasmen. Hendrix’s story captures what it’s like being a curvier Black woman learning and relearning that she can be all things: a woman, loving, sexual, spiritual, nurturing and free. She shows that there is no shame in the range of emotions that make her whole. In this book, Hendrix faces the painful progression of her mother’s Alzheimer’s, reflects on missed and new opportunities and redefines what romance and fulfillment look like in her life.

title: Victorian Psycho
author: Virginia Feito
description: Dear Reader, if you crave an exquisitely unhinged novel, look no further. Within these pages, you’ll find simmering rage, aristocrats behaving badly and more than a little murder. When our protagonist, Winifred Notty, arrives at Ensor House to become the Pounds’ family governess, we can’t help but admire her dark humor and sharp wit. But Winifred has grisly urges and deranged plans. Virginia Feito choreographs a literary waltz of intrusive thoughts and vile actions – I found it impossible to put down.

title: Daikon
author: Samuel Hawley
description: What if there had been three atomic bombs destined to fall on Japan at the end of World War II – and the first ended up in Japanese hands? From that, Samuel Hawley spins a compelling tale told entirely from the Japanese perspective. Deeply researched science; a window into nationalism in all its ugliness; love, resilience and humanity even as everything around you is destroyed … Daikon is alternative history at its best.

title: The Golden Road: How Ancient India Transformed the World
author: William Dalrymple
description: The Golden Road puts ancient and early medieval India at the heart of an empire of ideas, trade, science, religion and culture. In this masterful work, William Dalrymple, the co-host of a popular history podcast, aims to correct “India’s often forgotten position” as a cultural and economic superpower that, in his telling, transformed Asia and much of the world.

title: On Muscle: The Stuff That Moves Us and Why It Matters
author: Bonnie Tsui
description: I listened to this meditation on muscle over the course of a long bike ride. Physically, I pumped my legs over miles of pavement. Mentally, I was in Scotland with female weightlifting pioneer Jan Todd as she strained to lift the fabled, 700+ pound Dinnie Stones, or I was pondering how humpback whales propel their 50-ton bodies out of the water with the flick of a tail muscle – a feat that tests the limits of muscle fiber. Bonnie Tsui melds scientific fact with memoir and storytelling, weaving a thoughtful reflection on muscle as the stuff that allows us to weather hard things with grace. Like a long bike ride on a beautiful day, it was so lovely I didn’t want it to end.

title: Great Big Beautiful Life
author: Emily Henry
description: Journalist Alice Scott has stumbled upon what just might be her big break – a shot at writing the biography of a tragic heiress and onetime tabloid princess turned recluse no one has seen in years. But to win the book contract, Alice must compete with Pulitzer Prize-winning biographer Hayden Anderson. As Alice and Hayden continue to bump into each other on the fictional Little Crescent Island off the Georgia coast, they begin to see beneath the personas both portray to the outside world, and sparks fly. This is a story about romance, but also about family, secrets and betrayal.

title: Wild Dark Shore
author: Charlotte McConaghy
description: A father and his three (teen and tween) kids live, not exactly harmoniously, on a sinking research island off Antarctica. It’s home to the world’s biggest seed vault and no other humans – until a mysterious woman washes ashore in a storm. Suspicions arise and trust is tested as the family helps the woman regain her strength. Wild Dark Shore is a thrilling page-turner, but all the action and suspense disguise something deeper: a beautiful meditation on love, loss and resilience in the face of climate change.

title: Death of the Author
author: Nnedi Okorafor
description: This book will keep you guessing until the last chapter. The plot jumps back and forth between two connected stories: one about a human author and one about a robot obsessed with human stories. The book tackles some big themes, including fame and immigrant identity. But one of my favorite things about it is that the robot storyline is absolutely gripping. I couldn’t put this book down, and thank goodness I didn’t, or I would have missed the final twist!

title: A Silent Treatment: A Memoir
author: Jeannie Vanasco
description: Shortly after Jeannie Vanasco invites her mom to live in the basement apartment of her home, her mom starts giving her the silent treatment. Sometimes for just a few days, but once for half a year. Why? A Silent Treatment is both a memoir and a chronicle of Vanasco’s search for answers: She makes lists, texts her mom, consults her very patient husband, Chris, calls friends, and asks the internet. “Hey Google. Why might a parent use the silent treatment?” It’s because Jeannie Vanasco is a good writer that this memoir feels raw and intimate – kind of like listening in on someone else’s therapy session – but it’s because she’s a good person that it also feels like a remarkably kind, patient and empathetic book.

title: The Impossible Fortune
author: Richard Osman
description: A wedding guest asks for help and then promptly disappears, setting off another installment in Richard Osman’s Thursday Murder Club series. The central mystery is entertaining, of course – there’s a car bomb, a safe that can’t be opened, octogenarians trying to understand bitcoin (I mean, same). But the main event will always be Osman’s characters: There’s the core four sleuths – Elizabeth, Joyce, Ron and Ibrahim – who are as funny and warm as ever. But one of the things I love about this series is that with every book the world expands more and more – we meet new friends, cops, relatives and criminals (who we will inevitably love) – and get to grow up with them. Because Osman knows that even pensioners have more to learn about love, grief, aging, friendship and cryptocurrency.

title: Hungerstone
author: Kat Dunn
description: This gothic horror novel feels both classic and excitingly fresh. I absolutely devoured this reimagining of J. Sheridan Le Fanu’s Carmilla, where a listless, unhappy wife finds her world turned upside down with the arrival of a mysterious stranger. What follows is an enthralling story about female rage, desire and the visceral thrill of a woman finally discovering her own appetites. It’s a perfect “good for her” novel for anyone who likes their horror atmospheric and sharp.

title: The Manor of Dreams
author: Christina Li
description: Not all ghosts are listless spirits; some are secrets with a legacy. When a reclusive Hollywood starlet dies, her will forces two estranged families to live under the same roof in her decaying, creepy mansion. Christina Li masterfully tells a multigenerational family tale about what achieving the American dream gets you, and what it costs. It also has moments of true suspense that kept me turning the pages long into the night. It’s a quiet, atmospheric and deeply unsettling book that will stick with you long after you finish.

title: This Book Will Bury Me
author: Ashley Winstead
description: If, like me, you’ve ever found yourself a little too obsessed with a true-crime case, this book is for you. It’s also a cautionary tale. Trying to channel her grief after her father’s death, college student Jane Sharp joins a group of armchair web sleuths to solve a murder. With echoes of the 2022 killings of four University of Idaho students, Ashley Winstead turns a critical eye on the voyeurism of true crime. It’s a story that points out the dangers of seeking easy answers and questions our own culpability in turning human tragedy into entertainment. It’s a page-turner that will leave you unsettled and make you rethink your own true-crime consumption.

title: Animal Instinct
author: Amy Shearn
description: In the middle of reading Animal Instinct, I had to stop to text this to a friend: “Divorce is so HOT right now!” In this story, Rachel is stuck inside her Brooklyn apartment at the start of the COVID-19 shutdowns. She craves everything from flirting to full-on sex after her divorce, and as she cruises through the dating apps, she starts to think: What if I could create the perfect AI partner, who would always say just the right thing? She’s got the tech savvy to bring her idea to “life,” but it might take more than chat to satisfy her animal instincts.

title: Wreck
author: Catherine Newman
description: If you haven’t yet reached that stage of life when your body starts falling apart, it may sound terrifying. Wreck reveals the truth: Sure, it can be, but it can also be filled with warmth – a time to lean into your people and throw your hands up as each new indignity presents itself. That’s how Rocky (who you may remember from Catherine Newman’s prior novel, Sandwich) deals with it, anyway, and she makes a good case. Obsessed with news of a terrible accident in her community, navigating the byzantine medical system, Rocky lingers in beauty, too, from the fragrance of her grown daughter’s hair, to the (smug?) satisfaction of turning foraged grapes into sparkling jars of jelly. As a whole, Wreck is both a balm and wry smile – it’s only life, after all (with full credit to the Indigo Girls).

title: History Matters
author: David McCullough, Dorie McCullough Lawson (contributor), Michael Hill (contributor)
description: If history can be a comfort read, this is it. David McCullough’s daughter Dorie McCullough Lawson and his longtime researcher, Michael Hill, assembled this posthumous collection over two years. Some of the historian’s old manuscripts and files were kept in a New England barn, so the occasional acorn and nest turned up along with the historian’s glorious observations about Americans and their history. The essay subjects are diverse – painter Thomas Eakins, Harriet Beecher Stowe in Paris, “A Book on Every Bed” (it will melt your heart). One theme emerges that you might find reassuring in its own way: There was no “simpler time.”

title: The Catch
author: Yrsa Daley-Ward
description: This novel bends genres and time. It opens on the 30th birthday of twin sisters Clara and Dempsey. As children, after the death of their mother, they were raised in different families and have little in common – until that birthday, when they are pulled together after Clara swears she sees their dead mother, very much alive and also … 30 years old. The first half of The Catch is riveting. It’s a nearly impossible story to “land,” but an ending is perhaps beside the point – the story could go on and on.

title: These Days
author: Lucy Caldwell
description: It’s April 1941, just before the “Belfast Blitz,” Germany’s deadly bombing of the capital of Northern Ireland. Two sisters, Audrey and Emma Bell, are both testing boundaries – society’s and their own – to find out what kind of women they want to be. Lucy Caldwell so beautifully balances a book that feels intimate and deeply personal, as if you’re reading someone’s diary, with the greater historical resonance of this very real chapter of World War II history (one readers might not know).
tags: ["Historical Fiction", "Tales From Around The World", "Identity & Culture", "Family Matters"]

title: Tomorrow Is Yesterday: Life, Death, and the Pursuit of Peace in Israel/Palestine
author: Hussein Agha and Robert Malley
description: There’s no way to spin it: This is a book-length reflection on failure. Two insiders to the Middle East peace process (Hussein Agha, on behalf of the Palestinians; Robert Malley under Presidents Biden, Obama and Clinton) reflect on why, despite efforts spanning decades, there is still no lasting peace. A failure to address history – the ghosts of the past – is at the root, they argue. This book is gutting and illuminating.

title: Twelve Post-War Tales
author: Graham Swift
description: Like its title, the stories in this collection are deceptively simple. “Where were you when” is invoked explicitly in one; more often, the stories concern themselves, very quietly, with how great historical events linger in the lives of ordinary men and women. I’m still thinking about “Blushes” – when a 72-year-old doctor thinks back on his 10th birthday while driving to the hospital for a shift in the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic. I’ll be adding this book to my shelf. I’m not ready to part with it.

title: Mood Machine: The Rise of Spotify and the Costs of the Perfect Playlist
author: Liz Pelly
description: Both listeners and musicians are trapped in an abusive relationship with Spotify, the streaming service that has reshaped the modern music landscape. It has dramatically skewed the way we listen, making the experience more passive and the act of creating less tenable. Mood Machine masterfully interrogates this new paradigm. Part origin story, part investigation and part call to action, it creates a panoramic view of a flawed Spotify infrastructure that’s rapidly devaluing musical labor and engagement. Written by a fierce advocate for artists’ rights and one of the streaming economy’s most dogged critics, this rousing industry probe has the tenacity to challenge algorithmic hegemony and the conviction to imagine a more sustainable future.

title: Clown Town
author: Mick Herron
description: For me, the question hanging over the ninth outing for Mick Herron’s band of misfit spies in British intelligence was: Have the Slow Horses jumped the shark? Would the boorishness (and flatulence) of Jackson Lamb – played to perfection by Gary Oldman in the TV series – now seem formulaic? Would the bumbling ineptness of his team feel tiresome? Happily, the answer is no. Clown Town sparkles with the razor-sharp humor, complicated characters and drum-tight narrative that have marked the other eight books in the series. This one involves a secret left in the library of a legendary, but now deceased spy and how Lamb and his crew … Well, it doesn’t really matter. The book is funny, relevant and absolutely delightful.

title: Beautiful Ugly
author: Alice Feeney
description: Author Grady Green thinks he has the perfect marriage with an adoring wife – Abby – who will celebrate a long-awaited success with him. But when he calls her with the good news, the line goes dead and she doesn’t make it home to him. A year after Abby goes missing, he’s downtrodden and wrestling writer’s block when his agent convinces him that time on a remote Scottish island is what he needs to finish his next novel. Once there, he thinks he sees Abby among the inhabitants and it becomes clear that both they, and he, aren’t as they appear. Twisty reveals galore.

title: Black in Blues: How a Color Tells the Story of My People
author: Imani Perry
description: As we’ve become accustomed to scrolling through pictures mindlessly, it’s easy to forget that there can be deep meaning in what we see, stories in a single color. Imani Perry dives deep to explore what the color blue has represented for generations of Black people across continents. It’s a color that represents hope. A color that became a sound. It’s the history in the hue of indigo dye, so valued that indigo cloth was a major currency of the slave trade. It’s a color that, like Black culture itself, contains multitudes and Perry’s meditation renders it in profound clarity.

title: Careless People: A Cautionary Tale of Power, Greed, and Lost Idealism
author: Sarah Wynn-Williams
description: Sarah Wynn-Williams was a young, idealistic policy wonk and diplomat who saw the potential of Facebook to influence geopolitics for good and wanted to be a part of it. Making a persistent, passionate case for the influence that the social media site could have, she got in the door, where she helped rearrange the seating charts to position the company’s leaders alongside global heads of state. What starts as a kind of dishy peek inside a tech startup growing into a Goliath morphs into an exposé of the people in charge failing to understand their power. Meta disputes the book’s allegations and argues the author violated a nondisparagement clause in her severance agreement.

title: Girl on Girl: How Pop Culture Turned a Generation of Women Against Themselves
author: Sophie Gilbert
description: Girls navigating the path to womanhood in the early aughts faced an onslaught of media telling them who and how to be. Contradictory depictions of young women were everywhere across pop culture: purity culture clashed with Girls Gone Wild, reality TV made beauty and love commodities, models went from “super” women to teenage waifs. Sophie Gilbert explores the influence pornography had on culture at the time, providing vital insight into messages for young girls that were often objectifying even if they were sold as empowering. For anyone who enjoys You’re Wrong About or craving connective tissue to Monsters: A Fan’s Dilemma.

title: Good Dirt
author: Charmaine Wilkerson
description: Ebby Freeman’s idyllic childhood turned upside down the day she saw her big brother Baz shot and killed during a home invasion. Her peace shattered alongside the stoneware jar that broke the day Baz died, a jar that generations of her family had passed along starting in slavery to eventual prosperity. As she grows into adulthood, Ebby reckons with the lingering effects of prejudice and secrets in her broken engagement to a white man. This novel is a timely reminder that values, strength and connection sustain us more deeply than falsely taken power or the worship of status or things.

title: I See You've Called in Dead
author: John Kenney
description: As an obituary writer, Bud Stanley knows a thing or two about leaving a legacy.
