But when he writes and accidentally publishes his own fantastically fictional obituary one night, the consequences rewrite his own life story. While HR figures out whether they can fire someone coded as “deceased” in their system, Bud finally starts to cope with the emotional fallout of his divorce. In a journey that’s both laugh-out-loud funny and moving, Bud and his friends figure out what a life well lived really looks like.

title: Loved One
author: Aisha Muharrar
description: When someone dies suddenly – young, healthy, in pursuit of their ambitions – it’s hard to come to terms with the loss. Not only is that person gone, but so is the part of you who existed in relation to them. We meet Julia in the throes of this reckoning as she mourns her first love, Gabe, who became a close friend after their romance. At Gabe’s mother’s request, Julia is sent to retrieve some items from the home where he lived with Elizabeth, his most recent ex-girlfriend. The novel follows these three women as they attempt to move forward under the weight of their shared grief.

title: Memorial Days: A Memoir
author: Geraldine Brooks
description: Grief is both universal and extremely personal. When Geraldine Brooks’ vibrant husband, renowned fellow writer Tony Horwitz, died suddenly away from home, the shock was all-consuming. Moving through all of the paperwork, the practical tasks that needed tending didn’t allow Brooks to truly mourn the loss of her companion, lover, co-parent, family raconteur … the one who dealt with the accountant. Three years later, on a remote Australian island, Brooks really reckons with her grief and the ways in which our cultures and faiths support – or minimize – time for it. A beautiful ode to a life, a partnership and living with loss.

title: Not My Type: One Woman vs. a President
author: E. Jean Carroll
description: Journalist and columnist E. Jean Carroll says in her book that she was advised by counsel to keep her remarks short during two civil trials over her accusations that Donald Trump had raped and later defamed her. Now that she’s out of the witness box – and after the sexual abuse and defamation judgment against Trump – she’s telling the rest of her story. Carroll’s account of the preparation for, and unfolding of, the trials is part riveting legal drama and part courtroom-style compendium that’s utterly full of heart and conviction. Her insights are compelling and sharp about both the case itself and how our society judges women, views aging and treats survivors of sexual assault.

title: Not Quite Dead Yet
author: Holly Jackson
description: Twenty-seven-year-old Jet Mason has been stuck in neutral in her idyllic Vermont hometown. She’s gearing up to make changes when, on Halloween night, she’s viciously attacked and left for dead in her parents’ home. While she survives the attack, she learns she will not survive the brain aneurysm forming in its aftermath. Rather than go through a risky surgery, Jet decides she has wasted too much time being passive and is going to use her final week to solve her own pending murder. Duplicity abounds among her friends and family, and the tangled betrayals she unwinds set the entire community roiling.

title: Nothing More of This Land: Community, Power, and the Search for Indigenous Identity
author: Joseph Lee
description: Journalist Joseph Lee grew up spending summers on Martha’s Vineyard, working in his parents’ store and attending tribal camp. His book is an exploration of his identity as a member of the Aquinnah Wampanoag, the tribe that called the island home long before it became a getaway for wealthy vacationers. He highlights the ways in which tribal communities are applying the knowledge of their ancestors in our modern culture, and across a patchwork of tribal systems, he finds a common thread of momentum and growth that’s encouraging and affirming.

title: The Antidote
author: Karen Russell
description: A novel that’s both an epic and an omen, this work of historical fiction is rooted in Dust Bowl tragedies that transcend time and place. In a landscape that surely feels cursed, Karen Russell introduces us to, among others, a Prairie Witch, whose gift is radical listening, providing a kind of proto-therapy that takes the weight of “whatever they can’t stand to know” from her patrons. Histories we collectively cannot withstand and what they mean for our future are at the core of this National Book Award finalist, in which Russell masterfully renders the strange quotidian and imbues the everyday with menace or magic.

title: The Midwatch Institute for Wayward Girls
author: Judith Rossell
description: The orphanage nuns have had enough of Magdalena “Maggie” Fishbone, so they send the spirited girl off to the Midwatch Institute for Wayward Girls to be “tamed good and proper.” But as soon as they’ve arrived, Maggie and the other two unfortunate girls dropped off that day see a teacher shoot fireworks out of the organ and realize Midwatch isn’t just about manners. It turns out they’ve been sent to the Institute to be trained to fight crime and solve mysteries, which turns out to be a lot more fun than being proper. Captivating black-and-white illustrations capture the grit and determination of these wonderfully wayward girls. (For ages 8 to 12)

title: Becoming Ghost: Poetry
author: Cathy Linh Che
description: In 1976, the author’s parents, then Vietnam War survivors living in a refugee camp in the Philippines, were able to earn relatively good wages by becoming extras in Francis Coppola’s Apocalypse Now. They mostly played civilian casualties or Vietcong snipers who were background scenery or “ghosts” in the director’s operatic but ultimately hollow Vietnam parable. Cathy Linh Che restructures lines in Coppola’s filmscript to create dramatic monologues that lend texture and corporeality to her parents’ cinema-worthy history. Her approach is both poignant and humble. While wishing to rectify the past by serving as a vessel for her parents’ voices, Che is fully aware that such an attempt at capturing their elusive narratives is still a scripted effort. The collection was a finalist for the National Book Award.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Short Stories & Essays", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: Killing Stella
author: Marlen Haushofer, translated by Shaun Whiteside
description: Marlen Haushofer’s Killing Stella has much in common with the claustrophobic-yet-idyllic milieu of White Lotus or Patricia Smith’s Ripley novels. Originally published in German in 1958 and incisively rendered into English only recently by Irish translator Shaun Whiteside, this deceptively restrained novella by the Austrian author – framed as a written confession by an otherwise thoughtful housewife about her non-intervention regarding her husband’s casual seduction of a guileless teenager – anticipated Anthony Burgess’ A Clockwork Orange and Hannah Arendt’s famous “banality of evil” thesis. The novel’s chilling dissection of moral apathy, while confined to a small group of well-educated bourgeois in an urban European setting, is especially prescient since it implicates all of us. Our natural sins of omission can be extended to any marginalized individual or group, due to our cowardice, our insistence on the status quo, or our need to maintain emotional safety and social stability.

title: The Migrant Rain Falls in Reverse: A Memory of Vietnam
author: Vinh Nguyen
description: Inspired by Li-Young Lee’s poem “Visions and Interpretations,” in which “the migrant rain” represents a recurring burden, this lyrical memoir conjures an alternative fate for the author’s absent father, presumably drowned at sea while trying to escape Vietnam by boat. In Vinh Nguyen’s speculative universe, the father, while surviving the border crossing, still does not lead a happy life but succumbs to ill health after years of stress and hard labor. Via this poignant experiment, Nguyen astutely shows how a refugee’s migration narrative can be at once forward-oriented and tragically regressive; the idea of refuge cannot be defined by legal or political considerations but, as he explains, “something to be lived, something that is not yet over or that is still to come.”
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "Family Matters", "Identity & Culture", "Tales From Around The World", "History"]

title: We Computers
author: Hamid Ismailov, translated by Shelley Fairweather-Vega
description: This National Book Award finalist, an exuberant homage to all forms of translation and reinvention, is replete with infinite mirror effects. AI, its first-person plural narrator, is a Scheherazade-like computer program taught to analyze and generate literature. AI’s creator and rival, Jon Perse, a French poet and Lacan-trained psychologist, in turn acquires an extensive knowledge of Persian poetry from his Uzbek translator and colleague, Abdulhamid Ismail, whose initials are also AI – and whose name can be found overlapping the author Hamid Ismailov’s – suggesting that the computer program, its programmer, the Uzbek translator and the book’s real author are all intrinsically connected or reflected by their alternate selves.
tags: ["Love & Romance", "Identity & Culture", "Seriously Great Writing", "Tales From Around The World"]

title: A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck
author: Sophie Elmhirst
description: It’s 1972, and a young British couple revolts against conventional suburban life. They sell their possessions and build a boat, intent on sailing to New Zealand. But a whale upends their plans – their boat sinks and they find themselves in a brutal endurance test. Floating in a life raft in the Pacific Ocean, they kill sharks with their bare hands, fish with safety pins, befriend (and eat) sea turtles and struggle to stay alive. But it’s not just a survival story; it’s a moving tale about marriage, boredom and making the best use of time. It brought me back to the pandemic and what it felt like to lose track of days. This is a true story, beautifully written by Sophie Elmhirst, who pored over logbooks and journals documenting all 118 days of the shipwreck.

title: Toni at Random: The Iconic Writer's Legendary Editorship
author: Dana A. Williams
description: Years before becoming a household name as a novelist, Toni Morrison was an editor at Random House. Those years are the subject of Dana A. Williams’ Toni at Random. Morrison’s goal was to document the Black experience. But to get there, we learn just how deft a businesswoman Morrison was: She knew how to speak to bottom-line-obsessed corporate publishers and reclusive writers in equal measure. The book is full of tension – fights with writers over prose and with fellow editors for respect – but it always comes back to the craft, and the care Morrison took to getting it right on the page. Her brilliance was always evident – it’s just nice to have a fuller picture of it.

title: The Fate of the Day: The War for America, Fort Ticonderoga to Charleston, 1777-1780
author: Rick Atkinson
description: I’ve been eagerly waiting years for this book! This is the second volume of Rick Atkinson’s trilogy on the American Revolution. Atkinson makes good use of letters and diaries. You feel like you’re in the middle of a battle, with all the sights, sounds and tragedy. Harrowing tales of hand-to-hand fighting, scalping and desperate evacuations. Fine detail: the waxed mustaches of the Hessian forces, the number of rum barrels distributed to weary and ill-clad troops, the dull thud of cannonballs smacking into ships. The stench of makeshift hospitals, with piles of limbs stacked outside. He carefully lays out how the battles began, and the successes, mistakes and missed opportunities  – on both sides.

title: The Devil Reached Toward the Sky: An Oral History of the Making and Unleashing of the Atomic Bomb
author: Garrett M. Graff
description: This summer, the world marked 80 years since nuclear weapons were dropped on Japan. The author mostly features voices of the scientific and military personnel involved in the development of the bomb. But it’s interspersed with the memories of those whose lives were uprooted by the work in the United States because of eminent domain and those in Japan who had to live with the deadly, painful, lifelong consequences of the bomb. The audiobook version includes a full cast recording.

title: Bury Our Bones in the Midnight Soil
author: V.E. Schwab
description: Some authors quickly become “instant reads” – and this one is at the top of my list. Bury Our Bones in the Midnight Soil, the exquisite turn of phrase that V.E. Schwab uses, packs juicy bites of hunger, rage and desperation. Feminist horror that spans centuries, it weaves together the stories of María (1532 Spain), Charlotte (1827 London) and Alice (2019 Boston). With strong metaphors for queerness, power and desire draped in a style of light gothic horror, it’s perfect for those interested in bite-size introductions to a darker genre. Five stars.

title: Onyx Storm
author: Rebecca Yarros
description: If you haven’t picked up this series, let me be your sign from the universe telling you “DO IT NOW.” The third installment of the Empyrean series (starting with Fourth Wing) picks up right where the previous book left off, and we immediately embark on new adventures that test the limits of what our group of cadets knows to be truth, of the strengths of their friendships and of their unit, of their magical and personal bonds with their dragons, and of the fragility of the kingdom they thought they knew in the face of an unknowable enemy. The stakes are raised even higher, with the search for new information to save the world they love, as well as those they have previously considered enemies. This book made me laugh, swoon, cry, and cry again – and again – in the three days it took me to inhale it. I now sit and stare at the calendar, wondering when the next book will be released.

title: A Witch's Guide to Magical Innkeeping
author: Sangu Mandanna
description: Sera Swan broke a law of magic in a moment of immense emotion as a young witch and, as a result, lost her magic – and access to the magic community meant to educate and guide her. This lovely, cozy, whimsical fantasy takes us through love and loss and is wrapped up in magic, family, friends, healing and ghosts of memories past. Sera has a shot at a second chance: Will she trust herself and those around her enough to take it? Sangu Mandanna continues to amaze with a lovely standalone set in the same world of her previous A Very Secret Society of Irregular Witches. Thinking about re-reading this book makes me want to kick my feet in excitement.

title: Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future
author: Dan Wang
description: In a crowded field of China-themed books, Dan Wang’s book has already captured significant media and policy attention. While its core thesis isn’t entirely new, the timing makes it urgent. It forces Americans to confront a lingering question: How did the U.S. lose its ability to build, and can it reclaim that capacity today? It’s a book with China in the title, but America at its heart.

title: Indignity: A Life Reimagined
author: Lea Ypi
description: A viral social media post of her grandmother’s honeymoon photo in Mussolini’s Italy sparked Albanian philosopher Lea Ypi to reimagine Leman Ypi’s remarkable life. Indignity is part memoir, part historical reconstruction of the Balkans’ 20th-century turmoil: wars, dictatorships, forced migrations and sweeping societal upheavals. It is also a work of philosophy, exploring the meaning of dignity and its absence. With the narrative grace of a novelist and the depth of a philosopher, Ypi, whose previous memoir, Free, has been translated into 30 languages, crafts a story that feels both intimate and profound.
tags: ["Biography & Memoir", "History", "Tales From Around The World", "Family Matters"]

title: The Finest Hotel in Kabul: A People's History of Afghanistan
author: Lyse Doucet
description: Acclaimed Canadian journalist Lyse Doucet’s The Finest Hotel in Kabul is as elegant, incisive and poignant as her decades of reporting for the BBC. More than a chronicle of a landmark hotel, this book is a meditation on survival, memory and the human cost of decades of war in Afghanistan. Through vivid portraits of Afghan women and men whose lives – and deaths – intertwined with the Inter-Continental Kabul hotel, Doucet captures a nation’s struggle between hope and despair. Her storytelling blends journalistic rigor with literary grace, making these stories not just history, but testimony.

title: The South
author: Tash Aw
description: At times, Tash Aw’s The South evokes the quiet intensity of Chekhov. It explores the contradictions within a Malaysian family: generational divides, subtle tensions around sexuality, and class differences – set against the backdrop of late-1990s Malaysia during the Asian financial crisis. At its heart is Jay Lim, a teenager navigating desire and identity amid the disarray of a failing family farm. This coming-of-age novel, longlisted for the Booker Prize, is the first in a planned quartet. It’s a strong opening, and a compelling reason to anticipate the Lim family’s journey in the volumes to come.

title: Black Woods, Blue Sky
author: Eowyn Ivey, illustrated by Ruth Hulbert
description: This novel takes the canonical dangers that confront children in fairy tales – menacing creatures, unforgiving landscapes, unreliable adults – and refashions them into a story that feels new and surprising. Birdie is a single mother raising her young daughter, Emaleen, in rural Alaska. When Birdie falls in love with a recluse named Arthur, it’s a chance for a fresh start and a freer life in the wild. But their idyll is threatened by Arthur’s true nature. In this story, the woods are dark and scary. But they’re also a place that cultivates resilience, love and forgiveness.
